**Computer Numerical Control (CNC) Machining** is a **subtractive manufacturing** process in which pre-programmed computer software dictates the movement of factory tools and machinery (mills, lathes, routers, EDM). Material is *removed* from a solid workpiece (stock) to produce a customized part.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board Highlight:** Examiners often frame the comparison under the umbrella of *"Traditional vs. Modern Manufacturing Paradigms."* CNC represents the **digitally-controlled subtractive era (post-1950s)**, while AM represents the **digital layer-wise era (post-1986)**. Always cite ISO/ASTM 52900 when defining AM.

### 1.1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Sculptor's Dilemma

Imagine you want to create a **perfectly shaped chocolate elephant**.

- **CNC Machining** is like starting with a *huge block of chocolate* and using a very sharp, computer-guided knife to **carve away** everything that is *not* an elephant. The scraps (chips/swarf) are waste. If your elephant has a hollow inside, the knife must physically reach in — which is often impossible.

- **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is like having a *tiny precision chocolate nozzle* (a nozzle that extrudes melted chocolate, or a laser that fuses powder). The nozzle moves along a path and **lays down only the material** needed to form the elephant — layer by layer, from the bottom up. Hollow structures, internal lattices, and complex topologies are *easy*. There is almost **zero waste**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Summary:**
> - **CNC** = Sculpture *from* a block (**Subtractive**).
> - **AM** = Sculpture *with* material only (**Additive**).
> - **Subtractive** → Removal of material.
> - **Additive** → Addition of material.

### 1.1.3 Core Process Constants & Metrics

The following standardized metrics are universally accepted in **manufacturing science** and are **bolded** as high-yield KTU terminology:

- **Layer Thickness (*t*)** → measured in **µm** (typically **20–200 µm** for polymer AM).
- **Build Volume (*V_b*)** → measured in **mm³** or **cm³**.
- **Material Utilization Factor (*η*)** → dimensionless percentage.
- **Dimensional Tolerance (ISO 2768)** → measured in **± mm**.
- **Surface Roughness (*R_a*)** → measured in **µm**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Fundamental Operational Differences (Structured Logic)

The conceptual distinction between **AM** and **CNC** can be broken down along **7 critical axes**:

1. **Material Flow Direction**
   - **CNC:** Stock → Chips (material leaves the part).
   - **AM:** Filament/Powder/Resin → Solidified Part (material joins the part).

2. **Geometric Complexity vs. Accessibility**
   - **CNC:** Limited by **tool accessibility**. Internal cavities, undercuts, and complex internal lattices are *difficult or impossible* without multi-axis setups (5-axis).
   - **AM:** **Tool-access independent**. Internal channels, lattice structures, and topology-optimized geometries are *intrinsically producible*.

3. **Material Waste (Sustainability)**
   - **CNC:** High waste. Buy-back value of chips is limited.
   - **AM:** Near-zero waste; unmelted powder can be recycled.

4. **Lead Time & Cost Structure**
   - **CNC:** Cost is dominated by **machining time × hourly rate** (linear with complexity).
   - **AM:** Cost is dominated by **material volume × part volume** (weakly dependent on geometric complexity).

5. **Design Freedom & Customization**
   - **CNC:** Fixed tool library; design must conform to tool reach.
   - **AM:** **Mass customization** without retooling — ideal for patient-specific implants, dental crowns, and aerospace brackets.

6. **Mechanical Properties & Anisotropy**
   - **CNC:** Parts are typically **isotropic** (wrought billet stock).
   - **AM (especially SLM/DMLS):** Parts often exhibit **anisotropy** due to layer-wise bonding — strength in the Z-direction may be **30–60 % lower** than in the XY-plane.

7. **Tolerance & Surface Finish**
   - **CNC:** Tolerances of **±0.01 mm** achievable; surface roughness **R_a ≈ 0.4–1.6 µm**.
   - **AM (as-built):** Tolerances of **±0.1–0.3 mm**; surface roughness **R_a ≈ 5–20 µm**. (Post-processing required for high precision.)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are *frequently asked in numerical/derivation questions* in KTU ESE for manufacturing modules. Memorize these.

| **Parameter** | **Formula** | **Units** | **Application** |
|---|---|---|---|
| AM Build Time | $T_b = \dfrac{V_p}{R_d \cdot t}$ | hours | Time to print a part |
| Material Utilization (AM) | $\eta_{AM} = \dfrac{M_{final}}{M_{input}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Sustainability metric |
| Material Utilization (CNC) | $\eta_{CNC} = \dfrac{M_{final}}{M_{stock}} \times 100\,\%$ | % | Subtractive efficiency |
| Material Removal Rate (CNC) | $Z = w \cdot d \cdot f$ | mm³/min | Volumetric MRR for milling |
| Specific Energy Consumption | $E_s = \dfrac{P_{total}}{MRR}$ | J/mm³ | Energy per unit volume removed |
| Surface Roughness (AM) | $R_a \approx k \cdot t$ | µm | Empirical layer-thickness relation |
| Taylor's Tool Life (CNC) | $V \cdot T^n = C$ | m/min, min | Tool wear economics |
| Part Complexity Index | $C_{AM} \approx \dfrac{V_{internal}}{V_{external}}$ | dimensionless | Compares lattice freedom |

**Where:**
- $V_p$ = Part volume
- $R_d$ = Material deposition rate
- $t$ = Layer thickness
- $w$ = Width of cut, $d$ = Depth of cut, $f$ = Feed rate
- $P_{total}$ = Total power consumed
- $V$ = Cutting speed, $T$ = Tool life, $n$ & $C$ = empirical constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** In the **aerospace industry** (e.g., GE LEAP fuel nozzle), the same part produced by CNC requires *welding 18 separate pieces*, whereas AM produces it as **a single monolithic part** with **30 % weight reduction** and superior mechanical performance. This single case study can fetch 4–5 marks in KTU ESE if explained properly.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Comparison of Material Utilization (η)

Let us rigorously derive and compare the **material utilization factor** for both processes.

### For CNC (Subtractive Process):

A cylindrical workpiece of diameter $D$ and length $L$ is used to produce a final part of mass $M_{final}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Stock Volume: } V_{stock} &= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \\[6pt]
\text{Final Volume: } V_{final} &= \frac{M_{final}}{\rho} \\[6pt]
\text{Stock Mass: } M_{stock} &= \rho \cdot V_{stock} = \rho \cdot \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L \\[6pt]
\therefore \eta_{CNC} &= \frac{M_{final}}{M_{stock}} = \frac{V_{final}}{V_{stock}} \\[6pt]
\eta_{CNC} &= \frac{4 \cdot V_{final}}{\pi \cdot D^2 \cdot L} \times 100\,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** As complexity increases (smaller $V_{final}$), $\eta_{CNC}$ drops sharply because the bounding stock envelope remains roughly constant.

### For AM (Additive Process):

Material is deposited only where needed, plus support structures.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Material Input: } M_{input} &= M_{final} + M_{support} + M_{waste} \\[6pt]
\text{Typical: } M_{support} &\approx (0.1 \text{ to } 0.3) \cdot M_{final} \quad \text{(for parts needing supports)} \\[6pt]
\therefore \eta_{AM} &= \frac{M_{final}}{M_{final} + M_{support} + M_{waste}} \\[6pt]
\eta_{AM} &\approx \frac{1}{1.1 \text{ to } 1.3} \times 100\,\% \approx 77\% - 91\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Note:** For powder-based AM (SLS/SLM/DMLS), unmelted powder is *recovered and recycled*, pushing $\eta_{AM}$ above **95 %** in practice.

## 3.2 Numerical Worked Example — AM Build Time

**Problem (Typical KTU 2-mark style):** A part with volume $V_p = 24{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$ is to be built using FDM. The deposition rate is $R_d = 20 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$ and layer thickness $t = 0.2 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the build time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_b &= \frac{V_p}{R_d \cdot t} \\[6pt]
T_b &= \frac{24{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}{20 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s} \times 0.2 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
T_b &= \frac{24{,}000}{4} \text{ s} \\[6pt]
T_b &= 6{,}000 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
T_b &= \frac{6{,}000}{3{,}600} \text{ h} = 1.667 \text{ hours} \approx 1 \text{ hr } 40 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Notice that **build time does not depend on geometric complexity**, only on part volume. This is a *fundamental paradigm shift* from CNC, where complex parts take *proportionally longer* to machine.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Master Reference Table)

| **Attribute** | **CNC Machining** | **Additive Manufacturing** |
|---|---|---|
| Process Nature | **Subtractive** | **Additive** |
| Material Form | Solid billet / block | Powder / wire / filament / resin |
| Material Utilization ($\eta$) | 10 % – 40 % | 75 % – 95 % (with recycling: >95 %) |
| Geometric Complexity | Limited by tool reach | Virtually unlimited |
| Internal Features | Requires multi-axis tooling | Inherently producible |
| Dimensional Tolerance | $\pm 0.01$ mm | $\pm 0.1$ mm (as-built) |
| Surface Roughness ($R_a$) | 0.4 – 1.6 µm | 5 – 20 µm |
| Mechanical Anisotropy | Near-isotropic | Anisotropic (layer-dependent) |
| Tooling Cost | Moderate to high | Zero tooling |
| Lead Time (Single Part) | Hours to days | Hours to days (similar) |
| Lead Time (100 parts) | Scales linearly | Batch-printable in same build |
| Setup Time | Significant | Minimal |
| Skilled Labor Required | High (machinist) | Low–Moderate (CAD + operator) |
| Capital Cost (Machine) | ₹5L – ₹1Cr | ₹2L – ₹5Cr |
| Sustainability / Waste | High (chips, coolant) | Low (powder recyclable) |
| Best Suited For | High-tolerance, high-volume | Low-volume, complex, customized |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Functional Process Flow — CNC vs. AM

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CAD Model] --> B1[Generate G-Code via CAM Software]
    B1 --> C1[Fix Stock Block to CNC Worktable]
    C1 --> D1[Spindle Rotates + Tool Moves on Multi-Axis Path]
    D1 --> E1[Material Removal via Cutting]
    E1 --> F1[Chips and Swarf Generated as Waste]
    F1 --> G1[Finished Part from Block]
    G1 --> H1[Post-Processing: Deburr, Polish, Inspect]

    A2[CAD Model] --> B2[Slice via Slicer Software]
    B2 --> C2[Generate Layer Toolpath]
    C2 --> D2[Energy Source Activated: Laser or Nozzle]
    D2 --> E2[Material Deposition or Fusion Layer-by-Layer]
    E2 --> F2[Support Structures Added Where Needed]
    F2 --> G2[Finished Part on Build Platform]
    G2 --> H2[Post-Processing: Remove Supports, Cure, Polish]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Decision Tree for Process Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Part Design]) --> Q1{Internal Channels or Lattices Required?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> AM1[Choose AM Process: SLM or DMLS]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Tight Tolerance below 0.05 mm?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> CNC1[Choose CNC Milling or Turning]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Production Volume?}
    Q3 -- Below 50 units --> AM2[Choose AM for Cost-Effective Low Volume]
    Q3 -- Above 500 units --> CNC2[Choose CNC for High Volume Economics]
    Q3 -- Between 50 and 500 --> Q4{Material is Polymer?}
    Q4 -- Yes --> AM3[Choose Polymer AM: FDM or SLA]
    Q4 -- No --> CNC3[Choose CNC Machining]
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Schematic — Subtractive vs. Additive Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CNC_ZONE[Subtractive CNC Process]
        S1[Large Solid Block] -- Subtract Material --> S2[Carved Finished Part]
        S3[Waste Chips and Swarf] -. Generated .- S1
    end

    subgraph AM_ZONE[Additive Manufacturing Process]
        T1[Powder or Filament] -- Add Layer by Layer --> T2[Built-Up Finished Part]
        T3[Unfused Powder Recyclable] -. Leftover .- T1
    end
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`
**Q: Differentiate between Additive Manufacturing and CNC Machining with respect to material utilization and geometric complexity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Material Utilization:** CNC is subtractive; starting from a large stock block, typically only 10–40 % of the input material ends up in the final part, the rest becomes chips/waste. AM is additive; material is deposited only where needed, giving 75–95 % utilization (and >95 % with powder recycling in powder-bed processes).
2. **Geometric Complexity:** CNC is limited by tool accessibility — internal channels, undercuts, and lattice structures require multi-axis setups and are often impractical. AM has *no tool-access constraint*; complex internal topologies and lattice geometries are produced layer-by-layer with no additional cost.
3. *(Conclusion — 1 mark)* Therefore, AM is preferred for complex, customized, low-volume parts, while CNC is preferred for high-tolerance, high-volume, simple-geometry parts.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`
**Q: Define Additive Manufacturing as per ISO/ASTM 52900. List any four AM process categories.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** As per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015**, AM is *"the process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies."*
- **Four AM Process Categories (2 marks):**
  1. **Material Extrusion** (e.g., FDM)
  2. **Vat Photopolymerization** (e.g., SLA, DLP)
  3. **Powder Bed Fusion** (e.g., SLM, SLS, DMLS)
  4. **Directed Energy Deposition** (e.g., LMD, EBF³)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Many students **lose 3–4 marks** by writing generic definitions without citing **ISO/ASTM 52900** or by confusing **subtractive vs. formative** processes.
> - Always draw a **comparison table** in long-answer questions — examiners award **2 marks** purely for well-structured presentation.
> - Do **not** claim AM has *no* disadvantages. Acknowledge **anisotropy, post-processing, and limited tolerance** as disadvantages for full marks.
> - Numerical derivations (e.g., build time, $\eta$) must include **units and final boxed answer**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

**Q: With neat sketches, explain the fundamental differences between Additive Manufacturing (AM) and CNC Machining. Discuss in detail their relative merits and demerits with respect to:**
**(a) Material utilization, geometric complexity, and dimensional accuracy** *(7 marks)*
**(b) Mechanical properties, sustainability, and economic feasibility for low-volume production** *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Material Utilization, Geometric Complexity, and Dimensional Accuracy (7 marks):**

**Material Utilization:**
- CNC is *subtractive* — material is removed from a larger stock to produce the part. Material utilization $\eta_{CNC}$ typically ranges from **10 % to 40 %**, with the remainder becoming chips, swarf, and coolant-contaminated waste.
- AM is *additive* — material is deposited or fused only at required locations. Material utilization $\eta_{AM}$ is typically **75 % to 95 %**, and with powder recycling in SLM/SLS processes, it can exceed **95 %**.

**Geometric Complexity:**
- CNC is constrained by **tool accessibility**. Internal features (e.g., curved channels inside a turbine blade) require expensive multi-axis (4-axis, 5-axis) machines and custom fixturing. Some geometries — such as **closed internal lattice structures or topology-optimized organic shapes** — are practically impossible.
- AM is **tool-access independent**. The energy source (laser, nozzle, or projector) only needs to reach the build plane, and overhangs are supported by sacrificial support structures. Lattice structures, conformal cooling channels, and bionic designs are produced **without additional cost or time penalty**.

**Dimensional Accuracy:**
- CNC achieves tolerances of **±0.01 mm** with surface roughness **R_a ≈ 0.4–1.6 µm** in finishing passes, making it the gold standard for high-precision parts.
- AM (as-built) achieves tolerances of **±0.1–0.3 mm** with surface roughness **R_a ≈ 5–20 µm**. Post-processing (CNC machining, polishing, bead blasting, or coating) is required to reach precision-grade tolerances, increasing total cost and lead time.

**(b) Mechanical Properties, Sustainability, and Economic Feasibility (7 marks):**

**Mechanical Properties:**
- CNC-machined parts are derived from **wrought or rolled billets**, hence they exhibit **near-isotropic** mechanical behavior. Tensile, fatigue, and impact properties are consistent in all directions.
- AM parts — particularly in SLM/DMLS — exhibit **anisotropy** due to layer-wise bonding and directional thermal gradients. The Z-direction (build direction) typically shows **30–60 % lower tensile strength** than the XY-plane. However, post-process treatments like **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** can mitigate this.

**Sustainability:**
- CNC generates **significant waste** — chips, used coolant, and energy consumption is high due to the power-intensive spindle and chip-removal processes. Coolant disposal is an environmental concern.
- AM is **more sustainable** — minimal waste, recyclable powder, and lower energy consumption for small, complex parts. However, support structures and post-processing powders (in bed fusion) still generate waste that must be managed.

**Economic Feasibility for Low-Volume Production:**
- For **high-volume production (≥1000 units)**, CNC is **economically superior** due to the fixed-cost amortization of programming and fixturing.
- For **low-volume production (1–100 units)** — especially for complex parts — AM is **dramatically cheaper** because there is **no tooling cost**. For example, a complex aerospace bracket costing **₹5,00,000 in CNC tooling + ₹2,000 per part** can be produced via AM at **₹15,000 per part** with zero tooling, breaking even at very low volumes.

> *(Examiner's Note: Add a labeled comparison sketch — Subtractive (Block → Carved Part) vs. Additive (Powder → Built Part) — to fetch 2 presentation marks.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model – Alternative Choice]`

**Q: (a) Explain the term Additive Manufacturing (AM) with reference to ISO/ASTM 52900 classification. Discuss the seven process categories of AM in brief.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A polymer part with volume $V_p = 18{,}000 \text{ mm}^3$ is to be produced using Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). The deposition rate is $R_d = 15 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$ and the layer thickness is $t = 0.15 \text{ mm}$. Calculate the build time. If the same part is machined on a CNC mill with MRR = $3000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}$, calculate the CNC machining time. Compare the two processes with respect to (i) material utilization, (ii) geometric complexity, and (iii) surface finish.** *(7 marks)*

#### **Model Solution:**

**(a) Definition and ISO/ASTM 52900 Classification (7 marks):**

As per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015**, AM is the *"process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, in contrast to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies."*

The **seven standardized AM process categories** are:

1. **Material Extrusion** — Thermoplastic filament is heated and extruded through a nozzle (e.g., **FDM, FFF**).
2. **Vat Photopolymerization** — Liquid resin is selectively cured by UV light (e.g., **SLA, DLP**).
3. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** — Thermal energy fuses regions of a powder bed (e.g., **SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM**).
4. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** — Focused thermal energy (laser/plasma) fuses material as it is deposited (e.g., **LMD, EBF³**).
5. **Material Jetting** — Droplets of build material are jetted selectively (e.g., **PolyJet, MultiJet**).
6. **Binder Jetting** — Liquid binder is selectively deposited onto a powder bed (e.g., **BJ, ExOne**).
7. **Sheet Lamination** — Sheets of material are bonded and cut layer-by-layer (e.g., **LOM, UAM**).

---

**(b) Numerical Comparison (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — AM Build Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{b,AM} &= \frac{V_p}{R_d \cdot t} \\[6pt]
T_{b,AM} &= \frac{18{,}000}{15 \times 0.15} \\[6pt]
T_{b,AM} &= \frac{18{,}000}{2.25} \\[6pt]
T_{b,AM} &= 8{,}000 \text{ s} \\[6pt]
T_{b,AM} &= \frac{8{,}000}{3{,}600} = 2.222 \text{ hours} \approx 2 \text{ hr } 13 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — CNC Machining Time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{CNC} &= \frac{V_p}{MRR} \\[6pt]
T_{CNC} &= \frac{18{,}000 \text{ mm}^3}{3{,}000 \text{ mm}^3/\text{min}} \\[6pt]
T_{CNC} &= 6 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical Observation:** CNC is *faster in raw machining time*, but this is for **simple geometry**. For complex internal features, CNC requires **multi-axis setups, multiple operations, and tool changes**, which can push the time to several hours.

**Comparison Summary:**

| **Attribute** | **AM (FDM)** | **CNC Milling** |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Material Utilization** | ~85–95 % (only part + supports) | ~15–40 % (large stock → small part) |
| **(ii) Geometric Complexity** | Unlimited (internal channels easy) | Limited by tool reach |
| **(iii) Surface Finish** | R_a ≈ 10–20 µm (layer lines visible) | R_a ≈ 0.8–1.6 µm (mirror finish possible) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Distinction Between AM and CNC Machining**

- 🔹 **CNC** is **subtractive**; **AM** is **additive** — this is the single most important KTU board distinction.
- 🔹 Always cite **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** when defining AM in long answers.
- 🔹 The **seven AM process categories** are: Material Extrusion, Vat Photopolymerization, Powder Bed Fusion, Directed Energy Deposition, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, and Sheet Lamination.
- 🔹 **AM material utilization** ($\eta_{AM}$) ≈ 75–95 %; **CNC material utilization** ($\eta_{CNC}$) ≈ 10–40 %.
- 🔹 **CNC tolerance** ≈ ±0.01 mm; **AM tolerance** ≈ ±0.1 mm (as-built).
- 🔹 **CNC surface roughness** R_a ≈ 0.4–1.6 µm; **AM surface roughness** R_a ≈ 5–20 µm.
- 🔹 **AM is anisotropic** (Z-direction weaker); **CNC is near-isotropic** (wrought billet).
- 🔹 AM **build time formula**: $T_b = \dfrac{V_p}{R_d \cdot t}$ — independent of geometric complexity.
- 🔹 CNC **MRR formula**: $Z = w \cdot d \cdot f$ — proportional to geometric complexity.
- 🔹 AM is preferred for **low-volume, complex, customized** parts (aerospace brackets, dental crowns, implants).
- 🔹 CNC is preferred for **high-volume, high-tolerance, simple-geometry** parts (engine blocks, shafts, gears).
- 🔹 AM has **zero tooling cost**; CNC has **moderate-to-high tooling and fixturing cost**.
- 🔹 The **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** is the canonical KTU case study — 18 parts welded via CNC vs. 1 monolithic AM part with 30 % weight savings.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of AM Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of AM Processes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is the process of joining materials to make objects from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive manufacturing methodologies. According to **ASTM/ISO 52900:2015**, AM is formally defined as the "process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies."

The **Classification of AM Processes** refers to the systematic grouping of all additive manufacturing technologies into standardized categories based on their underlying physical principle of material consolidation, the state/feedstock form of the input material, and the energy source used for fusion or bonding.

## 1.2 Standardized ASTM/ISO 52900 Classification (7 Categories)

The internationally accepted classification, which KTU syllabus explicitly follows, divides all AM processes into **seven standardized categories**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Seven ASTM/ISO 52900 AM Process Categories**

| Sl. No. | Category | Abbreviation | Material State | Bonding Principle |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Vat Photopolymerization** | VP | Liquid resin | Photopolymerization (UV light) |
| 2 | **Material Extrusion** | ME | Thermoplastic filament/paste | Thermal extrusion & solidification |
| 3 | **Material Jetting** | MJ | Liquid droplets (photopolymer/wax) | Drop-on-demand jetting + UV cure |
| 4 | **Binder Jetting** | BJ | Powder + liquid binder | Adhesive bonding |
| 5 | **Powder Bed Fusion** | PBF | Powder (metal/polymer/ceramic) | Selective melting/sintering |
| 6 | **Directed Energy Deposition** | DED | Powder or wire | Focused thermal energy melting |
| 7 | **Sheet Lamination** | SL | Sheets/foils (paper, metal, polymer) | Adhesive/ultrasonic/welding bonding |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of AM like building a sandcastle layer by layer.** Imagine you have a bucket of sand and you want to build a complex sculpture. Instead of carving it out from a stone block (subtractive manufacturing like CNC milling), you add sand one grain at a time in exactly the right spots. Each layer is a thin slice of the final 3D shape. After laying down all the layers, you have the complete object.

**Different "AM techniques" are simply different ways of depositing and binding that sand:**

- **Vat Photopolymerization** → Imagine a tray of liquid honey. You shine a flashlight (UV) at specific spots, and only those spots harden into a solid layer. Sweep the flashlight across, harden the next slice, and you have a complete object.
- **Material Extrusion (FDM)** → Like using a cake icing gun. You squeeze out hot melted plastic in thin lines to draw each layer of the shape.
- **Powder Bed Fusion (SLS/SLM)** → Spread a thin blanket of flour, then use a magnifying glass (laser) to burn only the shape into the flour. Add another layer of flour, repeat.
- **Material Jetting** → Like an inkjet printer that sprays tiny droplets of liquid that instantly harden with UV light.
- **Binder Jetting** → Like using a glue stick to selectively glue grains of sand into a shape.
- **Directed Energy Deposition** → Like using a welding torch that melts metal powder as it is sprayed, building up a 3D shape from a molten puddle.
- **Sheet Lamination** → Like making a topographic map from stacked sheets of paper, gluing each contour map onto the next.

## 1.4 Importance in KTU 2024 Scheme Context

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why KTU Tests This Topic Heavily:**
> - It is the **foundational framework** for the entire AM course.
> - Every later module (processes, materials, applications) builds upon this classification.
> - Examiners test: identification, categorization, advantages/limitations, and process selection logic.
> - Direct questions appear as **2-mark short notes** in Part A and as full **14-mark descriptive questions** in Part B.

**Key Metrics to Remember:**
- **ASTM F2792** — Original US standard (now replaced/superseded by ISO 52900)
- **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** — Current global standard
- **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** — Updated standard (introduced hybrid terminology)
- Standard classification terminology ensures global **interoperability and unambiguous communication** between designers, manufacturers, and researchers.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Classification Framework

## 2.1 The Three Logical Dimensions of Classification

AM processes are classified along **three orthogonal logical dimensions**. Understanding these dimensions is essential for KTU exam answers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 1 — State of Feedstock (Material Form)**
> - Solid (powder, wire, sheet, filament)
> - Liquid (resin, paste, suspension, melt)
> - Gaseous (vapor-phase deposition processes — less common in KTU syllabus)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 2 — Source of Energy**
> - Laser (Nd:YAG, CO₂, fiber laser)
> - Electron beam (e-beam)
> - Plasma arc
> - UV light (LED, mercury lamp, laser)
> - Thermal (heated nozzle, resistive heating)

> [!NOTE]
> **Dimension 3 — Bonding / Consolidation Principle**
> - Photopolymerization (chemical crosslinking)
> - Thermal fusion (melting & solidification)
> - Sintering (diffusion bonding below melting point)
> - Adhesion (chemical binder, adhesive, welding)

## 2.2 Detailed Theoretical Breakdown of Each Category

### Category 1 — Vat Photopolymerization (VP)
**Working Principle:**
- A vat is filled with liquid photopolymer resin.
- A UV light source (laser or projector) selectively cures the resin layer-by-layer.
- The build platform moves downward (or the vat moves up) after each cured layer.

**Common Variants:**
- **SLA** — Stereolithography Apparatus (laser-scanned)
- **DLP** — Digital Light Processing (projector-based, cures entire layer at once)

**Typical Energy Density:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot d}$
Where $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $d$ = layer thickness.

---

### Category 2 — Material Extrusion (ME)
**Working Principle:**
- A thermoplastic filament (or paste/melt) is fed through a heated nozzle.
- Material is extruded and deposited along a toolpath.
- Material solidifies upon cooling to form the layer.

**Common Variants:**
- **FDM** — Fused Deposition Modeling (most popular, desktop-class)
- **FFF** — Fused Filament Fabrication (open-source variant)

**Volumetric Flow Rate Formula:**
$$Q = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot v_e$$
Where $D$ = nozzle diameter, $v_e$ = extrusion velocity.

---

### Category 3 — Material Jetting (MJ)
**Working Principle:**
- Liquid photopolymer (or wax) droplets are jetted via hundreds of nozzles (similar to inkjet printing).
- UV light cures each droplet instantly upon deposition.
- Multiple materials can be jetted simultaneously (multi-material capability).

**Key Feature:** Highest dimensional accuracy and surface finish among all AM processes ($\approx \pm 0.02$ mm).

---

### Category 4 — Binder Jetting (BJ)
**Working Principle:**
- A roller spreads a thin layer of powder (sand, metal, ceramic).
- A print head selectively deposits liquid binder droplets onto the powder bed.
- Bonded powder forms each layer; unbound powder supports overhangs.

**Distinctive Trait:** **No heat** is involved during printing — post-processing sintering/infiltration is required.

---

### Category 5 — Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)
**Working Principle:**
- A roller/recounter spreads a thin layer of powder.
- A high-energy source (laser or e-beam) selectively fuses the powder.
- Process repeats for each new layer.

**Sub-variants:**
- **SLS** — Selective Laser Sintering (polymer powder, partially sintered)
- **DMLS** — Direct Metal Laser Sintering (metal powder)
- **SLM** — Selective Laser Melting (metal powder, fully melted)
- **EBM** — Electron Beam Melting (metal, vacuum chamber)

**Energy Density (Critical KTU Formula):**
$$E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
Where $E_d$ = energy density (J/mm³), $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm).

---

### Category 6 — Directed Energy Deposition (DED)
**Working Principle:**
- Focused thermal energy (laser, e-beam, plasma arc) creates a melt pool.
- Material (powder or wire) is fed coaxially or laterally into the melt pool.
- 5-axis deposition possible (unlike most other AM processes).

**Sub-variants:**
- **LMD** — Laser Metal Deposition
- **EBAM** — Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing
- **WAAM** — Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing

**Deposition Rate Formula:**
$$R = \rho \cdot A \cdot v_f$$
Where $R$ = mass deposition rate, $\rho$ = material density, $A$ = cross-sectional area of deposition, $v_f$ = feed velocity.

---

### Category 7 — Sheet Lamination (SL)
**Working Principle:**
- Sheets of material (paper, polymer, metal) are bonded layer-by-layer.
- Cutting is done via laser or knife.
- Unbound areas are removed post-process.

**Sub-variants:**
- **LOM** — Laminated Object Manufacturing (paper)
- **UAM** — Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (metal foils, ultrasonic welding)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Comparison Table (Critical for Exams)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — at least 8 marks of every AM exam is based on this.**

| Process | ASTM Code | Feedstock | Energy Source | Accuracy | Key Application |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|:---:|:---|
| SLA | VP | Photopolymer resin | UV Laser | High ($\pm 0.1$ mm) | Prototypes, jewelry, dental |
| DLP | VP | Photopolymer resin | UV Projector | Very High | Same as SLA, faster |
| FDM/FFF | ME | Thermoplastic filament | Heated nozzle | Moderate ($\pm 0.2$ mm) | Concept models, fixtures |
| PolyJet | MJ | Photopolymer droplets | UV + Jetting | Highest ($\pm 0.02$ mm) | Multi-material prototypes |
| Binder Jetting | BJ | Powder + Binder | Thermal (no heat in print) | Moderate | Sand molds, large parts |
| SLS | PBF | Polymer powder | CO₂ Laser | Good | Functional polymer parts |
| SLM/DMLS | PBF | Metal powder | Fiber Laser / E-beam | High | Aerospace, medical implants |
| EBM | PBF | Metal powder | Electron Beam | High ($\pm 0.4$ mm) | Titanium orthopedic implants |
| LMD/WAAM | DED | Powder/Wire | Laser/Plasma Arc | Moderate | Large metal parts, repair |
| LOM/UAM | SL | Sheets/foils | Laser/Ultrasonic | Low–Moderate | Paper models, hybrid metal |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of the Classification System

> [!TIP]
> **Why Engineers Use This Classification:**
> 1. **Process Selection** — Engineers map design requirements to the appropriate process.
> 2. **Material Compatibility** — Determines available material palette.
> 3. **Cost Estimation** — Equipment cost, material cost, post-processing differ vastly across categories.
> 4. **Quality Control** — Each category has unique defect signatures (porosity for PBF, warping for ME, etc.).
> 5. **Standardization** — Universal vocabulary for global supply chains, patents, and academic literature.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations and Classification Logic

## 3.1 Derivation of Process Selection Logic (Analytical Flow)

> [!NOTE]
> The following derivation presents the **decision logic** an engineer follows when selecting an AM process based on design requirements. This is a critical exam topic — KTU frequently asks this as a 7-mark sub-question.

**Step 1 — Define Design Requirements (Inputs)**

Let the design requirements vector be:
$$\vec{D} = \{M, G, A, C, V\}$$

Where:
- $M$ = Material type (polymer / metal / ceramic / composite)
- $G$ = Geometric complexity (simple / moderate / highly complex / lattice/truss)
- $A$ = Accuracy required (tolerance class)
- $C$ = Cost ceiling (per part budget)
- $V$ = Production volume (one-off / low / batch)

**Step 2 — Define Process Capability Vector**

For each ASTM category $i \in \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$:
$$\vec{P_i} = \{M_i, G_i, A_i, C_i, V_i\}$$

**Step 3 — Compute Compatibility Score**

$$\text{Score}_i = w_1 \cdot f(M_i, M) + w_2 \cdot f(G_i, G) + w_3 \cdot f(A_i, A) + w_4 \cdot f(C_i, C) + w_5 \cdot f(V_i, V)$$

Where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 1$ are user-defined weights, and $f(x_i, x)$ is a compatibility function returning 1 for full match and 0 for no match.

**Step 4 — Optimal Process Selection**

$$P_{\text{opt}} = \underset{i \in \{1,...,7\}}{\arg\max} \quad \text{Score}_i$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result:** The process with the highest compatibility score is recommended. This analytical approach is widely used in industry via tools like the **Granta MI** materials selection database.

---

## 3.2 Energy Density Derivation for Powder Bed Fusion (Exhaustive)

**Given (Standard KTU Exam Problem Data):**
- Laser Power: $P = 200$ W
- Scan Speed: $v = 800$ mm/s
- Hatch Spacing: $h = 0.1$ mm
- Layer Thickness: $t = 0.03$ mm

**Step 1 — Identify the Governing Equation**

The volumetric energy density (VED) for laser powder bed fusion is:
$$E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Verify Unit Consistency**

$$\frac{P \text{ [W = J/s]}}{v \text{ [mm/s]} \cdot h \text{ [mm]} \cdot t \text{ [mm]}} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$$

Units are consistent.

**Step 3 — Substitute Numerical Values**

$$E_d = \frac{200 \text{ W}}{800 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.1 \text{ mm} \times 0.03 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate Denominator**

$$800 \times 0.1 = 80$$

$$80 \times 0.03 = 2.4$$

**Step 5 — Final Calculation**

$$E_d = \frac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Step 6 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!TIP]
> - For **steel/maraging steel**, optimal $E_d$ range is **60–120 J/mm³**.
> - For **aluminum alloys**, optimal range is **30–70 J/mm³**.
> - For **titanium (Ti-6Al-4V)**, optimal range is **40–90 J/mm³**.
> - $E_d$ below the range → lack of fusion porosity defects.
> - $E_d$ above the range → keyhole porosity and vaporization defects.

Our value of **83.33 J/mm³** falls in the optimal range for **Ti-6Al-4V** — a sound process choice.

---

## 3.3 Volumetric Throughput Comparison Across Categories

**Step 1 — Establish Build Rate Formula for Each Category**

For Material Extrusion:
$$R_{ME} = w \cdot t \cdot v_d$$

For Powder Bed Fusion:
$$R_{PBF} = A_{\text{bed}} \cdot t / t_{\text{cycle}}$$

For Directed Energy Deposition:
$$R_{DED} = \pi \cdot \frac{D^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

For Vat Photopolymerization:
$$R_{VP} = A_{\text{platform}} \cdot t / t_{\text{cycle}}$$

Where $t_{\text{cycle}}$ = time per layer (including recoating, exposure, settling).

**Step 2 — Comparative Result Table**

| Process | Build Rate (typical) | Volume Limitation |
|:---|:---:|:---|
| SLA/DLP | 20–40 cm³/hr | Small to medium |
| FDM | 10–30 cm³/hr | Medium |
| SLS | 200–1000 cm³/hr | Medium to large |
| SLM/DMLS | 5–20 cm³/hr | Small to medium (metal) |
| Binder Jetting | 500–5000 cm³/hr | Very large |
| DED (WAAM) | 50–500 cm³/hr | Very large (m-scale) |

**Step 3 — Engineering Interpretation**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> - **Highest accuracy** → Material Jetting > DLP > SLA
> - **Highest build rate** → Binder Jetting > DED > SLS
> - **Best mechanical properties** → DMLS/SLM (fully dense metal)
> - **Lowest cost** → FDM (desktop accessible)
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Classification Tree (Hierarchical Flow)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING] --> B[Feedstock Based]
    A --> C[Energy Source Based]
    A --> D[Bonding Principle Based]

    B --> B1[Liquid Feedstock]
    B --> B2[Solid Feedstock]
    B --> B3[Sheet Feedstock]

    B1 --> B1a[Photopolymer Resin]
    B1 --> B1b[Liquid Droplets]

    B2 --> B2a[Powder]
    B2 --> B2b[Filament]
    B2 --> B2c[Wire]

    B3 --> B3a[Paper Foils]
    B3 --> B3b[Metal Foils]

    B1a --> P1[VP - Vat Photopolymerization]
    B1b --> P2[MJ - Material Jetting]
    B2a --> P3[PBF - Powder Bed Fusion]
    B2a --> P4[BJ - Binder Jetting]
    B2a --> P5[DED - Directed Energy Deposition]
    B2b --> P6[ME - Material Extrusion]
    B2c --> P5
    B3a --> P7[SL - Sheet Lamination]
    B3b --> P7

    C --> C1[Laser Based]
    C --> C2[Electron Beam]
    C --> C3[UV Light]
    C --> C4[Thermal/Arc]

    D --> D1[Photopolymerization]
    D --> D2[Thermal Fusion]
    D --> D3[Sintering]
    D --> D4[Adhesion]
```

## 4.2 Process Capability Mapping (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN[Design Requirements] --> A1[Material]
    IN --> A2[Accuracy]
    IN --> A3[Size]
    IN --> A4[Volume]
    IN --> A5[Cost]

    A1 --> SEL[Selection Engine]
    A2 --> SEL
    A3 --> SEL
    A4 --> SEL
    A5 --> SEL

    SEL --> O1[VP - Resin Parts]
    SEL --> O2[ME - Plastic Parts]
    SEL --> O3[MJ - High Accuracy]
    SEL --> O4[BJ - Large Sand Molds]
    SEL --> O5[PBF - Metal/Polymer]
    SEL --> O6[DED - Large Metal]
    SEL --> O7[SL - Paper/Hybrid]
```

## 4.3 Process Cycle Functional Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[CAD Model] --> S2[STL / 3MF Conversion]
    S2 --> S3[Slicing into Layers]
    S3 --> S4[Layer N: Deposit/Spread Material]
    S4 --> S5[Layer N: Selective Energy Application]
    S5 --> S6[Layer N: Solidification]
    S6 --> S7{All Layers Done?}
    S7 -->|No| S4
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Post Processing]
    S8 --> S9[Finished Part]
```

## 4.4 Energy Source vs Material Compatibility Matrix (Block Schematic)

```mermaid
graph TD
    E1[Nd:YAG Laser 1064 nm] --> M1[Polymers]
    E1 --> M2[Metals]

    E2[CO2 Laser 10600 nm] --> M3[Polymers only]
    E2 --> M4[Non-metals]

    E3[Fiber Laser] --> M5[Metals]

    E4[Electron Beam] --> M6[Conductive Metals]
    E4 --> M7[Vacuum Environment]

    E5[UV LED 405 nm] --> M8[Photopolymers]

    E6[Plasma Arc] --> M9[Steel, Aluminum, Titanium]

    E7[Heated Nozzle] --> M10[Thermoplastics]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight for Exams:** The wavelength of the laser determines which material absorbs the energy efficiently. CO₂ lasers ($\lambda = 10.6 \mu$m) are absorbed by polymers but reflected by metals — that's why **metals require fiber or Nd:YAG lasers ($\lambda = 1.06 \mu$m)**. This is a frequently asked **2-mark conceptual question** in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define Additive Manufacturing. List the seven categories of AM processes as per ASTM/ISO 52900 standard.**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Additive Manufacturing (AM) is the process of joining materials to make parts from 3D model data, usually layer upon layer, as opposed to subtractive and formative manufacturing methodologies (ASTM/ISO 52900:2015).

**Seven Categories:**
1. **Vat Photopolymerization (VP)**
2. **Material Extrusion (ME)**
3. **Material Jetting (MJ)**
4. **Binder Jetting (BJ)**
5. **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)**
6. **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)**
7. **Sheet Lamination (SL)**

**[3 Marks — 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for listing all 7]**

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**Differentiate between Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Directed Energy Deposition (DED) with respect to feedstock form and energy source.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Feedstock** | Pre-deposited powder bed | Powder or wire fed coaxially/laterally |
| **Energy Source** | Laser or electron beam (over bed) | Laser, e-beam, or plasma arc (focused at nozzle) |
| **Build Envelope** | Limited to bed size | Large (multi-axis possible) |
| **Support Structure** | Required for overhangs | Often not required (5-axis deposition) |
| **Application** | Complex geometry, fine features | Large structural parts, repair |

**[3 Marks — 1 mark each for feedstock, energy, and one distinguishing feature]**

---

## PART B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **ASTM/ISO 52900 classification of AM processes** with neat sketches of working principles for any **four categories**. Mention at least **two example processes** under each category. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Compare the **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** and **Vat Photopolymerization (VP)** processes on the basis of: (i) feedstock form, (ii) energy source, (iii) typical accuracy, (iv) post-processing requirements, and (v) typical applications. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Question A

#### Part (a): ASTM/ISO 52900 Classification

> **Introduction (1 mark):** The ASTM/ISO 52900:2015 standard classifies all AM processes into seven categories based on the principle of material consolidation and feedstock state. This classification provides a globally accepted terminology.

**Four Categories Explained:**

**(1) Vat Photopolymerization (VP) — 1.5 marks**
- **Principle:** A vat of liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured layer-by-layer using a UV light source (laser in SLA, projector in DLP).
- **Working:** The build platform is submerged just below the resin surface. UV light traces/cures one cross-section. Platform descends by one layer thickness. New resin flows beneath, and the process repeats.
- **Examples:** Stereolithography (SLA), Digital Light Processing (DLP), Two-Photon Polymerization (2PP).
- **Sketch description:** Liquid resin → UV laser scan → cured layer → downward platform movement.

**(2) Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — 1.5 marks**
- **Principle:** A high-energy source selectively fuses regions of a powder bed.
- **Working:** A roller spreads a thin powder layer. A laser or e-beam fuses powder particles according to the slice geometry. Platform descends, new powder spread, process repeats. Unfused powder acts as support.
- **Examples:** Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS), Electron Beam Melting (EBM).
- **Sketch description:** Powder spread → laser scan → fused cross-section → platform down → repeat.

**(3) Material Extrusion (ME) — 1.5 marks**
- **Principle:** A thermoplastic material is extruded through a heated nozzle and deposited along a toolpath.
- **Working:** Filament is fed into a heated liquefier. The molten material is extruded through a nozzle that traces each layer. Material solidifies upon cooling.
- **Examples:** Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM), Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF).
- **Sketch description:** Filament feed → heated nozzle → extruded bead → cooled solid layer.

**(4) Directed Energy Deposition (DED) — 1.5 marks**
- **Principle:** Focused thermal energy melts material as it is being deposited.
- **Working:** Powder or wire is fed into a melt pool created by a laser, e-beam, or plasma arc. The deposition head can move in 3 to 5 axes.
- **Examples:** Laser Metal Deposition (LMD), Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM), Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM).
- **Sketch description:** Powder injection → laser/arc focus → melt pool → solidifying track.

---

#### Part (b): PBF vs VP Comparison Table

| Parameter | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | Vat Photopolymerization (VP) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **(i) Feedstock** | Powder (polymer, metal, ceramic) | Liquid photopolymer resin |
| **(ii) Energy Source** | Laser (CO₂, fiber, Nd:YAG) or electron beam | UV light (laser or projector) |
| **(iii) Typical Accuracy** | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.3$ mm | $\pm 0.05$ to $\pm 0.1$ mm (SLA), up to $\pm 0.02$ mm (DLP) |
| **(iv) Post-Processing** | Powder removal, support removal, heat treatment, HIP for metals | Resin drainage, UV post-cure, support removal, sanding |
| **(v) Applications** | Aerospace brackets, medical implants, functional metal parts | Dental crowns, jewelry, prototypes, hearing aids |

**[7 Marks — 1 mark per comparison point + 0.5 marks for distinguishing insight]**

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative for Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024, Sample Question]**

**(a)** Classify AM processes based on **(i) feedstock state** and **(ii) energy source**, with two examples for each class. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of **Binder Jetting (BJ)** and **Sheet Lamination (SL)** processes. Compare their **material capabilities** and **post-processing requirements**. **[7 Marks]**

---

### Model Answer — Question B

#### Part (a): Two Classification Schemes

**Classification by Feedstock State — 3.5 marks:**

| Class | Description | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Liquid** | Photopolymer resins, waxes, low-viscosity pastes | SLA, DLP, PolyJet, Material Jetting |
| **Solid Powder** | Fine powders of polymers, metals, ceramics | SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM, Binder Jetting |
| **Solid Wire/Filament** | Thermoplastic filament, metal wire | FDM, WAAM, EBAM |
| **Sheet/Foil** | Paper sheets, polymer foils, metal foils | LOM, UAM |

**Classification by Energy Source — 3.5 marks:**

| Class | Description | Examples |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Laser** | Nd:YAG, CO₂, fiber lasers | SLA, SLS, SLM, DMLS, LMD |
| **Electron Beam** | High-velocity electron stream | EBM, EBAM |
| **UV Light** | LED, mercury lamp, projector | DLP, PolyJet, 2PP |
| **Thermal/Arc** | Heated nozzle, plasma arc | FDM, WAAM |
| **No External Heat** | Liquid binder adhesion | Binder Jetting |

---

#### Part (b): Binder Jetting and Sheet Lamination

**Binder Jetting (BJ) — 3 marks:**

- **Working Principle:** A roller spreads a thin layer of powder across the build platform. A print head (similar to inkjet) deposits droplets of liquid binder selectively onto the powder where solidification is desired. The binder wicks into the powder and bonds adjacent grains. The platform descends by one layer thickness, a new powder layer is spread, and the process repeats.
- **Diagram elements:** Powder bed, powder dispenser roller, binder jetting head, build platform (descending step-by-step), unbound powder as natural support.
- **Material Capability:** Metals (steel, bronze, Inconel), sand (foundry molds), ceramics, polymers.
- **Post-Processing:** Depowdering, **infiltration** (with bronze or epoxy for metals), **sintering** (for densification), sometimes HIP.

**Sheet Lamination (SL) — 2.5 marks:**

- **Working Principle:** Sheets of material (paper, polymer, or metal foil) are successively bonded layer-by-layer. A laser or knife cuts the cross-sectional profile of each layer. Unbound material is removed post-build.
- **Diagram elements:** Sheet feed roller, heated roller/cutting head, build platform (rising), bonded stack, laser cutter.
- **Material Capability:** Paper (LOM), polymer foils, metal foils (UAM uses ultrasonic welding).
- **Post-Processing:** Decaking/cleaning (LOM), CNC machining to final dimensions (UAM).

**Comparison — 1.5 marks:**

| Aspect | Binder Jetting (BJ) | Sheet Lamination (SL) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Post-Processing** | Mandatory sintering/infiltration | Decaking or CNC machining |
| **Material Diversity** | Very high (powder-based) | Limited to available sheet stock |
| **Surface Finish** | Moderate (powdery texture) | Layered appearance (paper) or metallic (UAM) |
| **Cost** | Low (no heat during print) | Moderate (sheet material cost) |

**[Total: 7 marks — 3 + 2.5 + 1.5]**

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Wrong Naming Convention (–2 marks):** Students often write outdated terms like "rapid prototyping" or use obsolete process names. **Always use ASTM/ISO 52900 terminology** — write "Vat Photopolymerization" not just "stereolithography".
>
> 2. **Confusing PBF Sub-categories (–2 marks):** SLS ≠ SLM. SLS uses polymer with **partial sintering** below melting point; SLM uses metal with **complete melting**. Don't interchange them.
>
> 3. **Missing Energy Source Specification (–1 mark):** Always specify *which* energy source. Writing "laser" alone is incomplete; write "CO₂ laser for polymers" or "fiber laser for metals".
>
> 4. **Confusing DED with PBF (–2 marks):** Both use powder, but DED deposits powder *into* the energy focal zone (no pre-existing bed), while PBF fuses powder *already spread* on a bed. This distinction is frequently tested.
>
> 5. **Forgetting ASTM Standard Number (–1 mark):** Always cite **ASTM F2792 / ISO 52900** when defining AM or listing categories.
>
> 6. **Incomplete Diagrams (–1 mark):** Always label **build platform, energy source, feedstock input, and direction of layer formation** in any process sketch.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Points — Memorize Before Exam:**

- ✅ **Definition of AM:** Joining materials layer-by-layer from 3D model data (ASTM/ISO 52900).
- ✅ **Standard:** ASTM/ISO 52900:2015 (also 52900:2021 update).
- ✅ **7 Categories (Mnemonic: "V-Me-MJ-BJ-PBF-DED-SL"):**
  **V**at Photopolymerization, **Me** (Material Extrusion), **MJ** (Material Jetting), **BJ** (Binder Jetting), **PBF** (Powder Bed Fusion), **DED** (Directed Energy Deposition), **SL** (Sheet Lamination).
- ✅ **3 Classification Dimensions:** Feedstock state, Energy source, Bonding principle.
- ✅ **Energy Density Formula (must memorize):** $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ in **J/mm³**.
- ✅ **Optimal $E_d$ ranges:** Steel 60–120, Al 30–70, Ti-6Al-4V 40–90 J/mm³.
- ✅ **Highest accuracy:** Material Jetting (PolyJet, $\pm 0.02$ mm).
- ✅ **Largest build volume:** Binder Jetting and DED (WAAM up to meters).
- ✅ **Laser Wavelength Rule:** CO₂ ($\lambda = 10.6 \mu$m) → polymers; Fiber ($\lambda = 1.06 \mu$m) → metals.
- ✅ **Multi-axis capability:** Only **DED** supports true 5-axis deposition.
- ✅ **No heat during printing:** Only **Binder Jetting**.
- ✅ **Vacuum required:** **EBM** and **EBAM** (electron beam processes).
- ✅ **Photopolymerization:** Governs **VP** and **MJ** categories only.
- ✅ **Always cite ASTM standard number** when answering KTU questions.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Metal Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Metal Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Metal Systems in Additive Manufacturing?

In Additive Manufacturing (AM), a **Metal System** refers to the complete feedstock-to-part pipeline that includes the **raw metallic material** (in powder, wire, or sheet form), its **physical/chemical characteristics**, the **handling protocols**, and the **energy-matter interaction mechanism** used to consolidate it layer-by-layer into a functional 3D metal component.

According to the **ASTM F2792-12** standard, metal AM processes fall under the broad category of "Material Extrusion," "Powder Bed Fusion," "Directed Energy Deposition," and "Binder Jetting" — each requiring a different metal system configuration.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (PEPOT634 – Module 1):**
> *"Metal Systems encompasses the study of metal powders, wires, and binders used as feedstock in various additive manufacturing processes, including their production methods, characterization, and process compatibility."*

## 1.2 Why Metal Systems Matter in AM

Unlike polymers, metals cannot simply be melted and re-solidified repeatedly without significant consequences:
- **Oxidation sensitivity** — reactive at high temperatures
- **Thermal residual stresses** — caused by rapid melting/solidification
- **Anisotropic properties** — directional grain growth
- **Powder degradation** — reuse affects quality

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy: The "Premium Coffee Bean" Principle**
> Think of metal powder for AM like **specialty coffee beans** for an espresso machine. The final brew's quality depends entirely on:
> - **Bean size & uniformity** (powder size distribution)
> - **Moisture content** (oxygen/moisture sensitivity)
> - **Roast consistency** (powder morphology)
> - **Grind fineness** (flowability)
>
> A cheap, inconsistent grind will clog the machine, produce bitter shots, and damage equipment — just like poor metal powder clogs recoaters, produces porosity, and ruins builds.

## 1.4 Classification of Metal Feedstock Systems

Metal systems in AM are broadly classified into three physical forms:

| Form | Typical AM Process | Example Materials |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|
| **Metal Powder** | SLM, DMLS, EBM, BJAM | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, IN718, AlSi10Mg |
| **Metal Wire** | WAAM, EBF³ | Ti, Al, Steel wires (Ø 0.8–1.6 mm) |
| **Metal Paste/Binder** | Binder Jetting, Sheet Lamination | Inconel paste, Bronze + binder |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Metal Powder Production Methods

### A. Gas Atomization (GA) — Most Common for AM

**Principle:** A molten metal stream is disintegrated by high-velocity inert gas (Argon/Nitrogen) jets, forming spherical droplets that solidify into powder.

**Process Steps:**
1. Raw metal melted in induction furnace (typically **1500–1700 °C**)
2. Molten stream poured through a tundish/nozzle
3. High-pressure inert gas (3–8 MPa) impinges the stream
4. Droplets form via Rayleigh instability
5. Spherical particles solidify in flight
6. Powder collected, sieved, classified

> [!NOTE]
> Gas-atomized powders are **highly spherical** with **high flowability** — ideal for Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) processes.

### B. Plasma Atomization (PA)

- Uses **plasma torches** to melt a metal wire feedstock
- Yields ultra-spherical, satellite-free powders
- Used for **reactive metals** like Titanium
- Higher cost, lower throughput

### C. Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP)

- A metal bar (electrode) rotates at high speed (**10,000–15,000 rpm**)
- An electric arc melts the tip; centrifugal force ejects droplets
- Produces **highly spherical, low-impurity** powders
- Common for **Ti-6Al-4V** in aerospace

### D. Water Atomization (WA)

- Uses high-pressure **water jets** instead of gas
- Produces **irregular, satellite-laden** particles
- Lower cost; used for **Binder Jetting** (morphology less critical)

## 2.2 Critical Powder Characteristics for AM

| Property | Importance | Typical Specification |
|----------|------------|----------------------|
| **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** | Affects layer thickness, resolution, flow | 15–45 μm (PBF); 45–150 μm (DED) |
| **Morphology** | Spherical = better flow | Aspect ratio < 1.2 |
| **Flowability** | Critical for recoating | Hall flow < 30 s/50g |
| **Apparent Density** | Affects energy absorption | ASTM B212 |
| **Tap Density** | Indicates packing efficiency | ASTM B527 |
| **Specific Surface Area** | Oxidation risk | BET analysis |
| **Oxygen/Nitrogen Content** | Defect formation | < 0.1 wt% for Ti alloys |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Unit | Application |
|---------------------|------------|------|-------------|
| **Hall Flow Rate** | $t_{flow} = \dfrac{m_{50g}}{50g}$ | s/50g | Powder flowability |
| **Apparent Density** | $\rho_a = \dfrac{m}{V_a}$ | g/cm³ | Loose packing |
| **Tap Density** | $\rho_t = \dfrac{m}{V_t}$ | g/cm³ | Tapped packing |
| **Carr Index (Compressibility)** | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$ | % | Flow indicator |
| **Hausner Ratio** | $H_R = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$ | dimensionless | Flow indicator (HR < 1.25 = good flow) |
| **Laser Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | PBF process parameter |
| **Powder Layer Thickness** | $t_l$ (15–100 μm) | μm | Resolution control |
| **Volumetric Flow (DED)** | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_{wire}$ | mm³/s | Wire DED throughput |
| **Sphericity** | $\Psi = \dfrac{\pi^{1/3}(6V)^{2/3}}{A}$ | dimensionless | Morphology metric |
| **Yield Strength vs. Grain Size (Hall-Petch)** | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ | MPa | AM part strength |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** equation is the single most important formula in PBF-AM. Too little → lack of fusion (porosity); too much → keyholing (vaporization porosity, balling).

### 2.4 Why Hausner Ratio Matters

$$H_R < 1.25 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Good flowability (suitable for PBF)}$$

$$H_R > 1.4 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Poor flowability (unsuitable for thin layers)}$$

This is because **higher packing** under tapping means particles had air gaps that bridged — bad for uniform recoating.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for PBF

The Volumetric Energy Density governs melt pool formation in laser-based powder bed fusion.

### Step 1: Define the Melt Pool Volume

The laser beam moves with scan velocity **v** over a layer of thickness **t**, with hatch spacing **h** between adjacent scan tracks.

$$L_{scan} = v \cdot t_{exposure}$$

where $t_{exposure}$ is the dwell time per unit length.

### Step 2: Energy Delivered per Unit Length

The laser power is **P** (W = J/s). Energy delivered along a unit length of scan:

$$E_{line} = \dfrac{P}{v} \quad [\text{J/mm}]$$

### Step 3: Volume of Material Affected per Unit Length

For a single scan track, the cross-sectional area of the melt pool is approximately:

$$A_{melt} = h \cdot t$$

where **h** is hatch spacing and **t** is layer thickness.

### Step 4: Energy Density (Energy per Unit Volume)

$$E_v = \dfrac{E_{line}}{A_{melt}} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

### Step 5: Final Compact Form

$$\boxed{E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]}$$

**Logic of conversion:** Power (W) divided by velocity (mm/s) yields J/mm. Dividing again by the cross-section (mm × mm) gives J/mm³.

### Step 6: Numerical Example for KTU Exam Practice

**Given:**
- Laser Power $P = 200$ W
- Scan Velocity $v = 800$ mm/s
- Hatch Spacing $h = 0.1$ mm
- Layer Thickness $t = 0.03$ mm

**Compute:**

$$E_v = \dfrac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}$$

**Step-wise calculation:**

$$800 \times 0.1 = 80$$

$$80 \times 0.03 = 2.4$$

$$E_v = \dfrac{200}{2.4} = 83.33 \quad \text{J/mm}^3$$

> [!NOTE]
> Typical **optimal VED** for **316L stainless steel** in SLM is **60–100 J/mm³**. Our computed 83.33 J/mm³ is in the safe processing window.

## 3.2 Derivation: Carr Index and Hausner Ratio Relationship

### Step 1: Start with Definitions

$$\rho_a = \text{apparent (loose) density}, \quad \rho_t = \text{tap (packed) density}$$

### Step 2: Hausner Ratio Definition

$$H_R = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$$

### Step 3: Carr Index Definition (complement of density ratio)

$$CI = \dfrac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} = 1 - \dfrac{\rho_a}{\rho_t} = 1 - \dfrac{1}{H_R}$$

### Step 4: Solved Form

$$\boxed{CI = 1 - \dfrac{1}{H_R}}$$

This proves Carr Index and Hausner Ratio are mathematically equivalent indicators of flowability.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Powder Type vs. AM Process Compatibility

| Powder Type | Sphericity | Flowability | SLM/DMLS | EBM | DED | BJAM |
|-------------|------------|-------------|----------|-----|-----|------|
| Gas-Atomized | High | Excellent | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| PREP | Very High | Excellent | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ | ✅ |
| Plasma-Atomized | Very High | Excellent | ✅ | ✅ | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| Water-Atomized | Low (irregular) | Poor | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ | ✅ |
| Mechanically Milled | Variable | Poor | ❌ | ❌ | ❌ | ⚠️ |

> [!WARNING]
> Water-atomized powders are **NOT** suitable for PBF because irregular morphology causes recoater streaking and uneven layers — a common KTU exam trap!

## 3.4 Wire-Based Metal System Derivation (DED/WAAM)

For Wire-Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM), deposition rate is critical.

**Deposition rate formula:**

$$R_d = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho_{wire} \quad [\text{kg/s}]$$

**Where:**
- $d$ = wire diameter (mm)
- $v_{wire}$ = wire feed speed (mm/s)
- $\rho_{wire}$ = material density (g/cm³)

**Example:** For $d = 1.2$ mm, $v_{wire} = 5$ mm/s, $\rho = 7.85$ g/cm³ (steel):

$$R_d = \dfrac{\pi (1.2)^2}{4} \times 5 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$R_d = 1.131 \times 5 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.0444 \quad \text{kg/s} \approx 2.66 \quad \text{kg/min}$$

> [!NOTE]
> WAAM can deposit **2–4 kg/hour** — far exceeding PBF's **~20–100 g/hour**, making it preferred for large aerospace structural parts.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Metal System Pipeline in AM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Metal Stock] --> B[Powder Production]
    A2[Metal Wire Rod] --> C[Wire Preparation]
    A3[Metal Powder + Binder] --> D[Binder Jetting Feed]
    
    B --> B1[Gas Atomization]
    B --> B2[Plasma Atomization]
    B --> B3[PREP Process]
    B --> B4[Water Atomization]
    
    B1 --> E[Powder Characterization]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    B4 --> E
    
    E --> E1[PSD Analysis]
    E --> E2[Morphology SEM]
    E --> E3[Flowability Test]
    E --> E4[Chemistry O N H]
    
    E --> F[Feedstock Selection]
    C --> F
    D --> F
    
    F --> F1[Powder Bed Fusion SLM DMLS EBM]
    F --> F2[Directed Energy Deposition DED WAAM]
    F --> F3[Binder Jetting BJAM]
    
    F1 --> G[Final Metal Part]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G1[Green Part]
    G1 --> G2[Debinding and Sintering]
    G2 --> G
    
    G --> H[Post Processing Heat Treatment HIP Machining]
    H --> I[Qualified Component]
```

## 4.2 Process Selection Flowchart Based on Metal System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Select Feedstock Form] --> q1{Powder?}
    q1 -->|Yes| q2{Particle Size 15 to 45 um?}
    q1 -->|No| wirepath[Wire System]
    
    q2 -->|Yes| pbf[Powder Bed Fusion]
    q2 -->|No, larger| q3{45 to 150 um?}
    q3 -->|Yes| ded[Directed Energy Deposition]
    q3 -->|No| bj[Binder Jetting]
    
    wirepath --> waam[Wire Arc AM WAAM]
    wirepath --> ebf[Electron Beam Free Form EBF3]
    
    pbf --> outcome1[High Resolution Complex Geometry]
    ded --> outcome2[Large Parts Repair Cladding]
    bj --> outcome3[High Volume Batch Production]
    waam --> outcome4[Large Structural Aerospace Parts]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Gas Atomization (Block Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Furnace[Melting Furnace]
        F1[Induction Coil]
        F2[Vacuum or Inert Chamber]
    end
    
    subgraph Atomization[Atomization Zone]
        N1[Tundish Nozzle]
        GJ1[Gas Jet Ring Argon N2]
        GJ2[Gas Jet Ring Argon N2]
    end
    
    subgraph Tower[Atomization Tower]
        T1[In flight Solidification]
        T2[Cooling Zone]
    end
    
    subgraph Collection[Powder Collection]
        C1[Cyclone Separator]
        C2[Hopper]
        C3[Sieve Classifier]
    end
    
    F1 --> N1
    N1 --> GJ1
    GJ1 --> GJ2
    GJ2 --> T1
    T1 --> T2
    T2 --> C1
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
```

## 4.4 Effect of Process Parameters on AM Part Quality (Cause-Effect)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Process Parameters] --> P1[Laser Power]
    P --> P2[Scan Speed]
    P --> P3[Hatch Spacing]
    P --> P4[Layer Thickness]
    
    P1 --> E1[Energy Density]
    P2 --> E1
    P3 --> E1
    P4 --> E1
    
    E1 --> R1{Optimal Range}
    R1 -->|Too Low| D1[Lack of Fusion Porosity]
    R1 -->|Optimal| D2[Dense Part 99.5 percent plus]
    R1 -->|Too High| D3[Keyholing Balling Vaporization]
    
    D1 --> Q[Reduced Mechanical Properties]
    D2 --> Q2[High Quality Part]
    D3 --> Q
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]
**Differentiate between Gas Atomization and Water Atomization. State two advantages of gas-atomized powders in AM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Property | Gas Atomization | Water Atomization |
|----------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Atomizing medium | Inert gas (Ar, N₂) | High-pressure water |
| Particle morphology | Highly spherical | Irregular, dendritic |
| Surface quality | Smooth, low satellites | Rough, satellites common |
| Oxidation risk | Low | High (water reacts with metals) |
| Cost | High | Low |
| AM suitability | Excellent (PBF, DED) | Limited (BJAM only) |

**Two advantages of gas-atomized powders:**
1. **High sphericity** → excellent flowability, uniform recoating in PBF
2. **Low oxide content** → superior mechanical properties in final part

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Define Hausner Ratio. What is its significance in metal AM powder selection?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Hausner Ratio (H_R)** is defined as the ratio of tap density to apparent density of a powder:

$$H_R = \dfrac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$$

**Significance:**
- Indicates **flowability** of metal powder
- $H_R < 1.25$ → good flow → suitable for PBF processes
- $H_R > 1.4$ → poor flow → causes recoater streaking, non-uniform layers, defects
- Critical parameter in qualifying powders for aerospace and medical AM applications

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Gas Atomization process** for producing metal powders suitable for AM. Describe the resulting powder characteristics and why they are essential for Powder Bed Fusion. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A SLM machine uses the following parameters: Laser Power = 250 W, Scan Speed = 1000 mm/s, Hatch Spacing = 0.12 mm, Layer Thickness = 0.04 mm. Calculate the **Volumetric Energy Density** and comment on whether the part is likely to have defects. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Gas Atomization Process:**

1. **Melting:** Raw metal (e.g., Ti-6Al-4V) is melted in an induction furnace under vacuum or inert atmosphere (to prevent oxidation).
2. **Stream Formation:** The molten metal flows through a tundish and forms a thin, stable stream at the nozzle tip.
3. **Atomization:** High-pressure inert gas (Argon at 3–8 MPa) impinges on the molten stream from a ring of nozzles, breaking it into fine droplets via **Rayleigh instability**.
4. **Spheroidization:** Surface tension pulls the droplets into spheres during flight.
5. **Solidification:** Droplets solidify in the atomization tower (typically 5–8 m tall).
6. **Collection:** Powder is collected via cyclone separators and hoppers, then classified by sieving.

**Resulting Powder Characteristics Essential for PBF:**
- **Spherical morphology** → uniform flow, consistent layer deposition
- **Narrow size distribution (15–45 μm)** → controlled layer thickness, high resolution
- **Low satellite content** → reduces defects and clogging
- **Low oxygen/nitrogen content** → prevents embrittlement (especially critical for Ti alloys)
- **High apparent and tap density** → high packing fraction → dense final parts

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:**
- $P = 250$ W
- $v = 1000$ mm/s
- $h = 0.12$ mm
- $t = 0.04$ mm

**Formula:**

$$E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Calculation:**

$$v \cdot h \cdot t = 1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.04$$

$$= 1000 \times 0.0048 = 4.8 \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$E_v = \dfrac{250}{4.8} = 52.08 \quad \text{J/mm}^3$$

**Comment:**

- Typical optimal VED for **316L SS in SLM** is **60–100 J/mm³**
- Computed value **52.08 J/mm³ is BELOW the safe threshold**
- Likely defects: **Lack of fusion porosity**, weak inter-layer bonding, poor mechanical strength
- **Recommendation:** Increase power, decrease scan speed, or reduce hatch spacing

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Compare **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** and **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** with respect to metal system requirements, typical powders used, and application areas. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A WAAM system deposits steel wire of diameter 1.0 mm at a feed speed of 6 mm/s. Steel density = 7.85 g/cm³. Calculate the **deposition rate in kg/min** and discuss the build rate advantage of WAAM over PBF. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

| Aspect | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | Directed Energy Deposition (DED) |
|--------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Powder size** | 15–45 μm (fine) | 45–150 μm (coarse) |
| **Morphology requirement** | High sphericity, strict | Moderate, less strict |
| **Feedstock form** | Powder bed | Powder nozzle or wire |
| **Energy source** | Laser (SLM/DMLS) or Electron beam (EBM) | Laser + powder, or Arc + wire (WAAM) |
| **Layer thickness** | 20–100 μm | 0.5–few mm |
| **Build envelope** | Limited (typically < 500 mm) | Large (meters possible) |
| **Typical materials** | Ti-6Al-4V, IN718, 316L, AlSi10Mg | Ti, Steel, Inconel, tool steel |
| **Applications** | Aerospace brackets, medical implants, dental | Large structural parts, repair, cladding |
| **Surface finish** | Better (Ra 5–20 μm) | Rougher (post-machining required) |

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Formula:**

$$R_d = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} \cdot v_{wire} \cdot \rho_{wire}$$

**Calculation:**

$$\text{Cross-sectional area} = \dfrac{\pi (1.0)^2}{4} = 0.7854 \quad \text{mm}^2$$

$$\text{Volume rate} = 0.7854 \times 6 = 4.7124 \quad \text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\text{Mass rate} = 4.7124 \times 7.85 \times 10^{-3} = 0.03699 \quad \text{g/s}$$

**Convert to kg/min:**

$$R_d = 0.03699 \times 60 \times 10^{-3} = 0.002219 \quad \text{kg/min}$$

$$\approx 2.22 \quad \text{kg/hr}$$

**Build Rate Advantage:**
- PBF systems typically deposit **20–100 g/hr** of metal
- WAAM deposits **~2.22 kg/hr** in this case — **~20–100× faster**
- WAAM is preferred for **large structural aerospace parts** (e.g., wing ribs, fuselage sections) where build speed outweighs resolution needs
- **Trade-off:** Lower dimensional accuracy and surface finish require post-machining

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Metal Systems Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing Gas and Water Atomization** — students often interchange their morphologies. Remember: **Gas = Spherical**, **Water = Irregular**.
> 2. **Incorrect unit conversion in VED** — forgetting to convert hatch spacing and layer thickness to mm causes 10×–100× errors.
> 3. **Mixing up Hausner Ratio and Carr Index formulas** — they are related but **NOT identical**; always state the formula being used.
> 4. **Ignoring units in WAAM deposition rate** — wire diameter in mm and density in g/cm³ require careful unit consistency.
> 5. **Writing definitions without applications** — KTU examiners award marks for *why* a property matters, not just *what* it is.
> 6. **Skipping steps in numerical problems** — every transition step must be shown explicitly for full marks.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Metal Systems (Module 1)**

- ⭐ **ASTM F2792** is the standard classification for AM processes; metal AM falls under 4 categories: PBF, DED, Material Extrusion, Binder Jetting
- ⭐ **Three main metal feedstock forms:** Powder, Wire, Paste/Binder
- ⭐ **Gas Atomization** is the dominant powder production method → yields **spherical, flowable** powders
- ⭐ **Water Atomization** → irregular morphology → **NOT suitable for PBF** (only for BJAM)
- ⭐ **PREP** → premium quality, used for **Ti-6Al-4V in aerospace**
- ⭐ **Key powder properties:** PSD, Sphericity, Flowability (Hall flow, Hausner Ratio, Carr Index), Oxygen content
- ⭐ **Hausner Ratio threshold:** $H_R < 1.25$ = good flow
- ⭐ **Volumetric Energy Density (VED):** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ in J/mm³
  - Too low → Lack of fusion
  - Too high → Keyholing
  - Optimal range is **material-specific**
- ⭐ **PBF uses fine powders (15–45 μm); DED uses coarser powders (45–150 μm)**
- ⭐ **WAAM deposition rate >> PBF**, but with lower resolution
- ⭐ **Hall-Petch equation** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y d^{-1/2}$ explains why fine microstructures in AM yield high strength
- ⭐ **Reactive metals (Ti, Al)** require inert/vacuum environments throughout
- ⭐ **Powder reuse** is possible but degrades quality due to oxygen pickup and morphology change
- ⭐ **BJAM workflow:** Print → Curing → **Debinding** → **Sintering** → Infiltration (optional)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Exam Topics from this Module:**
> 1. Powder production methods (especially gas vs. water atomization)
> 2. Powder characterization techniques
> 3. VED calculation and defect analysis
> 4. Process selection based on metal system
> 5. Wire-arc AM (WAAM) deposition rate calculations

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hybrid Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Hybrid Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 1: Hybrid Systems in Additive Manufacturing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme)**
> **Hybrid Additive/Subtractive Manufacturing Systems** are integrated, single-platform manufacturing cells that seamlessly combine **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** processes (layer-by-layer material addition) with **Subtractive Manufacturing (SM)** processes (CNC milling, turning, drilling, grinding) within the same machine envelope. The objective is to overcome the geometric, surface-finish, and material limitations of standalone AM by leveraging the strengths of both process families.

### 1.1 Formal Classification (KTU Terminology)

A *hybrid system* in the AM context is defined by **three mandatory integration criteria** as per ASTM F42 standards:

| Criterion | Requirement |
|---|---|
| **Common Work Envelope** | Both additive and subtractive heads operate on the same part without re-fixturing |
| **Common Coordinate System** | Unified CAD/CAM toolpath; no datum loss between operations |
| **Common Process Controller** | Single NC kernel sequences additive and subtractive operations |

> [!NOTE]
> Simply placing an AM machine next to a CNC mill is **NOT** considered a hybrid system — it is a *multi-process cell*. True hybridization demands the **three criteria above**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sculptor + Plumber" Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are building a **sandcastle**. The *additive process* is like **shoveling wet sand** to form the rough bulk shape quickly. The *subtractive process* is like **carving and scraping** with a trowel to get sharp edges, smooth walls, and fine details. A child doing both with the same toolkit in the same sandbox is *working in a hybrid mode* — no need to move the sandcastle to a different table for finishing.

**Why this analogy works for engineering:**
- **AM** = Builds near-net shape, handles internal lattices, complex overhangs
- **SM** = Delivers critical tolerances, mirror surface finish, mating interfaces
- **Hybrid** = Both on the same machine → reduced lead time, eliminated clamping error, single setup

### 1.3 Why Hybrid Systems? — The Driving Motivation

| Limitation of Standalone AM | How Hybridization Solves It |
|---|---|
| Poor **surface roughness** (Ra 10–20 µm as-built) | In-situ milling brings Ra down to **0.8 µm** |
| Staircase effect on curved surfaces | Contour-parallel milling corrects the steps |
| Lack of **critical mating features** (threads, holes, flats) | Drilling/tapping in same setup |
| Internal stresses and warping | Subtractively stress-relieve via machining |
| Powder-trapped features (blind holes, slots) | Subtractive cleanup of trapped powder |
| Limited material library (mostly single alloys) | Enables multi-material AM + finishing of dissimilar joints |

### 1.4 Standard Industrial Metrics Used in Hybrid AM

> [!NOTE]
> Key performance indicators (KPIs) for hybrid systems (per ISO/ASTM 52900):
> - **Net Build Volume**: typically **300 × 300 × 300 mm** to **600 × 600 × 500 mm**
> - **Hybrid Lead-Time Reduction**: **30 % – 60 %** vs. separate AM + SM workflow
> - **Dimensional Tolerance after Hybrid Processing**: **±0.025 mm** to **±0.05 mm**
> - **Surface Finish (post-milling)**: **Ra ≤ 0.8 µm**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Architectural Breakdown of Hybrid Systems

A hybrid AM system can be decomposed into **five functional subsystems**:
