# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**List any three application areas of Additive Manufacturing and explain why AM is preferred over conventional manufacturing in one of them.**

**Model Answer:**

The three major application areas of AM are:

1. **Aerospace** — production of lightweight topology-optimized brackets, fuel nozzles, and turbine components.
2. **Medical and Dental** — patient-specific implants, prosthetics, and surgical guides derived from CT/MRI scans.
3. **Automotive** — rapid prototyping, low-volume end-use parts, and conformal cooling inserts.

**Aerospace — Why AM is preferred:**
- Weight reduction of **20–55%** through topology optimization, directly reducing fuel burn.
- **Part consolidation** (e.g., GE LEAP fuel nozzle: 18 parts → 1 part) eliminates joints and improves reliability.
- **Buy-to-Fly ratio** improved from ~20:1 to nearly 1:1, drastically reducing material waste.
- **Lead time** for spare parts reduced by 70–90%, critical for MRO operations.

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**Explain the role of Additive Manufacturing in the medical field with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

AM plays a transformative role in medicine by enabling **patient-specific (personalized)** solutions that traditional manufacturing cannot economically deliver.

**Examples:**

1. **Cranial Implants** — Custom titanium skull plates are 3D-printed from CT scan data, ensuring perfect anatomical fit for trauma patients. Manufactured using **SLM** with **Ti6Al4V ELI** (Extra Low Interstitial) grade for biocompatibility.

2. **Dental Crowns and Bridges** — Scanned using intra-oral scanners, designed in CAD, and printed via **DLP** using cobalt-chrome or zirconia, achieving accuracy of **≤ 25 μm**.

3. **Hearing Aids** — Custom-fitted shells are **SLA-printed** to match the unique ear canal geometry, with over **99%** of hearing aids worldwide now made via AM.

4. **Surgical Guides** — Patient-specific drill guides and cutting jigs for orthopedic and maxillofacial surgery, reducing operation time by 20–40%.

5. **Bioprinting** — Emerging area using **bio-inks** (hydrogels with living cells) to print tissue scaffolds for regenerative medicine.

---

## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the application of Additive Manufacturing in the **aerospace industry** with at least **four specific examples**. For each example, mention the AM process, material used, and the benefit obtained.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A conventional aerospace bracket has a mass of $1.8$ kg. After AM-based topology optimization, its mass becomes $0.99$ kg. If $2.5$ kg of raw Ti6Al4V powder is fed into the SLM machine for this part, calculate: (i) the percentage weight reduction, and (ii) the Buy-to-Fly ratio.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Aerospace Applications:**

| # | Component | AM Process | Material | Benefit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | LEAP Fuel Nozzle | DMLS | Co-Cr alloy | 18 parts → 1 part, weight 25% lighter, improved fuel atomization |
| 2 | Airbus A350 Bracket (pylon) | SLM | Ti6Al4V | 30% lighter than conventional, BTF ratio reduced from 8:1 to 1:1 |
| 3 | Rocket Engine Injector (SpaceX Raptor) | DMLS | Inconel 718 | Regenerative cooling channels, 1-piece construction, withstands 3000°C |
| 4 | Satellite Antenna Waveguide | SLM | Aluminum AlSi10Mg | 40% weight savings, single-piece complex internal channels |
| 5 | GEnx Engine T25 Sensor Housing | EBM | Ti6Al4V | Lead time reduced from 6 months to 2 weeks |

**Why AM in aerospace:**
- **Topology optimization** allows organic shapes that mimic natural load paths, removing material from low-stress regions.
- **Conformal cooling** in rocket thrust chambers allows uniform temperature distribution, extending component life.
- **Digital inventory** — files stored digitally and printed on demand at any global location, revolutionizing MRO logistics.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $W_{conv} = 1.8$ kg
- $W_{AM} = 0.99$ kg
- $m_{raw} = 2.5$ kg

**(i) Percentage weight reduction:**

$$
\Delta W = \frac{W_{conv} - W_{AM}}{W_{conv}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\Delta W = \frac{1.8 - 0.99}{1.8} \times 100\% = \frac{0.81}{1.8} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta W = 45\%}
$$

**(ii) Buy-to-Fly ratio:**

$$
BTF = \frac{m_{raw}}{m_{final}} = \frac{2.5}{0.99} \approx 2.53
$$

$$
\boxed{BTF \approx 2.53 : 1}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** — Always specify kg, mm, or μm. Writing only "0.81" without units is a common error that costs **1 mark**.
> 2. **Confusing weight reduction vs. Buy-to-Fly** — Weight reduction compares **final part mass**, but BTF compares **raw material to final part mass** (a different denominator).
> 3. **Not showing the percentage in BTF or in weight reduction** — Both metrics must be expressed with proper units; "45" without "%" loses a mark.
> 4. **Skipping the conversion logic** — Always write the substitution step *before* the final answer in numerical questions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024 — Alternative Choice]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the application of Additive Manufacturing in the **medical and dental field**. Discuss at least **five specific applications** with their process and material.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A custom cranial implant must fit a defect area of surface **6,200 mm²**. The AM process (SLM) deposits Ti6Al4V powder at a build rate of $R_v = 8.5$ $\text{cm}^3 / \text{hr}$. If the implant has a uniform thickness of $3.5$ mm, calculate the build time. (Density of Ti6Al4V = $4.43$ $\text{g/cm}^3$).

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Medical and Dental Applications:**

1. **Cranial Implants (Cranioplasty)** — Patient-specific skull plates, designed from CT data, manufactured by **SLM** using **Ti6Al4V ELI** (biocompatible, low allergenicity). Eliminates need for intra-operative shaping.

2. **Dental Crowns and Bridges** — **DLP (Digital Light Processing)** using **zirconia** or **cobalt-chrome** alloys. Achieves $\le 25 \mu m$ accuracy; marginal gap $< 50 \mu m$ which is clinically acceptable.

3. **Orthopedic Implants (Hip, Knee, Spinal)** — **EBM (Electron Beam Melting)** with **Ti6Al4V** enables **lattice/trabecular structures** that mimic cancellous bone, promoting osseointegration and reducing stress shielding.

4. **Hearing Aid Shells** — **SLA** with **biocompatible photopolymer (MED610/620)**. Custom-fitted to ear canal in **< 24 hours** turnaround; industry standard (99% market share).

5. **Surgical Guides and Anatomical Models** — **SLA/SLS** using **biocompatible resins** for drill guides, cutting templates, and pre-operative rehearsal models. Reduces surgical time by 20–40%.

6. **Prosthetics and Orthotics** — **FDM** for low-cost prosthetic sockets customized via 3D scanning. Lightweight, low cost (< **$50** per limb socket).

7. **Bioprinting of Tissues** — Emerging: **Bio-extrusion** of cell-laden hydrogels (alginate, GelMA) for cartilage, skin, and organoid printing.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- Surface area $A = 6{,}200$ $\text{mm}^2$
- Thickness $t = 3.5$ mm
- Build rate $R_v = 8.5$ $\text{cm}^3 / \text{hr}$
- Density $\rho = 4.43$ $\text{g/cm}^3$ (not needed for time calculation, but useful for mass verification)

**Step 1 — Compute the volume of the implant:**

$$
V = A \times t = 6{,}200 \text{ mm}^2 \times 3.5 \text{ mm}
$$

$$
V = 21{,}700 \text{ mm}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Convert volume to cm³ (since build rate is in cm³/hr):**

$$
1 \text{ cm}^3 = 1{,}000 \text{ mm}^3
$$

$$
V = \frac{21{,}700}{1{,}000} = 21.7 \text{ cm}^3
$$

**Step 3 — Compute build time:**

$$
t_{build} = \frac{V}{R_v} = \frac{21.7 \text{ cm}^3}{8.5 \text{ cm}^3/\text{hr}}
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{build} \approx 2.55 \text{ hours}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where students lose marks:**
> 1. **Unit conversion** — Most common mistake is keeping mm³ and dividing by cm³/hr, producing a wrong answer. Always convert **all** units to a **single consistent system** before substitution.
> 2. **Density trap** — The density value is provided to test whether students can identify *relevant* vs. *irrelevant* data. It is **not** needed for the build time calculation; using it incorrectly is a sign of weak problem analysis.
> 3. **Reporting time** — Express the answer in appropriate units (hours) or convert to "**2 hours 33 minutes**" for a more engineering-realistic answer. Both are acceptable, but unit clarity is essential.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (High-Yield for KTU Exams):**

- **AM Definition (ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900):** Joining of materials layer-by-layer from 3D model data.
- **7 Standard AM Process Categories:** Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Binder Jetting, Material Jetting, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination.
- **Aerospace AM:** Ti6Al4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg via SLM/DMLS/EBM. Key examples: GE LEAP fuel nozzle (18→1 part), Airbus A350 bracket.
- **Medical AM:** SLM, EBM, DLP, SLA. Key examples: cranial implants, dental crowns, hearing aids (~99% market share), surgical guides.
- **Automotive AM:** Bugatti brake caliper, jigs/fixtures, conformal cooling inserts.
- **Tooling AM:** Conformal cooling channels reduce cycle time by **20–40%**.
- **Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BTF):** $BTF = m_{raw} / m_{final}$ — typical AM values **1:1 to 5:1**, conventional aerospace **10:1 to 20:1**.
- **Weight Reduction Formula:** $\Delta W = [(W_{conv} - W_{AM}) / W_{conv}] \times 100\%$ — typical range **20–55%**.
- **Part Consolidation Factor (PCF):** $PCF = N_{conv} / N_{AM}$ — e.g., GE nozzle = 18.
- **Lead Time Reduction (LTR):** Typically **70–90%** vs. conventional.
- **Surface roughness ($R_a$):** As-built metal AM typically **5–20 μm**; often needs post-machining for tight-tolerance surfaces.
- **Build volume vs. accuracy trade-off:** Larger build volume generally reduces resolution.
- **Volume calculation for build time:** Always convert to consistent units; $t_{build} = V / R_v$.
- **Density is irrelevant for time-based problems** unless the question asks for **mass**.
- **Conformal cooling = AM's killer application** in injection mold tooling — eliminates hotspots and reduces warpage.
- **Hearing aid industry = 99% AM adoption** — most established AM mass-customization example.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Stereolithography (SL)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Stereolithography (SL)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Stereolithography (SL / SLA)** is a **vat photopolymerization** based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process that creates three-dimensional solid objects by selectively curing a **photopolymer liquid resin** layer-by-layer using a focused, computer-controlled beam of **ultraviolet (UV) light**. The process, patented by **Charles W. Hull in 1984** and commercialized by **3D Systems Corporation**, belongs to the ISO/ASTM 52900 category **"Vat Photopolymerization"** and operates on the principle of **photo-initiated chain-growth polymerization**, wherein photoinitiators within the resin absorb photon energy, dissociate into free radicals, and trigger crosslinking of monomers/oligomers to form a solidified polymer network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Stereolithography is historically the **first commercially available AM process** (1986) and is officially classified under the **"Vat Photopolymerization"** category in the ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 standard. It is a **7-axis process** (3 for part geometry + 1 for recoater + 1 for elevator + 2 for beam deflection via galvanometers).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you have a tray of **honey** (the photopolymer resin) and a **laser pointer** (the UV laser). If you sweep the laser pointer across the surface of the honey, nothing happens — but if the honey contained a special **photochemical** (a photoinitiator), the regions touched by the laser would instantly **harden into a solid plastic film**, just like cooking an egg white with heat.

Now imagine doing this **one thin slice at a time**: the laser draws the bottom slice of a 3D object, hardens it, the tray dips down by 0.1 mm, a new layer of liquid honey flows in, the laser draws the next slice, and so on. After several hundred slices, a solid 3D model emerges from the bath — **this is exactly how Stereolithography works!**

> [!NOTE]
> **SLA vs. SLS (Don't Confuse!):**
> - **SLA** uses **liquid resin + UV light** (Vat Photopolymerization).
> - **SLS** uses **powder + laser heat** (Powder Bed Fusion).
> These two are commonly confused by students in KTU exams.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Parameters

The hallmark physical constants and process metrics of SL are:

- **Layer thickness (h):** 25 µm to 100 µm (typical: 50 µm)
- **Wavelength of UV laser:** 355 nm (Nd:YVO₄ frequency-tripled) or 405 nm (GaN diode)
- **Curing depth (Cd):** Typically 0.1 mm to 0.5 mm
- **Penetration depth (Dp):** 100 µm to 500 µm (resin dependent)
- **Build envelope:** Up to 1500 × 750 × 550 mm (industrial)
- **Accuracy:** ± 0.1 mm (high precision)
- **Part density:** Approximately 1.1 g/cm³ to 1.2 g/cm³ for cured acrylate resin

> [!TIP]
> KTU examiners frequently award marks for citing the **correct ISO/ASTM category** and the **inventor + year**. Always mention *"Charles Hull, 1984"* and *"Vat Photopolymerization"* explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The Stereolithography process follows a precise, repeatable sequence known as the **"Layer-by-Layer Photopolymerization Cycle."** Each layer involves six logical sub-steps:

**Step 1 — Substrate Preparation (Initial Step):**
- A metal build platform is lowered into a vat containing the liquid photopolymer resin.
- The platform is positioned at a depth equal to **one layer thickness (h)** below the liquid surface.
- A **recoater blade (or wiper)** sweeps across the surface to ensure a uniform liquid film.

**Step 2 — Laser Beam Generation & Modulation:**
- A **UV laser source** (typically a He-Cd laser @ 325 nm, or a solid-state Nd:YVO₄ laser @ 355 nm, or a GaN diode @ 405 nm) emits a coherent beam.
- The beam is modulated by a **laser beam modulator** (acousto-optic or electro-optic) to switch on/off instantaneously.

**Step 3 — Beam Steering via Galvanometers:**
- Two high-speed **galvanometer mirrors** (X-axis and Y-axis) deflect the laser beam to scan the resin surface in a pre-computed **vector scan path** (also called "scan vectors" or "hatches").
- The scanner traces the cross-section of the part for the current layer.

**Step 4 — Photo-Initiated Polymerization (Curing):**
- Photons from the laser beam strike the resin, activating **photoinitiators** (e.g., BAPO, TPO, or Igracure 819).
- Free radicals propagate chain reactions that crosslink **acrylates or epoxides**, converting the liquid into a solid polymer network.
- The reaction threshold is governed by the **critical energy (Ec)** of the resin.

**Step 5 — Platform Lowering & Recoating:**
- After one layer is fully cured, the **z-axis elevator** lowers the platform by exactly **one layer thickness (h)**.
- The recoater blade re-spreads fresh liquid resin over the previously cured layer.
- A settling time (typically 1–5 seconds) allows the resin to level.

**Step 6 — Repetition & Part Removal:**
- Steps 2 to 5 are repeated **N times**, where N = (Part Height / h).
- After the final layer, the platform is raised, the part is removed, and excess resin is drained.
- The part is then **post-cured in a UV oven** to achieve full polymerization and final mechanical properties.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **The following table is the HIGHEST-YIELD content for KTU 2024 numerical problems.** Memorize the Jacob's Working Curve equation, critical energy, and energy density formulas.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description & Units |
|---|----------|---------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Curing Depth (Cd)** | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{E}{E_c}\right)$ | Cd in mm; Dp = penetration depth; E = exposure energy density (mJ/cm²); Ec = critical energy (mJ/cm²) |
| 2 | **Exposure Energy Density (E)** | $E = \dfrac{P}{V_s \cdot H_s}$ | P = laser power (mW); Vs = scan speed (mm/s); Hs = hatch spacing (mm) |
| 3 | **Volumetric Energy Density (Ev)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{V_s \cdot H_s \cdot L_s}$ | Ls = layer thickness (mm); units = mJ/mm³ |
| 4 | **Beer-Lambert Law (Optical)** | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$ | Intensity decays exponentially with depth z |
| 5 | **Number of Layers (N)** | $N = \dfrac{H_{total}}{h}$ | Htotal = total part height (mm); h = layer thickness (mm) |
| 6 | **Build Time (T)** | $T = N \cdot \left(\dfrac{A_{layer}}{V_s \cdot H_s} + t_{settle}\right)$ | Alayer = scan area per layer (mm²); tsettle = settling time (s) |
| 7 | **Polymerization Threshold** | $E \geq E_c$ | Curing only occurs if exposure ≥ critical energy |
| 8 | **Working Curve (Jacob's)** | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E) - D_p \cdot \ln(E_c)$ | Linear in ln(E); slope = Dp, intercept = −Dp·ln(Ec) |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

Stereolithography is the **gold standard** for industries demanding high resolution, smooth surface finish, and isotropic mechanical properties:

- **Biomedical:** Custom surgical guides, dental crowns, hearing-aid shells, anatomical models for pre-surgical planning.
- **Investment Casting:** SLA patterns are used as sacrificial patterns for "lost-wax" metal casting of turbine blades and jewelry.
- **Optical Components:** Prototyping of lenses, light-pipes, and microfluidic devices.
- **Automotive:** Concept models, master patterns for injection molding, aerodynamic validation.
- **Jewelry:** Indirect production via investment casting due to exceptional surface finish (Ra ≈ 0.4 µm after polishing).
- **Electronics:** MEMS packaging, micro-lattice structures, dielectric substrates.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU favourite 14-mark question pattern:** "Explain the working of Stereolithography with a neat block diagram and derive Jacob's Working Curve equation." → Always start with the **Beer-Lambert law** and integrate step-by-step.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Curing Depth (Jacob's Working Curve) Equation

This is the **most critical derivation** for KTU 2024 Module 2. We begin from the fundamental physics of light absorption in a photopolymer.

### Step A — Starting Point: Beer-Lambert Law

When a monochromatic UV beam of intensity $I_0$ enters a photopolymer resin, its intensity decays exponentially with depth $z$ due to absorption by photoinitiators.

The classical Beer-Lambert law for absorbing media states:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot z}$$

where:
- $I(z)$ = intensity at depth $z$ (mW/cm²)
- $I_0$ = incident intensity at the surface (mW/cm²)
- $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient of the resin (mm⁻¹)
- $z$ = depth measured downward from the surface (mm)

**Why this form?** The energy absorbed per unit volume at depth z is proportional to the local intensity. As light travels deeper, more photons are absorbed, and the surviving intensity drops exponentially.

### Step B — Convert Absorption Coefficient to Penetration Depth

For photopolymer physics, the **penetration depth** $D_p$ is defined as the depth at which the intensity falls to $1/e$ (≈ 36.8%) of its original value.

Setting $z = D_p$ and $I = I_0/e$:

$$\frac{I_0}{e} = I_0 \cdot e^{-\alpha \cdot D_p}$$

Taking the natural logarithm of both sides:

$$-1 = -\alpha \cdot D_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \alpha = \frac{1}{D_p}$$

Therefore, the Beer-Lambert law becomes:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

### Step C — Energy Density at Depth z

The **energy density** $E(z)$ delivered to depth $z$ is the product of the local intensity and the exposure time $t$:

$$E(z) = I(z) \cdot t = I_0 \cdot t \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

At the surface ($z = 0$):

$$E_0 = I_0 \cdot t$$

Substituting back:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

### Step D — Applying the Polymerization Threshold

Curing only occurs if the local energy density exceeds the **critical energy density** $E_c$ of the resin. At the boundary of the cured region, $E(z) = E_c$ and $z = C_d$ (the curing depth).

$$E_c = E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d/D_p}$$

Rearranging:

$$e^{-C_d/D_p} = \frac{E_c}{E_0}$$

$$e^{C_d/D_p} = \frac{E_0}{E_c}$$

### Step E — Take the Natural Logarithm

Taking $\ln$ of both sides:

$$\frac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = \ln(E_0) - \ln(E_c)$$

### Step F — Final Result: Jacob's Working Curve Equation

$$\boxed{C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** $C_d$ increases linearly with $\ln(E_0)$. Plotting $C_d$ vs. $\ln(E_0)$ gives a straight line with **slope = $D_p$** and **intercept = $-D_p \cdot \ln(E_c)$**. The point where $C_d = 0$ gives the critical exposure $E_0 = E_c$. This graph is famously called **"Jacob's Working Curve"** (after Dr. Paul F. Jacobs, SLA pioneer).

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 2024 Pattern)

**Problem:** A photopolymer resin has penetration depth $D_p = 0.30$ mm and critical energy $E_c = 8.5$ mJ/cm². The SLA machine exposes the resin with energy density $E_0 = 45$ mJ/cm². Calculate:
(a) the curing depth $C_d$, and
(b) the exposure required to cure exactly 0.40 mm deep.

### Solution (a):

Applying Jacob's equation:

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

$$C_d = 0.30 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{45}{8.5}\right)$$

$$C_d = 0.30 \cdot \ln(5.2941)$$

$$C_d = 0.30 \cdot 1.6667 = 0.500 \text{ mm}$$

### Solution (b):

For $C_d = 0.40$ mm, solve for $E_0$:

$$0.40 = 0.30 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{8.5}\right)$$

$$\frac{0.40}{0.30} = \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{8.5}\right)$$

$$1.3333 = \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{8.5}\right)$$

$$e^{1.3333} = \frac{E_0}{8.5}$$

$$3.7937 = \frac{E_0}{8.5} \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_0 = 8.5 \times 3.7937 = 32.25 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

> [!TIP]
> KTU board examiners often give $D_p$ in micrometres and $C_d$ in micrometres — keep units **consistent** throughout the calculation.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: SL Process Parameters vs. Outcomes

| Process Parameter ↑ | Effect on Curing Depth | Effect on Build Time | Effect on Part Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Laser Power (P)** | ↑ Increases (higher $E_0$) | ↓ Decreases | Risk of over-cure |
| **Scan Speed (Vs)** | ↓ Decreases (lower $E_0$) | ↑ Increases | Higher resolution |
| **Hatch Spacing (Hs)** | ↓ Decreases (lower $E_0$) | ↓ Decreases | Gaps if too large |
| **Layer Thickness (h)** | — | ↓ Decreases (fewer layers) | ↓ Z-resolution |
| **Penetration Depth (Dp)** | ↑ Increases | — | Defines vertical cure profile |
| **Critical Energy (Ec)** | — | — | Material-dependent threshold |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of the SLA Process

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the **full functional architecture** of a Stereolithography Apparatus, including all hardware subsystems, control loops, and process data flows. This is the diagram KTU examiners expect in a 14-mark question.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model in STL Format] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Layer-by-Layer Slice Data]
    C --> D[Process Controller CPU]
    D --> E1[Laser Source Control]
    D --> E2[Galvanometer X Mirror Control]
    D --> E3[Galvanometer Y Mirror Control]
    D --> E4[Z-Axis Elevator Control]
    D --> E5[Recoater Blade Drive]
    E1 --> F[UV Laser 355 nm or 405 nm]
    F --> G[Beam Expander Optics]
    G --> H1[X Galvanometer Mirror]
    H1 --> H2[Y Galvanometer Mirror]
    H2 --> I[Focusing F-Theta Lens]
    I --> J[Liquid Photopolymer Resin Vat]
    J --> K[Build Platform Z Stage]
    E4 --> K
    E5 --> L[Recoater Blade]
    L --> J
    K --> M[3D Cured Solid Part]
    M --> N[Cleaning Station IPA Wash]
    N --> O[UV Post-Cure Oven]
    O --> P[Finished SLA Part]
    D --> Q[Closed Loop Feedback]
    Q --> D
```

**Reading the Diagram:**

1. The **STL file** is sliced by software into 2D cross-sections.
2. The **Process Controller** coordinates all four subsystems (laser, scanners, elevator, recoater).
3. The **UV laser** is steered by two **galvanometer mirrors** through an **F-theta lens** onto the **resin vat**.
4. The **recoater blade** levels fresh resin after each layer.
5. The **build platform** lowers by exactly one layer thickness (h) after each cure cycle.
6. The finished green part is washed in **isopropanol (IPA)** and post-cured in a **UV oven**.

## 4.2 Photopolymerization Reaction Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[UV Photon hv] --> B[Photoinitiator Molecule PI]
    B --> C[Excited State PI asterisk]
    C --> D[Free Radical R dot]
    D --> E[Initiates Monomer M Chain]
    E --> F[Growing Polymer Chain Pn]
    F --> G[Crosslinked 3D Network]
    G --> H[Solid Cured Part]
    H -.Post Cure UV.-> H
```

**Reaction Sequence Decoded:**

- $PI \xrightarrow{h\nu} PI^* \rightarrow R^{\cdot} + \text{by-products}$
- $R^{\cdot} + M \rightarrow RM^{\cdot} \rightarrow \ldots \rightarrow P_n$
- $P_n + P_m \rightarrow \text{Crosslinked Solid}$

## 4.3 Component-Level Hardware Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HardwareLayer[Hardware Subsystems]
        direction TB
        LS[Laser Source Nd YVO4] --> MO[Modulator AOM or EOM]
        MO --> BE[Beam Expander]
        BE --> GX[Galvo X Mirror]
        GX --> GY[Galvo Y Mirror]
        GY --> FL[F Theta Lens]
        FL --> RV[Resin Vat with PDMS Window]
        RV --> BP[Build Platform]
        RC[Recoater Blade] --> RV
        ZM[Z Elevator Motor] --> BP
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer[Control and Software]
        direction TB
        STL[STL File] --> SL[Slicer Software]
        SL --> PC[Process Controller]
        PC --> LS
        PC --> GX
        PC --> GY
        PC --> ZM
        PC --> RC
    end
```

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board exams, drawing the **arrow showing the laser path** from source → scanner → lens → vat, and **showing recoater + Z-platform** as separate sub-systems, scores full marks.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define Stereolithography and name its inventor.** [KTU University Exam — May 2023]

**Model Answer:**

Stereolithography (SLA) is a **vat photopolymerization** based additive manufacturing process that fabricates three-dimensional solid objects from a **liquid photopolymer resin** by selectively curing it **layer-by-layer** using a **focused ultraviolet (UV) laser beam**. The process was invented by **Charles W. (Chuck) Hull in 1984**, and the first commercial SLA-1 machine was released by **3D Systems Corporation in 1987**. It is the **oldest** of all commercially available AM processes and is governed by the principle of **photo-initiated chain-growth polymerization**. The ISO/ASTM 52900:2021 classification places it under the category **"Vat Photopolymerization."**

### **Question 2: State and explain the Beer-Lambert law as applied to photopolymer curing.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Model Answer:**

The Beer-Lambert law, when applied to a photopolymer resin, states that the **intensity of a UV laser beam decreases exponentially with depth** as it travels through the resin due to absorption by photoinitiator molecules.

Mathematically:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity at the surface, $I(z)$ is the intensity at depth $z$, and $D_p$ is the **penetration depth** (a material constant defined as the depth at which intensity falls to $1/e$ of $I_0$). The exponential decay is the fundamental physical basis for the **cured-depth profile** and the **Jacob's Working Curve equation** in Stereolithography.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of Stereolithography (SLA) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of each of the following components: (i) UV laser, (ii) galvanometer mirrors, (iii) recoater blade, (iv) build platform, and (v) post-cure oven. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Derive the **Jacob's Working Curve equation** for curing depth $C_d$ as a function of exposure energy $E_0$, starting from the Beer-Lambert law. A photopolymer resin has $D_p = 0.25$ mm and $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm². Determine the energy density required to achieve a curing depth of 0.5 mm. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

The working of SLA proceeds as follows (block diagram shown in Section 4.1 above):

1. The **STL file** of the CAD model is fed to the **slicer software**, which decomposes it into thin horizontal cross-sections of thickness $h$ (typically 0.05–0.1 mm).
2. The **UV laser source** (e.g., 355 nm Nd:YVO₄) emits a coherent beam, which is controlled by an **acousto-optic modulator (AOM)** for on/off switching.
3. The beam is steered by two **galvanometer mirrors** — one for the X-direction and one for the Y-direction — across the resin surface in pre-computed **scan vectors** (hatches).
4. The **recoater blade** ensures a uniform film of liquid resin over the previously cured layer.
5. The **build platform** is initially positioned one layer thickness below the resin surface and is lowered by $h$ after each layer cure via the **Z-axis elevator**.
6. The cured part is removed and placed in a **post-cure UV oven** to complete polymerization and achieve full mechanical strength.

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| UV Laser | Provides photon energy to activate photoinitiators and trigger polymerization. |
| Galvanometer Mirrors | Steer the laser beam in X and Y to trace the 2D cross-section. |
| Recoater Blade | Spreads a uniform fresh liquid layer over the cured part. |
| Build Platform | Holds the part; moves in Z to expose new layers. |
| Post-Cure Oven | Completes crosslinking for full strength; eliminates tackiness. |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Derivation:** (As derived in Section 3.1 above, starting from Beer-Lambert law $I(z) = I_0 e^{-z/D_p}$, applying polymerization threshold $E(z) = E_c$ at $z = C_d$, and solving):

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

**Numerical Computation:**

Given: $D_p = 0.25$ mm, $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm², $C_d = 0.5$ mm.

$$0.5 = 0.25 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{10}\right)$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{E_0}{10}\right) = \frac{0.5}{0.25} = 2$$

$$\frac{E_0}{10} = e^2 = 7.389$$

$$E_0 = 10 \times 7.389 = 73.89 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

**Final Answer:** $E_0 \approx 73.89$ mJ/cm²

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Compare **Stereolithography (SLA)** with **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** in terms of: (i) material form, (ii) energy source, (iii) support structures, (iv) surface finish, (v) accuracy, and (vi) typical applications. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the **"Bottom-Up" vs. "Top-Down" SLA configurations**. State **two advantages** and **two limitations** of each configuration. **[7 Marks]**

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

| Parameter | SLA | SLS |
|---|---|---|
| **Material Form** | Liquid photopolymer resin | Powder (polymer, metal, ceramic) |
| **Energy Source** | UV laser (photochemical reaction) | CO₂ / fiber laser (thermal sintering) |
| **Support Structures** | Required (built simultaneously) | Not required (unsintered powder supports) |
| **Surface Finish** | Excellent (Ra ≈ 0.4 µm) | Good, but grainy (powder particles) |
| **Accuracy** | High (± 0.1 mm) | Moderate (± 0.3 mm) |
| **Material Utilization** | Wastage of liquid resin | Unused powder can be recycled (≈ 95%) |
| **Post-Processing** | UV post-cure, support removal | Powder removal, de-powdering |
| **Typical Applications** | Dental, jewelry, investment casting patterns, prototypes | Functional plastic parts, low-volume production, lattice structures |
| **AM Category (ISO/ASTM)** | Vat Photopolymerization | Powder Bed Fusion |

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Top-Down Configuration (Classical/Traditional SLA):**
- The laser scans the **top surface** of the vat from above.
- The build platform starts at the **top** and moves **downward** as each layer cures.
- The laser beam is steered via X-Y galvanometers.

**Bottom-Up Configuration (Modern Projection/Vector 3SP):**
- The laser / projector illuminates the resin from **below**, through a transparent (PDMS or glass) window.
- The build platform starts at the **bottom** of the vat and moves **upward** after each layer.
- Cured layer peels off the transparent window.

| Feature | Top-Down | Bottom-Up |
|---|---|---|
| **Resin Volume Required** | Large (full vat filled) | Very small (only thin film needed) |
| **Material Cost** | High | Low |
| **Oxygen Inhibition Issue** | None | Present (PDMS barrier controls it) |
| **Build Speed** | Slower (one point at a time) | Faster (DLP projects whole layer) |
| **Mechanical Support** | Easier for overhangs | More complex, may need supports |
| **Vat Maintenance** | Difficult (large resin volume) | Easy (small resin volume) |
| **Component Examples** | 3D Systems SLA-250 | EnvisionTEC Perfactory, 3D Systems Figure 4 |

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting the inventor:** Many students lose 1 mark by not citing *"Charles Hull, 1984."*
> 2. **Wrong ISO category:** Writing *"Powder Bed Fusion"* instead of *"Vat Photopolymerization"* costs 1 mark.
> 3. **Sign error in ln(E/Ec):** If $E < E_c$, $C_d$ becomes negative — this is physically meaningless. KTU examiners deduct marks if the student does not state the constraint $E_0 \geq E_c$.
> 4. **Unit inconsistency in numericals:** Always keep $C_d$ and $D_p$ in **the same units** (mm or µm, not both).
> 5. **Confusing SLA with SLS:** This is the single most common error in KTU answers.
> 6. **No mention of post-curing:** SLA parts must be UV post-cured — losing this loses 1 mark.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Memory Bank for Stereolithography (Module 2):**

- ✅ **Inventor:** Charles W. Hull, **1984** (commercial 1986/1987 by 3D Systems).
- ✅ **ISO/ASTM Category:** **Vat Photopolymerization**.
- ✅ **Material form:** **Liquid photopolymer resin** (acrylates, epoxies).
- ✅ **Energy source:** **UV laser** (355 nm Nd:YVO₄ or 405 nm GaN diode).
- ✅ **Governing law:** **Beer-Lambert law** — $I(z) = I_0 e^{-z/D_p}$.
- ✅ **Jacob's Working Curve:** $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0/E_c)$ — **must know derivation**.
- ✅ **Critical energy $E_c$:** Minimum energy density required to initiate polymerization.
- ✅ **Penetration depth $D_p$:** Material constant; depth at which $I = I_0/e$.
- ✅ **Two configurations:** **Top-Down** (laser above) and **Bottom-Up** (laser below, transparent window).
- ✅ **Sub-variants:** **Vector-scan SLA** (laser) and **Mask-projection SLA / DLP** (whole layer at once).
- ✅ **Build orientation:** Parts are built with the **largest face on the platform** for stability and fastest build.
- ✅ **Support structures:** Required for overhangs > 45° in SLA (not in SLS).
- ✅ **Post-processing:** IPA wash → support removal → **UV oven post-cure**.
- ✅ **Layer thickness range:** 25 µm to 100 µm (high Z-resolution).
- ✅ **Key equation constraints:** $E_0 \geq E_c$ and $C_d > 0$ always.
- ✅ **Common applications:** Dental, jewelry, investment casting patterns, hearing aids, microfluidics, biomedical prototypes.
- ✅ **Energy density formula:** $E_0 = P / (V_s \cdot H_s)$.
- ✅ **Number of layers:** $N = H_{total} / h$.
- ✅ **SLA vs. SLS — never confuse!**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, the term **"Materials"** refers to the broad portfolio of polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, and biomaterials—supplied in specific physical forms (powders, filaments, wires, resins, sheets, pellets, or pellets/pastes)—that are selectively joined, fused, solidified, or cured layer-by-layer under computer control to form a physical 3D object. Unlike conventional subtractive manufacturing, where raw material properties must accommodate heavy machining forces, AM materials must satisfy a stricter set of constraints: **flowability, thermal behavior under localized energy input, dimensional stability, recyclability, and final part performance.**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Anchor:**
> *"AM materials are feedstock materials, in standardized physical forms (powder, wire, filament, liquid resin, sheet, pellet), specifically engineered or selected to undergo controlled phase change (melting, sintering, curing, or bonding) under localized thermal or chemical energy to enable layer-wise fabrication."*

The KTU 2024 syllabus (PEPOT634 – Module 2) explicitly demands understanding of:

1. **Types of AM materials** (polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, biomaterials).
2. **Material forms and properties** required by each AM process family.
3. **Material selection criteria** and powder characterization.
4. **Process–Material compatibility matrix**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition

Think of AM materials like the **ingredients in a specialized kitchen**:

- **Thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, Nylon)** → behave like *chocolate* — melt when heated, solidify when cooled, and can be re-melted. This is why FDM/FFF works on them.
- **Photopolymers (Resins)** → behave like *toothpaste that hardens under UV light* — liquid until sunlight (UV) hits them, then instantly they harden into a solid shape.
- **Metal powders (Ti-6Al-4V, SS 316L)** → behave like *fine sand on a beach* — the grains must flow smoothly (like dry sand through an hourglass), pack densely, and fuse only where a high-power laser/sintering beam strikes.
- **Ceramics** → behave like *clay in a kiln*, but unlike traditional pottery, AM ceramics need binders that can be removed post-printing.
- **Biomaterials (PLA, PEEK, Hydrogels)** → behave like *scaffolding that the body accepts* — they must dissolve safely or integrate with living tissue.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** Examiners love analogies because they test conceptual clarity (Bloom Level 2 – *Understand*). A student who can explain *why* a metal powder needs good flowability (using a sand-hourglass analogy) scores higher than one who merely memorizes "HR < 1.25."

---

### 1.3 Classification Snapshot (Master View)

The KTU syllabus segments AM materials into **five primary families**:

| # | Material Family | Typical AM Process | Typical Form |
|---|-----------------|--------------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Polymers** | FDM, SLA, SLS, MJ, BJ | Filament, resin, powder |
| 2 | **Metals** | SLM, DMLS, EBM, LMD, WAAM | Powder, wire |
| 3 | **Ceramics** | SLA (with binders), BJ, LOM | Paste, powder, slurry |
| 4 | **Composites** | FDM, SLA, LMD | Filament + fiber, powder + binder |
| 5 | **Biomaterials** | BJ, SLA, MEW, bioplotting | Hydrogel, powder, pellet |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Takeaway:** Every AM process is *constrained* by its feedstock. You cannot use metal wire in an FDM machine; you cannot extrude photopolymer resin with a thermal nozzle. Material–process pairing is the **#1 short-answer question (3 marks)** pattern in KTU ESE for this module.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Polymers — The Most Mature AM Material Family

Polymers dominate the AM market (~60–70% by volume of parts produced). They are subdivided based on **thermal behavior** and **cure mechanism**.

#### 2.1.1 Thermoplastics (FDM / FFF / SLS / ME)

**Definition:** Polymers that *soften on heating and harden on cooling* without any permanent chemical change. They can be re-melted (theoretically infinite recyclability).

**Key Engineering Thermoplastics for AM:**

| Polymer | Glass Transition Tg (°C) | Melting Point Tm (°C) | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Typical AM Process |
|---------|--------------------------|------------------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** | ~60 | ~150 | ~50 | FDM |
| **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** | ~105 | ~220 | ~40 | FDM |
| **Nylon 12 (PA 12)** | ~40 | ~175 | ~45 | SLS |
| **PC (Polycarbonate)** | ~145 | ~260 | ~70 | FDM |
| **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** | ~143 | ~343 | ~100 | FDM (high-temp) |
| **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** | ~-30 | ~210 (soft range) | ~50 | FDM (flexible) |

**Why these properties matter in AM:**
- **Tg** determines the *minimum* build-chamber temperature required (to prevent warping).
- **Tm** determines the *nozzle/laser* setpoint temperature.
- **Crystallinity** affects shrinkage and dimensional accuracy.

> [!NOTE]
> **PLA vs ABS — the classic KTU comparison:**
> PLA is *easier* to print (lower warping, no enclosure needed) but is *brittle* and heat-sensitive. ABS gives tougher parts but **must** be printed in an enclosed, heated chamber (>60 °C) to prevent warping and layer-splitting. KTU frequently tests this trade-off.

#### 2.1.2 Photopolymers (SLA / DLP / CDLP / MJ)

**Definition:** Liquid resins that undergo **photo-polymerization** when exposed to specific wavelengths of light (typically 405 nm UV or 385 nm UV).

**Composition of a typical photopolymer resin:**
- **Monomers + Oligomers** (40–60%) — provide the backbone (e.g., acrylates, methacrylates, epoxides).
- **Photoinitiators** (1–3%) — absorb UV light and generate free radicals.
- **Reactive diluents** (10–30%) — control viscosity.
- **Additives** (1–5%) — pigments, stabilizers, fillers, inhibitors.

**Key Properties:**

$$\text{Cure Depth } (C_d) = D_p \, \ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)$$

where:
- $D_p$ = penetration depth (material constant, μm)
- $E$ = exposure energy (mJ/cm²)
- $E_c$ = critical exposure energy (mJ/cm²)

This is the **Jacob's Working Curve Equation**, used to set laser parameters in SLA.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Know the trade-off between **cured depth** and **green strength** (the strength of the part immediately after curing, before post-curing). Under-cured parts warp; over-cured parts are brittle.

#### 2.1.3 Thermosetting Polymers (rare, but mentioned in KTU)

**Phenolic and epoxy resins** can be used in AM via extrusion or binder jetting. Once cured, they **cannot** be re-melted (cross-linked network). Used for high-temperature tooling.

---

### 2.2 Metals — The Heart of Industrial AM

Metals are supplied as **fine powders** (for powder-bed and powder-feed processes) or **wires** (for DED/WAAM).

#### 2.2.1 Common AM Metal Alloys

| Metal Alloy | Process | Tensile Strength (MPa) | Application |
|-------------|---------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Ti-6Al-4V (Titanium Grade 5)** | SLM/DMLS/EBM | ~950 | Aerospace, biomedical implants |
| **SS 316L (Stainless Steel)** | SLM/DMLS/BJ | ~530 | Marine, chemical, surgical tools |
| **Inconel 718 (Ni-based superalloy)** | SLM/EBM/LMD | ~1100 | Jet engine, gas turbine |
| **AlSi10Mg (Aluminum alloy)** | SLM/DMLS | ~350 | Automotive, aerospace (lightweight) |
| **CoCrMo (Cobalt-Chrome)** | SLM/EBM | ~1000 | Dental crowns, orthopedic implants |
| **Maraging Steel 18Ni-300** | SLM/DMLS | ~2000 (aged) | Tooling, injection molds |

#### 2.2.2 Powder Characterization (High-Yield Topic!)

Powder characteristics **govern** whether a powder-bed or powder-feed process can produce a sound part. The KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly tests **four parameters**:

1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)**
2. **Flowability**
3. **Apparent (Bulk) Density**
4. **Tap (Tapped) Density**

**Particle Size Distribution (PSD):**
Most AM metal powders fall in the range **10–45 μm** for SLM/DMLS and **45–105 μm** for EBM. PSD is measured via **laser diffraction** (Malvern Mastersizer) or **sieve analysis**.

**Flowability:**
Measured by the **Hall Flowmeter** (ASTM B213) — the time for 50 g of powder to flow through a 2.54 mm orifice. A flow time **< 30 seconds** is acceptable; **> 60 seconds** indicates poor flowability.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why flowability matters:** In SLM, the recoater blade spreads each new powder layer in **< 0.5 seconds**. If the powder sticks, voids and defects propagate.

**Apparent and Tap Density:**
- **Apparent (bulk) density ($\rho_a$)**: mass of powder freely poured into a container ÷ volume of container.
- **Tap density ($\rho_t$)**: mass of powder ÷ volume *after* controlled mechanical tapping (e.g., 100 taps in a graduated cylinder).

These two densities feed into the **Hausner Ratio (HR)** and **Carr Index (CI)** — the most-tested metrics in KTU exams:

$$HR = \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a}$$

$$CI (\%) = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100$$

**Interpretation:**

| Hausner Ratio (HR) | Carr Index (CI %) | Flow Character |
|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1.00 – 1.11 | 0 – 10 | Excellent (free flowing) |
| 1.12 – 1.18 | 11 – 15 | Good |
| 1.19 – 1.25 | 16 – 20 | Fair (may cause issues) |
| 1.26 – 1.32 | 21 – 25 | Poor (cohesive) |
| > 1.32 | > 25 | Very poor (unsuitable for AM) |

---

### 2.3 Ceramics

Ceramics are extremely hard, brittle, and have **very high melting points** (>1500 °C), making direct melting in AM challenging. The KTU syllabus discusses two strategies:

#### 2.3.1 Indirect AM (Most Common)
- A **binder** is mixed with ceramic powder (e.g., alumina + photopolymer binder).
- The green part is printed via SLA or binder jetting.
- A **debinding** step removes the binder.
- A **sintering** step (often at 1600–1800 °C) densifies the part (with ~20–25% linear shrinkage).

#### 2.3.2 Direct AM
- **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** of ceramic powder (rare, due to thermal-shock cracking).
- **Stereolithography with ceramic-filled resins** (e.g., zirconia dental crowns).

**Common AM ceramics:** Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), Silicon Carbide (SiC), Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP, bioceramic), Hydroxyapatite (HA).

---

### 2.4 Composites

Composites combine **two or more distinct material phases** to achieve superior properties.

| Composite Type | Matrix | Reinforcement | Example AM Use |
|----------------|--------|----------------|-----------------|
| **FRP** | Thermoplastic (PLA, Nylon) | Carbon fiber, glass fiber, Kevlar | FDM/FFF strong lightweight parts |
| **MMC** | Metal (Al, Ti) | SiC particles, TiB₂ | LMD aerospace blades |
| **CMC** | Ceramic (SiC, Al₂O₃) | Carbon fibers, SiC fibers | SLS high-temp aerospace |
| **PMC** | Polymer (epoxy) | Fibers, nanoparticles | SLA, FDM functional parts |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** Carbon-fiber-reinforced nylon (e.g., Markforged Onyx + CF) is a recurring exam topic because it bridges the gap between consumer FDM and industrial-grade strength.

---

### 2.5 Biomaterials

Biomaterials are used in medical AM for **scaffolds, implants, prosthetics, and tissue engineering**.

**Key Requirements:**
- **Biocompatibility** (no toxic response).
- **Biodegradability** (for scaffolds, with controllable degradation rate).
- **Sterilizability** (autoclave, gamma radiation, EtO).
- **Mechanical match** with surrounding tissue (avoid *stress shielding*).

**Common AM Biomaterials:**
- **Metallic:** Ti-6Al-4V, CoCrMo, SS 316L (load-bearing implants).
- **Polymeric:** PLA, PLGA, PCL, PEG (degradable scaffolds).
- **Ceramic:** Hydroxyapatite, β-TCP (bone scaffolds).
- **Hydrogels:** Gelatin methacrylate (GelMA), alginate (tissue printing).

---

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are exam-ready and syllabus-aligned. Memorize them with units.**

| # | Formula / Parameter | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---------------------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | Cure Depth (SLA) | $C_d = D_p \ln(E / E_c)$ | μm |
| 2 | Hausner Ratio | $HR = \rho_t / \rho_a$ | dimensionless |
| 3 | Carr Index | $CI = (\rho_t - \rho_a)/\rho_t \times 100$ | % |
| 4 | Relative Density (Packing) | $\rho_{rel} = \rho_a / \rho_{true}$ | dimensionless (0–1) |
| 5 | Layer Thickness | $t_l = k \cdot P / (v \cdot \rho)$ | μm (k = energy coefficient) |
| 6 | Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | J/mm³ |
| 7 | Specific Surface Area (sphere) | $SSA = 6 / (d \cdot \rho_s)$ | m²/kg, d = diameter |
| 8 | Sintering Shrinkage (linear) | $\Delta L / L_0 = 1 - (\rho_g / \rho_s)^{1/3}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Porosity of green part | $\phi_g = 1 - \rho_g / \rho_{true}$ | dimensionless |
| 10 | Hall Flow Rate | $t_f = t_{50g} / 50$ | s/g (lower = better) |

> **Critical Notation:**
> $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm), $\rho_g$ = green density, $\rho_s$ = sintered density, $\rho_{true}$ = theoretical (solid) density.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Hausner Ratio & Carr Index from First Principles

**Given Data:**
A powder sample of mass $m = 100 \text{ g}$ is freely poured into a 100 cm³ graduated cylinder. The observed bulk volume is $V_a = 65 \text{ cm}^3$. After 100 standardized taps, the volume reduces to $V_t = 50 \text{ cm}^3$.

**Step 1 — Calculate Apparent (Bulk) Density:**

$$\rho_a = \frac{m}{V_a} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{65 \text{ cm}^3}$$

Performing the division:

$$\rho_a = 1.5385 \text{ g/cm}^3 \approx 1.54 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Tapped Density:**

$$\rho_t = \frac{m}{V_t} = \frac{100 \text{ g}}{50 \text{ cm}^3}$$

Performing the division:

$$\rho_t = 2.000 \text{ g/cm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Compute Hausner Ratio (HR):**

$$HR = \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a} = \frac{2.000}{1.5385}$$

Dividing the two:

$$HR \approx 1.300$$

**Step 4 — Compute Carr Index (CI):**

$$CI = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100 = \frac{2.000 - 1.5385}{2.000} \times 100$$

Computing the numerator first:

$$2.000 - 1.5385 = 0.4615$$

Dividing by 2.000:

$$\frac{0.4615}{2.000} = 0.2308$$

Multiplying by 100:

$$CI = 23.08\%$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation (KTU-required conclusion):**

$$HR = 1.30 \Rightarrow \text{Poor flowability}$$
$$CI = 23.08\% \Rightarrow \text{Poor / Cohesive}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Verdict:** This powder is at the *boundary* of acceptability for SLM/DMLS. To improve flow, one may: (a) use a powder with **spherical morphology** (atomized gas), (b) **wider PSD** (e.g., bimodal), or (c) apply **dry-flow additives** (e.g., fumed silica nanoparticles).

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for SLM Process

**Background:** VED controls melt-pool size, porosity, and residual stresses in SLM. KTU asks: *"Calculate the VED for a given SLM parameter set."*

**Given Data:**
- Laser Power: $P = 200 \text{ W}$
- Scan Speed: $v = 800 \text{ mm/s}$
- Hatch Spacing: $h = 0.1 \text{ mm}$
- Layer Thickness: $t = 0.03 \text{ mm}$

**Step 1 — Write the VED formula:**

$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values with units:**

$$VED = \frac{200 \text{ W}}{800 \text{ mm/s} \cdot 0.1 \text{ mm} \cdot 0.03 \text{ mm}}$$

**Step 3 — Compute denominator step-by-step:**

$$v \cdot h = 800 \times 0.1 = 80 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$(v \cdot h) \cdot t = 80 \times 0.03 = 2.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 4 — Divide:**

$$VED = \frac{200}{2.4}$$

$$VED \approx 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**

$$83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3 \in \text{recommended range (60–100 J/mm}^3\text{) for SS 316L}$$

> This means: **the parameter set produces a fully dense part** (typical benchmark for SS 316L). Insufficient VED → lack-of-fusion porosity; excessive VED → keyhole porosity and vaporization.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Cure Depth from Jacob's Equation (SLA)

**Given Data:**
- Penetration depth: $D_p = 200 \text{ μm}$
- Critical exposure: $E_c = 8 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$
- Laser exposure: $E = 35 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$

**Step 1 — Write Jacob's equation:**

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{E}{E_c}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Compute the ratio:**

$$\frac{E}{E_c} = \frac{35}{8} = 4.375$$

**Step 3 — Compute natural log:**

$$\ln(4.375) = 1.4759$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $D_p$:**

$$C_d = 200 \times 1.4759 = 295.18 \text{ μm}$$

**Step 5 — Practical implication:**

For a layer thickness $t = 100 \text{ μm}$, the cured depth (295 μm) ensures **solid bonding to the previous layer** but excessive depth causes **over-curing** (loss of dimensional accuracy in the X-Y plane). The laser power must be tuned to keep $C_d \approx 1.5 \times t$.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process–Material Compatibility

> This is the **#1 scoring table** in KTU ESE Module 2 questions.

| Process | Required Form | Compatible Material Family | Critical Material Property |
|---------|---------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| **FDM / FFF** | Filament (1.75 / 2.85 mm) | Thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon, PEEK, TPU) | Glass transition Tg, melt flow index (MFI) |
| **SLA** | Liquid resin | Photopolymers (acrylates, epoxides) | Viscosity, photoinitiator reactivity |
| **DLP** | Liquid resin | Photopolymers | UV absorbance, cure kinetics |
| **SLS** | Powder (50–100 μm) | Thermoplastics (Nylon 12, TPU), some metals | Tg, particle shape (spherical) |
| **MJF (Multi Jet Fusion)** | Powder + fusing agent | Nylon 12, TPU | Absorption spectrum, melt viscosity |
| **SLM / DMLS** | Powder (10–45 μm) | Metals (SS 316L, Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel) | Flowability, HR, CI, thermal conductivity |
| **EBM** | Powder (45–105 μm) | Ti-6Al-4V, CoCr, Inconel | Conductivity (e-beam couples only with conductive metals) |
| **LMD / DED** | Powder (50–150 μm) or wire | Metals (Ti, steel, Inconel) | Powder flow + laser absorption |
| **WAAM** | Wire (Ø 1.0–1.2 mm) | Steels, Ti, Al | Wire feed consistency, weldability |
| **Binder Jetting** | Powder (20–60 μm) | Metals, ceramics, sand | Binder–powder interaction, spreading |
| **Material Jetting** | Liquid photopolymer droplets | Photopolymers, wax | Viscosity, surface tension |
| **LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing)** | Sheets (paper, polymer, metal foil) | Paper, polymer, metal | Adhesive bond strength |

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**List the major material families used in additive manufacturing. For each family, give one typical AM process and one representative material.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Family | Process | Example Material |
|--------|---------|------------------|
| Polymers (thermoplastics) | FDM/FFF | PLA, ABS |
| Polymers (photopolymers) | SLA/DLP | Acrylate resin |
| Metals (powder) | SLM/DMLS/EBM | Ti-6Al-4V, SS 316L |
| Metals (wire) | WAAM/DED | ER70S steel wire |

| Biomaterials | Bioplotting, SLA | PLA, PCL, Hydroxyapatite |

*(1 mark for correct classification, 1 mark for correct process pairing, 1 mark for correct material example.)*

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Hausner Ratio and explain its significance in powder-based additive manufacturing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Hausner Ratio $HR = \rho_t / \rho_a$, where $\rho_t$ = tapped density and $\rho_a$ = apparent (bulk) density.
- **Significance (2 marks):**
  - It quantifies the **flowability** of a metal or polymer powder.
  - Lower HR (close to 1.0) → free-flowing powder, suitable for recoater-based AM (SLM, EBM).
  - Higher HR (> 1.32) → cohesive powder, prone to spreading defects, voids, and inconsistent layer density.
  - In SLM/DMLS, **HR < 1.25** is generally required for defect-free parts.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Module Questions (Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**Q-A.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question adapted]`

**(a)** Explain the classification of materials used in additive manufacturing. Discuss the properties of polymers used in **FDM** and **SLA** processes with suitable examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the critical powder characteristics required for **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** of metals. Derive the formulas for **Hausner Ratio** and **Carr Index**, and explain how they relate to flowability. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

- **Classification (2 marks):** The materials used in AM are classified into five families — **polymers, metals, ceramics, composites, and biomaterials.** Each family is supplied in a specific physical form (filament, resin, powder, wire, paste, sheet) determined by the AM process used.

- **Polymers for FDM (3 marks):**
  - FDM requires **thermoplastic filaments** (typically 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter).
  - Examples: **PLA** (easy to print, low warping, Tg ~60 °C, biodegradable); **ABS** (tough, Tg ~105 °C, requires heated enclosure >60 °C to prevent warping); **Nylon (PA 12/PA 6)** (tough, slightly hygroscopic — must be dried at 80 °C for 4–6 hours before printing); **PETG** (chemical-resistant, food-safe); **PEEK** (high-performance, requires 350–400 °C nozzle + 120 °C chamber).
  - Key properties: glass transition Tg, melting point Tm, melt flow index (MFI), moisture absorption, and shrinkage rate.

- **Polymers for SLA (2 marks):**
  - SLA uses **liquid photopolymer resins** that cure under UV light (typically 405 nm).
  - Examples: Standard acrylate resins (fast-curing, brittle), tough resins (rubber-toughened), castable resins (for lost-wax casting), clear resins (optical clarity), and ceramic-filled resins (zirconia dental crowns).
  - Key properties: **viscosity (typically 250–1500 cP)**, photoinitiator concentration (1–3%), cure depth (Jacob's equation), and green strength.

---

**Model Solution (b):**

- **Critical Powder Characteristics for SLM (4 marks):**
  1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD):** SLM requires 10–45 μm for fine resolution; broader PSD helps packing but increases surface roughness.
  2. **Particle Morphology:** Must be **spherical** (gas-atomized) for good flow and high packing density. Irregular particles cause spreading defects.
  3. **Flowability:** Governed by Hall Flow rate, HR, and CI.
  4. **Apparent and Tap Density:** Indicate packing efficiency.
  5. **Chemical Purity:** Oxygen and nitrogen content must be minimized (e.g., O < 0.1 wt%) to prevent embrittlement.
  6. **Thermal Conductivity:** Affects melt pool dynamics and residual stress.

- **Hausner Ratio Derivation (1.5 marks):**
  From measurements of apparent volume $V_a$ and tapped volume $V_t$ for the same powder mass $m$:
  $$\rho_a = m / V_a, \quad \rho_t = m / V_t$$
  $$\therefore HR = \frac{\rho_t}{\rho_a} = \frac{V_a}{V_t}$$
  *Interpretation:* HR < 1.25 → free-flowing; HR > 1.32 → poor flow.

- **Carr Index Derivation (1.5 marks):**
  $$CI = \frac{\rho_t - \rho_a}{\rho_t} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{\rho_a}{\rho_t}\right) \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100$$
  *Interpretation:* CI < 10 → excellent; CI > 25 → very poor.

- **Relation to Flowability:** Lower HR and CI → denser packing, smoother recoater action, lower porosity, and consistent layer thickness — all of which reduce defects in SLM.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**Q-B.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question adapted]`

**(a)** Compare **thermoplastics** and **photopolymers** as AM feedstock materials. Discuss their **forms**, **processing requirements**, **properties**, and **typical applications**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** equation used in metal AM. A laser power of **250 W**, scan speed of **1000 mm/s**, hatch spacing of **0.12 mm**, and layer thickness of **0.03 mm** are used in an SLM process. Calculate the VED and comment on its adequacy for **SS 316L**. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

| Parameter | Thermoplastics | Photopolymers |
|-----------|----------------|---------------|
| Form | Solid filament (1.75 / 2.85 mm) | Liquid resin |
| Process | FDM/FFF, SLS | SLA, DLP, CDLP |
| Energy source | Thermal (heated nozzle) | Photonic (UV/visible light) |
| Phase change | Melt → solidify | Liquid → cross-linked solid |
| Recyclability | High (can re-melt) | Low (cured resin cannot be re-used) |
| Strength | Moderate to high (FDM parts ~40–100 MPa) | Brittle in standard grades (30–60 MPa); tough resins ~70 MPa |
| Accuracy | ±0.2 mm | ±0.05 mm (higher) |
| Applications | Functional prototypes, jigs, fixtures, end-use parts | Jewellery, dental, casting patterns, optical parts |

*(7 marks: 1 mark introduction, 3 marks comparison table, 1 mark each for examples and applications.)*

---

**Model Solution (b):**

- **VED Equation (1 mark):**

$$VED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

where $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm). Units: **J/mm³**.

- **Substitution and Step-by-Step Calculation (4 marks):**

$$P = 250 \text{ W}, \quad v = 1000 \text{ mm/s}, \quad h = 0.12 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.03 \text{ mm}$$

$$v \cdot h = 1000 \times 0.12 = 120 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s}$$

$$(v \cdot h) \cdot t = 120 \times 0.03 = 3.6 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

$$VED = \frac{250}{3.6} = 69.44 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

- **Comment on Adequacy (2 marks):**
  - The recommended VED for **SS 316L** is in the range **60–100 J/mm³**.
  - $VED = 69.44 \text{ J/mm}^3$ lies **within the recommended range**, so the parameter set is expected to produce a **fully dense (>99.5%) SS 316L part** with minimal lack-of-fusion porosity.
  - If $VED < 60 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → insufficient melting → **lack-of-fusion porosity**.
  - If $VED > 100 \text{ J/mm}^3$ → excessive energy → **keyhole porosity** and balling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students commonly LOSE marks on this topic:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HR with CI.** Many students write "$HR = (\rho_t - \rho_a) / \rho_a$" — wrong. The correct Carr Index formula uses $\rho_t$ in the denominator, not $\rho_a$.
> 2. **Forgetting units in VED.** KTU examiners allocate 1 mark for correct units (J/mm³). Students who write only the number without units lose that mark.
> 3. **Misclassifying AM processes.** Saying *"FDM uses metal powder"* or *"SLM uses filament"* is a 3-mark killer. Always verify the **feedstock form** for the given process.
> 4. **Ignoring chamber temperature for FDM.** If asked to justify "why ABS warps," you must mention the **heated chamber (60–80 °C)** requirement, not just the glass transition.
> 5. **Skipping the empirical range.** When computing VED, always cite the **recommended range** for that material. Without a comment on adequacy, the 14-mark answer is incomplete.
> 6. **Photopolymer cure depth formula.** Forgetting that $E_c$ must be in the **denominator** (i.e., $E/E_c$, not $E_c/E$) is a common error.
> 7. **One-line answers in 7-mark sub-parts.** A 7-mark sub-question requires *at least* 4–5 supporting sentences or a structured table. A single-line answer will score a maximum of 2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Last-Minute Revision — KTU Module 2 — Materials**

- ⭐ **Five material families in AM:** Polymers, Metals, Ceramics, Composites, Biomaterials.
- ⭐ **Three thermoplastic superstar materials for FDM:** PLA, ABS, Nylon (12). PEEK is high-end.
- ⭐ **Photopolymer resins** are **liquid**, cured by **UV/visible light** using a **photoinitiator**.
- ⭐ **Jacob's Equation (SLA):** $C_d = D_p \ln(E/E_c)$ — controls layer cure.
- ⭐ **Most-used AM metal powders:** SS 316L, Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg, CoCrMo.
- ⭐ **Particle size:** 10–45 μm for SLM/DMLS, 45–105 μm for EBM.
- ⭐ **Powder morphology must be spherical** (gas-atomized) for good flow.
- ⭐ **Hausner Ratio:** $HR = \rho_t / \rho_a$. **Acceptable: HR < 1.25.**
- ⭐ **Carr Index:** $CI = (\rho_t - \rho_a) / \rho_t \times 100$. **Acceptable: CI < 20%.**
- ⭐ **Volumetric Energy Density:** $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$. **Typical range for SS 316L: 60–100 J/mm³.**
- ⭐ **Ceramics in AM** need **debinding + sintering** because they cannot be melted directly without cracking.
- ⭐ **Composites** combine **matrix + reinforcement** (e.g., Nylon + Carbon Fiber).
- ⭐ **Biomaterials** must satisfy **biocompatibility, biodegradability, sterilizability, mechanical match**.
- ⭐ **FDM feedstock = filament; SLA feedstock = resin; SLM/DMLS feedstock = powder; WAAM feedstock = wire; BJ feedstock = powder + liquid binder.**
- ⭐ **EBM only works with conductive metals** (electron beam couples with the powder's electrical conductivity).
- ⭐ **Process–Material compatibility is the #1 short-answer question pattern** in KTU ESE for this module.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SL Resin Curing Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SL Resin Curing Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

**Stereolithography (SL) Resin Curing Process** is a vat-photopolymerization additive manufacturing technique in which a focused ultraviolet (UV) light beam selectively cures (hardens) a liquid photopolymer resin layer-by-layer to build a solid three-dimensional object. The fundamental curing phenomenon is a **photo-initiated free-radical chain-growth polymerization** reaction that converts liquid monomers/oligomers into a cross-linked solid polymer network.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The SL curing process is governed by the *Beer–Lambert Law of Light Absorption* in conjunction with the *critical energy threshold* for photopolymerization. The relationship between exposure energy and cure depth is the single most important equation in the entire module.

**Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary):**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Photopolymer** | A liquid resin containing monomers/oligomers that polymerize upon UV exposure. |
| **Photoinitiator** | A light-sensitive chemical that absorbs UV photons and generates free radicals. |
| **Cure Depth ($C_d$)** | The thickness of resin that solidifies per layer (typically **0.05–0.15 mm**). |
| **Penetration Depth ($D_p$)** | The depth at which light intensity falls to **1/e (≈36.8 %)** of its surface value. |
| **Critical Energy ($E_c$)** | The minimum exposure energy required to initiate gelation/cure. |

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy – "Sunlight Hardening Spilled Nail Polish":**
> Imagine spilling clear nail polish (which is essentially a UV-curable photopolymer) on a tabletop. The puddle stays liquid in the shade, but the moment direct sunlight hits it, the puddle hardens into a glossy solid within seconds. **SL resin curing is the engineered, controlled, and ultra-precise version of this exact reaction.** Instead of a chaotic sunbeam, a computer-steered UV laser scans the resin surface, drawing the cross-section of your CAD model. Each scanned pixel receives just enough UV "sunlight" to convert liquid to solid, layer by layer, until a 3D part emerges from the vat.

**Why this matters:** The nail-polish analogy captures the three essentials—**(i) liquid starting material, (ii) light as the trigger, (iii) solid output**—without which the SL process is meaningless.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Wavelength of typical UV curing laser (HeCd):** $\lambda = 325 \text{ nm}$
- **Wavelength of typical UV curing laser (solid-state):** $\lambda = 355 \text{ nm}$
- **Standard layer thickness range:** **0.025 mm to 0.200 mm**
- **Speed of light in resin (approx.):** $c_{resin} \approx 2 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$
- **Avogadro's number (used in photoinitiator calculations):** $N_A = 6.022 \times 10^{23} \text{ mol}^{-1}$

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Photopolymerization Reaction – Step-by-Step Logic

The SL curing process is fundamentally a **free-radical chain polymerization** consisting of three classical stages:

**Stage 1 – Initiation (Photochemical Trigger)**
- A UV photon of energy $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ strikes a **photoinitiator** molecule (PI).
- The photoinitiator cleaves homolytically, producing **free radicals** (R•).
- The radical attacks a monomer C=C double bond, opening it and forming a new radical chain end.

**Stage 2 – Propagation (Chain Growth)**
- The newly formed chain-end radical sequentially adds more monomer units, elongating the polymer chain.
- This continues until the chain is terminated or the UV exposure ends.

**Stage 3 – Termination (Cure Completion)**
- Two growing radical chains combine (coupling) or disproportionate, ending the chain reaction.
- The region of resin that has been sufficiently irradiated becomes a **cross-linked solid gel**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic:** If any one of these three stages fails (no photon, no photoinitiator, or no monomer), **no curing occurs**. The part is built only where the laser writes.

### 2.2 Why the Process is Layer-by-Layer

The resin is held in a vat. A build platform is positioned exactly **one layer thickness ($z$)** below the resin surface. After each layer is cured:

1. The platform lowers by $\Delta z$.
2. A recoater blade sweeps a fresh liquid layer over the previously cured surface.
3. The UV laser draws the next cross-section.
4. The new layer bonds to the previous one via **chemical inter-layer adhesion**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Name / Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$ | **Jacob's Working Curve (Beer–Lambert)** | $C_d$ in mm; $E_0, E_c$ in mJ/cm² |
| 2 | $E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w}$ | Exposure energy at resin surface | mJ/cm² |
| 3 | $E_{photon} = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | Energy of a single UV photon | Joules (J) |
| 4 | $I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$ | Light intensity vs. depth (Beer–Lambert) | mW/cm² |
| 5 | $\Delta z = C_d - C_{overcure}$ | Effective layer thickness | mm |
| 6 | $T_{layer} = \dfrac{A_{cross}}{v_{scan} \cdot w_{beam}}$ | Time to cure one layer | seconds |
| 7 | $E_c$ (typical) | Critical exposure energy for SL resins | **8–15 mJ/cm²** |
| 8 | $D_p$ (typical) | Penetration depth of UV into resin | **0.10–0.30 mm** |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Trap:** The vertical bars `\|` for absolute value are NEVER used inside a KTU formula table cell. Use $\vert$ or $\mid$ to prevent markdown breakage.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The SL curing process is the cornerstone of industries requiring **micron-level accuracy**:
- **Dental crowns and aligners** (e.g., Invisalign®) – cured in **< 60 seconds per layer**.
- **Hearing-aid shells** – Formlabs/EnvisionTEC SL lines.
- **Microfluidic chips** – for biomedical research labs.
- **Investment-casting patterns** – for aerospace turbine blades.
- **Jewellery masters** – for lost-wax casting of gold/silver.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Jacob's Cure-Depth Equation (Beer–Lambert + Threshold)

This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. The logic flows from basic optics and the existence of a minimum energy threshold.

**Step 0 – Start with the Beer–Lambert Law of Light Attenuation**
A monochromatic UV beam travelling into the resin is attenuated exponentially. The intensity at depth $z$ below the resin surface is:

$$I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

where $I_0$ is the incident intensity at the surface and $D_p$ is the penetration depth (the constant that characterizes the resin's optical absorption).

**Step 1 – Introduce the Critical Energy Threshold ($E_c$)**
Curing does **not** begin at zero exposure. A minimum energy density, the critical energy $E_c$, must be deposited. Mathematically, the condition for gelation is:

$$E(z) \ge E_c$$

**Step 2 – Express the Energy Delivered at Depth $z$**
If the beam of intensity $I$ dwells on a point for time $t$, the energy delivered is $E = I \cdot t$. The energy that reaches depth $z$ (from a beam that entered the surface with intensity $I_0$) is:

$$E(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} \cdot t$$

But $E_0 = I_0 \cdot t$ is the *surface* exposure energy. Substituting:

$$E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p}$$

**Step 3 – Apply the Curing Condition (Textual Logic)**
"Cure occurs at the deepest depth $z = C_d$ where the energy is just equal to $E_c$." Beyond that depth, the resin remains liquid.

$$E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d/D_p} = E_c$$

**Step 4 – Isolate the Exponential (Algebraic Transition)**
Divide both sides by $E_0$:

$$e^{-C_d/D_p} = \dfrac{E_c}{E_0}$$

**Step 5 – Take the Natural Logarithm of Both Sides**
Using the identity $\ln(e^x) = x$:

$$-\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_c}{E_0}\right)$$

**Step 6 – Apply $\ln(a/b) = -\ln(b/a)$ and Multiply by $D_p$**

$$\dfrac{C_d}{D_p} = \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)$$

**Step 7 – Final Canonical Form of Jacob's Working Curve**

$$\boxed{\,C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Inference for KTU:** For curing to occur at all, we **must** have $E_0 > E_c$ (because the log of a number $\le 1$ is $\le 0$, giving $C_d \le 0$, i.e., no solid resin).

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of the Exposure Energy Formula $E_0 = P/(v \cdot w)$

**Step 0 – Define each variable textually.**
- $P$ = laser power (W = J/s) at the resin surface.
- $v$ = laser scan speed (cm/s) along the path.
- $w$ = laser spot width (cm) – the diameter of the focused beam.
- $E_0$ = energy deposited per unit area (J/cm²).

**Step 1 – Energy delivered per unit time.**
A laser of power $P$ delivers $P$ joules every second. So the energy per second = $P$.

**Step 2 – Area swept per second by the moving spot.**
The beam of width $w$ sweeps a length $v$ in one second. The swept area is $v \cdot w$ (cm²/s).

**Step 3 – Energy per unit area (definition of $E_0$).**
Energy density is total energy divided by area covered:

$$E_0 = \dfrac{\text{Energy per second}}{\text{Area per second}} = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w}$$

**Step 4 – Final Expression with Units.**

$$E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w} \quad [\text{J/cm}^2]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Tip:** If asked to double the cure depth, note from Jacob's equation that $C_d \propto \ln(E_0)$, so doubling $E_0$ (e.g., by halving scan speed) does NOT double $C_d$. This logarithmic dependence is a frequent KTU Part B sub-question.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem Style)

**Problem:** An SL machine uses a resin with $D_p = 0.20$ mm and $E_c = 10$ mJ/cm². The laser power is $P = 80$ mW, scan speed $v = 50$ cm/s, and spot width $w = 0.01$ cm. Find (a) the exposure energy $E_0$, and (b) the cure depth $C_d$.

**Part (a) – Compute $E_0$:**

$$E_0 = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w} = \dfrac{80 \text{ mW}}{50 \text{ cm/s} \times 0.01 \text{ cm}} = \dfrac{80}{0.5} = 160 \text{ mJ/cm}^2$$

**Part (b) – Compute $C_d$ using Jacob's equation:**

$$C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{E_0}{E_c}\right) = 0.20 \cdot \ln\!\left(\dfrac{160}{10}\right) = 0.20 \cdot \ln(16)$$

Since $\ln(16) = 2.7726$:

$$C_d = 0.20 \times 2.7726 = 0.5545 \text{ mm}$$

**Result:** $E_0 = 160$ mJ/cm² and $C_d \approx 0.55$ mm.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Reading:** A cure depth of 0.55 mm is well above a typical 0.1 mm layer setting, which means a substantial overcure is anchoring the part to the build platform — exactly what is desired for the first few anchor layers.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Effect of Each Process Parameter

| Parameter ↑ | Effect on $E_0$ | Effect on $C_d$ | Practical Consequence |
|-------------|-----------------|-----------------|------------------------|
| Laser power $P$ ↑ | $E_0$ ↑ | $C_d$ ↑ (logarithmically) | Faster cure, but risk of over-polymerization |
| Scan speed $v$ ↑ | $E_0$ ↓ | $C_d$ ↓ | Faster builds, thinner layers |
| Spot width $w$ ↑ | $E_0$ ↓ | $C_d$ ↓ | Coarser resolution |
| Layer thickness ↑ | — | Requires $C_d$ > layer thickness | Poor inter-layer bonding if $C_d$ too small |
| Photoinitiator concentration ↑ | $E_c$ ↓ | $C_d$ ↑ at same $E_0$ | More reactive, but yellows over time |
| Resin temperature ↑ | $D_p$ ↑ slightly | $C_d$ ↑ | Lower viscosity, faster cure |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram – SL Resin Curing Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD Model STL File] --> B[Slicer Software]
    B --> C[Layer Slice Data 25 to 200 microns]
    C --> D[UV Laser Controller]
    D --> E[Galvanometer Mirrors XY Scan Head]
    E --> F[Focused UV Beam 325 to 355 nm]
    F --> G[Resin Vat Liquid Photopolymer]
    G --> H{Photoinitiator Absorbs Photon}
    H --> I[Free Radicals Generated]
    I --> J[Free Radical Chain Polymerization]
    J --> K[Cured Solid Layer C_d micrometers]
    K --> L[Build Platform Lowers by delta z]
    L --> M[Recoater Blade Sweeps Fresh Resin]
    M --> F
    K --> N[Completed 3D Part]
    N --> O[UV Post Curing Oven]
    O --> P[Final Finished Part]

    style A fill:#f9f,stroke:#333
    style K fill:#9f9,stroke:#333
    style N fill:#ff9,stroke:#333
    style P fill:#9ff,stroke:#333
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram – Photopolymerization Reaction Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Photoinitiator PI] -->|UV photon h nu| P2[Excited PI Star]
    P2 --> P3[Free Radical R dot]
    P3 -->|attacks monomer M| P4[Initiated Chain R M dot]
    P4 -->|adds more M| P5[Propagating Chain R M n dot]
    P5 -->|terminates with another R dot| P6[Dead Polymer Chain Cured Solid]

    style P1 fill:#eef
    style P2 fill:#fef
    style P3 fill:#fee
    style P6 fill:#efe
```

### 4.3 Schematic of the Cure-Depth vs Exposure-Energy "Working Curve"

```mermaid
graph LR
    X[Exposure Energy E0 mJ per cm squared] -->|ln E0| Y[Cure Depth Cd in mm]
    Y -->|Slope Dp| Z[Penetration Depth]
    Y -->|X intercept Ec| W[Critical Energy Threshold]

    style X fill:#cff
    style Y fill:#fcf
    style Z fill:#ffc
    style W fill:#cff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation of the Working Curve:**
> - **Slope of line** = $D_p$ (penetration depth of the resin).
> - **X-intercept** = $E_c$ (critical energy).
> - The line is **linear on a semi-log plot** ($C_d$ on linear axis, $E_0$ on log axis). This is a classic KTU viva question: *"Why is the working curve a straight line on a semi-log plot?"* — Answer: because the relationship is logarithmic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A – Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)
