5. **Laser Scanning:** A **CO₂ laser** (wavelength 10.6 µm) is steered by **galvanometer mirrors** and **f-theta lenses** to trace the cross-section geometry. The local powder temperature rises to the sintering point (~180 °C), and adjacent particles fuse by surface diffusion and neck growth.
6. **Platform Lowering:** The build platform moves down by one layer thickness.
7. **Recoating:** A new powder layer is spread; laser scans the next cross-section.
8. **Repetition:** The cycle repeats (typically 1000+ times for a typical part).
9. **Cooling:** After completion, the chamber cools slowly over 8–12 hours to relieve residual stresses.
10. **Depowdering:** Loose, unsintered powder is removed by brushing and vacuuming, revealing the finished part.

**Main Components of an SLS Machine (block diagram in Section 4):**
- **CO₂ Laser Source** — provides the energy to sinter
- **Galvanometer Scanner System** — directs the laser beam
- **F-Theta Lens** — focuses the beam on the powder bed
- **Recoater Blade / Roller** — spreads new powder layers
- **Build Platform** — moves vertically downward
- **Powder Feed Bed** — supplies fresh powder
- **Heated Build Chamber** — maintains pre-heat temperature
- **Temperature Control System** — monitors and regulates chamber temperature
- **Exhaust & Filtration System** — removes fumes and suspended particles
- **Control Computer & Software**

*(Working principle explanation: 4 marks, Components list with brief description: 3 marks)*

---

**(b) Process Parameters & Volumetric Energy Density (7 marks):**

**Important Process Parameters of SLS:**

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (PA12) |
|-----------|--------|----------------------|
| Laser Power | $P$ | 5–30 W |
| Scan Speed | $v$ | 1000–5000 mm/s |
| Hatch Spacing | $h$ | 0.2–0.5 mm |
| Layer Thickness | $t$ | 0.08–0.15 mm |
| Chamber Temperature | $T_c$ | 170–180 °C |
| Scan Pattern | – | Zigzag / Chessboard |
| Powder Particle Size | – | 20–80 µm |
| Atmosphere | – | Inert (N₂) to suppress oxidation |

**Volumetric Energy Density (VED):**

The Volumetric Energy Density is the most critical process parameter that governs the degree of fusion and final part properties. It is defined as:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \text{(J/mm}^3\text{)}$$

**Influence on Part Quality:**

- **Low VED (< 0.04 J/mm³):** Insufficient energy → particles do not fully sinter → **porous, weak, chalky** parts with low tensile strength and rough surface.
- **Optimum VED (0.08–0.12 J/mm³):** Particles fully sinter → **dense (> 95%), strong** parts with good surface finish and mechanical properties comparable to injection moulding.
- **High VED (> 0.15 J/mm³):** Excess energy → **polymer degradation, chain scission, yellowing, smoke, and reduced mechanical strength**.

**Example:** For $P = 12$ W, $v = 2000$ mm/s, $h = 0.3$ mm, $t = 0.1$ mm:

$$E_v = \frac{12}{2000 \times 0.3 \times 0.1} = \frac{12}{60} = 0.20 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

This is excessive — reduce power to ~5 W for optimum results.

*(Parameters list with units: 3 marks, VED formula & derivation: 2 marks, Influence on part quality with examples: 2 marks)*

---

**Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Differentiate between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the applications, advantages, and limitations of SLS. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) SLS vs. SLM (7 marks):**

| Feature | SLS | SLM |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Process Mechanism** | Sintering — particles fuse below melting point | Full melting — particles melt completely |
| **State of Fusion** | Partial / solid-state | Complete / liquid-state |
| **Materials** | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals (Ti6Al4V, SS 316L, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718) |
| **Laser Type** | CO₂ (10.6 µm) | Fiber laser (~1.06 µm) |
| **Laser Power** | Low (5–30 W) | High (200–1000 W) |
| **Supports Required** | No (powder bed self-supports) | Yes (mandatory for overhangs) |
| **Part Density** | 80–98% | > 99% (near-wrought) |
| **Mechanical Properties** | Anisotropic, lower than injection moulding | Near-isotropic, comparable to wrought |
| **Inert Gas** | Not always required | Always (Argon / Nitrogen) |
| **Applications** | Prototyping, low-volume polymer parts | Functional aerospace, medical implants |
| **Cost** | Lower (polymer powders cheaper) | Higher (metal powders expensive) |
| **Build Speed** | Moderate | Slow (high power, fine detail) |

*(Each row: 0.5 marks; minimum 12 rows for full 7 marks)*

---

**(b) Applications, Advantages, and Limitations of SLS (7 marks):**

**Applications (3 marks):**

1. **Aerospace:** Lightweight lattice structures, ducting, and topology-optimized brackets.
2. **Automotive:** Custom jigs, fixtures, ergonomic interior prototypes, low-volume parts.
3. **Medical:** Patient-specific orthotics, prosthetics, surgical planning models, and guides (using biocompatible PA12).
4. **Consumer Goods:** Customized eyewear (e.g., Carbon's M2 prints), shoe midsoles (Adidas uses SLS for lattice soles).
5. **Industrial Tooling:** Investment casting patterns (using polystyrene powder).
6. **Architecture & Education:** Conceptual models, complex geometric studies.

**Advantages (2 marks):**

1. **No support structures needed** — powder bed is self-supporting.
2. **High material utilization** — unsintered powder is reused (up to 80–90% recyclability for PA12).
3. **Good mechanical properties** — especially in tension and impact.
4. **Complex geometry possible** — including internal channels, lattices, undercuts.
5. **No post-curing required** — unlike SLA.
6. **Wide range of polymer materials** available.

**Limitations (2 marks):**

1. **Rough surface finish** (typically 6–10 µm Ra) — not as smooth as SLA.
2. **Long build times** for large parts (often 12–48 hours).
3. **Limited to polymers** (in pure SLS) — metals require SLM/DMLS.
4. **Shrinkage & warping** if chamber temperature is not well controlled.
5. **Porosity** — parts may not be fully dense (typically 95–98%).
6. **High initial machine cost** (industrial systems: ₹1.5–5 Crore).
7. **Anisotropic properties** — Z-axis strength is typically lower than X-Y.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often **forget to mention the role of the recoater roller/blade** and **pre-heating of the build chamber** — both are critical to scoring full marks in working-principle questions. Always include the **formula for VED with correct units (J/mm³)** — examiners specifically check for this in 14-mark numerical questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **SLS** = Powder Bed Fusion + **CO₂ laser** + **sintering** (not melting) of polymer powder.
- ✅ Most common material: **Polyamide 12 (PA12)**.
- ✅ Typical **layer thickness: 0.1 mm**; **laser wavelength: 10.6 µm**.
- ✅ **No support structures** are required — unfused powder provides natural support.
- ✅ **Volumetric Energy Density** formula: $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — must be in **J/mm³**.
- ✅ Optimum VED for PA12: **0.08–0.12 J/mm³** (yields density > 95%).
- ✅ Pre-heating chamber to ~170 °C (just below PA12's 176 °C melting point) reduces warping.
- ✅ Cooling time is **8–12 hours** — slow cooling is critical to prevent residual stress.
- ✅ Powder recyclability is **80–90%** — major cost advantage.
- ✅ **SLS ≠ SLM** — SLS sinters polymers; SLM fully melts metals.
- ✅ Key application areas: aerospace, automotive, medical (orthotics/prosthetics), consumer goods.
- ✅ Typical surface roughness: **6–10 µm Ra** (rougher than SLA).
- ✅ Build volumes up to **550 × 550 × 750 mm** in industrial systems (e.g., EOS P 770).
- ✅ Common OEM brands: **EOS (Germany), 3D Systems (USA), HP (Multi Jet Fusion), Farsoon, Sintratec**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Materials ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Materials in Additive Manufacturing (AM)** refer to the carefully engineered feedstocks — in the form of **powders, wires, filaments, pellets, resins, sheets, or pastes** — that are selectively consolidated, fused, cured, sintered, or polymerized by an energy source (thermal, optical, chemical, or electronic) to build a three-dimensional object layer-by-layer from a digital CAD model. According to the **ASTM F2792-12** standard (the foundational taxonomy referenced by KTU 2024 Scheme), the material's form, rheology, and thermophysical response directly govern which of the **seven AM process categories** (Vat Photopolymerization, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Binder Jetting, Material Jetting, Directed Energy Deposition, Sheet Lamination) can be employed. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cook's Pantry" Intuition Imagine AM as **advanced precision cooking**: - **The Printer** is the *stove / oven*. - **The Energy Source** (laser, electron beam, UV light, extruder heater) is the *fire / heat*. - **The Material** is the *ingredient* (flour, sugar, dough). Just as a master chef cannot bake a soufflé with sand, an engineer cannot print a load-bearing titanium hip implant with chocolate-grade PLA filament. The **material dictates the process**, the **process dictates the parameters**, and the **parameters dictate the final part properties**. Selecting the wrong "ingredient" leads to porosity, delamination, residual stress, or outright process failure. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2):** Students must understand the taxonomy of materials across polymer, metal, ceramic, composite, and bio-material families — and critically — the **material-process-property interdependency** that governs AM part qualification. ### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Laser Power | $P$ | **Watts (W)** | | Scan Speed | $v$ | **mm/s** | | Hatch Spacing | $h$ | **µm or mm** | | Layer Thickness | $t$ | **µm or mm** | | Powder Particle Size | $d$ | **10 – 100 µm typical** | | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v$ | **J/mm³** | | Powder Tap Density | $\rho_{tap}$ | **g/cm³** | | Hausner Ratio | $HR$ | **Dimensionless** | --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of AM Materials (Theory) Additive manufacturing materials can be classified along **three orthogonal axes**: *material family*, *physical form*, and *process compatibility*. #### **Axis A — By Material Family** 1. **Polymeric Materials** (~70% of current AM market by volume) - **Thermoplastics**: Extrudable, re-meltable (e.g., PLA, ABS, PA12, PEEK, ULTEM). - **Thermosets**: Photo-curable resins (e.g., acrylates, epoxies, vinyl esters). - **Elastomers**: TPE, TPU (flexible filaments/resins). 2. **Metallic Materials** (highest industrial value) - **Powder form**: Stainless steel 316L, Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, Inconel 718/625, CoCrMo. - **Wire form**: Ti-6Al-4V, stainless steel, aluminum alloys (for DED / WAAM). - **Granule / Pellet form**: Bound metal deposition (markforged). 3. **Ceramic Materials** - **Oxides**: Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiO₂. - **Non-oxides**: SiC, Si₃N₄, BN. - **Binders**: Used in binder jetting + sintering route. 4. **Composite Materials** - **Fiber-reinforced**: Carbon-fiber + nylon/PEEK (FDM pellets), chopped CF/PLA. - **Particulate-reinforced**: Metal Matrix Composites (MMC), Ceramic Matrix Composites (CMC). - **Nanocomposites**: Graphene, CNT, nano-clay reinforced polymers. 5. **Biomaterials** (medical & tissue engineering) - **Bio-inert**: Ti-6Al-4V, CoCrMo, PEEK. - **Bio-active**: Hydroxyapatite (HA), Bioglass, tricalcium phosphate (TCP). - **Bio-degradable**: PLA, PLGA, PCL, PLLA. 6. **Smart / Functional Materials** - Shape memory alloys (Nitinol), piezoelectric polymers, conductive inks (graphene, silver). #### **Axis B — By Physical Form** - **Powder** → PBF, DED, Binder Jetting - **Filament** (1.75 mm / 2.85 mm) → FDM/FFF - **Pellet / Granule** → Large-format extrusion, screw-based systems - **Liquid Resin** → SLA, DLP, MJ - **Wire** (Ø 0.8 – 1.6 mm) → DED, WAAM, EBF³ - **Sheet / Tape** → LOM, ultrasonic consolidation - **Paste / Slurry** → Robocasting, DIW (Direct Ink Writing) #### **Axis C — By Process Compatibility** | Process (ISO/ASTM) | Preferred Material Form | Material Family Examples | |---|---|---| | Vat Photopolymerization (SLA, DLP) | Liquid photopolymer resin | Acrylates, epoxies | | Material Extrusion (FDM, FFF) | Thermoplastic filament | PLA, ABS, TPU, PEEK | | Powder Bed Fusion (SLM, DMLS, EBM) | Metal powder (10–45 µm) | Ti6Al4V, 316L, Inconel | | Directed Energy Deposition (DED, LMD) | Powder or wire | Ti, Ni-superalloys, CoCr | | Binder Jetting | Powder + liquid binder | Sand, metal, ceramic | | Material Jetting | Photopolymer droplets + wax | Multi-material resins | | Sheet Lamination (LOM, UC) | Foil / sheet | Paper, metal tapes | ### 2.2 The 'Why' Behind Material-Process Selection > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** The *fundamental property* dictating compatibility is the material's **thermal response to the energy source** — specifically its *melt temperature*, *glass transition temperature (Tg)*, *crystallinity*, *optical absorption*, and *thermal conductivity*. - **For polymers in FDM**: $T_{nozzle} > T_m$ (semi-crystalline) or $T_{nozzle} > T_g$ (amorphous). - **For metals in PBF**: Powder must **absorb laser/electron energy efficiently**; melting point must be reachable by the source (e.g., EBM requires conductive powders; SLM requires good laser coupling). - **For resins in SLA**: Photopolymer must absorb UV light at the source wavelength (typically **355 nm** for Nd:YAG tripled or **405 nm** for low-cost DLP), and exhibit **critical cure depth** $D_c$ and **penetration depth** $D_p$ per the **Jacobs working curve**. ### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table summarizes **all high-yield formulas** for KTU Module 2 (Materials). Use $\vert$ instead of $\vert$ to avoid markdown table breakage. | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric Energy Density for laser-based PBF/DED | J/mm³ | | 2 | $HR = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{bulk}}$ | Hausner Ratio (powder flowability index) | dimensionless | | 3 | $CI = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$ | Carr Index (Compressibility) | % | | 4 | $SSA = \dfrac{6}{d \cdot \rho_p}$ | Specific Surface Area of spherical powder | m²/kg | | 5 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{true}}$ | Relative (packing) density | dimensionless | | 6 | $\rho_{packed} = \phi \cdot \rho_{true}$ | Packing density (φ = packing factor) | g/cm³ | | 7 | $C_d = D_p \ln\left(\dfrac{E_{max}}{E_c}\right)$ | Jacobs Cure Depth Equation (resin) | mm | | 8 | $\rho_{part} = (1-\Pi) \cdot \rho_{true}$ | Part density with porosity Π | g/cm³ | | 9 | $D_{50}$ | Median powder particle diameter | µm | | 10 | $A_p = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4}$ | Cross-sectional area of spherical powder particle | mm² | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility & Industrial Relevance - **Aerospace**: Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718 enable **lightweighting** (topology-optimized brackets, turbine blades with internal cooling channels). - **Biomedical**: Patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V cranial implants and CoCrMo dental crowns are produced via SLM with **±0.05 mm geometric fidelity**. - **Automotive**: Polymer composite AM enables rapid tooling, jigs, fixtures, and **end-use parts** (e.g., Bugatti's 3D-printed brake caliper). - **Tooling**: Binder-jetted sand molds and printed conformal cooling inserts drastically reduce injection-molding cycle times. - **Electronics**: Conductive filaments enable embedded circuits; ceramic AM enables RF components. --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) The **Volumetric Energy Density** is the single most important KTU-tractable parameter in laser-based AM. It quantifies the energy delivered per unit volume of consolidated material. **Step 1 — Identify the geometric volume melted per unit time.** The laser sweeps across a single track of width $h$ (hatch spacing), length $v \cdot \Delta t$ in time $\Delta t$, and depth $t$ (layer thickness). The volume melted per unit time is: $$ V_{melt} = v \cdot h \cdot t \quad \text{[mm}^3\text{/s]} $$ **Step 2 — Identify the energy delivered per unit time.** Power $P$ is energy per unit time: $$ E_{in} = P \cdot \Delta t \quad \text{[J]} $$ **Step 3 — Divide energy by volume to obtain volumetric energy density.** $$ E_v = \frac{E_{in}}{V_{melt}} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot \Delta t} $$ **Step 4 — Cancel $\Delta t$ to obtain the closed-form expression.** $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ The units are $\dfrac{\text{W}}{\text{mm/s} \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$. > [!NOTE] > **Energy Density Window for Ti-6Al-4V (SLM):** Typically $60 \leq E_v \leq 120 \text{ J/mm}^3$. Below this window → lack of fusion porosity. Above → keyhole porosity and vaporization. **This is the #1 KTU Module-2 exam calculation.** --- ### 3.2 Worked Example — Energy Density Calculation **Problem (KTU-style):** In an SLM process for 316L stainless steel, the laser power is set to $P = 200 \text{ W}$, scan speed $v = 800 \text{ mm/s}$, hatch spacing $h = 0.12 \text{ mm}$, and layer thickness $t = 0.03 \text{ mm}$. Determine the volumetric energy density. Will the part likely suffer from lack-of-fusion or keyholing? **Step 1 — Substitute the given values into the VED formula.** $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03} $$ **Step 2 — Compute the denominator.** $$ 800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03 = 800 \times 0.0036 = 2.88 $$ **Step 3 — Compute the final VED value.** $$ E_v = \frac{200}{2.88} = 69.44 \text{ J/mm}^3 $$ **Step 4 — Compare with the empirical processing window for 316L SS.** The empirical "safe" window for 316L SS via SLM is approximately $60 \leq E_v \leq 110 \text{ J/mm}^3$. Since $69.44 \text{ J/mm}^3$ falls **within this window**, the part is expected to be **near the lower bound** — slightly toward lack-of-fusion porosity. Recommend marginal increase in $P$ to $\approx 230 \text{ W}$ to reach $\approx 80 \text{ J/mm}^3$ for optimal density. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of Powder Flowability Indices (Hausner Ratio & Carr Index) **Step 1 — Define bulk (apparent) density $\rho_{bulk}$.** The freely-poured powder mass $m$ occupying volume $V_{bulk}$: $$ \rho_{bulk} = \frac{m}{V_{bulk}} $$ **Step 2 — Define tap density $\rho_{tap}$.** After controlled mechanical tapping, the powder compacts to volume $V_{tap} < V_{bulk}$: $$ \rho_{tap} = \frac{m}{V_{tap}} $$ **Step 3 — Hausner Ratio (HR).** $$ HR = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{bulk}} = \frac{V_{bulk}}{V_{tap}} $$ **Step 4 — Carr Index (Compressibility, CI).** $$ CI = \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100 = \left(1 - \frac{1}{HR}\right) \times 100 $$ **Interpretation (ASTM B213 / B527 standards):** | HR Range | CI (%) | Flow Character | |---|---|---| | 1.00 – 1.11 | ≤ 10 | Excellent | | 1.12 – 1.18 | 11 – 15 | Good | | 1.19 – 1.25 | 16 – 20 | Fair | | 1.26 – 1.32 | 21 – 25 | Passable | | 1.33 – 1.45 | 26 – 31 | Poor | | > 1.46 | > 32 | Very Poor | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Pitfall:** Many students incorrectly state HR = $\rho_{bulk}/\rho_{tap}$. The **correct** formula is HR = $\rho_{tap}/\rho_{bulk}$ — a higher HR means **worse flow** because the powder compacts dramatically on tapping. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Specific Surface Area of a Powder Particle For a **single spherical particle** of diameter $d$ and density $\rho_p$: **Step 1 — Surface area of one particle.** $$ A_{particle} = \pi d^2 $$ **Step 2 — Volume of one particle.** $$ V_{particle} = \frac{\pi d^3}{6} $$ **Step 3 — Mass of one particle.** $$ m_{particle} = \rho_p \cdot V_{particle} = \frac{\pi d^3 \rho_p}{6} $$ **Step 4 — Specific surface area (SSA) per unit mass.** $$ SSA = \frac{A_{particle}}{m_{particle}} = \frac{\pi d^2}{\frac{\pi d^3 \rho_p}{6}} = \frac{6}{d \cdot \rho_p} $$ > **Higher SSA → higher reactivity (oxidation, sintering kinetics), higher moisture absorption, lower flowability.** Fine powders (<20 µm) have very high SSA and require inert handling. --- ### 3.5 Jacobs Cure Depth Equation (for Vat Photopolymerization) **Step 1 — Beer-Lambert law for light attenuation in resin.** The light intensity at depth $z$ below the surface: $$ E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} $$ where $D_p$ is the **penetration depth** (resin property) and $E_0$ is the surface exposure. **Step 2 — Curing occurs only where $E(z) \geq E_c$ (critical exposure).** **Step 3 — Solve for cure depth $C_d$:** $$ C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_{max}}{E_c}\right) $$ This is the **Jacobs working curve** — the analytical foundation of SLA / DLP printability. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — AM Material Taxonomy ```mermaid flowchart TD A[AM MATERIALS]:::root --> B[POLYMERS]:::fam A --> C[METALS]:::fam A --> D[CERAMICS]:::fam A --> E[COMPOSITES]:::fam A --> F[BIOMATERIALS]:::fam A --> G[SMART MATERIALS]:::fam B --> B1[Thermoplastics
PLA, ABS, PA12, PEEK] B --> B2[Thermosets
UV Resin Acrylates] B --> B3[Elastomers
TPU, TPE] C --> C1[Steel Alloys
316L, 17-4PH] C --> C2[Titanium
Ti6Al4V, CP-Ti] C --> C3[Aluminum
AlSi10Mg, 6061] C --> C4[Nickel Superalloys
Inconel 718, 625] C --> C5[Cobalt Chrome
CoCrMo] D --> D1[Oxides
Al2O3, ZrO2] D --> D2[Non-Oxides
SiC, Si3N4] D --> D3[Phosphates
HA, TCP] E --> E1[FRP
CF-PEEK, CF-Nylon] E --> E2[MMC
AlSi10Mg + SiC] E --> E3[Nanocomposite
Graphene, CNT] F --> F1[Bio-inert
Ti, CoCr, PEEK] F --> F2[Bio-active
HA, Bioglass] F --> F3[Bio-resorbable
PLA, PLGA, PCL] G --> G1[Shape Memory
Nitinol] G --> G2[Conductive
Graphene Inks] G --> G3[Piezoelectric
PVDF] classDef root fill:#1a1a2e,stroke:#f0a500,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:3px classDef fam fill:#16213e,stroke:#e94560,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.2 Process-Material Compatibility Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Vat Photo
polymerization]:::p --> R1[Photopolymer
Resin]:::m P2[Material
Extrusion]:::p --> R2[Thermoplastic
Filament]:::m P3[Powder Bed
Fusion]:::p --> R3[Metal Powder
10 to 45 um]:::m P4[Directed Energy
Deposition]:::p --> R4[Metal Powder
or Wire]:::m P5[Binder
Jetting]:::p --> R5[Sand Metal or
Ceramic Powder]:::m P6[Material
Jetting]:::p --> R6[Photopolymer
Droplets]:::m P7[Sheet
Lamination]:::p --> R7[Paper Foil
or Metal Tape]:::m R1 --> O1[High Resolution
Prototypes]:::o R2 --> O2[Functional Parts
and Tools]:::o R3 --> O3[Aerospace
Biomedical]:::o R4 --> O4[Repair Large
Structures]:::o R5 --> O5[Molds Casting
Patterns]:::o R6 --> O6[Multi material
Models]:::o R7 --> O7[Paper Prototypes
Laminated Metal]:::o classDef p fill:#0f3460,stroke:#16c79a,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef m fill:#533483,stroke:#f9b208,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef o fill:#1b4332,stroke:#d4a373,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` ### 4.3 Material Property Decision Matrix (Schematic Decision Tree) ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{Required
Strength}:::d -->|High| Q2{Operating
Temperature}:::d Q1 -->|Low to Medium| P1[Polymer Family
PLA ABS TPU]:::res Q2 -->|>300 C| M1[Ni Superalloys
Inconel CoCrMo]:::res Q2 -->|150 to 300 C| M2[Ti Alloys
PEEK ULTEM]:::res Q2 -->|<150 C| M3[Al Alloys
316L Steel]:::res Q3{Biological
Contact?}:::d -->|Yes| B1[Bio compatible
Ti6Al4V PEEK HA]:::res Q3 -->|No| M3 Q4{Wear
Resistance?}:::d -->|Yes| C1[Ceramics
Al2O3 ZrO2]:::res Q4 -->|No| M2 classDef d fill:#2d3047,stroke:#e63946,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px classDef res fill:#1d3557,stroke:#a8dadc,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q1.** Differentiate between **thermoplastic** and **thermoset** polymers with respect to their AM processability and recyclability. **Model Answer:** - **Thermoplastics**: Linear/branched chains, soften on heating (reversible), **remeltable and recyclable**. Examples: PLA, ABS, PA12, PEEK. Used in **FDM/FFF** (extrusion-based). No chemical crosslinking during printing. - **Thermosets**: 3-D crosslinked network, undergo **irreversible curing** under UV/heat. Cannot be remelted. Examples: acrylate, epoxy, vinyl ester resins. Used in **SLA/DLP/Material Jetting**. Recyclability is **poor** — they degrade rather than remelt. - AM Implication: Thermoplastics enable **multi-cycle waste reuse** (support material, failed prints), whereas thermosets are single-use but offer superior **chemical resistance and thermal stability** in the finished part. --- > **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **Q2.** What is the **Hausner Ratio**? State the threshold value above which a metal powder is considered to have poor flowability for PBF processes. **Model Answer:** The Hausner Ratio (HR) is a dimensionless index of powder flowability, defined as: $$ HR = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{bulk}} $$ It quantifies the **compressibility** of a powder bed. A higher HR indicates greater inter-particle friction and poorer flow. **KTU Threshold (ASTM-referenced):** - $HR \leq 1.25$ → Acceptable for PBF. - $HR > 1.25$ → Poor flow, risk of uneven layer spreading, voids, and recoater streaking. --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) > **[KTU ESE Model Question — Internal Choice Pattern]** --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** **(a) [7 Marks]** Classify the various **metallic materials** used in additive manufacturing. Discuss the typical powder characteristics (size, morphology, flowability) required for the Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) process. **(b) [7 Marks]** A Selective Laser Melting (SLM) machine processes Ti-6Al-4V powder with the following parameters: Laser power $P = 280$ W, scan speed $v = 1200$ mm/s, hatch spacing $h = 0.10$ mm, and layer thickness $t = 0.030$ mm. Calculate the volumetric energy density and comment on the expected defect type. --- **Model Solution — Q.A(a):** **Classification of Metallic AM Materials:** | Category | Alloys | Typical AM Use | |---|---|---| | **Steel Alloys** | 316L, 17-4PH, Maraging 300, H13 | Marine, tooling, molds | | **Titanium Alloys** | Ti-6Al-4V, CP-Ti, Ti-5553 | Aerospace (lightweight), biomedical implants | | **Aluminum Alloys** | AlSi10Mg, AlSi12, Scalmalloy | Automotive, aerospace brackets | | **Nickel Superalloys** | Inconel 718, Inconel 625, Hastelloy X | Turbine blades, high-temperature service | | **Cobalt-Chrome** | CoCrMo, ASTM F75 | Dental, orthopedic implants | | **Refractory** | Tungsten, Tantalum | Radiation shielding, defense | | **Precious** | Gold, Silver, Platinum | Jewelry, dental, electronics | **Required Powder Characteristics for PBF:** 1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)**: Typically **15–45 µm** for SLM; **45–105 µm** for EBM. A Gaussian or near-Gaussian distribution is preferred. **D50** (median) is the most-reported metric. 2. **Particle Morphology**: Predominantly **spherical** (gas-atomized) — ensures good flowability and high packing density. Irregular/elongated particles cause recoater jams. 3. **Flowability**: HR ≤ 1.25, Carr Index ≤ 20% (preferably ≤ 10%). 4. **Apparent/Tap Density**: High $\rho_{tap}$ (≥ 50% of true density) ensures dense packing and minimum porosity. 5. **Chemistry/Purity**: Low oxygen (< 0.1 wt% for Ti), low nitrogen; controlled residual elements (e.g., Al, V content in Ti-6Al-4V). 6. **Internal Porosity**: Hollow particles trap gas → defects. Plasma-atomized or PREP (Plasma Rotating Electrode Process) powders have near-zero internal porosity. 7. **Moisture Content**: Must be < 0.05% — moisture causes porosity and hydrogen embrittlement. --- **Model Solution — Q.A(b):** **Step 1 — Identify the formula.** $$ E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$ **Step 2 — Substitute values.** $$ E_v = \frac{280}{1200 \times 0.10 \times 0.030} $$ **Step 3 — Compute denominator.** $$ 1200 \times 0.10 \times 0.030 = 1200 \times 0.003 = 3.6 $$ **Step 4 — Compute $E_v$.** $$ E_v = \frac{280}{3.6} = 77.78 \text{ J/mm}^3 $$ **Step 5 — Compare with Ti-6Al-4V processing window.** The empirical "safe" window for SLM of Ti-6Al-4V is **$60 \leq E_v \leq 110 \text{ J/mm}^3$**. Since **77.78 J/mm³ lies comfortably within this window**, the part is expected to achieve **> 99.5% relative density** with minimal defect formation. The energy density is biased toward the **optimal mid-range**, balancing melt-pool stability against keyhole formation. **Defect Commentary:** - Not low enough to cause **lack-of-fusion porosity**. - Not high enough to cause **keyhole porosity or balling**. - **Conclusion**: Optimum energy density. Part likely meets aerospace-grade density (> 99.5%). --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various **polymeric materials** used in additive manufacturing. Compare the properties and processing routes of **PLA, ABS, PA12, and PEEK** filaments used in FDM. **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **biomaterials** used in additive manufacturing. Discuss the requirements for a material to be used as a **patient-specific implant** (cranial plate, dental crown) and explain why Ti-6Al-4V and CoCrMo are preferred. --- **Model Solution — Q.B(a):** **Polymeric AM Materials — Overview:** Additive manufacturing uses polymers in three principal forms: **thermoplastic filaments** (FDM/FFF), **photopolymer resins** (SLA/DLP/MJ), and **thermoplastic pellets/granules** (large-format extrusion, pellet FFF). **Comparative Table — Common FDM Filaments:** | Property | PLA | ABS | PA12 (Nylon) | PEEK | |---|---|---|---|---| | **Tensile Strength (MPa)** | 50–60 | 30–45 | 45–55 | 90–100 | | **Glass Transition (Tg)** | 60 °C | 105 °C | 50 °C | 143 °C | | **Melting Point (Tm)** | 150–160 °C (amorphous region) | Amorphous | 180 °C | 343 °C | | **Nozzle Temp.** | 190–220 °C | 230–260 °C | 240–270 °C | 380–420 °C | | **Bed Temp.** | 50–60 °C | 95–110 °C | 80–100 °C | 120–160 °C | | **FDM Processability** | Excellent (low warp) | Moderate (warps — needs enclosure) | Excellent (tough, slightly hygroscopic) | Difficult (high-temp, expensive machine) | | **Drying Required** | No | Yes | **Yes (critical)** | **Yes (mandatory)** | | **Key Use** | Prototyping, hobby | Functional prototypes, enclosures | End-use parts, jigs | Aerospace, medical implants | **Processing Routes:** - **PLA (Polylactic Acid)**: Biodegradable; low shrinkage; ideal for beginners; derived from corn-starch. - **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)**: Petroleum-based; tougher than PLA; requires heated bed + enclosed chamber to avoid warping. - **PA12 (Nylon 12)**: Semi-crystalline; absorbs moisture rapidly → must be dried at 80 °C for 4–6 h before printing. Excellent chemical resistance and fatigue life. - **PEEK (Polyether Ether Ketone)**: High-performance thermoplastic; requires **specialized industrial FDM machines** with heated chamber up to 250 °C; used in aerospace (bracket, clamp) and implantable medical devices (cranial, spinal). --- **Model Solution — Q.B(b):** **Biomaterials in AM:** Biomaterials are classified by their **host response**: 1. **Bio-inert** (no host reaction): Ti-6Al-4V, CoCrMo, PEEK, alumina, zirconia. Used for **load-bearing** implants. 2. **Bio-active** (bonds with bone): Hydroxyapatite (HA), Bioglass®, tricalcium phosphate (TCP). Used for **bone scaffolds**. 3. **Bio-resorbable** (dissolves over time): PLA, PLGA, PCL, PLLA. Used for **tissue engineering scaffolds** and **temporary fixation devices**. **Requirements for Patient-Specific Implants (e.g., Cranial Plate, Dental Crown):** 1. **Biocompatibility** (ISO 10993): No cytotoxicity, no sensitization, no irritation. 2. **Osseointegration capability** (for bone-contacting implants). 3. **Mechanical adequacy**: Modulus close to bone to avoid stress-shielding (CoCrMo ~210 GPa, Ti-6Al-4V ~110 GPa, cortical bone ~15–30 GPa). 4. **Corrosion resistance** in physiological saline (37 °C, pH 7.4, Cl⁻ environment). 5. **Fatigue endurance** under cyclic physiological loads. 6. **Custom geometry** from CT/MRI scan — enabled uniquely by AM. 7. **Sterilizability** (autoclave, gamma, EtO). **Why Ti-6Al-4V and CoCrMo Are Preferred:** - **Ti-6Al-4V**: Excellent strength-to-weight ratio (~900 MPa UTS), passive TiO₂ layer confers outstanding corrosion resistance, biocompatible, MRI-compatible (non-ferromagnetic), and processable via SLM/EBM with > 99.5% density. Used in cranial plates, spinal cages, femoral stems. - **CoCrMo**: Superior wear resistance (used in articulating joint surfaces), higher modulus (~210 GPa) for rigid dental frameworks, excellent fatigue strength, biostable. Used in dental crowns/bridges and hip femoral heads. Both are processable by SLM with controlled porosity (in bone-contact regions) to promote **osseointegration**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Module 2 (Materials):** > 1. **Hausner Ratio inversion error**: Students often write $HR = \rho_{bulk}/\rho_{tap}$ — the correct formula is the **inverse**. Marks deducted: 1 mark. > 2. **Confusing $E_v$ with $E_{surface}$**: KTU expects **volumetric** energy density in J/mm³, not surface energy density in J/mm². > 3. **Forgetting units in VED calculation**: The denominator must yield mm³/s; ensure $v$ is in **mm/s**, not m/s. > 4. **Mixing up bioresorbable vs. bioinert**: PLA is **bioresorbable** (not bio-inert). PEEK is **bio-inert** (not resorbable). > 5. **Citing "PEEK is a metal" or "PLA is a thermoset"**: These are catastrophic conceptual errors in Part A. > 6. **Forgetting drying of hygroscopic filaments**: PA12, PEEK, and PC **must** be dried before FDM to avoid stringing, voids, and hydrolysis. Examiners test this routinely. --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 2: Materials** - [ ] AM materials exist in **7 forms**: powder, filament, pellet, resin, wire, sheet, paste. - [ ] **ASTM F2792** defines the 7 process categories; material-process compatibility is non-negotiable. - [ ] **Three dominant polymer families**: Thermoplastic (FDM), Thermoset (SLA/DLP), Elastomer (TPU). - [ ] **Five dominant metal families**: Steel, Ti, Al, Ni-superalloys, CoCr. - [ ] **Ceramics require binder** route: binder jetting + sintering (cannot be laser-melted directly due to cracking). - [ ] **Composite AM**: Fiber-reinforced (FDM pellets), MMC (metal+ceramic), nanocomposites (CNT/graphene). - [ ] **Biomaterial triad**: Bio-inert / Bio-active / Bio-resorbable. - [ ] **Volumetric Energy Density** $E_v = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ is the **#1 KTU calculation**. - [ ] **Safe VED window for Ti-6Al-4V (SLM)**: **60–110 J/mm³**. - [ ] **Hausner Ratio** $HR = \rho_{tap}/\rho_{bulk}$; threshold for PBF: **HR ≤ 1.25**. - [ ] **Carr Index**: $CI = (1 - 1/HR) \times 100$; < 15% is good. - [ ] **Specific Surface Area**: $SSA = 6/(d \cdot \rho_p)$ — fine powders oxidize faster. - [ ] **Gas-atomized spherical powders** are preferred for PBF (vs. water-atomized irregular). - [ ] **PSD range for SLM**: 15–45 µm; **for EBM**: 45–105 µm. - [ ] **PEEK** requires 380–420 °C nozzle + 250 °C chamber → industrial-grade FDM only. - [ ] **PA12** is highly hygroscopic → mandatory drying at 80 °C for 4–6 h. - [ ] **Ti-6Al-4V** is the gold standard for biomedical AM (cranial, spinal, dental). - [ ] **Inconel 718/625** dominate high-temperature aerospace AM (turbine, combustor). - [ ] **Jacobs cure depth**: $C_d = D_p \ln(E_{max}/E_c)$ — governs SLA/DLP printability. - [ ] **Lack-of-fusion** = low $E_v$; **Keyhole** = high $E_v$. - [ ] **Smart materials**: Nitinol (shape memory), PVDF (piezo), graphene inks (conductive). - [ ] Material **oxygen content** must be **< 0.1 wt%** for reactive alloys (Ti, Al). - [ ] **Carbon-fiber reinforced filaments** are abrasive → require **hardened steel or ruby nozzles**. ---

topics.title: Powder Fusion Mechanism
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Powder Fusion Mechanism

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Powder Fusion Mechanism** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the controlled thermo-physical process by which discrete powder particles (metallic, polymeric, ceramic, or composite) are selectively consolidated into a solid, fully-dense (or near-fully-dense) layer using a directed, high-intensity energy source. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEPOT634)** syllabus, the powder fusion mechanism is the foundational sub-process of Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Directed Energy Deposition (DED) systems, where thermal energy delivered by a laser or electron beam induces one of three primary physical transitions: **solid-state sintering**, **partial melting with liquid-phase sintering**, or **complete melting and re-solidification (fusion welding)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Powder fusion is NOT a single phenomenon. The examiner expects students to differentiate between **Sintering**, **Partial Melting**, and **Full Melting** mechanisms, and to map each to its corresponding AM technology (SLS, DMLS, SLM, EBM).

The three canonical powder fusion routes recognized in the ISO/ASTM 52900 standard and endorsed by the KTU 2024 curriculum are:

1. **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** — Polymer or metal powder heated below the melting point; particles bond via solid-state diffusion (no liquid phase).
2. **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) / Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** — Metal powder completely melted by a high-power laser to form a fully dense weld pool.
3. **Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** — Metal powder fused in a high-vacuum environment using a focused electron beam, characterized by elevated chamber temperatures (~700 °C) that reduce thermal gradients.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Snow-to-Iceberg Analogy":**
> Imagine a snowy mountain peak during spring. When a focused sunbeam (laser) strikes a small patch of snow (powder bed), three things can happen:
>
> - **Mild sunlight** → snow grains stick together loosely but stay individual crystals → this is **Sintering** (SLS).
> - **Moderate sunbeam** → surface snow melts and water trickles down, refreezing grains together → this is **Partial Melting** (DMLS / Liquid Phase Sintering).
> - **Intense concentrated laser** → the entire snow patch instantly melts into a puddle of water that refreezes as one solid block of clear ice → this is **Full Melting / Fusion** (SLM).
>
> The powder fusion mechanism in AM is essentially an engineer controlling the *intensity of a tiny artificial sun* sweeping across a bed of metallic "snow" to build a 3D object layer by layer.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics

The following metrics are routinely used in KTU board examinations to characterize powder fusion:

- **Laser Power (P)**: Typically **200 W to 1 kW** for industrial SLM systems.
- **Scan Speed (v)**: **100 mm/s to 10,000 mm/s** depending on the technology.
- **Hatch Spacing (h)**: **50 µm to 200 µm**.
- **Layer Thickness (t)**: **20 µm to 100 µm**.
- **Spot Diameter**: **50 µm to 500 µm** depending on fiber laser optics.
- **Absorptivity (A) of metal powder to Nd:YAG laser (1.06 µm)**: **0.30 to 0.70** (significantly higher than solid bulk metal due to multiple reflection paths).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Sequential Sub-Mechanisms of Powder Fusion

Every powder fusion event in PBF/DED occurs as a tightly coupled sequence of four physical phenomena. The KTU examiner frequently tests the student's ability to enumerate and explain these in correct order.

### Sub-Mechanism 1 — Powder–Energy Coupling (Absorption)

When the laser (or electron beam) strikes the powder bed, the photon/electron energy is **absorbed** by the top few particle layers. The efficiency of this step is governed by the **Absorptivity (A)** of the powder, which depends on:

- Powder material's intrinsic optical/electrical properties.
- Powder morphology (spherical vs. irregular).
- Powder packing density (loose powder absorbs more than dense powder due to multiple internal reflections — a phenomenon called the **"cavity effect"**).
- Wavelength of the incident radiation (e.g., 1.06 µm for fiber lasers, 10.6 µm for CO₂ lasers — most metals reflect CO₂ strongly but absorb fiber-laser radiation better).

> **Engineering insight:** Fiber lasers (Nd:YAG, Yb-fiber) are preferred over CO₂ lasers for metals because the **1.06 µm wavelength** is absorbed **3 to 10 times more efficiently** by typical engineering metals.

### Sub-Mechanism 2 — Heat Transfer into the Powder Bed

The absorbed energy propagates into the powder bed via three mechanisms operating simultaneously:

- **Conduction** through particle–particle contact points (dominant within the solidified layer).
- **Convection** through the shielding gas (typically Argon or Nitrogen) — critical for removing vaporized metal plume (the "vapor recoil" or "Knudsen" effect).
- **Radiation** from the molten pool back to surrounding powder (dominant at temperatures > 1500 K).

The **thermal penetration depth** (depth to which heat conducts during laser exposure) is approximated as:

$$\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \tau_{int}}$$

where $\alpha$ is the **thermal diffusivity** of the powder bed (typically $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-5}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ for metals) and $\tau_{int}$ is the **laser–material interaction time** (function of spot size and scan speed).

### Sub-Mechanism 3 — Phase Change (Melt Pool Formation)

Once the local temperature exceeds the **melting point (T_m)**, a transient **melt pool** forms. Its geometry is critical:

- A **wide, shallow melt pool** → balling, lack-of-fusion defects.
- An **optimal keyhole-shaped pool** (deep, narrow) → high density, but risk of keyhole porosity.
- An **overly deep pool** → evaporation of low-boiling alloying elements (e.g., Mg, Zn in Al alloys).

The melt pool is stabilized by a balance between **Marangoni convection** (surface-tension-driven flow) and **buoyancy-driven flow**.

### Sub-Mechanism 4 — Solidification & Track Formation

As the energy source moves on, the melt pool cools at rates of **$10^{3}$ to $10^{8}\,\text{K/s}$** (extreme compared to conventional casting's $10^{0}$ to $10^{2}\,\text{K/s}$). This rapid cooling produces:

- **Fine cellular/dendritic microstructures**.
- **Metastable phases** (e.g., supersaturated solid solutions in Ti-6Al-4V).
- **Residual stresses** from steep thermal gradients (a major KTU exam topic).

## 2.2 Why Powder Fusion is Fundamentally Different from Bulk Metal Welding

The KTU examiner often asks: *"Why is the melt pool in powder-bed fusion smaller and more unstable than in bulk laser welding?"* The answer lies in three powder-specific effects:

| # | Powder-Specific Effect | Consequence |
|---|------------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Reduced thermal conductivity** of powder (≈1% to 10% of bulk) | Heat stays localized → smaller, hotter melt pool |
| 2 | **High surface-area-to-volume ratio** | Faster melting, but also faster oxidation risk |
| 3 | **Balling phenomenon** (cylindrical track breaks into spheres due to Plateau–Rayleigh instability) | Loss of densification, porosity, delamination |

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **single most important reference** for solving numerical problems on powder fusion. Memorize these for ESE (End Semester Examination).

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Definitions & Units |
|---|----------|---------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = Laser power [W]; $v$ = scan speed [mm/s]; $h$ = hatch spacing [mm]; $t$ = layer thickness [mm]. Units: $\text{J/mm}^{3}$. Optimal range for most metals: **60–120 J/mm³**. |
| 2 | **Line Energy Density (LED)** | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Energy per unit length of scan. Units: **J/mm**. |
| 3 | **Areal Energy Density (AED)** | $E_A = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Energy per unit area. Units: **J/mm²**. |
| 4 | **Thermal Penetration Depth** | $\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \tau_{int}}$ | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity [m²/s]; $\tau_{int}$ = interaction time [s]. |
| 5 | **Interaction Time** | $\tau_{int} = \dfrac{d}{v}$ | $d$ = laser spot diameter [mm]. |
| 6 | **Cooling Rate (Rosenthal-type estimate)** | $\dfrac{dT}{dt} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T - T_0)^2}{P}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity [W/m·K]; $T$ = peak temperature [K]; $T_0$ = substrate temperature [K]. |
| 7 | **Beer–Lambert Absorption (depth-resolved)** | $I(z) = I_0 \exp(-\beta z)$ | $\beta$ = extinction coefficient; $z$ = depth [m]. |
| 8 | **Melt Pool Aspect Ratio Criterion** | $\dfrac{\text{Depth}}{\text{Width}} \le 0.5$ | Empirical threshold for conduction-mode (stable) melting. |
| 9 | **Balling Instability Criterion** | $\dfrac{L}{\text{D}} > \pi$ | Where $L$ = track length, $D$ = track diameter (Plateau–Rayleigh instability). |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The VED equation is used in industry to set initial process parameters. A VED that is **too low (< 40 J/mm³)** causes lack-of-fusion porosity; a VED that is **too high (> 200 J/mm³)** causes keyhole porosity, balling, and vaporization of alloying elements.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The VED is not a "given" — it can be derived rigorously from first principles as shown below.

### Step 1 — Define the Laser Energy Delivered per Unit Time

The laser delivers power $P$ [W = J/s]. As the scan head moves at velocity $v$ [mm/s], the energy deposited per unit **length** of scan is:

$$E_L = \frac{\text{Energy}}{\text{Length}} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{P}{v}$$

### Step 2 — Convert Line Energy to Areal Energy

The laser does not produce a perfect zero-width line. It deposits energy across the **hatch spacing $h$** [mm]. The energy per unit **area** of one scanned track is therefore:

$$E_A = \frac{E_L}{h} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$$

### Step 3 — Convert Areal Energy to Volumetric Energy

Finally, each scanned layer has thickness $t$ [mm]. The energy delivered per unit **volume** of consolidated material is:

$$E_v = \frac{E_A}{t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

This is the canonical KTU formula. Note that the dimensions verify correctly:

$$\frac{[\text{W}]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \frac{[\text{J/s}]}{[\text{mm}^3/\text{s}]} = \left[\frac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}\right]$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — Thermal Penetration Depth

This derivation justifies why heat does not penetrate infinitely into the powder bed during the laser dwell time.

### Step 1 — Start with the 1-D Heat Conduction Equation (semi-infinite solid)

$$\frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \alpha \frac{\partial^2 T}{\partial z^2}$$

### Step 2 — Apply the boundary condition of a constant surface heat flux $q_0$ at $z = 0$

The classical solution (Carslaw & Jaeger, *Conduction of Heat in Solids*, 1959) yields the temperature field:

$$T(z, t) = T_0 + \frac{2 q_0}{\sqrt{\pi \rho c k}} \sqrt{t} \cdot \exp\!\left(-\frac{z^2}{4 \alpha t}\right) - \frac{q_0 z}{k} \, \text{erfc}\!\left(\frac{z}{2\sqrt{\alpha t}}\right)$$

### Step 3 — Define thermal penetration depth as the depth at which $T$ drops by a factor of $1/e$ of the surface value

Setting the exponential term $\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{z^2}{4 \alpha t}\right) = e^{-1}$ gives:

$$\frac{z^2}{4 \alpha t} = 1 \implies z = 2\sqrt{\alpha t}$$

For engineering estimation, the leading-order approximation (dominant first term) is:

$$\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \tau_{int}}$$

### Step 4 — Numerical Sanity Check for Ti-6Al-4V

- $\alpha \approx 7 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$
- $\tau_{int} = d / v$. With $d = 0.1\,\text{mm}$, $v = 1000\,\text{mm/s}$: $\tau_{int} = 10^{-4}\,\text{s}$.

$$\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{(7 \times 10^{-6}) \times (10^{-4})} \approx 2.6 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 26\,\mu\text{m}$$

This matches typical melt-pool depths observed experimentally in Ti-6Al-4V SLM, confirming the model.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Board Style

**Problem (Modeled on KTU University Exam, June 2024):**
*"In a Selective Laser Melting process, a fiber laser of power 400 W is used to scan Ti-6Al-4V powder. The scan speed is 1200 mm/s, hatch spacing is 0.10 mm, and layer thickness is 0.030 mm. Calculate (a) the volumetric energy density, (b) the line energy density, and (c) comment on whether the process will likely produce a fully dense part."*

### Solution:

**(a) Volumetric Energy Density:**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{400}{(1200) \cdot (0.10) \cdot (0.030)}$$

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{400}{3.6} \approx 111.11\,\text{J/mm}^3}$$

**(b) Line Energy Density:**

$$E_L = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{400}{1200} = 0.333\,\text{J/mm}$$

**(c) Comment:**

Since $E_v \approx 111\,\text{J/mm}^3$ lies within the optimal window of **60–120 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V (per KTU reference data), the process is **expected to produce a near-fully-dense part** with minimal lack-of-fusion defects, provided the absorptivity is stable and the powder layer is uniformly spread.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defect Modes

| Process Window | $E_v$ Range (J/mm³) | Dominant Defect | Mitigation Strategy |
|----------------|---------------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Under-fusion | $< 40$ | Lack-of-fusion porosity, poor inter-track bonding | Increase $P$, decrease $v$, $h$, or $t$ |
| Optimal conduction mode | 60 – 120 | Minimal defects | Maintain scan strategy consistency |
| Over-fusion / Keyhole | $> 200$ | Keyhole porosity, vaporization, balling | Decrease $P$, increase $v$, use defocused beam |
| Excessive re-melting | $> 300$ | Recoater interference, spatter, alloy depletion | Multi-scan strategy, pre-heating |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Flow of the Powder Fusion Mechanism

The following Mermaid block represents the **sequential energy–matter interactions** in a single laser scan across a metal powder bed, mapped at the block-functional level. It explicitly avoids physical drawing (which cannot be rendered in Mermaid) and instead traces process causation.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Energy Source: Laser or Electron Beam] --> A1[Beam Steering Optics or Electromagnetic Lens]
    A1 --> A2[Focused Spot on Powder Bed]
    A2 --> B1{Powder Layer State}
    B1 --> B2[Loose Powder Bed, Low Thermal Conductivity]
    B2 --> C1[Photon or Electron Absorption]
    C1 --> C2[Local Temperature Rise Above Melting Point]
    C2 --> C3[Melt Pool Formation with Marangoni Convection]
    C3 --> C4[Track Solidification Behind Beam]
    C4 --> C5[Heat Affected Zone and Residual Stress Build Up]
    C5 --> D1[Recoater Deposits Next Powder Layer]
    D1 --> A2
    C3 --> E1[Shielding Gas Flow Removes Vapor Plume]
    E1 --> E2[Spatter Collection Filter]
    B1 --> F1[Chamber Atmosphere Argon or Vacuum]
    F1 --> C1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Cause-Effect Chain of Defect Formation

This second diagram maps how each process parameter deviation propagates through the fusion mechanism to produce a specific defect. This is a high-frequency KTU conceptual question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[High Scan Velocity] --> D1[Reduced Interaction Time]
    D1 --> E1[Insufficient Energy Input]
    E1 --> R1[Lack of Fusion Porosity]

    P2[Excessive Laser Power] --> D2[Over Melting]
    D2 --> E2[Vapor Recoil Pressure]
    E2 --> R2[Keyhole Porosity]

    P3[Poor Powder Flowability] --> D3[Non Uniform Layer]
    D3 --> E3[Variable Hatch Overlap]
    E3 --> R3[Balling and Track Discontinuity]

    P4[High Thermal Gradient] --> D4[Steep Cooling Rate]
    D4 --> E4[Non Uniform Shrinkage]
    E4 --> R4[Residual Stress and Warping]
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Melt Pool Geometry and Stability Criterion

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M1[Melt Pool Geometry Inputs] --> M2{Laser Power to Scan Speed Ratio}
    M2 -->|Low| M3[Conduction Mode Wide and Shallow Pool]
    M2 -->|Medium| M4[Stable Keyhole Like Pool Optimal Densification]
    M2 -->|High| M5[Deep Vapor Driven Keyhole Porosity and Spatter]
    M3 --> M6[Aspect Ratio Depth over Width Less Than 0.5]
    M4 --> M7[Aspect Ratio Depth over Width Between 0.5 and 1.5]
    M5 --> M8[Aspect Ratio Depth over Width Greater Than 1.5]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagrams:** In Mermaid's standard library, **process flows** (rectangular nodes) connect to **decision nodes** (diamond-shaped) which fork the flow into mutually exclusive outcomes. Trace the path from `Energy Source` in the first diagram and you will encounter exactly one decision (powder layer state), which gates the entire fusion cascade.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the term "Volumetric Energy Density" (VED) in the context of powder bed fusion. State its typical optimal range for most engineering metals.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation Breakdown):**

- **Definition (2 marks):** Volumetric Energy Density is the amount of laser energy delivered per unit volume of consolidated powder in PBF processes. It is mathematically expressed as:
$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
where $P$ = laser power, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness.

- **Optimal range (1 mark):** For most engineering metals (Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Inconel 718), the optimal VED lies in the range **60 to 120 J/mm³**.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Distinguish between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM) in terms of the underlying powder fusion mechanism.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks — Allocation Breakdown):**

| Feature | SLS | SLM |
|---------|-----|-----|
| **Fusion mechanism** | Solid-state sintering below melting point | Complete melting above melting point |
| **Melt pool formation** | No distinct melt pool | Distinct transient melt pool |
| **Density achievable** | Typically 60–95% (metals) | ≥ 99% (near-full density) |
| **Energy density required** | Lower (10–40 J/mm³) | Higher (60–120 J/mm³) |
| **Material application** | Polymers (PA12, PA11) primarily; some metals via DMLS variants | Pure metals and alloys exclusively |

*(Full 3 marks: 1 mark for sintering vs. melting distinction, 1 mark for density comparison, 1 mark for material application.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] Explain in detail the **four sequential sub-mechanisms** that occur during powder fusion in a Selective Laser Melting process. For each sub-mechanism, identify the dominant physical phenomenon and one process parameter that controls it.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] A Ti-6Al-4V powder bed is processed with a 370 W Yb-fiber laser. The scan speed is 1100 mm/s, hatch spacing is 0.11 mm, and layer thickness is 0.030 mm. Calculate the **volumetric energy density** and the **line energy density**, then determine whether the parameter set falls in the **conduction-mode (optimal)** or **keyhole-mode (over-fusion)** regime.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The four sequential sub-mechanisms are:

1. **Powder–Energy Coupling (Absorption)** [1.5 Marks]: The laser photons strike the powder particles. Absorption efficiency depends on material optical properties, wavelength (fiber laser 1.06 µm is preferred for metals), and the cavity effect in loose powder. **Controlling parameter:** laser wavelength and absorptivity $A$.

2. **Heat Transfer into Powder Bed** [1.5 Marks]: The absorbed energy propagates via conduction (through particle contacts), convection (shielding gas removes vapor plume), and radiation (from the hot melt pool). The thermal penetration depth is $\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \tau_{int}}$. **Controlling parameter:** scan speed $v$ (via interaction time).

3. **Phase Change (Melt Pool Formation)** [2 Marks]: Once temperature exceeds $T_m$, a melt pool forms. Its stability is governed by Marangoni convection (surface tension gradient) and recoil pressure from vaporization. The melt pool aspect ratio $\text{Depth}/\text{Width}$ determines conduction-mode vs. keyhole-mode melting. **Controlling parameter:** laser power $P$.

4. **Solidification and Track Formation** [2 Marks]: Rapid cooling ($10^3$ to $10^8\,\text{K/s}$) produces fine dendritic microstructures and residual stresses. **Controlling parameter:** hatch spacing $h$ and substrate pre-heating temperature $T_0$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Volumetric Energy Density**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{370}{(1100)(0.11)(0.030)} = \frac{370}{3.63}$$

$$\boxed{E_v \approx 101.93\,\text{J/mm}^3}$$

**Step 2: Line Energy Density**

$$E_L = \frac{P}{v} = \frac{370}{1100} = 0.336\,\text{J/mm}$$

**Step 3: Regime Determination**

Since $E_v \approx 101.93\,\text{J/mm}^3$ falls within the optimal conduction-mode window of **60–120 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V, the parameter set is in the **conduction-mode (optimal)** regime. The melt pool is expected to be wide and shallow (aspect ratio < 0.5), with minimal keyhole porosity and good inter-track bonding. *(1 mark allocated for the regime conclusion.)*

---

### Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks) — *Alternative*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

> **(a)** [7 Marks] With the aid of a neat schematic, explain the **melt pool dynamics** in laser powder fusion. Discuss the role of **Marangoni convection**, **buoyancy force**, and **vapor recoil pressure** in determining the melt pool shape and stability.

> **(b)** [7 Marks] Derive an expression for **thermal penetration depth** in a powder bed during laser exposure. Given that the thermal diffusivity of 316L stainless steel powder is $4.5 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{m}^2/\text{s}$ and the laser spot diameter is 0.08 mm with a scan speed of 800 mm/s, calculate the thermal penetration depth.

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The melt pool is a transient, high-temperature (~2500–3500 K for steels) liquid region whose shape governs final part density. Three competing forces act on it:

- **Marangoni Convection** [2.5 Marks]: Surface tension $\gamma$ is a function of temperature $T$ and active-element concentration $C$ (e.g., sulfur or oxygen). The gradient $\frac{d\gamma}{dT}$ drives fluid flow from hot (low $\gamma$) to cold (high $\gamma$) regions. If $\frac{d\gamma}{dT} < 0$ (typical), flow is outward → **wide, shallow pool**. If surface-active elements reverse the sign, flow is inward → **deep, narrow pool**.

- **Buoyancy Force** [2 Marks]: Hotter liquid in the center is less dense, causing upward flow in the center and downward flow at edges. Contributes to a toroidal flow pattern but is generally weaker than Marangoni flow (by ~2 orders of magnitude in SLM).

- **Vapor Recoil Pressure** [2.5 Marks]: At high power density, the metal vaporizes and exerts a recoil pressure (≈ $0.1 P_{atm}$ at boiling point) that depresses the melt pool surface into a **keyhole**. This deep cavity enhances energy absorption via multiple internal reflections but is unstable and causes porosity if it collapses.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Step 1: Derivation (already shown in §3.2)** — Start from the 1-D heat conduction equation with constant surface heat flux boundary condition; apply the Carslaw–Jaeger solution. The leading-order estimate gives:

$$\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \cdot \tau_{int}}$$

*(Full 4 marks for derivation steps — see Section 3.2.)*

**Step 2: Calculate Interaction Time**

$$\tau_{int} = \frac{d}{v} = \frac{0.08\,\text{mm}}{800\,\text{mm/s}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{s}$$

**Step 3: Calculate Thermal Penetration Depth**

$$\delta_{th} = \sqrt{(4.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-4})} = \sqrt{4.5 \times 10^{-10}}$$

$$\boxed{\delta_{th} \approx 2.12 \times 10^{-5}\,\text{m} = 21.2\,\mu\text{m}}$$

This value is consistent with the experimentally observed single-track melt-pool depth in 316L SS under similar process parameters. *(1 mark for numerical consistency comment.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Powder Fusion Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency in VED**: Students frequently mix mm and µm. If hatch spacing is given as **100 µm**, you must convert it to **0.10 mm** before applying the formula. Failure to do so causes a VED that is off by a factor of 1000, leading to **full 7-mark loss** in Part B numericals.
>
> 2. **Conflating SLS, DMLS, and SLM**: Many students write "SLS melts the powder" — this is incorrect for the KTU 2024 syllabus. SLS = sintering (no melt pool); DMLS ≈ partial-melt liquid-phase sintering; SLM = full melting. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Skipping the Defect-Regime Comment**: In numerical problems, examiners allocate **1 mark** for stating whether the calculated VED corresponds to under-fusion, optimal, or keyhole regimes. Students who only write the number and stop will lose this mark.
>
> 4. **Writing "Marangoni Effect" without Explaining Direction**: A common KTU-2024 pitfall. You must specify *which direction* the surface-tension gradient drives the flow (hot→cold or cold→hot) and the consequence (wide-shallow vs. deep-narrow pool).

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Powder Fusion Mechanism (Module 2)**
>
> - **Powder fusion is NOT one process**: differentiate **Sintering (SLS)**, **Partial Melting (DMLS)**, and **Full Melting (SLM/EBM)**.
> - **Master the VED formula**: $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$. Optimal range for metals: **60–120 J/mm³**.
> - **Remember the four sub-mechanisms in order**: Absorption → Heat Transfer → Phase Change → Solidification.
> - **Know the three melt-pool forces**: Marangoni (dominant), Buoyancy (weak), Vapor Recoil (keyhole trigger).
> - **Balling is the Plateau–Rayleigh instability** ($L/D > \pi$); it is a defect, not a feature.
> - **Powder has 1–10% of bulk thermal conductivity** — this is why melt pools are small and hot.
> - **Cooling rates in PBF are extreme**: $10^3$ to $10^8\,\text{K/s}$, leading to fine dendritic microstructures and high residual stress.
> - **Fiber lasers (1.06 µm) > CO₂ lasers (10.6 µm)** for metals due to higher absorptivity.
> - **EBM operates at high vacuum and elevated chamber temperature (~700 °C)**, reducing thermal gradients compared to SLM.
> - **Lack-of-fusion porosity** ← low VED; **Keyhole porosity** ← high VED. Both are the KTU-favorite defect regimes.
> - **Absorptivity of loose powder > solid bulk** (cavity effect) — an important conceptual point tested in Part A.
> - **Thermal penetration depth formula**: $\delta_{th} \approx \sqrt{\alpha \tau_{int}}$; interaction time $\tau_{int} = d / v$.
> - **Laser scan strategies**: stripe, chessboard, fractal — used to manage residual stress and distortion.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLS Metal and Ceramic Part Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** SLS Metal and Ceramic Part Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is a Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a high-power laser selectively scans and consolidates successive layers of powdered material (polymer, metal, or ceramic) according to sliced CAD data. For **metals**, the process branches into **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)** — solid-state sintering below melting point — and **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** — complete melting. For **ceramics**, SLS is challenging because of high melting points, low thermal shock resistance, and brittleness; therefore, an **indirect (multi-step) route** is most common, where a powder bed of ceramic mixed with a polymeric binder is laser-scanned, followed by post-processing such as **debinding** and **infiltration**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** SLS for *metals* is treated as a high-energy powder bed fusion variant, while SLS for *ceramics* is a hybrid process — laser consolidation is only the *first* stage; the green part must subsequently undergo binder burnout and densification (sintering / infiltration / HIP) to achieve a functional ceramic or metal-ceramic composite.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The 3D Pancake Printer"

Imagine a chef making a multi-layered pancake cake. Instead of pouring batter onto a hot griddle, picture a machine that:
1. Spreads a **very thin layer of metallic "flour"** (powder) on a flat plate,
2. Uses a **laser "spatula"** to trace a 2D pancake shape — but instead of cooking, the laser *fuses* the powder into a solid metal layer,
3. Lowers the plate by the thickness of one pancake, spreads a fresh layer, and repeats.

After hundreds of such layers, you don't have a cake — you have a **fully dense, complex 3D metal part** that could never be cast or machined in one piece. For **ceramics**, the analogy changes: the chef first uses a *mild laser* to glue the powder together with a polymer binder (a "half-cooked pancake"), then bakes it slowly in a furnace to burn off the glue and finally soak it with a metal melt to make it strong — analogous to debinding and infiltration.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Solidification rate of metal melt pool:** $\mathbf{10^3}$ to $\mathbf{10^6}$ K/s
- **Typical laser power (metal SLS/SLM):** $\mathbf{200}$ to $\mathbf{1000}$ W (Yb-fiber or CO₂)
- **Layer thickness (metals):** $\mathbf{20}$ to $\mathbf{100\ \mu m}$
- **Layer thickness (ceramics, indirect):** $\mathbf{50}$ to $\mathbf{200\ \mu m}$
- **Build chamber atmosphere:** **Inert gas** — Argon (O₂ < **1000 ppm**) or Nitrogen
- **Oxygen threshold to avoid oxidation:** typically < **0.1 %** for reactive powders (Ti, Al)
- **Laser spot diameter:** $\mathbf{50}$ to $\mathbf{500\ \mu m}$

> [!NOTE]
> **Ceramic SLS Reality Check:** Pure ceramic powders (Al₂O₃, SiC, ZrO₂) **cannot be directly melted** by a standard SLS laser because their melting points exceed 2000 °C and they undergo thermal shock cracking. This is why the *indirect* route with a sacrificial polymer binder is the industry standard.

---

## 1.4 Classification Snapshot (KTU Exam Favourite)

| Branch | Process | Material State During Laser | Post-Processing |
|---|---|---|---|
| **DMLS** | Direct Metal Laser Sintering | Solid-state sintering (T < T_m) | Stress relief, HIP optional |
| **SLM** | Selective Laser Melting | Full melting (T > T_m) | Stress relief, HIP common |
| **Indirect SLS-Metal** | Binder-assisted sintering | Binder melts/fuses powder | Debinding + Sintering + Infiltration |
| **Indirect SLS-Ceramic** | Binder + ceramic | Binder fuses ceramic particles | Debinding + Sintering + Infiltration (Si, epoxy, Al) |
| **Direct SLS-Ceramic** | High-power laser melting | Localized melt pool | Slow cooling / HIP |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The SLS Process Chain — Step-by-Step Logic

The operation of a metal/ceramic SLS system is governed by a tightly sequenced thermomechanical routine. Below is the granular logic that examiners love to test:

### Stage 1 — Powder Deposition
- A **recoater blade or roller** spreads a uniform layer of powder across the build platform.
- Layer thickness $t$ is one of the four cardinal process parameters.
- For **metals**: gas-atomized spherical powder, particle size **10–45 µm**.
- For **ceramics (indirect)**: bimodal powder mix of ceramic + polymer binder (e.g., 60 % Al₂O₃ + 40 % polyamide).

### Stage 2 — Laser Scanning
- A galvanometer mirror system steers the laser beam along the slice contour.
- The laser delivers energy density high enough to **sinter or melt** the powder locally.
- Hatch pattern (typically 0°/90°, 45°/135°, or island/checkerboard) controls thermal history.

### Stage 3 — Platform Lowering
- Build piston descends by exactly $t$ (one layer).
- Powder supply piston rises to deliver fresh powder.

### Stage 4 — Repeat
- Stages 1–3 repeat for every layer (typically 1000–10 000 layers for a real part).

### Stage 5 — Post-Processing
- For metals: **stress relief** at 600–900 °C → optional **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** at 1100–1250 °C, 100–200 MPa.
- For ceramics: **debinding** (slow heating to burn polymer) → **sintering** (densification) → **infiltration** with metal/glass to fill residual porosity.

## 2.2 Why Metals and Ceramics Behave Differently — The Physics

- **Metals** conduct heat well ($\sim 10$ to $\sim 400$ W/m·K). The melt pool is mobile, surface tension drives Marangoni flow, and the melt solidifies dendritically. Vaporization of low-melting alloying elements (e.g., Al, Zn, Mg) must be controlled via **energy density** tuning.
- **Ceramics** have very low thermal conductivity ($\sim 1$ to $\sim 30$ W/m·K) and extremely high melting points (Al₂O₃ = 2072 °C; SiC = 2730 °C). Direct laser melting causes:
  - Thermal-shock microcracks
  - Balling due to high viscosity melt
  - Sublimation (e.g., SiC → Si + C)
- Hence the **indirect polymer-binder route** is dominant in industry and academic research.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Energy density is the single most important process parameter in SLS.** It governs porosity, density, microstructure, residual stress, and distortion. KTU examiners routinely ask students to compute or interpret it.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Quantity | Formula | Description & Units |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $\displaystyle E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = laser power (W), $v$ = scan speed (mm/s), $h$ = hatch spacing (mm), $t$ = layer thickness (mm). Units: **J/mm³** |
| Linear Energy Density (LED) | $\displaystyle E_l = \frac{P}{v}$ | Energy per unit scan length (J/mm) |
| Surface Energy Density (SED) | $\displaystyle E_s = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | Energy per unit area (J/mm²) |
| Solidification cooling rate | $\displaystyle \dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $T_m$ melting, $T_0$ substrate temp. (K/s) |
| Melt pool depth (approx.) | $\displaystyle d \approx \sqrt{\frac{4 \alpha P}{\pi \rho c_p v h (T_m - T_0)}}$ | $\alpha$ = diffusivity, $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat |
| Marangoni number | $\displaystyle Ma = \frac{-d\gamma/dT \cdot \Delta T \cdot L}{\mu \alpha}$ | $\gamma$ = surface tension, $\mu$ = dynamic viscosity |
| Densification (sintering) | $\displaystyle \frac{\Delta L}{L_0} = A \cdot t^n \cdot e^{-Q/RT}$ | Creep/sintering kinetics, $n \approx 0.5$, $Q$ = activation energy |
| Porosity target | $< 0.5\,\%$ (metal SLM) | Aerospace grade |
| Hatch spacing rule of thumb | $h \approx 0.7 \cdot d_{spot}$ | Ensures melt-pool overlap |

> [!WARNING]
> In the table above, **never** use the vertical bar `|` symbol inside formulas — it breaks Markdown table rendering. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute value or "such that" expressions.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Sector | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Topology-optimized Ti-6Al-4V brackets, fuel injector nozzles (GE, Airbus) |
| **Medical** | Patient-specific Ti cranial implants, porous acetabular cups (spinal cages with ceramic coating) |
| **Tooling** | Conformal cooling inserts (DMLS in H13, Maraging 300) |
| **Automotive** | Inconel 718 turbine housings, AlSi10Mg lightweight nodes |
| **Energy** | Ceramic heat-shield prototypes, SiC rocket nozzle liners (indirect SLS + Si infiltration) |
| **Electronics** | AlN and Al₂O₃ ceramic substrates for power modules |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

The VED is the *total laser energy delivered per unit volume of consolidated material*. We derive it from the geometry of the melt track.

### Step 1 — Energy delivered per unit length of a single scan track
A laser of power $P$ scanning at velocity $v$ deposits energy along a track of length $L = v \cdot \Delta t$ in time $\Delta t$. The energy per unit length (linear energy density) is therefore:

$$
E_l = \frac{P \cdot \Delta t}{L} = \frac{P \cdot \Delta t}{v \cdot \Delta t} = \frac{P}{v}
$$

The unit is $\text{J/mm}$ when $P$ is in W and $v$ in mm/s.

### Step 2 — Energy per unit area of one scanned line
Each scanned line has width equal to the **hatch spacing** $h$. The area covered by a line of length $L$ is $A = L \cdot h$. So the energy per unit area is:

$$
E_s = \frac{E_l \cdot L}{L \cdot h} = \frac{E_l}{h} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h}
$$

Units: $\text{J/mm}^{2}$.

### Step 3 — Energy per unit volume of the consolidated layer
The layer thickness is $t$. The volume consolidated per unit length is $V = L \cdot h \cdot t$. Hence:

$$
E_v = \frac{E_s \cdot A \cdot t}{V} = \frac{E_s}{t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

This is the **canonical VED formula** universally used in SLS/DMLS/SLM literature.

### Step 4 — Engineering interpretation
A low $E_v$ → insufficient fusion → porosity (lack of fusion defects). A high $E_v$ → over-melting → keyhole porosity, vaporization, balling. There exists an optimal $E_v$ window for each material.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner's Trick:** Many students write $E_v = \dfrac{P}{vht}$ and stop. To get full marks, also state the **units (J/mm³)** and the **engineering interpretation** (low/high regime). Marks are reserved for *interpretation*, not just algebraic recall.

### Step 5 — Worked Numerical Example
A DMLS machine processes Ti-6Al-4V with $P = 280$ W, $v = 1200$ mm/s, $h = 0.1$ mm, $t = 0.03$ mm. Compute $E_v$.

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{280}{1200 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}
$$

Compute the denominator first:

$$
1200 \times 0.1 = 120, \quad 120 \times 0.03 = 3.6
$$

Therefore:

$$
E_v = \frac{280}{3.6} \approx 77.78\ \text{J/mm}^{3}
$$

For Ti-6Al-4V, the recommended $E_v$ window is roughly **60–120 J/mm³** for > 99.5 % density, so 77.78 J/mm³ is in a healthy operating range.

## 3.2 Derivation of Hatch-Overlap Condition for Continuous Melt Tracks

For two adjacent scan tracks to metallurgically bond, the **melt pool width** $w$ must exceed the **hatch spacing** $h$ with adequate overlap. Using a simple heat-conduction argument, the effective melt-pool width can be approximated as:

$$
w \approx 2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \alpha P}{\pi \rho c_p (T_m - T_0) v h}}
$$

The condition $w \ge k \cdot h$ (where $k \approx 1.2$ to $1.5$ for stable overlap) is solved iteratively. Engineers usually adopt the empirical rule:

$$
h \approx 0.7 \cdot d_{spot}
$$

This ensures 30 % overlap between adjacent melt tracks. The "0.7 rule" is a **high-yield KTU short-answer fact**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Defect vs. Energy Density

| Defect | Cause | Energy Density Regime | Cure |
|---|---|---|---|
| Lack of fusion (LoF) | $E_v$ too low | $E_v < 50$ J/mm³ (for Ti) | Increase $P$, decrease $v$ |
| Keyhole porosity | $E_v$ too high, vaporization | $E_v > 120$ J/mm³ (for Ti) | Decrease $P$, increase $v$ |
| Balling | High viscosity + low wetting | $E_v$ at extremes, or high oxygen | Optimize $E_v$, lower O₂ |
| Cracking (ceramics) | Thermal shock | Any direct melt | Use indirect route, pre-heating |
| Delamination | Residual stress | High cooling rate | Pre-heating, scan-strategy optimization |
| Porosity (post-process) | Incomplete densification | Indirect SLS | Longer sintering time, higher $T$, HIP |

## 3.4 Derivation of Cooling Rate (Approximate)

For a moving point heat source, the cooling rate at the trailing edge of the melt pool scales as:

$$
\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}
$$

Inserting Ti-6Al-4V data: $k \approx 21$ W/m·K, $T_m - T_0 \approx 1500$ K, $P = 280$ W:

$$
\dot{T} \approx \frac{2 \pi (21)(1500)^2}{280} \approx 1.06 \times 10^{6}\ \text{K/s}
$$

This is why SLS produces **fine martensitic α' microstructures** in Ti alloys — the cooling rate is five orders of magnitude higher than in casting.

> [!TIP]
> The above cooling-rate derivation is a **favourite 7-mark derivation** in KTU ESE. Show every algebraic step; partial credit is awarded per step.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Metal/Ceramic SLS System

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAD["CAD Model STL / 3MF"] --> SLICER["Slicer Software"]
    SLICER --> GCODE["Layer Slice G-code"]
    GCODE --> GALVO["Galvanometer Scanner Controller"]
    GALVO --> LASER["Fiber Laser 200 to 1000 W Yb"]
    LASER --> OPTICS["F-Theta Lens Collimator"]
    OPTICS --> CHAMBER["Build Chamber Argon Atmosphere O2 less than 1000 ppm"]
    POWDER1["Metal Powder Hopper Ti6Al4V Inconel 316L"] --> RECOATER["Recoater Blade Roller"]
    POWDER2["Ceramic Binder Mix Al2O3 PA12"] --> RECOATER
    RECOATER --> CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --> PLATFORM["Build Platform Z Stage"]
    PLATFORM --> CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --> GREEN["Green Part Stage 1"]
    GREEN --> DEBIND["Debinding Furnace 600 C 2 to 5 C per min"]
    DEBIND --> SINTER["Sintering Furnace 1500 to 1700 C"]
    SINTER --> INFIL["Infiltration Al Si Epoxy Stage 2 Ceramic"]
    INFIL --> FINAL["Final Dense Part Stage 3"]
    CHAMBER -.HIP optional.-> HIP["Hot Isostatic Press 1100 C 150 MPa"]
    HIP --> FINAL
```

## 4.2 Process-Decision Flowchart — Choosing the Right SLS Route

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    START["Material Selection"] --> Q1{"Is the material pure ceramic?"}
    Q1 -- "No metal alloy" --> METAL["Direct SLM DMLS Argon chamber full melting"]
    Q1 -- "Yes ceramic Al2O3 ZrO2 SiC" --> CERAMIC["Use Indirect SLS route"]
    METAL --> M1{"Reactive metal Ti Al?"}
    M1 -- "Yes" --> M2["O2 less than 100 ppm preheat 200 C"]
    M1 -- "No steel Inconel" --> M3["Standard atmosphere possible"]
    M2 --> DONE["Build and stress relief and HIP"]
    M3 --> DONE
    CERAMIC --> C1["Mix ceramic with 30 to 50 percent polymer binder"]
    C1 --> C2["Laser scan 50 to 200 W low energy density"]
    C2 --> C3["Debind at 600 C slow ramp"]
    C3 --> C4{"Final application?"}
    C4 -- "Structural high T" --> C5["Sinter plus Si infiltration"]
    C4 -- "Biomedical" --> C6["Sinter plus glass ceramic infiltration"]
    C5 --> READY["Final ceramic composite part"]
    C6 --> READY
```

## 4.3 Schematic — Melt Pool Cross-Section (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph MELTPOOL["Melt Pool Dynamics"]
        A1["Laser Beam Centerline"]
        A2["Vapor Depression Keyhole if E_v too high"]
        A3["Convection Marangoni Flow"]
        A4["Conduction into Substrate"]
        A5["Solidified Track behind scan"]
        A6["Unmelted Powder ahead of scan"]
    end
```
