```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[Input Parameters]

    end

    subgraph MONITOR[Real-Time Monitoring]

    end

        STRESS[Stress Relief Furnace]
        HIP[Hot Isostatic Pressing]
        CNC[CNC Machining of Critical Seats]

    end

    CAD --> SLICE
    SLICE --> PARA
    PARA --> OPTICS
    POWDER --> RECOAT
    RECOAT --> CHAMBER
    SOURCE --> OPTICS
    OPTICS --> CHAMBER
    CHAMBER --> MP
    CHAMBER --> O2
    CHAMBER --> THERM
    MP --> CT
    O2 --> CT
    THERM --> CT
    CT --> OPTICS
    CHAMBER --> STRESS
    STRESS --> HIP
    HIP --> CNC
    CNC --> QC
```

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Typical SLM Build Sequence (Layer-by-Layer)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Step 1: Lower Build Platform by Layer Thickness t] --> L2[Step 2: Recoat Powder Layer]
    L2 --> L3[Step 3: Laser Pre-Scan to Heat Substrate]
    L3 --> L4[Step 4: Selective Laser Melting per Slice Geometry]
    L4 --> L5[Step 5: Melt Pool Monitoring and Feedback]
    L5 --> L6{Is build complete?}
    L6 -- No --> L1
    L6 -- Yes --> L7[Step 6: Cool Down in Argon Atmosphere]
    L7 --> L8[Step 7: Powder Removal and Powder Recycling]
    L8 --> L9[Step 8: Part Extraction and Stress Relief]
    L9 --> L10[Step 9: Support Removal and Surface Finishing]
    L10 --> L11[Step 10: Quality Inspection and HIP if Required]
```

---

### 4.4 Application Sector Distribution (PBF Industrial Adoption)

```mermaid
pie title PBF Adoption Across Sectors (Indicative, 2024 Industry Survey)
    AEROSPACE : 30
    MEDICAL : 28
    AUTOMOTIVE : 12
    TOOLING : 11
    DENTAL : 9
    DEFENSE : 5
    CONSUMER : 3
    ENERGY : 2
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
**List any three major applications of Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) in the medical field. State the typical material used.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Three major medical applications of SLS are:

1. **Patient-specific anatomical models** for pre-surgical planning and training (e.g., heart, skull, and spine models derived from CT/MRI data).
2. **Surgical drill guides and cutting jigs** used in orthopedic and dental surgery to improve accuracy of bone resection and implant placement.
3. **Customized prosthetic sockets and orthotic devices** that are tailored to the patient's residual limb geometry for superior comfort and fit.

**Typical material used:** **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)** — a biocompatible, sterilizable polymer that is FDA-approved for short-term skin contact. *(1 mark for the three applications, 1 mark for the material choice, 1 mark for the rationale of PA12.)*

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
**Why is Electron Beam Melting (EBM) preferred over Selective Laser Melting (SLM) for manufacturing large Ti-6Al-4V aerospace components? Mention any two reasons.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Higher build speed and larger build volume:** EBM operates at scan speeds up to **1000 m/s** with beam powers of **3 – 15 kW** (vs. SLM's 200 W – 1 kW lasers), enabling faster production of large parts like wing ribs and landing gear brackets.
2. **Vacuum environment:** EBM operates at **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar vacuum**, eliminating oxygen contamination and making it ideal for **reactive metals** like titanium, which are otherwise prone to oxide formation in SLM's argon atmosphere.
3. *(Bonus point)* **Reduced residual stress:** The high pre-heating temperature (≥ 700°C) in EBM's powder bed reduces thermal gradients, minimizing part distortion — though EBM parts often have slightly lower surface finish (Ra 20 – 30 µm) compared to SLM (Ra 5 – 15 µm). *(1 mark per reason, 1 mark for technical accuracy and material justification.)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Model]**

**(a) Describe in detail the major applications of Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) processes in the aerospace industry. Discuss at least four specific components and the materials used. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain with a neat flowchart how the closed-loop monitoring of melt pool temperature and oxygen content is achieved in a Selective Laser Melting (SLM) system for Inconel 718. Justify why the VED must be maintained between 60 and 90 J/mm³ for this alloy. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a) — 7 marks:**

Powder Bed Fusion processes (SLM, DMLS, EBM) have revolutionized the aerospace industry by enabling the manufacture of lightweight, topology-optimized, and geometrically complex components that are impossible to fabricate using conventional subtractive methods. The four major aerospace applications are:

1. **Fuel Injector Nozzles for Jet Engines (LEAP Engine by CFM/GE Aviation):**
   - **Process:** DMLS (EOS M 400-4 system).
   - **Material:** Inconel 718 (a nickel-based superalloy).
   - **Benefit:** Replaces an 18-part assembly (made by brazing and welding) with a single monolithic component. Results in **25% weight reduction**, **15% fuel efficiency improvement**, and 60% reduction in manufacturing lead time. *(2 marks)*

2. **Satellite and Spacecraft Structural Brackets:**
   - **Process:** SLM (e.g., Concept Laser M2).
   - **Material:** Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5).
   - **Benefit:** Topology-optimized bionic designs reduce mass by **up to 60%** while maintaining stiffness. Critical for payload cost (each kg in LEO costs ~$20,000 to launch). *(2 marks)*

3. **Turbine Blades and Combustor Liners (High-Temperature Sections):**
   - **Process:** EBM (Arcam Q20plus) for TiAl; DMLS for Inconel 625 / Hastelloy X.
   - **Material:** γ-TiAl intermetallics, Ni-superalloys.
   - **Benefit:** EBM's high build temperature (> 700°C) produces parts that survive higher in-service temperatures. Internal conformal cooling channels (impossible in cast blades) improve turbine efficiency. *(1.5 marks)*

4. **Landing Gear and Hydraulic Manifold Components:**
   - **Process:** DMLS.
   - **Material:** 17-4 PH Stainless Steel, Maraging 300 (1.2709).
   - **Benefit:** Internal lattice and honeycombed structures provide strength-to-weight ratios 2–3× superior to forged equivalents. *(1.5 marks)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b) — 7 marks:**

**Closed-Loop Monitoring in SLM for Inconel 718:**

The flowchart of the closed-loop monitoring system is:

1. **Layer deposition** → A recoater deposits a uniform **30–40 µm** layer of Inconel 718 powder over the build platform. *(0.5 mark)*
2. **Pre-heating** → A defocused laser or IR heater preheats the powder bed to **80–150°C** to reduce thermal gradients. *(0.5 mark)*
3. **Selective melting** → The fiber laser (~**1070 nm wavelength**, 200–500 W) is steered by galvo-scanners to melt the powder according to the slice geometry. *(0.5 mark)*
4. **Melt pool monitoring** → A **coaxial or off-axis IR photodiode / CMOS camera** (typically operating at 800–1700 nm) captures the melt pool's thermal emission in real time. The melt pool temperature must be maintained in the range of **2200 – 2600°C** (above Inconel's melting point of ~1336°C but below the vaporization threshold). *(1 mark)*
5. **Oxygen monitoring** → A **zirconia-based O₂ sensor** continuously measures the oxygen concentration. For Inconel 718, the limit is **< 1000 ppm** (often < 100 ppm) to prevent oxide inclusions. The chamber is flooded with **high-purity Argon (99.999%)** until O₂ drops below the threshold before melting begins. *(1 mark)*
6. **Closed-loop feedback** → If the melt pool temperature deviates from the setpoint, the control system adjusts **laser power (P)** and **scan speed (v)** to bring the VED back to the target window. If O₂ rises above the limit, the system halts and re-purges the chamber. *(1 mark)*

**Justification for the 60–90 J/mm³ VED Window for Inconel 718:**

The Volumetric Energy Density $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ must be controlled within **60 – 90 J/mm³** for the following reasons:

- **Below 60 J/mm³ (lack-of-fusion regime):** Insufficient energy causes unmelted powder particles to remain trapped between scan tracks, creating **lack-of-fusion porosity** and delamination defects. The relative density falls below **97%**, and fatigue life drops by an order of magnitude. *(1 mark)*
- **Above 90 J/mm³ (keyholing regime):** Excessive energy causes localized vaporization of the alloy, creating a vapor-depression cavity (keyhole). When this cavity collapses, it traps gas bubbles, producing **keyhole porosity** and rough surfaces. The rapid cooling also induces micro-cracks. *(1 mark)*
- **Within 60 – 90 J/mm³ (conduction mode):** The melt pool is stable, energy transfer occurs primarily by **thermal conduction** rather than vaporization, yielding > **99.5% relative density** with minimal defects — the certification requirement for aerospace components. *(0.5 mark)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - Students often **forget to mention the ASTM/ISO standard** (F3055 for SLM, F3091 for DMLS) when discussing aerospace applications — losing 1 mark.
> - In numerical VED problems, **unit conversion errors** (e.g., mixing µm and mm) are the most common mistake. Always convert µm → mm *before* substituting.
> - Do **not** confuse **SLS (polymer)** with **DMLS (metal)** in the answer. Many students incorrectly attribute the LEAP nozzle to SLS.
> - Mentioning **HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing)** as a mandatory post-processing step for aerospace parts earns a bonus mark and demonstrates depth.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [Alternative Choice for Internal Option]**

**(a) Discuss the applications of PBF processes in the medical and dental fields. Compare SLM and SLS for implant manufacturing with respect to material, biocompatibility, surface finish, and mechanical strength. (7 marks)**

**(b) Compute the Volumetric Energy Density (VED) for a Ti-6Al-4V spinal cage built by SLM with the following parameters: P = 280 W, v = 800 mm/s, h = 0.1 mm, t = 0.03 mm. Comment on the quality of the resulting part with reference to the recommended VED window of 60 – 120 J/mm³ for Ti-6Al-4V. (7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a) — 7 marks:**

**Applications of PBF in Medical and Dental Fields:**

1. **Orthopedic Implants:** Patient-specific hip, knee, and spinal cages (Ti-6Al-4V ELI) — built by SLM from CT data.
2. **Cranial & Maxillofacial Implants:** Custom skull plates for trauma/defect reconstruction.
3. **Dental Restorations:** Co-Cr crowns, bridges, and removable partial denture frameworks (SLM).
4. **Surgical Guides:** SLS-printed PA12 drill guides for implantology.
5. **Hearing Aids:** SLS of PA12 (a market that has been 100% PBF-produced since 2017).

**Comparison Table — SLM vs SLS for Implant Manufacturing:**

| Parameter | **SLM** | **SLS** |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| Material | **Ti-6Al-4V ELI**, **Co-Cr**, **316L SS** | **Polyamide 12 (PA12)**, PEEK |
| Biocompatibility | Excellent (metallic, long-term implant) | Good for short-term / external use only |
| Surface Finish (as-built) | Ra 5 – 15 µm | Ra 10 – 20 µm |
| Mechanical Strength | UTS 900 – 1100 MPa (Ti-6Al-4V) | UTS 45 – 50 MPa (PA12) — not load-bearing |
| Application | Load-bearing implants | Surgical guides, anatomical models |
| Cost per part | High (metal powder, inert gas) | Low (polymer powder) |
| Post-processing | Stress relief, HIP, CNC, polishing | Bead blasting, dyeing, autoclave sterilization |

*(Allocate ~1 mark per row × 5 rows = 5 marks, plus 2 marks for applications discussion.)*

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b) — 7 marks:**

**Given:**
- $P = 280$ W
- $v = 800$ mm/s
- $h = 0.1$ mm
- $t = 0.03$ mm

**Step 1:** Apply the VED formula.

$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the values.

$$
E_v = \dfrac{280}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the denominator.

$$
\begin{aligned}
v \cdot h \cdot t &= 800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03 \\[4pt]
&= 800 \times 0.003 \\[4pt]
&= 2.4 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4:** Final calculation.

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \dfrac{280}{2.4} \\[4pt]
&\approx 116.67 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5: Quality Comment:**

The calculated VED of **116.67 J/mm³** is at the **upper boundary** of the recommended Ti-6Al-4V processing window (60 – 120 J/mm³). The part is likely to:

- Be **fully dense** (> 99%), as energy is sufficient for complete melting.
- Be **at risk of mild keyholing** because the energy approaches the upper limit. A small reduction in laser power (e.g., to 250 W) or an increase in scan speed to 900 mm/s would shift VED to ~**93 J/mm³**, providing a safer processing margin for a load-bearing spinal cage. *(3 marks for numerical work + 2 marks for comment + 1 mark for recommendation + 1 mark for units and unit consistency.)*

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-minute revision bullets — print and memorize:**

- [ ] **PBF = Powder Bed Fusion**, an ISO/ASTM 52900 category; sub-processes: **SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM, MJF**.
- [ ] **VED formula:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — units must be **W, mm/s, mm, mm** → result in **J/mm³**.
- [ ] **VED too low** ⇒ lack-of-fusion porosity; **VED too high** ⇒ keyholing porosity. Always state this trade-off.
- [ ] **SLS** uses **polymers** (PA12, PA11, TPU); **SLM/DMLS** use **metals**; **EBM** uses **metals in vacuum**; **MJF** uses **fusing agent + IR lamp** on polymers.
- [ ] **Aerospace flagship application:** LEAP engine fuel nozzle (Inconel 718, DMLS, 18 parts → 1 part, 25% weight ↓).
- [ ] **Medical flagship application:** Patient-specific Ti-6Al-4V cranial/spinal implants via SLM.
- [ ] **Dental flagship application:** Co-Cr crowns and bridges via SLM.
- [ ] **Tooling flagship application:** Conformal cooling channels in injection mold inserts (cycle time ↓ 40%).
- [ ] **Defence application:** On-demand field replacement of legacy parts.
- [ ] **Jewelry application:** Lost-wax casting patterns (SLS castable polyamide/wax).
- [ ] **MJF advantage over SLS:** Better surface finish, higher throughput, watertight parts.
- [ ] **EBM advantage over SLM:** Faster, larger builds, no oxygen contamination, but rougher surface.
- [ ] **Oxygen limits:** Ti-6Al-4V < 1000 ppm; reactive metals < 100 ppm; build chamber flooded with **Ar (99.999%)**.
- [ ] **Post-processing for aerospace:** **Stress relief → HIP → CNC finishing → CT inspection** (mandatory).
- [ ] **Relative density target for aerospace:** **> 99.5%** (achievable only with optimized VED + HIP).
- [ ] **Always cite the relevant ASTM standard** where applicable: F3055 (SLM), F3091 (DMLS), F3301 (PBF general).
- [ ] **Number to memorize for 3-mark questions:** Typical VED windows — Ti-6Al-4V (60–120), Inconel 718 (55–90), 316L SS (50–100), AlSi10Mg (30–70) J/mm³.
- [ ] **Cooling rate for SLM:** $10^5$ – $10^6$ °C/s (amorphous / fine α′ martensite in Ti).
- [ ] **Unsintered powder** acts as **inherent support** — eliminates the need for dedicated support structures in most SLS/SLM parts (except SLM overhangs > 45°).
- [ ] **Powder reusability:** Up to **80 – 95%** for polymers; lower (60–80%) for reactive metals due to oxidation/aging.
- [ ] **Industry acronyms you must know:** **OEM** (Original Equipment Manufacturer), **HIP** (Hot Isostatic Pressing), **UTS** (Ultimate Tensile Strength), **VED** (Volumetric Energy Density), **AM** (Additive Manufacturing).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Extrusion-Based Systems: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Extrusion-Based Systems: Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM)** is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing (AM) process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier, melted to a semi-liquid state, and selectively extruded through a computer-controlled nozzle to build a three-dimensional part layer by layer on a build platform. FDM was invented by **Scott Crump** in **1988** and subsequently commercialized by **Stratasys Inc.** The open-source equivalent, **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)**, was popularized after the expiration of the original FDM patents and forms the basis of most desktop 3D printers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** FDM is classified under **ASTM/ISO 52900 Category "Material Extrusion"** — the most widely used AM process class globally, holding the largest installed base among all polymer AM technologies.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hot Glue Gun That Draws in 3D"

Imagine holding a **hot glue gun** in your hand. As you squeeze the trigger, melted glue oozes out and instantly hardens on whatever surface it touches. Now imagine that:

1. The glue gun moves in the **X and Y directions** (left–right and forward–backward) on its own, drawing a precise outline.
2. Every time the outline finishes, the surface **drops down a tiny bit** (in the **Z direction**), and a **new layer is drawn on top of the previous one**.
3. The gun repeats this until a full 3D object is built from the **bottom up**.

That is **exactly how FDM works** — except instead of glue, a precisely controlled **thermoplastic filament** is melted and deposited, and instead of a hand, a **computer-controlled gantry or robotic arm** guides the nozzle along paths calculated by **slicing software** (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D, etc.).

A second powerful analogy is that of a **cake decorator piping icing in concentric layers** to form a 3D cake sculpture — each layer is thinner than the one before's outline, and they bond as they cool.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics to Memorize:**
> - **Standard Filament Diameter:** **1.75 mm** (most common) or **2.85 mm** (legacy Stratasys).
> - **Typical Nozzle Diameter:** **0.2 mm – 1.0 mm** (0.4 mm is industry default).
> - **Typical Layer Height:** **0.05 mm – 0.4 mm**.
> - **Road (Bead) Width:** Usually **1.1 × to 1.5 ×** the nozzle diameter.
> - **Nozzle Temperature Range:** **180 °C – 300 °C** (material dependent).
> - **Build Platform Temperature:** **25 °C – 110 °C** (material dependent).

### 1.3 Position Within the AM Process Classification

```
Material Extrusion (ISO/ASTM 52900)
        │
        ├── Filament-based    → FDM / FFF      (covered in depth)
        ├── Pellet/Granule    → FGF (Fused Granulate Fabrication)
        ├── Paste-based       → Robocasting / DIW (Direct Ink Writing)
        └── Slurry-based      → 3D Concrete Printing, Ceramic Extrusion
```

> [!NOTE]
> All material extrusion processes share the same fundamental physics: a **viscous or viscoelastic material is pushed through a die, deposited as a bead, and bonds to previously deposited material** — what differs is the material form (filament, pellet, paste) and the dispensing mechanism (screw, plunger, or pinch-roller extruder).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Working Principle — Step by Step

The FDM process follows a rigorously sequenced workflow. Each step must be executed correctly for dimensional accuracy and part quality.

**Step 1 — Digital Input (CAD → STL)**
- A **3D solid model** is created in CAD software (SolidWorks, Fusion 360, CATIA, etc.).
- The model is exported as an **STL (Standard Tessellation Language)** or **3MF** file — a triangulated surface mesh.
- *Why:* The printer controller only understands geometry as a faceted mesh, not a smooth B-rep solid.

**Step 2 — Slicing (STL → G-code)**
- The mesh is imported into a **slicer** (Cura, PrusaSlicer, IdeaMaker).
- The slicer virtually cuts the model into **horizontal cross-sections** of thickness equal to the chosen **layer height (h)**.
- For each layer, it generates a **toolpath** comprising:
  - **Perimeter (outer wall)** paths,
  - **Infill** paths (internal criss-cross pattern),
  - **Travel moves** (non-deposition jumps),
  - **Support structures** (for overhangs > ~45°).
- The output is **G-code** — a text file of machine instructions (e.g., `G1 X10 Y20 E0.5 F1500`).

**Step 3 — Machine Setup & Pre-Heating**
- The **nozzle (liquefier)** is heated to the material's specified **extrusion temperature** (e.g., 210 °C for PLA, 240 °C for ABS).
- The **build platform** is heated to the specified **bed temperature** (e.g., 60 °C for PLA, 100 °C for ABS) to prevent warping.

**Step 4 — Filament Feeding**
- A thermoplastic **filament** from a spool is driven into the **liquefier** by a **drive mechanism** — typically a pair of **counter-rotating knurled/hobbed gears** driven by a **stepper motor** (or a direct-drive extruder).
- *Why:* Continuous feeding ensures a constant hydrostatic pressure pushes the melt through the nozzle.

**Step 5 — Melting & Extrusion**
- Inside the **liquefier** (a heated metal block with a narrow internal channel, typically **30–40 mm long, 1.8–2.0 mm diameter**), the filament melts due to conduction from the surrounding heater cartridge.
- The molten polymer is extruded through a **brass (or hardened steel, ruby, or tungsten carbide) nozzle** of diameter **d_n**, typically 0.4 mm.

**Step 6 — Controlled Deposition**
- The nozzle (or build platform) moves in **X-Y** with a programmed **print speed (v)**.
- The extruded bead — called a **"road" or "track"** — has a **width (w)** and **height (h)**.
- The bead bonds to the previously deposited material through **thermal welding** — the heat of the freshly extruded polymer partially re-melts the surface of the previous layer, creating a fused joint.

**Step 7 — Layer Completion & Z-Step**
- When a layer is finished, the **build platform lowers by one layer height (h)** in the Z direction — **or** the nozzle rises by h, depending on machine architecture (bed-slinger vs. core XY vs. delta).
- *Why:* This brings the new layer's starting plane to a precise offset, ready for the next deposition.

**Step 8 — Repetition Until Part Completion**
- Steps 5–7 are repeated until all N layers are deposited.

**Step 9 — Cooling, Support Removal & Post-Processing**
- The part is allowed to cool on the platform (active cooling fan may assist).
- **Support structures** are mechanically removed (snapped, broken, or dissolved in water for soluble supports like PVA/HIPS).
- Optional post-processing: **sanding, painting, vapor smoothing, epoxy coating, or annealing**.

### 2.2 The Core Physics: Why the Bead Bonds to the Layer Below

The bond strength between adjacent roads and layers is governed by:

1. **Thermal Fusion (Welding)**: The newly extruded polymer at temperature **T_extrude** is in contact with the previously deposited layer at temperature **T_previous**. If **T_previous > T_softening** (glass transition T_g for amorphous polymers; melting T_m for semi-crystalline polymers), a diffusion weld forms. This is governed by the **reptation theory of polymer welding** (de Gennes, Wool).
2. **Squeeze Flow**: As the new bead is deposited on top of the previous one, the hydrostatic pressure of extrusion plus the weight of the nozzle causes the bead to **partially flatten**, increasing contact area and improving adhesion.
3. **Surface Energy**: Clean, freshly extruded surfaces have high surface energy, promoting molecular entanglement across the interface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Exam Question:** *"Why does FDM produce anisotropic parts?"*
> **Answer:** Because the inter-layer bond is weaker than the intra-layer bond (roads within the same layer are welded together in the molten state, but the inter-layer weld depends on reheating the previously cooled surface to only a fraction of its melt state). Hence, **Z-direction strength is typically 30–70 % of X-Y strength**.

### 2.3 Process Parameters That Govern Part Quality

| Category | Parameter | Typical Range | Effect on Quality |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Thermal** | Nozzle temperature (T_n) | 180–300 °C | Higher → better bonding, more stringing, possible degradation |
| **Thermal** | Bed temperature (T_b) | 25–110 °C | Higher → reduces warping, improves first-layer adhesion |
| **Thermal** | Chamber/Enclosure temp | 25–90 °C | Higher → reduces warping (critical for ABS, PC, Nylon) |
| **Kinematic** | Print speed (v) | 30–150 mm/s | Higher → faster but lower quality, possible under-extrusion |
| **Geometric** | Layer height (h) | 0.05–0.4 mm | Lower → smoother surface, longer build time |
| **Geometric** | Road width (w) | 0.2–1.0 mm | Wider → faster build, lower resolution |
| **Geometric** | Nozzle diameter (d_n) | 0.2–1.0 mm | Smaller → higher resolution, slower |
| **Material** | Infill density | 0–100 % | Higher → stronger, heavier, more material |
| **Material** | Infill pattern | Grid, Gyroid, Honeycomb, Triangular | Affects strength-to-weight ratio & print time |
| **Process** | Cooling fan speed | 0–100 % | Higher → better surface, but may cause warping |
| **Process** | Retraction distance/speed | 0.5–5 mm / 25–60 mm/s | Reduces stringing and oozing |

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Critical for Numerical Problems)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **⚠ LaTeX Note for Markdown Tables:** The vertical pipe `\vert` symbol has been used in place of `\mid` to denote absolute value, ensuring table syntax is not broken.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Filament Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q_f = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f$ | $d_f$ = filament diameter [mm], $v_f$ = filament feed rate [mm/s] | Cross-sectional area × linear speed = volumetric throughput |
| 2 | **Deposited Bead Volumetric Flow Rate** | $Q_b = v \cdot w \cdot h$ | $v$ = print head speed [mm/s], $w$ = road width [mm], $h$ = layer height [mm] | Volume deposited per unit time at the nozzle |
| 3 | **Filament Feed Rate (Derived)** | $v_f = \dfrac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}$ | Same as above | Used to set stepper motor E-axis feed rate in G-code |
| 4 | **Mass Flow Rate** | $\dot{m} = \rho\, Q$ | $\rho$ = polymer density [g/cm³], $Q$ = volumetric flow [mm³/s] | Material consumption rate |
| 5 | **Total Number of Layers** | $N = \dfrac{H}{h}$ | $H$ = total part height [mm], $h$ = layer height [mm] | Determines build duration |
| 6 | **Theoretical Build Time (Approx.)** | $T_{build} = \dfrac{N \cdot L_{path}}{v} = \dfrac{H \cdot L_{path}}{h \cdot v}$ | $L_{path}$ = average path length per layer [mm] | Quick estimation for project planning |
| 7 | **Road Width – Nozzle Diameter Ratio** | $w \approx (1.1 \text{ to } 1.5)\, d_n$ | $d_n$ = nozzle diameter [mm] | Empirical design rule for slicer settings |
| 8 | **Volumetric Throughput Constraint** | $Q_{max} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\, d_n^{\,2}\, v_{max,\,melt}$ | $v_{max,melt}$ = max melt velocity in nozzle | Sets upper limit on print speed before under-extrusion |
| 9 | **Effective Part Volume (with infill)** | $V_{eff} = V_{shell} + \phi \cdot V_{core}$ | $\phi$ = infill fraction (0–1) | Material cost estimation |
| 10 | **Material Mass Required** | $m = \rho \cdot V_{eff} + m_{supports}$ | $m_{supports}$ = support mass | Total filament mass per part |

### 2.5 Thermoplastic Materials Commonly Used in FDM

| Material | Glass Transition T_g | Melt Temp T_m | Nozzle Temp | Bed Temp | Key Property |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLA** (Polylactic Acid) | ~60 °C | ~180 °C | 190–220 °C | 50–60 °C | Biodegradable, easy to print, low warping |
| **ABS** (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | ~105 °C | ~220 °C | 220–250 °C | 95–110 °C | Tough, heat-resistant; needs enclosure (warps) |
| **PETG** (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol) | ~80 °C | ~230 °C | 220–250 °C | 70–85 °C | Tough, chemical-resistant, low warping |
| **TPU** (Thermoplastic Polyurethane) | varies | ~220 °C | 210–230 °C | 40–60 °C | Flexible elastomer (Shore A 85–95) |
| **Nylon (PA12/PA6)** | ~50 °C | ~220 °C | 240–270 °C | 80–100 °C | Strong, tough, hygroscopic — must be dried |
| **PC** (Polycarbonate) | ~147 °C | ~270 °C | 260–300 °C | 100–115 °C | Very strong, transparent; needs enclosed heated chamber |
| **PEEK** (Polyetheretherketone) | ~143 °C | ~343 °C | 360–400 °C | 120–160 °C | High-performance thermoplastic; needs industrial machine |
| **PVA** (Polyvinyl Alcohol) | ~60 °C | ~190 °C | 180–210 °C | 50–60 °C | Water-soluble — used for support structures |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of FDM:**
> FDM is the **workhorse of rapid prototyping** in mechanical, automotive, aerospace, and biomedical engineering. It is extensively used for:
> - **Concept models** and design verification,
> - **Jigs, fixtures, and tooling** (low-volume manufacturing aids),
> - **Investment casting patterns** (e.g., lost-wax burnout),
> - **Patient-specific anatomical models** (CT/MRI-based surgical planning),
> - **Architectural mock-ups,**
> - **Functional end-use parts** (in low-volume / customized production).

### 2.6 Advantages and Limitations of FDM

**Advantages:**
- Low capital cost (desktop machines from ~₹15,000 / $200).
- Wide material range (commodity to engineering thermoplastics).
- Minimal post-processing for simple parts.
- Relatively simple workflow — accessible to non-experts.
- Suitable for office environments (low noise, low fume with PLA).
- Minimal material wastage (no support on flat overhangs; unused filament reusable).

**Limitations:**
- **Anisotropic mechanical properties** (weak Z-direction).
- **Layer-line surface finish** (visible stair-stepping).
- **Slow build times** for large parts.
- **Limited part size** (constrained by build volume).
- **Support structures required** for overhangs > ~45° (waste material + post-processing).
- **Dimensional accuracy** typically ±0.2 mm (poorer than SLA or SLS).
- **No true multi-material capability** in basic systems (advanced machines have dual nozzles).
- **Hygroscopic materials** (Nylon, PC) must be dried before printing.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Fundamental Relationship Between Filament Feed Rate and Print Parameters

This is the **single most important derivation** in FDM and is regularly asked in KTU examinations. It is derived from **conservation of mass (continuity equation)** applied to an incompressible polymer melt.

**Given:**
- Filament diameter: $d_f$
- Filament feed rate (linear velocity at which filament enters liquefier): $v_f$
- Print head speed: $v$
- Road (bead) width at nozzle exit: $w$
- Layer height: $h$

**Step 1 — Volumetric flow rate of the solid filament entering the liquefier.**

The cross-sectional area of the filament (circular) is:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}
$$

The volumetric flow rate of the filament into the system is:

$$
Q_f = A_f \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f
$$

*Textual Logic:* We multiply the cross-sectional area of the cylindrical filament by the linear speed at which it advances — this gives the volume of material entering the heater per second.

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of the extruded bead leaving the nozzle.**

The cross-section of the deposited road is approximately **rectangular** with width $w$ and height $h$ (this is the standard engineering assumption, though in reality the road cross-section is a slightly rounded "loaf" shape).

$$
A_b = w \cdot h
$$

The volumetric flow rate of the deposited bead is:

$$
Q_b = A_b \cdot v = v \cdot w \cdot h
$$

*Textual Logic:* As the print head moves at speed $v$, in one second it sweeps out a rectangular strip of length $v$, width $w$, and thickness $h$. The volume of polymer deposited per second is the area times the length.

**Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation).**

Assuming the polymer melt is **incompressible** (a valid assumption for polymer melts above their melting point) and that there is **no leakage or back-flow**, the volume entering the liquefier per second must equal the volume leaving the nozzle per second:

$$
Q_f = Q_b
$$

Substituting:

$$
\frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f = v \cdot w \cdot h
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the filament feed rate $v_f$.**

$$
v_f = \frac{v \cdot w \cdot h}{\frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}} = \frac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}
$$

**Final Result:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_f = \frac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Interpretation:**
> - If $v_f$ is set **lower** than this value → **under-extrusion** (gaps, weak parts, holes in top surfaces).
> - If $v_f$ is set **higher** than this value → **over-extrusion** (blobs, poor dimensional accuracy, clogged nozzle).
> - This equation is the **physical foundation of the E (extruder) axis** values in every FDM G-code file.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Throughput Limit of the Nozzle (Maximum Print Speed)

The polymer melt cannot flow through the nozzle faster than the **melt flow physics** allows. The maximum safe velocity inside the nozzle channel is governed by the melt's rheology.

For a **Newtonian approximation** of the polymer melt, the Hagen-Poiseuille equation gives the volumetric flow rate through a cylindrical channel of length $L_n$ and diameter $d_n$ under pressure drop $\Delta P$:

$$
Q_{max} = \frac{\pi\, d_n^{\,4}\, \Delta P}{128\, \mu\, L_n}
$$

Setting this equal to the deposition flow rate:

$$
v \cdot w \cdot h \le Q_{max}
$$

Solving for the **maximum allowable print speed**:

$$
v_{max} = \frac{\pi\, d_n^{\,4}\, \Delta P}{128\, \mu\, L_n\, w\, h}
$$

*Textual Logic:* Above $v_{max}$, the extruder cannot supply material fast enough, the upstream pressure in the liquefier drops, the melt flow becomes non-uniform, and **under-extrusion** occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical Insight:** In real FDM machines, $v_{max}$ is typically limited by the **stepper motor torque** and the **drive gear grip** on the filament, not by melt rheology. The mechanical limit is usually reached well before the rheological limit.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Build Time Estimation

The total build time of a simple prismatic part is approximated as:

**Step 1 — Number of layers:**

$$
N = \frac{H}{h}
$$

*where $H$ = total part height in Z, $h$ = layer height.*

**Step 2 — Path length per layer:**

For a solid rectangular part of dimensions $L \times W$ with perimeter walls and infill:

$$
L_{path} = L_{perimeter} + L_{infill}
$$

A simple approximation (ignoring acceleration/deceleration losses):

$$
L_{path} \approx 2(L + W) + \phi \cdot L \cdot W \cdot k_{infill}
$$

*where $\phi$ = infill density fraction, $k_{infill}$ = a pattern-dependent constant (e.g., $1/2$ for a 45° grid, $1/\sqrt{3}$ for triangular).*

**Step 3 — Total build time:**

$$
T_{build} = N \cdot \frac{L_{path}}{v} = \frac{H \cdot L_{path}}{h \cdot v}
$$

*Textual Logic:* We multiply the time per layer (path length ÷ speed) by the number of layers.

**Final boxed form:**

$$
\boxed{\,T_{build} = \frac{H \cdot L_{path}}{h \cdot v}\,}
$$

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam-Style)

**Problem:**
> An FDM system uses a filament of diameter **1.75 mm**, road width **0.45 mm**, layer height **0.2 mm**, and print speed **50 mm/s**. Calculate:
> 1. The filament feed rate in mm/s.
> 2. The mass flow rate in g/s, given polymer density $\rho = 1.24$ g/cm³ (PLA).
> 3. The volumetric flow rate in mm³/s.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Filament feed rate:**

Using the derived formula:

$$
v_f = \frac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}
$$

Substituting values (all in mm and s):

$$
v_f = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 0.45 \times 0.2}{\pi \times (1.75)^2}
$$

Evaluating the numerator:

$$
4 \times 50 \times 0.45 \times 0.2 = 18.0 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

Evaluating the denominator:

$$
\pi \times (1.75)^2 = \pi \times 3.0625 = 9.6211 \text{ mm}^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_f = \frac{18.0}{9.6211} = 1.871 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate:**

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f = \frac{\pi}{4} \times (1.75)^2 \times 1.871
$$

$$
Q = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 3.0625 \times 1.871 = 4.500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

*Cross-check using the deposition formula:*
$Q = v \cdot w \cdot h = 50 \times 0.45 \times 0.2 = 4.5$ mm³/s ✓ (matches)

**Step 3 — Mass flow rate:**

Convert density to consistent units: $\rho = 1.24 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 1.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$

$$
\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q = 1.24 \times 10^{-3} \times 4.5 = 5.58 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}
$$

Convert to grams per hour for a practical figure:

$$
\dot{m} = 5.58 \times 10^{-3} \times 3600 \approx 20.09 \text{ g/hr}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answers:**
> 1. $v_f \approx \mathbf{1.871 \text{ mm/s}}$ (or equivalently $\approx 112.3 \text{ mm/min}$)
> 2. $Q = \mathbf{4.5 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}}$
> 3. $\dot{m} \approx \mathbf{5.58 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/s}} \approx 20.1 \text{ g/hr}$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Diagram A — FDM Process Flowchart (End-to-End Workflow)

This flowchart captures every operational step from CAD model to finished part, including the layer-by-layer deposition loop that is the defining feature of FDM.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Create 3D CAD Model] --> A2[Step 2: Export as STL or 3MF Mesh File]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Import into Slicer Software]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Configure Layer Height, Infill, Speed, Supports]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Generate G-code Toolpath File]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Heat Nozzle and Build Platform to Setpoint Temperatures]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Drive Filament into Liquefier via Extruder Gears]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8: Filament Melts in Heated Liquefier Block]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9: Molten Polymer Extrudes Through Nozzle]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10: Print Head Moves in X-Y to Deposit Road on Build Platform]
    A10 --> A11{Step 11: Is Current Layer Complete?}
    A11 -- No --> A9
    A11 -- Yes --> A12[Step 12: Lower Build Platform by One Layer Height h in Z]
    A12 --> A13{Step 13: Are All N Layers Deposited?}
    A13 -- No --> A9
    A13 -- Yes --> A14[Step 14: Cool Part and Detach from Build Platform]
    A14 --> A15[Step 15: Remove Support Structures Manually or by Dissolution]
    A15 --> A16[Step 16: Optional Post-Processing: Sand, Paint, Anneal, Vapor Smooth]
    A16 --> NodeFinish[Final Part Ready]
```

### 4.2 Diagram B — FDM System Block Architecture (Functional Components & Interconnections)

This block diagram shows the **hardware subsystems** of a typical FDM machine and the **control and material flow** between them.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Filament Spool Holder] --> B2[Filament Drive Mechanism: Hobbed Gears on Stepper Motor]
    B2 --> B3[Heated Liquefier Block: Heater Cartridge plus Thermistor]
    B3 --> B4[Brass or Hardened Steel Nozzle: Diameter 0.2 to 1.0 mm]
    B4 --> B5[Build Platform: Heated Bed with PEI or Glass Surface]
    B5 --> B6[X Y Z Motion System: Stepper Motors with Timing Belts or Lead Screws]
    B6 --> B4
    B7[Mainboard Controller: Marlin or Klipper Firmware] --> B2
    B7 --> B3
    B7 --> B5
    B7 --> B6
    B8[Enclosure: Optional Heated Chamber] -.-> B3
    B8 -.-> B5
    B9[Part Cooling Fan] --> B4
    B10[User Interface: LCD Screen or Web Interface] --> B7
    B11[Host Computer Running Slicer] --> B7
```

### 4.3 Diagram C — The Three Coordinate Axes of FDM (Road, Layer, Build Direction)

A schematic block representation of how the three fundamental geometric parameters (road width $w$, layer height $h$, and part height $H$) interact in the FDM deposition scheme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Print Head Trajectory in X Y Plane] --> C2[Deposited Road of Width w and Height h]
    C2 --> C3[Adjacent Roads Laid Side by Side with Small Overlap]
    C3 --> C4[One Complete Horizontal Layer]
    C4 --> C5[Build Platform Descends by h in Z Axis]
    C5 --> C6[Next Layer Deposited on Top]
    C6 --> C7{More Layers Needed?}
    C7 -- Yes --> C5
    C7 -- No --> C8[Final Solid 3D Part of Total Height H]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams in Examinations:** When KTU asks for a "neat sketch" or "block diagram" of the FDM process, any of the three diagrams above is acceptable. **Diagram B (block architecture)** is the most commonly drawn and scored answer.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam Pattern (PEPOT634):** Answer **both** Part A questions. Each carries **3 marks**. Total for Part A: 6 marks. Focus: direct recall, short definitions, and one-line comparisons.

---

**Part A — Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**
**Define Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM). List any four thermoplastic materials commonly used in FDM.**

**Model Answer:**

Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) is a material extrusion-based additive manufacturing process in which a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated liquefier, melted, and selectively extruded through a computer-controlled nozzle to build a three-dimensional part layer by layer on a build platform.

**Four thermoplastic materials used in FDM:**

1. **PLA (Polylactic Acid)** — biodegradable, easy to print, low warping.
2. **ABS (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene)** — tough, heat-resistant; requires heated bed and enclosed chamber.
3. **PETG (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol)** — tough, chemical-resistant, low warping.
4. **TPU (Thermoplastic Polyurethane)** — flexible elastomer for rubber-like parts.

*(Acceptable alternatives: PC, Nylon (PA12/PA6), PEEK, PVA, HIPS.)*

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4 materials (0.5 each).

---

**Part A — Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**
**Compare FDM and SLA (Stereolithography) in terms of (a) raw material, (b) layer bonding mechanism, and (c) one key limitation of FDM.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | FDM | SLA |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Raw material** | Thermoplastic filament (solid) | Photopolymer liquid resin |
| **(b) Layer bonding mechanism** | Thermal welding (re-melting of previous layer surface) | Photopolymerization (UV/laser curing of resin) |
| **(c) Key FDM limitation** | Anisotropic mechanical properties (weaker Z-direction strength) | — |

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark each for (a), (b), and (c) — total 3 marks.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module 2 Internal Choice

> **KTU Pattern:** Answer **any ONE** of the two Part B questions. Each carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Provide sub-part answers with diagrams where requested.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — July 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat block diagram**, explain the working principle of the Fused Deposition Modelling (FDM) process. List **six advantages** and **four limitations** of FDM. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Discuss the **various process parameters** that affect the quality of FDM parts. Group them into **thermal, kinematic, geometric, and material** categories with typical values. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part B, Q.A (a)**

**Working Principle of FDM:**

The FDM process works on the principle of **selective material extrusion of a thermoplastic melt**. A continuous filament of thermoplastic material is fed from a spool into a **heated liquefier** by a **drive mechanism** (hobbed gears coupled to a stepper motor). Inside the liquefier, the filament melts to a semi-liquid state. The molten polymer is then extruded through a fine **nozzle** of diameter 0.2–1.0 mm and deposited as a thin bead (called a **road**) on a **build platform**. The nozzle traverses in the X-Y plane under computer control, following a path generated by a **slicing software**. After each layer is completed, the build platform descends by one **layer height (h)** in the Z direction, and a new layer is deposited on top of the previous one. The bonding between successive layers occurs through **thermal welding** — the heat of the freshly extruded bead partially re-melts the surface of the previous layer, creating a fused joint. This cycle is repeated until the entire 3D part is built.

*(Insert Diagram B from Section 4.2 — FDM System Block Architecture.)*

**Six Advantages of FDM:**
1. Low capital cost and widely accessible.
2. Wide range of thermoplastic materials available.
3. Minimal material wastage; unused filament is reusable.
4. Simple workflow; minimal training required.
5. Suitable for office environments (low noise, low fume with PLA).
6. Good for functional prototypes and end-use low-volume parts.

**Four Limitations of FDM:**
1. **Anisotropic mechanical properties** — Z-direction strength is significantly lower than X-Y.
2. **Visible layer lines** on the surface finish (stair-stepping).
3. **Support structures** required for overhangs greater than ~45°, leading to material wastage and post-processing.
4. **Slow build times** for large or highly detailed parts.

**Mark Distribution:** 3 marks for working principle + 1 mark for diagram + 1.5 marks for advantages + 1.5 marks for limitations.

#### **Model Solution — Part B, Q.A (b)**

**Process Parameters Affecting FDM Part Quality:**

**1. Thermal Parameters:**
- **Nozzle temperature (T_n)**: 180–300 °C. Higher temperatures improve inter-layer bonding but may cause stringing, oozing, and polymer degradation.
- **Build platform temperature (T_b)**: 25–110 °C. Higher bed temperatures reduce warping, especially for ABS, PC, and Nylon.
- **Enclosure/Chamber temperature**: 25–90 °C. Critical for engineering thermoplastics to prevent differential cooling and warping.
- **Cooling fan speed**: 0–100 %. Active cooling improves surface finish of small features but can cause warping in ABS.

**2. Kinematic Parameters:**
- **Print speed (v)**: 30–150 mm/s. Higher speed reduces build time but degrades quality.
- **Acceleration and jerk settings**: Affect print quality at corners and small features.
- **Retraction distance and speed**: 0.5–5 mm / 25–60 mm/s. Reduces stringing during travel moves.

**3. Geometric Parameters:**
- **Layer height (h)**: 0.05–0.4 mm. Smaller layer heights yield smoother surfaces but increase build time.
- **Road width (w)**: 0.2–1.0 mm. Wider roads give faster builds but lower resolution.
- **Nozzle diameter (d_n)**: 0.2–1.0 mm. Smaller nozzles yield finer detail but slower build.

**4. Material Parameters:**
- **Infill density**: 0–100 %. Higher density improves strength at the cost of material and time.
- **Infill pattern**: Grid, Gyroid, Honeycomb, Triangular. Affects strength-to-weight ratio and print direction.
- **Material type**: PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC — each has different optimal parameters.
- **Number of perimeter walls**: 1–5. More walls improve strength and surface quality.

**Mark Distribution:** 1.75 marks per category (4 categories × 1.75 = 7 marks).

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With the help of a **neat sketch**, explain the **main components of an FDM system** and their functions. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Starting from the **continuity equation**, derive the relationship between the **filament feed rate (v_f)** and the printing parameters (print speed $v$, road width $w$, layer height $h$, filament diameter $d_f$). An FDM system uses a filament of diameter **1.75 mm**, nozzle/road width **0.45 mm**, layer height **0.2 mm**, and print speed **50 mm/s**. Calculate the **filament feed rate in mm/s**. **[7 marks]**

#### **Model Solution — Part B, Q.B (a)**

**Main Components of an FDM System:**

**1. Filament Spool and Feed Mechanism:**
A spool of thermoplastic filament (1.75 mm or 2.85 mm diameter) is mounted on a holder. The filament is fed into the liquefier by a **drive mechanism** consisting of a pair of **counter-rotating hobbed (knurled) gears** driven by a **stepper motor** (extruder motor). This is the "E-axis" of the printer.

**2. Liquefier (Heated Block):**
A metal block (usually aluminum) containing a precisely sized channel through which the filament passes. It is heated by a **heater cartridge** and monitored by a **thermistor**. The temperature is maintained at the material's specified extrusion temperature (e.g., 210 °C for PLA).

**3. Nozzle:**
A small orifice (typically brass, 0.4 mm diameter) at the bottom of the liquefier through which the molten polymer is extruded. Specialized nozzles include **hardened steel** (for abrasive composites), **ruby-tipped** (for maximum wear resistance), and **tungsten carbide** (for high-temperature materials like PEEK).

**4. Build Platform (Heated Bed):**
A flat surface on which the part is built. Usually heated to prevent warping and equipped with a build surface (PEI sheet, glass with adhesive, or BuildTak).

**5. Motion System (X-Y-Z):**
Three orthogonal axes driven by **stepper motors** via **timing belts** (for X-Y) and **lead screws** (for Z). Configurations include Cartesian, CoreXY, and delta architectures.

**6. Controller and Firmware:**
A **mainboard** (e.g., RAMPS, SKR, Duet) running **firmware** (Marlin, Klipper) that interprets G-code and drives the motors, heaters, and fans. Closed-loop control is provided by **thermistors** and **endstops**.

**7. Enclosure (Optional):**
A heated chamber that maintains a stable ambient temperature, critical for engineering thermoplastics like ABS, PC, and Nylon to prevent warping.

**8. Cooling Fan:**
A **part cooling fan** (axial blower) that directs air at the freshly deposited bead to solidify it rapidly, improving overhang quality and surface finish.

*(Insert Diagram B from Section 4.2 — FDM System Block Architecture.)*

**Mark Distribution:** 0.7 marks per component (8 components × 0.7 ≈ 5.6 marks) + 1.4 marks for sketch and organization.

#### **Model Solution — Part B, Q.B (b)**

**Derivation of the Filament Feed Rate Equation:**

**Given:**
- Filament diameter: $d_f$
- Filament feed rate: $v_f$
- Print head speed: $v$
- Road width: $w$
- Layer height: $h$

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the filament:**

The filament is circular in cross-section, so its cross-sectional area is:

$$
A_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of the solid filament:**

Multiplying the cross-sectional area by the linear feed rate gives the volume of material entering the liquefier per second:

$$
Q_f = A_f \cdot v_f = \frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f
$$

**Step 3 — Cross-sectional area of the deposited bead:**

The deposited road is approximately rectangular with width $w$ and height $h$:

$$
A_b = w \cdot h
$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric flow rate of the deposited bead:**

As the print head moves at speed $v$ in the X-Y plane, in one second it sweeps out a rectangular strip of length $v$. The volume of polymer deposited per second is:

$$
Q_b = A_b \cdot v = v \cdot w \cdot h
$$

**Step 5 — Apply the continuity equation (conservation of mass):**

For an incompressible polymer melt with no leakage, the volumetric flow rate entering the liquefier must equal the volumetric flow rate leaving the nozzle:

$$
Q_f = Q_b
$$

$$
\frac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}\, v_f = v \cdot w \cdot h
$$

**Step 6 — Solve for $v_f$:**

$$
v_f = \frac{v \cdot w \cdot h}{\dfrac{\pi}{4}\, d_f^{\,2}} = \frac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Substitute numerical values:**

Given: $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $w = 0.45$ mm, $h = 0.2$ mm, $v = 50$ mm/s.

$$
v_f = \frac{4 \times 50 \times 0.45 \times 0.2}{\pi \times (1.75)^2}
$$

Numerator:

$$
4 \times 50 \times 0.45 \times 0.2 = 18.0
$$

Denominator:

$$
\pi \times (1.75)^2 = \pi \times 3.0625 = 9.6211
$$

Therefore:

$$
v_f = \frac{18.0}{9.6211} \approx 1.871 \text{ mm/s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\,v_f \approx 1.871 \text{ mm/s} \;\; (\text{equivalently } \approx 112.3 \text{ mm/min})\,}
$$

**Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for setting up the problem, 3 marks for derivation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for numerical evaluation, 1 mark for final answer with units.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ Where Students Lose Marks in FDM Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the Filament Diameter Squared:** In the derivation of $v_f$, students often write $d_f$ (not $d_f^{\,2}$) in the denominator. KTU examiners **deduct 2 marks** for this — it is the single most common error.
>
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Numerical Problems:** If you are given $d_f$ in mm, $v$ in mm/s, $w$ in mm, $h$ in mm, then $v_f$ is in **mm/s**. Do not mix cm and mm in the same calculation.
>
> 3. **Confusing "Road Width" with "Nozzle Diameter":** They are **not** the same. Road width is set in the slicer and is typically **1.1× to 1.5× the nozzle diameter**. Using the wrong variable yields incorrect results.
>
> 4. **Omitting the Diagram:** KTU Part B questions explicitly ask for a "neat sketch" or "block diagram." A well-labeled diagram carries **1 to 1.5 marks**. A blank answer with only text loses these marks.
>
> 5. **Writing "FDM is the same as 3D printing":** 3D printing is a **generic term** for all AM processes. FDM is a **specific** material extrusion process. Examiners expect precise terminology.
>
> 6. **Forgetting the Inventor and Year:** KTU values the historical context — credit was originally given to **Scott Crump (1988, Stratasys)**. Mentioning this earns a half-mark bonus in some valuation keys.
>
> 7. **Confusing "extrusion temperature" with "build platform temperature":** Students frequently interchange these. Nozzle T is 180–300 °C; bed T is 25–110 °C. Mixing them up loses 1 mark.
>
> 8. **Skipping the "Why" in working principle questions:** Don't just list steps — explain *why* each step matters (e.g., why the bed is heated: to prevent warping due to differential thermal contraction).

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Read This in the Last 15 Minutes Before the Exam:**

- ☐ **FDM = Material Extrusion** process, ISO/ASTM 52900 category.
- ☐ **Inventor:** Scott Crump, **1988**, **Stratasys** (commercialized).
- ☐ **Raw form:** Thermoplastic filament, **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm** diameter.
- ☐ **Core equation (most-tested formula):** $\;v_f = \dfrac{4\, v\, w\, h}{\pi\, d_f^{\,2}}\;$
- ☐ **Key volumetric flow identity:** $Q_f = Q_b$ (continuity).
- ☐ **Bead cross-section is approximated as a rectangle:** $A_b = w \times h$.
- ☐ **Standard nozzle diameter:** 0.4 mm; **layer height range:** 0.05–0.4 mm.
- ☐ **Road width ≈ 1.1× to 1.5× nozzle diameter.**
- ☐ **Bonding mechanism:** Thermal welding / polymer reptation diffusion.
- ☐ **Z-direction strength < X-Y strength** → FDM parts are **anisotropic**.
- ☐ **Build platform descends by h** after each layer (or nozzle rises by h).
- ☐ **Common thermoplastics:** PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, Nylon, PC, PEEK, PVA.
- ☐ **ABS warps** → needs heated bed **and** enclosed chamber.
- ☐ **Nylon is hygroscopic** → must be **dried** before printing.
- ☐ **PVA is water-soluble** → used as **support material** for soluble-breakaway supports.
- ☐ **Supports required for overhangs** > ~45° from vertical.
- ☐ **Advantages (6):** low cost, material range, low waste, simple workflow, office-safe (PLA), functional parts.
- ☐ **Limitations (4):** anisotropy, layer lines, supports required, slow.
- ☐ **Applications:** rapid prototyping, jigs/fixtures, anatomical models, investment casting patterns, low-volume end-use parts.
- ☐ **Typical tolerance:** ±0.2 mm (worse than SLA/SLS).
- ☐ **The slicer converts** STL/3MF → G-code with layer-by-layer toolpaths.
- ☐ **Build time estimate:** $T_{build} = \dfrac{H \cdot L_{path}}{h \cdot v}$.
- ☐ **Number of layers:** $N = \dfrac{H}{h}$.
- ☐ **Two motion architectures:** Bed-slinger (printer bed moves in Y) and CoreXY (stationary bed, gantry moves in X-Y).
- ☐ **Always draw a labeled block diagram** in working-principle questions — earns 1–1.5 marks.
- ☐ **Examiner's most-tested topics:** (1) Working principle + diagram, (2) Components, (3) Derivation of $v_f$, (4) Numerical calculation using $v_f$ formula, (5) Advantages vs. limitations, (6) Material list with one-line property.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Principles in Additive Manufacturing

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU 2024 Standard Terminology):**
> The **Principles of Additive Manufacturing (AM)** refer to the fundamental physical, chemical, and mechanical mechanisms governing the layer-by-layer conversion of raw material (in liquid, solid, or powder form) into a solid, three-dimensional functional part, governed by controlled energy input and precise spatial deposition according to sliced CAD data.

According to **ASTM F2792 – Standard Terminology for Additive Manufacturing Technologies**, AM principles are categorized based on the **state of the feedstock material** at the point of energy interaction. The three master principles in Module 2 are:

| Master Principle | Material State | Energy Source | Representative Process |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Liquid-Based | Photopolymer / Resin | UV Laser / Projector | Stereolithography (SLA), DLP |
| Solid-Based | Filament / Sheet / Wire | Thermal / Pressure | FDM, Sheet Lamination |
| Powder-Based | Metal / Polymer / Ceramic Powder | Laser / Electron Beam / Binder | SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM, BJ |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plain-English Intuition:**
> Imagine building a sandcastle by adding **one thin horizontal slice of wet sand at a time**, where each slice is shaped by stamping or carving before the next slice is added on top. **Additive Manufacturing works on the exact same logic** — except the "wet sand" is replaced by photopolymer resin, molten plastic, or metal powder, and the "stamp" is replaced by a precisely controlled laser, electron beam, or extrusion nozzle following a digital blueprint.

### The Universal AM Workflow (Applicable to ALL Principles):

1. **Digital Creation:** A 3D CAD model is designed (or reverse-engineered via 3D scanning).
2. **Slicing:** Software (e.g., Cura, Magics) divides the model into hundreds/thousands of **horizontal cross-sectional layers** (typically **0.02 mm to 0.3 mm** thick).
3. **Layer Formation:** According to the chosen principle, material is deposited/cured/fused in the exact 2D shape of the current layer.
4. **Bonding:** The new layer fuses with the previously solidified layer (through polymerization, welding, sintering, or adhesion).
5. **Repetition:** Steps 3–4 repeat until the entire 3D geometry is realized.
6. **Post-Processing:** Support removal, curing, surface finishing, and inspection.

---

## 1.3 Core Operating Vocabulary

| Term | Meaning |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Voxel** | The 3D equivalent of a pixel — the smallest addressable volume element cured/deposited. |
| **Layer Thickness (z-resolution)** | Height of each deposited slice, typically **0.025 mm to 0.5 mm**. |
| **Hatch Spacing** | Distance between two adjacent laser scan lines, usually **0.05 mm to 0.2 mm**. |
| **Recoating** | Spreading a fresh layer of powder or resin before each pass. |
| **Build Volume** | Maximum printable dimensions of the AM machine (e.g., **300 × 300 × 400 mm³**). |

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Principle 1 — Liquid-Based Systems (Vat Photopolymerization)

> [!NOTE]
> **Operating Logic:** A vat of **photopolymer resin** is selectively cured (hardened) point-by-point or layer-by-layer using **UV light**.

### Mechanism Steps:
1. Build platform is positioned at a depth equal to **one layer thickness** below the resin surface.
2. A **UV laser (SLA)** or **projector (DLP)** traces/cures the cross-section.
3. **Photopolymerization** occurs — photo-initiators in the resin absorb UV photons and trigger chain-growth crosslinking of monomers/oligomers.
4. The platform descends by one layer thickness, fresh resin flows in, and the next layer is cured atop the previous one.
5. Process repeats until completion; the part is then **washed in IPA** and **post-cured in UV oven**.

### Governing Photochemistry Equation (Stevenson's Law analogue for AM):

$$
E = P \cdot t
$$

where **E** = Energy density (J/mm²), **P** = Power per unit area (W/mm²), **t** = Exposure time (s).

---

## 2.2 Principle 2 — Solid-Based Systems (Material Extrusion & Sheet Lamination)

### (A) Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) / Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)
- A **thermoplastic filament** (ABS, PLA, PETG, Nylon) is fed into a heated nozzle (**180 °C – 300 °C**).
- The nozzle extrudes a semi-molten bead (**bead width ≈ 0.4 mm**) onto the build platform.
- The bead bonds to the previous layer via **thermal fusion** and **molecular diffusion** at the interface.

### (B) Sheet Lamination (LOM — Laminated Object Manufacturing)
- Sheets of paper, plastic, or metal are bonded layer-by-layer using **adhesive** or **thermal bonding**.
- A laser or blade **trims/cuts** the contour of each layer.
- Used for **paper prototyping, metal-clad laminates, and large sand-casting patterns**.

---

## 2.3 Principle 3 — Powder-Based Systems (Bed Fusion)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This is the most examination-relevant principle for KTU Module 2.**

### Sub-categorization:
| Process | Energy | Material State at Fusion | Full Melting? |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **SLS** (Selective Laser Sintering) | CO₂ Laser | Polymer / Metal | No (sintered) |
| **SLM** (Selective Laser Melting) | Fiber Laser | Metal | Yes (fully molten) |
| **DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) | Fiber Laser | Metal Alloy | Partial melt |
| **EBM** (Electron Beam Melting) | Electron Beam | Metal (Ti, Nb) | Yes |
| **BJ** (Binder Jetting) | Liquid Binder + No Heat | Metal / Sand | No (binder only) |

### Mechanism (Bed Fusion — SLS/SLM):
1. A **recoater blade** spreads a thin powder layer (**20 µm – 100 µm**) across the build platform.
2. A **high-power laser** scans the cross-section, fusing powder particles via **sintering (SLS)** or **full melting (SLM)**.
3. Platform descends by one layer thickness; recoater spreads the next powder layer.
4. Loose, unfused powder acts as **natural support** — no sacrificial support structures required.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formulas for KTU Module 2 Examination**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable To |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_v$ = Volumetric energy density, $P$ = Laser power, $v$ = Scan speed, $h$ = Hatch spacing, $t$ = Layer thickness | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | SLM, DMLS, SLS |
| 2 | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | $E_a$ = Areal energy density | $\text{J/mm}^2$ | All laser-based AM |
| 3 | $T_b = \dfrac{N \cdot t_l}{R_z}$ | $T_b$ = Build time, $N$ = Number of layers, $t_l$ = Layer thickness, $R_z$ = Z-axis resolution | s or h | All AM processes |
| 4 | $N = \dfrac{H}{t_l}$ | $N$ = Number of layers, $H$ = Total part height | dimensionless | All AM |
| 5 | $\rho_{rel} = \dfrac{\rho_{built}}{\rho_{bulk}} \times 100\%$ | Relative density (key quality metric) | % | Powder-bed fusion |
| 6 | $w_{bead} = k \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{Q}{v}}$ | $w_{bead}$ = Bead width, $Q$ = Volumetric flow rate, $v$ = Scan speed | mm | FDM, DED |
| 7 | $C_d = \dfrac{D_{crit}}{D_{nozzle}}$ | $C_d$ = Curl/dimensional factor | dimensionless | FDM |
| 8 | $\eta_{layer} = \dfrac{V_{solid}}{V_{total}}$ | Layer solid fraction | dimensionless | All AM |

### Typical KTU Energy Density Ranges to Memorize:

| Material | Process | Recommended $E_v$ |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | SLM | **60 – 120 J/mm³** |
| **316L Stainless Steel** | SLM | **80 – 150 J/mm³** |
| **Inconel 718** | SLM | **90 – 140 J/mm³** |
| **AlSi10Mg** | SLM | **30 – 70 J/mm³** |
| **PA12 Nylon** | SLS | **0.04 – 0.06 J/mm²** |

### Real-World Engineering Utility:
- **Aerospace:** SLM-printed Ti-6Al-4V turbine blades with internal lattice cooling channels (impossible to machine).
- **Biomedical:** EBM-printed patient-specific titanium hip implants with porous osseointegration surfaces.
- **Automotive:** SLS-printed PA12 functional prototypes and end-use interior brackets.
- **Tooling:** Binder-jetted sand molds for complex investment casting patterns.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density for Powder-Bed Fusion

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU Module 2 — examiners routinely award 4–5 marks on this exact derivation.

### Starting Premise:
The energy delivered by a moving laser over a single scanned track must equal the energy required to fully melt (or sinter) the powder volume beneath that track.

### Given Parameters:
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (mm)

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Define the effective build volume of one hatch line.**
A single scanned line of length $L$, width $h$, and depth $t$ creates a fused volume:

$$
V_{line} = L \cdot h \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Express the time required for the laser to traverse that line.**

$$
\Delta t_{scan} = \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 3 — Express the total energy delivered during that traversal.**

$$
E_{delivered} = P \cdot \Delta t_{scan} = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the energy per unit volume (volumetric energy density).**

$$
E_v = \dfrac{E_{delivered}}{V_{line}} = \dfrac{P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}}{L \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 5 — Cancel the length $L$ from numerator and denominator.**

$$
\boxed{E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}} \quad \text{[units: J/mm}^3\text{]}
$$

### Engineering Interpretation:

| Condition | Effect on Part |
| :--- | :--- |
| $E_v$ too **low** | Lack of fusion → porosity, cracking, delamination |
| $E_v$ too **high** | Keyholing, vaporization, residual stresses, balling |
| $E_v$ **optimal** | > 99.5 % density, fine dendritic microstructure |

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Build Time Estimation

### Given:
A part of total height $H$ (mm), built at layer thickness $t_l$ (mm), with effective vertical motion speed $R_z$ (mm/s).

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Calculate the number of layers.**

$$
N = \dfrac{H}{t_l}
$$

**Step 2 — The total vertical traverse distance is $H$ (the build platform descends from 0 to $H$).**

**Step 3 — The total build time is therefore:**

$$
T_b = \dfrac{N \cdot t_l}{R_z} = \dfrac{H}{R_z}
$$

Wait — a more accurate KTU-friendly version includes the **recoating time** per layer ($t_r$):

$$
\boxed{T_b = \dfrac{N \cdot t_l}{R_z} + N \cdot t_r + T_{scan}}
$$

where $T_{scan}$ = total laser scan time (summed over all layers).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — FDM Bead Width (Volumetric Conservation)

### Given:
- Filament diameter $d_f$ (mm), typically **1.75 mm** or **2.85 mm**
- Filament feed rate $F$ (mm/s)
- Nozzle diameter $d_n$ (mm)
- Print head traverse speed $v$ (mm/s)

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of the incoming filament:**

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_f^{\,2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate of incoming plastic:**

$$
Q_{in} = A_f \cdot F
$$

**Step 3 — Conservation of mass (incompressible melt):**

$$
Q_{in} = Q_{out} \implies A_f \cdot F = w_{bead} \cdot t_l \cdot v
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for bead width:**

$$
\boxed{w_{bead} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_f^{\,2} \cdot F}{4 \cdot t_l \cdot v}}
$$

### Verification (Sanity Check):
For PLA with $d_f = 1.75$ mm, $F = 3$ mm/s, $t_l = 0.2$ mm, $v = 60$ mm/s:

$$
w_{bead} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2 \cdot 3}{4 \cdot 0.2 \cdot 60} = \dfrac{28.86}{48} \approx 0.60 \text{ mm}
$$

This is realistic for a **0.4 mm nozzle** extruding a slightly wider bead (typical over-extrusion ratio ≈ 1.5).

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`**
**Differentiate between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM) based on the principle of powder fusion.**

#### Model Answer:
| Parameter | SLS | SLM |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Fusion State** | Partial sintering — powder particles surface-fuse but remain solid-core | Full melting — powder particles are completely melted into a molten pool |
| **Temperature** | Heated close to melting point (Tm) but not crossing it | Exceeds Tm to form a true liquid weld pool |
| **Energy Density** | Lower ($E_a \approx$ 0.04 – 0.06 J/mm²) | Higher ($E_v \approx$ 60 – 150 J/mm³) |
| **Typical Material** | PA12 Nylon, TPU | Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Inconel |
| **Porosity** | Higher (20 – 35 %) | Lower (< 1 %) |

SLS is a **solid-state diffusion-based bonding**, whereas SLM achieves **true metallurgical fusion**.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`**
**Explain the principle of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). State any two limitations.**

#### Model Answer:
**Principle:** A **thermoplastic filament** is fed into a heated liquefier (180 °C – 300 °C) via a drive gear. The molten polymer is extruded through a nozzle (typical diameter **0.2 – 1.2 mm**) onto the build platform along a toolpath dictated by the slicer. The material solidifies upon cooling and fuses to the previously deposited layer via **thermal bonding and polymer chain interdiffusion**. Layer by layer, the 3D part is constructed.

**Limitations:**
1. **Anisotropic mechanical strength** — Z-direction bond strength is significantly weaker than X-Y due to incomplete inter-layer fusion.
2. **Visible stair-stepping** on curved surfaces; poor surface finish (Ra ≈ 10 – 20 µm) compared to SLA (Ra ≈ 1 µm).
3. **Limited to thermoplastics** — cannot directly print metals/ceramics (composite filaments excepted).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**`[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2]`**

**(a)** Explain the principle of **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** with a neat schematic. Discuss the role of **volumetric energy density** on the resulting part density. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V part is to be manufactured via SLM using the following parameters:
- Laser power $P = 200$ W
- Scan speed $v = 800$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing $h = 0.12$ mm
- Layer thickness $t = 0.03$ mm

Calculate the **volumetric energy density**. State whether the value falls in the optimal window for Ti-6Al-4V. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):
SLM belongs to the **powder-bed fusion** family under AM. The principle involves:

1. A **build chamber** is filled with an inert gas (Argon or Nitrogen) to keep oxygen content **< 50 ppm**, preventing oxidation of reactive metals.
2. A **recoater blade** spreads a uniform layer of metal powder (typically **20 – 60 µm** particle size) over the build platform.
3. A high-power **fiber laser** (typically 200 W – 1 kW, wavelength 1060 – 1080 nm) is steered by **galvanometer scanners** along the cross-section defined by the slicer.
4. The laser fully melts the powder into a **molten weld pool**, which solidifies by rapid conduction into the previously solidified layer (cooling rates **10⁴ – 10⁶ K/s**).
5. Platform descends by one layer thickness; the cycle repeats.

**Schematic (text representation):**

```
    [Fiber Laser 1064 nm]
             ↓
       [Galvanometer XY]
             ↓
   ┌─────────────────────┐
   │   Powder Bed (Ar)   │
   │  ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  │  ← Recoated layer
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │  ← Newly melted layer
   │  ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  │  ← Previous layer
   └─────────────────────┘
          [Build Platform]  ← descends by t
```

**Role of Volumetric Energy Density ($E_v$):**
$$E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
- **Too low $E_v$:** Insufficient melting → **lack of fusion porosity**, balling, layer delamination, cracks.
- **Too high $E_v$:** Excessive melting → **keyholing porosity** (vapor depression), spattering, residual stresses, build failure.
- **Optimal $E_v$:** Densification > **99.5 %**, fine acicular martensitic α′ microstructure in Ti-alloys.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Substitute the given values into the volumetric energy density formula:**

$$
E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 2 — Insert numerical values:**

$$
E_v = \dfrac{200}{800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the denominator:**

$$
800 \times 0.12 \times 0.03 = 800 \times 0.0036 = 2.88
$$

**Step 4 — Final computation:**

$$
\boxed{E_v = \dfrac{200}{2.88} = 69.44 \text{ J/mm}^3}
$$

**Step 5 — Compare with the optimal window:**

The optimal window for Ti-6Al-4V SLM is **60 – 120 J/mm³**.

Since **69.44 J/mm³ lies within this range**, the parameter set is **valid and acceptable** for producing a near-fully-dense Ti-6Al-4V part.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**
**`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 2]`**

**(a)** With a suitable diagram, explain the **principle of Stereolithography (SLA)**. Mention the role of **photo-initiators** in the photopolymer resin. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** An FDM printer extrudes a PLA filament of diameter **1.75 mm** at a feed rate of **4 mm/s**. The nozzle has a diameter of **0.4 mm** and moves at **60 mm/s** with a layer thickness of **0.2 mm**. Determine the **bead width** deposited on the bed. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to (a):
**Principle of Stereolithography:**
SLA is a **vat photopolymerization** process where a liquid photopolymer resin is selectively cured by a focused **UV laser (typically 355 nm)** under computer control.

**Process Steps:**
1. The build platform is positioned **one layer thickness (typically 25 – 100 µm)** below the surface of a vat filled with photopolymer resin.
2. A **galvanometer-steered UV laser** traces the cross-section of the part, point-by-point, onto the resin surface.
3. **Photopolymerization** occurs: the UV photons activate the photo-initiator, which generates free radicals that initiate crosslinking of acrylate/epoxy monomers into a rigid solid.
4. After one layer is cured, the platform descends by one layer thickness. A **sweeping blade re-levels** the resin.
5. The cycle repeats; the completed part is lifted out, washed in **isopropyl alcohol (IPA)** to remove uncured resin, and **post-cured in a UV oven** for final mechanical strength.

**Schematic (text representation):**

```
   [UV Laser 355 nm]
         ↓
   [Galvanometer Mirror]
         ↓
      ← scanned →
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ← Cured layer (top)
   ░░░░░░░░░░░░░░░  ← Liquid photopolymer resin
   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓  ← Cured part (previous layers)
   ═══════════════  ← Build platform
```

**Role of Photo-initiators:**
Photo-initiators (commonly **Irgacure 819**, **TPO**, or **BAPO**) are **UV-absorbing molecules** added in trace concentrations (**0.5 – 3 wt %**). Upon absorbing UV photons, they undergo **Type I cleavage** to produce **free radicals**, which attack the C=C double bonds of acrylate monomers, triggering **chain-growth polymerization**. Without photo-initiators, the resin would remain a stable liquid indefinitely under ambient light.

---

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the given parameters:**
- Filament diameter: $d_f = 1.75$ mm
- Feed rate: $F = 4$ mm/s
- Layer thickness: $t_l = 0.2$ mm
- Nozzle traverse speed: $v = 60$ mm/s

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area of the incoming filament:**

$$
A_f = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_f^{\,2}}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot (1.75)^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot 3.0625}{4} = 2.405 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate of incoming PLA:**

$$
Q_{in} = A_f \cdot F = 2.405 \times 4 = 9.621 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply conservation of mass (incompressible melt):**

$$
Q_{in} = w_{bead} \cdot t_l \cdot v
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for bead width:**

$$
w_{bead} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{t_l \cdot v} = \dfrac{9.621}{0.2 \times 60} = \dfrac{9.621}{12}
$$

$$
\boxed{w_{bead} = 0.802 \text{ mm}}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:** The bead width (0.802 mm) is approximately **2× the nozzle diameter (0.4 mm)**, indicating typical over-extrusion consistent with real FDM behavior. The result is physically reasonable.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Units Mistake (Costs 1–2 marks):** Students often forget to convert hatch spacing and layer thickness into **mm** before computing $E_v$, leading to an answer off by a factor of 1000.
> 2. **Confusing SLS with SLM (Costs 2 marks):** Examiners will deduct marks if SLS is described as "melting the powder." Always state that SLS is **sintering** (solid-state), while SLM is **melting** (liquid-state).
> 3. **Forgetting Photopolymerization:** In SLA answers, simply saying "the resin hardens" is **incomplete**. You must explicitly mention **photo-initiator → free radical → chain-growth polymerization**.
> 4. **FDM bead width sanity check:** If you compute $w_{bead}$ less than $d_n$, you have made an error in the conservation equation.
> 5. **Optimal $E_v$ windows are process- and material-specific** — do not write a single universal value; always cite the material-specific range.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Module 2 — Principles**

- ✅ AM is classified into **3 master principles** based on feedstock state: **Liquid, Solid, Powder** (ASTM F2792).
- ✅ **SLA** = UV laser + photopolymer resin + vat photopolymerization (best surface finish).
- ✅ **FDM** = thermoplastic filament + heated nozzle extrusion (low cost, anisotropic strength).
- ✅ **SLS** = partial sintering of polymer powder (PA12), no sacrificial supports needed.
- ✅ **SLM** = full melting of metal powder (Ti, SS, Inconel), requires inert atmosphere.
- ✅ **EBM** = electron beam + vacuum chamber, used for reactive metals like **Titanium**.
- ✅ **DMLS** = partial melting of metal alloys (conceptually between SLS and SLM).
- ✅ **Binder Jetting** = liquid binder droplets on powder bed (no heat, used for sand molds).
- ✅ **Key Formula #1:** $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — Volumetric energy density (J/mm³).
- ✅ **Key Formula #2:** $N = \dfrac{H}{t_l}$ — Number of layers in a part.
- ✅ **Key Formula #3:** $w_{bead} = \dfrac{\pi \cdot d_f^{\,2} \cdot F}{4 \cdot t_l \cdot v}$ — FDM bead width via mass conservation.
- ✅ **Ti-6Al-4V optimal $E_v$** for SLM = **60 – 120 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **316L SS optimal $E_v$** for SLM = **80 – 150 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **AlSi10Mg optimal $E_v$** for SLM = **30 – 70 J/mm³**.
- ✅ **PA12 Nylon optimal $E_a$** for SLS = **0.04 – 0.06 J/mm²**.
- ✅ **Hatch spacing** typically **0.05 – 0.20 mm**; **layer thickness** typically **20 – 100 µm** for metal AM.
- ✅ **Inert gas requirement:** Argon for SLM, Vacuum for EBM, None for SLS (polymer).
- ✅ **Photo-initiator concentration** in resin = **0.5 – 3 wt %**.
- ✅ **FDM build chamber** typically heated to **40 – 60 °C** to reduce warping in ABS.
- ✅ **Build time formula:** $T_b = \dfrac{H}{R_z} + N \cdot t_r + T_{scan}$.
- ✅ **Unfused powder in SLS/SLM doubles as natural support** — no support structures needed.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview
