- **Anisotropy Index** = $\sigma_{\text{Z}} / \sigma_{\text{XY}}$; typical **0.70–0.90** (a key drawback).
- **Build Rate** = $5$–$50$ cm³/hr (AM) vs. $10^3$–$10^5$ cm³/hr (injection molding).
- **Top 3 Benefits**: Design freedom, part consolidation, zero tooling.
- **Top 3 Drawbacks**: Anisotropy, surface roughness, slow build rate.
- **Industrial Showcases**: GE LEAP nozzle (consolidation), Bugatti brake caliper (lightweighting), patient-specific implants (customization).
- **Mitigations for Drawbacks**: HIP, scanning strategy rotation, support optimization algorithms, post-machining of critical interfaces.
- **Decision Rule**: AM is optimal for **$<1000$ parts/year, high complexity, expensive materials, customization required**. Otherwise, conventional manufacturing is preferred.
- **KTU 2024 Hot Topic**: Expect a 7-mark question linking benefits to **DfAM (Design for Additive Manufacturing)** with an industrial case study.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Applications of Extrusion-Based Processes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Applications of Extrusion-Based Processes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Extrusion-Based Additive Manufacturing (AM)** is a material extrusion process category defined by **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** as an additive manufacturing process in which material is selectively dispensed through a nozzle or orifice to build parts layer-by-layer. The material is pushed (extruded) in a continuous or semi-continuous form — typically a **molten thermoplastic filament, viscous paste, slurry, hydrogel, or food-grade dough** — to form a 2D pattern which then solidifies to constitute one layer of the final 3D object. The most common commercial variants under KTU 2024 syllabus PEPOT634 include: - **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** / **Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF)** - **Direct Ink Writing (DIW)** / **Robocasting** - **3D Concrete / Cementitious Printing** - **Bioprinting (Bio-extrusion)** - **Food Printing (Chocolate, Dough, Sugar)** - **Pellet / Granulate Extrusion (FGF — Fused Granulate Fabrication)** ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > Think of extrusion-based 3D printing as a **mechanical cake-icing machine mounted on a moving gantry**. Imagine a baker piping decorative icing onto a flat cake, but instead of icing, the machine pushes molten plastic, cement paste, or even living cell-laden bio-ink. After finishing one layer (say, a 2D outline of a flower), the nozzle rises a tiny step equal to one **layer height (typically 0.1 mm to 0.4 mm)** and prints the next layer right on top. The icing hardens as it cools or cures, locking each layer to the previous one. Repeat this 200–2000 times and the baker has "printed" a structural component, a prosthetic, or even a chocolate sculpture. The two governing "levers" of the process are: - **Volumetric flow rate** of material out of the nozzle - **Traverse speed** of the print head These two parameters together decide whether you get a clean, dense bead or a weak, porous mess. ## 1.3 Standard Metrics Used in Extrusion AM | Metric | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | Nozzle diameter | $d_n$ | **0.1 – 3.0** | mm | | Layer height | $h$ | **0.05 – 1.0** | mm | | Print speed | $v$ | **10 – 150** | mm/s | | Bead width | $w_b$ | **0.3 – 5.0** | mm | | Nozzle temperature | $T_n$ | **180 – 260** (FDM) | °C | | Bed temperature | $T_b$ | **40 – 110** (FDM) | °C | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle (Step-by-Step Logic) 1. **Material Feed:** A solid filament (Ø 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm), pellet, or syringe of paste is delivered to a liquefier chamber by a driven roller / plunger / screw. 2. **Phase Change / Viscosity Reduction:** The material is heated (FDM) or sheared (paste extrusion) to a workable viscosity $\eta < 10^3 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ for thermoplastics, or $10^2$–$10^6 \, \text{Pa}\cdot\text{s}$ for pastes. 3. **Pressure-Driven Flow:** A piston, screw, or pneumatic pressure $P$ forces the fluid through a circular orifice of diameter $d_n$. 4. **Deposition:** A gantry or robotic arm traces a 2D toolpath while the bead is deposited. 5. **Solidification:** Material cools, gels, or cures (UV, chemical, thermal) to retain its deposited shape. 6. **Layer Stacking:** The build platform descends (or nozzle ascends) by one layer height $h$ and the cycle repeats. ## 2.2 Key Engineering "Why's" and "How's" - **Why a circular nozzle?** It produces a uniform cross-sectional bead, which is the geometric basis of slicing software. - **Why heat in FDM?** Polymer melt viscosity drops by ~10× per 10 °C — making extrusion energetically feasible. - **Why use a heated bed?** Reduces **warping** caused by differential thermal contraction. - **Why shear-thinning in DIW?** The ink must flow under nozzle shear ($\dot{\gamma} > 10^2 \, s^{-1}$) yet hold its shape at rest ($G' > G''$). - **Why a roller in FDM?** Converts rotational motor motion into unidirectional linear filament feed at a controlled rate $Q_f$. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > Below are the **high-yield formulas** examiners love to test for the 2-mark and 7-mark sub-questions. | # | Formula | LaTeX | Meaning / Application | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Volumetric flow rate from nozzle | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} \cdot v_d$ | $d_n$ = nozzle diameter, $v_d$ = deposition velocity of extruded jet | | 2 | FDM filament feed equivalence | $Q_f = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4} \cdot v_d = \dfrac{\pi d_f^{2}}{4} \cdot v_f$ | Relates filament feed speed $v_f$ (Ø $d_f$) to print head speed | | 3 | Hagen–Poiseuille flow (paste) | $Q = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{4}}{128 \mu L}\Delta P$ | For viscous pastes, $\mu$ = viscosity, $L$ = nozzle length | | 4 | Bead width (geometric) | $w_b = \dfrac{4 Q}{\pi d_n^{2}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{h}$ | Links flow, nozzle and layer height | | 5 | Hagen number / Printability | $H_a = \dfrac{\tau_y L}{\rho g D}$ | Dimensionless balance of yield stress $\tau_y$ vs. gravity collapse | | 6 | Buckling criterion (FDM) | $F_{cr} = \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L)^{2}}$ | When $F_{cr} < $ extrusion force, filament buckles between rollers | | 7 | Print time estimate | $t_p = \dfrac{V_{part}}{Q} \cdot k$ | $k$ = infill/density factor, $V_{part}$ = part volume | | 8 | Aspect ratio (overhang) | $\theta_{max} \approx 45^{\circ}$ | Unsupported overhang limit without support material | **Real-world utility:** Equations (1)–(4) are the heart of **slicer firmware** (Cura, PrusaSlicer, Simplify3D). Engineers re-derive these to validate custom material profiles for novel polymers, pastes and bio-inks. Equation (6) is the **mechanical failure limit** that determines whether a flexible TPU (low $E$, low $I$) can be pushed through a long, narrow Bowden tube. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Equivalence Between Filament Feed and Bead Deposition (FDM) This is the **fundamental equation that ties filament diameter to print head motion** — frequently asked for 7 marks. **Given:** - Filament diameter: $d_f$ - Filament feed speed: $v_f$ - Nozzle diameter: $d_n$ - Print head / deposition velocity: $v_d$ **Find:** The relationship $v_f = f(d_n, d_f, v_d)$. ### Step-by-step Expansion The volumetric flow rate of molten polymer leaving the nozzle must equal the volumetric feed rate of the solid filament entering the liquefier (conservation of mass, assuming incompressible melt): $$ \begin{aligned} Q_{out} &= Q_{in} \\[4pt] \dfrac{\pi\, d_n^{2}}{4}\, v_d &= \dfrac{\pi\, d_f^{2}}{4}\, v_f \end{aligned} $$ Cancel the common factor $\pi/4$ on both sides: $$ \begin{aligned} d_n^{2}\, v_d &= d_f^{2}\, v_f \\[4pt] v_f &= \left(\dfrac{d_n}{d_f}\right)^{2} v_d \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Interpretation:** For the standard $d_f = 1.75 \, \text{mm}$ and $d_n = 0.4 \, \text{mm}$, the ratio is $(0.4 / 1.75)^2 \approx 0.052$. Thus the filament must be fed roughly **19× slower** than the print head moves — a small, controlled pull. **Numerical Check:** If $v_d = 60 \, \text{mm/s}$, then $v_f = 0.052 \times 60 \approx 3.13 \, \text{mm/s}$, a typical FDM value. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Bead Width from Geometric Consistency The deposited bead is approximately a cylindrical segment of width $w_b$ and height $h$. Volume per unit length is $w_b \cdot h$. Equating to flow per unit length $Q/v_d$: $$ \begin{aligned} Q &= w_b \cdot h \cdot v_d \\[4pt] w_b &= \dfrac{Q}{h \cdot v_d} = \dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}\, v_d}{4 h\, v_d} \\[4pt] w_b &= \dfrac{\pi d_n^{2}}{4 h} \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Interpretation:** Bead width is **inversely proportional to layer height** — doubling layer height halves bead width if $Q$ is constant. This is why slicers automatically re-compute $Q$ when layer height is changed. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Buckling Force in the FDM Liquefier The filament is a slender column of length $L$ (between feed rollers and melt zone), Euler pinned at both ends. By Euler's formula: $$ \begin{aligned} F_{cr} &= \dfrac{\pi^{2} E I}{(K L)^{2}}, \quad K = 1 \, (\text{pinned–pinned}) \\[4pt] I &= \dfrac{\pi d_f^{4}}{64} \quad \text{(second moment of area, circular section)} \\[4pt] F_{cr} &= \dfrac{\pi^{2} E \cdot \pi d_f^{4}}{64 L^{2}} = \dfrac{\pi^{3} E\, d_f^{4}}{64 L^{2}} \end{aligned} $$ **Logical Interpretation:** Buckling force scales with $d_f^4$ — a small filament-diameter increase dramatically improves pushability. This is why Bowden-tube FDM printers with long guide tubes sometimes struggle with stiff filaments and why 3 mm filaments are used in some industrial systems. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Material → Process → Application Mapping > [!IMPORTANT] > This is the **single most important KTU table** for the "Applications" topic — examiners expect you to recall at least 3 such rows for full marks. | Material Family | Process Variant | Extrusion Mechanism | Representative Applications | |---|---|---|---| | **Thermoplastics** (ABS, PLA, PETG, TPU, PEEK) | FDM / FFF | Thermal melt, Ø 1.75 / 2.85 mm filament | Concept prototypes, jigs & fixtures, drone frames, prosthetics, automotive tooling | | **Engineering composites** (CF/GF-reinforced nylon, chopped fiber pellets) | FGF / pellet extrusion | Heated screw extruder | Aerospace brackets, structural jigs, low-volume production | | **Ceramics** (Alumina, Zirconia, bioactive glass) | Robocasting / DIW | Plunger/screw through paste | Bone scaffolds, dental crowns, fuel-cell electrolyte structures | | **Cementitious / Geopolymers** | Contour Crafting, Concrete Printing | Pump + nozzle | Walls, columns, formwork-free architecture (e.g., Dubai's 3D-printed office) | | **Hydrogels / Bio-inks** (alginate, GelMA, collagen + cells) | Bio-extrusion | Pneumatic / piston at 4–37 °C | Tissue engineering — skin, cartilage, bone; organ-on-chip; drug screening | | **Edible pastes** (chocolate, sugar fondant, dough, meat paste) | Food 3D printing | Syringe / screw | Personalised nutrition, decorative confectionery, plant-based meat shaping | | **Conductive inks** (silver, copper, PEDOT:PSS) | Direct ink writing | Pneumatic | Printed antennas, flexible/stretchable electronics, EMI shielding, solar cells | | **Wax / Investment casting patterns** | Solidscape / MJM-type | Thermal jet | Lost-wax patterns for jewellery, turbine blades | ## 3.5 Domain-Wise Application Deep-Dive > [!NOTE] > The remaining sub-sections expand on the matrix above and prepare you for 14-mark descriptive answers. ### A. **Prototyping & Product Design (FDM/FFF)** - **Use case:** Functional prototypes, ergonomic mock-ups, presentation models. - **Why extrusion?** Low capital cost (~₹15,000–₹3 lakh desktop), wide material range, in-house iteration turnaround of 4–24 hours. - **Engineering Example:** Automotive OEMs (Mahindra, Tata Motors) use FDM to print master patterns for clay modelling and ergonomic cabin mock-ups. ### B. **Tooling & End-Use Parts (FDM with engineering polymers)** - **Use case:** Injection mould inserts, jigs, fixtures, and **low-volume end-use parts** (e.g., aerospace clips, PEEK brackets). - **Materials:** ULTEM (PEI), PEEK, PEKK — printed in industrial systems (Stratasys F900, Roboze) with chamber temperatures up to **180 °C** and build envelopes of **914 × 610 × 914 mm**. - **Advantage:** Replace CNC aluminium tooling, lead time 1–2 weeks vs. 6–10 weeks. ### C. **Medical & Dental (Multi-material FDM + DIW + Bio-extrusion)** - **Patient-specific anatomical models** from CT/MRI scans, printed in PLA/ABS for surgical planning. - **Surgical guides** in sterilizable biocompatible resin (via FDM with PC-ISO). - **Orthotics & Prosthetics:** Custom hand and limb sockets printed in lightweight PLA/PETG with TPU cushions. - **Dental:** Clear aligner models (via resin, but study models via FDM), occlusal splints, denture base try-ins. - **Bioprinting:** Layered deposition of cell-laden bio-ink to form living tissues — skin patches for burn patients, cartilage for knee repair, and bone scaffolds that resorb as native bone regrows. ### D. **Aerospace & Automotive** - **Weight-critical brackets** printed in CF-PEEK or CF-PEI. - **Ducting, cable harnesses** printed in TPU as flexible functional parts. - **Tooling:** Layup tools for composite lay-up (Boeing 787, Airbus A350) — directly printed moulds eliminate CNC machining of large epoxy tooling. - **One-shot parts:** SpaceX uses FDM-printed components inside Crew Dragon interior non-critical structures. ### E. **Architecture & Construction (Contour Crafting, Concrete Printing)** - **Wall systems:** Layered concrete printed within 24–48 hours vs. weeks of masonry. - **Formwork-free structures:** Eliminates timber/metal formwork, saving up to **60 % of construction cost**. - **Examples:** Dubai's 3D-printed office (6.1 m tall, 2016), Winsun's 10 houses in 24 hours (Shanghai, 2014), India's Tvasta builds India's first 3D-printed house (IIT-Madras alumni, 2022). - **Material system:** Ordinary Portland cement + sand + accelerator (to gain green strength before next layer is added). ### F. **Electronics & Functional Devices (DIW with conductive inks)** - **Printed antennas** on curved substrates for IoT. - **Stretchable interconnects** for wearable health monitors. - **Embedded sensors:** Strain gauges and thermistors co-printed into structural parts. - **EMI shielding** patterns on aerospace housings. ### G. **Food Industry** - **Personalised nutrition:** Altered macronutrient geometry for dysphagia patients, athletes, astronauts. - **Decorative confectionery:** 3D chocolate sculptures (3D Systems' CocoJet, byBeeHex). - **Plant-based meat shaping:** Mimics fibrous muscle geometry. ### H. **Art, Education & Cultural Heritage** - **Sculpture & installation art:** Large-scale polymeric and clay extrusions (e.g., works by Joshua Harker). - **STEM education:** Low-cost FDM in schools and university labs. - **Heritage replication:** Replication of museum artifacts for touch-based learning. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Extrusion-Based AM System ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Material Supply Module
Filament Spool or Paste Cartridge] --> B[Drive System
Roller / Piston / Screw / Pneumatic] B --> C[Liquefier / Mixing Chamber
Thermal or Shear-Induced Viscosity Drop] C --> D[Nozzle / Orifice
Diameter 0.1 to 3.0 mm] D --> E[Deposition Track
2D Toolpath via Gantry or Robot] E --> F[Build Platform
Heated Bed 40 to 110 deg C] F --> G[Solidification
Cooling Gelling Curing or UV] G --> H[Layer Stacking
Z Increment h 0.05 to 1.0 mm] H --> B F --> I[Process Sensors
Temperature Pressure Optical Monitoring] I --> J[Closed Loop Controller
Feedback to Drive and Liquefier] ``` ## 4.2 Process Family Tree (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Extrusion-Based AM
ISO ASTM 52900] --> B[Material Extrusion MAT] B --> C[FDM / FFF
Thermoplastics] B --> D[DIW / Robocasting
Pastes and Slurries] B --> E[Bio-extrusion
Cell Laden Inks] B --> F[3D Concrete Printing
Cementitious] B --> G[Food Printing
Edible Pastes] C --> C1[Engineering Polymers PEEK PEI ULTEM] C --> C2[Composites CF GF Reinforced] C --> C3[Elastomers TPU TPE] D --> D1[Ceramics Alumina Zirconia] D --> D2[Metals via Binder or Paste] D --> D3[Conductive Inks Ag Cu PEDOT] E --> E1[Skin and Cartilage] E --> E2[Bone Scaffolds] E --> E3[Organ on Chip] F --> F1[Contour Crafting] F --> F2[Pumped Concrete] F --> F3[Geopolymer Systems] G --> G1[Chocolate and Sugar] G --> G2[Dough and Plant Based Meat] G --> G3[Personalised Nutrition] ``` ## 4.3 Process Flow — From STL to Finished Part ```mermaid flowchart TD A[3D CAD Model STEP STL] --> B[Slicer Software
Cura PrusaSlicer Simplify3D] B --> C[Layer Slicing
Layer Height h] C --> D[Toolpath Generation
Perimeter Infill Support] D --> E[G Code Export
Motion and Extrusion Commands] E --> F[Printer Firmware
Interprets G Code] F --> G[Layer by Layer Build
Extrusion Loop Active] G --> H[Post Processing
Support Removal Annealing Sintering] H --> I[Finished Part] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading Guide for Students:** The **drive system** is where extrusion force $F$ is generated; the **liquefier** is where material becomes printable; the **nozzle** is where geometry is fixed; and the **build platform / solidification** block is where the part gains strength. Every sensor and feedback path targets one of these four physical blocks. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout** > - **Never confuse "extrusion-based" with "binder jetting" or "vat polymerisation".** These are different ISO/ASTM process categories. Mark deduction of **2 marks** is routine. > - **Do not write vague answers like "FDM is used in medical".** Always specify the *type* of medical application (e.g., "patient-specific anatomical model for pre-surgical planning") — KTU examiners award the application mark only when the answer is domain-specific. > - **For 14-mark questions, ALWAYS structure the answer** into (i) definition, (ii) working principle, (iii) material–process–application mapping (table), (iv) at least one **real industrial case study** with name and year, (v) advantages/limitations. A bullet-only answer rarely crosses 10/14. > - **Avoid mixing units.** FDM diameter is in **mm**, chamber temperature in **°C**, viscosity in **Pa·s** — examiners deduct for sloppy units. --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Question Paper)]** **List any three material-extrusion AM processes and state one specific industrial application of each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** 1. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** – used in *automotive* for ergonomic cabin mock-ups and ergonomic testing of dashboard layouts (Mahindra Research Valley uses FDM for interior prototype evaluation). *(1 mark)* 2. **Direct Ink Writing (Robocasting)** – used in *dental ceramics* to print zirconia crowns and bridge substructures with controlled porosity for cement penetration. *(1 mark)* 3. **Bioprinting (bio-extrusion)** – used in *regenerative medicine* to print skin patches for burn-victim treatment; clinically demonstrated by Universidad Carlos III de Madrid and Wake Forest Institute. *(1 mark)* ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]** **Why is the build plate (bed) heated in FDM? What is the typical temperature range for PLA and ABS?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - The heated bed keeps the **first deposited layer above its glass-transition temperature** ($T_g$), preventing **differential thermal contraction** and the resulting **warping / corner-lifting** failure. *(2 marks)* - Typical ranges: **PLA → 50–70 °C**; **ABS → 95–110 °C** (ABS additionally requires a heated enclosure to avoid layer delamination). *(1 mark)* --- ## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern) > **Internal-Choice Convention:** KTU ESE allows you to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B from each module-choice set. We provide BOTH alternatives below so you can practise both. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Applications of Extrusion AM in Industry** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain the working principle of Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) with a neat block diagram. List six thermoplastic materials used in FDM and state one application of each.** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (5 marks):** - A thermoplastic filament (Ø 1.75 / 2.85 mm) is fed by a **motor-driven roller** into a heated **liquefier** maintained at **180–260 °C** depending on polymer. - The polymer melts and is extruded through a **brass / hardened-steel nozzle** of diameter **0.2–1.0 mm** under controlled volumetric flow. - The **X-Y gantry** traces the cross-section of the current layer; the build platform descends in Z by one **layer height (0.1–0.4 mm)** after each layer. - Solidification occurs by **convective cooling**; adhesion between layers arises from **inter-diffusion of polymer chains** across the interface while the new layer is still above $T_g$. **Block Diagram (embedded as reference):** - Feed Roller → Liquefier → Nozzle → X-Y Gantry → Heated Bed → Part **Six Materials & Applications (2 marks, 1⁄3 mark each pair):** | Material | Key Property | Application | |---|---|---| | PLA | Biodegradable, low warp | Education, consumer prototypes | | ABS | Tough, heat-resistant | Automotive interior prototypes, LEGO®-type parts | | PETG | Tough + chemically resistant | Food-contact containers, mechanical brackets | | TPU 95A | Elastomeric (Shore A) | Flexible gaskets, shoe midsoles, vibration dampers | | PC (Polycarbonate) | High $T_g$ (~147 °C), strong | Functional prototypes, electrical housings | | PEEK | High-performance thermoplastic | Aerospace brackets, implantable-grade devices | --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Discuss in detail the application of extrusion-based AM in the construction industry. Mention the specific process, materials, advantages, and at least one real-world project.** **Model Solution:** **Process:** **Contour Crafting** (Khoshnevis, USC) and **pumped-concrete 3D printing** are the dominant variants. **Materials:** Specialised cementitious mortars — Portland cement + silica sand + fly-ash + accelerators (sodium silicate) + retarders. Typical printable mortar viscosity is **10–50 Pa·s** with a yield stress of **1–5 kPa** so that the bead does not slump under its own weight (governed by the **Hagen number $H_a$**, Formula 5 in §2.3). **Layer thickness:** 10–30 mm (much larger than FDM, due to aggregate size). **Advantages (4 marks):** 1. **Formwork elimination** — saves up to **60 % of construction cost and 70 % of construction time**. 2. **Architectural freedom** — curved walls, optimised topology, integrated reinforcement channels. 3. **Reduced labour** — onsite manpower reduced by 50–80 %. 4. **Lower material waste** — only the volume of the final wall is deposited, no cutting of formwork timber. 5. **Faster turnaround** — entire 200 m² houses printed in **< 24 hours**. **Real-World Projects (3 marks):** - **Dubai's 3D-Printed Office (2016):** A 6.1 m tall, 250 m² office built using a **17 m tall gantry printer** in 17 days, designed by **Killa Design** and **Winsun**. - **Tvasta (India, 2022):** India's first 3D-printed house (IIT-Madras incubated) — single-storey 600 sq.ft home printed in **5 days** using a locally-developed concrete-printing system. - **ICON's Wolf Ranch (Texas, 2023):** A community of 100 3D-printed houses using **Lavacrete** material. **Limitations (1 mark):** - Reinforcement placement (rebar / mesh) is still largely manual; IS-codes for 3D-printed concrete are still evolving in India. - Surface finish requires post-processing. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – Sample Paper 2024 Scheme]** #### Part (a) — 7 Marks **Explain Direct Ink Writing (DIW / Robocasting) for ceramics. Discuss the rheological requirements of the ink, the material systems used, and three industrial applications.** **Model Solution:** **Definition (1 mark):** DIW is an extrusion-based AM process in which a **shear-thinning ceramic paste** is deposited through a fine nozzle (typically 100–1000 µm) to form green bodies that are later **dried, debinded and sintered** to full density. **Rheological Requirements (3 marks):** - The ink must exhibit **shear-thinning (pseudoplastic)** behaviour: viscosity drops by **2–3 orders of magnitude** under nozzle shear ($\dot{\gamma} \approx 10^2$–$10^3 \, s^{-1}$). - At rest, it must have a **yield stress $\tau_y > \rho g D / 2$** to prevent slumping of the printed bead — typically **0.1–10 kPa**. - **Storage modulus $G'$ must exceed loss modulus $G''$** at low frequency to retain shape after deposition. - Common formulation additives: **hydroxyapatite, methylcellulose, Pluronic F-127, fumed silica** as thickeners/binders. **Material Systems (1 mark):** - Structural ceramics: **Alumina (Al₂O₃)**, **Zirconia (ZrO₂)**, **Silicon Carbide (SiC)**. - Bioactive: **Bioactive glass (45S5)**, **β-tricalcium phosphate (β-TCP)**, **hydroxyapatite**. **Three Industrial Applications (2 marks):** 1. **Biomedical scaffolds:** 3D porous hydroxyapatite scaffolds with controlled pore architecture (300–500 µm) for bone regeneration. 2. **Solid oxide fuel cells (SOFCs):** Robocast electrolyte layers (YSZ — yttria-stabilised zirconia) with controlled thickness and interfacial area. 3. **Investment casting cores and moulds:** Robocast silica cores for hollow turbine blades (SiC/Si₃N₄) that are later leached out. 4. (Bonus) Catalytic substrates: cordierite monoliths with hierarchical porosity. --- #### Part (b) — 7 Marks **Describe the working of extrusion-based bioprinting. What are the key bio-ink requirements? List four specific medical applications with the type of cell/tissue used.** **Model Solution:** **Working Principle (2 marks):** - A **bio-ink** (cell-laden hydrogel) is loaded into a **sterile syringe** and extruded through a fine nozzle (Ø 80–600 µm) at **physiological temperature (4–37 °C)** under **pneumatic or piston pressure** (typically 0.5–5 bar). - After deposition, the bio-ink is **cross-linked** (ionic — CaCl₂ for alginate; photo — UV for GelMA; thermal — gelatin) to lock the geometry. - The build is maintained in a **humidified, temperature-controlled incubator (37 °C, 5 % CO₂)**. - Layer-by-layer, a 3D tissue construct is formed with embedded living cells. **Bio-ink Requirements (2 marks):** 1. **Biocompatibility** — must not elicit immune response. 2. **Printability** — shear-thinning, fast gelation post-extrusion. 3. **Cell viability** — shear stress in nozzle < 5 kPa to avoid membrane rupture; > 85 % cell viability post-printing. 4. **Tunable degradation** — degrades at the rate of native tissue regeneration. 5. **Mechanical integrity** — matches stiffness of target tissue (brain ~ 1 kPa, cartilage ~ 1 MPa, bone ~ 10 MPa). **Four Medical Applications (3 marks, 0.75 each):** | Application | Cell / Tissue Type | Material System | |---|---|---| | Skin grafts for burns | Keratinocytes + fibroblasts | Collagen / fibrin / GelMA | | Cartilage repair | Chondrocytes / MSCs | Alginate + nanofibrillated cellulose | | Bone regeneration | Osteoblasts / MSCs | Hydroxyapatite-laden GelMA / silk fibroin | | Vascularised tissue patches | iPSC-derived endothelial cells + fibroblasts | GelMA + sacrificial Pluronic ink for channels | **Challenges (Bonus, 0.5 mark):** Vascularisation beyond 200 µm thickness; regulatory approval (FDA / ICMR); scale-up and sterility maintenance. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!TIP] > Read this section 5 minutes before the exam — it is the highest density of marks-per-line in these notes. - **Extrusion-based AM** is officially classified as **"Material Extrusion (MAT)"** under **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** — never confuse with **VAT polymerisation (Vat)** or **Binder Jetting (BJT)**. - **FDM** dominates **prototyping & tooling**; **DIW** dominates **ceramics & electronics**; **bio-extrusion** dominates **tissue engineering**; **concrete printing** dominates **construction**. - The **fundamental volumetric equivalence** $d_f^2 v_f = d_n^2 v_d$ is the **#1 most-tested 2-mark derivation** in this module. - **Nozzle Ø** typically **0.1–3.0 mm**; **layer height 0.05–1.0 mm**; **print speed 10–150 mm/s**. - **Heated bed** prevents warping — **PLA 50–70 °C, ABS 95–110 °C**. - **Bead width formula** $w_b = \pi d_n^2 / (4 h)$ — inversely proportional to layer height. - **Euler buckling** sets the upper limit of push force for flexible filaments; $F_{cr} \propto d_f^4$. - **Hagen number $H_a$** governs **printability of self-supporting pastes** (cement, ceramic inks). - **Real-world case studies** to memorise for 14-mark answers: 1. **Dubai 3D-printed office (2016, Winsun)** 2. **Tvasta's 3D-printed house, India (2022)** 3. **ICON's Wolf Ranch, Texas (2023)** 4. **SpaceX Crew Dragon FDM interior parts** 5. **Boeing 787 layup tooling** - **Six industrial thermoplastic materials** for FDM: **PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, PC, PEEK** — know one application each. - **Bio-ink essentials:** **biocompatible, shear-thinning, fast gelation, cell viability > 85 %**. - **4 medical applications of bio-extrusion:** skin grafts (keratinocytes), cartilage (chondrocytes), bone (osteoblasts), vascular patches (endothelial cells). - **DIW ceramics:** **Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiC, bioactive glass, β-TCP, hydroxyapatite** — sintered post-deposition. - **Concrete printing advantages:** **60 % cost reduction, 70 % time reduction, 80 % labour reduction, near-zero formwork waste** (numbers good for 1-mark side-statements). - **Process parameter relationships to remember:** higher **layer height** → lower **bead width**, lower **part density**, higher **print speed**; higher **nozzle temperature** → lower **viscosity**, higher **bond strength**, more **stringing**. - **Examiner's golden rule:** always answer *which extrusion process* + *which material* + *which industrial application* + *one quantitative or named case study*. This structure alone carries a 7-mark sub-question to full marks. - **Avoid these common errors:** 1. Calling FDM "rapid prototyping" only — it is *additive manufacturing* in KTU's 2024 nomenclature. 2. Confusing **FFF (open-source, e.g., Prusa, Creality)** with **FDM (Stratasys trademarked)** — both are MAT, but the syllabus expects you to mention the trademark distinction once for full credit. 3. Writing "3D printing in medical" without naming a **tissue type**. 4. Forgetting units in numerical derivation — guaranteed 1-mark deduction.

topics.title: Sheet Lamination AM Processes: Bonding Mechanisms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Sheet Lamination AM Processes: Bonding Mechanisms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sheet Lamination** is a category of Additive Manufacturing (AM) in which successive layers of sheet-form feedstock (paper, polymer, metal, or composite foils) are sequentially stacked and **bonded together** to build a three-dimensional object. According to the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2021** standard, sheet lamination belongs to the broader **Material Extrusion / Material Jetting** family boundary and is classified under **"Sheet Lamination"** (process category 5).

The defining characteristic of sheet lamination is that the **layer formation step is decoupled from the bonding step** — sheets are pre-formed (rolled, cast, or extruded) and then *joined* using one of several physical or chemical bonding mechanisms.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Focus (Module 2):** The bonding mechanism — *not* the sheet material — is the primary differentiator between sheet lamination variants. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

### Key Bonding Mechanism Categories (KTU 2024 Taxonomy)

| Bonding Type | Process Example | Energy Source | Typical Material |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Adhesive Bonding** | LOM (Laminated Object Manufacturing) | Heated roller + adhesive | Paper, polymer |
| **Thermal Bonding** | LOM (thermal variant) | Laser/Heated platen | Thermoplastics |
| **Ultrasonic (Metallurgical) Bonding** | UAM (Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing) | Ultrasonic vibration | Metal foils |
| **Chemical/Solvent Bonding** | Selective lamination | Solvent activation | Polymer sheets |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy: The Sticky-Notebook Model**
> Imagine you are building a 3D map of a city by cutting shapes out of pages of a sticky notebook and sticking them one on top of the other. Each page is a *sheet*, your hand pressure is the *bonding force*, and the glue on the back is the *adhesive layer*. Sheet lamination AM works **exactly** this way — except a **laser** or **heated roller** or **ultrasonic horn** does the cutting and bonding, with **micrometre-level precision** and full **digital control**.

**Two key parameters to internalize:**
- **Bond Strength (σ_b):** The maximum load per unit area a bonded interface can sustain before failure. Typical target value for LOM paper: **σ_b ≥ 0.5 MPa** (shear).
- **Inter-layer Temperature (T_i):** The temperature at the bonding interface during joining, must exceed the **glass transition (T_g)** for amorphous polymers or approach the **melting point (T_m)** for semi-crystallines.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Bonding Mechanisms — Structured Logic Breakdown

### Mechanism 1: Adhesive Bonding (Most Common in LOM)

- **Working Principle:** A thin adhesive layer (typically **hot-melt EVA** or **polyurethane**, thickness **25–100 µm**) is pre-coated on the underside of each sheet.
- **Activation:** The heated roller (typically **T_roller = 80–120 °C**) softens the adhesive, allowing polymer chain interdiffusion across the interface.
- **Bond Formation:** Upon cooling, the adhesive solidifies forming **van der Waals + mechanical interlocking** bonds.
- **Why this works:** Chain mobility at temperatures above T_g enables **reptation** (snake-like diffusion of polymer chains across the interface), creating entanglements that yield high peel strength.

### Mechanism 2: Thermal Bonding (Direct Fusion)

- **Working Principle:** No separate adhesive; the sheet material itself is brought to a tacky state by localized heating.
- **Heat Source:** Heated platen, IR lamp, or CO₂ laser (10.6 µm wavelength).
- **Critical Condition:** T_interface ≥ T_g (amorphous) or T_m (semicrystalline).
- **Limitation:** Excessive heat → thermal degradation, warping, delamination in subsequent layers.

### Mechanism 3: Ultrasonic Metallurgical Bonding (UAM)

- **Working Principle:** A sonotrode vibrates the top sheet against the substrate at **ultrasonic frequency f = 20–40 kHz** with amplitude **A = 10–50 µm** under static pressure **P = 20–50 MPa**.
- **Why metals bond below melting point:** The combination of cyclic shear strain + normal force **breaks surface oxides** and exposes nascent metal, which then forms **metallurgical bonds** via diffusion across the interface.
- **Result:** Solid-state bond with bond strength approaching **base-material strength** (up to **90% of parent metal UTS** for aluminum foils).

### Mechanism 4: Chemical/Solvent Bonding

- **Working Principle:** A solvent (e.g., acetone for ABS, cyclohexanone for PVC) briefly dissolves the top **5–20 µm** of the polymer surface.
- **Bond Formation:** Applied sheets fuse at the molecular level as the solvent evaporates/diffuses away.
- **Use Case:** Common in low-cost **Selective Lamination** systems.

### Mechanism 5: Mechanical Interlocking (Cladding)

- **Working Principle:** Sheets have micro-features (perforations, embossed patterns) that physically interlock.
- **Use Case:** Less common in commercial AM; more academic interest.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Mandatory table for KTU Numerical Questions. Memorize the symbols and units.**

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_b = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | $\sigma_b$ = bond strength [Pa], F = failure force [N], A = bonded area [m²] | Universal strength check |
| 2 | $T_{int} = T_{roller} - \Delta T_{loss}$ | Interface temperature from roller temperature minus conduction loss | LOM thermal design |
| 3 | $E_{bond} = P \cdot t_b$ | Bonding energy: P = power [W], t_b = bonding dwell time [s] | Energy audit |
| 4 | $P_{us} = f \cdot A^2 \cdot \rho \cdot c$ | Ultrasonic power (approx.) for sonotrode | UAM design |
| 5 | $\sigma_{peel} = \dfrac{F_{peel}}{w}$ | $\sigma_{peel}$ = peel strength [N/m], w = peel width | Adhesive selection |
| 6 | $D_{diff} = \sqrt{2 D_s t}$ | Diffusion depth D_diff [m], D_s = surface diffusivity [m²/s], t = time [s] | Solid-state bonding |
| 7 | $R_{th} = \dfrac{L}{k \cdot A}$ | Thermal resistance across bonded layer | Heat flow analysis |
| 8 | $\dot{\gamma} = 2\pi f A$ | Shear strain rate in ultrasonic welding | UAM process window |
| 9 | $\tau = \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Viscous shear stress in tacky adhesive | Polymer rheology |
| 10 | $F_{bond} = P_n \cdot A \cdot \mu$ | Frictional bond force (P_n = normal pressure) | Cladding bond estimate |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **LOM** → Sand casting patterns, architectural models, visual prototypes.
- **UAM** → **Functionally graded materials**, **embedded sensors**, **cooling channels** in aerospace, **battery cell** fabrication.
- **CBAM (Composite Based AM, by Impossible Objects)** → High-strength carbon-fiber parts.
- **SDL (Selective Deposition Lamination, by Mcor Technologies)** → Full-color paper prototypes for medical visualization.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Ultrasonic Bonding Energy at the Interface

**Problem statement:** Derive the volumetric energy density delivered to the bonding interface during UAM, given horn frequency f, amplitude A, normal pressure P_n, and weld time t_w.

**Step 1 — Identify the cyclic shear strain at the interface.**

The sonotrode produces sinusoidal displacement. For a peak amplitude A and angular frequency $\omega = 2\pi f$:

$$
u(t) = A \sin(2\pi f t)
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the shear strain rate on the thin interfacial layer of thickness δ.**

The shear strain rate is the velocity gradient across the interface:

$$
\dot{\gamma}(t) = \frac{1}{\delta} \frac{du}{dt} = \frac{2\pi f A}{\delta} \cos(2\pi f t)
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the peak shear strain rate (maximum magnitude).**

$$
\dot{\gamma}_{peak} = \frac{2\pi f A}{\delta}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine the peak shear stress (viscous/plastic regime).**

Treating the interface as a thin layer with effective viscosity η:

$$
\tau_{peak} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}_{peak} = \frac{2\pi f A \eta}{\delta}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the volumetric power dissipation per unit area.**

Power per unit bonded area is the product of shear stress and strain rate integrated over one cycle. The mean value over a cycle is:

$$
\bar{P}_{diss} = \frac{1}{2} \tau_{peak} \dot{\gamma}_{peak} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} f^{2} A^{2} \eta}{\delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 6 — Compute the total energy density delivered during weld time t_w.**

$$
E_{int} = \bar{P}_{diss} \cdot t_w = \frac{2 \pi^{2} f^{2} A^{2} \eta \, t_w}{\delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 7 — Engineering interpretation.**

Higher $E_{int}$ promotes greater **oxide disruption** and **atomic diffusion** across the interface, increasing bond strength. However, exceeding a threshold causes **fatigue micro-cracking** — hence an **optimum process window** exists.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Numerically:** For aluminum 3003 foils, typical values are f = 20 kHz, A = 30 µm, δ = 5 µm, η ≈ 10⁶ Pa·s (effective at interface), t_w = 0.5 s. Substituting yields $E_{int} \approx 7.1 \times 10^{6}$ J/m³ — sufficient for metallurgical bond formation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Bond Strength from Laminated Sheet Stack

**Problem statement:** For a stack of n sheets bonded by adhesive, derive the apparent shear bond strength and identify the failure criterion.

**Step 1 — Define geometric parameters.**

- Sheet thickness: t_s
- Adhesive layer thickness: t_a
- Total stack thickness: $H = n \cdot t_s + (n-1) \cdot t_a$
- Cross-section area: A

**Step 2 — Apply axial loading F along the stacking direction.**

The stress on each bond plane is:

$$
\sigma_{bond} = \frac{F}{A}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the maximum shear stress (Tresca) failure criterion at the interface.**

Failure occurs when:

$$
\sigma_{bond} \geq \tau_{adh} = \tau_{yield,\,adh}
$$

**Step 4 — Express the safety factor.**

$$
N_{safety} = \frac{\tau_{adh} \cdot A}{F}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to peel loading (more critical for LOM).**

For a peeling load $F_{peel}$ acting on a width w, the peel stress is:

$$
\sigma_{peel} = \frac{F_{peel}}{w}
$$

**Step 6 — Identify the dominant failure mode.**

For LOM paper stacks: $t_a \ll t_s$, hence **peel failure** dominates over shear failure. KTU questions often ask *why* paper LOM parts delaminate easily along Z — the answer is **low interlaminar peel strength**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering takeaway:** That is why LOM parts are reinforced with **epoxy infiltration** post-processing — to raise the apparent peel strength from ~0.3 kN/m to over **3 kN/m**.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Mechanism | Bond Strength (typical) | Cycle Time | Suitable Material | Energy Source | Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Adhesive (LOM) | 0.5–2 MPa | 5–30 s/layer | Paper, polymer | Heated roller | Peel |
| Thermal fusion | 5–20 MPa | 2–10 s/layer | Thermoplastic | Laser/IR | Shear |
| Ultrasonic (UAM) | 50–200 MPa | 0.5–2 s/layer | Metal foils | Ultrasonic horn | Shear/ductile |
| Solvent | 2–8 MPa | 10–60 s/layer | Polymer | Solvent | Shear |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Ultrasonic bonding produces **two orders of magnitude** higher strength than adhesive bonding — this is the single most important comparison to remember.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Flowchart of Sheet Lamination Bonding Mechanisms

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Sheet Lamination AM]:::root --> B[Bonding Mechanism]
    B --> C[Adhesive Bonding]
    B --> D[Thermal Bonding]
    B --> E[Ultrasonic Metallurgical]
    B --> F[Chemical Solvent]
    B --> G[Mechanical Interlock]
    C --> C1[LOM Paper]
    C --> C2[SDL]
    D --> D1[Heated Roller LOM]
    D --> D2[Laser LOM]
    E --> E1[UAM Aluminum]
    E --> E2[UAM Copper]
    F --> F1[Selective Lamination]
    G --> G1[Cladded Foils]

    classDef root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef mech fill:#fde68a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    class C,D,E,F,G mech
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an LOM System (Heated Roller + Laser)

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IN[Input]
        i1[Sheet Feed Roller]
        i2[Adhesive-Coated Foil Roll]
    end

    subgraph PROC[Processing Station]
        p1[Heated Roller Assembly]
        p2[Build Platform Platen]
        p3[CO2 Laser Cutter 10.6um]
        p4[X-Y Galvo Scanner]
    end

    subgraph CTRL[Control Unit]
        c1[STL Slicer Module]
        c2[Z-Axis Stepper]
        c3[Path Planner]
    end

    subgraph OUT[Output]
        o1[Waste Cross-Hatch Grid]
        o2[3D Laminated Part]
    end

    i1 --> p1
    i2 --> p1
    p1 --> p2
    c1 --> c3
    c3 --> p4
    p4 --> p3
    p3 --> o1
    p2 --> o2
    c1 --> c2
    c2 --> p2
    p1 --> c1
```

## 4.3 Block Architecture of an Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM) Cell

```mermaid
graph TD
    subgraph FEED[Material Feed]
        f1[Metal Foil Roll 1]
        f2[Metal Foil Roll 2 Optional]
    end

    subgraph WELD[Ultrasonic Welding Head]
        w1[Sonotrode 20kHz]
        w2[Anvil Roller]
        w3[Static Pressure Actuator 20 to 50 MPa]
    end

    subgraph POST[Post Processing CNC]
        po1[Contour Milling Spindle]
        po2[Vacuum Bed]
    end

    f1 --> w1
    w1 --> w2
    w3 --> w1
    w2 --> po1
    po1 --> po2

    OUT3[Finished Metal Part]:::out --> po2
    classDef out fill:#16a34a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Block Diagrams (and not physical drawings)?** Mermaid cannot render realistic 3D parts; the *block-level functional flow* is the standard KTU-recommended way to communicate machine architecture in theory exams. Use these templates directly in your answer sheets.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Differentiate between adhesive bonding and ultrasonic metallurgical bonding in sheet lamination AM. (3 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

**Model Answer (3 marks allocation shown):**

| Aspect | Adhesive Bonding | Ultrasonic Metallurgical Bonding |
|---|---|---|
| **Energy source** (1 mark) | Thermal energy (heated roller) | Ultrasonic vibration (20–40 kHz) + static pressure |
| **Bond type** (1 mark) | Mechanical interlocking + van der Waals | Metallurgical (solid-state diffusion) |
| **Bond strength** (1 mark) | 0.5–2 MPa | 50–200 MPa |

---

### Q2. Define "peel strength" and state its significance in LOM. (3 marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**Model Answer:**

Peel strength $\sigma_{peel}$ is defined as the force per unit width required to propagate a separation along a bonded interface.

$$
\sigma_{peel} = \frac{F_{peel}}{w} \quad \text{[N/m]}
$$

**Significance (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) LOM parts fail predominantly by **peel (mode I)** rather than shear, because the interlayer adhesive plane has weak out-of-plane strength.
- (1 mark) Typical paper-LOM peel strength is only **0.2–0.5 kN/m**, which is why **epoxy infiltration** is used as a post-process.
- (1 mark) Higher peel strength ⇒ better Z-direction mechanical integrity ⇒ suitability for functional prototypes and patterns.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read Carefully:**
> - Students **lose 2–3 marks** by writing only the *process name* without explaining the **bonding mechanism (the "why")**.
> - Students **lose another 2 marks** by skipping the **material suitability** discussion.
> - Students **lose 1 mark** for not writing units in formulas.
> - **Always include a labeled diagram** (mermaid or hand-drawn) — it earns 2 marks even if your text is weak.

---

### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 model]**

**(a) Explain in detail the working principle of Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the heated roller and the cross-hatch pattern. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Working Principle (3 marks):** LOM, developed by Helisys Inc. (now Cubic Technologies), builds parts by sequentially **laminating adhesive-coated paper sheets** (typically 0.1–0.2 mm thick) using a **heated roller** and **laser cutting** the cross-sectional contour of each layer.

**Step-by-step process (2 marks):**
1. A fresh sheet is fed over the build platform.
2. The **heated roller (T ≈ 80–120 °C)** traverses the platform, melting the adhesive (usually **EVA or polyethylene**) and bonding the new sheet to the previous layer.
3. A **CO₂ laser (10.6 µm)** cuts the contour of the current cross-section (sliced from STL).
4. The laser also cuts a **cross-hatch pattern** in the waste region to allow easy removal later.
5. The platform descends by one layer thickness, and the cycle repeats.

**Role of Heated Roller (1 mark):** Activates adhesive by raising the interface above T_g of the polymer adhesive (typically 60–80 °C), enabling polymer chain interdiffusion and bond formation upon cooling.

**Role of Cross-Hatch (1 mark):** Divides the unused paper into small **tiles (~10 × 10 mm)** that can be easily broken off and vacuumed away after build, drastically reducing post-processing time.

**(b) Describe the various bonding mechanisms used in sheet lamination processes and compare adhesive bonding with ultrasonic bonding. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Five Bonding Mechanisms (4 marks — 0.8 each):**

1. **Adhesive Bonding:** Heated roller activates pre-coated adhesive. Bond = polymer chain entanglement.
2. **Thermal Bonding:** Direct fusion of sheet material; no separate adhesive; requires T ≥ T_m or T_g.
3. **Ultrasonic Metallurgical Bonding:** 20 kHz sonotrode + static pressure breaks surface oxides → atomic diffusion → solid-state bond.
4. **Chemical/Solvent Bonding:** Solvent briefly dissolves surface; sheets fuse on solvent evaporation.
5. **Mechanical Interlocking:** Micro-features physically interlock sheets.

**Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Adhesive Bonding | Ultrasonic Bonding |
|---|---|---|
| Bond strength | 0.5–2 MPa | 50–200 MPa |
| Temperature | 80–120 °C (roller) | < 0.4 T_m (cold process) |
| Material | Paper, polymer, composites | Metals (Al, Cu, Ti) |
| Process speed | Slow (5–30 s/layer) | Fast (0.5–2 s/layer) |
| Limitations | Weak Z-direction, peel failure | Limited to ductile metals |

---

### **Question B (Choice 2) — 14 Marks**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 model]**

**(a) Explain the Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM) process with a block diagram. Derive the expression for volumetric energy density at the bond interface. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Process (3 marks):** UAM, commercialized by **Fabrisonic LLC**, bonds metal foils (typically 100–200 µm thick) using a sonotrode vibrating at **f = 20–40 kHz** under normal pressure of **20–50 MPa**. After bonding, an integral **CNC mill** removes excess material to define the part geometry.

**Step-by-step (1 mark each):**
1. Foil fed over the build table.
2. Sonotrode presses down and vibrates ultrasonically.
3. Friction + cyclic shear disrupts surface oxides.
4. Atomic diffusion across clean metal surfaces forms a **metallurgical bond** in solid state (no melting).
5. CNC mill contours the part to net shape.
6. Platform descends; cycle repeats.

**Derivation (3 marks):** *(Reproduce derivation 3.1 from Section 3 above, including the final result $E_{int} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} f^{2} A^{2} \eta \, t_w}{\delta^{2}}$)*.

**(b) A UAM machine operates at f = 20 kHz, amplitude A = 30 µm, interfacial viscosity η = 1.2 × 10⁶ Pa·s, interface thickness δ = 5 µm, and weld time t_w = 0.6 s. Compute the volumetric energy density at the bond interface. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — State the formula (1 mark):**

$$
E_{int} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} f^{2} A^{2} \eta \, t_w}{\delta^{2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values (2 marks):**

$$
E_{int} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} (20 \times 10^{3})^{2} (30 \times 10^{-6})^{2} (1.2 \times 10^{6}) (0.6)}{(5 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the numerator (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Numerator} = 2 \times 9.8696 \times 4 \times 10^{8} \times 9 \times 10^{-10} \times 1.2 \times 10^{6} \times 0.6
$$

$$
= 19.739 \times 4 \times 10^{8} \times 9 \times 10^{-10} \times 7.2 \times 10^{5}
$$

$$
= 5.124 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/m³ (intermediate)}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by denominator (1 mark):**

$$
E_{int} = \frac{5.124 \times 10^{6}}{2.5 \times 10^{-11}} = 2.05 \times 10^{17} \text{ J/m³}
$$

**Step 5 — Sanity check / Comment (1 mark):** This energy density is more than sufficient to disrupt native Al₂O₃ oxide layers (~1 nm thick) and initiate metallic bond formation, consistent with experimentally observed bond strengths of **80–90% of parent metal UTS** in UAM Al-3003 builds.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Print this!)**

- ☐ **Sheet lamination = bonding of pre-formed sheets** (NOT extrusion, NOT jetting).
- ☐ **LOM uses adhesive + heated roller + CO₂ laser**; typical materials = paper/polymer.
- ☐ **UAM uses ultrasonic sonotrode + static pressure**; typical materials = metal foils (Al, Cu, Ti).
- ☐ **Five bonding mechanisms:** Adhesive, Thermal, Ultrasonic, Chemical, Mechanical.
- ☐ **Bond strength order (ascending):** Adhesive < Thermal ≈ Chemical < Ultrasonic.
- ☐ **Peel strength is the weak link** in LOM → mitigated by epoxy infiltration.
- ☐ **UAM is a *solid-state* process** — no melting → retains base-material properties.
- ☐ **Cross-hatch pattern** in LOM enables easy waste removal.
- ☐ **Key formula (UAM):** $E_{int} = \frac{2 \pi^{2} f^{2} A^{2} \eta \, t_w}{\delta^{2}}$ — memorize and derive.
- ☐ **Key formula (general strength):** $\sigma_b = F/A$; **Peel:** $\sigma_{peel} = F_{peel}/w$.
- ☐ **KTU Pitfall:** Always write **units**; always include a **block diagram**; always explain the **"why"** of the bonding mechanism — not just the name.
- ☐ **Real-world applications:** LOM → casting patterns; UAM → aerospace graded materials, embedded sensors, battery cells.

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 2 — Sheet Lamination Bonding Mechanisms Notes.**
> *Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme PEPOT634 — NEP 2020 Aligned Outcome-Based Education.*

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview of AM Materials

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Additive Manufacturing (AM) Materials** are defined as the consumable feedstock substances — in physical forms such as **powder, filament, wire, liquid resin, sheet, or pellet** — that are selectively joined, fused, or solidified layer-by-layer under controlled energy sources (thermal, photonic, chemical, or mechanical) to fabricate a near-net-shape three-dimensional part as per ASTM F2792-12 standards.

The ASTM F2792-12 standard classifies AM processes into **seven broad categories** (VAT Photopolymerization, Material Jetting, Binder Jetting, Material Extrusion, Powder Bed Fusion, Sheet Lamination, Directed Energy Deposition), and each category mandates specific material families with strict rheological, thermal, and mechanical compatibility criteria.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The "Recipe vs. Cooking" Model

Imagine Additive Manufacturing as **3D cooking**:

- The **3D Printer** is the oven/microwave.
- The **CAD model** is the recipe (instructions).
- The **Material** is the ingredient (flour, sugar, chocolate).

Just like a cake cannot be made with raw rice alone, a metal aerospace bracket cannot be printed using PLA plastic. The material must **match the AM process chemistry and physics**.

| Real-World Analogy | Additive Manufacturing Counterpart |
|---|---|
| Flour (loose powder) | Metal powder (for SLM, EBM, BJ) |
| Noodle dough (extruded) | Thermoplastic filament (for FDM) |
| Candle wax (melted layer-by-layer) | Photopolymer resin (for SLA) |
| Solder wire (fed and melted) | Metal wire (for DED, WAAM) |

---

## 1.3 Broad Classification of AM Materials

The materials universe in AM is broadly partitioned into **five functional families**:

> [!NOTE]
> **The Big Five AM Material Families**
> 1. **Polymers** — Thermoplastics, Thermosets, Elastomers
> 2. **Metals & Alloys** — Steels, Titanium, Aluminium, Nickel-based superalloys
> 3. **Ceramics** — Oxides, Carbides, Nitrides, Bio-ceramics
> 4. **Composites** — Fiber-reinforced, Particle-reinforced
> 5. **Biomaterials & Smart Materials** — Hydrogels, Shape Memory Alloys (SMA), Piezoelectric

---

## 1.4 Standard Physical Forms of AM Feedstock

| Form | Typical Dimensions | Processes Used |
|---|---|---|
| Powder (metals/polymers) | Particle size: **15–100 µm** | SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM, BJ |
| Filament (1.75 mm / 2.85 mm) | Diameter tolerance: ±0.05 mm | FDM/FFF |
| Liquid Resin | Viscosity: **250–1000 cP** | SLA, DLP, Material Jetting |
| Wire | Diameter: **0.8–1.6 mm** | DED, WAAM, EBF³ |
| Pellets | Ø 3–5 mm | Pellet-based extrusion (large scale) |
| Sheet/foil | Thickness: **0.1–0.2 mm** | LOM, Sheet Lamination |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always mention the **form of feedstock** along with the material name. For example, instead of writing "PA12 nylon is used in SLS", write "PA12 nylon in powder form (particle size ~55 µm) is used in SLS".

---

## 1.5 Critical Material Selection Criteria for AM

The selection of a material for a specific AM process is governed by **four pillars** of property assessment:

$$ \text{Printability} = f(\underbrace{\text{Rheology}}_{\text{Flow}}, \underbrace{\text{Thermal}}_{\text{Melt/Solidify}}, \underbrace{\text{Optical}}_{\text{Cure}}, \underbrace{\text{Mechanical}}_{\text{Strength}}) $$

The four pillars are explained below:

- **Rheological Properties** — Viscosity, surface tension, flowability (for powders: Hausner Ratio < **1.25**).
- **Thermal Properties** — Glass transition temperature ($T_g$), melting point ($T_m$), crystallization temperature ($T_c$), coefficient of thermal expansion (CTE).
- **Optical Properties** — For photopolymers: critical exposure energy $E_c$ and penetration depth $D_p$ (Jacob's Working Curve parameters).
- **Mechanical Properties** — Tensile strength, Young's modulus, elongation at break, fatigue resistance.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Polymer Materials for Additive Manufacturing

### 2.1.1 Thermoplastics (FDM / FFF Dominant)

Thermoplastics are the most commercially mature AM materials due to their reversible melt-solidify behavior. They are supplied as **filaments (Ø 1.75 mm or 2.85 mm)** and extruded through a heated nozzle (typical extrusion temperature: **190–260 °C**).

| Polymer | Glass Transition ($T_g$) | Melting Point ($T_m$) | Tensile Strength | AM Suitability |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **PLA** (Polylactic Acid) | 60 °C | 150–160 °C | 50 MPa | High — biodegradable, easy to print |
| **ABS** (Acrylonitrile Butadiene Styrene) | 105 °C | 200–250 °C | 40 MPa | High — requires heated bed (~110 °C) |
| **PETG** (Polyethylene Terephthalate Glycol) | 80 °C | 220–230 °C | 50 MPa | High — chemical resistant |
| **Nylon (PA6, PA12)** | 50–60 °C | 175–260 °C | 45–85 MPa | High — used in SLS too |
| **TPU** (Thermoplastic Polyurethane) | -30 °C | 200–220 °C | 40–60 MPa | Medium — elastomeric |
| **PC** (Polycarbonate) | 147 °C | 265–300 °C | 60–70 MPa | High — needs 270 °C extrusion |
| **PEEK** (Polyetheretherketone) | 143 °C | 343 °C | 90–100 MPa | High-performance, aerospace |

### 2.1.2 Photopolymer Resins (SLA / DLP / Material Jetting)

Photopolymers are **liquid thermosetting resins** that undergo **photopolymerization** under UV light (typically **365–405 nm** wavelength). The process is governed by the Beer-Lambert Law of light attenuation:

$$ C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) $$

Where:
- $C_d$ = **Cure depth** (µm)
- $D_p$ = **Penetration depth** of the resin (µm)
- $E_0$ = **Incident energy dose** at the surface (mJ/cm²)
- $E_c$ = **Critical exposure energy** required to initiate gelation (mJ/cm²)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jacob's Working Curve:** Plotting $C_d$ versus $\ln(E_0)$ yields a straight line whose slope is $D_p$ and whose x-intercept (where $C_d = 0$) gives $E_c$. This is the **single most tested formula** in KTU AM exams.

### 2.1.3 Polymer Powders for Powder Bed Fusion (SLS)

In **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**, polymer powders (typically **PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK**) are partially melted by a CO₂ laser (wavelength ~**10.6 µm**). The key parameter is the **energy density** applied:

$$ E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$

Where:
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (mm)
- $E_d$ = Volumetric energy density (J/mm³)

**Typical SLS polymer:** PA12 (Nylon 12) with particle size **45–90 µm**, Hausner ratio **< 1.20**, bulk density **~0.45 g/cm³**.

---

## 2.2 Metal Materials for Additive Manufacturing

### 2.2.1 Why Metals in AM Are Critical

Metal AM has revolutionized **aerospace, medical, and automotive** industries because of its ability to produce **complex lattice structures, conformal cooling channels, and lightweight topologies** impossible by conventional machining.

### 2.2.2 Common Metal Powder Chemistries

| Metal / Alloy | Composition | AM Process | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **316L Stainless Steel** | Fe-Cr18-Ni10-Mo2 | SLM, DMLS, BJ | Medical implants, marine |
| **17-4 PH Steel** | Fe-Cr16-Ni4-Cu4 | SLM, DMLS | Aerospace structural |
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | Ti-Al6-V4 | SLM, EBM, DED | Aerospace, biomedical implants |
| **AlSi10Mg** | Al-Si10-Mg0.4 | SLM, DMLS | Automotive, lightweight |
| **Inconel 718** | Ni-Cr19-Fe18-Nb5 | SLM, EBM, DED | Jet engine hot sections |
| **Co-Cr-Mo** | Co-Cr28-Mo6 | SLM, EBM | Dental, orthopedic |
| **Copper (Cu)** | Pure Cu / CuCrZr | SLM, DED | Heat exchangers, inductors |
| **Maraging Steel 300** | Fe-Ni18-Co9-Mo5 | SLM, DED | Tooling, dies |

> [!NOTE]
> **Maraging steel 300** is the most widely used tool steel in metal AM because it achieves **~58 HRC** hardness after a simple aging treatment at **490 °C for 6 hours**, without distortion.

### 2.2.3 Powder Characterization Metrics

The quality of a metal powder for AM is judged by:

- **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** — Laser diffraction method (ISO 13320).
- **Flowability** — Hall Flowmeter funnel test; Hausner Ratio $HR = \rho_{tapped}/\rho_{bulk}$.
- **Apparent Density** — ASTM B212.
- **Tap Density** — ASTM B527.
- **Sphericity** — Image analysis; should be **> 0.85** for good flow.
- **Internal Porosity** — Computed Tomography (CT) scanning.

---

## 2.3 Ceramic Materials for AM

Ceramics are notoriously difficult to process in AM due to their **high melting points (often > 2000 °C)** and **brittle nature**. Three approaches exist:

1. **Indirect Printing (Binder Jetting + Sintering)** — Ceramic powder + binder → green part → sintering at **1200–1700 °C**.
2. **Slurry-based (SLA / DLP with ceramic-filled resin)** — Photopolymer + ceramic filler (e.g., Al₂O₃, ZrO₂, SiC).
3. **Powder Bed Fusion with Preheating** — Used for SiC, Si₃N₄ with high-power lasers.

| Ceramic | Melting Point | AM Process | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alumina (Al₂O₃)** | 2072 °C | BJ + Sintering | Bioceramics, cutting tools |
| **Zirconia (ZrO₂)** | 2715 °C | DLP (ceramic-filled resin) | Dental crowns |
| **Silicon Carbide (SiC)** | 2730 °C | BJ + Sintering, PBF | Aerospace thermal structures |
| **Tricalcium Phosphate (TCP)** | 1670 °C | BJ, Extrusion | Bone scaffolds |

---

## 2.4 Composite Materials for AM

Composites combine a **matrix material** with a **reinforcement** to yield superior mechanical/thermal properties.

| Composite Type | Matrix | Reinforcement | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fiber-reinforced** | PA12, PEEK, PLA | Carbon fiber, Glass fiber | Aerospace stiffeners |
| **Particle-reinforced** | ABS, Nylon | Metal, ceramic particles | Functional parts |
| **Bio-composites** | PLA, PHA | Wood, Bamboo fibers | Sustainable products |
| **Nanocomposites** | Photopolymer resin | Graphene, CNT, nano-clay | Electronics, EMI shielding |

The **rule of mixtures** (a frequently tested concept) gives the effective property:

$$ E_c = E_m \cdot V_m + E_f \cdot V_f $$

Where $E_c$ = composite modulus, $E_m$, $E_f$ = matrix and fiber moduli, $V_m$, $V_f$ = volume fractions with $V_m + V_f = 1$.

---

## 2.5 Biomaterials and Smart Materials

### 2.5.1 Biomaterials

- **Titanium Ti-6Al-4V** — bone implants (biocompatible, osseointegrative).
- **PEEK** — spinal cages (radiolucent, bone-like modulus ~3–4 GPa).
- **PLGA, PCL, PLA** — biodegradable scaffolds for tissue engineering.
- **Hydroxyapatite (HA)** — bone graft substitutes.

### 2.5.2 Smart Materials

- **Shape Memory Alloys (SMA)** — NiTi (Nitinol); recovers shape upon heating above transformation temperature ($A_f$).
- **Piezoelectric polymers (PVDF)** — sensors and actuators.
- **Hydrogels** — responsive to pH, temperature; used in 4D bioprinting.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_d = D_p \cdot \ln(E_0 / E_c)$ | Cure depth in photopolymerization | µm |
| 2 | $E_d = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ | Volumetric energy density in PBF | J/mm³ |
| 3 | $E_c = E_m V_m + E_f V_f$ | Rule of mixtures (modulus) | GPa |
| 4 | $HR = \rho_{tapped} / \rho_{bulk}$ | Hausner ratio (flowability) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\phi = \rho_{apparent} / \rho_{true}$ | Packing fraction / Relative density | dimensionless |
| 6 | $\alpha_L = \Delta L / (L_0 \cdot \Delta T)$ | Linear thermal expansion | µm/m·°C |
| 7 | $\Delta H_{fusion}$ | Enthalpy of fusion (latent heat) | J/g |
| 8 | $\eta(T) = \eta_0 \exp[E_a / (RT)]$ | Arrhenius viscosity model | Pa·s |

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Why this matters in industry:**
> - **GE Aviation LEAP fuel nozzle** — printed in **Co-Cr alloy** using DMLS; consolidated **20 parts into 1**, reducing weight by **25%** and improving fuel efficiency.
> - **Align Technology Invisalign** — uses **SLA-printed dental models** from photopolymer resin, producing **millions** of aligners annually.
> - **NASA rocket injectors** — Inconel 718 DED-printed components withstand **3000 °C** combustion gases.
>
> The **material** is the silent hero — without it, no AM process can succeed.
---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Jacob's Working Curve (Cure Depth Equation)

**Problem Statement:** A photopolymer resin has penetration depth $D_p = 0.35$ mm and critical energy $E_c = 12$ mJ/cm². The exposure energy used in an SLA printer is $E_0 = 75$ mJ/cm². Find the cure depth $C_d$.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing equation:**

The Beer-Lambert law for light absorption in a photopolymer gives the local intensity at depth $z$:

$$ I(z) = I_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} $$

where $I_0$ is the intensity at the surface. Since energy $E$ is proportional to intensity for a fixed exposure time, we have:

$$ E(z) = E_0 \cdot e^{-z/D_p} $$

**Step 2 — Apply the gelation criterion:**

Cure (gelation) occurs only where $E(z) \geq E_c$. The maximum depth where this holds is $z = C_d$. So at $z = C_d$, we set $E(z) = E_c$:

$$ E_c = E_0 \cdot e^{-C_d/D_p} $$

**Step 3 — Solve algebraically for $C_d$:**

$$ \frac{E_c}{E_0} = e^{-C_d/D_p} $$

$$ \ln\left(\frac{E_c}{E_0}\right) = -\frac{C_d}{D_p} $$

$$ C_d = D_p \cdot \ln\left(\frac{E_0}{E_c}\right) $$

**Step 4 — Substitute the numerical values:**

$$ C_d = 0.35 \, \text{mm} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{75}{12}\right) $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the natural logarithm:**

$$ \frac{75}{12} = 6.25 $$

$$ \ln(6.25) \approx 1.8326 $$

**Step 6 — Compute the final cure depth:**

$$ C_d = 0.35 \times 1.8326 \approx 0.6414 \, \text{mm} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ \boxed{C_d \approx 0.641 \, \text{mm} = 641 \, \mu\text{m}} $$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Volumetric Energy Density in Powder Bed Fusion

**Problem Statement:** An SLM machine uses laser power $P = 200$ W, scan speed $v = 800$ mm/s, hatch spacing $h = 0.12$ mm, and layer thickness $t = 30$ µm. Calculate the volumetric energy density $E_d$ and comment on whether it lies in the conduction or keyhole regime.

**Step 1 — State the governing equation:**

$$ E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} $$

**Step 2 — Convert all units to consistent SI (mm, s):**

- $P = 200$ W (already in J/s)
- $v = 800$ mm/s
- $h = 0.12$ mm
- $t = 30 \, \mu\text{m} = 0.030 \, \text{mm}$

**Step 3 — Compute the denominator:**

$$ v \cdot h \cdot t = 800 \times 0.12 \times 0.030 $$

$$ = 800 \times 0.0036 $$

$$ = 2.88 \, \text{mm}^3/\text{s} $$

**Step 4 — Compute $E_d$:**

$$ E_d = \frac{200}{2.88} \approx 69.44 \, \text{J/mm}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Compare to reference ranges for Ti-6Al-4V:**

| Regime | Energy Density Range | Result |
|---|---|---|
| Lack of Fusion | $E_d < 40$ J/mm³ | Porosity, weak parts |
| **Conduction Mode (optimal)** | **40–100 J/mm³** | **✓ Current value** |
| Keyhole Mode | $E_d > 100$ J/mm³ | Deep vapor cavities |

**Final Answer:**

$$ \boxed{E_d \approx 69.44 \, \text{J/mm}^3 \quad \text{(Conduction regime, optimal)}} $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Rule of Mixtures for a Carbon-Fiber Reinforced PEEK Composite

**Problem Statement:** A continuous carbon-fiber reinforced PEEK composite has fiber volume fraction $V_f = 0.45$. Given $E_{PEEK} = 3.5$ GPa and $E_{CF} = 230$ GPa, calculate the longitudinal modulus $E_L$ of the composite.

**Step 1 — Apply the rule of mixtures (Voigt model — iso-strain):**

$$ E_L = E_f \cdot V_f + E_m \cdot (1 - V_f) $$

**Step 2 — Substitute the volume fractions:**

$$ V_m = 1 - V_f = 1 - 0.45 = 0.55 $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the modulus values:**

$$ E_L = (230)(0.45) + (3.5)(0.55) $$

**Step 4 — Compute each term:**

$$ 230 \times 0.45 = 103.5 \, \text{GPa} $$

$$ 3.5 \times 0.55 = 1.925 \, \text{GPa} $$

**Step 5 — Sum:**

$$ E_L = 103.5 + 1.925 = 105.425 \, \text{GPa} $$

**Final Answer:**

$$ \boxed{E_L \approx 105.4 \, \text{GPa}} $$

This value is consistent with reported experimental data for CF/PEEK (typically **95–110 GPa**), confirming the validity of the iso-strain assumption.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process-Material Compatibility Map

| AM Process (ASTM F2792) | Compatible Material | Form | Energy Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| VAT Photopolymerization (SLA, DLP) | Photopolymer resin, Ceramic-filled resin | Liquid | UV laser / projector |
| Material Jetting | Photopolymer, Wax | Liquid droplets | UV lamp |
| Binder Jetting | Metal powder, Sand, Plaster | Powder | Print head binder |
| Material Extrusion (FDM) | Thermoplastics, Composites | Filament / Pellets | Heated nozzle |
| Powder Bed Fusion (SLM) | Metals (steel, Ti, Al) | Powder (15–45 µm) | Fiber laser (~1070 nm) |
| Powder Bed Fusion (SLS) | Polymers (PA12, PA11) | Powder (45–90 µm) | CO₂ laser (~10.6 µm) |
| Powder Bed Fusion (EBM) | Ti-6Al-4V, Co-Cr | Powder (45–105 µm) | Electron beam |
| Directed Energy Deposition | Metals (wire/powder) | Wire / Powder | Laser / Plasma / Arc |
| Sheet Lamination (LOM, UAM) | Paper, Metal foil, Polymer | Sheet | Laser / Ultrasonic |
---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 AM Material Family Tree (Hierarchical Classification)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[AM Materials]
```
