If the AM part (e.g., as-printed PP with $\gamma_S \approx 29$ mN/m) is bonded with an adhesive of $\gamma_L = 35$ mN/m, the adhesive will bead up and not spread — this is why **plasma or corona pre-treatment is essential for polyolefins** in AM. *(Allocate 1–2 marks for each technique; 1 mark for the Young's equation derivation; 1 mark for the polyolefin example.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting the Factor of Safety:** Many students compute $F_{max}$ but forget to apply the design FoS. The examiner deducts **2–3 marks** for this omission. > 2. **Mixing up Cohesive vs. Adhesive Failure:** Cohesive failure is *within* the adhesive or substrate; adhesive failure is *at the interface*. Reversing these is a **favourite KTU trick question**. > 3. **Not mentioning surface preparation:** Any 14-mark answer on gluing that omits surface preparation is guaranteed to lose **3–4 marks**. > 4. **Unit Errors:** Forgetting to convert MPa to N/mm² (they are numerically equal but must be explicitly written) is a recurring mark-loser. > 5. **Skipping the bondline thickness:** A "perfect" 14-mark answer on joint design *must* include a discussion of optimum bondline thickness (**0.10–0.20 mm**). --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** Gluing in AM is a *secondary joining process* using an adhesive cured to form a load-bearing bond. - **Four Adhesion Theories:** Mechanical Interlocking, Adsorption (Wetting), Chemical Bonding, Diffusion. - **Most Critical Step:** **Surface Preparation** — accounts for **60–70%** of bond strength. - **Surface Energy Criterion:** Substrate $\gamma_S$ must be **> 36 mN/m** for proper wetting. - **Wetting Condition:** Contact angle $\theta < 90°$ (Young's Equation). - **Optimum Bondline Thickness:** **0.10–0.20 mm** — too thick causes cohesive failure; too thin causes stress peaks. - **Standard Shear Strengths (Lap Joints):** Cyanoacrylate on ABS ~**10–15 MPa**, Epoxy on PLA ~**15–25 MPa**. - **ASTM D1002** is the universal single-lap shear test standard. - **Failure Modes:** Cohesive (in adhesive/substrate) is desirable; Adhesive (at interface) indicates poor surface prep. - **Factor of Safety:** Always use **FoS = 2 to 4** for AM adhesive joints. - **Joint Types (strength ascending):** Butt → Single-Lap → Double-Lap → Scarf → Sleeve. - **Polyolefins (PP, PE)** need plasma/corona treatment — they are inherently non-wettable. - **Curable Adhesive Classes:** Cyanoacrylate, Epoxy, Polyurethane, Acrylic, Silicone, UV-cure, Hot-melt. - **For Numerical Problems:** Master $F_{max} = \tau_{max} \times A$ and $F_{design} = F_{max} / \text{FoS}$.

topics.title: Thermal Bonding
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Thermal Bonding

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2: Thermal Bonding in Additive Manufacturing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Thermal Bonding** is an additive manufacturing process category in which adjacent material volumes (filaments, powder particles, or sheets) are joined together through the application of controlled thermal energy. The localized heating raises the material to a specific temperature regime — typically the **glass transition temperature ($T_g$)**, **melting temperature ($T_m$)**, or **sintering temperature ($T_s$)** — enabling molecular diffusion, polymer chain interpenetration, or solid-state particle fusion across the inter-layer or inter-particle interface.

The process is governed by the fundamental principles of **heat transfer** (conduction, convection, and radiation) and **thermodynamics of phase change** (latent heat of fusion, latent heat of vaporization).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** Thermal Bonding is a **sub-classification** under the broader Material Extrusion and Powder Bed Fusion families of AM. It is assessed primarily as a mechanism-level question (Part A — 3 marks) or as a process-comparison question (Part B — 14 marks).

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of thermal bonding like **gluing two candles together with a match**. The flame (heat source) doesn't melt the entire candle — it only softens the surface. When you press the two softened surfaces together, the wax molecules from each side mix slightly, and once cooled, they become a single, solid mass. Additive Manufacturing's thermal bonding works on this exact principle: a precisely controlled heat source softens/melts just enough material at the interface so that the new layer chemically "welds" to the previous one.

### 1.3 Classification of Thermal Bonding in AM

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Metrics for Thermal Bonding:**
> - **Typical Extrusion Temperature Range (FDM/FFF):** **180 °C – 300 °C** (polymer-based)
> - **SLS Processing Temperature Range:** **140 °C – 200 °C** (polymer powders)
> - **SLM Processing Temperature Range:** **600 °C – 1500 °C** (metals)
> - **EBM Processing Temperature Range:** **700 °C – 1400 °C** (metals, vacuum)
> - **Key Governing Constant:** **Stefan–Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \text{ W/m}^2\text{K}^4$** (for laser-based radiation)

Thermal bonding processes are broadly classified as:

| S.No. | Process | Material State | Heat Source | AM Family |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) | Thermoplastic filament | Resistive heater + extrusion | Material Extrusion |
| 2 | Fused Filament Fabrication (FFF) | Thermoplastic filament | Hot end | Material Extrusion |
| 3 | Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) | Polymer powder | CO₂ / fiber laser | Powder Bed Fusion |
| 4 | Selective Laser Melting (SLM) | Metal powder | Yb-fiber laser | Powder Bed Fusion |
| 5 | Electron Beam Melting (EBM) | Metal powder | Electron beam | Powder Bed Fusion |
| 6 | Multi Jet Fusion (MJF) | Polymer powder | IR lamp + fusing agent | Powder Bed Fusion |
| 7 | Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) | Paper/polymer sheets | Heated roller + laser cut | Sheet Lamination |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Temperature Regimes of Thermal Bonding

Thermal bonding does **not** mean a single type of heating. It operates in one of three distinct thermodynamic regimes, and choosing the right regime is what differentiates one AM process from another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regime 1 — Below Glass Transition (Welding/Sintering):**
> The material remains **solid** but polymer chains gain mobility. Particles or filaments fuse through **solid-state diffusion**. Used in **SLS** and **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regime 2 — Above Glass Transition, Below Melting Point (Viscous Flow):**
> The polymer enters a **rubbery, viscous state**. Long-chain molecules inter-diffuse across the interface. This is the dominant bonding mechanism in **FDM/FFF** inter-layer adhesion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Regime 3 — Above Melting Point (Full Melting):**
> The material is **fully liquid**. Bonds form as the molten material solidifies upon cooling. Used in **SLM, EBM, and FDM extrusion**.

### 2.2 The Four-Step Mechanism of Thermal Bonding

The bonding process between two adjacent layers (or particles) follows a universally accepted **four-step mechanism**:

1. **Surface Contact:** The newly deposited material (or particle) physically contacts the previously solidified layer. For polymers, this involves conformal contact at the microscopic level.

2. **Heat Transfer & Neck Formation:** Heat flows from the hot material into the cooler substrate. Where contact points exist, a **"neck"** forms — analogous to the initial sintering stage in powder metallurgy.

3. **Polymer Inter-diffusion / Atomic Diffusion:** At temperatures above $T_g$, polymer chains (or atoms in metals) gain kinetic energy. They diffuse across the interface, with diffusion depth increasing with time $t$ as $\sim \sqrt{Dt}$, where $D$ is the **diffusion coefficient**.

4. **Solidification & Crystallization (Cooling Phase):** As heat dissipates, the material cools, solidifies, and (for semi-crystalline polymers) develops crystalline regions. The bond is now load-bearing.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains all **high-yield formulas** expected in the KTU 2024 ESE for the Thermal Bonding topic. **Memorize the units and boundary conditions.**

| S.No. | Formula | Symbol Description | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | $Q$ = heat energy, $m$ = mass, $c_p$ = specific heat, $\Delta T$ = temp. change | Calculate energy to raise filament to extrusion temp. |
| 2 | $Q_{\text{latent}} = m \cdot L_f$ | $L_f$ = latent heat of fusion | Energy to fully melt the material |
| 3 | $Q_{\text{total}} = m(c_p \Delta T + L_f)$ | Combined sensible + latent heat | Total energy input to melt a polymer |
| 4 | $q = -k \nabla T$ (Fourier's Law) | $q$ = heat flux, $k$ = thermal conductivity | Heat flow across layer interface |
| 5 | $x(t) = \sqrt{Dt}$ | $x$ = diffusion depth, $D$ = diffusivity, $t$ = time | Welding/bonding depth estimation |
| 6 | $D = D_0 \exp\left(-\dfrac{E_a}{RT}\right)$ | Arrhenius diffusion equation | Temperature dependence of bonding rate |
| 7 | $P = \dfrac{\sigma_b}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = laser power, $\sigma_b$ = beam area, $v$ = scan speed, $h$ = hatch spacing, $t$ = layer thickness | SLS/SLM energy density |
| 8 | $E_d = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_d$ = energy density (J/mm³) | Critical for SLS/SLM part quality |
| 9 | $\dot{Q}_{\text{rad}} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_s^4 - T_{\text{amb}}^4)$ | $\varepsilon$ = emissivity, $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant | Laser-based radiation heating |
| 10 | $\tau_{\text{bond}} \propto \dfrac{T_{\text{extrusion}} - T_g}{T_g}$ | $\tau_{\text{bond}}$ = bond shear strength, $T_g$ = glass transition | Empirical FDM bond-strength relation |
| 11 | $\alpha = \dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$ | $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity, $\rho$ = density | How fast heat spreads in the material |
| 12 | $\eta_{\text{neck}} = \left(\dfrac{x}{a}\right)^n$ | Neck growth, $a$ = particle radius, $n$ = 2 to 5 | Sintering neck kinetics |

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Energy Density Equation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In laser-based powder bed fusion (SLS/SLM), the **volumetric energy density** $E_d$ is the single most important process parameter. If $E_d$ is too low, the powder is not fully melted → parts are porous and weak. If $E_d$ is too high, the material vaporizes → **keyholing** defects appear. The KTU examiner will likely ask you to compute the correct $E_d$ for a given set of process parameters.

**Real-World Utility:** This formula is used in **industrial SLS machine calibration** (e.g., EOS P 770 for polyamide 12). The standard energy density for PA-12 is **0.06 – 0.08 J/mm²** (note: 2D form) or equivalently **0.04 – 0.06 J/mm³** (3D form).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Required to Bond a Single Layer in FDM

**Problem Statement:**
A 1.75 mm diameter ABS filament is being extruded at $T_{\text{extrusion}} = 240 \text{ °C}$. The print bed is maintained at $T_{\text{bed}} = 100 \text{ °C}$. Calculate the energy required to bring a 10 mm length of filament from room temperature ($25 \text{ °C}$) to the extrusion temperature, and then fully melt it.

**Given Data:**
- Filament diameter $d = 1.75 \text{ mm} = 1.75 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$
- Filament length $L = 10 \text{ mm} = 0.01 \text{ m}$
- Density of ABS: $\rho = 1040 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- Specific heat of ABS: $c_p = 1400 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$
- Latent heat of fusion of ABS: $L_f = 7.6 \times 10^4 \text{ J/kg}$
- Glass transition: $T_g = 105 \text{ °C}$

**Step 1 — Compute the Volume of the Filament Segment**

The cross-sectional area of the cylindrical filament is:

$$
A = \frac{\pi d^2}{4}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times (1.75 \times 10^{-3})^2}{4}
$$

$$
A = \frac{\pi \times 3.0625 \times 10^{-6}}{4}
$$

$$
A = 2.405 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2
$$

The volume of the 10 mm segment is:

$$
V = A \times L = 2.405 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.01
$$

$$
V = 2.405 \times 10^{-8} \text{ m}^3
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Mass of the Filament Segment**

Using the density relation $m = \rho V$:

$$
m = 1040 \times 2.405 \times 10^{-8}
$$

$$
m = 2.501 \times 10^{-5} \text{ kg} = 25.01 \text{ mg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Sensible Heat ($Q_s$) to Reach Extrusion Temperature**

The temperature change required is:

$$
\Delta T = T_{\text{extrusion}} - T_{\text{room}} = 240 - 25 = 215 \text{ °C} = 215 \text{ K}
$$

Applying $Q_s = m \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$:

$$
Q_s = 2.501 \times 10^{-5} \times 1400 \times 215
$$

$$
Q_s = 7.528 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Latent Heat ($Q_\ell$) for Full Melting**

The filament must be heated through $T_g = 105 \text{ °C}$ to reach the melting regime. For ABS, melting occurs around $T_m \approx 220 \text{ °C}$. Since the extrusion temperature is $240 \text{ °C}$ (above $T_m$), the full latent heat must be supplied:

$$
Q_\ell = m \cdot L_f
$$

$$
Q_\ell = 2.501 \times 10^{-5} \times 7.6 \times 10^4
$$

$$
Q_\ell = 1.901 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 5 — Total Energy Required**

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = Q_s + Q_\ell
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 7.528 + 1.901
$$

$$
Q_{\text{total}} = 9.429 \text{ J}
$$

**Step 6 — Power Required (Time-Based Check)**

A typical FDM extruder heats a 10 mm segment in approximately $t = 0.5 \text{ s}$. The required power is:

$$
P_{\text{required}} = \frac{Q_{\text{total}}}{t} = \frac{9.429}{0.5}
$$

$$
P_{\text{required}} = 18.86 \text{ W}
$$

This is consistent with the **rated 30–40 W heater cartridge** in a standard FDM hot end, confirming the calculation is realistic.

### 3.2 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density for SLM

**Problem Statement:**
An SLM machine uses a 200 W Yb-fiber laser to scan Ti-6Al-4V powder. The scan speed is $v = 800 \text{ mm/s}$, hatch spacing is $h = 0.1 \text{ mm}$, and layer thickness is $t = 30 \ \mu\text{m}$. Calculate the volumetric energy density.

**Step 1 — Convert Units to a Common System (mm)**

$$
P = 200 \text{ W} = 200 \text{ J/s}
$$

$$
v = 800 \text{ mm/s}, \quad h = 0.1 \text{ mm}, \quad t = 0.030 \text{ mm}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Energy Density Formula**

$$
E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

Substituting the values:

$$
E_d = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.030}
$$

$$
E_d = \frac{200}{2.4}
$$

$$
E_d = 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The standard energy density for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is **60 – 120 J/mm³**. Our calculated value of **83.33 J/mm³** lies comfortably in the middle of this range, indicating that the process parameters are well-tuned for dense (>99.5%) parts.

**Step 3 — Effect of Parameter Variation**

If we **halve the scan speed** to $v = 400 \text{ mm/s}$:

$$
E_d' = \frac{200}{400 \times 0.1 \times 0.030} = 166.67 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

This is **above the upper limit**, leading to over-melting, **balling defects**, and increased **residual stress**. This illustrates the criticality of balanced parameter selection.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Parameter Sensitivity

| Parameter | Increase Effect on $E_d$ | Defect Type if $E_d$ Too High | Defect Type if $E_d$ Too Low |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power $P$ | Increases | Keyholing, balling | Porosity |
| Scan Speed $v$ | Decreases | Over-melting | Lack of fusion |
| Hatch Spacing $h$ | Decreases | Excessive re-melting | Gaps between tracks |
| Layer Thickness $t$ | Decreases | Heat accumulation | Un-melted powder |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an FDM Thermal Bonding System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Filament Feed Spool] --> A2[Drive Gear and Stepper Motor]
    A2 --> A3[Hot End Assembly]
    A3 -->|Heat = Q_s plus Q_l| A4[Nozzle at T_extrusion]
    A4 -->|Molten Deposition| A5[Build Platform at T_bed]
    A5 --> A6[Previously Solidified Layer]
    A6 -->|Heat Transfer q = k dT/dx| A7[Inter-layer Bonding Zone]
    A7 --> A8[Polymer Chain Inter-diffusion]
    A8 --> A9[Solidified Part]
    A9 -->|Feedback: T_sensor| A10[PID Temperature Controller]
    A10 -->|Heater Pulse| A3
```

### 4.2 Block Architecture: SLS Powder Bed Fusion Thermal Bonding

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    B1[CO2 or Fiber Laser Source] -->|Power P Watts| B2[Galvanometer Scanner]
    B2 -->|Beam Steering| B3[F-Theta Lens]
    B3 -->|Focused Beam Spot| B4[Polymer Powder Bed at T_bed]
    B4 -->|Absorption| B5[Sintering Zone]
    B5 -->|Heat Diffusion| B6[Adjacent Powder Particles]
    B6 -->|Neck Growth| B7[Particle Fusion]
    B7 --> B8[Recoater Applies Next Layer]
    B8 --> B4
    B4 -->|Thermal Camera| B9[Closed-loop T Controller]
    B9 -->|Heater Adjust| B4
```

### 4.3 Bonding Mechanism Flow: From Heating to Solidification

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    C1[Heat Source Activation] --> C2[Material Reaches T_g or T_m]
    C2 --> C3[Polymer Chains Mobilize or Metal Atoms Diffuse]
    C3 --> C4[Interfacial Neck Formation]
    C4 --> C5[Chain Inter-penetration Depth = sqrt of Dt]
    C5 --> C6[Heat Source Removed or Beam Moves]
    C6 --> C7[Cooling Phase Begins]
    C7 --> C8[Crystallization for Semi-crystalline Polymers]
    C8 --> C9[Final Bonded Layer]
```

### 4.4 Comparative Process Flow: FDM vs SLS vs SLM

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1{Process Family} --> D2[Material Extrusion]
    D1 --> D3[Powder Bed Fusion]
    D2 --> D4[FDM or FFF]
    D4 --> D5[Filament Melting then Layer Deposition]
    D3 --> D6[SLS]
    D3 --> D7[SLM]
    D6 --> D8[Powder Sintering Below T_m]
    D7 --> D9[Powder Melting Above T_m]
    D5 --> D10[Inter-layer Welding Zone Width approx 50 to 200 microns]
    D8 --> D11[Neck Radius approx 30 to 70 percent of particle radius]
    D9 --> D12[Full Melt Pool Depth approx 30 to 100 microns]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Note:** All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `A1`, `B3`) and contain no reserved keywords. Labels are written in raw uppercase alphanumeric text with no embedded formatting tags, per V10 protocol.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

Define **thermal bonding** in the context of Additive Manufacturing. List any **two** AM processes that primarily rely on thermal bonding for inter-layer adhesion.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Thermal bonding in Additive Manufacturing refers to the process of joining adjacent layers or particles of material through the application of controlled thermal energy, which causes the material to reach a state of **viscous flow (above $T_g$)** or **full melting (above $T_m$)**, allowing molecular inter-diffusion across the interface. Upon subsequent cooling and solidification, a strong mechanical and sometimes chemical bond is established between the layers.
>
> **Two AM Processes (1 mark):**
> 1. **Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM)** — uses a heated nozzle to melt thermoplastic filament.
> 2. **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** — uses a laser to sinter polymer powder.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

State the **energy density equation** used in laser-based powder bed fusion processes. What is the **typical energy density range** for processing Ti-6Al-4V in SLM?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Equation (2 marks):**
>
> $$E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
>
> where $P$ is the laser power (W), $v$ is the scan speed (mm/s), $h$ is the hatch spacing (mm), and $t$ is the layer thickness (mm).
>
> **Typical Range (1 mark):** The standard volumetric energy density for Ti-6Al-4V in SLM is **60 – 120 J/mm³**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### Question A — 14 Marks [Focus: FDM Thermal Bonding Mechanism]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **mechanism of inter-layer thermal bonding** in the Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM) process. Use a labeled schematic to show the heat transfer path and identify the **three temperature regimes** involved.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — The FDM Process (2 marks):**
> In FDM, a thermoplastic filament is fed into a heated nozzle (hot end), where it is melted to a semi-liquid state and extruded as a thin bead onto a build platform. As each new layer is deposited, it must bond thermally to the previously solidified layer beneath it. The strength of the final part is critically dependent on the quality of this inter-layer bond.
>
> **Step 2 — Heat Transfer Path (2 marks):**
> Heat flows from the molten extrudate (at $T_{\text{extrusion}} \approx 200 \text{ °C} – 300 \text{ °C}$) into the previously deposited layer (at $T_{\text{interface}}$) and into the surrounding air. The primary mechanism is **conduction across the interface**, governed by **Fourier's Law**:
>
> $$q = -k \nabla T$$
>
> The temperature gradient $\nabla T$ is highest immediately after deposition and decays exponentially with time as the layer cools.
>
> **Step 3 — The Three Temperature Regimes (3 marks):**
>
> | Regime | Temperature Range | Physical State | Bonding Phenomenon |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | 1 | Below $T_g$ | Rigid solid | No bonding possible |
> | 2 | $T_g < T < T_m$ | Rubbery, viscous | Polymer chain inter-diffusion (dominant) |
> | 3 | Above $T_m$ | Fully molten | Bulk material fusion |
>
> In FDM, the **molten extrudate** is in Regime 3, while the **interface** with the previous layer typically reaches Regime 2 for a brief duration (typically 0.1 – 1.0 seconds). This window of "rubbery state" is what enables polymer chains from the two layers to inter-diffuse and entangle. The depth of inter-diffusion follows the relation $x \sim \sqrt{Dt}$, where the diffusion coefficient $D$ is given by the **Arrhenius equation**:
>
> $$D = D_0 \exp\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **process parameters** that influence the bond strength in FDM parts. How does **printing in a heated enclosure** improve part quality?

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Key Process Parameters (4 marks):**
>
> 1. **Extrusion Temperature ($T_{\text{extrusion}}$):** Higher temperatures increase the thermal energy available at the interface, extending the time the polymer spends above $T_g$. This directly increases inter-diffusion depth.
> 2. **Build Platform Temperature ($T_{\text{bed}}$):** A heated bed (typically 60 °C – 110 °C for ABS) reduces the cooling rate, giving the interface more time above $T_g$.
> 3. **Print Speed:** Slower printing → longer thermal exposure → stronger bonds.
> 4. **Layer Thickness:** Thinner layers → larger contact area per unit volume → stronger bonds.
> 5. **Nozzle Diameter & Bead Width:** Influences contact area and heat retention.
>
> **Step 2 — Heated Enclosure Effect (3 marks):**
> A heated enclosure (chamber) maintains the ambient temperature around the part at 40 °C – 70 °C. This:
> - **Reduces thermal gradients** in the part, minimizing **residual stress** and **warping**.
> - **Slows the cooling rate**, extending the duration above $T_g$ and improving **inter-layer adhesion**.
> - **Reduces differential shrinkage** between layers, which is the primary cause of **delamination** in tall, thin parts.
> - Particularly critical for **high-temperature engineering polymers** like **PEEK, ULTEM**, and **polycarbonate**.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks [Focus: SLS / SLM Thermal Bonding & Energy Density]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Differentiate between **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** and **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** in terms of their thermal bonding mechanism, temperature regime, materials processed, and part density.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Thermal Bonding Mechanism (3 marks):**
>
> | Feature | SLS | SLM |
> |---|---|---|
> | Bonding Mechanism | Solid-state sintering (partial fusion) | Full melting |
> | Temperature Regime | $T_g < T < T_m$ (for polymers) | $T > T_m$ (full melt) |
> | Atomic Mobility | Surface diffusion, grain boundary diffusion | Complete liquid-phase atomic mobility |
> | Cooling | Solid-state with minor densification | Rapid solidification, possible metallurgical phase change |
>
> **Step 2 — Materials and Application (2 marks):**
> - **SLS** is primarily used for **thermoplastic polymers** (PA-12, PA-11, TPU) and some **metals** in **Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS)**.
> - **SLM** is used almost exclusively for **metals** (Ti-6Al-4V, 316L SS, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg) and produces near-fully-dense parts.
>
> **Step 3 — Part Density (2 marks):**
> - **SLS polymer parts** typically achieve **90 – 95%** theoretical density, with **15 – 30% porosity** inherent to the process.
> - **SLM metal parts** achieve **>99.5%** density, comparable to wrought metals. However, SLM parts can suffer from **residual porosity** if the energy density is poorly tuned.

**(b) [7 Marks]** An SLM machine processes 316L stainless steel with the following parameters: Laser power $P = 250 \text{ W}$, scan speed $v = 1000 \text{ mm/s}$, hatch spacing $h = 0.08 \text{ mm}$, layer thickness $t = 30 \ \mu\text{m}$. Calculate the **volumetric energy density** and comment on whether the part is likely to be **defect-free**. Justify with reference to the standard range for 316L SS.

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 1 — Unit Conversion:**
> - $v = 1000 \text{ mm/s}$
> - $h = 0.08 \text{ mm}$
> - $t = 30 \ \mu\text{m} = 0.030 \text{ mm}$
>
> **Step 2 — Apply the Energy Density Formula (3 marks):**
>
> $$E_d = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$
>
> $$E_d = \frac{250}{1000 \times 0.08 \times 0.030}$$
>
> $$E_d = \frac{250}{2.4}$$
>
> $$E_d = 104.17 \text{ J/mm}^3$$
>
> **Step 3 — Compare with Standard Range (2 marks):**
> The standard volumetric energy density for **316L stainless steel in SLM** is **80 – 150 J/mm³**. Our calculated value of **104.17 J/mm³** lies **comfortably within** this range, indicating:
> - **Good melt pool formation** with no lack-of-fusion defects.
> - **No over-melting or keyholing** (which would occur above ~150 J/mm³).
> - Expected **part density > 99.5%**.
>
> **Step 4 — Defect Analysis (2 marks):**
> A likely outcome is a **defect-free, near-fully-dense part** with minimal porosity. The microstructure will consist of fine cellular subgrains (characteristic of rapid solidification), with possible **anisotropy** in mechanical properties along the build direction due to layer-by-layer thermal cycling.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make on Thermal Bonding Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $T_g$ and $T_m$:** Many students write "thermal bonding occurs above the **melting point**" — this is **wrong for SLS**, where bonding occurs **below $T_m$** (sintering regime). Always state the **correct regime** for the specific process.
> 2. **Forgetting Units in $E_d$ Calculation:** If you mix mm and μm in the denominator, the answer will be off by a factor of **1000**. KTU examiners specifically test unit conversion ability.
> 3. **Ignoring Latent Heat:** In FDM energy calculations, students often compute only **sensible heat** ($Q_s = mc_p\Delta T$) and forget the **latent heat of fusion** ($Q_\ell = mL_f$). This leads to a ~20% under-estimation of energy.
> 4. **Stating "FDM and SLS are the same":** They are NOT the same. FDM uses **filament extrusion**; SLS uses **powder bed fusion**. The bonding mechanism is fundamentally different.
> 5. **Skipping the Schematic:** In a 7-mark sub-question, a labeled diagram is **mandatory** and carries 1–2 marks by itself. Always include a flowchart or process schematic.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Thermal Bonding** is the joining of materials in AM through controlled heating to a regime above $T_g$ or $T_m$.
- The **three temperature regimes** are: sub-$T_g$ (no bonding), between $T_g$ and $T_m$ (viscous/diffusive bonding — used in SLS), and above $T_m$ (full melting — used in FDM and SLM).
- The **four-step mechanism** is: surface contact → neck formation → inter-diffusion → solidification.
- The **key Arrhenius relation** is $D = D_0 \exp(-E_a/RT)$ — increasing temperature exponentially increases diffusion rate.
- The **energy density equation** $E_d = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ is the **single most important formula** for SLS/SLM, with typical ranges of **60–120 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V and **80–150 J/mm³** for 316L SS.
- **FDM** uses **resistive heating**; **SLS/SLM** uses **laser radiation**; **EBM** uses an **electron beam in vacuum**.
- **Heated enclosures** in FDM reduce warping and improve inter-layer bond strength.
- The **diffusion depth** scales as $x \sim \sqrt{Dt}$ — the time-temperature superposition is the foundation of all thermal bonding kinetics.
- For **KTU 2024 ESE**, expect either a **3-mark definition question** or a **7+7 mark question** combining mechanism + energy density calculation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LOM and UC Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** LOM and UC Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)

### Formal Definition
**Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM)** is a sheet-based Additive Manufacturing (AM) process, classified under the ASTM F2792 category of *Sheet Lamination*, in which successive layers of paper, polymer, or metal-backed foils are adhesively bonded (or thermally welded) and simultaneously contoured by a computer-controlled laser (or knife) cutter to form a three-dimensional solid object. The unwanted cross-hatched regions of each sheet are left in place to serve as built-in support material, eliminating the need for secondary support structures.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Sticky-Paper Stack"
Imagine you take **50 sheets of gummed paper**, brush glue on each, and stack them one on top of the other. Now imagine you take a pair of scissors and, *after laying each sheet*, snip out the shape of a small tree from the topmost sheet. When you keep going up the stack, layer by layer, you eventually get a paper tree with branches and a trunk. That is essentially LOM — except a **laser beam** acts as the scissors, a **heated roller** applies the glue/pressure, and the *un-cut* portions of the sheet remain behind to act as free scaffolding.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LOM is one of the oldest commercial AM processes (originally patented by **Michael Feygin**, 1985, and commercialized by **Helisys Inc.** as the *LOM-1010*). It is the only AM process in which the support material is *part of the feedstock sheet itself* — no separate support generation software is required.

### 1.2 Ultrasonic Consolidation (UC) / Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)

### Formal Definition
**Ultrasonic Consolidation (UC)**, also known as **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**, is a solid-state, low-temperature AM process classified under *Sheet Lamination* (and sometimes *Hybrid Manufacturing*) in which metal tapes/foils (typically 0.1–0.3 mm thick) are bonded layer-by-layer using high-frequency **ultrasonic vibrations (20–40 kHz)** combined with static normal pressure. The process is performed at temperatures **below 50 % of the parent metal's melting point**, producing fully dense metallurgical bonds without melting, recast structure, or HAZ (heat-affected zone) defects.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Cosmic Vibration Weld"
Think of stacking **aluminum kitchen foil sheets** on a kitchen counter. Now imagine a small vibrating metal block (running at 20,000 times per second) pressed down on the top sheet with a gentle force. The microscopic *scrubbing* motion of that block literally welds the top sheet to the sheet below — *without any sparks, heat, or melting*. After one weld, the platform drops by the thickness of one foil, a new foil is fed in, and the cycle repeats. That is UC in a kitchen-counter nutshell.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Differentiation:** Unlike LOM (which uses **adhesives or thermal bonding + laser cutting**), UC uses **purely mechanical/metallurgical ultrasonic welding** and requires **post-process CNC milling** to define the final part geometry because foils are bonded as full rectangles first, then *subtractively* shaped.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to compare LOM and UC. Always remember the *three key contrasts*: (1) **LOM = adhesive/laser** vs. **UC = ultrasonic/mechanical**, (2) **LOM = supports built-in** vs. **UC = supports NOT required due to solid bonding**, (3) **LOM = paper/plastic focus** vs. **UC = metal focus**.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 LOM — Process Logic Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Operational Cycle
1. **Sheet Feed:** A spool/feed mechanism advances a fresh roll of material (paper, PVC, or metal-foil-backed sheet) over the build platform.
2. **Heated Roller Lamination:** A heated roller (temperature typically **150 °C – 200 °C** for paper-based LOM) presses the new sheet onto the previous stack. Thermoplastic adhesive (often EVA or acrylic) activates and bonds the layer.
3. **Laser Contour Cutting:** A **CO₂ laser** (wavelength **10.6 μm**, power **25–50 W**) traces the 2-D cross-section of the current slice, vaporizing material along the contour.
4. **Cross-Hatch Cutting:** The laser also cuts a **decross-hatch grid** into the *waste* regions of the sheet. This is the signature LOM feature — waste cubes become easy to remove manually.
5. **Z-Stage Lowering:** The build platform descends by exactly **one layer thickness (δ)**. Typical δ = **0.05 mm to 0.5 mm** depending on material.
6. **Iteration:** Steps 1–5 repeat for each slice in the sliced CAD model.
7. **Post-Processing (De-cubing):** Operator manually removes the hatched waste cubes with a brush/knife. Paper LOM parts are often sealed with epoxy for strength.

### The 'Why' Behind the Logic
- **Why heated roller?** Cheap, large-area bonding without a vacuum or autoclave.
- **Why decross-hatch?** Because LOM doesn't use soluble supports, the only way to free the part is to dice the waste into manageable cubes.
- **Why CO₂ laser?** Paper absorbs strongly at 10.6 μm — a cheap, efficient coupling.

### LOM Process Parameter Table

| Parameter | Typical Range / Value | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer thickness (δ) | 0.05 – 0.5 | mm | Resolution in Z |
| Laser type | CO₂ | — | Couples with paper/plastic absorption |
| Laser power | 25 – 50 | W | Cutting speed control |
| Roller temperature | 150 – 200 | °C | Activates adhesive |
| Roller pressure | 0.2 – 0.5 | MPa | Bond quality |
| Build envelope | up to 800 × 500 × 500 | mm³ | Among the largest in AM |
| Material wastage | 30 – 60 | % | High vs. powder AM |

---

## 2.2 UC — Process Logic Breakdown

### Step-by-Step Operational Cycle
1. **Base Plate Preparation:** An aluminium or steel substrate plate is clamped to the anvil.
2. **Foil Feed:** A metal foil (typically Al 3003, Al 6061, Cu, Ti, SS 304) of thickness **0.1–0.3 mm** is fed between the sonotrode and the previously bonded stack.
3. **Ultrasonic Welding Pass:** The sonotrode descends with a static normal force (F) of **500–2500 N** while vibrating at frequency **f = 20 kHz (or 40 kHz)** with amplitude **a = 10–50 μm** peak-to-peak.
4. **Bonding Mechanism Activation:** The combination of normal force + tangential scrubbing breaks surface oxides and asperities, enabling *atomic diffusion* and *mechanical interlocking* across the interface.
5. **Z-Stage Lowering:** Stage drops by one foil thickness (typically **0.1–0.3 mm**).
6. **New Foil Bonding:** A new foil is fed and welded over the entire build area (full-rectangle weld).
7. **Cyclic Additive Build:** Steps 3–6 repeat until the part is fully laminated.
8. **Subtractive Shaping (CNC Milling):** Since the foils are full rectangles, a separate **CNC mill** (often integrated into the same machine) removes material to define the *true 3-D geometry* — a *hybrid* additive + subtractive process.

### The 'Why' Behind the Logic
- **Why ultrasonic vibration?** It locally plasticizes the surface (acoustic softening) without bulk heating, so dissimilar metals can bond without forming brittle intermetallics.
- **Why full-area bonding then milling?** Foils are too thin to support a contour-only build; over-welding provides structural rigidity during the build.
- **Why hybrid?** UC cannot do overhangs without supports; milling adds geometric freedom and surface finish.

### UC Process Parameter Table

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ultrasonic frequency | f | 20 (or 40) | kHz | Determines scrubbing cycles per second |
| Vibration amplitude | a | 10 – 50 | μm | Surface plasticization intensity |
| Normal welding force | F | 500 – 2500 | N | Contact pressure |
| Weld speed | v | 30 – 100 | mm/s | Linear travel of sonotrode |
| Foil thickness | δ | 0.1 – 0.3 | mm | Z-resolution |
| Process temperature | T | < 0.5·T_m | °C / K | Solid-state criterion |
| Bond strength (Al–Al) | σ_b | 80 – 250 | MPa | Approaches parent metal strength |

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Critical Equations)

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Description | Variables / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $$E_w \;=\; \dfrac{F \cdot a \cdot f}{v}$$ | Weld energy per unit length (UC) | $F$ = N, $a$ = m, $f$ = Hz, $v$ = m/s, $E_w$ = J/m |
| 2 | $$P \;=\; F \cdot 2\pi f \cdot a$$ | Average acoustic power delivered to interface (UC) | $P$ = W |
| 3 | $$E_d \;=\; \dfrac{P}{v \cdot w}$$ | Energy density per unit area (UC) | $w$ = weld width (m), $E_d$ = J/m² |
| 4 | $$\Delta t_{build} \;=\; \dfrac{H}{\delta} \cdot \tau_{layer}$$ | LOM / UC build time | $H$ = part height, $\delta$ = layer thickness, $\tau_{layer}$ = cycle time per layer |
| 5 | $$\sigma_b \;\le\; \sigma_{parent}$$ | Theoretical bond strength limit | $\sigma_b$ = bond tensile strength, $\sigma_{parent}$ = base foil UTS |
| 6 | $$T_{process} \;<\; 0.5 \cdot T_{melt}$$ | Solid-state bonding criterion (UC) | $T$ in K |
| 7 | $$R_a^{final} \;\approx\; R_a^{milled} \;\approx\; 0.4 - 1.6 \; \mu m$$ | Final surface roughness (UC after milling) | μm |
| 8 | $$W_{waste} \;=\; V_{part} \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{sheet}}{V_{part}} - 1\right)$$ | Material wastage fraction (LOM) | $V$ = volume |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** Equation (1) — *Weld Energy per unit length* — is the single most testable formula for UC. Be ready to compute $E_w$ given combinations of $F$, $a$, $f$, and $v$. Equation (6) — *solid-state criterion* — appears in nearly every CO-2 question on UC.

### Real-World Engineering Utility
- **LOM:** Sand-casting & investment-casting pattern production (no thermal expansion mismatch); large-scale architectural mock-ups; low-cost concept models in automotive styling.
- **UC:** Embedded smart structures (sensors/wiring inside metal matrix); cladding of dissimilar metals (Al–Cu, Al–Ti) for aerospace and EV battery applications; nuclear and defence components requiring low residual stress; cryogenic applications needing no HAZ softening.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Weld Energy per Unit Length in UC

**Goal:** Derive an expression for the weld energy density delivered to the foil interface per unit length of sonotrode travel.

### Assumptions
1. The sonotrode oscillates as a simple harmonic oscillator with peak amplitude $a$ (m) at frequency $f$ (Hz).
2. The sonotrode contacts the foil with a constant normal force $F$ (N).
3. The relative sliding motion between sonotrode and foil occurs at the contact interface; tangential force $F_t$ is approximately equal to $F$ in the gross slip regime (conservative bound).
4. The process operates at a constant linear weld speed $v$ (m/s).

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Velocity of the vibrating surface.**
The sonotrode tip moves sinusoidally:
$$x(t) \;=\; a \sin(2\pi f t)$$

The instantaneous velocity of the tip is the time derivative:
$$v_{tip}(t) \;=\; \frac{dx}{dt} \;=\; 2\pi f a \cos(2\pi f t)$$

**Step 2 — Instantaneous frictional power dissipated.**
The instantaneous power dissipated against friction at the interface is:
$$p(t) \;=\; F_t \cdot \vert v_{tip}(t) \vert$$

Using the conservative assumption $F_t \approx F$ and substituting the velocity expression:
$$p(t) \;=\; F \cdot \vert 2\pi f a \cos(2\pi f t) \vert$$

**Step 3 — Time-averaged power.**
Averaging over one full period $T = 1/f$:
$$P \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} p(t)\, dt \;=\; \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} F \cdot 2\pi f a \,\vert \cos(2\pi f t)\vert\, dt$$

Since the average of $\vert \cos(2\pi f t) \vert$ over a full period is $2/\pi$:
$$P \;=\; F \cdot 2\pi f a \cdot \frac{2}{\pi} \;=\; 4 F f a$$

This is the **RMS-equivalent** average acoustic power transferred to the interface.

**Step 4 — Convert to energy per unit length of weld.**
The sonotrode travels a distance $dx = v\, dt$ in time $dt$. The energy delivered per unit travel length is:
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{P}{v}$$

Substituting $P = 4 F f a$:
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{4 F f a}{v}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Justification:** Some texts drop the factor of 4 (or write $P = 2\pi F f a$, retaining the mean of $\cos^2 = 1/2$) to obtain the cleaner textbook form:
> $$\boxed{\,E_w \;=\; \dfrac{F \cdot a \cdot f}{v}\,}$$
> The factor is convention-dependent. The KTU 2024 syllabus accepts the simplified $Faf/v$ form for full credit.

### Worked Numerical Example
A UC system welds aluminium foil with the following parameters:
- $F = 1500$ N
- $a = 25 \, \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $f = 20$ kHz $= 20{,}000$ Hz
- $v = 50$ mm/s $= 0.05$ m/s

Compute the weld energy per unit length.

**Solution:**
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{F \cdot a \cdot f}{v} \;=\; \frac{1500 \times (25 \times 10^{-6}) \times 20{,}000}{0.05}$$

Step by step:
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{1500 \times 25 \times 10^{-6} \times 20{,}000}{0.05}$$

$$\text{Numerator} \;=\; 1500 \times 25 \times 20{,}000 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 750 \times 10^{0} \;=\; 750$$

$$E_w \;=\; \frac{750}{0.05} \;=\; 15{,}000 \; \text{J/m}$$

$$\boxed{E_w \;=\; 15 \; \text{kJ/m}}$$

This is well within the **5–25 kJ/m** operating window for sound Al–Al ultrasonic consolidation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LOM Build Time

**Goal:** Estimate total build time for a LOM part.

### Assumptions
1. Each layer takes a constant time $\tau_{layer}$ (s) including feed + laminate + cut + decross.
2. Part is approximated as a rectangular block of height $H$ and uniform cross-section.

### Derivation

Total number of layers:
$$N_{layers} \;=\; \frac{H}{\delta}$$

Total build time:
$$\Delta t_{build} \;=\; N_{layers} \times \tau_{layer} \;=\; \frac{H}{\delta}\cdot \tau_{layer}$$

### Worked Example
A LOM part is **120 mm tall** with layer thickness **δ = 0.2 mm** and cycle time **τ = 25 s/layer**.

$$N \;=\; \frac{120}{0.2} \;=\; 600 \; \text{layers}$$

$$\Delta t_{build} \;=\; 600 \times 25 \;=\; 15{,}000 \; \text{s} \;=\; 4 \; \text{h}\; 10 \; \text{min}$$

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: LOM vs. UC

| Attribute | LOM | UC |
|---|---|---|
| ASTM F2792 Category | Sheet Lamination | Sheet Lamination (Hybrid) |
| Bonding mechanism | Adhesive + thermal | Solid-state ultrasonic |
| Material focus | Paper, polymer, metal-foil | Metals (Al, Cu, Ti, SS) |
| Cutting/shaping | Laser contour + decross | CNC milling (post-process) |
| Need for support | Built-in via decross waste | Not required (rigid foils) |
| Maximum part size | Very large (800 mm+) | Medium (300 × 300 mm typical) |
| Surface finish (as-built) | Poor (stepped) | Moderate |
| Surface finish (post-CNC) | N/A | Excellent (Ra < 1.6 μm) |
| Process temperature | 150 – 200 °C | < 0.5 T_m (cold) |
| Material waste | 30 – 60 % | Low (~5 – 15 %) |
| Typical accuracy | ± 0.1 mm | ± 0.05 mm (after milling) |
| Best application | Casting patterns, mock-ups | Embedded electronics, multi-material |

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 LOM Process — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[CAD Model] --> node2[Slicer Software]
    node2 --> node3[2D Slice Data per Layer]
    node3 --> node4{Build Controller}
    node4 --> node5[Sheet Feed Roller]
    node5 --> node6[Heated Lamination Roller]
    node6 --> node7[Build Platform with Previous Layers]
    node4 --> node8[CO2 Laser Head]
    node8 --> node9[Contour Cut on Current Sheet]
    node8 --> node10[Decross Hatch on Waste Area]
    node9 --> node11{All Layers Done?}
    node10 --> node11
    node11 --> node12[Manual De-cubing of Waste]
    node12 --> node13[Sealing / Finishing]
    node11 -->|No| node14[Lower Z-Stage by one delta]
    node14 --> node5
    node11 -->|Yes| node15[Finished LOM Part]
    stylenode1[fill:#dde7ff,stroke:#003a8c]
    style node15 fill:#c8f7c5,stroke:#2a7f2a
```

## 4.2 UC/UAM System — Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[High Frequency Power Supply 20 kHz] --> nodeB[Piezoelectric Transducer]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Booster Amplifier]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Sonotrode Horn]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Metal Foil 0.1 to 0.3 mm]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Previously Bonded Stack]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Anvil Bed with Pneumatic Force]
    nodeH[Static Normal Force F 500 to 2500 N] --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeI[Build Platform with Z Axis]
    nodeJ[Foil Feed Spool] --> nodeE
    nodeI --> nodeK{All Layers Built?}
    nodeK -->|No| nodeL[Index Z by foil thickness]
    nodeL --> nodeD
    nodeK -->|Yes| nodeM[CNC Milling Subtracter]
    nodeM --> nodeN[Final UC Part with Embedded Features]
    style nodeN fill:#c8f7c5,stroke:#2a7f2a
    style nodeA fill:#fff4cc,stroke:#a07a00
```

## 4.3 LOM vs. UC — Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeS1[Start Application Requirement] --> nodeS2{Need Metal Part?}
    nodeS2 -->|No, paper or polymer is OK| nodeS3{Need Part Larger than 500 mm?}
    nodeS3 -->|Yes| nodeS4[Choose LOM]
    nodeS3 -->|No| nodeS5[Consider FDM or SLA]
    nodeS2 -->|Yes, metal required| nodeS6{Need Embedded Sensors or Dissimilar Metal Bonding?}
    nodeS6 -->|Yes| nodeS7[Choose UC]
    nodeS6 -->|No| nodeS8{Need High Resolution and Fine Surface Finish?}
    nodeS8 -->|Yes| nodeS9[Consider SLM DMLS]
    nodeS8 -->|No, cost sensitive| nodeS10[Choose LOM with metal foil backing]
    style nodeS4 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003a8c
    style nodeS7 fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#a00
    style nodeS10 fill:#d6f0d6,stroke:#2a7f2a
```

## 4.4 UC Bonding Mechanism — Layer-By-Layer Bond Formation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeB1[Top Foil Unbonded] --> nodeB2[Sonotrode Applies Normal Force plus Vibration]
    nodeB2 --> nodeB3[Surface Asperities Plasticize and Shear]
    nodeB3 --> nodeB4[Surface Oxides Disrupt and Disperse]
    nodeB4 --> nodeB5[Fresh Metal Surfaces Exposed]
    nodeB5 --> nodeB6[Atomic Diffusion across Interface]
    nodeB6 --> nodeB7[Mechanical Interlocking Forms]
    nodeB7 --> nodeB8[Metallurgical Solid State Bond Established]
    style nodeB8 fill:#c8f7c5,stroke:#2a7f2a
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper]**

**Q1. Differentiate between LOM and FDM as sheet-based versus extrusion-based AM processes.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
- LOM is a *Sheet Lamination* process; FDM is a *Material Extrusion* process.
- In LOM, *pre-formed sheets* (paper/foil) are bonded by a heated roller and cut by a CO₂ laser. In FDM, *thermoplastic filament* is melted and extruded through a heated nozzle.
- LOM's support material is the *un-cut waste* of the sheet itself; FDM requires *separately deposited support structures*.
- LOM typically uses *larger layer thickness (0.1–0.5 mm)* and is suited for large parts; FDM achieves finer resolution (0.1–0.3 mm) and is more common for polymer prototypes.

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]**

**Q2. State and explain the solid-state bonding criterion in Ultrasonic Consolidation.** *(3 marks)*

**Model Answer:**
The solid-state bonding criterion in UC is:
$$T_{process} \;<\; 0.5 \cdot T_{melt}$$
where both temperatures are in Kelvin. This ensures that bonding occurs purely by *atomic diffusion, mechanical interlocking, and oxide dispersion* — without reaching the parent metal's melting point. As a result, UC produces parts with **no HAZ (heat-affected zone)**, **no recast structure**, **no residual thermal stress**, and **no brittle intermetallic phases**, which is why dissimilar metals (Al–Cu, Al–Ti) can be bonded without property degradation.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Module Internal Choice Questions

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Model Q with Internal Choice]**

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) — *LOM-Focused*

**(a)** Explain the Laminated Object Manufacturing (LOM) process in detail with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of the heated roller, CO₂ laser, and decross-hatching. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and ASTM category:** LOM is a *Sheet Lamination* AM process (ASTM F2792) where successive layers of paper/plastic/metal-foil are bonded and laser-cut to form 3-D parts.

**2. Step-by-step cycle (5 marks equivalent):**
- i. *Sheet feed:* Fresh sheet is rolled onto the build platform from a supply spool.
- ii. *Lamination:* A heated roller (150–200 °C) applies pressure (0.2–0.5 MPa), activating the adhesive and bonding the sheet to the previous layer.
- iii. *Contour cutting:* A CO₂ laser (10.6 μm, 25–50 W) traces the cross-section outline.
- iv. *Decross-hatching:* The same laser cuts a grid pattern on the *waste* regions to convert them into small removable cubes.
- v. *Z-stage indexing:* The build platform descends by one layer thickness (δ = 0.05–0.5 mm).
- vi. *Iteration:* Steps 1–5 repeat for the next slice.

**3. Role of components (2 marks equivalent):**
- *Heated roller:* Provides fast, large-area bonding using thermoplastic adhesive; cheaper than autoclave.
- *CO₂ laser:* Efficiently absorbed by paper/plastic at 10.6 μm; precisely vaporizes the contour.
- *Decross-hatch:* Solves the support-removal problem in the absence of soluble supports.

**(b)** Discuss the materials used, advantages, limitations, and applications of LOM. Compare it with FDM in a tabular form. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Materials (2 marks):** Paper (most common — LOM-1010), PVC, FRP composites, metal-foil-backed sheets (rare).

**2. Advantages (2 marks):**
- Very large build envelope possible (up to 800 × 500 × 500 mm³).
- Low material cost (paper).
- No separate support structures required.
- High speed for large cross-sections.

**3. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Poor surface finish (stepped, requires sealing).
- High material wastage (30–60 %).
- Limited material range.
- Long post-processing (de-cubing is manual).

**4. Applications (1 mark):**
- Sand-casting and investment-casting patterns.
- Architectural models.
- Concept prototypes in automotive design.
- Foundry tooling.

**5. Comparison table (1 mark):** FDM vs. LOM (deposited filament vs. bonded sheets; supports vs. decross waste; polymer vs. paper-dominant).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) — *UC-Focused* (Internal Choice)

**(a)** Explain the Ultrasonic Consolidation (UC) process with a neat functional block diagram. Discuss the role of the transducer, booster, and sonotrode. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Definition and classification (1 mark):** UC is a solid-state, low-temperature Sheet Lamination (Hybrid) AM process that bonds metal foils using ultrasonic vibrations (20 kHz) and static normal force without melting.

**2. Process cycle (4 marks):**
- i. *Foil feed:* 0.1–0.3 mm metal foil is fed onto the previously bonded stack.
- ii. *Welding:* Sonotrode vibrates at f = 20 kHz with amplitude a = 10–50 μm, under normal force F = 500–2500 N, traversing at weld speed v = 30–100 mm/s.
- iii. *Bond formation:* Surface oxides break, asperities deform, fresh metal surfaces contact, atomic diffusion creates metallurgical bond.
- iv. *Z-index:* Stage drops by one foil thickness.
- v. *Cumulative build:* Steps 1–4 repeat.
- vi. *Subtractive milling:* Integrated CNC mill shapes the final geometry.

**3. Role of components (2 marks):**
- *Piezoelectric transducer:* Converts high-frequency electrical input (20 kHz) into mechanical vibration.
- *Booster:* Amplifies (or attenuates) the vibration amplitude before transmission.
- *Sonotrode (horn):* Couples vibration to the workpiece; provides the welding pressure contact area.
- *Anvil:* Provides reactive support and holds the build platform.

**(b)** A UC system is set to weld an aluminium foil with the following parameters: F = 1800 N, a = 30 μm, f = 20 kHz, v = 60 mm/s. Compute the weld energy per unit length, $E_w$. Justify whether the operating window is suitable for Al–Al bonding. List four industrial applications of UC. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**1. Numerical computation (4 marks):**
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{F \cdot a \cdot f}{v}$$

Substitute:
$$E_w \;=\; \frac{1800 \times (30 \times 10^{-6}) \times 20{,}000}{0.06}$$

Numerator:
$$1800 \times 30 \times 10^{-6} \;=\; 0.054$$
$$0.054 \times 20{,}000 \;=\; 1080$$

$$E_w \;=\; \frac{1080}{0.06} \;=\; 18{,}000 \; \text{J/m} \;=\; 18 \; \text{kJ/m}$$

**2. Operating window justification (1 mark):** The Al–Al UC window lies in the range **5–25 kJ/m**. The computed 18 kJ/m is *well within* this window, predicting good bond formation without over-welding or interface cracking.

**3. Industrial applications of UC (2 marks):**
- *Embedded smart structures:* Sensors, optical fibres, and copper traces laminated inside aluminium matrices for aerospace structural health monitoring.
- *Multi-material cladding:* Al–Cu battery tabs and Al–Ti transition joints for weight-critical EV and aerospace structures.
- *Functionally graded materials (FGM):* Layered compositions for thermal management in nuclear and space applications.
- *Cryogenic and defence components:* Low-residual-stress parts for missile fins, satellite brackets, and superconducting magnet supports.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing LOM's ASTM category** as "Material Extrusion" — *it is Sheet Lamination*. (−1 mark)
> 2. **Forgetting the solid-state criterion** $T < 0.5 T_m$ in UC answers — examiners treat this as a *core outcome* (CO-2: Understand process physics). (−2 marks)
> 3. **Skipping units** in $E_w = Faf/v$ calculations. The unit is **J/m**, not J. (−1 mark)
> 4. **Mixing up "decross-hatch"** with "support structures" in LOM — they are *not* the same; decross-hatch *dices* the in-built waste. (−1 mark)
> 5. **Omitting the hybrid (additive + subtractive) nature** of UC. UC alone cannot make a part — CNC milling *must* be mentioned. (−1 mark)
> 6. **Wrong laser type:** Always write **CO₂** laser for LOM, not Nd:YAG. (−0.5 mark)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **LOM = Sheet Lamination** (ASTM F2792), uses **paper/PVC/foil**, bonded by **heated roller + adhesive**, shaped by **CO₂ laser (10.6 μm)**, waste is **decross-hatched** (no soluble supports).
- **UC = Sheet Lamination / Hybrid**, uses **metal foils (Al, Cu, Ti, SS)**, bonded by **ultrasonic vibration (20 kHz) + normal force (500–2500 N)**, shaped by **post-process CNC milling**.
- **Master formula for UC:** $E_w = Faf/v$ (J/m), operating window for Al–Al is **5–25 kJ/m**.
- **Solid-state criterion:** $T_{process} < 0.5\,T_{melt}$ — no melting, no HAZ, no intermetallics.
- **LOM build envelope** is one of the **largest** in AM (up to 800 mm) — great for cast patterns and architectural mock-ups.
- **UC excels at** *embedded electronics*, *dissimilar metal bonding*, *cladding*, *FGM*, and *low-stress defence parts*.
- **LOM materials** are dominantly *non-metallic*; **UC materials** are *exclusively metallic*.
- **LOM** is *additive-only*; **UC** is *additive + subtractive hybrid*.
- **LOM surface finish** is poor (stepped, requires sealing); **UC finish** is excellent after milling ($R_a \approx 0.4 - 1.6 \; \mu m$).
- **LOM typical δ = 0.05–0.5 mm**; **UC typical δ = 0.1–0.3 mm**.
- **Energy density formula:** $E_d = P/(v \cdot w)$ — useful when comparing welding energy per unit area across machines.
- **Examiner's Favourite 14-Mark Traps:** Forgetting decross-hatch, omitting CNC milling in UC, missing solid-state criterion, unit errors in $E_w$.
- **One-line signature:** "LOM = laser + glue + paper", "UC = sound + pressure + metal".

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powder Bed Fusion AM Processes: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powder Bed Fusion AM Processes: Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — Formal Definition

**Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** is a category of Additive Manufacturing (AM) processes defined by the **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard, in which thermal energy selectively fuses regions of a powder bed. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT634, Module 3), PBF encompasses all AM technologies that use a **bed of powdered material** (polymer, metal, ceramic, or composite) and a **focused thermal source** (laser or electron beam) to consolidate successive layers into a 3D solid object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT634 – Module 3):** The 2024 scheme categorizes SLS strictly under *Powder Bed Fusion of Polymers*, distinguishing it from **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)** and **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)**, which deal with metallic powders. Students must NOT confuse SLS with SLM in board answers.

## 1.2 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Formal Definition

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is the pioneering Powder Bed Fusion process patented by **Dr. Carl Deckard** and **Dr. Joe Beaman** at the University of Texas at Austin in **1989** (US Patent 4,863,538, expired in 2002). In SLS, a **CO₂ laser** scans a polymer powder bed, raising the local temperature just **below the melting point** of the polymer so that adjacent particles **sinter (diffusion-bond)** together without fully melting. The unfused powder acts as a natural **support structure**, eliminating the need for dedicated support scaffolding.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sand Castle & Sun" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a beach where you want to build a sandcastle. Instead of using a blowtorch (which would melt/glassify the sand), you use a **magnifying glass to focus sunlight** onto a thin layer of damp sand. The surface grains fuse gently into a crust while the deeper sand remains loose. After a thin crust forms, you sprinkle another layer of damp sand on top, and repeat. The **loose sand beneath** acts as a built-in mold/support.
>
> This is exactly how SLS works: the **laser = magnifying glass**, the **polymer powder = damp sand**, and the **partially fused (sintered) layer = crust**. The unsintered powder below is your automatic support material.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Layer Thickness (t):** Typically **0.1 mm** for polymers (range 0.05–0.15 mm).
- **Build Volume (EOS P 770 example):** 700 mm × 380 mm × 580 mm.
- **Typical Laser Power (P):** 30–100 W (CO₂ laser, **λ ≈ 10.6 μm**).
- **Standard Powder Particle Size:** **10–100 μm** diameter (D50 ≈ 50 μm).

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The SLS process executes the following sequence for **every layer**:

1. **Build Chamber Pre-heat:** The build platform and powder bed are pre-heated to a temperature **T_preheat** that lies **just below the polymer's melting point (T_m)**. This minimizes the thermal gradient the laser must impose and reduces thermal distortion.
2. **Powder Deposition:** A **recoater blade or rotating roller** spreads a thin, uniform layer of fresh powder from the feed chamber across the build platform.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A **CO₂ laser** (10.6 μm wavelength, well-absorbed by polymers) traces the 2D cross-section of the part, following **scan vectors** defined by the slicer. The laser energy fuses (sinters) the powder particles.
4. **Platform Increment:** The build platform descends by **one layer thickness (t)**.
5. **Repeat:** Steps 2–4 are repeated for **n = Total Height / t** layers.
6. **Cooling & Depowdering:** After the build completes, the chamber cools slowly (to avoid warping), and operators manually brush/siphon the unsintered powder to reveal the part.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Pre-heating below T_m:** Prevents **curl and warpage**. If the powder bed were cold, the steep thermal gradient would cause the sintered layer to curl upward at the edges due to non-uniform shrinkage.
- **CO₂ laser wavelength choice:** Polymers like **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)** absorb strongly at **10.6 μm**, making the CO₂ laser ideal. (Fiber lasers at 1.06 μm pass through polymer powder with minimal absorption — they are unsuitable for SLS of polymers.)
- **Loose powder as support:** Sintered parts are embedded in a "powder cake." For complex overhangs, lattices, and internal channels, this loose powder provides **omnidirectional support without dedicated scaffolds**, which is SLS's biggest design-for-manufacturing (DFM) advantage.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymer SLS) | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power | $P$ | **30 – 100 W** | Energy delivered per unit time |
| Scan Speed | $v$ | **1 – 8 m/s** (≈ 4000–25000 mm/s) | Speed of laser spot |
| Hatch Spacing | $h$ | **0.2 – 0.5 mm** | Distance between adjacent scan lines |
| Layer Thickness | $t$ | **0.05 – 0.15 mm** | Vertical resolution |
| Preheat Temperature | $T_{pre}$ | $T_m - 5°C$ to $T_m - 2°C$ | Reduces thermal shock |
| Powder Bed Temperature | $T_{bed}$ | ≈ $T_{pre}$ | Maintained throughout build |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single most-tested computation in the SLS section of KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | Quantifies total energy input per unit volume of powder |
| 2 | **Build Time Estimate** | $T_{build} = \dfrac{N_{layers} \cdot A_{layer}}{v \cdot w_{laser}} \cdot k$ | hours | Where $N_{layers} = H/t$, $w_{laser}$ = effective scan width, $k$ = overhead factor |
| 3 | **Sintering Temperature Window** | $T_{sinter} = T_m - \Delta T$ | °C | $\Delta T$ = 2–10 °C for most semi-crystalline polymers |
| 4 | **Layer Count** | $N = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t}$ | dimensionless | $H_{part}$ = part height |
| 5 | **Cooling Strain** | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ = CTE, used to estimate residual stress |
| 6 | **Hatch Fill Density** | $\rho_{hatch} = \dfrac{w_{laser}}{h}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 1$ to avoid porosity gaps |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The symbol $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is written using `\vert` in LaTeX. Do **not** use the vertical pipe `|` in a markdown table — it will break the table parser. KTU examiners deduct marks for unreadable answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Lightweight polymer jigs, fixtures, and ducting (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Medical:** Patient-specific orthotics, prosthetics, and anatomical models (PA2200 is **biocompatible — ISO 10993-1** certified).
- **Automotive:** Rapid prototyping of interior trim and functional prototypes (BMW, Tesla).
- **Foundry Patterns:** Investment casting patterns (SLS parts can be burnt out cleanly, leaving a shell mold).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The VED is the single most important parameter controlling **part density, mechanical strength, and porosity** in SLS. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define Linear Energy Density (LED)

The **linear energy density** is the energy delivered per unit length of the laser scan path.

$$
LED = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}
$$

*Text:* Here, $P$ is the laser power in watts (J/s) and $v$ is the scan speed in mm/s. Dividing energy per second by length per second yields energy per unit length.

### Step 2 — Define Areal Energy Density (AED)

The laser spot overlaps adjacent scan lines by the **hatch spacing $h$**. The energy per unit area of a single scanned layer is:

$$
AED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}
$$

*Text:* Multiplying the scan length by the hatch spacing gives the effective area covered per unit time. Energy per time divided by area per time yields energy per unit area.

### Step 3 — Extend to Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

A layer has thickness $t$. The volume of material sintered per unit scan length is $h \cdot t$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
\text{Units: } & \frac{[\text{W}]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The energy input (Joules) per unit volume of fused material is the definitive process "knob" for SLS.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** An SLS machine uses a 50 W CO₂ laser, scan speed of 5 m/s, hatch spacing of 0.3 mm, and layer thickness of 0.1 mm. Calculate the VED.

**Given:**
- $P = 50 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ J/s}$
- $v = 5 \text{ m/s} = 5000 \text{ mm/s}$
- $h = 0.3 \text{ mm}$
- $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50 \text{ J/s}}{5000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.3 \text{ mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50}{150} \text{ J/mm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 0.333 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* Note that 1 J/mm³ = 10⁹ J/m³ = 1 GJ/m³. For PA12, the recommended VED window is **0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³**. Our result of 0.333 falls within this window, indicating a healthy sintered part.

### Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis

| Effect of ↑ parameter on VED | Direction | Consequence on Part |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ $P$ | VED ↑ | Over-sintering, degradation, smoke |
| ↑ $v$ | VED ↓ | Under-sintering, porous, weak part |
| ↑ $h$ | VED ↓ | Gaps between scan lines |
| ↑ $t$ | VED ↓ | Incomplete layer fusion |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Build Time Estimate

### Step 1 — Number of Layers

$$
N = \frac{H_{part}}{t}
$$

*Text:* Total part height divided by layer thickness yields the layer count.

### Step 2 — Scan Path Length per Layer

For a part of cross-sectional area $A_{layer}$ and an effective laser scan width $w_{laser}$:

$$
L_{scan} \approx \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser}}
$$

*Text:* This assumes serpentine (raster) scanning filling the cross-section.

### Step 3 — Pure Laser Scanning Time

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{L_{scan}}{v} = \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* Distance divided by speed equals the time to trace the layer.

### Step 4 — Total Build Time with Overhead

$$
T_{build} = N \cdot T_{scan} \cdot k_{overhead} = \frac{H_{part} \cdot A_{layer} \cdot k}{t \cdot w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* The overhead factor $k$ (typically **1.2 – 1.5**) accounts for recoater motion, laser on/off, and powder feed delays.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A part is 100 mm tall, has a 50 mm × 50 mm square cross-section, $t = 0.1$ mm, $w_{laser} = 0.4$ mm, $v = 4000$ mm/s, $k = 1.3$. Estimate build time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{100}{0.1} = 1000 \text{ layers} \\[6pt]
A_{layer} &= 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2 \\[6pt]
T_{scan} &= \frac{2500}{0.4 \times 4000} = \frac{2500}{1600} = 1.5625 \text{ s/layer} \\[6pt]
T_{build} &= 1000 \times 1.5625 \times 1.3 = 2031.25 \text{ s} \approx 33.85 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The 1.3 overhead factor accounts for real-world recoater delays. Pure scan time would have been ~26 min.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SLS vs. Competing PBF Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask "Differentiate between SLS, SLM, and DMLS." Master this matrix.**

| Attribute | **SLS** | **SLM** (Selective Laser Melting) | **DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material State | Powder is **sintered** (not melted) | Powder is **fully melted** | Powder is **sintered** (solid-state diffusion) |
| Materials | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals only (Ti, Al, SS 316L, Inconel) | Metals only (SS, Inconel, CoCr, Ti) |
| Laser | **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** |
| Temperature | Below T_m (sintering) | Above T_m (melting) | Below T_m (sintering) |
| Support | **Not required** (loose powder supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) |
| Part Density | **80 – 95%** | **≥ 99%** (near-fully dense) | **≥ 95%** |
| Surface Finish | Rough (≈ 5–10 μm Ra after bead blast) | Better (≈ 4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Patent Status | **Expired 2002** (open technology) | Still has residual IP (varies) | **EOS patent expired 2016** |
| Typical Use | Functional polymer prototypes, low-volume polymer parts | High-performance aerospace/medical metal parts | Metal prototypes, dental, jewelry |

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SLS Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["LASER SOURCE - CO2 10.6 micrometer 30 to 100 W"] --> node2["BEAM STEERING MIRRORS - GALVANOMETER SCANNER"]
    node2 --> node3["F-THETA LENS - FOCUSES BEAM ON POWDER BED"]
    node3 --> node4["BUILD CHAMBER - INERT N2 ATMOSPHERE BELOW T_M"]
    node4 --> node5["POWDER BED - POLYMER PARTICLES 10 TO 100 MICRON"]
    node5 --> node6["RECOATER ROLLER OR BLADE - DEPOSITS NEW LAYER"]
    node6 --> node7["FEED CHAMBER - RESERVOIR OF FRESH POWDER"]
    node7 --> node6
    node4 --> node8["BUILD PLATFORM - DESCENDS BY LAYER THICKNESS T"]
    node8 --> node4
    node4 --> node9["PREHEAT HEATERS - MAINTAIN T_PRE BELOW T_MELT"]
    node9 --> node4
    node4 --> node10["SINTERED PART IN POWDER CAKE - AFTER BUILD"]
    node10 --> node11["DEPOWDERING STATION - BRUSH AND SUCTION"]
    node11 --> node12["FINISHED SLS PART"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Layer-by-Layer Process Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START BUILD"] --> stepB["PREHEAT CHAMBER TO T_PRE - JUST BELOW T_M"]
    stepB --> stepC["RECOATER SPREADS FRESH POWDER LAYER OF THICKNESS T"]
    stepC --> stepD{"SLICER CHECKS - IS THIS LAYER INSIDE PART CROSS SECTION"}
    stepD -- YES --> stepE["CO2 LASER SCANS CROSS SECTION AT POWER P AND SPEED V"]
    stepD -- NO --> stepF["LASER OFF - POWDER LEFT LOOSE AS NATURAL SUPPORT"]
    stepE --> stepG["LAYER COMPLETE - SINTERED TRACKS FUSE"]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH["BUILD PLATFORM DESCENDS BY T MM"]
    stepH --> stepI{"ALL N LAYERS COMPLETE - N = H PART / T"}
    stepI -- NO --> stepC
    stepI -- YES --> stepJ["SLOW CONTROLLED COOLING TO AMBIENT"]
    stepJ --> stepK["DEPOWDERING - REMOVE LOOSE UNSINTERED POWDER"]
    stepK --> stepL["FINISHED SLS PART READY FOR POST PROCESSING"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — VED Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    paramA["LASER POWER P IN W"] --> ved["VED = P DIVIDED BY V H T IN J PER MM3"]
    paramB["SCAN SPEED V IN MM PER S"] --> ved
    paramC["HATCH SPACING H IN MM"] --> ved
    paramD["LAYER THICKNESS T IN MM"] --> ved
    ved --> outA["VED LESS THAN 0.20 - UNDERSINTERED - POROUS WEAK"]
    ved --> outB["VED 0.20 TO 0.45 - OPTIMAL - DENSE STRONG"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Explain the principle of Selective Laser Sintering with a neat sketch. Why is a CO₂ laser preferred over a fiber laser for SLS of polymers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SLS is a Powder Bed Fusion process in which a **CO₂ laser (10.6 μm wavelength)** selectively scans a pre-heated polymer powder bed to sinter (diffusion-bond) particles together layer by layer. The powder bed is maintained at a temperature **just below the polymer's melting point (T_m - 2 to 10 °C)** to minimize thermal gradients.

A **CO₂ laser is preferred** because polymer powders (e.g., PA12) exhibit strong absorption bands in the **mid-infrared (10.6 μm)** region, whereas their absorption of fiber-laser wavelengths (1.06 μm) is very low. Using a fiber laser would result in poor energy coupling and inadequate sintering. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*List any three advantages and two limitations of SLS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **No support structures required** — the unfused powder bed acts as natural support.
2. **Material efficiency** — unsintered powder can be **reused** (typically 70–80% refresh rate for PA12).
3. **Complex geometries** are possible, including lattice structures and internal channels. **(2 marks)**

**Limitations:**
1. **Rough surface finish** (typically 5–10 μm Ra) requires post-processing.
2. **Lower part density** (80–95%) compared to injection molding or SLM. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Standard Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Pattern]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the Selective Laser Sintering process. Discuss the critical process parameters affecting part quality. Also, calculate the Volumetric Energy Density for the following case: Laser Power = 40 W, Scan Speed = 4 m/s, Hatch Spacing = 0.25 mm, Layer Thickness = 0.1 mm. Comment on the suitability of the obtained value for PA12 powder.*

#### Part (a) — Working Principle & Diagram (7 marks)

The Selective Laser Sintering process operates on the principle of **solid-state thermal fusion** of polymer powder using a focused CO₂ laser. The major subsystems are:

1. **CO₂ Laser Source** (10.6 μm, 30–100 W) — provides the thermal energy.
2. **Galvanometer Scan Head** with F-theta lens — directs the beam across the powder bed.
3. **Build Chamber** — sealed, inert (N₂) atmosphere, pre-heated to $T_{pre} < T_m$.
4. **Build Platform** — descends by layer thickness $t$ after each layer is sintered.
5. **Feed Chamber & Recoater** — supplies and spreads fresh powder.
6. **Pre-heat Heaters** — maintain uniform bed temperature.

The process steps are: **(i)** chamber pre-heat, **(ii)** powder deposition by recoater, **(iii)** laser sintering of the cross-section, **(iv)** platform descent, **(v)** repeat. After the build, **slow cooling** prevents warpage, followed by **depowdering**. **(7 marks — 4 for diagram, 3 for description)**

#### Part (b) — Process Parameters & VED Calculation (7 marks)

**Critical process parameters:**
- **Laser Power (P):** Higher P → denser part, but risk of degradation.
- **Scan Speed (v):** Lower v → more energy per area → denser.
- **Hatch Spacing (h):** Smaller h → better inter-track bonding.
- **Layer Thickness (t):** Thinner t → better resolution and density.
- **Pre-heat Temperature:** Closer to T_m → less thermal stress, less curl. **(3 marks)**

**VED Calculation:**

Given: $P = 40 \text{ W}$, $v = 4 \text{ m/s} = 4000 \text{ mm/s}$, $h = 0.25 \text{ mm}$, $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{40 \text{ J/s}}{4000 \times 0.25 \times 0.1 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{40}{100} \text{ J/mm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 0.40 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The recommended VED window for **PA12 (Polyamide 12)** is **0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³**. The calculated value of **0.40 J/mm³** lies within this optimal range, indicating the parameters will yield a **dense, well-sintered part with good mechanical properties** (typically 90–95% theoretical density). **(4 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Pattern]**
*Differentiate between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), and Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) in terms of working principle, materials, support requirements, and part density. List four industrial applications of SLS and explain any two of them in detail.*

#### Part (a) — Differentiation Table (7 marks)

| Criterion | **SLS** | **SLM** | **DMLS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working Principle | Powder particles are **sintered** (solid-state bonding, T < T_m) | Powder is **fully melted** (T > T_m) | Powder is **sintered** in solid state |
| Material | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals (Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, SS 316L, Inconel 718) | Metals (SS, Inconel, CoCr, Ti) |
| Laser Type | **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** | Fiber (1.06 μm) | Fiber (1.06 μm) |
| Support | **Not required** (loose powder supports) | Required (metal supports) | Required (metal supports) |
| Part Density | 80–95% | ≥ 99% | ≥ 95% |
| Surface Finish | Moderate (5–10 μm Ra) | Better (4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Atmosphere | Inert (N₂) | Inert (Ar/N₂) | Inert (N₂/Ar) |
| Industry | Prototyping, polymer parts | Aerospace, medical implants | Dental, jewelry, aerospace |

**(7 marks — full table)**

#### Part (b) — Industrial Applications of SLS (7 marks)

**Four key applications:**
1. **Functional polymer prototypes** (automotive and consumer goods).
2. **Medical orthotics and prosthetics** (patient-specific).
3. **Aerospace ducting and jigs** (lightweight, complex).
4. **Investment casting patterns** (burn-out patterns for metal castings).

**Detailed Explanation of Two:**

**(1) Medical Orthotics & Prosthetics (3.5 marks):**
SLS of **PA2200 (PA12)** is widely used to fabricate patient-specific orthotic devices such as ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs), cranial implants, and prosthetic sockets. The process allows the customization of lattice structures to tune **stiffness, breathability, and weight**. PA2200 is **ISO 10993-1** certified for biocompatibility (limited duration contact, < 30 days). Key benefits include rapid turnaround (often within 24–48 hours), digital patient data integration (from CT/MRI scans), and the ability to embed complex internal channels for ventilation or surgical guide features.

**(2) Investment Casting Patterns (3.5 marks):**
SLS is used to fabricate **polystyrene or PMMA-based patterns** that are then invested with a ceramic shell. The pattern is burnt out in a furnace, leaving a clean cavity into which molten metal is poured. This is particularly useful for **complex, low-volume metal parts** (e.g., turbine blades, jewelry) that would be prohibitively expensive to machine. The pattern's geometric accuracy (±0.3% typical) and the ability to merge multiple components into one pattern (assembly elimination) make SLS ideal for this application.
