## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **SLS vs SLM Confusion:** Writing "SLS melts the powder" costs **2 marks minimum**. SLS **sinters** (solid-state, below T_m); SLM **melts** (above T_m). This is the #1 KTU trap question.
> 2. **Wrong Laser Wavelength:** Students often write "SLS uses a fiber laser." Wrong — SLS uses **CO₂ (10.6 μm)**. SLM/DMLS use **fiber (1.06 μm)**.
> 3. **VED Unit Errors:** Writing the VED unit as "W/mm³" instead of "J/mm³" is a **1-mark deduction**. Energy density must be in **Joules per mm³**.
> 4. **Forgetting the "No Support" Advantage:** Many students miss this unique SLS benefit. Examiners specifically test DFM awareness; mentioning it **earns easy 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** A question asking "with a neat sketch" without a labeled diagram is auto-deducted **2–3 marks**, even if the description is correct.
> 6. **Unit Conversion in Numericals:** Scan speed is commonly given in m/s in KTU papers but must be converted to mm/s for the VED formula. Forgetting this **invalidates the numerical answer**.

- ⭐ **SLS = Polymer Powder Bed Fusion** (NOT metal; metal versions are SLM/DMLS).

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — Formal Definition

**Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** is a category of Additive Manufacturing (AM) processes defined by the **ISO/ASTM 52900** standard, in which thermal energy selectively fuses regions of a powder bed. According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PEPOT634, Module 3), PBF encompasses all AM technologies that use a **bed of powdered material** (polymer, metal, ceramic, or composite) and a **focused thermal source** (laser or electron beam) to consolidate successive layers into a 3D solid object.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEPOT634 – Module 3):** The 2024 scheme categorizes SLS strictly under *Powder Bed Fusion of Polymers*, distinguishing it from **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)** and **SLM (Selective Laser Melting)**, which deal with metallic powders. Students must NOT confuse SLS with SLM in board answers.

## 1.2 Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) — Formal Definition

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is the pioneering Powder Bed Fusion process patented by **Dr. Carl Deckard** and **Dr. Joe Beaman** at the University of Texas at Austin in **1989** (US Patent 4,863,538, expired in 2002). In SLS, a **CO₂ laser** scans a polymer powder bed, raising the local temperature just **below the melting point** of the polymer so that adjacent particles **sinter (diffusion-bond)** together without fully melting. The unfused powder acts as a natural **support structure**, eliminating the need for dedicated support scaffolding.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Sand Castle & Sun" Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy:** Imagine a beach where you want to build a sandcastle. Instead of using a blowtorch (which would melt/glassify the sand), you use a **magnifying glass to focus sunlight** onto a thin layer of damp sand. The surface grains fuse gently into a crust while the deeper sand remains loose. After a thin crust forms, you sprinkle another layer of damp sand on top, and repeat. The **loose sand beneath** acts as a built-in mold/support.
>
> This is exactly how SLS works: the **laser = magnifying glass**, the **polymer powder = damp sand**, and the **partially fused (sintered) layer = crust**. The unsintered powder below is your automatic support material.

## 1.4 Key Standard Metrics

- **Layer Thickness (t):** Typically **0.1 mm** for polymers (range 0.05–0.15 mm).
- **Build Volume (EOS P 770 example):** 700 mm × 380 mm × 580 mm.
- **Typical Laser Power (P):** 30–100 W (CO₂ laser, **λ ≈ 10.6 μm**).
- **Standard Powder Particle Size:** **10–100 μm** diameter (D50 ≈ 50 μm).

---

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

The SLS process executes the following sequence for **every layer**:

1. **Build Chamber Pre-heat:** The build platform and powder bed are pre-heated to a temperature **T_preheat** that lies **just below the polymer's melting point (T_m)**. This minimizes the thermal gradient the laser must impose and reduces thermal distortion.
2. **Powder Deposition:** A **recoater blade or rotating roller** spreads a thin, uniform layer of fresh powder from the feed chamber across the build platform.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A **CO₂ laser** (10.6 μm wavelength, well-absorbed by polymers) traces the 2D cross-section of the part, following **scan vectors** defined by the slicer. The laser energy fuses (sinters) the powder particles.
4. **Platform Increment:** The build platform descends by **one layer thickness (t)**.
5. **Repeat:** Steps 2–4 are repeated for **n = Total Height / t** layers.
6. **Cooling & Depowdering:** After the build completes, the chamber cools slowly (to avoid warping), and operators manually brush/siphon the unsintered powder to reveal the part.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Pre-heating below T_m:** Prevents **curl and warpage**. If the powder bed were cold, the steep thermal gradient would cause the sintered layer to curl upward at the edges due to non-uniform shrinkage.
- **CO₂ laser wavelength choice:** Polymers like **Polyamide 12 (PA12 / Nylon 12)** absorb strongly at **10.6 μm**, making the CO₂ laser ideal. (Fiber lasers at 1.06 μm pass through polymer powder with minimal absorption — they are unsuitable for SLS of polymers.)
- **Loose powder as support:** Sintered parts are embedded in a "powder cake." For complex overhangs, lattices, and internal channels, this loose powder provides **omnidirectional support without dedicated scaffolds**, which is SLS's biggest design-for-manufacturing (DFM) advantage.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range (Polymer SLS) | Physical Effect |
|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power | $P$ | **30 – 100 W** | Energy delivered per unit time |
| Scan Speed | $v$ | **1 – 8 m/s** (≈ 4000–25000 mm/s) | Speed of laser spot |
| Hatch Spacing | $h$ | **0.2 – 0.5 mm** | Distance between adjacent scan lines |
| Layer Thickness | $t$ | **0.05 – 0.15 mm** | Vertical resolution |
| Preheat Temperature | $T_{pre}$ | $T_m - 5°C$ to $T_m - 2°C$ | Reduces thermal shock |
| Powder Bed Temperature | $T_{bed}$ | ≈ $T_{pre}$ | Maintained throughout build |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master this table — it is the single most-tested computation in the SLS section of KTU ESE papers.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $VED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $\text{J/mm}^3$ | Quantifies total energy input per unit volume of powder |
| 2 | **Build Time Estimate** | $T_{build} = \dfrac{N_{layers} \cdot A_{layer}}{v \cdot w_{laser}} \cdot k$ | hours | Where $N_{layers} = H/t$, $w_{laser}$ = effective scan width, $k$ = overhead factor |
| 3 | **Sintering Temperature Window** | $T_{sinter} = T_m - \Delta T$ | °C | $\Delta T$ = 2–10 °C for most semi-crystalline polymers |
| 4 | **Layer Count** | $N = \dfrac{H_{part}}{t}$ | dimensionless | $H_{part}$ = part height |
| 5 | **Cooling Strain** | $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ = CTE, used to estimate residual stress |
| 6 | **Hatch Fill Density** | $\rho_{hatch} = \dfrac{w_{laser}}{h}$ | dimensionless | Must be $\geq 1$ to avoid porosity gaps |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The symbol $\vert x \vert$ (absolute value) is written using `\vert` in LaTeX. Do **not** use the vertical pipe `|` in a markdown table — it will break the table parser. KTU examiners deduct marks for unreadable answer sheets.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace:** Lightweight polymer jigs, fixtures, and ducting (Boeing, Airbus).
- **Medical:** Patient-specific orthotics, prosthetics, and anatomical models (PA2200 is **biocompatible — ISO 10993-1** certified).
- **Automotive:** Rapid prototyping of interior trim and functional prototypes (BMW, Tesla).
- **Foundry Patterns:** Investment casting patterns (SLS parts can be burnt out cleanly, leaving a shell mold).

---

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The VED is the single most important parameter controlling **part density, mechanical strength, and porosity** in SLS. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define Linear Energy Density (LED)

The **linear energy density** is the energy delivered per unit length of the laser scan path.

$$
LED = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}
$$

*Text:* Here, $P$ is the laser power in watts (J/s) and $v$ is the scan speed in mm/s. Dividing energy per second by length per second yields energy per unit length.

### Step 2 — Define Areal Energy Density (AED)

The laser spot overlaps adjacent scan lines by the **hatch spacing $h$**. The energy per unit area of a single scanned layer is:

$$
AED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}
$$

*Text:* Multiplying the scan length by the hatch spacing gives the effective area covered per unit time. Energy per time divided by area per time yields energy per unit area.

### Step 3 — Extend to Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

A layer has thickness $t$. The volume of material sintered per unit scan length is $h \cdot t$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
\text{Units: } & \frac{[\text{W}]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The energy input (Joules) per unit volume of fused material is the definitive process "knob" for SLS.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** An SLS machine uses a 50 W CO₂ laser, scan speed of 5 m/s, hatch spacing of 0.3 mm, and layer thickness of 0.1 mm. Calculate the VED.

**Given:**
- $P = 50 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ J/s}$
- $v = 5 \text{ m/s} = 5000 \text{ mm/s}$
- $h = 0.3 \text{ mm}$
- $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50 \text{ J/s}}{5000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.3 \text{ mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50}{150} \text{ J/mm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 0.333 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* Note that 1 J/mm³ = 10⁹ J/m³ = 1 GJ/m³. For PA12, the recommended VED window is **0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³**. Our result of 0.333 falls within this window, indicating a healthy sintered part.

### Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis

| Effect of ↑ parameter on VED | Direction | Consequence on Part |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ $P$ | VED ↑ | Over-sintering, degradation, smoke |
| ↑ $v$ | VED ↓ | Under-sintering, porous, weak part |
| ↑ $h$ | VED ↓ | Gaps between scan lines |
| ↑ $t$ | VED ↓ | Incomplete layer fusion |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Build Time Estimate

### Step 1 — Number of Layers

$$
N = \frac{H_{part}}{t}
$$

*Text:* Total part height divided by layer thickness yields the layer count.

### Step 2 — Scan Path Length per Layer

For a part of cross-sectional area $A_{layer}$ and an effective laser scan width $w_{laser}$:

$$
L_{scan} \approx \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser}}
$$

*Text:* This assumes serpentine (raster) scanning filling the cross-section.

### Step 3 — Pure Laser Scanning Time

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{L_{scan}}{v} = \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* Distance divided by speed equals the time to trace the layer.

### Step 4 — Total Build Time with Overhead

$$
T_{build} = N \cdot T_{scan} \cdot k_{overhead} = \frac{H_{part} \cdot A_{layer} \cdot k}{t \cdot w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* The overhead factor $k$ (typically **1.2 – 1.5**) accounts for recoater motion, laser on/off, and powder feed delays.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A part is 100 mm tall, has a 50 mm × 50 mm square cross-section, $t = 0.1$ mm, $w_{laser} = 0.4$ mm, $v = 4000$ mm/s, $k = 1.3$. Estimate build time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{100}{0.1} = 1000 \text{ layers} \\[6pt]
A_{layer} &= 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2 \\[6pt]
T_{scan} &= \frac{2500}{0.4 \times 4000} = \frac{2500}{1600} = 1.5625 \text{ s/layer} \\[6pt]
T_{build} &= 1000 \times 1.5625 \times 1.3 = 2031.25 \text{ s} \approx 33.85 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The 1.3 overhead factor accounts for real-world recoater delays. Pure scan time would have been ~26 min.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SLS vs. Competing PBF Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask "Differentiate between SLS, SLM, and DMLS." Master this matrix.**

| Attribute | **SLS** | **SLM** (Selective Laser Melting) | **DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material State | Powder is **sintered** (not melted) | Powder is **fully melted** | Powder is **sintered** (solid-state diffusion) |
| Materials | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals only (Ti, Al, SS 316L, Inconel) | Metals only (SS, Inconel, CoCr, Ti) |
| Laser | **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** |
| Temperature | Below T_m (sintering) | Above T_m (melting) | Below T_m (sintering) |
| Support | **Not required** (loose powder supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) |
| Part Density | **80 – 95%** | **≥ 99%** (near-fully dense) | **≥ 95%** |
| Surface Finish | Rough (≈ 5–10 μm Ra after bead blast) | Better (≈ 4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Patent Status | **Expired 2002** (open technology) | Still has residual IP (varies) | **EOS patent expired 2016** |
| Typical Use | Functional polymer prototypes, low-volume polymer parts | High-performance aerospace/medical metal parts | Metal prototypes, dental, jewelry |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — SLS Machine Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    node1["LASER SOURCE - CO2 10.6 micrometer 30 to 100 W"] --> node2["BEAM STEERING MIRRORS - GALVANOMETER SCANNER"]
    node2 --> node3["F-THETA LENS - FOCUSES BEAM ON POWDER BED"]
    node3 --> node4["BUILD CHAMBER - INERT N2 ATMOSPHERE BELOW T_M"]
    node4 --> node5["POWDER BED - POLYMER PARTICLES 10 TO 100 MICRON"]
    node5 --> node6["RECOATER ROLLER OR BLADE - DEPOSITS NEW LAYER"]
    node6 --> node7["FEED CHAMBER - RESERVOIR OF FRESH POWDER"]
    node7 --> node6
    node4 --> node8["BUILD PLATFORM - DESCENDS BY LAYER THICKNESS T"]
    node8 --> node4
    node4 --> node9["PREHEAT HEATERS - MAINTAIN T_PRE BELOW T_MELT"]
    node9 --> node4
    node4 --> node10["SINTERED PART IN POWDER CAKE - AFTER BUILD"]
    node10 --> node11["DEPOWDERING STATION - BRUSH AND SUCTION"]
    node11 --> node12["FINISHED SLS PART"]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Layer-by-Layer Process Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["START BUILD"] --> stepB["PREHEAT CHAMBER TO T_PRE - JUST BELOW T_M"]
    stepB --> stepC["RECOATER SPREADS FRESH POWDER LAYER OF THICKNESS T"]
    stepC --> stepD{"SLICER CHECKS - IS THIS LAYER INSIDE PART CROSS SECTION"}
    stepD -- YES --> stepE["CO2 LASER SCANS CROSS SECTION AT POWER P AND SPEED V"]
    stepD -- NO --> stepF["LASER OFF - POWDER LEFT LOOSE AS NATURAL SUPPORT"]
    stepE --> stepG["LAYER COMPLETE - SINTERED TRACKS FUSE"]
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH["BUILD PLATFORM DESCENDS BY T MM"]
    stepH --> stepI{"ALL N LAYERS COMPLETE - N = H PART / T"}
    stepI -- NO --> stepC
    stepI -- YES --> stepJ["SLOW CONTROLLED COOLING TO AMBIENT"]
    stepJ --> stepK["DEPOWDERING - REMOVE LOOSE UNSINTERED POWDER"]
    stepK --> stepL["FINISHED SLS PART READY FOR POST PROCESSING"]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — VED Parameter Influence Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    paramA["LASER POWER P IN W"] --> ved["VED = P DIVIDED BY V H T IN J PER MM3"]
    paramB["SCAN SPEED V IN MM PER S"] --> ved
    paramC["HATCH SPACING H IN MM"] --> ved
    paramD["LAYER THICKNESS T IN MM"] --> ved
    ved --> outA["VED LESS THAN 0.20 - UNDERSINTERED - POROUS WEAK"]
    ved --> outB["VED 0.20 TO 0.45 - OPTIMAL - DENSE STRONG"]
    ved --> outC["VED GREATER THAN 0.45 - OVERSINTERED - DEGRADATION"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Pattern]**
*Explain the principle of Selective Laser Sintering with a neat sketch. Why is a CO₂ laser preferred over a fiber laser for SLS of polymers?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

SLS is a Powder Bed Fusion process in which a **CO₂ laser (10.6 μm wavelength)** selectively scans a pre-heated polymer powder bed to sinter (diffusion-bond) particles together layer by layer. The powder bed is maintained at a temperature **just below the polymer's melting point (T_m - 2 to 10 °C)** to minimize thermal gradients.

A **CO₂ laser is preferred** because polymer powders (e.g., PA12) exhibit strong absorption bands in the **mid-infrared (10.6 μm)** region, whereas their absorption of fiber-laser wavelengths (1.06 μm) is very low. Using a fiber laser would result in poor energy coupling and inadequate sintering. **(3 marks)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**
*List any three advantages and two limitations of SLS.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Advantages:**
1. **No support structures required** — the unfused powder bed acts as natural support.
2. **Material efficiency** — unsintered powder can be **reused** (typically 70–80% refresh rate for PA12).
3. **Complex geometries** are possible, including lattice structures and internal channels. **(2 marks)**

**Limitations:**
1. **Rough surface finish** (typically 5–10 μm Ra) requires post-processing.
2. **Lower part density** (80–95%) compared to injection molding or SLM. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — Standard Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Pattern]**
*With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of the Selective Laser Sintering process. Discuss the critical process parameters affecting part quality. Also, calculate the Volumetric Energy Density for the following case: Laser Power = 40 W, Scan Speed = 4 m/s, Hatch Spacing = 0.25 mm, Layer Thickness = 0.1 mm. Comment on the suitability of the obtained value for PA12 powder.*

#### Part (a) — Working Principle & Diagram (7 marks)

The Selective Laser Sintering process operates on the principle of **solid-state thermal fusion** of polymer powder using a focused CO₂ laser. The major subsystems are:

1. **CO₂ Laser Source** (10.6 μm, 30–100 W) — provides the thermal energy.
2. **Galvanometer Scan Head** with F-theta lens — directs the beam across the powder bed.
3. **Build Chamber** — sealed, inert (N₂) atmosphere, pre-heated to $T_{pre} < T_m$.
4. **Build Platform** — descends by layer thickness $t$ after each layer is sintered.
5. **Feed Chamber & Recoater** — supplies and spreads fresh powder.
6. **Pre-heat Heaters** — maintain uniform bed temperature.

The process steps are: **(i)** chamber pre-heat, **(ii)** powder deposition by recoater, **(iii)** laser sintering of the cross-section, **(iv)** platform descent, **(v)** repeat. After the build, **slow cooling** prevents warpage, followed by **depowdering**. **(7 marks — 4 for diagram, 3 for description)**

#### Part (b) — Process Parameters & VED Calculation (7 marks)

**Critical process parameters:**
- **Laser Power (P):** Higher P → denser part, but risk of degradation.
- **Scan Speed (v):** Lower v → more energy per area → denser.
- **Hatch Spacing (h):** Smaller h → better inter-track bonding.
- **Layer Thickness (t):** Thinner t → better resolution and density.
- **Pre-heat Temperature:** Closer to T_m → less thermal stress, less curl. **(3 marks)**

**VED Calculation:**

Given: $P = 40 \text{ W}$, $v = 4 \text{ m/s} = 4000 \text{ mm/s}$, $h = 0.25 \text{ mm}$, $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{40 \text{ J/s}}{4000 \times 0.25 \times 0.1 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{40}{100} \text{ J/mm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 0.40 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:** The recommended VED window for **PA12 (Polyamide 12)** is **0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³**. The calculated value of **0.40 J/mm³** lies within this optimal range, indicating the parameters will yield a **dense, well-sintered part with good mechanical properties** (typically 90–95% theoretical density). **(4 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – 2024 Scheme, Module 3 Pattern]**
*Differentiate between Selective Laser Sintering (SLS), Selective Laser Melting (SLM), and Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) in terms of working principle, materials, support requirements, and part density. List four industrial applications of SLS and explain any two of them in detail.*

#### Part (a) — Differentiation Table (7 marks)

| Criterion | **SLS** | **SLM** | **DMLS** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working Principle | Powder particles are **sintered** (solid-state bonding, T < T_m) | Powder is **fully melted** (T > T_m) | Powder is **sintered** in solid state |
| Material | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals (Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, SS 316L, Inconel 718) | Metals (SS, Inconel, CoCr, Ti) |
| Laser Type | **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** | Fiber (1.06 μm) | Fiber (1.06 μm) |
| Support | **Not required** (loose powder supports) | Required (metal supports) | Required (metal supports) |
| Part Density | 80–95% | ≥ 99% | ≥ 95% |
| Surface Finish | Moderate (5–10 μm Ra) | Better (4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Atmosphere | Inert (N₂) | Inert (Ar/N₂) | Inert (N₂/Ar) |
| Industry | Prototyping, polymer parts | Aerospace, medical implants | Dental, jewelry, aerospace |

**(7 marks — full table)**

#### Part (b) — Industrial Applications of SLS (7 marks)

**Four key applications:**
1. **Functional polymer prototypes** (automotive and consumer goods).
2. **Medical orthotics and prosthetics** (patient-specific).
3. **Aerospace ducting and jigs** (lightweight, complex).
4. **Investment casting patterns** (burn-out patterns for metal castings).

**Detailed Explanation of Two:**

**(1) Medical Orthotics & Prosthetics (3.5 marks):**
SLS of **PA2200 (PA12)** is widely used to fabricate patient-specific orthotic devices such as ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs), cranial implants, and prosthetic sockets. The process allows the customization of lattice structures to tune **stiffness, breathability, and weight**. PA2200 is **ISO 10993-1** certified for biocompatibility (limited duration contact, < 30 days). Key benefits include rapid turnaround (often within 24–48 hours), digital patient data integration (from CT/MRI scans), and the ability to embed complex internal channels for ventilation or surgical guide features.

**(2) Investment Casting Patterns (3.5 marks):**
SLS is used to fabricate **polystyrene or PMMA-based patterns** that are then invested with a ceramic shell. The pattern is burnt out in a furnace, leaving a clean cavity into which molten metal is poured. This is particularly useful for **complex, low-volume metal parts** (e.g., turbine blades, jewelry) that would be prohibitively expensive to machine. The pattern's geometric accuracy (±0.3% typical) and the ability to merge multiple components into one pattern (assembly elimination) make SLS ideal for this application.

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **SLS vs SLM Confusion:** Writing "SLS melts the powder" costs **2 marks minimum**. SLS **sinters** (solid-state, below T_m); SLM **melts** (above T_m). This is the #1 KTU trap question.
> 2. **Wrong Laser Wavelength:** Students often write "SLS uses a fiber laser." Wrong — SLS uses **CO₂ (10.6 μm)**. SLM/DMLS use **fiber (1.06 μm)**.
> 3. **VED Unit Errors:** Writing the VED unit as "W/mm³" instead of "J/mm³" is a **1-mark deduction**. Energy density must be in **Joules per mm³**.
> 4. **Forgetting the "No Support" Advantage:** Many students miss this unique SLS benefit. Examiners specifically test DFM awareness; mentioning it **earns easy 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** A question asking "with a neat sketch" without a labeled diagram is auto-deducted **2–3 marks**, even if the description is correct.
> 6. **Unit Conversion in Numericals:** Scan speed is commonly given in m/s in KTU papers but must be converted to mm/s for the VED formula. Forgetting this **invalidates the numerical answer**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read the night before the exam:**

- ⭐ **SLS = Polymer Powder Bed Fusion** (NOT metal; metal versions are SLM/DMLS).
- ⭐ **SLS uses CO₂ laser (10.6 μm)**, NOT fiber laser.
- ⭐ **Sintering = below T_m** (solid-state); **Melting = above T_m** (used in SLM).
- ⭐ **No support structures required** — unsintered powder provides natural support.
- ⭐ **VED Formula (MUST memorize):** $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ in **J/mm³**.
- ⭐ **Optimal VED for PA12: 0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³.**
- ⭐ **Typical parameters:** P = 30–100 W, v = 1–8 m/s, h = 0.2–0.5 mm, t = 0.05–0.15 mm.
- ⭐ **Pre-heat temperature: T_m - 2 to 10 °C** (reduces curl and warpage).
- ⭐ **Part density: 80–95%** (lower than SLM's ≥ 99%).
- ⭐ **Material reuse:** ~70–80% of unsintered PA12 powder is reusable.
- ⭐ **Inventors:** Dr. Carl Deckard & Dr. Joe Beaman, University of Texas at Austin, 1989.
- ⭐ **Patent expired in 2002** — opened the technology to widespread commercialization.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Functional prototypes, medical orthotics, aerospace ducting, investment casting patterns.
- ⭐ **PA2200 = ISO 10993-1 biocompatible** — used for medical devices.
- ⭐ **Cooling rate must be slow** to avoid residual stresses and warpage.
- ⭐ **Surface roughness 5–10 μm Ra** — post-processing (bead blasting, tumbling) often required.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

The VED is the single most important parameter controlling **part density, mechanical strength, and porosity** in SLS. Let us derive it rigorously.

### Step 1 — Define Linear Energy Density (LED)

The **linear energy density** is the energy delivered per unit length of the laser scan path.

$$
LED = \frac{P}{v} \quad \text{[J/mm]}
$$

*Text:* Here, $P$ is the laser power in watts (J/s) and $v$ is the scan speed in mm/s. Dividing energy per second by length per second yields energy per unit length.

### Step 2 — Define Areal Energy Density (AED)

The laser spot overlaps adjacent scan lines by the **hatch spacing $h$**. The energy per unit area of a single scanned layer is:

$$
AED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h} \quad \text{[J/mm}^2\text{]}
$$

*Text:* Multiplying the scan length by the hatch spacing gives the effective area covered per unit time. Energy per time divided by area per time yields energy per unit area.

### Step 3 — Extend to Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

A layer has thickness $t$. The volume of material sintered per unit scan length is $h \cdot t$. Therefore:

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
\text{Units: } & \frac{[\text{W}]}{[\text{mm/s}] \cdot [\text{mm}] \cdot [\text{mm}]} = \frac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The energy input (Joules) per unit volume of fused material is the definitive process "knob" for SLS.

### Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Typical Problem)

> **Problem (KTU Pattern):** An SLS machine uses a 50 W CO₂ laser, scan speed of 5 m/s, hatch spacing of 0.3 mm, and layer thickness of 0.1 mm. Calculate the VED.

**Given:**
- $P = 50 \text{ W} = 50 \text{ J/s}$
- $v = 5 \text{ m/s} = 5000 \text{ mm/s}$
- $h = 0.3 \text{ mm}$
- $t = 0.1 \text{ mm}$

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
VED &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50 \text{ J/s}}{5000 \text{ mm/s} \times 0.3 \text{ mm} \times 0.1 \text{ mm}} \\[6pt]
&= \frac{50}{150} \text{ J/mm}^3 \\[6pt]
&= 0.333 \text{ J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* Note that 1 J/mm³ = 10⁹ J/m³ = 1 GJ/m³. For PA12, the recommended VED window is **0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³**. Our result of 0.333 falls within this window, indicating a healthy sintered part.

### Step 5 — Sensitivity Analysis

| Effect of ↑ parameter on VED | Direction | Consequence on Part |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ $P$ | VED ↑ | Over-sintering, degradation, smoke |
| ↑ $v$ | VED ↓ | Under-sintering, porous, weak part |
| ↑ $h$ | VED ↓ | Gaps between scan lines |
| ↑ $t$ | VED ↓ | Incomplete layer fusion |

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Total Build Time Estimate

### Step 1 — Number of Layers

$$
N = \frac{H_{part}}{t}
$$

*Text:* Total part height divided by layer thickness yields the layer count.

### Step 2 — Scan Path Length per Layer

For a part of cross-sectional area $A_{layer}$ and an effective laser scan width $w_{laser}$:

$$
L_{scan} \approx \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser}}
$$

*Text:* This assumes serpentine (raster) scanning filling the cross-section.

### Step 3 — Pure Laser Scanning Time

$$
T_{scan} = \frac{L_{scan}}{v} = \frac{A_{layer}}{w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* Distance divided by speed equals the time to trace the layer.

### Step 4 — Total Build Time with Overhead

$$
T_{build} = N \cdot T_{scan} \cdot k_{overhead} = \frac{H_{part} \cdot A_{layer} \cdot k}{t \cdot w_{laser} \cdot v}
$$

*Text:* The overhead factor $k$ (typically **1.2 – 1.5**) accounts for recoater motion, laser on/off, and powder feed delays.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A part is 100 mm tall, has a 50 mm × 50 mm square cross-section, $t = 0.1$ mm, $w_{laser} = 0.4$ mm, $v = 4000$ mm/s, $k = 1.3$. Estimate build time.

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \frac{100}{0.1} = 1000 \text{ layers} \\[6pt]
A_{layer} &= 50 \times 50 = 2500 \text{ mm}^2 \\[6pt]
T_{scan} &= \frac{2500}{0.4 \times 4000} = \frac{2500}{1600} = 1.5625 \text{ s/layer} \\[6pt]
T_{build} &= 1000 \times 1.5625 \times 1.3 = 2031.25 \text{ s} \approx 33.85 \text{ min}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Text:* The 1.3 overhead factor accounts for real-world recoater delays. Pure scan time would have been ~26 min.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SLS vs. Competing PBF Processes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 examiners frequently ask "Differentiate between SLS, SLM, and DMLS." Master this matrix.**

| Attribute | **SLS** | **SLM** (Selective Laser Melting) | **DMLS** (Direct Metal Laser Sintering) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Material State | Powder is **sintered** (not melted) | Powder is **fully melted** | Powder is **sintered** (solid-state diffusion) |
| Materials | Polymers (PA12, PA11, TPU, PEEK) | Metals only (Ti, Al, SS 316L, Inconel) | Metals only (SS, Inconel, CoCr, Ti) |
| Laser | **CO₂ (10.6 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** | **Fiber (1.06 μm)** |
| Temperature | Below T_m (sintering) | Above T_m (melting) | Below T_m (sintering) |
| Support | **Not required** (loose powder supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) | Required (sacrificial metal supports) |
| Part Density | **80 – 95%** | **≥ 99%** (near-fully dense) | **≥ 95%** |
| Surface Finish | Rough (≈ 5–10 μm Ra after bead blast) | Better (≈ 4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Patent Status | **Expired 2002** (open technology) | Still has residual IP (varies) | **EOS patent expired 2016** |
| Typical Use | Functional polymer prototypes, low-volume polymer parts | High-performance aerospace/medical metal parts | Metal prototypes, dental, jewelry |

| Surface Finish | Moderate (5–10 μm Ra) | Better (4–8 μm Ra) | Similar to SLM |
| Atmosphere | Inert (N₂) | Inert (Ar/N₂) | Inert (N₂/Ar) |
| Industry | Prototyping, polymer parts | Aerospace, medical implants | Dental, jewelry, aerospace |

**(7 marks — full table)**

#### Part (b) — Industrial Applications of SLS (7 marks)

**Four key applications:**
1. **Functional polymer prototypes** (automotive and consumer goods).
2. **Medical orthotics and prosthetics** (patient-specific).
3. **Aerospace ducting and jigs** (lightweight, complex).
4. **Investment casting patterns** (burn-out patterns for metal castings).

**Detailed Explanation of Two:**

**(1) Medical Orthotics & Prosthetics (3.5 marks):**
SLS of **PA2200 (PA12)** is widely used to fabricate patient-specific orthotic devices such as ankle-foot orthoses (AFOs), cranial implants, and prosthetic sockets. The process allows the customization of lattice structures to tune **stiffness, breathability, and weight**. PA2200 is **ISO 10993-1** certified for biocompatibility (limited duration contact, < 30 days). Key benefits include rapid turnaround (often within 24–48 hours), digital patient data integration (from CT/MRI scans), and the ability to embed complex internal channels for ventilation or surgical guide features.

**(2) Investment Casting Patterns (3.5 marks):**
SLS is used to fabricate **polystyrene or PMMA-based patterns** that are then invested with a ceramic shell. The pattern is burnt out in a furnace, leaving a clean cavity into which molten metal is poured. This is particularly useful for **complex, low-volume metal parts** (e.g., turbine blades, jewelry) that would be prohibitively expensive to machine. The pattern's geometric accuracy (±0.3% typical) and the ability to merge multiple components into one pattern (assembly elimination) make SLS ideal for this application.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark Deductions — Read Carefully:**
> 1. **SLS vs SLM Confusion:** Writing "SLS melts the powder" costs **2 marks minimum**. SLS **sinters** (solid-state, below T_m); SLM **melts** (above T_m). This is the #1 KTU trap question.
> 2. **Wrong Laser Wavelength:** Students often write "SLS uses a fiber laser." Wrong — SLS uses **CO₂ (10.6 μm)**. SLM/DMLS use **fiber (1.06 μm)**.
> 3. **VED Unit Errors:** Writing the VED unit as "W/mm³" instead of "J/mm³" is a **1-mark deduction**. Energy density must be in **Joules per mm³**.
> 4. **Forgetting the "No Support" Advantage:** Many students miss this unique SLS benefit. Examiners specifically test DFM awareness; mentioning it **earns easy 1–2 marks**.
> 5. **Skipping the Diagram:** A question asking "with a neat sketch" without a labeled diagram is auto-deducted **2–3 marks**, even if the description is correct.
> 6. **Unit Conversion in Numericals:** Scan speed is commonly given in m/s in KTU papers but must be converted to mm/s for the VED formula. Forgetting this **invalidates the numerical answer**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Read the night before the exam:**

- ⭐ **SLS = Polymer Powder Bed Fusion** (NOT metal; metal versions are SLM/DMLS).
- ⭐ **SLS uses CO₂ laser (10.6 μm)**, NOT fiber laser.
- ⭐ **Sintering = below T_m** (solid-state); **Melting = above T_m** (used in SLM).
- ⭐ **No support structures required** — unsintered powder provides natural support.
- ⭐ **VED Formula (MUST memorize):** $VED = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ in **J/mm³**.
- ⭐ **Optimal VED for PA12: 0.20 – 0.45 J/mm³.**
- ⭐ **Typical parameters:** P = 30–100 W, v = 1–8 m/s, h = 0.2–0.5 mm, t = 0.05–0.15 mm.
- ⭐ **Pre-heat temperature: T_m - 2 to 10 °C** (reduces curl and warpage).
- ⭐ **Part density: 80–95%** (lower than SLM's ≥ 99%).
- ⭐ **Material reuse:** ~70–80% of unsintered PA12 powder is reusable.
- ⭐ **Inventors:** Dr. Carl Deckard & Dr. Joe Beaman, University of Texas at Austin, 1989.
- ⭐ **Patent expired in 2002** — opened the technology to widespread commercialization.
- ⭐ **Applications:** Functional prototypes, medical orthotics, aerospace ducting, investment casting patterns.
- ⭐ **PA2200 = ISO 10993-1 biocompatible** — used for medical devices.
- ⭐ **Cooling rate must be slow** to avoid residual stresses and warpage.
- ⭐ **Surface roughness 5–10 μm Ra** — post-processing (bead blasting, tumbling) often required.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Additive Manufacturing (AM)**, **Materials** refer to the broad spectrum of feedstocks—metals, polymers, ceramics, composites, and hybrid systems—that are consolidated layer-by-layer through controlled thermal, chemical, or mechanical energy sources to fabricate functional parts. Unlike subtractive manufacturing, AM materials are delivered in **specific pre-formatted states** (powders, wires, filaments, liquid resins, pellets, or sheets), and the *as-supplied form-factor* is inseparable from the process selection itself.

Under the **ASTM/ISO 52900:2021** taxonomy adopted by KTU 2024, the seven AM process categories are largely defined by the **material state and energy binding mechanism**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):**
> The material choice in AM is dictated by three inseparable pillars — **(i) Material Form**, **(ii) Energy Source Compatibility**, and **(iii) End-use Functional Requirements**. Mastery of powder characteristics (flow, morphology, size distribution, thermal conductivity) is non-negotiable for any board examination question on materials.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Cake Analogy" 🎂

Think of AM like baking a cake. The **material** is the batter (powder, filament, or resin). The **machine** is the oven (laser, electron beam, extruder, or UV lamp). The **process parameters** are the temperature and timing.

Just as you cannot bake a soufflé with bread dough, you cannot laser-sinter a flexible TPU filament the same way you sinter Ti-6Al-4V powder. The **material's physical state** (powder grain size, melt viscosity, photo-reactivity) dictates:

- How much **energy** it needs to consolidate.
- How it flows from the **feeder/recoater** to the build platform.
- How the final part behaves under **load, heat, and corrosion**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:**
> Imagine a **paintbrush artist** (the AM process). The brush technique, stroke speed, and pressure all depend on whether the artist is using **watercolor, oil, or acrylic**. The paint = the material. The brush = the energy source. A wrong pairing yields a ruined canvas — exactly what happens in AM when material-process compatibility is violated.

## 1.3 Classification of AM Materials (KTU Board-Ready Framework)

AM materials are classified along **four orthogonal axes**:

| Axis | Categories |
|------|------------|
| **By Chemistry** | Metals, Polymers, Ceramics, Composites, Hybrid (multi-material) |
| **By Physical Form** | Powder, Wire, Filament, Liquid Resin, Pellets, Sheet/film |
| **By Process Family** | Powder Bed Fusion (PBF), Directed Energy Deposition (DED), Vat Photopolymerization (VPP), Material Extrusion (MEX), Material Jetting (MJT), Binder Jetting (BJT), Sheet Lamination (SHL) |
| **By Functional Role** | Structural, Biocompatible, Conductive, Dielectric, Magnetic, Smart/responsive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Definition — Powder Flowability:**
> A powder's ability to spread uniformly across the build platform under gravity/recoater action. Quantified by the **Hall Flow Rate (sec/50g)** and the **Carr Index** (compressibility %). These directly govern layer uniformity and defect density.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Material Taxonomy — Expanded View for KTU 2024

### 2.1.1 Metal Powders (Most Board-Exam Relevant)

Metal powders dominate the KTU Module 3 syllabus. They are produced via:

1. **Gas Atomization** — molten metal disintegrated by high-pressure inert gas (Ar, N₂). Yields **spherical** particles — preferred for PBF and DED.
2. **Plasma Atomization** — wire feedstock melted by plasma jets. Highest sphericity, used for **titanium and reactive alloys**.
3. **Plasma Rotating Electrode Process (PREP)** — centrifugal atomization. Used for **Ti-6Al-4V aerospace grade**.
4. **Water Atomization** — irregular morphology, cheaper, used in **Binder Jetting**.
5. **Mechanical Milling/Crushing** — angular particles, suitable for **SHL and some DED**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** Spherical particles flow better, pack denser (~60-65% theoretical), and absorb laser energy uniformly. Irregular particles pack poorly (~40-50%) but offer superior mechanical interlocking for SHL/BJT.

### 2.1.2 Polymer Feedstocks

| Form | Typical Materials | Compatible Processes |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|
| **Filament (1.75/2.85 mm)** | PLA, ABS, PETG, TPU, PEEK, PEKK, ULTEM | FDM (Material Extrusion) |
| **Powder (50–100 µm)** | PA12 (Nylon), PA11, TPU, PEEK | SLS (PBF Polymer) |
| **Liquid Resin** | Acrylates, Epoxy-acrylates, Ceramers | SLA, DLP (VPP) |
| **Pellets** | Recycled polymers, high-viscosity resins | Large-format MEX, Pellet FDM |

### 2.1.3 Ceramics & Composites

- **Ceramic AM routes**: VPP (photopolymer-loaded with Al₂O₃/ZrO₂), Binder Jetting (silica sand, gypsum), Material Extrusion (robocasting of pastes).
- **Composites**: short-fiber reinforced (carbon-fiber-PLA), continuous fiber (Markforged), functionally graded materials (FGMs) made by DED.

## 2.2 Critical Powder Characteristics (PPCs) — The KTU Must-Know

The **ASTM F3049** standard defines the powder property metrics tested for every PBF/DED metal powder lot:

1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** — measured by laser diffraction (e.g., Malvern Mastersizer). Standard range for **LPBF**: **15–45 µm**; for **EBM**: **45–105 µm**.
2. **Particle Shape (Aspect Ratio)** — image analysis via SEM. Sphericity ≥ 0.85 preferred.
3. **Flow Rate (Hall/Carney)** — sec for 50g to flow through a calibrated orifice.
4. **Apparent Density** — mass per unit volume of loose powder.
5. **Tap Density** — density after mechanical tapping (e.g., 100 taps).
6. **Hausner Ratio** = Tap Density / Apparent Density.
7. **Carr Index (Compressibility)** = (Tap − Apparent) / Tap × 100 %.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick — "Hausner-Happy" Rule:**
> If **Hausner Ratio < 1.25** → powder flows **well**. If > 1.4 → **poor flowability**, expect recoater streaks and porosity defects.

## 2.3 Energy Density — The Master Equation

The single most important KTU equation linking material and process is the **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad \left[\text{J/mm}^3\right]$$

where:

- $P$ = Laser/E-beam power (**W**)
- $v$ = Scan speed (**mm/s**)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (**mm**)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (**mm**)

A related formulation using **scan spacing ($s$)** instead of hatch spacing is also accepted:

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot d \cdot s}$$

where $d$ is the effective beam diameter.

### 2.3.1 KTU Material-Specific Energy Windows (Cheat Sheet)

| Material Class | Typical $E_v$ Window (J/mm³) | Failure if Too Low | Failure if Too High |
|----------------|------------------------------|--------------------|---------------------|
| **Ti-6Al-4V (LPBF)** | 60 – 120 | Lack of fusion (LoF) porosity | Keyhole porosity, vaporization |
| **316L Stainless Steel** | 80 – 150 | Lack of fusion | Balling, spatter |
| **AlSi10Mg** | 30 – 70 | Porosity, poor wetting | Evaporation of Mg, keyhole |
| **Inconel 718** | 100 – 200 | Unmelted particles | Cracking (residual stress) |
| **PA12 (SLS)** | 0.04 – 0.06 (per area, not volume) | Insufficient sintering | Curl, degradation |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|------------------|---------------|-------|---------------------|
| 1 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v = P / (v h t)$ | J/mm³ | Energy delivered per unit volume |
| 2 | Linear Energy Density | $E_l = P / v$ | J/mm | Energy per unit scan length |
| 3 | Areal Energy Density | $E_a = P / (v \cdot d)$ | J/mm² | Energy per unit spot area |
| 4 | Packing Density | $\rho_p = m_p / V_b$ | g/cm³ | Loose powder density |
| 5 | Hausner Ratio | $H_R = \rho_{tap} / \rho_{app}$ | dimensionless | Flowability indicator (<1.25 = good) |
| 6 | Carr Index | $CI = (\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app})/\rho_{tap} \times 100$ | % | Compressibility (<15 = good flow) |
| 7 | Layer thickness time | $t_l = t_{layer} / v_{build}$ | s/layer | Build time per layer |
| 8 | Laser absorption (Beer-Lambert) | $I(z) = I_0 e^{-\alpha z}$ | W/m² | Energy decay through powder bed |
| 9 | Porosity target | $P_{rel} = 1 - (\rho_{part}/\rho_{theory})$ | % | Often target < 0.5 % for aerospace |
| 10 | Specific surface area (powder) | $SSA = 6/(\rho_s \cdot d_{50})$ | m²/g | Critical for binder jetting & oxidation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Pipe Safeguard:** All table rows use $\vert$ / LaTeX formatting to avoid breaking markdown table syntax with literal pipe characters.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace (GE Aviation, Rolls-Royce)**: Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718 LPBF blades — material purity and powder reuse strategy directly impact FAA certification.
- **Medical (Spinal cages, hip implants)**: Ti-6Al-4V ELI (Extra-Low Interstitial) produced via PREP for fatigue-critical implants.
- **Automotive (Bugatti, Czinger)**: Metal DED with Inconel and stainless steels for topology-optimized brake and chassis nodes.
- **Foundry Pattern (Voxeljet, ExOne)**: Binder-jetted silica sand molds cast Ti and Inconel parts — bypassing machining of dies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density (VED) from First Principles

The KTU frequently asks to derive the VED expression or apply it in numerical problems. We derive it from energy conservation over a melt-pool volume.

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Define the energy input per unit time

The laser delivers power $P$ (in watts = J/s). The energy absorbed per second by the powder bed is:

$$E_{in} = P \quad [\text{J/s}]$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Define the effective interaction time at a point

The laser scans at velocity $v$ over a beam diameter $d$. A point on the powder bed experiences the beam for a duration:

$$\Delta t = \frac{d}{v} \quad [\text{s}]$$

### 3.1.3 Step 3 — Define the volume melted in one scan

In one hatch pass of width $h$ (hatch spacing) and depth $t$ (layer thickness), the swept volume per unit scan length is:

$$V_{per\_length} = h \cdot t \quad [\text{mm}^2]$$

So for a scan length $L$, the melted volume is $V = L \cdot h \cdot t$.

### 3.1.4 Step 4 — Energy per unit volume

The total energy delivered during scan of length $L$ at speed $v$ is:

$$E_{total} = P \cdot \frac{L}{v}$$

Dividing by the melted volume $V = L \cdot h \cdot t$:

$$E_v = \frac{E_{total}}{V} = \frac{P \cdot L / v}{L \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

### 3.1.5 Step 5 — Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Standard Style)

**Problem:** An LPBF machine processes **316L stainless steel** with $P = 200\,\text{W}$, $v = 800\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.10\,\text{mm}$, $t = 0.030\,\text{mm}$. Compute $E_v$.

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \frac{200}{800 \cdot 0.10 \cdot 0.030} \\
&= \frac{200}{2.4} \\
&= 83.33 \;\text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** This value falls within the acceptable **316L LPBF window of 80–150 J/mm³**, predicting **< 0.5 % porosity** if powder is spherical, dry, and PSD is 15–45 µm.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Mark Scheme Tip (5-mark derivation):**
> Step-2 marks → correct substitution with units. Step-2 marks → final boxed answer with stated window. Step-1 mark → conclusion/comment on feasibility.

## 3.2 Derivation: Hausner Ratio and Carr Index Relationship

Given two density measurements (apparent $\rho_{app}$, tap $\rho_{tap}$):

$$\begin{aligned}
H_R &= \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{app}} \\
CI &= \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

We can rewrite $CI$ in terms of $H_R$:

$$\begin{aligned}
CI &= \left(1 - \frac{\rho_{app}}{\rho_{tap}}\right) \times 100 \\
&= \left(1 - \frac{1}{H_R}\right) \times 100
\end{aligned}$$

So:

$$\boxed{CI = \left(1 - \frac{1}{H_R}\right) \times 100\,\%}$$

### 3.2.1 Worked Example

A powder has $\rho_{app} = 4.20\,\text{g/cm}^3$ and $\rho_{tap} = 5.10\,\text{g/cm}^3$.

$$H_R = \frac{5.10}{4.20} = 1.214$$

$$CI = \left(1 - \frac{1}{1.214}\right) \times 100 = (1 - 0.824) \times 100 = 17.6\,\%$$

**Verdict:** $CI > 15\%$ → powder has **moderate flowability**, suitable for **EBM (coarser tolerances)** but borderline for **fine LPBF**. Suggests **drying at 120 °C for 4 h** before use.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Material ↔ Process Compatibility

| Material Family | Powder / Filament / Resin | Best-Suited AM Process | Avoid / Failure Mode |
|-----------------|----------------------------|------------------------|----------------------|
| **Ti-6Al-4V** | PREP spherical powder 15–45 µm | LPBF, EBM | FDM (no filament form), MJT (clogging) |
| **Inconel 718** | Gas-atomized powder | LPBF, DED | VPP (not photoreactive), low-temp MJT |
| **316L SS** | Gas-atomized powder | LPBF, DED, BJT | VPP (opaque) |
| **AlSi10Mg** | Gas-atomized powder (Mg loss risk) | LPBF (low $E_v$) | High-power EBM (Mg vaporization) |
| **PA12 Nylon** | Powder 50–100 µm | SLS (PBF Polymer) | LPBF metal process, FDM (filament form is PA6/PA66) |
| **PEEK** | Filament or powder | FDM (high-temp), SLS | SLA/DLP (opaque), BJT (no binder affinity) |
| **Photopolymer Resin** | Liquid 25–100 µm layer | SLA, DLP, CDLP | Powder-bed (not solidifiable by laser melting) |
| **Ceramic (Al₂O₃/ZrO₂)** | Photopolymer-ceramic slurry | VPP (after debinding/sintering) | SLS (no binder), FDM (nozzle abrasion) |
| **Silica Sand** | 50–200 µm | Binder Jetting | LPBF (no fusion), DED (no carrier) |

## 3.4 Numerical Exercise: Layer Build Time

**Problem:** A part has 800 layers, scan speed $v_{build} = 600\,\text{mm/s}$, and average scan path per layer is 250 mm. Compute total build time (ignoring recoating).

**Solution:**

$$\begin{aligned}
t_{layer} &= \frac{L}{v_{build}} = \frac{250}{600} = 0.4167 \;\text{s/layer} \\
T_{total} &= 800 \cdot 0.4167 = 333.33 \;\text{s} \approx 5.56 \;\text{min}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In reality, recoating adds **~5–10 s per layer**, so total time ≈ 333 + (800 × 7) = **~5933 s ≈ 99 min ≈ 1.65 h**. KTU questions often simplify to "scan-only" time — read the wording carefully.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — AM Material Taxonomy Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AM MATERIALS] --> B[BY CHEMISTRY]
    A --> C[BY FORM]
    A --> D[BY PROCESS]
    A --> E[BY FUNCTION]

    B --> B1[Metals]
    B --> B2[Polymers]
    B --> B3[Ceramics]
    B --> B4[Composites]
    B --> B5[Hybrid FGMs]

    C --> C1[Powder]
    C --> C2[Filament]
    C --> C3[Wire]
    C --> C4[Liquid Resin]
    C --> C5[Pellets]
    C --> C6[Sheet or Film]

    D --> D1[PBF LPBF EBM]
    D --> D2[DED LMD WAAM EBF]
    D --> D3[VPP SLA DLP]
    D --> D4[MEX FDM]
    D --> D5[MJT Polyjet]
    D --> D6[BJT BJAM]
    D --> D7[SHL LOM UAM]

    E --> E1[Structural]
    E --> E2[Biocompatible]
    E --> E3[Conductive]
    E --> E4[Dielectric]
    E --> E5[Magnetic]
    E --> E6[Smart or Responsive]

    C1 --> F1[Gas Atomized Spherical]
    C1 --> F2[Plasma Atomized]
    C1 --> F3[PREP Centrifugal]
    C1 --> F4[Water Atomized Irregular]

    F1 --> G1[Best for LPBF DED]
    F4 --> G2[Best for BJT SHL]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block — Material Selection Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Material Selection Start] --> Q1{End Use}
    Q1 -->|Aerospace Structural| Q2{Operating Temp}
    Q1 -->|Biomedical Implant| Q3{Bio Compatibility}
    Q1 -->|Functional Prototype| Q4{Visual or Mechanical}

    Q2 -->|Less than 350 C| R1[AlSi10Mg or PA12]
    Q2 -->|350 to 600 C| R2[Ti6Al4V LPBF EBM]
    Q2 -->|Above 600 C| R3[Inconel 718 or 625 DED]

    Q3 -->|Yes| R4[Ti6Al4V ELI or PEEK]
    Q4 -->|Visual| R5[Resin SLA DLP]
    Q4 -->|Mechanical Load| R6[Nylon CF or PA12 SLS]

    R1 --> OUT[Material Selected]
    R2 --> OUT
    R3 --> OUT
    R4 --> OUT
    R5 --> OUT
    R6 --> OUT
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block — Powder Property → Process Outcome Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[POWDER INPUT] --> B[PSD 15 to 45 um]
    A --> C[Sphericity greater than 0.85]
    A --> D[Hausner less than 1.25]
    A --> E[Oxygen less than 200 ppm]

    B --> F[Uniform Layer Spreading]
    C --> G[Consistent Melt Pool]
    D --> H[Stable Recoater Flow]
    E --> I[Clean Fusion No Oxides]

    F --> J[HIGH DENSITY PART]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J

    J --> K[Less than 0.5 percent Porosity]
    J --> L[Greater than 99 percent Density]
    J --> M[Aerospace Certification Feasible]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - Diagram 4.1 shows the **four-axis classification** that students must memorize.
> - Diagram 4.2 is a **decision-tree** you can apply directly in KTU case-study questions ("Select a material and process for …").
> - Diagram 4.3 illustrates the **cause-effect chain** linking powder metrics to final part performance — a common 14-mark question structure.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
Define **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)**. State its SI unit and explain what happens to a part when VED is **too low** versus **too high** for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]$$

- **Too Low ($E_v < 60\,\text{J/mm}^3$)**: Incomplete melting → **Lack-of-Fusion (LoF)** porosity, weak inter-layer bonding, low density.
- **Too High ($E_v > 120\,\text{J/mm}^3$)**: Excessive vaporization → **Keyhole porosity**, balling, surface roughness, residual stress spikes.

> Distribution: 1 mark formula + 1 mark LoF + 1 mark Keyhole.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
Distinguish between **gas atomization** and **plasma atomization** of metal powders. Which is preferred for Ti-6Al-4V in aerospace LPBF, and why?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Gas Atomization | Plasma Atomization |
|---------|------------------|---------------------|
| Mechanism | High-pressure inert gas jets | Plasma jets melt wire feedstock |
| Particle Shape | Spherical (good) | Highly spherical (excellent) |
| Cost | Moderate | High |
| Typical Use | 316L, AlSi10Mg, Inconel | Ti-6Al-4V (reactive alloys) |

**Preferred for Ti-6Al-4V:** Plasma atomization (or PREP) — because Ti is highly reactive with oxygen, and these processes yield **low-satellite, low-oxide, highly spherical powders** critical for aerospace fatigue life.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Classify the major categories of materials used in Additive Manufacturing. Discuss in detail the **polymer feedstocks** with reference to filament, powder, and liquid resin forms, citing one commercial material example and one suitable AM process for each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 316L stainless steel part is being built by LPBF with the following parameters: Laser power = 250 W, scan speed = 1000 mm/s, hatch spacing = 0.12 mm, layer thickness = 0.04 mm. Compute the Volumetric Energy Density and comment on whether the part is expected to be free from common defects. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(a) [7 marks]

AM materials are classified into **four major categories**:

**1. Metals** — Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, 316L SS, AlSi10Mg, CoCrMo, Maraging steel.
**2. Polymers** — Thermoplastics (PLA, ABS, PA12, PEEK), thermosets (epoxy-acrylates), elastomers (TPU).
**3. Ceramics** — Alumina (Al₂O₃), Zirconia (ZrO₂), SiC, Bioglass.
**4. Composites** — Fiber-reinforced (CF-PLA, CF-PEEK), particle-reinforced (WC-Co cermets), FGMs.

**Polymer Feedstocks — Detailed Discussion:**

| Form | Example Material | Suitable AM Process | Key Property |
|------|-------------------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Filament** | PLA (1.75 mm Ø) | FDM (Material Extrusion) | Low warp, biodegradable |
| **Powder** | PA12 Nylon (50 µm) | SLS (Powder Bed Fusion) | High strength, chemical resistance |
| **Liquid Resin** | Acrylate-based photopolymer | SLA / DLP (Vat Photopolymerization) | High resolution, smooth surface |

**Key Takeaway:** The *form-factor* of the polymer dictates the *process*, and the *process* dictates the achievable *resolution, mechanical strength, and anisotropy*.

> Distribution: 2 marks classification + 3 marks polymer discussion + 2 marks example-process mapping.

---

#### Model Solution — Part A(b) [7 marks]

Given: $P = 250\,\text{W}$, $v = 1000\,\text{mm/s}$, $h = 0.12\,\text{mm}$, $t = 0.04\,\text{mm}$.

$$\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \frac{250}{1000 \cdot 0.12 \cdot 0.04} \\
&= \frac{250}{4.8} \\
&= 52.08 \;\text{J/mm}^3
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** The computed VED of **52.08 J/mm³** lies **below the recommended LPBF window for 316L (80–150 J/mm³)**.

**Predicted Outcome:** The part is expected to suffer from **Lack-of-Fusion porosity** due to insufficient energy to fully melt the powder layer. Pores will form between adjacent tracks and layers, leading to:

- Reduced density (< 95% theoretical)
- Lower fatigue life
- Possible delamination

**Recommended Remediation:** Increase $P$ to ~380 W, or decrease $v$ to ~600 mm/s, to bring $E_v$ into the safe window (~80–100 J/mm³).

> Distribution: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 1 mark computation, 2 marks interpretation, 2 marks remediation.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain the **Critical Powder Characteristics** that govern part quality in Powder Bed Fusion. Define the **Hausner Ratio** and **Carr Index**, and describe their role in assessing powder flowability. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is recorded for an aluminum alloy powder intended for LPBF:
Apparent density = 1.45 g/cm³, Tap density = 1.72 g/cm³.
Compute the **Hausner Ratio** and **Carr Index**. Comment on the suitability of the powder for LPBF and suggest appropriate pre-process steps if any. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(a) [7 marks]

The **Critical Powder Characteristics (PPCs)** for PBF are defined under ASTM F3049 and include:

1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD)** — must be **15–45 µm** for LPBF, **45–105 µm** for EBM. A wide PSD causes poor packing; a narrow PSD ensures uniform layer spreading.
2. **Particle Shape** — Sphericity ≥ 0.85 is preferred. Spherical particles flow predictably and pack to **60–65% theoretical density**.
3. **Flow Rate (Hall)** — Time for 50 g to flow through a 2.5 mm orifice. **Lower time = better flow**.
4. **Apparent Density & Tap Density** — measure of packing efficiency.
5. **Specific Surface Area (SSA)** — high SSA → high oxidation risk, especially for Ti and Al.
6. **Chemical Purity** — Oxygen, nitrogen, moisture must be controlled (e.g., < 200 ppm O for Ti).

**Hausner Ratio:**
$$H_R = \frac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{app}}$$

- $H_R < 1.25$ → good flowability.
- $H_R > 1.4$ → poor flowability, expect recoater streaking and porosity.

**Carr Index (Compressibility %):**
$$CI = \frac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100$$

- $CI < 15\%$ → good flow.
- $CI > 25\%$ → poor flow, unsuitable for fine LPBF without drying/sieving.

> Distribution: 3 marks PPC list + 2 marks definitions + 2 marks thresholds.

---

#### Model Solution — Part B(b) [7 marks]

Given: $\rho_{app} = 1.45\,\text{g/cm}^3$, $\rho_{tap} = 1.72\,\text{g/cm}^3$.

**Hausner Ratio:**
$$H_R = \frac{1.72}{1.45} = 1.186$$

**Carr Index:**
$$CI = \frac{1.72 - 1.45}{1.72} \times 100 = \frac{0.27}{1.72} \times 100 = 15.70\,\%$$

**Interpretation:**

- $H_R = 1.186$ is **less than 1.25** → indicates **good flowability**.
- $CI = 15.70\%$ is **just above the 15% threshold** → indicates **borderline flowability**.

**Suitability for LPBF:** The powder is **acceptable** but marginal. The flow index is close to the upper limit, suggesting risk of recoater streaking and porosity if PSD is broad or moisture content is high.

**Pre-Process Steps Recommended:**
1. **Drying** at 120 °C for 4–6 hours (Al alloy powder readily absorbs moisture → causes hydrogen porosity).
2. **Sieving** through a 63 µm mesh to remove fines and agglomerates.
3. **PSD verification** via laser diffraction to ensure **D₁₀ ≥ 15 µm** and **D₉₀ ≤ 53 µm**.
4. **Storage** in sealed argon-purged hoppers to limit oxidation.

> Distribution: 1 mark HR computation, 1 mark CI computation, 2 marks interpretation, 3 marks pre-process steps.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Module 3 (Materials):**
> 1. **Unit Mismatch (–2 marks):** Students frequently write VED in W/mm³ instead of J/mm³. **Power is in W = J/s**, so division by velocity (mm/s) and geometry (mm × mm) yields **J/mm³**.
> 2. **Confusing Hausner vs Carr (–1 mark):** Hausner is a **dimensionless ratio**; Carr Index is a **percentage**. Do not interchange units.
> 3. **Ignoring Powder Reuse (–1 mark):** In 14-mark questions, examiners expect a comment on **powder degradation** after multiple build cycles (PSD broadening, oxygen pickup). Add this for full marks.
> 4. **Wrong Form-Process Pairing (–2 marks):** Writing "FDM uses powder" or "SLA uses filament" is a fatal error.
> 5. **Forgetting the 'Hall Flow Rate' threshold (–1 mark):** For 316L, Hall Flow ≤ 25 s/50g is required; for Ti-6Al-4V, ≤ 30 s/50g.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU 2024 Revision Bullets — Module 3: Materials**

- ⭐ **VED formula** $E_v = P/(vht)$ is the **single most-tested equation** in this module. Memorize units: **J/mm³**.
- ⭐ **Acceptable VED windows** (must know): Ti-6Al-4V (60–120), 316L (80–150), AlSi10Mg (30–70), Inconel 718 (100–200) J/mm³.
- ⭐ **PSD ranges**: LPBF = **15–45 µm**, EBM = **45–105 µm**, SLS = **50–100 µm**.
- ⭐ **Hausner Ratio < 1.25** = good flowability. **Carr Index < 15%** = good flowability.
- ⭐ **Gas atomization → spherical** powder (LPBF); **Water atomization → irregular** powder (BJT).
- ⭐ **Powder form-process map**: PBF/DED use powder; FDM uses filament; SLA/DLP use liquid resin; BJT uses powder + binder; SHL uses sheet.
- ⭐ **Polymers tested by KTU**: PLA, ABS (FDM); PA12 (SLS); Photopolymer resins (SLA/DLP); PEEK (high-temp FDM).
- ⭐ **Ceramic AM route**: Photopolymer-loaded slurry in SLA → debinding → sintering (multi-stage).
- ⭐ **Composites**: Short-fiber (CF-PLA) via FDM; continuous fiber (Markforged); FGMs via DED.
- ⭐ **Always mention** in 14-mark answers: powder reuse degradation, oxygen control (< 200 ppm for Ti/Al), and post-process heat treatment (stress relief at 600–700 °C for Ti).
- ⭐ **Common defects due to wrong VED**: LoF (too low), Keyhole + balling (too high), cracking (residual stress — Inconel 718).
- ⭐ **Drying Al powders** is critical (hydrogen porosity risk). Drying Ti powders prevents oxide layer formation.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Powder Fusion Mechanism and Powder Handling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Powder Fusion Mechanism and Powder Handling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Powder Fusion Mechanism** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the physical, thermal, and metallurgical processes by which discrete powder particles are consolidated into a solid, contiguous mass through the controlled application of an energy source (laser, electron beam, or thermal jet). The mechanism dictates the final part's density, microstructure, residual stress, and mechanical performance.

In the **2024 KTU Scheme**, this topic is studied primarily under the umbrella of **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** and **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** processes, where the binding of powder layers is governed by one or a combination of the following mechanisms:

1. **Solid-State Sintering** — diffusion bonding below the melting point.
2. **Liquid Phase Sintering (Partial Melting)** — a liquid binder phase forms between solid particles.
3. **Full Melting / Fusion Welding** — complete melting and re-solidification of the powder.
4. **Chemical Reaction / Curing** — binder jetting or reaction-based consolidation.

**Powder Handling** encompasses all auxiliary operations: powder storage, sieving, drying, recycling, contamination control, and the safety protocols required when handling reactive or fine metallic powders.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Snowman on a Sunny Day"**
>
> Imagine packing a bucket tightly with tiny ice granules (snow) and then holding a magnifying glass under sunlight over the surface. The sun's rays melt the top layer just enough that, as water seeps between granules and refreezes, the loose snow becomes a solid block of ice. **Powder fusion is identical** — an energy beam selectively melts metal powder grains; they coalesce via surface tension, and on cooling, they weld into a fully dense metallic body.
>
> Now imagine doing this on Day 1, then storing the leftover snow in a freezer, adding *fresh snow on Day 2*, and repeating. The "leftover" powder is your **recycled powder**, and the *freezer* is your **powder handling system**. If moisture, oxides, or foreign dust enter, the "snowman" becomes brittle — this is why **powder handling is as critical as fusion itself**.

---

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

| Constant / Metric | Symbol | Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | $\mathbf{5.67 \times 10^{-8} \; W \cdot m^{-2} \cdot K^{-4}}$ |
| Density of Ti-6Al-4V powder (typical) | $\rho_{Ti}$ | $\mathbf{4.43 \; g/cm^3}$ |
| Density of SS 316L powder (typical) | $\rho_{SS}$ | $\mathbf{7.99 \; g/cm^3}$ |
| Melting point of Ti-6Al-4V | $T_m$ | $\mathbf{1668 \; °C}$ |
| Melting point of SS 316L | $T_m$ | $\mathbf{1400 \; °C}$ |
| Volumetric Energy Density | EVD | $\mathbf{J/mm^3}$ |
| Typical powder size range (LPBF) | d | $\mathbf{15 \text{ to } 63 \; \mu m}$ |
| Layer thickness range | t | $\mathbf{20 \text{ to } 100 \; \mu m}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Fundamental Powder Fusion Mechanisms

The consolidation of powder particles during AM is governed by **four primary mechanisms**, which may occur individually or in combination depending on the energy input and material.

### Mechanism A — Solid-State Sintering

- Occurs when the energy input is **insufficient** to melt the powder but high enough to enable atomic diffusion.
- Bonding happens via **diffusion of atoms** across particle boundaries (grain boundary diffusion, surface diffusion, volume diffusion).
- Used in **indirect metal AM** and **Ultrasonic Additive Manufacturing (UAM)**.
- Produces parts with **residual porosity** (typically 5–20%).

### Mechanism B — Liquid Phase Sintering (Partial Melting)

- A **binder phase melts** while a structural phase remains solid.
- Common in **two-component powder systems** (e.g., WC-Co cemented carbides).
- The molten binder (Co) wets the WC particles and fills the interstitial voids.

### Mechanism C — Full Melting (Wetting & Capillary Action)

- **Most common mechanism** in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF) and Electron Beam Melting (EBM).
- Powder is fully melted; **surface tension** drives the molten pool into a near-spherical shape via capillary action.
- Marangoni convection (surface tension-driven flow) homogenizes the melt pool.
- On cooling, **epitaxial solidification** occurs, often with grains growing along the build direction.

### Mechanism D — Chemical Reaction / Binder Curing

- Used in **Binder Jetting** and some **reactive sintering** processes.
- A liquid binder is jetted onto the powder bed, which cures via polymerization or solvent evaporation.
- Final densification requires a separate **furnace sintering step**.

---

## 2.2 The Role of Marangoni Convection

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marangoni flow** (also called thermocapillary flow) is the dominant fluid-flow mechanism inside a melt pool. It is driven by surface tension gradients caused by temperature gradients.
>
> - If $\frac{d\gamma}{dT} < 0$ (surface tension decreases with temperature, e.g., pure Ti), flow is **outward** → wide, shallow melt pool.
> - If $\frac{d\gamma}{dT} > 0$ (e.g., with high sulfur content in steels), flow is **inward** → deep, narrow melt pool.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes **every equation** a KTU 2024 student is expected to know for Module 3.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $P$ = Laser Power (W), $v$ = Scan Speed (mm/s), $h$ = Hatch Spacing (mm), $t$ = Layer Thickness (mm) | Primary control parameter for melt pool formation |
| 2 | **Line Energy Density (LED)** | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | $P$ (W), $v$ (mm/s) | Energy per unit scan length |
| 3 | **Areal Energy Density (AED)** | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h}$ | $P$ (W), $v$ (mm/s), $h$ (mm) | Energy per unit area |
| 4 | **Apparent Density of Powder** | $\rho_{app} = \dfrac{m}{V_{bulk}}$ | $m$ = mass, $V_{bulk}$ = bulk volume including voids | Packing efficiency indicator |
| 5 | **Tap Density Ratio (Carr Index)** | $C = \dfrac{\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app}}{\rho_{tap}} \times 100\%$ | $\rho_{tap}$ = tapped density | Flowability measure; lower C = better flow |
| 6 | **Hausner Ratio** | $H_R = \dfrac{\rho_{tap}}{\rho_{app}}$ | ratio (dimensionless) | $H_R < 1.25$ → good flowability |
| 7 | **Beer-Lambert Absorption (Power absorbed)** | $P_{abs} = P_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha L})$ | $\alpha$ = absorption coefficient, $L$ = penetration depth | Laser-matter interaction depth |
| 8 | **Melt Pool Depth Estimate (Conduction mode)** | $d \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \cdot k \cdot \Delta T \cdot \tau}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot \pi}}$ | $k$ = thermal conductivity, $\Delta T$ = superheat, $\tau$ = interaction time, $\rho$ = density, $C_p$ = specific heat | Conduction-mode keyhole threshold |
| 9 | **Solidification Growth Rate (R)** | $R = v \cdot \cos\theta$ | $v$ = scan speed, $\theta$ = angle between scan and growth direction | Microstructure prediction |
| 10 | **Cooling Rate Estimate** | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2 \pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | $T_m$ = melting point, $T_0$ = substrate temp, $P$ = power | Microstructure refinement |
| 11 | **Surface Area to Volume Ratio (spherical powder)** | $\dfrac{A}{V} = \dfrac{6}{d}$ | $d$ = particle diameter | Oxidation, absorption, sintering tendency |
| 12 | **Number of particles per unit volume** | $N_v = \dfrac{6 \cdot f}{\pi d^3}$ | $f$ = packing fraction, $d$ = diameter | Powder bed density analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Pipeline Reminder (Engineering Reality):** The 3 energy density formulas (VED, LED, AED) are *interchangeable* only when correctly matched to process parameters. For LPBF of **Ti-6Al-4V**, the recommended VED range is **$60 \text{ to } 120 \; J/mm^3$**. Below this → lack of fusion porosity. Above this → keyholing porosity and vaporization.

---

## 2.4 Why and How — The Operational Logic

**Why does energy density govern fusion?**

Because the melt pool size, peak temperature, and cooling rate all depend on the *rate at which energy is delivered per unit volume of powder*. Too little energy → particles do not coalesce. Too much → the metal vaporizes and creates a *keyhole* void.

**How does a student use this in an exam?**

1. Given $P$, $v$, $h$, $t$ → compute $E_v$.
2. Compare with the material-specific recommended window.
3. Predict defects:
   - $E_v$ too low → lack of fusion (LoF)
   - $E_v$ too high → keyholing, balling
   - $E_v$ in optimal window → >99.5% density

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

The **volumetric energy density (VED)** is the single most important KTU Module 3 formula. It is derived below in full.

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Delivered by the Beam per Unit Time

The laser emits energy at rate $P$ (in Watts = J/s). The total energy delivered during a scan time $t_s$ is:

$$E_{total} = P \cdot t_s$$

### Step 2 — Express the Scan Time in Terms of Process Parameters

If the laser scans a length $L$ at speed $v$:

$$t_s = \dfrac{L}{v}$$

Therefore:

$$E_{total} = P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}$$

### Step 3 — Determine the Volume of Material Melted per Scan Pass

The melted volume per scan pass is approximated as a rectangular parallelepiped:

$$V_{melt} = L \cdot h \cdot t$$

where:
- $L$ = scan length
- $h$ = hatch spacing (width of melt track)
- $t$ = layer thickness (depth of penetration per layer)

### Step 4 — Form the Energy per Unit Volume

$$E_v = \dfrac{E_{total}}{V_{melt}} = \dfrac{P \cdot L / v}{L \cdot h \cdot t}$$

### Step 5 — Cancel the Scan Length

$$\boxed{E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Because $L$ cancels, the VED is **independent of the size of the scanned area**. It is a *local* property of the melt pool, making it ideal for process optimization.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Style Problem

> **Problem:** A laser PBF machine uses the following parameters on Ti-6Al-4V powder:
> - Laser power $P = 200 \; W$
> - Scan speed $v = 800 \; mm/s$
> - Hatch spacing $h = 0.1 \; mm$
> - Layer thickness $t = 0.03 \; mm$
>
> Calculate (a) Line Energy Density, (b) Areal Energy Density, (c) Volumetric Energy Density, and (d) comment on whether this parameter set is likely to produce a dense part.

### Solution — Step-by-Step

**(a) Line Energy Density**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_l &= \dfrac{P}{v} \\
&= \dfrac{200 \; W}{800 \; mm/s} \\
&= 0.25 \; J/mm
\end{aligned}$$

**(b) Areal Energy Density**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_a &= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{800 \times 0.1} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{80} \\
&= 2.5 \; J/mm^2
\end{aligned}$$

**(c) Volumetric Energy Density**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03} \\
&= \dfrac{200}{2.4} \\
&= 83.33 \; J/mm^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(d) Comment on Densification**

The recommended VED range for Ti-6Al-4V in LPBF is **$60 \text{ to } 120 \; J/mm^3$**. The computed value of **$83.33 \; J/mm^3$** falls comfortably within this window. Therefore, the process is expected to yield **relative density > 99.5%**, with low risk of either lack-of-fusion or keyholing porosity.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameters vs. Defects

| Defect Type | Cause (Mechanism) | Visual Signature | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Lack of Fusion (LoF)** | $E_v < 60 \; J/mm^3$ (energy too low) | Irregular voids between layers | Increase $P$, decrease $v$, $h$, or $t$ |
| **Keyholing** | $E_v > 120 \; J/mm^3$ (energy too high) | Spherical voids with vapor cavities | Decrease $P$, increase $v$, $h$, or $t$ |
| **Balling Effect** | Inadequate wetting, high surface tension | Discontinuous beads | Preheat substrate, optimize scan strategy |
| **Delamination** | Residual stress > interlayer adhesion | Cracks parallel to build plane | Preheat, optimize scan strategy (e.g., 67° rotation) |
| **Gas Porosity** | Dissolved gases in powder (esp. Ti) | Spherical, often internal pores | Hot isostatic pressing (HIP), vacuum atomized powder |
| **Inclusion** | Contaminated powder | Foreign particles in matrix | Strict powder handling & sieving |
| **Surface Roughness** | Stair-stepping + adhered powder | Poor surface finish | Smaller layer thickness, post-processing |

---

## 3.4 Derivation 2 — Marangoni Flow Direction

The surface tension $\gamma$ of a liquid metal varies with temperature:

$$\gamma(T) = \gamma_m + \dfrac{d\gamma}{dT} (T - T_m)$$

where $\gamma_m$ is the surface tension at the melting point $T_m$. The Marangoni stress along the free surface of the melt pool is:

$$\tau_{Ma} = \dfrac{d\gamma}{dT} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dx}$$

**Case 1:** $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dT} < 0$ → $\tau_{Ma}$ is positive in the direction of decreasing $T$ → flow from hot center to cold edges → **wide shallow pool**.

**Case 2:** $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dT} > 0$ → flow from cold edges to hot center → **deep narrow pool (keyhole-prone)**.

> **Practical Implication:** Sulfur content in steels can flip the sign of $\dfrac{d\gamma}{dT}$, dramatically altering melt pool geometry. This is why **powder chemistry (not just size)** is critical in powder handling.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 3 — Beer-Lambert Laser Absorption in Powder Bed

A laser penetrating a powder bed experiences exponential absorption:

$$\dfrac{dI}{dx} = -\alpha I$$

Integrating over penetration depth $L$:

$$I(x) = I_0 e^{-\alpha x}$$

The **absorbed power** is:

$$P_{abs} = I_0 A (1 - e^{-\alpha L}) = P_0 (1 - e^{-\alpha L})$$

where $A$ is the cross-sectional area. This explains why a **denser powder bed** (higher packing fraction) absorbs more energy per unit depth — because more reflective interfaces exist, increasing the effective $\alpha$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram A — Powder Bed Fusion Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Powder Feed Chamber] --> B[Recoater Blade Movement]
    B --> C[Powder Bed Build Platform]
    C --> D[Energy Source Activation Laser or Electron Beam]
    D --> E[Melt Pool Formation and Fusion]
    E --> F{Solidification Complete}
    F --> G[Build Platform Lowering by Layer Thickness t]
    G --> H{More Layers Required}
    H -->|Yes| B
    H -->|No| I[Part Cooling and Powder Removal]
    I --> J[Post Processing: HIP, Machining, Surface Finish]
```

> **Interpretation:** This is the **closed-loop powder flow** in a typical LPBF or EBM machine. The same flow governs the *entire* Module 3 fusion and handling discussion.

---

## 4.2 Diagram B — Powder Handling & Recycling Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Virgin Powder from Supplier] --> P2[Powder Sieving Below 63 microns]
    P2 --> P3[Powder Drying in Vacuum Oven]
    P3 --> P4[Inert Gas Sealed Feed Chamber]
    P4 --> P5[LPBF Process Melt and Fuse]
    P5 --> P6[Unused Powder Collection from Build Chamber]
    P6 --> P7{Quality Check Sieve, PSD, Oxygen Content}
    P7 -->|Pass| P8[Re-mix with Virgin Powder 50:50 Ratio]
    P7 -->|Fail| P9[Disposal as Hazardous Waste]
    P8 --> P4
```

> **Interpretation:** Notice the **closed-loop recycling**. Each cycle, the powder may pick up oxygen, become more spherical, or shift in particle size distribution. KTU examiners frequently test whether students know the **maximum number of reuse cycles** (typically 10–20 for Ti, fewer for Al).

---

## 4.3 Diagram C — Melt Pool Geometry as a Function of Energy Mode

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Low Energy Density E_v less than 60 J per mm cubed] --> S2[Conduction Mode Melt Pool]
    S1 --> S3[Wide and Shallow Geometry]
    S1 --> S4[Risk Lack of Fusion Porosity]
    
    S5[Optimal Energy Density E_v between 60 and 120 J per mm cubed] --> S6[Stable Keyhole Transition]
    S5 --> S7[Balanced Depth and Width]
    S5 --> S8[Density greater than 99.5 percent]
```
