```mermaid
    S9[Excessive Energy Density E_v greater than 120 J per mm cubed] --> S10[Deep Keyhole Mode]
    S9 --> S11[Deep and Narrow Geometry]
    S9 --> S12[Risk Vaporization and Keyhole Porosity]
    
    S2 -.compares with.-> S6
    S6 -.compares with.-> S10
```

> **Interpretation:** The diagram visually maps the **energy-density → melt-pool-mode → defect** chain, which is a recurring high-yield KTU question.

---

## 4.4 Diagram D — Powder Characteristics → Part Quality Causal Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F1[Particle Size Distribution] --> F2[Packing Density]
    F3[Particle Shape Sphericity] --> F4[Flowability Hall Flow Test]
    F5[Chemistry Oxygen Nitrogen] --> F6[Mechanical Properties]
    F7[Moisture Content] --> F8[Porosity and Defects]
    
    F2 --> F9[Final Part Density]
    F4 --> F9
    F6 --> F9
    F8 --> F9
```

> **Interpretation:** A KTU favourite — students must connect **four powder-property branches** to the *single outcome* of part density.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

> **Define Volumetric Energy Density (VED) in the context of Laser Powder Bed Fusion. State its recommended range for Ti-6Al-4V.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is defined as the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder during LPBF and is given by:

$$E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

where $P$ is laser power (W), $v$ is scan speed (mm/s), $h$ is hatch spacing (mm), and $t$ is layer thickness (mm). The recommended range for Ti-6Al-4V is **$60 \text{ to } 120 \; J/mm^3$**, which yields relative density > 99.5%. **(3 marks — 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for range with reasoning.)**

---

### Question A2 — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

> **Differentiate between the Hausner Ratio and the Carr Index. Mention the acceptable values for good powder flowability.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Property | Hausner Ratio ($H_R$) | Carr Index ($C$) |
|---|---|---|
| Formula | $H_R = \rho_{tap}/\rho_{app}$ | $C = (\rho_{tap} - \rho_{app})/\rho_{app} \times 100\%$ |
| Acceptable value (good flow) | $H_R < 1.25$ | $C < 15\%$ |
| Type of indicator | Ratio (dimensionless) | Percentage |

Both metrics quantify **powder flowability** based on the difference between apparent and tapped densities. Lower values indicate **better flow**. **(3 marks — 1 for each formula, 1 for acceptable values.)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Question (Module 3 Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Per KTU 2024 Scheme, every Part B question in the End Semester Examination carries **14 marks** with an internal choice. Students must answer EITHER Question A **OR** Question B.

---

### **Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]`**

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **mechanism of powder fusion** in Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF). Discuss the role of Marangoni convection in melt-pool dynamics. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V part is to be built on an LPBF machine with the following parameters:
> - Laser power $P = 250 \; W$
> - Scan speed $v = 1000 \; mm/s$
> - Hatch spacing $h = 0.12 \; mm$
> - Layer thickness $t = 0.03 \; mm$
>
> Calculate the **(i)** Line Energy Density, **(ii)** Volumetric Energy Density, and **(iii)** determine if the parameter set is suitable for dense part production. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Mechanism of powder fusion in LPBF (5 marks):**

1. **Powder deposition:** A recoater blade spreads a thin layer (typically 20–100 μm) of metal powder across the build platform.
2. **Energy absorption:** The laser beam hits the powder bed. A portion of the energy is absorbed (governed by Beer-Lambert law) and converted to heat.
3. **Melt pool formation:** Powder particles within the beam spot fully melt. Surface tension drives the molten metal into a near-circular cross-section via capillary action.
4. **Solidification:** As the beam moves away, rapid cooling (~$10^4$–$10^6$ K/s) causes **epitaxial grain growth**, with grains tending to align with the build direction.
5. **Layer bonding:** Each new layer partially remelts the previous layer, ensuring metallurgical bonding.

**Role of Marangoni convection (2 marks):**

Marangoni convection arises from surface tension gradients ($\partial\gamma/\partial T$) within the melt pool. It governs:
- **Melt pool shape** (wide-shallow vs deep-narrow).
- **Mass transport** of alloying elements.
- **Denuded zones** and **spatter ejection**.

For Ti-6Al-4V (with $\partial\gamma/\partial T < 0$ for low S content), flow is outward, producing a wider, shallower pool.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Line Energy Density (2 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_l &= \dfrac{P}{v} \\
&= \dfrac{250 \; W}{1000 \; mm/s} \\
&= 0.25 \; J/mm
\end{aligned}$$

**(ii) Volumetric Energy Density (3 marks):**

$$\begin{aligned}
E_v &= \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.03} \\
&= \dfrac{250}{3.6} \\
&= 69.44 \; J/mm^3
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Suitability Assessment (2 marks):**

Since $69.44 \; J/mm^3$ lies within the recommended Ti-6Al-4V window of $60 \text{ to } 120 \; J/mm^3$, the parameter set is **suitable** for producing dense parts. However, it is close to the lower bound, so any further reduction in $P$ or increase in $v$ may induce lack-of-fusion porosity. Recommended monitoring of relative density using Archimedes' principle.

---

### **Question B (Internal Choice Alternative) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**

> **(a)** Discuss the **various powder characteristics** that govern the quality of parts built by Powder Bed Fusion. Explain the significance of the **Hall Flow Test** and **Particle Size Distribution**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **powder handling and recycling protocol** in an LPBF facility. Why is **oxygen control** critical, especially for reactive metals like titanium and aluminum? **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Powder characteristics (4 marks):**

1. **Particle Size Distribution (PSD):** Optimal LPBF range is **15–63 μm**. Too coarse → poor surface finish and incomplete melting. Too fine → poor flow, high oxidation risk.
2. **Particle Shape:** **High sphericity (>0.9)** is desired. Irregular shapes cause poor flow and uneven melting.
3. **Apparent and Tap Densities:** Determines **packing fraction** (typically 50–65% for LPBF).
4. **Flowability:** Measured by **Hall Flow Test** (ASTM B213) — time taken for 50 g of powder to flow through a calibrated funnel. Lower time = better flow.
5. **Chemistry:** Oxygen, nitrogen, and hydrogen content must be tightly controlled (Ti: O < 0.15 wt%; Al: O < 0.05 wt%).
6. **Moisture content:** Must be <0.02% to prevent porosity.

**Hall Flow Test (2 marks):**
A standardized funnel (aperture 2.54 mm) is used. 50 g of powder is loaded; the time to fully discharge is recorded in seconds/50 g. Used to verify flowability.

**PSD significance (1 mark):**
A bimodal or wide distribution improves packing fraction by allowing smaller particles to fill interstices between larger ones.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Powder handling and recycling protocol (4 marks):**

1. **Reception & Sieving:** Virgin powder sieved to remove satellite particles and agglomerates.
2. **Drying:** Vacuum drying at 80–120 °C for 4–6 h to remove moisture.
3. **Storage:** Inert-gas (Ar/N₂) sealed containers to prevent oxidation.
4. **Loading:** Pneumatic or gravity transfer into machine hopper under argon.
5. **Collection:** Unused powder collected post-build via vacuum or brushing.
6. **Re-sieving & Re-mixing:** Re-sieved and blended with 30–50% virgin powder.
7. **Cycle limit:** Typical reuse cycles — Ti (15–20), SS316L (30+), AlSi10Mg (5–10).

**Why oxygen control is critical (3 marks):**

- Ti and Al are **highly reactive** with oxygen, especially in molten state.
- **Oxygen pickup** causes:
  - **Embrittlement** (interstitial O atoms pin dislocations).
  - **Inclusion formation** (TiO₂, Al₂O₃).
  - **Reduced fatigue life.**
- Standards: ASTM F2924 for Ti-6Al-4V (O < 0.20 wt%).
- Use of **vacuum or high-purity argon atmosphere** (<50 ppm O₂) is mandatory.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks in this Module:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units in $E_v$:** A bare numerical answer like "83.33" without **$J/mm^3$** loses **0.5–1 mark**. Always write the unit.
> 2. **Confusing LED, AED, VED:** Examiners will *trap* students by giving parameters in mixed units. **Always convert mm/s → mm/s, μm → mm** before substitution.
> 3. **Writing "powder fusion = melting"** — this is incomplete. Fusion involves **melting + wetting + Marangoni flow + solidification**. A single-word answer loses up to **2 marks**.
> 4. **Skipping the recycling discussion** in powder-handling questions — examiners often allocate **2–3 marks** specifically for "how many reuse cycles" and "why oxygen is controlled."
> 5. **Misapplying VED to wrong materials:** The optimal VED for AlSi10Mg is **30–60 J/mm³**, not 60–120. Always quote the material-specific range.
> 6. **Using VED as a universal parameter:** VED has limited predictive power because it does not account for **absorption coefficient**, **scan strategy**, or **preheat temperature**. Mention this nuance for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3**

- ✅ **Volumetric Energy Density formula:** $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ — **the single most important equation** in this module.
- ✅ **Recommended VED windows (memorize):**
  - Ti-6Al-4V → $60$–$120 \; J/mm^3$
  - SS 316L → $80$–$120 \; J/mm^3$
  - AlSi10Mg → $30$–$60 \; J/mm^3$
  - Inconel 718 → $60$–$100 \; J/mm^3$
- ✅ **Four fusion mechanisms:** Solid-state sintering, Liquid phase sintering, Full melting, Chemical/binder curing.
- ✅ **Two dominant defects from VED mismatch:**
  - Low VED → **Lack of Fusion (LoF)**
  - High VED → **Keyholing / Vaporization**
- ✅ **Marangoni direction** is set by $\partial\gamma/\partial T$ sign — sulfur flips it.
- ✅ **Hall Flow Test** = flowability metric (seconds per 50 g).
- ✅ **Carr Index < 15%** and **Hausner Ratio < 1.25** = good flow.
- ✅ **PSD for LPBF:** $15 \text{ to } 63 \; \mu m$ (gas-atomized, spherical).
- ✅ **Powder reuse cycles:** Ti (~15–20), AlSi10Mg (~5–10), SS316L (~30+).
- ✅ **Critical atmosphere requirement:** O₂ < 50 ppm for Ti and Al.
- ✅ **Beer-Lambert law** governs laser penetration into powder bed.
- ✅ **Recoater blade type** (silicone rubber vs. carbon fibre brush) affects powder spreading uniformity.
- ✅ **Three safety concerns** when handling reactive powders:
  1. Pyrophoricity (Ti, Al fines)
  2. Dust explosion risk (particle size < 53 μm)
  3. Inhalation hazard (especially beryllium-bearing alloys)
- ✅ **Cooling rate in LPBF:** $10^4$–$10^6 \; K/s$ — leads to fine microstructures and high residual stress.

> **End of Module 3 Notes — Powder Fusion Mechanism and Powder Handling**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: SLS Metal and Ceramic Part Creation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** SLS Metal and Ceramic Part Creation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

**Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** is an additive manufacturing process that uses a high-power laser to selectively fuse (sinter) particles of powdered material — typically polymers, metals, or ceramics — by scanning cross-sections generated from a 3D digital model on the surface of a powder bed. In the **metal and ceramic variant**, the laser either partially sinters (with subsequent infiltration/secondary processing) or fully melts the powder to form a dense, functional part layer-by-layer.

For **metals**, the technique is commonly branded as:
- **DMLS — Direct Metal Laser Sintering** (EOS GmbH terminology)
- **SLM — Selective Laser Melting** (when full melting is achieved)
- **Indirect SLS** (when a polymer binder is used and post-processing steps like debinding and sintering follow)

For **ceramics**, because pure ceramic powders are extremely difficult to sinter by laser alone due to their **high melting points (often > 2000 °C)** and **low thermal shock resistance**, the process usually requires:
- **Polymer-binder-assisted indirect SLS** followed by debinding and furnace sintering
- **Pre-ceramic polymer (polymer-derived ceramics, PDCs)** route
- **Slurry-based or resin-mixed ceramic powder** approach

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In KTU board examinations, students MUST distinguish between **Direct** and **Indirect** SLS for both metals and ceramics. Examiners routinely award a 3-mark question on this exact classification.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Laser Welding Pen for Powder"

Imagine you have a tray filled with fine beach sand (the powder bed). Now imagine using a highly precise, computer-controlled glue gun (the laser) that traces a 2D shape on top of the sand, sticking the grains together along the path of the gun. Once one shape is traced, you sprinkle a fresh thin layer of sand on top, and the gun traces the next layer — bonding it to the layer below. After 1000 such layers, you dig out the object: it's buried inside the loose sand.

- The **laser** is the glue gun.
- The **powder** is the loose sand.
- The **un-sintered powder** around the part acts as a natural **support material**, which is why SLS uniquely requires no dedicated support structures.
- For **metals**, the laser must reach the metal's melting point — like using a soldering iron instead of a glue gun.
- For **ceramics**, the temperatures are so extreme that we use a *glue (polymer binder)* first, then bake the entire part in a furnace to make it truly ceramic.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Reference Values Used Throughout This Module:**

| Parameter | Typical Value (Metal SLS) | Typical Value (Ceramic SLS) |
|---|---|---|
| Laser Type | Yb-fiber laser ($\lambda \approx 1070$ nm) | CO$_2$ laser ($\lambda \approx 10.6\ \mu$m) for binders |
| Laser Power ($P$) | **200 W – 1000 W** | **5 W – 50 W** (binder only) |
| Layer Thickness ($t$) | **20 – 100 µm** | **50 – 200 µm** |
| Powder Particle Size | **15 – 45 µm** | **10 – 60 µm** |
| Build Chamber | Inert gas (N$_2$ or Ar), O$_2$ < **0.1 %** | Air or inert depending on binder |
| Scan Speed ($v$) | **200 – 2000 mm/s** | **100 – 1000 mm/s** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism — Six Logical Steps

The **SLS process for metals and ceramics** follows a recurring six-step logic cycle per layer:

1. **Powder Deposition:** A recoater blade or rotating roller spreads a thin, uniform layer (20–100 µm for metals, 50–200 µm for ceramics) of fresh powder across the build platform.
2. **Powder Bed Pre-Heating:** The entire bed is heated to a temperature just below the material's melting/sintering point (typically 80–90 % of melting for metals). This reduces the thermal gradient and minimizes residual stresses and warping.
3. **Laser Scanning:** A focused laser beam, steered by galvanometer mirrors, selectively scans the cross-section of the part according to the sliced CAD data. Energy input causes localized melting (metals) or binder melting (ceramics/polymer route).
4. **Layer Solidification:** The scanned track rapidly cools, fusing to the previous layer. For metals, this forms a metallurgical bond; for indirect ceramics, the polymer binder locks the ceramic grains in shape.
5. **Platform Lowering:** The build platform descends by exactly one layer thickness, and a new powder layer is deposited.
6. **Repetition:** Steps 2–5 are repeated until the full 3D geometry is complete. Un-sintered powder is later recovered and reused.

## 2.2 Why Metals and Ceramics Behave Differently Under the Laser

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Why" Behind Process Selection:**

**Metals** possess:
- High thermal conductivity ($\sim 15$ to $\sim 400\ \mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$)
- Moderate melting points (Steel: ~1500 °C, Ti: ~1670 °C, Al: ~660 °C)
- Good laser absorption at fiber-laser wavelength ($\sim 1070$ nm)

→ Therefore, metals can be **directly melted** by a fiber laser with relatively high energy density.

**Ceramics** possess:
- Very low thermal conductivity ($\sim 2$ to $\sim 30\ \mathrm{W/m\cdot K}$)
- Extremely high melting points (Al$_2$O$_3$: ~2072 °C, SiC: ~2700 °C, ZrO$_2$: ~2715 °C)
- Poor laser absorption and brittleness → thermal shock cracking

→ Therefore, ceramics **cannot be directly melted** in most commercial SLS systems. The **indirect (binder-assisted) route** is mandatory.

## 2.3 The Two Material Routes Compared

### (a) Direct Metal SLS (DMLS / SLM)
- A high-power fiber laser fully melts the metal powder in an inert atmosphere (N$_2$ or Ar).
- Produces near-fully-dense parts (relative density > **95 %**, often > **99 %**).
- No post-processing sintering required; however, **support structures are usually needed** to anchor overhangs and dissipate heat.
- Examples: EOS M 290 (DMLS), SLM Solutions SLM 800.

### (b) Indirect Metal SLS (Binder-Based)
- A CO$_2$ or diode laser selectively melts a polymer binder mixed with metal powder.
- Produces a "**green part**" held together by binder.
- Post-processing:
  1. **Debinding** — thermal or chemical removal of binder.
  2. **Furnace Sintering** — diffusion bonding at high temperature (typically 0.7–0.9 of melting point).
- Final shrinkage of **15 % – 20 %** must be compensated in CAD scaling.

### (c) Indirect Ceramic SLS
- Powder is a mixture of ceramic particles (e.g., Al$_2$O$_3$, SiC) with a sacrificial polymer binder.
- Laser melts only the binder → green ceramic part.
- Debinding (slow heating, ~1 °C/min to ~600 °C) removes polymer.
- Furnace sintering at high temperature densifies the part (often HIP — Hot Isostatic Pressing — applied afterwards).
- Final shrinkage compensation: **20 % – 30 %**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are mathematical certainties and frequently tested in KTU board exams.**

| Formula Symbol | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Variables / Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Energy Density (ED)** | Volumetric Energy Density | $$ED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$ | $P$ = Laser power [W]; $v$ = scan speed [mm/s]; $h$ = hatch spacing [mm]; $t$ = layer thickness [mm] | Governs melt-pool formation, density, balling, porosity |
| **Relative Density** | Density Ratio | $$\rho_r = \frac{\rho_{part}}{\rho_{theoretical}} \times 100\%$$ | $\rho_{part}$ [g/cm³]; $\rho_{theoretical}$ [g/cm³] | Quality metric; KTU requires > 95 % for SLM |
| **Shrinkage Compensation** | Linear Scaling | $$L_{CAD} = L_{final} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - s}$$ | $L_{CAD}$ = dimension in CAD [mm]; $L_{final}$ = dimension in part [mm]; $s$ = fractional linear shrinkage | Used in indirect SLS post-sintering |
| **Volumetric Shrinkage** | From Linear | $$\Delta V \approx 3s \quad \text{(for small s)}$$ | $\Delta V$ = volumetric shrinkage fraction; $s$ = linear shrinkage | Estimates binder-removal porosity |
| **Thermal Gradient** | Driving Force for Residual Stress | $$\nabla T = \frac{T_{melt} - T_{bed}}{\delta}$$ | $T_{melt}$ [°C]; $T_{bed}$ [°C]; $\delta$ = solidified layer thickness [mm] | Higher $\nabla T$ → more residual stress & cracking |
| **Peclet Number** | Thermal Regime | $$Pe = \frac{v \cdot L}{\alpha}$$ | $v$ = scan speed [mm/s]; $L$ = melt-pool length [mm]; $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity [mm²/s] | Indicates conduction- vs convection-dominated cooling |
| **Specific Energy** | Energy per Unit Mass | $$E_s = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t \cdot \rho_{powder}}$$ | $\rho_{powder}$ = powder packing density [g/cm³] | Predicts melting regime (conduction vs keyhole) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Avoid using `\|` or `|` in KTU answer sheets — always use plain notation for absolute values. The same applies to LaTeX tables as instructed.**

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **Aerospace:** SLM of Ti-6Al-4V for topology-optimized brackets (Airbus A350, GE LEAP fuel nozzle).
- **Medical:** Patient-specific Ti alloy cranial implants and Co-Cr knee implants.
- **Automotive (Motorsport):** AlSi10Mg and Inconel 718 functional prototypes.
- **Foundry:** Indirect SLS of sand (e.g., Voxeljet, ExOne) for rapid investment casting patterns.
- **Dental:** Co-Cr and zirconia frameworks via indirect ceramic SLS.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Volumetric Energy Density (ED) Equation

The Energy Density is the **single most-tested equation** in KTU exams on SLS. Below is the exhaustive derivation.

### Step 1 — Define the Energy Delivered by the Laser per Unit Time

The laser delivers power $P$ [W] continuously. The energy delivered in time $dt$ is:

$$dE_{delivered} = P \cdot dt$$

This represents the **rate of energy emission** by the laser.

### Step 2 — Define the Interaction Time at a Given Point

As the laser beam moves at scan speed $v$ [mm/s] across a hatch spacing $h$ [mm], each powder region is exposed to the beam for a duration equal to the time the beam takes to traverse one hatch:

$$t_{interaction} = \frac{h}{v}$$

This represents the **residence time of the laser over each square unit** of the layer.

### Step 3 — Energy Delivered per Unit Area of the Surface (Areal Energy Density)

Multiplying the power by the interaction time gives the energy deposited per unit area of one scanned track:

$$E_{areal} = P \cdot t_{interaction} = P \cdot \frac{h}{v} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v}$$

The unit is **J/mm²** — energy per unit area of the printed surface.

### Step 4 — Extend to the Volume of Powder Affected (Volumetric Energy Density)

To find the energy delivered per unit **volume** of powder, we divide the areal energy by the layer thickness $t$ [mm]:

$$ED = \frac{E_{areal}}{t} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v \cdot t}$$

Rearranging to the canonical form used in all KTU textbooks:

$$\boxed{\,ED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}\,}$$

The unit is **J/mm³**.

### Step 5 — Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard)

Given for a metal SLS process:
- $P = 370$ W
- $v = 1000$ mm/s
- $h = 0.12$ mm
- $t = 0.03$ mm

Calculation:

$$ED = \frac{370}{1000 \times 0.12 \times 0.03}$$

$$ED = \frac{370}{3.6}$$

$$\boxed{ED \approx 102.78\ \mathrm{J/mm^3}}$$

This value is typical for **steel (316L)** SLM and lies within the optimal window of **60 – 120 J/mm³**, ensuring near-full density.

## 3.2 Derivation of Shrinkage Compensation

For indirect SLS, the part shrinks uniformly during debinding and sintering. If the final desired length is $L_{final}$ and the linear shrinkage fraction is $s$, then the CAD dimension must be scaled up:

$$L_{CAD} - s \cdot L_{CAD} = L_{final}$$

Factorizing:

$$L_{CAD} (1 - s) = L_{final}$$

Solving for $L_{CAD}$:

$$\boxed{\,L_{CAD} = \frac{L_{final}}{1 - s}\,}$$

**Numerical Example:** If the final dimension must be **100 mm** and the shrinkage is **18 % (s = 0.18)**, the CAD dimension must be:

$$L_{CAD} = \frac{100}{1 - 0.18} = \frac{100}{0.82}$$

$$\boxed{L_{CAD} \approx 121.95\ \mathrm{mm}}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Thermal Gradient Effect on Residual Stress

The thermal gradient drives residual stress $\sigma_r$ by elastic-strain mismatch. A simplified plane-strain approximation:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{E \cdot \alpha_{th} \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus [GPa]
- $\alpha_{th}$ = coefficient of thermal expansion [1/K]
- $\Delta T = T_{solidus} - T_{bed}$ [K]
- $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio

The KTU board rarely asks the full elasticity derivation but frequently asks the **simplified thermal gradient form**:

$$\nabla T = \frac{T_{melt} - T_{bed}}{\delta}$$

This quantifies how rapidly temperature drops across one solidified layer — the **driving force for warping and cracking**. Lowering $\nabla T$ by **pre-heating the build platform** is the most important practical mitigation strategy.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiners frequently ask: "How does increasing X affect Y?" Use this matrix as a quick revision reference.**

| Parameter ↑ (Increased) | Effect on Density | Effect on Surface Roughness | Effect on Residual Stress | Effect on Build Time |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Power ($P$) | ↑ (up to a limit) | ↓ smoother | ↑ increases | ↓ faster |
| Scan Speed ($v$) | ↓ reduces | ↑ rougher | ↓ reduces | ↑ slower |
| Hatch Spacing ($h$) | ↓ reduces | ↑ rougher | ↓ reduces | ↑ slower |
| Layer Thickness ($t$) | ↓ reduces | ↑ rougher | Mixed | ↑ much faster |
| Pre-Heat Temp | ↑ improves | Mild improvement | ↓↓ strongly reduces | No change |

**Optimal ED Window for Stainless Steel 316L:** **60 – 120 J/mm³**
**Below 60 J/mm³** → Lack of fusion porosity (balling).
**Above 120 J/mm³** → Keyhole porosity, vaporization, spatter.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of SLS Metal & Ceramic Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[CAD Model Input STL File] --> B1[Slicing Software Layer Thickness 20 to 100 um]
    B1 --> C1[Build Platform Pre-Heating Just Below Melting Point]
    C1 --> D1[Recoater Spreads Powder Layer Metal or Ceramic Binder Mix]
    D1 --> E1[Galvanometer Scanner Steers Laser Beam]
    E1 --> F1[Laser Sintering or Melting Selected Cross Section]
    F1 --> G1{Process Type}
    G1 -->|Direct Metal SLM or DMLS| H1[Full Metallurgical Bond Layer Solidifies]
    G1 -->|Indirect Binder Route Metal or Ceramic| H2[Binder Melts Green Part Formed]
    H1 --> I1[Build Platform Lowers by One Layer]
    H2 --> I1
    I1 --> J1{More Layers Required}
    J1 -->|Yes| D1
    J1 -->|No| K1[Part Cooling in Inert Atmosphere]
    K1 --> L1{Indirect Route}
    L1 -->|Yes| M1[Debinding Furnace Removes Binder]
    M1 --> N1[Sintering Furnace Densifies Part 15 to 30 percent Shrinkage]
    L1 -->|No Direct| O1[Support Removal and Surface Finishing]
    N1 --> O1
    O1 --> P1[Final Metal or Ceramic Part]

    style A1 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G1 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style L1 fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Schematic of Melt Pool Dynamics and Defect Formation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A2[Laser Beam Incident] --> B2[Absorption in Powder Bed]
    B2 --> C2{Melt Pool Regime}
    C2 -->|Low Energy Density| D2[Conduction Mode Stable Shallow Melt Pool]
    C2 -->|Optimal Energy Density| E2[Keyhole Transition Dense Part]
    C2 -->|Excessive Energy Density| F2[Deep Keyhole Voids and Evaporation]
    D2 --> G2[Defect Lack of Fusion Porosity Balling]
    E2 --> H2[Defect Free Near Full Density]
    F2 --> I2[Defect Keyhole Porosity Cracks Spatter]

    style A2 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E2 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G2 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style I2 fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Material Selection Decision Tree for SLS

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Start Material Selection] --> B3{Is Material a Polymer}
    B3 -->|Yes Pure Polymer| C3[Use Standard SLS Nozzle or Bed]
    B3 -->|No| D3{Is Material a Metal}
    D3 -->|Yes| E3{Melting Point Below 1700 C and High Conductivity}
    E3 -->|Yes| F3[Direct SLM or DMLS Fiber Laser Argon Atmosphere]
    E3 -->|No Refractory Metal| G3[Indirect SLS Binder Route Plus Furnace Sintering]
    D3 -->|No Ceramic| H3[Ceramic Powder Mixed With Polymer Binder]
    H3 --> I3[Indirect SLS Then Debinding and Furnace Sintering at 1300 to 1700 C]
    F3 --> J3[Dense Functional Metal Part]
    G3 --> J3
    I3 --> K3[Dense Functional Ceramic Part]

    style A3 fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style J3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style K3 fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) and Selective Laser Melting (SLM).**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | DMLS | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| Melting | Partial sintering at grain boundaries | Complete melting of powder |
| Density | 95–99 % | > 99 % |
| Temperature | Lower than melting point | At or above melting point |
| Trademarked By | EOS GmbH | Generic academic term / SLM Solutions |

**Awarding scheme:** 1 mark for correct melting definition; 1 mark for density comparison; 1 mark for distinguishing industrial vs academic nomenclature.

### Question 2 — [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Why are ceramics not directly laser sintered in commercial SLS systems? Give two reasons.**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Extremely high melting points** of ceramics (e.g., Al$_2$O$_3$ ~2072 °C, SiC ~2700 °C) exceed the energy capability of standard SLS lasers without causing evaporation.
2. **Low thermal shock resistance** — direct laser heating causes steep thermal gradients, leading to **micro-cracking and catastrophic fracture** during cooling.

**Awarding scheme:** 1 mark each for the two reasons. A third optional point — poor laser absorption — may earn bonus credit.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS) process for metal powders**, highlighting the role of the build platform pre-heating and inert atmosphere. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Define **Volumetric Energy Density** and derive its formula. For a process with $P = 400$ W, $v = 800$ mm/s, $h = 0.1$ mm, $t = 0.04$ mm, compute the energy density and state whether it lies in the optimal window for **316L stainless steel**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Schematic description (3 marks):** Draw a labeled block diagram showing:
- Powder reservoir and recoater blade
- Build platform with pre-heating coils
- Galvanometer-scanned laser beam (fiber laser, $\lambda \approx 1070$ nm)
- Inert gas inlet (Ar/N$_2$) and O$_2$ analyzer (< 0.1 %)
- Build chamber with temperature controller

**Functional explanation (3 marks):**
1. Powder is spread in a thin layer (20–100 µm).
2. Build platform pre-heats the powder to **~80–90 % of metal's melting point** to reduce thermal gradients and minimize residual stress.
3. Inert gas (Ar or N$_2$) prevents **oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al) and eliminates porosity from oxygen-related defects.
4. Laser selectively melts powder according to sliced CAD data; melt pool solidifies into a dense metallic bond.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Pre-heating and inert atmosphere are essential for **dimensionally accurate, oxidation-free, near-fully-dense** metal SLS parts.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Definition (2 marks):**

$$ED = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Where $ED$ has units of **J/mm³**, representing the laser energy delivered per unit volume of powder.

**Derivation (3 marks):**

$$\text{Energy per unit time} = P$$

$$\text{Residence time over a hatch} = \frac{h}{v}$$

$$\text{Areal energy density} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v}$$

$$\text{Volumetric energy density} = \frac{P \cdot h}{v \cdot t} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

**Numerical Calculation (2 marks):**

$$ED = \frac{400}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.04} = \frac{400}{3.2}$$

$$\boxed{ED = 125\ \mathrm{J/mm^3}}$$

**Verdict:** Optimal ED window for 316L stainless steel is **60 – 120 J/mm³**. A computed value of **125 J/mm³** lies **slightly above** the optimum, indicating a risk of **keyhole porosity and metal vaporization**. Recommendation: reduce power to **~384 W** or increase scan speed to **~1040 mm/s**.

### Question B — Alternative Choice — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Discuss the **indirect SLS route for ceramic part creation**, including the role of binders, debinding, and sintering. Mention typical shrinkage values. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **linear shrinkage compensation formula**? A ceramic part needs a final length of **80 mm**. The sintering shrinkage is **22 %**. Calculate the CAD dimension required. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

**Process Outline (3 marks):**
1. Powder preparation: Ceramic powder (e.g., Al$_2$O$_3$, ZrO$_2$) is blended with **15–30 % by volume of polymer binder** (e.g., polyamide, phenolic resin).
2. SLS of green part: A low-power CO$_2$ laser ($\lambda \approx 10.6\ \mu$m) melts only the binder, locking ceramic grains into the desired 2D shape.
3. Layer-by-layer buildup continues until the green part is complete.

**Post-Processing (3 marks):**
- **Debinding:** Slow thermal cycle (1 °C/min to ~600 °C) in a furnace pyrolyzes and removes the polymer binder, leaving a fragile "brown part" with interconnected porosity.
- **Sintering:** Furnace heated to 1300–1700 °C (depending on ceramic) drives **solid-state diffusion**, densifying the part to > 95 % theoretical density.
- Optional **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** further closes residual porosity.

**Shrinkage (1 mark):**
- Typical **linear shrinkage: 20 – 30 %** (volumetric ~50 %).
- Must be compensated in CAD scaling; otherwise tolerances exceed ± 5 %.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Formula (3 marks):**

$$L_{CAD} = \frac{L_{final}}{1 - s}$$

Where $s$ = linear shrinkage fraction.

**Substitution (2 marks):**

$$L_{CAD} = \frac{80}{1 - 0.22} = \frac{80}{0.78}$$

$$L_{CAD} = 102.56\ \mathrm{mm}$$

**Final Answer (2 marks):**

$$\boxed{L_{CAD} \approx 102.56\ \mathrm{mm}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark-Deduction Traps:**
>
> 1. **Confusing volumetric with linear shrinkage** — Some students use $s^3$ instead of $s$ for CAD scaling, losing **2 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting units in ED** — Always write **J/mm³**. Examiners deduct ½ mark if units are absent.
> 3. **Ignoring anisotropic shrinkage** — In real ceramics, shrinkage along Z (build direction) differs from XY. Examiners award bonus marks for this awareness.
> 4. **Mixing up DMLS and SLM** — DMLS is technically a trade-marked brand (EOS); SLM is generic. Examiners expect you to recognize both terms but assign them correctly.
> 5. **Skipping the inert atmosphere requirement** — Ti and Al will oxidize catastrophically without Ar. Always mention this in any metal SLS answer.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — "Must Know Before Entering the Exam Hall":**

- **SLS definition:** Powder-bed fusion using a laser to selectively fuse material layer-by-layer.
- **Direct route (metals):** Laser fully melts metal powder in inert atmosphere; produces dense parts; needs supports.
- **Indirect route (metals & ceramics):** Laser melts polymer binder; green part is then **debound and furnace sintered**; shrinkage of **15–30 %** compensated in CAD.
- **Why ceramics use indirect route:** High melting point + low thermal shock resistance → cracking if directly laser-melted.
- **ED formula (HIGH PRIORITY):** $ED = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ in J/mm³.
- **Optimal ED for 316L:** 60 – 120 J/mm³; below → lack-of-fusion; above → keyhole porosity.
- **Pre-heating** reduces residual stress by lowering $\nabla T$.
- **Inert gas** (Ar / N$_2$) prevents oxidation of reactive metals.
- **Shrinkage compensation:** $L_{CAD} = \dfrac{L_{final}}{1 - s}$.
- **Typical materials (KTU focus):** Ti-6Al-4V, 316L stainless steel, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg, Al$_2$O$_3$, ZrO$_2$, SiC.
- **Process defects to mention in answers:** Lack-of-fusion porosity, keyhole porosity, balling, residual stress, delamination, stair-stepping.
- **Post-processing for indirect SLS:** Debinding (~600 °C) → Sintering (1300–1700 °C) → Optional HIP.
- **No dedicated support structures needed in indirect SLS** (loose powder supports the part).
- **Direct SLM DOES need support structures** due to high thermal gradients and overhangs.
- **Key distinction for board exams:** "Sintering" = below melting point, "Melting" = at/above melting point.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Melting (EBM)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Electron Beam Melting (EBM)** is a high-vacuum, powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process belonging to the broader family of *Directed Energy Deposition (DED)* and *Powder Bed Fusion* technologies. In EBM, a high-velocity, focused beam of electrons—generated by a heated tungsten filament cathode inside an electron gun—is electromagnetically steered (deflected) across a thin, pre-spread layer of electrically conductive metal powder. The kinetic energy of the accelerated electrons is converted into **thermal energy** upon impact with the powder bed, causing localized melting and fusion of the powder particles into a solid, near-net-shape metallic part layer-by-layer.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition Snapshot:**
> EBM is a powder bed fusion (PBF) process where the energy source is a high-power electron beam. The process operates in a **vacuum environment (~10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar)** to prevent electron scattering by air molecules and to suppress oxidation of reactive metals such as titanium.

The EBM process was pioneered by **Arcam AB (Sweden)** in the 1990s, primarily for medical implants (orthopedic and dental) and aerospace components made from **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5 titanium alloy)**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English Explanation)

Imagine you are sitting in a completely sealed, airless room. In one corner, there is a super-powerful **electron gun**—a device that shoots a stream of tiny invisible "energy bullets" (electrons) at nearly the speed of light. These bullets are steered around the room using powerful **magnets**, like a flashlight beam that you can flick in any direction with a switch.

On the floor, an automatic shovel spreads a very thin layer (typically **50–100 µm**) of fine metal powder—say, titanium dust. The electron beam is rapidly scanned over this dust, line-by-line, in a pattern defined by a computer (a 2D slice of your 3D CAD model). Wherever the bullets hit, the dust instantly melts and welds together into a solid metal cross-section.

Once one layer is finished, the build platform (floor) **drops down** by exactly one layer thickness. A new layer of powder is spread on top, and the electron beam melts the next cross-section. The new layer fuses with the previous one because the beam re-melts a tiny bit of the underlying solid. Repeating this thousands of times, you "grow" a fully dense metal part from a vat of powder—like growing a crystal, but sculpted by an invisible electron artist.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a vacuum?** Electrons are **negatively charged particles** that get deflected by collisions with gas molecules. In air, the electron beam would scatter in less than a millimeter, completely ruining the part. Vacuum gives the electrons a clear path. It also protects reactive metals like titanium from catching fire (oxidizing).

## 1.3 Standard Engineering Metrics & Constants

| Parameter | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerating Voltage | **60** | kV |
| Beam Current | **1 – 50** | mA |
| Beam Power | **60 W – 3.6 kW** (typical 1–3 kW) | W / kW |
| Build Chamber Vacuum | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵** | mbar |
| Build Plate Temperature | **up to 700** | °C (for Ti-6Al-4V) |
| Layer Thickness | **50 – 100** | µm |
| Beam Spot Size (diameter) | **100 – 200** | µm |
| Scan Speed | **up to 10,000** | mm/s |
| Powder Particle Size | **45 – 105** | µm |
| Build Envelope (Arcam Q20plus) | **Ø 350 × 380** | mm |
| Speed of electrons (60 kV) | **~1.25 × 10⁸** | m/s (≈ 0.42c) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — Kinetic-to-Thermal Energy Conversion:**
> The energy delivered by the electron beam is purely **kinetic** ($KE = \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2$). Upon collision with the powder, this kinetic energy is absorbed by the lattice atoms as heat, melting the powder. This is fundamentally different from laser-based PBF, where energy is delivered as **photons (electromagnetic radiation)**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

### Step 1: Vacuum Establishment & Build Preparation
The build chamber is evacuated to a high vacuum ($\approx 10^{-4}$ mbar). A metallic start plate (typically the same alloy as the powder) is pre-heated to a temperature just below the powder's sintering/melting range (e.g., **~700 °C for Ti-6Al-4V**). Pre-heating drastically reduces thermal gradients, minimizing residual stress and distortion.

### Step 2: Powder Layer Deposition
A powder hopper/feed system drops a measured quantity of powder, and a **recoater blade** (or roller) spreads it evenly across the build plate to a precise layer thickness $t$ (usually 50–100 µm). The recoater moves from one side of the build envelope to the other.

### Step 3: Pre-heating (Multi-Beam Scanning) — *Unique to EBM*
Before selective melting, EBM uses a **defocused, high-speed electron beam** to gently pre-heat the entire powder layer. This is done by rapidly scanning the beam in a pre-defined pattern (often a "snake" or "zigzag" pattern) at lower power. The powder particles **lightly sinter** (fuse at contact points), which:
- Improves electrical/thermal conductivity of the powder bed.
- Prevents **"smoke" events** (electrostatic repulsion of powder particles in the vacuum due to charge buildup—essentially powder getting blown away from the build area by charged particles).
- Reduces the energy needed for full melting.

### Step 4: Selective Melting
The focused electron beam is steered by **electromagnetic deflection coils** to trace the cross-section of the current layer (sliced from the 3D CAD model). At each spot, electrons strike the powder, transfer kinetic energy, and melt the powder into a fully dense metallic track.

> [!NOTE]
> **Charge Neutralization:** A small amount of helium gas is bled into the chamber (partial pressure ~$10^{-3}$ mbar) during melting. The He atoms are ionized by the electron beam, creating a weakly conductive plasma that **neutralizes the negative charge** accumulating on the powder bed. Without this, the entire powder bed would "explode" electrically (the smoke phenomenon).

### Step 5: Platform Lowering & Recoating
After a layer is complete, the build platform (Z-axis piston) **lowers by one layer thickness** $t$. The recoater spreads a fresh layer of powder on top. The process repeats.

### Step 6: Cooling & Part Recovery
Once the build is complete, the entire powder cake is allowed to cool under vacuum (often for **6–10 hours** because the build plate is at 700 °C). The fully dense part is then dug out of the surrounding sintered powder, which is largely **recyclable**.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why vacuum?** Prevents electron scattering & oxidation of titanium/aluminum alloys.
- **Why pre-heat at 700 °C?** Ti-6Al-4V has poor thermal conductivity; without pre-heat, the thermal gradient between the molten pool (~1700 °C) and the surrounding powder (~25 °C) causes severe residual stress, distortion, and cracking.
- **Why a defocused pre-heat beam?** To sinter (not melt) the powder, giving it enough conductivity to dissipate the charge from the melting beam.
- **Why He gas bleed?** Acts as a charge-dissipation medium, analogous to a ground wire.
- **Why is the beam steered by magnets, not mirrors?** Electrons are charged; only electromagnetic (or electrostatic) fields can deflect them. Mirrors (used in laser systems) have no effect on electrons.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Electron Beam Power** | $P = V \cdot I$ | $V$ = accelerating voltage (kV); $I$ = beam current (mA); $P$ in **W** |
| 2 | **Kinetic Energy of an Electron** | $KE = e \cdot V$ | $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C; $V$ in volts; $KE$ in Joules |
| 3 | **Electron Velocity** (non-relativistic approx.) | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 e V}{m_e}}$ | $m_e = 9.11 \times 10^{-31}$ kg |
| 4 | **Energy Density (Areal)** | $E_a = \dfrac{P}{v_s \cdot d}$ | $v_s$ = scan speed (mm/s); $d$ = hatch spacing (mm); $E_a$ in J/mm² |
| 5 | **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** | $VED = \dfrac{P}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t}$ | $t$ = layer thickness (mm); $VED$ in **J/mm³** |
| 6 | **Spot Size / Beam Diameter** | $d_{spot} = f(D, I, V)$ | Depends on focus coil current; smaller current = finer spot |
| 7 | **Specific Energy (per unit mass)** | $E_m = \dfrac{P}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t \cdot \rho}$ | $\rho$ = powder bed density (g/cm³); $E_m$ in J/g |
| 8 | **Build Rate (volume)** | $\dot{V} = v_s \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f$ | $f$ = fill factor (ratio of solid area to total area) |
| 9 | **Wavelength of de Broglie wave (electron)** | $\lambda = \dfrac{h}{m_e v}$ | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s; quantum mechanical effect, often negligible |
| 10 | **Stefan's Law (melt pool cooling)** | $q = \varepsilon \sigma (T^4 - T_\infty^4)$ | $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}$ W/m²K⁴; radiation dominates in vacuum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the single most-tested formula in EBM (and laser PBF) numerical problems. Memorize it in the form:
> $$VED = \frac{P}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t} = \frac{V \cdot I}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t}$$
> Typical VED for Ti-6Al-4V in EBM: **40 – 120 J/mm³** (much higher than laser PBF due to higher power and lower scan speed needed for conductivity management).

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Why EBM? |
|---|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Turbine blades, structural brackets, satellite components | Lightweight Ti alloys; high build speed; low residual stress |
| **Medical Implants** | Hip stems, spinal cages, dental crowns | Ti-6Al-4V is biocompatible; EBM parts have rough surface (good for bone ingrowth—**osseointegration**) |
| **Automotive (Racing)** | Custom brackets, lightweight chassis nodes | Topology-optimized bionic structures |
| **Defense** | Hypersonic vehicle components | High-temperature alloys (Inconel 718, TiAl) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Signature Surface Finish of EBM:** Parts come out of the machine with a **grainy, sandpaper-like surface** (Ra ≈ 20–40 µm) because sintered powder particles stick to the outside of the part. This is actually a *feature* for orthopedic implants, but a *defect* for aerospace flow paths.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivations

### Derivation 1: Kinetic Energy of an Accelerated Electron

An electron is released from rest (cathode) and accelerated through a potential difference $V$. By the work-energy theorem, the work done by the electric field equals the kinetic energy gained.

**Given:**
- Charge of electron: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C
- Accelerating voltage: $V$ (in Volts)
- Initial velocity: $u = 0$

**Step 1 — Work done by the electric field on the electron:**

The electric force on the electron is $F = eE$, and work done over a distance $d$ (where $V = E \cdot d$) is:

$$W = F \cdot d = eE \cdot d = eV$$

**Step 2 — Equate to kinetic energy (work-energy theorem):**

$$KE = W \implies \frac{1}{2} m_e v^2 = eV$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the final velocity $v$:**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 eV}{m_e}}$$

**Step 4 — Substituting standard values for $V = 60{,}000$ V (60 kV, typical EBM):**

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times (1.602 \times 10^{-19}) \times (6 \times 10^4)}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{\frac{1.9224 \times 10^{-14}}{9.11 \times 10^{-31}}}$$

$$v = \sqrt{2.1104 \times 10^{16}} = 1.4529 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This velocity is about **48% the speed of light**, so the *relativistic* correction becomes significant. A more accurate EBM calculation uses the relativistic kinetic energy:
> $$KE_{rel} = (\gamma - 1) m_e c^2, \quad \gamma = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 - (v/c)^2}}$$
> For KTU numerical problems, the non-relativistic formula is accepted unless explicitly stated.

---

### Derivation 2: Beam Power and Energy Density

In an EBM process operating on Ti-6Al-4V, the following parameters are set:

- Accelerating voltage: $V = 60$ kV
- Beam current: $I = 8$ mA
- Scan speed: $v_s = 1000$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing: $d = 0.1$ mm
- Layer thickness: $t = 0.05$ mm

**Step 1 — Calculate the electron beam power:**

$$P = V \cdot I = (60 \times 10^3 \text{ V}) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ A}) = 480 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the Volumetric Energy Density (VED):**

$$VED = \frac{P}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t}$$

$$VED = \frac{480}{1000 \times 0.1 \times 0.05}$$

$$VED = \frac{480}{5} = 96 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

A VED of **96 J/mm³** lies in the upper-end optimal range for Ti-6Al-4V EBM (40–120 J/mm³). It indicates sufficient energy for full melting without excessive evaporation or porosity.

---

### Derivation 3: Build Time Estimation

**Given:**
- Part volume: $V_p = 20{,}000$ mm³
- Layer thickness: $t = 0.05$ mm
- Hatch spacing: $d = 0.1$ mm
- Scan speed: $v_s = 1000$ mm/s
- Fill factor (solid area per layer): $f = 0.25$ (a typical hollow bracket)
- Number of layers: $n = \dfrac{\text{part height}}{t} = \dfrac{50}{0.05} = 1000$ layers

**Step 1 — Total scan length per layer (approximation):**

Average scan length per layer $\approx \dfrac{V_p}{n \cdot d \cdot t}$ is non-trivial; instead, use the volumetric build rate formula directly.

**Step 2 — Volumetric build rate $\dot{V}$:**

$$\dot{V} = v_s \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f$$

$$\dot{V} = 1000 \times 0.1 \times 0.05 \times 0.25 = 1.25 \text{ mm}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 3 — Convert to mm³/hour:**

$$\dot{V} = 1.25 \times 3600 = 4500 \text{ mm}^3/\text{h}$$

**Step 4 — Total build time:**

$$T_{build} = \frac{V_p}{\dot{V}} = \frac{20{,}000}{1.25} = 16{,}000 \text{ s}$$

$$T_{build} = \frac{16{,}000}{3600} \approx 4.44 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is **build time only**. The **total cycle time** must also include: pre-heating (1–2 h), cooling under vacuum (6–10 h), and powder removal. Total: ~12–16 hours per build.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (EBM vs. Other AM Processes)

| Parameter | **EBM** | SLM (Laser PBF) | DED (Laser Cladding) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Electron beam | Fiber/Disk laser | Laser + powder feed |
| Environment | **Vacuum** | Inert gas (Ar/N₂) | Inert gas shroud |
| Max Power | **3.6 kW** (high) | ~1 kW (typical) | ~10 kW (very high) |
| Build Speed | **High** | Medium | Very high (cm³/min) |
| Residual Stress | **Low** (high pre-heat) | High | Medium |
| Material Range | **Conductive metals only** | Wide (incl. polymers/metal) | Wide (no powder bed) |
| Surface Finish | **Rough (Ra 20-40 µm)** | Smooth (Ra 5-20 µm) | Very rough |
| Feature Size | **0.3 – 0.8 mm** typical | 0.1 – 0.3 mm | 1 – 5 mm |
| Cost | **Very high (>€1M)** | High (€0.5–1M) | Medium |
| Typical Materials | Ti-6Al-4V, CoCr, Inconel 718 | Stainless, Al, Ti, maraging steel | Ti, Inconel, stainless |
| Support Structures | **Not needed** for most overhangs | Required for overhangs > 45° | Not required |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** EBM does **not** use the same "support structures" logic as laser PBF. The surrounding **sintered powder cake itself acts as the support** during the build. This is why EBM can print nearly-horizontal overhangs without dedicated supports.

---

## 3.3 Summary of Governing Equations (Quick Recall)

$$\boxed{P = V \cdot I} \quad \boxed{KE = eV} \quad \boxed{v = \sqrt{\frac{2eV}{m_e}}}$$

$$\boxed{VED = \frac{P}{v_s \cdot d \cdot t}} \quad \boxed{\dot{V} = v_s \cdot d \cdot t \cdot f} \quad \boxed{T_{build} = \frac{V_p}{\dot{V}}}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 EBM Machine Block Architecture (Mermaid)

The following diagram shows the **functional block architecture** of an Arcam-style EBM system, mapping the interactions of the electron optical column, the powder handling system, and the control electronics.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[High Voltage Power Supply 60 kV DC] --> B[Electron Gun with W Filament Cathode]
    B --> C[Anode and Electromagnetic Focusing Lens]
    C --> D[Electromagnetic Deflection Coils XY Scan]
    D --> E[Build Chamber with Vacuum]
    E --> F[Heated Build Platform Z Axis]
    F --> G[Powder Bed Layer 50 to 100 microns]
    G --> H[Recoater Blade Powder Hopper]
    H --> I[Vacuum Pumps 10 to 4 mbar]
    I --> E
    J[Helium Gas Inlet Charge Neutralization] --> E
    K[CAD Slicer Software STL to 2D Layers] --> L[EBM Control System Real Time]
    L --> D
    L --> B
    M[Cooling System Water Chilled] --> B
    M --> C
```

## 4.2 Process Workflow (Layer-by-Layer Sequence)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1[Step 1 Vacuum Pump Down to 10 to 4 mbar] --> S2[Step 2 Build Plate Pre Heat to 700 C]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3 Recoat Powder Layer Thickness 50 microns]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4 Defocused Beam Pre Heat Sintering Pass]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5 Focused Beam Selective Melting of Cross Section]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6 Lower Build Platform by 50 microns]
    S6 --> S3
    S6 --> S7{All Layers Complete?}
    S7 -->|No| S3
    S7 -->|Yes| S8[Step 8 Vacuum Cool Down 6 to 10 hours]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9 Powder Removal and Part Recovery]
```

## 4.3 Electron Optical Column Detail (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    F1[Tungsten Filament Cathode Heated to 2500 C] --> F2[Wehnelt Cylinder Bias Control Emission]
    F2 --> F3[Anode Aperture 60 kV Acceleration]
    F3 --> F4[Magnetic Condenser Lens Beam Collimation]
    F4 --> F5[Magnetic Objective Lens Final Focus]
    F5 --> F6[Electromagnetic Deflection Coils XY Steered Scan]
    F6 --> F7[Vacuum Window to Build Chamber]
    F7 --> F8[Powder Bed Target Impact Site]
    F8 --> F9[Heat Generation Kinetic to Thermal Conversion]
    F9 --> F10[Localized Melting of Metal Powder]
```

## 4.4 Process Parameter Control Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[User Inputs CAD Model and Material] --> P2[Slicer Generates Scan Vectors]
    P2 --> P3[Control Unit Sets Beam Current I]
    P2 --> P4[Control Unit Sets Scan Speed vs]
    P2 --> P5[Control Unit Sets Hatch Spacing d]
    P2 --> P6[Control Unit Sets Layer Thickness t]
    P3 --> P7[Electron Beam Generation]
    P4 --> P7
    P5 --> P7
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8[Powder Bed Melting]
    P8 --> P9[Real Time Monitoring IR Pyrometer]
    P9 -->|Feedback| P3
    P9 -->|Feedback| P4
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->
