# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]
**Q: List any three major applications of Selective Laser Melting (SLM) in the aerospace industry. Explain why SLM is preferred over conventional manufacturing for these applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Three major applications of SLM in aerospace are:
1. **Topology-optimized structural brackets** — SLM enables complex lattice structures that reduce mass by 40–60% while maintaining load-bearing capacity.
2. **Conformal-cooled turbine blades and injection molds** — Internal cooling channels follow the part contour, improving heat transfer and extending service life.
3. **Rocket engine injectors and combustion chambers** — Complex internal swirl geometries (e.g., SpaceX Raptor) are printed as a single piece, eliminating assembly welds.

SLM is preferred because (1 mark): it enables **geometric freedom** (internal channels, lattices), (1 mark) provides **serial-production quality** with densities > 99.5%, and (1 mark) drastically reduces **buy-to-fly ratio** from 10:1 (machining) to 1:1 (additive).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]
**Q: Why is Electron Beam Melting (EBM) preferred over Selective Laser Melting (SLM) for certain titanium aerospace components?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
EBM is preferred for **reactive titanium alloys** because it operates in a **high vacuum** (1 mark), preventing oxygen and nitrogen contamination that embrittles Ti6Al4V (1 mark). Additionally, EBM's high beam power (up to 7 kW) enables faster build rates and reduces residual stress via high build-platform temperature (~700°C), which acts as in-situ stress relief (1 mark). However, EBM has coarser surface finish (~25–40 µm Ra) and lower feature resolution than SLM, so it suits large aerospace structural parts more than fine medical implants.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks and offers an internal choice between two questions of equal difficulty. Sub-parts are typically (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Module 3]

**Q: (a)** Discuss in detail the application of Powder Bed Fusion processes in the **medical and dental sectors**. Cover at least four distinct use cases, the materials used, and the engineering benefits PBF provides over conventional manufacturing. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A Selective Laser Melting process uses the following parameters: Laser power $P = 370 \, \text{W}$, scan speed $v = 1100 \, \text{mm/s}$, hatch spacing $h = 0.12 \, \text{mm}$, and layer thickness $t = 0.04 \, \text{mm}$.
- (i) Compute the volumetric energy density. **(3 marks)**
- (ii) Comment on whether this energy density lies within the typical process window for SLM of Ti6Al4V. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) Explain what happens to the part density if $E_v$ is (a) too low and (b) too high. **(2 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question A

**Part (a) — Medical & Dental Applications of PBF (7 marks):**

**Use Case 1: Orthopedic Implants (Hip, Knee, Spinal Cages) — 2 marks**
PBF (SLM/EBM) prints patient-specific Ti6Al4V implants (per ASTM F2924 / ISO 5832-3). Lattice structures mimic the **elastic modulus of cortical bone (~17 GPa)**, reducing the **stress-shielding effect** that causes bone resorption around stiff solid metal implants. Conventional forging/machining cannot produce internal lattices.

**Use Case 2: Dental Crowns, Bridges, and Copings — 2 marks**
SLM/DMLS of Co-Cr (ASTM F75) and titanium produces dental restorations with **> 99.5% density**, eliminating the porosity-induced fractures seen in cast restorations. Digital workflow: intra-oral scan → CAD → print in 1–2 hours. PBF also enables **biocompatible surface textures** that improve osseointegration.

**Use Case 3: Surgical Guides and Anatomical Models — 1.5 marks**
SLS and MJF print PA12 (medical-grade, autoclavable) surgical drill guides and pre-operative anatomical models. Surgeons rehearse complex procedures on 1:1 scale models, reducing operating time by 20–30%.

**Use Case 4: Custom Prosthetics and Hearing Aid Shells — 1.5 marks**
SLS of PA12 prints **bespoke prosthetic sockets** and **in-ear hearing aid shells** customized from ear scans. Production cost and lead time drop from days (manual fabrication) to hours (digital scan + print).

**Engineering Benefits Summary:** (covered within sub-parts above)
- Patient-specific customization
- Internal lattice architecture
- Reduced lead time (days → hours)
- Improved biocompatibility via controlled surface texture
- Compliance with **ASTM F2924, ISO 5832, ASTM F75**

---

**Part (b) — Energy Density Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Volumetric Energy Density — 3 marks**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

$$E_v = \frac{370 \, \text{W}}{1100 \, \text{mm/s} \times 0.12 \, \text{mm} \times 0.04 \, \text{mm}}$$

$$E_v = \frac{370}{1100 \times 0.12 \times 0.04} = \frac{370}{5.28} = 70.08 \, \text{J/mm}^3$$

**(ii) Process Window Check — 2 marks**
The computed $E_v = 70.08 \, \text{J/mm}^3$ lies **within** the typical SLM process window for Ti6Al4V, which is **60–120 J/mm³**. The part is expected to achieve **> 99.5% relative density** with minimal defects. The chosen parameters are therefore **qualified for production**.

**(iii) Effect of $E_v$ on Part Density — 2 marks**
- **If $E_v$ is too low** (e.g., < 50 J/mm³ for Ti6Al4V): Insufficient energy to fully melt the powder layer, leading to **lack-of-fusion porosity** and unmelted powder trapped between layers. Mechanical strength and fatigue life drop sharply.
- **If $E_v$ is too high** (e.g., > 150 J/mm³): Excess energy causes **keyhole-mode melting**, vaporization of alloying elements, and **gas-entrapped porosity**. Residual stresses also rise due to steep thermal gradients, increasing distortion and crack risk.

---

### Question B (Alternative 14-Mark Question) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]

**Q: (a)** Compare the application suitability of **Selective Laser Sintering (SLS)** and **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** for (i) polymer functional prototypes, (ii) aerospace metallic brackets, and (iii) medical implants. Justify your choice of process in each case. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **functional architecture of a typical SLM machine**, clearly identifying the role of the laser source, scanner, build platform, powder recoater, and atmosphere control. Discuss how each subsystem enables the production of complex aerospace brackets. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — SLS vs SLM Application Comparison (7 marks):**

| Application | Preferred Process | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Polymer functional prototypes** | **SLS** (PA12) | SLS uses **lower-cost CO₂ lasers** and **polymer powders** (1.5 marks). Parts are robust, slightly porous (~95% density), and well-suited for snap-fit assemblies. SLS is **2–3× cheaper** than SLM for polymer parts (1 mark). SLM for polymers is rarely used due to thermal degradation of materials. (0.5 mark) |
| **(ii) Aerospace metallic brackets** | **SLM** (Ti6Al4V) | SLM achieves **> 99.5% density** in metals, meeting **AMS 4911 / ASTM F2924** aerospace specs (1.5 marks). Fine feature resolution (~100 µm) allows topology-optimized lattices reducing mass by 40–60% (1 mark). SLS cannot process structural metals — only polymer-bound "green" parts. (0.5 mark) |
| **(iii) Medical implants** | **SLM** (Ti6Al4V) and **EBM** (Ti6Al4V) | SLM is preferred for **fine-feature implants** (dental, spinal cages) due to high resolution and surface finish (~10 µm Ra) (1.5 marks). EBM is preferred for **large orthopedic implants** (hip stems) due to high build rate and stress-relieved microstructure (1 mark). Both are biocompatible and meet ASTM F2924. (0.5 mark) |

**Part (b) — SLM Machine Functional Architecture (7 marks):**

A typical SLM machine has the following subsystems (refer to Mermaid block diagram in Section 4.3 of these notes):

1. **Laser Source (Fiber Laser, ~200–1000 W)** — Provides the thermal energy. (1 mark)
2. **Galvanometer Scanner + F-Theta Lens** — Deflects the laser beam at high speed (~7 m/s) across the powder bed to selectively melt each cross-section. (1.5 marks)
3. **Build Platform (Z-Axis)** — Lowers by layer thickness (20–60 µm) after each layer is fused. The platform is typically pre-heated to ~80–200°C to reduce thermal gradients. (1.5 marks)
4. **Powder Recoater (Blade or Roller)** — Spreads a uniform layer of fresh powder from the feed chamber across the build platform. Recoating accuracy is critical for layer consistency. (1.5 marks)
5. **Atmosphere Control (Inert Gas — Argon or Nitrogen)** — Maintains **O₂ < 0.1% (typically < 100 ppm)** to prevent oxidation of reactive metals like Ti and Al. Laser-safe, sealed build chamber. (1.5 marks)

**Application Link:** For an **aerospace bracket**, the multi-laser scanner (e.g., EOS M 400-4 with 4 × 400 W lasers) enables **build rates up to 100 cm³/h**, allowing serial production of topology-optimized Ti6Al4V brackets with internal lattice structures that are impossible to machine.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing SLS with SLM:** Many students write "SLS" when referring to metal printing. SLS is for **polymers and some polymers-bound "green" metal parts**. Metal printing is **SLM or DMLS**. (Lose 1–2 marks per wrong usage.)
> 2. **Forgetting anisotropy:** PBF parts have **5–15% lower tensile strength along the Z (build) direction** vs. XY plane. Always mention anisotropy in any mechanical-property discussion.
> 3. **Wrong units in $E_v$:** Common errors — using $v$ in m/s instead of mm/s, or $h$ in µm instead of mm. Unit conversion errors alone cost 2 marks in numericals.
> 4. **Ignoring the powder's role as natural support:** Many students describe PBF without mentioning that **un-melted powder acts as in-situ support**, which is a primary reason PBF dominates lattice and overhang applications.
> 5. **Conflating EBM's vacuum with "no atmosphere":** EBM operates in vacuum, but a controlled helium partial pressure is used during the build. The vacuum prevents oxidation; the helium stabilizes the electron beam.
> 6. **Forgetting ISO/ASTM 52900 nomenclature:** KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to use the **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** standardized terms (Powder Bed Fusion, Material Extrusion, etc.), not vendor-specific names.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exams:**

- ✅ **Definition:** Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) is an AM process in which thermal energy (laser or electron beam) selectively fuses regions of a powder bed, as per **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015**.

- ✅ **Five PBF Variants:** **SLS, SLM, DMLS, EBM, MJF**. Know the energy source and material for each.

- ✅ **Volumetric Energy Density Formula:** $E_v = P / (v h t)$ in J/mm³. Typical SLM window for Ti6Al4V is **60–120 J/mm³**.

- ✅ **Key Equation — Linear Energy Density:** $E_l = P / v$ in J/mm. Useful for understanding track integrity.

- ✅ **Build Rate Equation:** $\dot{V} = v h t$ in mm³/s. Industrial SLM machines achieve 5–20 cm³/h per laser.

- ✅ **Six Major Application Sectors:**
  1. **Aerospace** — structural brackets, turbine blades, rocket injectors
  2. **Medical** — orthopedic implants, dental crowns, surgical guides
  3. **Automotive** — functional prototypes, lightweight brackets
  4. **Tooling** — conformal-cooled injection mold inserts
  5. **Energy** — heat exchangers with TPMS lattices
  6. **Consumer goods** — jewelry, eyewear, hearing aids

- ✅ **Anisotropy:** Build-direction (Z) strength is **5–15% lower** than XY plane strength.

- ✅ **Surface Roughness:** SLM ~10–20 µm Ra (as-built); EBM ~25–40 µm Ra; SLS polymer ~6–12 µm Ra.

- ✅ **Standards to Remember:**
  - **ASTM F2924** — SLM Ti6Al4V for surgical implants
  - **ASTM F75** — Co-Cr for dental
  - **AMS 4911 / 7000-series** — aerospace Ti6Al4V
  - **ISO/ASTM 52900:2015** — AM terminology
  - **ASTM F3055** — SLM AlSi10Mg

- ✅ **Powder Recyclability:** > 95% powder reuse is a major economic driver for PBF.

- ✅ **Why PBF over conventional methods:** Geometric freedom, mass reduction (40–60%), in-situ powder support, buy-to-fly ratio reduced from 10:1 to 1:1, customization at unit cost = 1.

- ✅ **EBM Vacuum Advantage:** EBM's vacuum environment prevents oxygen embrittlement in reactive alloys (Ti, Nb, Zr).

- ✅ **Industry Users to Remember:** Airbus (A350 brackets), Boeing (787 parts), GE Aviation (LEAP fuel nozzles), SpaceX (Raptor injectors), Stryker (spinal implants), Align Technology (Invisalign molds), Fitbit & Apple (lattice-structured watch components).

- ✅ **Examiner's favourite question patterns:**
  1. "Compare SLS and SLM for [application]" → 7 marks
  2. "Compute $E_v$ and comment on the process window" → 3 + 4 marks
  3. "Discuss PBF applications in aerospace/medical with examples" → 7–14 marks
  4. "Draw the functional block diagram of a PBF machine" → 7 marks
  5. "Explain the role of un-melted powder as in-situ support" → 3 marks

> **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Applications of Powder Bed Fusion Processes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Directed Energy Deposition AM Processes: Process Description
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Directed Energy Deposition AM Processes: Process Description

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** is an Additive Manufacturing (AM) process defined by **ISO/ASTM 52900** as a process in which **focused thermal energy** is used to fuse materials by melting them as they are being deposited. In DED, the heat source (laser, electron beam, plasma arc, or electric arc) and the material delivery system (powder nozzle or wire feeder) are **co-axially or coaxially aligned** and move together along a pre-defined toolpath, enabling **multi-axis (typically 4-axis and 5-axis)** deposition onto a substrate to build near-net-shape or fully dense metallic parts.

The KTU 2024 Scheme classifies DED as one of the **seven official AM process categories**, distinct from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Material Extrusion due to its **coaxial energy-material delivery architecture** and **simultaneous multi-axis build capability**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]  
> **Conceptual Analogy: The "Smart Welding Glaciers"**  
> Imagine a **high-tech ice-cream vending machine** mounted on a robotic arm. The nozzle continuously squirts melted ice cream (the molten material) while a powerful blowtorch (the energy source) instantly freezes/melts the surface to fuse it. As the robot arm glides in 3D space, a sculpture grows layer by layer. This is exactly how DED works: a focused energy beam creates a **tiny molten pool** on the substrate, while feedstock (powder or wire) is injected into this pool, solidifying instantly to form the part. Unlike a 3D printer confined to a box, the robot can swing around in **any direction**, allowing it to build on **existing parts, repair broken components, or create large free-form structures**—something an FDM printer can never do.

### 1.3 Core Defining Metrics of DED

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> - **Deposition Rate:** Typically **0.1 to 10 kg/hr** (orders of magnitude higher than PBF).
> - **Build Volume:** Theoretically **unlimited** because it is constrained only by the robotic reach (often **5+ meters**).
> - **Feature Resolution:** Lower than PBF, with minimum wall thicknesses of **≈ 0.5 to 1 mm**.
> - **Energy Density Range:** **10² to 10⁴ J/mm³** depending on the energy source.
> - **Minimum Oxygen Content:** Deposition chamber or shielding gas must maintain **O₂ ≤ 50 ppm** for reactive metals like titanium.

### 1.4 Classification of DED Sub-Processes

The DED family is differentiated by the **type of energy source** used to create the melt pool. Per KTU Module 3 syllabus:

| Sub-Process | Energy Source | Feedstock Form | Acronym | Key Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Laser Engineered Net Shaping | CO₂ / Fiber Laser | Powder | **LENS** | Aerospace Ti-6Al-4V parts |
| Laser Metal Deposition | Fiber Laser (often diode) | Powder | **LMD / LMDW** | Blade repair, cladding |
| Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing | Electron Beam (vacuum) | Wire or Powder | **EBAM / EBF³** | Large Ti aircraft structures |
| Plasma Transferred Arc AM | Plasma Arc | Powder | **PTA-AM** | Hard-facing, mining tools |
| Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing | GMAW / GTAW Arc | Wire | **WAAM** | Large marine/structural parts |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

> [!NOTE]  
> **The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Stage of DED**

1. **CAD to Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is sliced into 2D layers (typical layer thickness: **0.25 mm to 2 mm**). A **toolpath strategy** is generated (raster, contour, or spiral).
2. **Substrate Preparation:** A metal substrate (similar to base material) is mounted on a CNC table or robot. Surface is cleaned and preheated (**150 °C – 200 °C** common for Ti alloys).
3. **Coaxial Alignment:** The energy source and material delivery system converge at a single point — the **focal plane**. This coaxial geometry ensures material is always delivered into the melt pool regardless of nozzle orientation.
4. **Melt Pool Formation:** The energy source (laser, e-beam, or arc) strikes the substrate, creating a small molten pool typically **1 mm to 10 mm** in diameter.
5. **Material Injection:** Powder particles (typical size **45–150 µm**) or wire (typical diameter **0.8–1.6 mm**) are injected into the melt pool via inert carrier gas (Argon or Helium).
6. **Solidification:** As the heat source moves away, the molten pool solidifies at high cooling rates (**10³ to 10⁵ K/s**), forming a dense metallic bead.
7. **Layer-by-Layer Build:** The deposition head moves up (Z-axis) and the process repeats, building the part from bottom to top.
8. **Multi-Axis Capability:** For complex geometries, the deposition head is mounted on a **5-axis or 6-axis robotic arm**, allowing deposition on curved surfaces, inside cavities, or onto existing parts.

### 2.2 The Coaxial Nozzle Architecture

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> The **coaxial nozzle** is the heart of DED. Three concentric streams converge:
> - **Inner stream:** Focused energy beam (laser or e-beam).
> - **Middle stream:** Powder particles carried by inert gas (carrier gas flow rate typically **2–6 L/min**).
> - **Outer stream:** Shielding gas (Ar/He) to protect the melt pool from oxidation.
> 
> This symmetrical arrangement allows **gravity-independent** material delivery, which is why DED can deposit on **overhangs, vertical walls, and curved surfaces** — a critical advantage over PBF.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Yield Equations for KTU University Examination (Module 3)**

| Formula / Parameter | Expression | Description / Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Volumetric Energy Density (VED) | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Energy per unit volume; controls density & defects. P = power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing, t = layer thickness. Units: **J/mm³** |
| Linear Energy Density (LED) | $E_l = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | Energy per unit length of bead. Units: **J/mm** |
| Powder Catchment Efficiency | $\eta_c = \dfrac{m_{deposited}}{m_{delivered}} \times 100\%$ | Ratio of powder that enters the melt pool. Typical: **30–70%** |
| Cooling Rate (Approximate) | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | Rosenthal's 3D heat-flow approximation. k = thermal conductivity. |
| Dilution Ratio (Cladding) | $D = \dfrac{A_{substrate}}{A_{substrate} + A_{deposit}}$ | Fraction of substrate melted into coating. Critical for repair/cladding. |
| Solidification Growth Velocity | $R = v \cdot \cos\theta$ | Where $\theta$ is angle between scan vector and solidification front. |
| Deposition Rate (Powder DED) | $R_d = \eta_c \cdot \dot{m}_{powder}$ | $\dot{m}_{powder}$ is powder mass flow rate (g/min). |
| Aspect Ratio (Melt Pool) | $AR = \dfrac{W}{D}$ | W = width, D = depth. AR < 0.5 indicates **keyhole** regime. |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | $t_{min} \approx 1.5 \cdot d_{nozzle}$ | Empirical; based on coaxial nozzle exit diameter. |
| Hatch Spacing Optimization | $h \approx 0.6 \cdot W$ | Typical overlap of **40%** to ensure fusion between tracks. |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> DED is the **go-to technology** in industries where **part size, repair economics, or material versatility** matter:
> - **Aerospace:** Manufacturing near-net-shape blisks (blade + disk) and structural brackets from **Inconel 718** and **Ti-6Al-4V**, reducing buy-to-fly ratios from 20:1 (machined) to **2:1**.
> - **Defense & Marine:** **WAAM** for ship propellers and submarine components (savings of **60–80%** material).
> - **Oil & Gas:** Cladding of valves and drill bits with **Stellite** or **Inconel** overlays for wear/corrosion resistance.
> - **Automotive (Motorsport):** Rapid prototyping of **lightweight aluminum** chassis components.
> - **Repair Industry:** Restoration of **turbine blades, die tools, and gears** with matched-alloy deposition.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density and Its Physical Meaning

The **Volumetric Energy Density (VED)** is the most critical process parameter in DED. Its derivation is rooted in dimensional reasoning from first principles.

**Starting Premise:** Energy input to the melt pool is delivered at rate $P$ (watts). This energy is distributed over the volume of material that is solidified per unit time.

**Step 1: Identify the volumetric deposition rate.**
The deposition head moves at scan speed $v$ (mm/s), writes tracks of width $h$ (mm) and lays layers of thickness $t$ (mm). The volume of material solidified per second is:

$$
V_{sec} = v \cdot h \cdot t
$$

**Step 2: Form the energy-to-volume ratio.**
Energy delivered per second is $P$ (Joules/sec). Energy delivered per unit volume is therefore:

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{V_{sec}} = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}
$$

**Step 3: State the units.**
With $P$ in W (J/s), $v$ in mm/s, $h$ in mm, and $t$ in mm:

$$
E_v = \frac{J/s}{(mm/s) \cdot mm \cdot mm} = \frac{J}{mm^3}
$$

**Step 4: Interpret thresholds for KTU-level numerical reasoning.**

$$
E_v = \begin{cases}
< 30 \text{ J/mm}^3 \Rightarrow \text{Lack of fusion, porosity} \\
30 - 100 \text{ J/mm}^3 \Rightarrow \text{Conduction mode, dense part} \\
100 - 300 \text{ J/mm}^3 \Rightarrow \text{Keyhole threshold, risk of voids} \\
> 300 \text{ J/mm}^3 \Rightarrow \text{Excessive vaporization, balling}
\end{cases}
$$

**Step 5: Engineering implication.**
A typical DED build with **P = 1000 W**, **v = 10 mm/s**, **h = 1 mm**, **t = 0.5 mm** gives:

$$
E_v = \frac{1000}{10 \times 1 \times 0.5} = 200 \text{ J/mm}^3
$$

This is in the **upper conduction / near-keyhole** regime — dense but with risk of micro-voids. A KTU examiner will expect the student to **comment on the threshold interpretation**, not just plug in numbers.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Rosenthal's 3D Heat Flow Equation for DED

Rosenthal's equation gives the **temperature field around a moving point heat source**, which is the theoretical foundation for understanding melt-pool geometry in DED.

**Step 1: Define the moving coordinate system.**
Let the heat source move along the x-axis at velocity $v$. The 2D radial distance from the source is:

$$
R = \sqrt{(x - vt)^2 + y^2 + z^2}
$$

**Step 2: State Rosenthal's solution (3D, point source).**

$$
T - T_0 = \frac{P}{2\pi k R} \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{v(x + R)}{2\alpha}\right)
$$

Where:
- $T_0$ = preheat temperature (°C)
- $P$ = laser/beam power (W)
- $k$ = thermal conductivity (W/m·K)
- $\alpha$ = thermal diffusivity (m²/s) = $\dfrac{k}{\rho c_p}$
- $v$ = scan speed (m/s)

**Step 3: Derive the cooling rate at the trailing edge of the melt pool.**

As $R \to 0$ (at the heat source) and moving just behind the pool, differentiate with respect to time:

$$
\dot{T} = -2\pi k \frac{(T - T_0)^2}{P}
$$

This is the famous **inverse relationship** — **higher power reduces cooling rate** (relevant for WAAM with low power but high deposition).

**Step 4: Compute cooling rate for Ti-6Al-4V with $P = 1500$ W and $T_m = 1655$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C:**

$$
\dot{T} = \frac{2\pi (7) (1655 - 25)^2}{1500} = \frac{2\pi \times 7 \times 2,659,000}{1500}
$$

$$
\dot{T} = \frac{2 \times 3.1416 \times 7 \times 2,659,000}{1500} \approx 78,000 \text{ K/s}
$$

This matches the typical DED cooling rate of **10⁴ to 10⁵ K/s**, producing a **martensitic α' microstructure** in Ti-6Al-4V — a high-yield exam discussion point.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Process Parameters

> [!NOTE]  
> **Comparative Data Table for KTU University Exam (Module 3)**

| Parameter | LMD (Laser + Powder) | EBAM (Electron Beam + Wire) | WAAM (Arc + Wire) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Fiber Laser (~1 kW) | Electron Beam (~3–42 kW) | GMAW/GTAW Arc |
| Feedstock | Powder (45–150 µm) | Wire (0.8–1.6 mm) | Wire (1.0–1.2 mm) |
| Deposition Rate | 0.1–0.5 kg/hr | 2–7 kg/hr | 1–10 kg/hr |
| Feature Resolution | **High** (~0.5 mm) | Medium (~2 mm) | Low (~3 mm) |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas (Ar shroud) | Vacuum (≤ 10⁻³ Pa) | Shielding gas (Ar/CO₂) |
| Typical Material | Ti, Inconel, SS 316L | Ti-6Al-4V (aerospace) | Al, Steel, Ti |
| Minimum Wall Thickness | 0.5–1 mm | 2–3 mm | 3–5 mm |
| Achievable Tolerance | ±0.1 mm | ±0.5 mm | ±1.0 mm |
| Surface Roughness (Ra) | 5–15 µm | 20–50 µm | 50–200 µm |
| Build Envelope | ~1 m³ (chamber) | 8 m × 1.5 m × 1.5 m | Unlimited (robot reach) |
| Cooling Rate (K/s) | 10³–10⁴ | 10³–10⁴ | 10¹–10² |
| Power Efficiency | ~30–40% | ~70% (in vacuum) | ~60–80% |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: DED Process Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[CAD 3D Model] --> B[Slicing Software Layer Generation]
    B --> C[Toolpath Generation 5-Axis Strategy]
    C --> D[Substrate Mounting and Preheating]
    D --> E[Coaxial Nozzle Assembly]
    E --> F[Energy Source Laser EB or Arc]
    E --> G[Powder Feeder or Wire Drive]
    E --> H[Shielding Gas Supply Argon or Helium]
    F --> I[Focused Heat Source on Substrate]
    G --> J[Material Stream Injected to Melt Pool]
    H --> K[Protective Atmosphere Around Pool]
    I --> L[Molten Weld Pool]
    J --> L
    K --> L
    L --> M[Solidified Bead Formation]
    M --> N{Z Increment}
    N --> O[Layer Completed]
    O --> P{More Layers?}
    P -->|Yes| E
    P -->|No| Q[Final Part Removal and Post Processing]
    Q --> R[Final DED Manufactured Component]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: DED Sub-Process Family Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Directed Energy Deposition DED] --> B[Laser Based DED]
    A --> C[Electron Beam Based DED]
    A --> D[Plasma Based DED]
    A --> E[Arc Based DED]
    B --> B1[LENS Laser Engineered Net Shaping]
    B --> B2[LMD Laser Metal Deposition]
    B --> B3[Direct Energy Deposition DED Powder]
    C --> C1[EBAM Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing]
    C --> C2[EBF3 Electron Beam Free Form Fabrication]
    C --> C3[EBM Wire Deposition Vacuum]
    D --> D1[PTA Powder Plasma Arc Deposition]
    E --> E1[GMAW Gas Metal Arc WAAM]
    E --> E2[GTAW Gas Tungsten Arc WAAM]
    E --> E3[PAAM Plasma Arc Additive Manufacturing]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Sequence Diagram: Process Flow of a Single Bead Deposition

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CNC as CNC or Robot Controller
    participant Laser as Energy Source
    participant Powder as Powder Feeder
    participant Gas as Shielding Gas Unit
    participant Substrate as Substrate
    CNC->>Laser: Activate beam at point P1
    CNC->>Gas: Open Ar flow 15 L per min
    CNC->>Powder: Start flow 5 g per min
    Laser->>Substrate: Heat to melting point
    Powder->>Substrate: Inject into melt pool
    Substrate->>Substrate: Solidify as bead
    CNC->>CNC: Move to next point P2 at scan speed v
    Note over CNC,Substrate: Repeat for each track and layer
    CNC->>Laser: Deactivate at end of build
    CNC->>Powder: Stop flow
    CNC->>Gas: Continue for 30 s cool down
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Concept Map: Energy Delivery and Material Flow Zones

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Z1[Energy Delivery Zone]
        E1[Laser or EB or Arc Source]
        E2[Focal Point Convergence]
        E3[Heat Flux on Substrate]
    end
    subgraph Z2[Material Delivery Zone]
        M1[Powder Hopper or Wire Spool]
        M2[Carrier Gas or Drive Motor]
        M3[Coaxial Nozzle Convergence]
    end
    subgraph Z3[Melt Pool Zone]
        P1[Substrate Surface]
        P2[Molten Metal Pool]
        P3[Marangoni Flow Driven]
    end
    subgraph Z4[Solidification Zone]
        S1[Trailing Edge of Pool]
        S2[Columnar or Equiaxed Grains]
        S3[Residual Stress Field]
    end
    E3 --> P2
    M3 --> P2
    P2 --> S1
    S1 --> S2
    S2 --> S3
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Style]**  
> *Define Directed Energy Deposition (DED) as per ISO/ASTM 52900 and list any **three** distinguishing features that differentiate it from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF).*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
DED is an AM process in which focused thermal energy is used to fuse materials by melting them as they are being deposited (1 mark).  
Three distinguishing features from PBF: **(i)** coaxial delivery of energy and material (vs. bed-based powder spreading), **(ii)** 5-axis robotic capability allowing deposition on curved/existing surfaces (vs. planar 2.5-axis), **(iii)** significantly higher deposition rate (kg/hr) and larger build envelopes (vs. confined powder bed chamber) (3 × ½ = 1½ marks).  
*(Conclusion: 1 mark for coherent answer.)*

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Style]**  
> *What is the function of the coaxial nozzle in DED? Why is shielding gas essential during deposition of titanium alloys?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The coaxial nozzle converges the focused energy beam, powder stream, and shielding gas into a single focal point (1 mark). This ensures the material is delivered precisely into the melt pool irrespective of the nozzle's orientation in space (1 mark).  
Shielding gas (Argon/Helium) is essential for titanium because Ti is highly reactive with oxygen and nitrogen at elevated temperatures — it forms brittle $\alpha$-case layer that degrades fatigue life. Maintaining **O₂ < 50 ppm** prevents contamination (1 mark).

---

### 5.2 Part B Question Choice (14 Marks Each)

> **Part B Question A — [KTU University Exam Pattern - Module 3 Typical]**

> **Q3.(a) [7 marks]**  
> *With a neat schematic, explain the working principle of **Laser Metal Deposition (LMD)**. Discuss the role of coaxial nozzle, powder catchment efficiency, and volumetric energy density in determining the part quality.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Working Principle (3 marks):**  
In LMD, a high-power fiber laser (typically 0.5–4 kW) is focused onto a substrate, creating a melt pool of approximately 1–10 mm diameter. Simultaneously, metal powder (45–150 µm particle size) is injected through a coaxial nozzle using argon as carrier gas. As the deposition head traverses along the programmed toolpath, the molten metal solidifies to form a continuous bead. Subsequent layers are deposited on top, building the 3D part.

**Coaxial Nozzle Role (1.5 marks):**  
The coaxial nozzle aligns the laser beam, powder stream, and shielding gas concentrically. This enables **gravity-independent** material delivery and allows the head to be oriented in **any direction** in 3D space (overhangs, vertical walls, curved surfaces), giving LMD its 5-axis capability.

**Powder Catchment Efficiency (1 mark):**  
$\eta_c$ = (mass deposited / mass delivered) × 100%. Typical values: **30–70%**. Higher $\eta_c$ reduces waste, lowers cost, and improves process stability. It depends on carrier gas flow rate, powder size distribution, and nozzle stand-off distance.

**VED and Part Quality (1.5 marks):**  
$E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$.  
- Low $E_v$ (< 30 J/mm³) → lack of fusion, porosity.  
- Optimal $E_v$ (30–100 J/mm³) → dense, fine-grained microstructure.  
- Excess $E_v$ (> 300 J/mm³) → keyhole porosity, vaporization, balling.  
Careful tuning of $P$, $v$, $h$, and $t$ is essential to achieve **> 99.5% density**.

---

> **Q3.(b) [7 marks]**  
> *Compare **DED** with **PBF** in terms of: (i) deposition rate, (ii) surface finish, (iii) build envelope, (iv) material range, (v) multi-axis capability, (vi) post-processing, and (vii) typical industrial applications. State any **two** limitations of DED.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

| S.No. | Parameter | DED | PBF |
|---|---|---|---|
| (i) | Deposition Rate | **0.1–10 kg/hr** (high) | **0.05–0.2 kg/hr** (low) |
| (ii) | Surface Finish (Ra) | **5–200 µm** (poor) | **5–20 µm** (excellent) |
| (iii) | Build Envelope | **Unlimited** (robot reach) | **Limited** (~500 mm³) |
| (iv) | Material Range | Wide (incl. graded, multi-material) | Narrow (single alloy) |
| (v) | Multi-Axis | **5–6 axis capable** | 2.5-axis (Z only) |
| (vi) | Post-Processing | **Heavy** (machining needed) | Minimal |
| (vii) | Applications | Repair, large parts, cladding | Precision parts, medical, dental |

*(Table: 7 × 0.5 = 3.5 marks; for clarity and comparison: 1 mark)*

**Two Limitations of DED (2.5 marks):**  
1. **Lower dimensional accuracy** and surface finish compared to PBF — extensive post-machining is required.  
2. **Residual stress and distortion** are higher due to large thermal gradients — necessitates in-situ monitoring and post-process heat treatment.  
*(Supporting explanation: 1.5 marks.)*

---

> **Part B Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

> **Q4.(a) [7 marks]**  
> *Describe the **Wire Arc Additive Manufacturing (WAAM)** process. Discuss the three main arc modes (GMAW, GTAW, PAW) used in WAAM with their relative advantages.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

WAAM uses an **electric arc** as the heat source and **wire** as feedstock, making it the highest-deposition-rate DED variant.

**GMAW-based WAAM (Gas Metal Arc) (2 marks):**  
- Uses a consumable wire electrode and inert shielding gas (Ar/CO₂).  
- Deposition rate: **1–4 kg/hr**.  
- Suitable for **steel and aluminum**.  
- **Advantage:** High productivity, easy automation, lower cost.  
- **Limitation:** Lower dimensional accuracy, requires inter-pass milling for complex shapes.

**GTAW-based WAAM (Gas Tungsten Arc) (2 marks):**  
- Uses a non-consumable tungsten electrode and a separate filler wire.  
- Deposition rate: **0.5–1.5 kg/hr**.  
- Suitable for **titanium and reactive metals** with excellent purity.  
- **Advantage:** Superior surface quality, precise heat input control.  
- **Limitation:** Slower process, requires skilled setup.

**PAW-based WAAM (Plasma Arc) (2 marks):**  
- Uses a constricted plasma arc as the heat source.  
- Deposition rate: **0.5–2 kg/hr**.  
- **Advantage:** Higher energy concentration than GTAW, deeper penetration, suitable for **thick sections** and refractory metals.  
- **Limitation:** More complex equipment, higher cost.

**Concluding remark (1 mark):**  
The choice of arc mode is governed by **material chemistry, deposition rate requirement, and geometric tolerance** of the target part. WAAM is best suited for **large structural components** in shipbuilding, aerospace, and civil engineering.

---

> **Q4.(b) [7 marks]**  
> *Explain the **process parameters** governing DED part quality. Discuss in detail how **volumetric energy density (VED)** influences the resulting microstructure, density, and defect formation in laser-based DED of Inconel 718.*

**Model Solution (7 marks):**

**Key Process Parameters (3 marks):**  
The principal process parameters in DED are: **laser power (P)**, **scan speed (v)**, **hatch spacing (h)**, **layer thickness (t)**, **powder feed rate ($\dot{m}$)**, **carrier gas flow rate**, **oxygen content**, and **substrate preheat temperature (T₀)**.

**VED Definition and Formula (1 mark):**

$$
E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} \quad [\text{J/mm}^3]
$$

**Influence of VED on Inconel 718 (3 marks):**

1. **At low $E_v$ (< 30 J/mm³):**  
   - Insufficient melting → **lack-of-fusion** defects.  
   - Porosity from trapped gas.  
   - Reduced part density (< 95%).  
   - Weak inter-layer bonding.

2. **At optimal $E_v$ (30–100 J/mm³):**  
   - Conduction-mode melting → **dense (> 99.5%)** parts.  
   - Fine dendritic microstructure with **Laves phase** distributed in interdendritic regions.  
   - Good mechanical properties: UTS ≈ 1100 MPa, YS ≈ 700 MPa.

3. **At high $E_v$ (> 150 J/mm³):**  
   - Transition to **keyhole** mode → deep, narrow vapor cavity.  
   - Keyhole collapses → spherical **gas porosity**.  
   - Elemental vaporization (Mn, Cr loss) alters chemistry.  
   - Excessive Nb evaporation → reduced $\gamma''$ precipitation.

**Microstructural Impact:**  
Rapid solidification ($10^3$–$10^5$ K/s) creates a **columnar grain structure** aligned with the build direction due to epitaxial growth, producing **mechanical anisotropy** — a critical consideration for qualification per **AMS 7000-series** standards.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks in DED Questions**
> 
> 1. **Confusing DED with PBF:** Many students write "DED uses a powder bed" — this is **wrong**. DED has **no powder bed**; powder is injected coaxially with the energy beam. This single error can cost **2–3 marks**.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting units in VED formula:** Writing $E_v = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ without the **J/mm³** unit is a partial-credit killer. Always **state the unit explicitly**.
> 
> 3. **Ignoring multi-axis capability:** A common high-value point in KTU Module 3 is the **5-axis deposition head**. Students often wrongly state "DED is restricted to vertical build" — this loses 1 mark easily.
> 
> 4. **Not distinguishing LMD vs WAAM vs EBAM:** Each sub-process has distinct deposition rates, atmospheres (Ar shroud vs vacuum), and feedstocks. Generic "DED" answers are penalized.
> 
> 5. **Skipping the role of shielding gas:** For Ti/Al, mention **O₂ < 50 ppm** requirement and **α-case formation** in Ti — examiners reward this depth.
> 
> 6. **No mention of catchment efficiency or dilution ratio:** These are **direct KTU syllabus terms** and skipping them is a guaranteed 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]  
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 3 (DED — Process Description)**

- **DED Definition:** Coaxial delivery of focused energy + material to fuse as deposited (ISO/ASTM 52900).
- **Four Energy Sources:** Laser, Electron Beam, Plasma Arc, Electric Arc (GMAW/GTAW).
- **Two Feedstock Forms:** Powder (45–150 µm) and Wire (0.8–1.6 mm).
- **Coaxial Nozzle:** Aligns energy + powder + shielding gas in a single focal point.
- **Five-Axis Capability:** Unique among AM processes — enables repair and curved-surface deposition.
- **Deposition Rate Hierarchy:** WAAM > EBAM > LMD > PBF.
- **Key Formula:** $E_v = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ with units **J/mm³**.
- **Optimal $E_v$ Range:** **30–100 J/mm³** for laser-based DED of Ni/Ti alloys.
- **Shrinkage / Distortion:** Higher in DED than PBF — requires in-situ monitoring and post-machining.
- **Powder Catchment Efficiency:** **30–70%** — a major cost driver.
- **Shielding Gas:** Argon or Helium; **O₂ ≤ 50 ppm** for Ti alloys to prevent $\alpha$-case.
- **Microstructure:** Columnar dendritic grains aligned with build direction due to **epitaxial growth**.
- **Cooling Rate:** $10^3$–$10^5$ K/s — produces fine dendritic spacing and metastable phases.
- **Industrial Niche:** Aerospace blisks, turbine repair, marine structures, large Ti brackets.
- **Post-Processing:** **Mandatory** — typically 5-axis CNC machining + heat treatment + HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing).
- **Comparison Anchor:** DED = high rate + large scale; PBF = high precision + small parts.

---

**END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — DIRECTED ENERGY DEPOSITION: PROCESS DESCRIPTION**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**

> - **Module:** Module Module 3

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Material Delivery** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the **controlled, precise, and repeatable transport of raw material** (in the form of powder, filament, wire, liquid resin, binder solution, or sheet) from a storage reservoir to the **build chamber or deposition zone** where it is selectively solidified, fused, or cured to construct a three-dimensional part layer-by-layer.

It is the **second critical subprocess** in the AM chain (after digital design and before fusion/solidification), and it directly governs the **throughput, resolution, surface finish, and mechanical integrity** of the printed component.

According to the **ASTM F2792-12** standard (which classifies AM processes into seven categories), each process employs a distinct material delivery strategy:

| ASTM F2792 Category | Material State | Delivery Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| Vat Photopolymerization (VP) | Liquid Resin | Recoating blade over a vat |
| Material Extrusion (ME) | Thermoplastic Filament / Paste | Drive gear extrusion |
| Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) | Powder | Recoater blade / roller |
| Material Jetting (MJ) | Liquid Droplets | Piezo/Thermal printhead |
| Binder Jetting (BJ) | Powder + Liquid Binder | Jetting head + recoater |
| Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | Powder / Wire | Co-axial nozzle feed |
| Sheet Lamination (SL) | Foil/Sheet | Roller feed + cutting |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cake Decorator" Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of Material Delivery as the "feeding system" of a 3D pen that draws in mid-air.**

Imagine you are a **pastry chef building a wedding cake** one thin layer at a time:
- You cannot pour the entire batter at once — it would collapse.
- Instead, you **spread a thin, even layer** of batter using a spatula.
- Then you **bake/solidify** that layer.
- You **repeat** the process, each time carefully adding a new layer.

The **"how you supply the batter"** is exactly what Material Delivery is in AM:
- **How does the raw material reach the build platform?**
- **How is it spread into a uniform thin layer?**
- **How is its flow rate controlled?**

In a **powder bed fusion** machine, the "spatula" is a **counter-rotating roller or doctor blade** that scoops powder from the hopper and spreads it into a bed of thickness **typically 20–100 μm**. In an **FDM printer**, the "spatula" is a **hobbed gear driven by a stepper motor** that pushes a 1.75 mm filament into a heated nozzle.

---

## 1.3 Why Material Delivery is a High-Yield KTU Topic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Material Delivery sits at the **intersection of mechanical design, fluid/powder mechanics, and process control**. It is a favorite KTU examiner area because:
> 1. It is **mechanism-driven** (easy to diagram and explain).
> 2. It connects directly to **process parameters** (layer thickness, scan speed, build rate).
> 3. It has **measurable engineering metrics** (flow rate, deposition accuracy, recoating speed).

The **engineering metrics** typically tested in KTU are:
- **Volumetric deposition rate** ($\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$) — bolded as a primary deliverable metric.
- **Layer thickness** (typically **30–100 μm** for polymer PBF, **20–40 μm** for metal PBF).
- **Recoating speed** (typically **50–300 mm/s**).
- **Powder flowability** (measured by **Hall Flow rate**, ASTM B213).
- **Droplet volume** in jetting (typically **1–80 picolitres**).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Material Delivery Systems

Material Delivery in AM is best understood by grouping it into **four functional families**, each tied to the physical state of the feedstock:

### Family A — Powder Delivery Systems
Used in **PBF, BJ, and DED**.

1. **Gravity-fed hopper + Counter-rotating Roller (Recoater)**
   - Powder is held in an **elevated hopper** above the build platform.
   - A **hardened steel or ceramic roller** rotates against a soft **doctor blade**.
   - As the build platform lowers by one layer height, the roller traverses the platform, depositing a fresh layer.

2. **Vibratory Feeder**
   - Powder is transported using **controlled vibration** of the hopper base.
   - Used for **fine powders (<20 μm)** where gravity flow is unreliable.

3. **Pneumatic Conveying (for DED)**
   - Powder is entrained in an **inert carrier gas (Argon)** and transported through a nozzle.
   - Used in **laser metal deposition (LMD)**.

### Family B — Filament / Wire Delivery Systems
Used in **ME (FDM/FFF) and DED-Wire**.

1. **Direct Drive Extruder**
   - **Stepper motor + hobbed gear** grip the filament directly above the hotend.
   - Compact, high torque, suitable for rigid filaments.

2. **Bowden Extruder**
   - Drive motor is **separated** from the hotend by a **PTFE (Teflon) guide tube**.
   - Lower moving mass → higher print speeds.

3. **Wire Feed for DED**
   - Uses a **four-roll wire feeder** to push a continuous metal wire (typically **0.8–1.6 mm diameter**) into a **laser or arc melting pool**.

### Family C — Liquid Droplet Delivery
Used in **MJ and BJ**.

1. **Piezoelectric Printhead**
   - A voltage pulse deforms a **piezo crystal**, ejecting a droplet of **1–80 pL**.
   - Droplet frequency: up to **30 kHz**.

2. **Thermal Inkjet**
   - A thin resistor rapidly heats the liquid, forming a **vapor bubble** that ejects the droplet.

### Family D — Vat/Sheet Delivery
1. **Vat Photopolymerization Recoating**
   - A **doctor blade** re-spreads uncured resin over the build platform after each cured layer.
2. **Sheet Lamination Feed**
   - A **heated roller + ultrasonic cutter** feeds foil from a roll.

---

## 2.2 The 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Mechanism

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple delivery methods?** Because each AM process has unique **rheological, thermal, and resolution requirements**. A powder needs **flowability + uniform spreading**, a filament needs **melt-flow viscosity control**, and a liquid needs **surface tension + wetting control**.

### Step-by-Step Logic of Powder Bed Recoating

1. **Build platform lowers** by exactly **one layer thickness (h)**.
2. **Powder hopper supplies** a metered volume of powder above the build area.
3. **Recoater roller translates** horizontally at velocity $v_r$.
4. **Doctor blade/roller edge** levels the powder to a uniform bed thickness equal to $h$.
5. **Excess powder** is collected or returned to the hopper.
6. **Energy source** (laser/electron beam) selectively fuses the powder pattern.
7. **Cycle repeats** for the next layer.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they form the basis of 70% of numerical questions in KTU ESE for this module.**

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units | Engineering Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Deposition Rate (PBF) | $Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ | Powder volume delivered per second |
| 2 | Mass Deposition Rate (PBF) | $\dot{m} = \rho_p \cdot v_r \cdot w \cdot h$ | $\text{g/s}$ | Powder mass flow during recoating |
| 3 | Recoating Time per Layer | $t_r = L / v_r$ | $\text{s}$ | Time to spread one layer |
| 4 | Layer Build Time | $t_{layer} = t_r + t_{scan}$ | $\text{s}$ | Total time per layer (recoat + fuse) |
| 5 | Filament Volumetric Flow (FDM) | $Q_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | $\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$ | Volume of plastic melted per second |
| 6 | Mass Flow Rate (FDM) | $\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$ | $\text{g/s}$ | Plastic mass extruded per second |
| 7 | Build Time Estimate | $T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{Q_v} \cdot n_{layers}$ | $\text{s}$ or hours | Approximate total build duration |
| 8 | Powder Packing Fraction | $\phi = \frac{\rho_{bulk}}{\rho_{true}}$ | dimensionless | Ratio of bulk to true density |
| 9 | Droplet Volume (Jet Printing) | $V_d = \frac{\pi d_d^3}{6}$ | $\text{pL}$ or $\mu\text{m}^3$ | Volume of each ejected droplet |
| 10 | Droplet Frequency Limit | $f_d \le \frac{v_{max}}{d_d}$ | $\text{Hz}$ | Max droplet placement rate |

Where the standard symbols used throughout this table are:
- $v_r$ = recoater velocity (mm/s)
- $w$ = recoater width (mm)
- $h$ = layer thickness (mm)
- $\rho_p$ = powder bulk density (g/cm³)
- $L$ = build platform length (mm)
- $d_f$ = filament diameter (mm)
- $v_f$ = filament feed velocity (mm/s)
- $\rho_f$ = filament density (g/cm³)
- $d_d$ = droplet diameter (μm)

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Material Delivery systems are **the throughput bottleneck** of every AM machine. In **industrial production**:
> - **EOS M 400-4** (metal PBF): uses a **multi-laser recoater system** with recoating speed of **~200 mm/s** and layer thickness **40 μm**.
> - **Stratasys F900 (FDM)**: uses a **dual direct-drive extruder** with filament diameters of **1.75 mm** at feed rates up to **~10 mm³/s**.
> - **3D Systems ProJet CJP (Binder Jetting)**: uses a **piezo printhead** ejecting **80 pL droplets** at **30 kHz** over a powder bed of **100 μm layers**.

Understanding the delivery system is essential for **process engineer roles, R&D of new feedstocks, and quality control in production environments**.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Volumetric Deposition Rate in Powder Bed Recoating

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is the **single most important derivation** for KTU numerical questions on this topic.

**Problem Statement:** A powder bed fusion system uses a recoater of width $w$ moving at velocity $v_r$ to spread a powder layer of thickness $h$. Derive the expression for the volumetric deposition rate $Q_v$.

**Step 1 — Visualize the Control Volume**

Imagine a rectangular box of powder being delivered to the build area. In time $\Delta t$, the recoater sweeps a distance $v_r \cdot \Delta t$, depositing powder across the width $w$ to a thickness $h$.

**Step 2 — Write the Volume Element**

The volume of powder deposited in time $\Delta t$ is:

$$V = (v_r \cdot \Delta t) \cdot w \cdot h$$

**Step 3 — Define Volumetric Deposition Rate**

By definition, the volumetric deposition rate is volume per unit time:

$$Q_v = \frac{V}{\Delta t} = \frac{(v_r \cdot \Delta t) \cdot w \cdot h}{\Delta t}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel $\Delta t$**

$$\boxed{Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h \quad \left[\frac{\text{mm}^3}{\text{s}}\right]}$$

This is the **canonical equation** for powder bed material delivery rate.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mass Deposition Rate Including Packing Fraction

Real powders do **not pack perfectly**; they have a packing fraction $\phi < 1$.

**Step 1 — Start with Volumetric Deposition Rate**

$$Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by True Material Density**

The mass of fully dense material in this volume is:

$$\dot{m}_{solid} = Q_v \cdot \rho_{true}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Packing Fraction**

Because only a fraction $\phi$ of the volume is actually solid material (the rest is air gaps):

$$\dot{m} = \phi \cdot \rho_{true} \cdot v_r \cdot w \cdot h$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the Bulk Density**

Since $\rho_{bulk} = \phi \cdot \rho_{true}$, we can also write:

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot v_r \cdot w \cdot h \quad \left[\frac{\text{g}}{\text{s}}\right]}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Recoating Time Per Layer

**Step 1 — Define the Recoating Distance**

The recoater must traverse the full length $L$ of the build platform.

**Step 2 — Use the Fundamental Velocity Relation**

$$v_r = \frac{L}{t_r}$$

**Step 3 — Rearrange for Time**

$$\boxed{t_r = \frac{L}{v_r} \quad [\text{s}]}$$

This shows that **recoating time is inversely proportional to recoater velocity** — doubling $v_r$ halves $t_r$.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Build Time Estimate for FDM

**Step 1 — Volumetric Flow Through the Nozzle**

The filament (diameter $d_f$) is fed at velocity $v_f$, so the volume entering the melt zone per second is:

$$Q_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

**Step 2 — Mass Flow**

$$\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot Q_f = \rho_f \cdot \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} \cdot v_f$$

**Step 3 — Total Build Time**

For a part of volume $V_{part}$:

$$T_{build} = \frac{V_{part}}{Q_f} + t_{setup} + t_{cooldown}$$

In ideal conditions (ignoring setup and cooldown):

$$\boxed{T_{build} = \frac{4 \cdot V_{part}}{\pi d_f^2 \cdot v_f}}$$

---

## 3.5 Numerical Worked Example (KTU-Style)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Solved Example:** A metal PBF machine has a build platform of length $L = 250$ mm and width $w = 250$ mm. The recoater moves at $v_r = 100$ mm/s with a layer thickness $h = 0.04$ mm. The powder bulk density is $\rho_{bulk} = 4.5$ g/cm³. Calculate:
> (a) the volumetric deposition rate,
> (b) the mass deposition rate,
> (c) the recoating time per layer.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Volumetric Deposition Rate:**

$$Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h = 100 \times 250 \times 0.04$$

Convert units consistently: $h = 0.04$ mm = $0.04 \times 10^{-3}$ m. Working in mm³/s:

$$Q_v = 100 \cdot 250 \cdot 0.04 = 1000 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}$$

Convert to cm³/s: $1000 \;\text{mm}^3 = 1 \;\text{cm}^3$, so

$$\boxed{Q_v = 1.0 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**Part (b) — Mass Deposition Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot Q_v = 4.5 \;\frac{\text{g}}{\text{cm}^3} \cdot 1.0 \;\frac{\text{cm}^3}{\text{s}}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 4.5 \;\text{g/s}}$$

**Part (c) — Recoating Time Per Layer:**

$$t_r = \frac{L}{v_r} = \frac{250}{100}$$

$$\boxed{t_r = 2.5 \;\text{s}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Insight:** Always ensure **unit consistency**. If $h$ is in μm, convert to mm before applying $Q_v = v_r w h$. Mixing units is the **#1 cause of mark loss** in KTU numericals.
