# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Part A questions test **recall and concise explanation**. Answers should be **3–4 sentences with a diagram** wherever possible.

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Explain the role of the recoater in Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) Additive Manufacturing.**

**Model Answer:**
The recoater in PBF is the **primary material delivery component** responsible for spreading a thin, uniform layer of powder across the build platform after each fused layer. It consists of a **hopper that meters powder** and a **counter-rotating roller or doctor blade** that traverses the build area at a controlled velocity $v_r$ (typically **50–300 mm/s**). The recoater must deliver a layer of precise thickness $h$ (typically **30–100 μm** for polymers, **20–40 μm** for metals) with high **uniformity and packing density**. Any inconsistency directly affects **surface roughness, dimensional accuracy, and porosity** of the final part.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any three material delivery mechanisms used in Additive Manufacturing and state one advantage of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Mechanism | Process Used | Advantage |
|---|---|---|
| Counter-rotating roller recoater | Powder Bed Fusion | High uniformity, repeatable layer thickness |
| Hobbed gear filament extruder | FDM / FFF | Precise volumetric control of molten polymer |
| Piezoelectric printhead | Material Jetting / Binder Jetting | Droplet volume down to 1 pL, multi-material capable |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Scheme mandates **internal choice** in Part B. Students must answer **either the full OR alternative question**. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### Question A `(14 Marks)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With a neat schematic, explain the working of a powder delivery and recoating system used in Selective Laser Melting (SLM). Discuss the role of layer thickness, recoater speed, and powder flowability.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A powder bed SLM machine operates with the following parameters: build platform length $L = 200$ mm, recoater velocity $v_r = 150$ mm/s, recoater width $w = 200$ mm, layer thickness $h = 30$ μm, and powder bulk density $\rho_{bulk} = 4.4$ g/cm³. Calculate:
(i) the volumetric deposition rate,
(ii) the mass deposition rate,
(iii) the recoating time per layer,
(iv) the total mass of powder consumed if the part has 1500 layers.**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Solution to Part (a)

> **Schematic (drawn by student):** A labeled diagram showing the **powder hopper on top, recoater roller sweeping horizontally, build platform below, laser source, and excess powder tray**.

**Working Explanation:**

1. **Powder Storage:** Metal powder (typically **Ti-6Al-4V, 316L stainless steel, or Inconel 718**) with particle size **15–45 μm** is stored in a hopper above the build chamber.

2. **Powder Metering:** A **vibratory feeder or gravity-fed slit** delivers a controlled quantity of powder to the recoater.

3. **Recoating:** A **hardened steel or ceramic roller** rotates opposite to the direction of travel, spreading powder evenly across the build platform as it lowers by one layer thickness (typically **20–40 μm**).

4. **Recoater Speed ($v_r$):** Typically **100–300 mm/s**. Higher speeds risk **non-uniform spreading and void formation**; lower speeds reduce throughput.

5. **Layer Thickness ($h$):** Smaller $h$ gives **better surface finish and resolution** but increases total build time. Common values: **20–40 μm**.

6. **Powder Flowability:** Measured by **Hall Flow rate (ASTM B213)** and **angle of repose**. Poor flowability causes **defects, lack-of-fusion porosity, and recoater streaking**.

7. **Atmosphere:** The entire process occurs under **inert gas (Argon)** to prevent oxidation.

---

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $L = 200$ mm
- $v_r = 150$ mm/s
- $w = 200$ mm
- $h = 30$ μm $= 0.030$ mm
- $\rho_{bulk} = 4.4$ g/cm³
- Number of layers $n = 1500$

**(i) Volumetric Deposition Rate:**

$$Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h = 150 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.030$$

$$\boxed{Q_v = 900 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} = 0.9 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Mass Deposition Rate:**

$$\dot{m} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot Q_v = 4.4 \cdot 0.9$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m} = 3.96 \;\text{g/s}}$$

**(iii) Recoating Time Per Layer:**

$$t_r = \frac{L}{v_r} = \frac{200}{150}$$

$$\boxed{t_r = 1.333 \;\text{s}}$$

**(iv) Total Mass of Powder Consumed for 1500 Layers:**

First, calculate volume of powder per layer (assuming it covers the entire build area $L \times w$):

$$V_{layer} = L \cdot w \cdot h = 200 \cdot 200 \cdot 0.030 = 1200 \;\text{mm}^3 = 1.2 \;\text{cm}^3$$

Total powder volume:

$$V_{total} = n \cdot V_{layer} = 1500 \cdot 1.2 = 1800 \;\text{cm}^3$$

Total powder mass:

$$m_{total} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot V_{total} = 4.4 \cdot 1800$$

$$\boxed{m_{total} = 7920 \;\text{g} = 7.92 \;\text{kg}}$$

---

### Question B `(14 Marks)` `(Alternative Choice)` `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the filament delivery mechanism in Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM). With a block diagram, describe the role of the extruder, hot end, and build platform in material delivery.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**In an FDM printer, a filament of diameter $d_f = 1.75$ mm is fed at velocity $v_f = 5$ mm/s. The filament density is $\rho_f = 1.24$ g/cm³ (PLA). Calculate:
(i) the volumetric flow rate of the filament,
(ii) the mass flow rate,
(iii) the volume of plastic consumed in 1 hour of continuous printing,
(iv) the mass consumed in the same hour.**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Solution to Part (a)

**Block Diagram:**

```
[Filament Spool] → [Guide Tube] → [Drive Gear + Stepper Motor]
                                      ↓
                              [Heated Hot End ~210°C]
                                      ↓
                          [Molten Filament Extrusion]
                                      ↓
                        [Build Platform + Cooling Fan]
```

**Explanation:**

1. **Filament Spool:** Holds the thermoplastic filament (typically **PLA, ABS, PETG, Nylon**) of diameter **1.75 mm or 2.85 mm**.

2. **Drive Mechanism:** A **stepper motor turns a hobbed gear** that grips and pushes the filament. Two configurations exist:
   - **Direct Drive:** Motor mounted directly above the hotend — high torque, suitable for flexible filaments.
   - **Bowden Setup:** Motor separated by a **PTFE tube** — lighter carriage, higher speeds.

3. **Hot End:** A **heated metal block** (typically containing a **40 W heater cartridge and thermistor**) melts the filament at **180–260°C** depending on material. The molten plastic is forced through a **brass or hardened steel nozzle** of diameter **0.2–1.0 mm**.

4. **Build Platform:** The extruded molten bead is deposited on a **heated bed (50–110°C)** to prevent warping. A **cooling fan** rapidly solidifies the deposited layer.

5. **Control:** The **feed velocity $v_f$** is controlled by the stepper motor, directly determining the **volumetric extrusion rate**.

---

#### Solution to Part (b)

**Given Data:**
- $d_f = 1.75$ mm
- $v_f = 5$ mm/s
- $\rho_f = 1.24$ g/cm³
- Time $t = 1$ hour $= 3600$ s

**(i) Volumetric Flow Rate of Filament:**

Cross-sectional area:

$$A_f = \frac{\pi d_f^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.75)^2}{4} = \frac{\pi \cdot 3.0625}{4} \approx 2.405 \;\text{mm}^2$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_f = A_f \cdot v_f = 2.405 \cdot 5$$

$$\boxed{Q_f \approx 12.03 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s}}$$

**(ii) Mass Flow Rate:**

Convert $Q_f$ to cm³/s: $12.03 \;\text{mm}^3/\text{s} = 0.01203 \;\text{cm}^3/\text{s}$

$$\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot Q_f = 1.24 \cdot 0.01203$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_f \approx 0.01492 \;\text{g/s}}$$

**(iii) Volume of Plastic Consumed in 1 Hour:**

$$V_{1h} = Q_f \cdot t = 12.03 \cdot 3600$$

$$\boxed{V_{1h} = 43{,}308 \;\text{mm}^3 \approx 43.31 \;\text{cm}^3}$$

**(iv) Mass Consumed in 1 Hour:**

$$m_{1h} = \dot{m}_f \cdot t = 0.01492 \cdot 3600$$

$$\boxed{m_{1h} \approx 53.7 \;\text{g}}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Material Delivery Questions:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Converting only some variables (e.g., keeping $h$ in μm while using mm for others) is the **#1 cause of wrong answers**. Always convert $h$ to mm before substitution.
> 2. **Confusing Packing Fraction:** Students often use $\rho_{true}$ instead of $\rho_{bulk}$ for powder bed calculations. **For powder deposition rate, use $\rho_{bulk}$.**
> 3. **Forgetting the Recoating Time:** When asked for "build time," many students forget that each layer requires both **recoating time + fusion/scan time**. KTU examiners specifically look for $t_{layer} = t_r + t_{scan}$.
> 4. **Ignoring the n-Layers Factor:** When asked for total powder consumed, always multiply by the **number of layers**.
> 5. **No Diagram in 7-Mark Answers:** A sub-part worth 7 marks **must include a labeled schematic**. Without it, expect a **1.5–2 mark deduction**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Material Delivery in AM**

- **Definition:** Material Delivery = controlled transport of feedstock (powder, filament, wire, liquid, sheet) to the build zone.
- **ASTM F2792-12** classifies AM processes into **7 categories**, each with a distinct delivery mechanism.
- **Powder Bed Recoating Equation:** $Q_v = v_r \cdot w \cdot h$
- **Mass Deposition Rate:** $\dot{m} = \rho_{bulk} \cdot v_r \cdot w \cdot h$
- **Recoating Time:** $t_r = L / v_r$
- **FDM Filament Flow:** $Q_f = (\pi d_f^2 / 4) \cdot v_f$
- **Mass Flow in FDM:** $\dot{m}_f = \rho_f \cdot (\pi d_f^2 / 4) \cdot v_f$
- **Packing Fraction:** $\phi = \rho_{bulk} / \rho_{true}$ — used to relate bulk and true densities.
- **Powder flowability** is measured by **Hall Flow rate (ASTM B213)** and **angle of repose**.
- **Layer thickness ranges:** PBF polymers **30–100 μm**, PBF metals **20–40 μm**.
- **Recoater velocity:** typically **50–300 mm/s**.
- **Filament diameters:** **1.75 mm** (most common) or **2.85 mm**.
- **Droplet volumes in jetting:** **1–80 picolitres**.
- **Build atmosphere for metal PBF:** **Inert gas (Argon)** to prevent oxidation.
- **Hot end temperature in FDM:** **180–260°C** depending on material.
- **Total build time** must account for $t_r$, $t_{scan}$, and number of layers $n$.
- **Always use consistent units** — convert μm to mm and mm³ to cm³ as needed.
- **Diagrams are mandatory** in 7-mark sub-parts; without them, expect mark deductions.
- **KTU Module 3 frequently tests:** classification of delivery systems, recoater working, numerical on $Q_v$ / $\dot{m}$ / $t_r$, and FDM extrusion.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS)** is a proprietary Directed Energy Deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process, classified under ASTM/ISO 52900 category **"Directed Energy Deposition" (DED)**, in which a high-power laser beam is used as the thermal energy source to create a molten pool on a substrate, while metallic powder (or wire) is simultaneously injected through coaxial or multi-jet nozzles into the melt pool. The part is built layer-by-layer under controlled atmosphere (typically argon or nitrogen) by rastering the laser-deposition head along numerically defined toolpaths, producing near-net-shape metallic components with full density (>99.5%).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LENS is the most commercially significant **powder-based DED** process. It must be distinguished from SLM/SLS (Powder Bed Fusion) — in LENS, powder is *blown* through nozzles, not spread as a bed. The process was originally developed by **Sandia National Laboratories, USA (1990s)** and commercialized by **Optomec Inc.** under the LENS® trademark.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "3D Welding Pen"

Imagine holding a high-tech **fountain pen that writes with molten metal**:

- Instead of ink, **fine metal powder** flows through the pen.
- Instead of gravity feeding, a **laser beam acts as the "heater"** that melts the powder the instant it touches the writing surface.
- As you move the pen (held by a robot arm), it leaves behind a **solid, welded metal trail** — layer by layer — building a 3D sculpture out of thin air.

That is exactly what LENS does — except the "pen" is a **deposition head mounted on a 5-axis CNC gantry or robotic arm**, moving with **micrometer precision**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key takeaway:** LENS = **Laser (heat)** + **Engineered (CNC-controlled)** + **Net Shaping (near-final geometry)**. The "Net Shaping" in the name emphasizes that LENS aims to produce parts requiring minimal post-machining.

### 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following physical constants govern LENS process physics:

- **Density of common LENS powders:** Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) ≈ **4.43 g/cm³**, Stainless Steel 316L ≈ **8.0 g/cm³**, Inconel 718 ≈ **8.19 g/cm³**.
- **Typical laser power range:** **100 W – 18 kW** (fiber lasers or Nd:YAG, now mostly 1 µm fiber-coupled diode lasers).
- **Typical build envelope:** **~300 mm × 300 mm × 400 mm** (Optomec 850-R); large systems reach **~900 mm × 1500 mm × 900 mm**.
- **Deposition rate:** **0.1 – 1.0 kg/h** (small nozzles) up to **>3 kg/h** (large coaxial nozzles).
- **Spot diameter of melt pool:** **0.5 – 5 mm**.
- **Layer thickness:** **0.1 – 1.0 mm**.
- **Chamber atmosphere:** Argon (O₂ < 50 ppm) or Nitrogen; oxygen-sensitive alloys (Ti) require **<10 ppm O₂**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why atmosphere control?** Reactive metals like titanium and aluminum form brittle oxides (TiO₂, Al₂O₃) that destroy mechanical properties. An **inert gas enclosure** is non-negotiable for these alloys in LENS.

### 1.4 Classification within Additive Manufacturing

LENS falls under the following taxonomy:

| ASTM/ISO 52900 Category | Sub-category | Energy Source | Feedstock |
|---|---|---|---|
| Directed Energy Deposition (DED) | Powder-based DED (LENS, DMD, DLF) | Laser | Powder |

It is distinct from:
- **PBF-LB/M (Selective Laser Melting)** — powder bed, not coaxial feed.
- **DED with wire (WAAM, EBF³)** — wire feed, not powder.
- **DED with electron beam (EBM-DED)** — vacuum + electron beam, not laser.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 LENS Process Architecture — Functional Breakdown

The LENS system consists of **five integrated subsystems**:

1. **Laser Source** — Fiber laser (1070 nm wavelength) or Nd:YAG; CW (continuous wave) operation preferred for stable melt pool.
2. **Powder Delivery System** — Multiple powder hoppers → auger/rotating-disk feeders → coaxial nozzle with argon carrier gas (typical carrier flow: 2–6 L/min).
3. **Deposition Head (Nozzle Assembly)** — Coaxial or multi-jet; focuses laser and converges powder streams into the melt pool.
4. **Motion System** — 3-axis to 5-axis CNC gantry **or** articulated robotic arm (e.g., Fanuc, KUKA).
5. **Atmosphere Control Chamber** — Sealed glove box flushed with Ar or N₂; O₂ sensor feedback loop.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The LENS process follows a deterministic 8-step sequence:

1. **CAD model import** → sliced into 2D layers by CAM software (e.g., Optomec CS, Materialise Magics).
2. **Path planning** — toolpath generation with hatch spacing, contour, and infill strategies.
3. **Chamber purge** — evacuate air, back-fill with argon until O₂ < 50 ppm.
4. **Substrate preheating** — laser scans substrate to remove moisture and reduce thermal gradients.
5. **Deposition cycle** (repeated per layer):
   - (a) Laser ON → forms melt pool on substrate/previous layer.
   - (b) Powder + carrier gas injected → particles impinge on melt pool, melt, solidify.
   - (c) Head rasters along X-Y path according to CNC G-code.
   - (d) Z-axis increments by layer thickness.
6. **In-process monitoring** — melt pool imaging (pyrometer + CMOS camera), IR thermography.
7. **Cool-down** — chamber remains sealed with inert gas flow until <100 °C.
8. **Post-processing** — stress relief (HIP — Hot Isostatic Pressing), CNC machining, surface finishing.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering insight:** The melt pool in LENS is **~3–10× larger** than in PBF-LB/M (which is typically 50–150 µm). This is why LENS parts have **coarser microstructures** but **higher deposition rates**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following equations are **exam-critical**. Memorize the energy-density formula and the mass-deposition relation.

| # | Parameter / Quantity | Symbol | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Volumetric Energy Density | $E_v$ | $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | J/mm³ | P = laser power, v = scan speed, h = hatch spacing, t = layer thickness |
| 2 | Linear Energy Density | $E_L$ | $E_L = \dfrac{P}{v}$ | J/mm | Energy per unit length of track |
| 3 | Powder Feed Rate | $\dot{m}_p$ | $\dot{m}_p = \rho_p \cdot A_{nozzle} \cdot v_p$ | g/s | $\rho_p$ = powder density, $v_p$ = gas-borne velocity |
| 4 | Mass Deposition Rate | $\dot{m}_{dep}$ | $\dot{m}_{dep} = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_p$ | g/s | $\eta$ = powder catchment efficiency (typically 30–70%) |
| 5 | Build Volume Rate | $\dot{V}_{build}$ | $\dot{V}_{build} = v \cdot h \cdot t$ | mm³/s | Material volume added per second |
| 6 | Dilution Ratio (cladding) | $D_r$ | $D_r = \dfrac{A_{melt-substrate}}{A_{melt-total}}$ | dimensionless | Critical for repair applications |
| 7 | Cooling Rate (approx.) | $\dot{T}$ | $\dot{T} \approx \dfrac{2\pi k(T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$ | K/s | Rosenthal-type estimate |
| 8 | Powder Mass per Layer | $m_L$ | $m_L = \rho_{bulk} \cdot A_{layer} \cdot t$ | g | $\rho_{bulk}$ = bulk density of powder stream on substrate |

> **Note on notation:** In the table above, all bars and dividers use LaTeX `\vert` or `\dfrac` to avoid breaking markdown table pipes.

### 2.4 The "Why" Behind Energy Density

The **volumetric energy density** $E_v$ is the single most important process parameter in LENS (and in PBF as well). It determines:

- **Low $E_v$ (< 40 J/mm³):** Lack-of-fusion porosity, weak inter-layer bonding.
- **Optimal $E_v$ (40–120 J/mm³):** Dense (>99.5%), well-bonded tracks.
- **High $E_v$ (> 150 J/mm³):** Keyhole porosity, vaporization, balling, excessive dilution.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb:** $E_v$ between **60 – 100 J/mm³** is the "sweet spot" for most LENS processing of Ti-6Al-4V and Inconel 718.

### 2.5 Real-World Utility of LENS

LENS is the **workhorse of metal AM for large parts and repairs**:

- **Aerospace:** Ti-6Al-4V structural brackets, blisks (blade-integrated disks), Inconel 718 turbine casings.
- **Defense:** Refurbishment of damaged turbine blades on F-16/F-18 engines (Optomec holds major USAF contracts).
- **Medical:** Custom porous titanium implants (acetabular cups, spinal cages).
- **Tooling:** Inconel/Stellite cladding of extrusion screws and dies for wear resistance.
- **Energy:** Repair of nuclear valve bodies, steam turbine blades.
- **Hybrid manufacturing:** LENS head mounted on a CNC mill — additive head builds near-net shape, milling head finishes critical features in one setup.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density $E_v$

**Starting principle:** Energy density is defined as the laser energy *deposited per unit volume of material*.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered per unit time:**
The laser emits energy at rate $P$ (Watts). In time $dt$, energy delivered is:

$$dE = P \, dt$$

**Step 2 — Distance traveled in time $dt$ at scan speed $v$:**

$$dx = v \, dt$$

**Step 3 — Volume deposited during one track pass:**

Consider a single scan track of width $h$ (hatch spacing) and depth $t$ (layer thickness). As the laser moves a distance $dx$, it deposits material of volume:

$$dV = h \cdot t \cdot dx$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $dx = v \, dt$:**

$$dV = h \cdot t \cdot v \, dt$$

**Step 5 — Form the energy-to-volume ratio:**

$$E_v = \frac{dE}{dV} = \frac{P \, dt}{h \cdot t \cdot v \, dt}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $dt$:**

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

> **Dimensional check:** $\dfrac{\text{W}}{(\text{mm/s}) \cdot \text{mm} \cdot \text{mm}} = \dfrac{\text{J/s}}{\text{mm}^3/\text{s}} = \dfrac{\text{J}}{\text{mm}^3}$ ✓

### 3.2 Derivation of Powder Catchment Efficiency $\eta$

**Step 1 — Total powder mass delivered:**

$$\dot{m}_p = \dot{m}_{carrier} \cdot \frac{\rho_p}{\rho_{carrier,bulk}}$$

**Step 2 — Mass reaching the melt pool (Gaussian distribution on substrate):**

The powder stream forms a Gaussian footprint with most particles overshooting the melt pool. Only the central portion is caught:

$$\dot{m}_{dep} = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_p$$

**Step 3 — Empirical form of $\eta$ for coaxial nozzles:**

$$\eta = \eta_0 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{d_{spot}^2}{2 \sigma^2}\right)$$

where $\eta_0 \approx 0.85$ (ideal), $\sigma$ is the Gaussian width, and $d_{spot}$ is the melt-pool diameter.

**Step 4 — Typical value in practice:**

For $d_{spot} = 1$ mm and $\sigma = 1.2$ mm:

$$\eta = 0.85 \cdot \exp\left(-\frac{1.0}{2 \cdot 1.44}\right) = 0.85 \cdot \exp(-0.347) = 0.85 \cdot 0.707 \approx 0.60$$

> **Result:** About **60% catchment efficiency** is realistic for well-aligned coaxial nozzles.

### 3.3 Derivation of Effective Cooling Rate (Rosenthal Approximation)

For a moving point source on a semi-infinite plate:

$$T - T_0 = \frac{P}{2\pi k R}$$

where $R = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 + z^2}$. At the solid-liquid interface, the cooling rate along the trailing edge is:

$$\dot{T} = -\frac{2\pi k (T_m - T_0)^2}{P}$$

**Step-by-step substitution for Ti-6Al-4V:**

Using $T_m = 1660$ °C, $T_0 = 25$ °C, $k \approx 7$ W/(m·K), $P = 500$ W:

$$\dot{T} = \frac{2\pi \cdot 7 \cdot (1635)^2}{500}$$

$$\dot{T} = \frac{14\pi \cdot 2.67 \times 10^6}{500} = \frac{1.175 \times 10^8}{500}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{T} \approx 2.35 \times 10^5 \text{ K/s}}$$

> **Interpretation:** Cooling rates of $\sim 10^4$–$10^5$ K/s produce **fine martensitic α′** in Ti-6Al-4V, which requires subsequent HIP + solution treatment to convert to equilibrium α+β.

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A LENS system deposits Ti-6Al-4V with the following parameters:
- Laser power: $P = 800$ W
- Scan speed: $v = 10$ mm/s
- Hatch spacing: $h = 0.5$ mm
- Layer thickness: $t = 0.3$ mm
- Powder density: $\rho_p = 4.43$ g/cm³
- Powder feed rate: $\dot{m}_p = 0.4$ g/s
- Catchment efficiency: $\eta = 0.65$

**Find:**
(i) Volumetric energy density $E_v$.
(ii) Mass deposition rate $\dot{m}_{dep}$.
(iii) Build time for a part of volume $V = 12{,}000$ mm³ (assume 100% dense part).

**Solution:**

**(i) Volumetric energy density:**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{800}{10 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.3}$$

$$E_v = \frac{800}{1.5} = 533 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

Wait — this exceeds the safe upper bound! Let me recheck. Hmm, the issue is that $P/v$ alone gives $E_L = 80$ J/mm which is normal; dividing by $h \cdot t = 0.15$ mm² gives **533 J/mm³**. But the **published optimal range for Ti-6Al-4V in LENS is ~60–100 J/mm³**. The numerical is artificially high — the correct interpretation is that the input values are illustrative; in reality either $P$ is lower, $v$ higher, or $h \cdot t$ larger. For the exam, we report the **computed value and comment on its process-physics implication**.

**Reported answer:**
$$E_v = 533.3 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Comment:** This value is high — keyhole porosity likely; recommend reducing $P$ to ~150 W or increasing $v$ to 50 mm/s.

**(ii) Mass deposition rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{dep} = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_p = 0.65 \cdot 0.4 = 0.26 \text{ g/s}$$

**(iii) Build time:**

Mass of part:

$$m = \rho_p \cdot V = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3 \cdot 12{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 53.16 \text{ g}$$

Build time:

$$t_{build} = \frac{m}{\dot{m}_{dep}} = \frac{53.16}{0.26} = 204.5 \text{ s} \approx 3.4 \text{ min}$$

> **Note:** Real-world build time is much higher due to inter-pass cooling, contour scanning, and chamber purge — typical efficiency factor **0.3–0.5**.

Adjusted realistic build time:
$$t_{build,real} = \frac{3.4 \text{ min}}{0.4} \approx 8.5 \text{ min}$$

### 3.5 Comparative Analytical Matrix — LENS vs Other Metal AM Processes

| Parameter | LENS (DED-powder) | SLM (PBF-LB/M) | EBM (PBF-EB) | WAAM (DED-wire) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Fiber laser | Fiber laser | Electron beam | Arc / laser |
| Atmosphere | Inert gas chamber | Inert gas chamber | Vacuum | Inert shielding |
| Min. feature size | ~1.0 mm | ~0.1 mm | ~0.3 mm | ~3.0 mm |
| Surface roughness Ra | 10–30 µm | 5–20 µm | 10–25 µm | 50–200 µm |
| Deposition rate | 0.1–1.0 kg/h | 0.02–0.1 kg/h | 0.05–0.2 kg/h | 1–10 kg/h |
| Build volume | Medium (300×300×400) | Small (250×250×350) | Small–Medium | Very large (>1 m) |
| Materials | Ti, Inconel, SS, Tool steel | Wide metals | Ti, some alloys | Steels, Ti, Al |
| Residual stress | High | Very high | Low | Very high |
| Typical $E_v$ range (J/mm³) | 40–120 | 50–150 | 30–80 | 5–30 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — LENS Process Architecture

```mermaid
    A[CAD Model .STL] --> B[CAD Slicer Software]
    B --> C[G-code / CL-File Toolpath]
    C --> D[CNC Controller / Robot Teach Pendant]

    subgraph A_CHAMBER[Inert Chamber Ar or N2 Atmosphere]
        D --> E[5-Axis Motion System Gantry or Robotic Arm]
        E --> F[LENS Deposition Head]
        subgraph A_HEAD[Deposition Head Internal]
            F1[Optical Assembly Collimator Lens Focusing Lens] --> F2[Coaxial Powder Nozzle]
        end
        F --> F1
        F2 --> G[Powder Inlet from Hoppers]
    end

    subgraph A_SOURCE[Exterior Sources]
        H[High Power Fiber Laser 1kW to 18kW 1070nm] -->|Beam Delivery Fiber| F1
        I[Powder Hopper 1] -->|Carrier Gas Ar| G
        J[Powder Hopper 2] -->|Optional Multi-Material| G
        K[Gas Cabinet Ar N2 Supply] -->|Purge and Shielding| A_CHAMBER
        L[Closed Loop O2 Sensor] -->|Feedback to K| A_CHAMBER

    end

    F --> N[Melt Pool on Substrate]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — LENS Process Sequence (Layer-by-Layer)

```mermaid
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Chamber Purge]

    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Inject Powder via Coaxial Nozzle]

    S9 -->|Yes| S11[Step 10: Cool Down in Inert Gas]

    style S1 fill:#f9d5e5

```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Energy Density Decision Tree

```mermaid

    Calc --> EvLow{Ev less than 40 J per mm3?}

    Calc --> EvOpt{Ev between 40 and 120?}
    EvOpt -->|Yes| A2[OPTIMAL Dense Part greater than 99.5 percent Fine Microstructure]
    Calc --> EvHigh{Ev greater than 150?}
```


<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper KTU]**
*Define Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS). List any four advantages of LENS over conventional manufacturing.*

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

LENS is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** additive manufacturing process developed by Sandia National Laboratories and commercialized by Optomec Inc., in which a high-power laser melts metallic powder fed through a coaxial nozzle to build fully dense parts layer-by-layer.

**Four advantages of LENS:**
1. **Near-net-shape** manufacturing with minimal material waste.
2. **Multi-material capability** — multiple powder hoppers can feed different alloys simultaneously (e.g., functionally graded materials).
3. **Large build envelope** and ability to **repair existing parts** (blades, dies) without re-machining from solid.
4. **High deposition rate** (0.1–3 kg/h) compared to PBF processes.
5. Reduced buy-to-fly ratio in aerospace (e.g., titanium blisks).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Distinguish between Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) and Directed Energy Deposition (DED) with respect to powder delivery, energy source, and typical build volume.*

**Model Answer (6 lines table format):**

| Aspect | PBF (e.g., SLM) | DED (LENS) |
|---|---|---|
| Powder Delivery | Pre-spread bed (recoater) | Coaxial nozzle injection |
| Energy Source | Laser (fiber, Nd:YAG) | Laser (fiber) or e-beam |
| Typical Build Volume | 250 × 250 × 350 mm | 300 × 300 × 400 mm and larger |
| Min. Feature | ~0.1 mm | ~1.0 mm |
| Atmosphere | Inert chamber | Inert chamber or local shielding |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!NOTE]
> As per KTU 2024 ESE regulation, students must answer **one full question** of 14 marks from the given choice.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of LENS**. Discuss the role of the coaxial nozzle and atmosphere control. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **process parameters of LENS** and their influence on the build quality. Derive the volumetric energy density formula. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Working Principle (7 marks):**

LENS works on the principle of **laser-induced localized melting** combined with **coaxial powder injection**.

- **Step 1:** A high-power fiber laser (typically 1–18 kW, wavelength 1070 nm) is delivered through an optical fiber to the deposition head.
- **Step 2:** The laser is focused through a collimating-focusing lens assembly to create a **small spot (0.5–5 mm diameter)** on the substrate, generating a controlled melt pool (~1500–3000 °C depending on alloy).
- **Step 3:** Metal powder from one or more hoppers is metered by a precision feeder and entrained in **argon carrier gas (2–6 L/min)**.
- **Step 4:** The powder stream is injected through a **coaxial nozzle** — typically 4 powder jets arranged symmetrically around the central laser axis at 30°/90°/150°/270° — converging into the melt pool.
- **Step 5:** The deposition head moves under CNC/robotic control in X-Y, depositing a track. After one layer, Z increments and the next layer is deposited.
- **Role of coaxial nozzle:** Ensures powder converges at the **same focal point as the laser**, independent of head orientation — enables **5-axis deposition on curved substrates**.
- **Role of atmosphere control:** Reactive metals (Ti, Al) oxidize rapidly above 400 °C. The sealed chamber is purged to **O₂ < 50 ppm** (often < 10 ppm for Ti), and an oxygen sensor closes the loop.

**(b) Process Parameters (7 marks):**

The key LENS process parameters are:

1. **Laser Power (P):** Higher P increases melt-pool size and depth. Excess P → keyhole porosity; low P → lack of fusion.
2. **Scan Speed (v):** Faster scan → smaller melt pool, higher cooling rate → finer grains but possibly weaker bonding.
3. **Hatch Spacing (h):** Smaller h → better overlap → higher density but lower throughput.
4. **Layer Thickness (t):** Smaller t → better resolution, more remelting per layer → lower porosity.
5. **Powder Feed Rate ($\dot{m}_p$):** Insufficient powder → porous tracks; excess powder → unmelted particles.
6. **Oxygen content:** Even 100 ppm O₂ embrittles Ti welds.
7. **Carrier gas flow rate:** Affects powder focus and shielding.

**Derivation of Volumetric Energy Density (already shown in Section 3.1):**

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

**Optimal window:** $E_v$ between **40 – 120 J/mm³** for most engineering alloys.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the **classification of LENS under the ASTM/ISO 52900 standard** and differentiate LENS from Selective Laser Melting (SLM). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LENS machine deposits **Inconel 718** with the following parameters:
- $P = 600$ W, $v = 15$ mm/s, $h = 0.4$ mm, $t = 0.25$ mm, $\dot{m}_p = 0.3$ g/s, $\eta = 0.7$, $\rho_p = 8.19$ g/cm³.

Calculate:
1. Volumetric energy density $E_v$.
2. Mass deposition rate $\dot{m}_{dep}$.
3. Build time for a part of volume **20,000 mm³**.
4. Comment on whether the $E_v$ value is in the optimum range. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification & Comparison (7 marks):**

Under **ASTM/ISO 52900:2021**, additive manufacturing processes are classified into **seven categories**:

1. Material Extrusion (MEX)
2. Vat Photopolymerization (VPP)
3. Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) — subdivided into PBF-LB (laser), PBF-EB (electron beam)
4. Directed Energy Deposition (DED) — subdivided into DED-LB (laser-based, includes LENS) and DED-Arc (wire-arc)
5. Material Jetting (MJT)
6. Binder Jetting (BJT)
7. Sheet Lamination (SHL)

LENS belongs to **DED-LB** — Directed Energy Deposition using a laser with powder feedstock.

**Differences LENS vs SLM:**

| Aspect | LENS | SLM |
|---|---|---|
| Process family | DED | PBF |
| Powder delivery | Coaxial nozzle | Recoater-spread bed |
| Build chamber | Inert gas chamber | Inert gas chamber |
| Layer thickness | 0.1–1 mm | 0.02–0.1 mm |
| Minimum feature | ~1 mm | ~0.1 mm |
| Deposition rate | High (up to 3 kg/h) | Low (0.02–0.1 kg/h) |
| Multi-material | Yes (multi-hopper) | Limited |
| Repair capability | Excellent | Poor |
| Surface roughness | High (Ra 10–30 µm) | Low (Ra 5–20 µm) |

**(b) Numerical (7 marks):**

**(1) Volumetric energy density:**

$$E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t} = \frac{600}{15 \cdot 0.4 \cdot 0.25} = \frac{600}{1.5} = \boxed{400 \text{ J/mm}^3}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mistake #1:** Many students forget to multiply $h$ and $t$ together first. Always compute the denominator explicitly: $v \cdot h \cdot t = 15 \times 0.4 \times 0.25 = 1.5$ mm³/s. Marks lost if dimensional analysis is skipped.

**(2) Mass deposition rate:**

$$\dot{m}_{dep} = \eta \cdot \dot{m}_p = 0.7 \cdot 0.3 = \boxed{0.21 \text{ g/s}}$$

**(3) Build time:**

Part mass:
$$m = \rho_p \cdot V = 8.19 \times 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3 \cdot 20{,}000 \text{ mm}^3 = 163.8 \text{ g}$$

Theoretical build time:
$$t_{ideal} = \frac{m}{\dot{m}_{dep}} = \frac{163.8}{0.21} = \boxed{780 \text{ s} \approx 13 \text{ min}}$$

Realistic build time (with idle/cooldown factor ~0.4):
$$t_{real} = \frac{13}{0.4} = \boxed{32.5 \text{ min}}$$

**(4) Comment on $E_v$:**

The calculated $E_v = 400$ J/mm³ is **significantly above the optimum range** (40–120 J/mm³). Expected defects:
- **Keyhole porosity** due to excessive vaporization.
- **Excessive dilution** with substrate.
- **Elemental loss** (Mn, Cr burn-off in Inconel).

**Recommendation:** Reduce $P$ to ~150 W or increase $v$ to ~60 mm/s to bring $E_v$ into the 60–100 J/mm³ safe window.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LENS:**

- ✅ LENS = **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** process; laser + coaxial powder feed; ASTM/ISO 52900 category **DED-LB**.
- ✅ Developed by **Sandia National Labs (1990s)**; commercialized by **Optomec Inc.**
- ✅ Required atmosphere: **Argon/Nitrogen with O₂ < 50 ppm** (often < 10 ppm for titanium).
- ✅ Five subsystems: **Laser, Powder Feeder, Deposition Head, Motion System, Atmosphere Chamber**.
- ✅ Energy density formula (memorize): $E_v = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ — units **J/mm³**.
- ✅ Optimal $E_v$ window: **40 – 120 J/mm³**; below → lack of fusion; above → keyhole porosity.
- ✅ Powder catchment efficiency $\eta$: typically **0.3 – 0.7** for coaxial nozzles.
- ✅ Cooling rate estimate (Rosenthal): $\dot{T} \sim 10^4$–$10^5$ K/s.
- ✅ Layer thickness: **0.1 – 1.0 mm** (much larger than PBF).
- ✅ Deposition rate: **0.1 – 3 kg/h** (much higher than PBF).
- ✅ **LENS vs SLM:** LENS is for **large parts, repairs, multi-material, high deposition**; SLM is for **fine features, intricate geometry, high accuracy**.
- ✅ **Applications:** Aerospace blisks (Ti-6Al-4V), turbine repair, biomedical implants, tooling, nuclear valve repair, hybrid CNC-LENS.
- ✅ **Multi-material / functionally graded materials (FGM):** Possible by feeding different powders from multiple hoppers through a single coaxial head.
- ✅ **Post-processing mandatory:** Stress relief, **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** at ~920 °C / 100 MPa for Inconel, CNC finish machining.
- ✅ **Microstructure:** Columnar prior-β grains along build direction in LENS Ti-6Al-4V (due to directional heat flow); needs HIP + heat treatment for fatigue-critical parts.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Marks Are Lost:**
> 1. Confusing LENS with SLM (powder bed fusion).
> 2. Wrong unit on energy density (must be **J/mm³**, NOT J/mm or W/mm²).
> 3. Forgetting the catchment-efficiency factor $\eta$ in mass-deposition calculations.
> 4. Not commenting on process physics — always say whether computed $E_v$ is in optimum range.
> 5. Missing the role of inert atmosphere for titanium.
> 6. Forgetting the conversion: $1 \text{ g/cm}^3 = 10^{-3} \text{ g/mm}^3$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)** is a **Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** additive manufacturing process defined by ASTM/ISO 52900 (Standard Terminology for AM) as a process in which **focused thermal energy** (typically a **laser beam**, **electron beam**, or **plasma/electric arc**) is used to **fuse materials** by melting them as they are being deposited, layer upon layer, onto a substrate from a **coaxial or multi-jet nozzle** that simultaneously delivers metal in **powder or wire form**.

In KTU 2024 scheme terminology, DMD is categorized under the **"Directed Energy Deposition"** family (ISO/ASTM 52900 Process Category **DED**), distinct from Powder Bed Fusion (PBF) processes like SLM and DMLS. The trademarked variants include **Laser Metal Deposition (LMD)**, **Laser Engineered Net Shaping (LENS®)**, and **Electron Beam Additive Manufacturing (EBAM®)**.

### 1.2 Process Taxonomy (KTU 2024 Classification)

| Energy Source | Feedstock Form | Commercial Name |
|---------------|----------------|-----------------|
| Laser | Powder | LMD, LENS, DMD |
| Laser | Wire | LMD-W, Wire-arc AM |
| Electron Beam | Wire/Plasma | EBAM, EBF³ |
| Plasma Arc | Powder/Wire | PA-DED, WAAM |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Precision Welding Pen"

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine a high-tech, computer-controlled welding pen that "draws" a 3D metal part in thin air.**
>
> - The **laser beam** is the **invisible flame** at the pen's tip — a focused beam of light hot enough (often **> 3000 °C**) to instantly melt any metal it touches.
> - The **metal powder (or wire)** is the **"ink"** — sprayed coaxially with the laser so it lands precisely where the laser is melting.
> - As the head moves along a path defined by a **CNC gantry or robotic arm**, it leaves behind a tiny, solidified bead of metal — much like icing a cake in 3D.
> - When one layer is done, the head lifts up by the thickness of one layer (**typically 0.25–1 mm**) and draws the next layer on top.
> - The whole build happens inside a sealed chamber flooded with **inert gas (Argon)** to keep oxygen and moisture out — otherwise the molten metal would oxidize and become brittle.
>
> The end result? A fully dense, near-net-shape metal component built **without any tooling, molds, or fixtures**, layer by layer, straight from a 3D CAD file.

## 1.4 Defining Physical & Process Metrics

The following standard metrics govern DMD performance and are **frequently tested in KTU ESE**:

- **Laser Power (P):** **0.1 kW to 10+ kW** (most industrial systems: 1–4 kW fiber laser)
- **Scan / Traverse Speed (v):** **5 to 1500 mm/min** (typical: 200–800 mm/min)
- **Powder Feed Rate (ṁ):** **1 to 30 g/min** per nozzle
- **Beam Diameter (d):** **0.5 mm to 6 mm** (typical: 2–4 mm)
- **Layer Thickness (t):** **0.1 mm to 2 mm** (typical: 0.25–0.5 mm)
- **Hatch Spacing (h):** **0.3 mm to 5 mm** (typical: 0.6–1.2 mm)
- **Standoff Distance:** **5 mm to 25 mm** (nozzle-to-substrate)
- **Build Envelope:** **Up to 1.5 m × 1.5 m × 1.5 m** (much larger than PBF)
- **Deposition Rate:** **0.05 to 0.5 kg/h** (laser-powder), up to **2–10 kg/h** (wire-arc)
- **Oxygen (O₂) Level in Chamber:** **< 50 ppm** (often < 10 ppm for reactive alloys like Ti, Al)
- **Deposition Efficiency:** **60%–95%** (powder not melted = waste)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The DMD process follows a strict **closed-loop sequence** governed by synchronized sub-systems:

### Step 1 — **CAD Slicing & Path Planning**
- The 3D CAD model is sliced into **2D contours per layer** of thickness *t*.
- A **scan strategy** is generated: unidirectional, bidirectional, spiral, or contour-parallel.
- For multi-axis systems, **5-axis toolpaths** allow deposition on curved/overhang surfaces.

### Step 2 — **Beam–Powder Coupling at the Melt Pool**
- A **fiber-coupled laser** (wavelength ~ **1064 nm** for metals) is delivered through the central axis of the deposition head.
- Metal powder is entrained in a **carrier gas (Argon, 1–5 L/min)** and injected through **2–4 annular nozzles** converging at the focal point.
- The laser and powder streams intersect at the substrate, creating a **moving melt pool** of dimensions roughly **1–3 mm wide** and **0.3–1 mm deep**.

### Step 3 — **Solidification Microstructure Formation**
- Solidification occurs at velocities **~ 10–100 mm/s**, producing **directional columnar grains** aligned with the heat-flow direction (anti-parallel to build).
- **Cooling rates**: **10³ to 10⁵ K/s** — much faster than casting, leading to refined microstructures.
- This results in **yield strength typically 10–20% higher** than the wrought equivalent.

### Step 4 — **Layer Stacking & Inter-Layer Bonding**
- After one layer is complete, the Z-axis lifts by *t*, and the next layer is deposited.
- The previous layer is **re-melted (dilution zone)**, creating a **metallurgical bond** (not just mechanical adhesion).
- **Inter-layer temperature** is monitored (often via **pyrometer**) to control residual stress.

### Step 5 — **Closed-Loop Process Control**
- **Melt-pool monitoring** (CCD camera, photodiode, or thermal imaging at ~1 kHz) provides feedback.
- Power, feed rate, and travel speed are **PID-controlled in real time** to maintain constant melt-pool size.
- Modern systems use **AI/ML-based controllers** to compensate for thermal accumulation.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" — Physical Justification

| Question | Engineering Rationale |
|----------|----------------------|
| **Why use a laser instead of just an arc?** | Laser gives a **small, well-defined melt pool** → finer features, less HAZ, better dimensional accuracy. |
| **Why coaxial powder delivery?** | Ensures powder is delivered **regardless of build direction** → enables **multi-axis / 5-axis deposition**. |
| **Why inert gas (Argon) shrouding?** | Prevents **oxidation & nitridation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg, Ni-superalloys). |
| **Why is DMD faster than SLM?** | DMD uses **continuous powder/wire feed** across an open envelope, whereas SLM scans a point-by-point powder bed. DMD can achieve **~10–100× higher deposition rates** for large parts. |
| **Why is DMD's surface rougher than SLM?** | DMD's **layer thickness is ~5–10× larger** (0.25–1 mm vs. 0.02–0.08 mm in SLM), so staircase effects dominate. Typical **Ra = 10–30 µm** for DMD vs. **5–15 µm** for SLM. |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these equations — they are the most-tested DMD formulas in KTU ESE and Module Tests.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|-----------------|----------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{P}{v \cdot d \cdot t}$ | $E$ = Volumetric Energy Density, $P$ = Laser Power, $v$ = Scan Speed, $d$ = Beam Dia., $t$ = Layer Thickness | $J/mm^3$ | Predicts **lack-of-fusion** if $E < E_{min}$ and **keyholing/evaporation** if $E > E_{max}$ |
| 2 | $E_s = \dfrac{P}{\dot{m}}$ | $E_s$ = Specific Energy, $\dot{m}$ = Mass Feed Rate | $J/g$ | **Energy per gram** of deposited metal — directly affects dilution and porosity |
| 3 | $VDR = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot v$ | $VDR$ = Volumetric Deposition Rate | $mm^3/s$ | Theoretical **max build speed** assuming 100% capture |
| 4 | $MDR = VDR \cdot \rho$ | $MDR$ = Mass Deposition Rate, $\rho$ = Material Density | $g/s$ | Real-world **throughput** in kg/h |
| 5 | $AR = \dfrac{D}{W}$ | $AR$ = Aspect Ratio, $D$ = Depth of melt pool, $W$ = Width | dimensionless | **Process stability**: $AR < 0.6$ ensures conduction mode; $AR > 0.6$ risks keyhole porosity |
| 6 | $\eta_{dep} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_{deposited}}{\dot{m}_{fed}} \times 100\%$ | $\eta_{dep}$ = Deposition Efficiency | % | Powder **utilization** (typically 60–95%) |
| 7 | $D_{dil} = \dfrac{A_{sub}}{A_{total}} \times 100\%$ | $D_{dil}$ = Dilution % | % | Fraction of substrate melted into the deposit (typical 5–25%) |
| 8 | $h_{conv} = 2.5 \cdot (P \cdot v)^{0.33}$ | $h_{conv}$ = Convective heat-transfer coefficient (empirical for DMD) | $W/(m^2 \cdot K)$ | Used in **FEM thermal modeling** of DMD |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Aerospace (Ti-6Al-4V):** Blisks, turbine blade repair, structurally optimized brackets.
- **Automotive:** Custom hydraulic manifolds, bimetallic engine valves.
- **Oil & Gas:** Hard-facing of drill bits, Inconel overlays on corrosion-prone components.
- **Marine & Defense:** Large aluminum-steel transition pieces, hull section repair.
- **Medical (Biomedical):** Patient-specific **Ti implants** with controlled porosity.
- **MRO (Maintenance, Repair, Overhaul):** Direct deposition onto worn parts — **a market worth over USD 1 billion** as of 2024.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Volumetric Energy Density from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> **Goal:** Derive the canonical DMD energy density equation $E = P / (v \cdot d \cdot t)$ from the definition of energy per unit volume.

**Step 1 — Definition of Volumetric Energy Density**

Volumetric energy density is defined as the **laser energy delivered per unit volume of material processed**.

$$E = \frac{E_{delivered}}{V_{processed}}$$

Where the delivered energy over a time interval $T$ is:

$$E_{delivered} = P \cdot T$$

**Step 2 — Geometry of the Processed Volume**

Consider the **laser traversing a single linear track** of length $L$ at scan speed $v$, with beam diameter $d$ and layer thickness $t$. The time taken is:

$$T = \frac{L}{v}$$

The volume of material melted along this track (approximated as a rectangular box for simplicity) is:

$$V_{processed} = L \cdot d \cdot t$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into the Energy Density Equation**

$$E = \frac{P \cdot T}{V_{processed}} = \frac{P \cdot \dfrac{L}{v}}{L \cdot d \cdot t}$$

**Step 4 — Cancel Common Terms**

The length $L$ appears in both numerator and denominator:

$$E = \frac{P}{v \cdot d \cdot t}$$

> This is the **canonical DMD energy density equation**, expressing **Joules per cubic millimetre** of metal deposited.

**Step 5 — Unit Verification**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left[ E \right] &= \frac{[W]}{[mm/s] \cdot [mm] \cdot [mm]} \\
&= \frac{J/s}{mm^3 / s} = \frac{J}{mm^3}
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ Units check out.

**Step 6 — Practical Bounds for Ti-6Al-4V (KTU-Favorite Material)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{min} &\approx 30 \; J/mm^3 \quad (\text{lack-of-fusion threshold}) \\
E_{opt} &\approx 60{-}100 \; J/mm^3 \quad (\text{conduction mode, low porosity}) \\
E_{max} &\approx 150{-}200 \; J/mm^3 \quad (\text{keyholing / vaporization})
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Deposition Rate (MDR) from Volumetric Rate

**Step 1 — Start with Volumetric Deposition Rate**

Assume a **cylindrical track** of diameter $d$ being deposited at speed $v$. The cross-section is circular:

$$A = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2$$

The volumetric rate is therefore:

$$VDR = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot v$$

**Step 2 — Convert Volume Rate to Mass Rate**

Using density $\rho$:

$$MDR = VDR \cdot \rho = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot v \cdot \rho$$

**Step 3 — Practical Example (Typical DMD, Ti-6Al-4V)**

Given: $d = 3 \; mm$, $v = 600 \; mm/min = 10 \; mm/s$, $\rho_{Ti} = 4.43 \; g/cm^3 = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \; g/mm^3$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
VDR &= \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (3)^2 \cdot 10 \\
&= 0.7854 \cdot 9 \cdot 10 \\
&= 70.69 \; mm^3/s
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
MDR &= 70.69 \cdot 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \\
&= 0.313 \; g/s \\
&= 1.13 \; kg/h
\end{aligned}
$$

✅ This matches real-world DMD specifications (typical 0.5–2 kg/h for laser-powder DMD).

## 3.3 Derivation: Aspect Ratio Stability Criterion

**Step 1 — Heat Balance at the Melt Pool**

The depth $D$ of the melt pool is approximately proportional to $P / (v \cdot k)$, where $k$ is thermal conductivity. The width $W$ is approximately equal to the beam diameter $d$ (with some spread).

$$
D \approx \frac{P}{v \cdot k \cdot d}, \qquad W \approx d
$$

**Step 2 — Aspect Ratio Definition**

$$
AR = \frac{D}{W} \approx \frac{P}{v \cdot k \cdot d^2}
$$

**Step 3 — Stability Criterion**

For **conduction-mode (stable) melting**, the aspect ratio must satisfy:

$$
AR < 0.6
$$

Above this threshold, **vaporization of metal** occurs at the bottom of the pool, creating a **vapor capillary (keyhole)**. This traps gas bubbles during solidification, producing **keyhole porosity** — a common KTU exam question topic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conducting mode** = wide, shallow pool → sound, low-porosity deposits.
> **Keyhole mode** = deep, narrow pool → entrapment of gas → porosity defects.
> DMD prefers **conduction mode**; the transition occurs at $AR \approx 0.6$.

## 3.4 Analytical Process-Parameter Matrix (KTU High-Yield)

| Parameter ↑ | Effect on Energy Density $E$ | Effect on Dilution | Effect on Porosity | Effect on Build Rate | Risk |
|-------------|------------------------------|--------------------|--------------------|----------------------|------|
| **Power $P$ ↑** | ↑ | ↑ | ↓ (initially) | ↑ | Evaporation if too high |
| **Scan Speed $v$ ↑** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (lack-of-fusion) | ↓ | Unmelted regions |
| **Layer Thickness $t$ ↑** | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | ↑ | Poor inter-layer bonding |
| **Beam Dia. $d$ ↑** | ↓ | ↓ | ↑ | ↑ | Loss of resolution |
| **Powder Feed $\dot{m}$ ↑** | $E_s$ ↓ | ↓ | ↑ (gas entrapment) | ↑ | Incomplete melting |
| **Hatch Spacing $h$ ↑** | (No direct $E$ effect) | — | ↑ (gaps between tracks) | ↓ | Track overlap loss |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: DMD Process Architecture (Functional Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CAD[CAD Model 3D] --> SL[Slicer Software]
    SL --> TP[Toolpath Generation G-code]
    TP --> CTRL[Process Controller PLC]
    CTRL --> LS[Laser Source 1064 nm Fiber Laser]
    CTRL --> PF[Powder Feeder 1-4 hoppers]
    CTRL --> CNCR[CNCRobot 5-axis motion]
    LS --> DH[Deposition Head Coaxial Nozzle]
    PF --> DH
    CNCR --> DH
    DH --> MP[Melt Pool on Substrate]
    MP --> SLD[Solidified Track Layer n]
    SLD --> NL[Next Layer n+1]
    MON[Melt Pool Monitor Pyrometer + CCD] --> CTRL
    GAS[Inert Gas Supply Argon O2 less than 50 ppm] --> DH
    GAS --> CH[Sealed Build Chamber]
    CH --> SLD
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: DMD Process Flow (Sequential)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Start CAD File STL] --> B[Slice into Layers thickness t]
    B --> C[Generate Scan Path]
    C --> D{Purge Chamber with Argon}
    D -- O2 less than 50 ppm --> E[Pre-heat Substrate Optional]
    E --> F[Ignite Laser + Open Powder Valve]
    F --> G[Deposit Layer n melt + solidify]
    G --> H{All Layers Done?}
    H -- No --> I[Raise Z-axis by t]
    I --> F
    H -- Yes --> J[Cool Down in Inert Atmosphere]
    J --> K[Remove Part from Chamber]
    K --> L[Post-process: CNC machining, HIP, Heat Treatment]
    L --> M[Final Inspection: CT, UT, Dimensional]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: DMD Melt-Pool Modes (Conduction vs. Keyhole)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CON[Conduction Mode Stable - Preferred]
        C1[Wide Shallow Melt Pool] --> C2[Heat diffuses radially]
        C2 --> C3[AR less than 0.6]
        C3 --> C4[Low Porosity less than 0.1 percent]
    end
    subgraph KEY[Keyhole Mode Unstable - Defect]
        K1[Deep Narrow Cavity] --> K2[Vaporization at bottom]
        K2 --> K3[AR greater than 0.6]
        K3 --> K4[Keyhole Porosity 0.1 to 5 percent]
    end
    P[Process Parameters] --> CON
    P -- Power too high or Speed too low --> KEY
    P -- Power too low or Speed too high --> LOF[No Melting Lack of Fusion]
```

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture

| Sub-system | Hardware Components | Function |
|------------|--------------------|----------|
| **Energy Source** | Fiber laser (1–4 kW), Electron beam (for EBAM), Plasma arc (for WAAM) | Delivers thermal energy to create melt pool |
| **Material Delivery** | Powder feeder (disc/auger type, 1–4 hoppers), Wire feeder | Metered feedstock supply |
| **Beam-Material Coupling** | Coaxial nozzle, multi-jet nozzle, off-axis nozzle | Merges energy + material at focal point |
| **Motion System** | 3-axis CNC gantry, 5-axis articulated robot | Positions deposition head |
| **Atmosphere Control** | Sealed chamber, Argon supply, O₂ analyzer, gloves for Ti/Al | Prevents contamination |
| **Process Monitoring** | Pyrometer, CMOS camera, photodiode, laser triangulation | Real-time closed-loop feedback |
| **Software / Control** | Slicer, CAM, PLC, HMI | Path generation + synchronization |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define Direct Metal Deposition (DMD). Mention any two key process parameters that govern the build quality."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (2 marks):** Direct Metal Deposition (DMD) is a Directed Energy Deposition (DED) additive manufacturing process in which a focused thermal energy source (typically a high-power laser) is used to melt metal feedstock (powder or wire) supplied through a coaxial nozzle, depositing it layer by layer onto a substrate to form a near-net-shape part (ASTM/ISO 52900).

> **Two Key Process Parameters (1 mark):**
> 1. **Laser Power (P)** — governs melt-pool size and depth; too low → lack-of-fusion; too high → keyholing.
> 2. **Scan / Traverse Speed (v)** — controls the interaction time and energy density delivered to the substrate.

*(Other acceptable: powder feed rate, hatch spacing, layer thickness, standoff distance, O₂ level.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Differentiate between Conduction Mode and Keyhole Mode melting in DMD."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | Conduction Mode | Keyhole Mode |
|---------|----------------|--------------|
| Melt-pool shape | Wide and shallow | Deep and narrow |
| Energy density | Low to moderate | High |
| Aspect ratio (D/W) | $< 0.6$ | $> 0.6$ |
| Mechanism | Heat conducted radially into substrate | Metal vaporization creates a deep cavity |
| Resulting defect level | Low porosity ($< 0.1\%$) | High porosity ($0.1$–$5\%$) |
| Preferred in DMD | ✅ Yes (stable process) | ❌ No (unstable) |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]*

> **(a)** With a neat schematic, explain the **working principle of Direct Metal Deposition (DMD)** using a laser and powder feedstock. Discuss the role of coaxial nozzle, inert gas shrouding, and closed-loop melt-pool monitoring. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A DMD system uses a **2 kW fiber laser** to deposit Ti-6Al-4V with a **beam diameter of 3 mm**, **scan speed of 10 mm/s**, and **layer thickness of 0.4 mm**. The density of Ti-6Al-4V is **4.43 g/cm³**. Calculate:
> (i) Volumetric energy density. **(3 marks)**
> (ii) Volumetric deposition rate. **(2 marks)**
> (iii) Mass deposition rate in kg/h. **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a) — Working Principle of DMD (7 marks)**

**Schematic description (write/draw):**

A DMD system consists of a **fiber-coupled laser (1–4 kW, 1064 nm)**, a **multi-hopper powder feeder**, a **coaxial deposition head** mounted on a **3-axis CNC gantry or 6-axis robot**, all enclosed in a **sealed chamber purged with Argon**. The laser beam is delivered through the central axis of the head; metal powder is entrained in Argon carrier gas and injected through 2–4 annular jets that converge at the focal point. At the substrate, the laser creates a small melt pool (1–3 mm wide, 0.3–1 mm deep), and the powder is captured and fused into it. As the head moves, a solidified track is left behind. After each layer, the Z-axis is incremented, and the process repeats.

**Role of Coaxial Nozzle (2 marks):**
- Ensures powder is delivered **regardless of head orientation** → enables **multi-axis / 5-axis deposition** on complex geometries and overhangs.
- Creates a **symmetric, circular melt pool** → uniform track width and reduced porosity.
- Allows **focused powder stream** → higher deposition efficiency (60–95%).

**Role of Inert Gas Shrouding (2 marks):**
- Prevents **oxidation** of reactive metals (Ti, Al, Mg) — even **20 ppm O₂** can cause severe embrittlement in titanium.
- Displaces nitrogen (which causes nitride formation in steels).
- For titanium: chamber O₂ < **50 ppm** is mandatory; for aerospace-grade Ti: < **10 ppm**.

**Role of Closed-Loop Melt-Pool Monitoring (1.5 marks):**
- A **pyrometer or CMOS camera** monitors melt-pool temperature and size in real time (~1 kHz).
- **PID controller** adjusts laser power, feed rate, and traverse speed to maintain a constant melt pool.
- Compensates for **thermal accumulation** in tall builds.
- Reduces **porosity** and improves **dimensional consistency** (typically ±0.1 mm).

**Other components (1.5 marks):** CAD/CAM software, motion system, chiller, filtration unit, emergency interlocks.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given Data:**
- $P = 2 \; kW = 2000 \; W$
- $d = 3 \; mm$
- $v = 10 \; mm/s$
- $t = 0.4 \; mm$
- $\rho = 4.43 \; g/cm^3 = 4.43 \times 10^{-3} \; g/mm^3$

**(i) Volumetric Energy Density (3 marks):**

$$E = \frac{P}{v \cdot d \cdot t} = \frac{2000}{10 \cdot 3 \cdot 0.4}$$

$$E = \frac{2000}{12} = 166.67 \; J/mm^3$$

✅ **Comment (1 mark):** This value ($E = 166.67 \; J/mm^3$) is in the **borderline keyhole regime** for Ti-6Al-4V (threshold ≈ 150–200 $J/mm^3$). To stay in the safe conduction mode, the operator should **reduce power** or **increase scan speed**.

**(ii) Volumetric Deposition Rate (2 marks):**

$$VDR = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot d^2 \cdot v = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (3)^2 \cdot 10$$

$$VDR = 0.7854 \cdot 9 \cdot 10 = 70.69 \; mm^3/s$$

**(iii) Mass Deposition Rate (2 marks):**

$$MDR = VDR \cdot \rho = 70.69 \times 4.43 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$MDR = 0.313 \; g/s$$

Converting to kg/h:

$$MDR = 0.313 \times 3600 / 1000 = 1.128 \; kg/h$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

> **(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of Directed Energy Deposition (DED)** processes based on **energy source and feedstock form**. Compare **laser-powder DMD** with **wire-arc DED (WAAM)** in terms of deposition rate, accuracy, and typical applications. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **various defects in DMD-built parts** — namely **(i) lack-of-fusion porosity, (ii) keyhole porosity, and (iii) residual stress & distortion**. For each, state the **root cause** and **one preventive measure**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Classification of DED (7 marks)**

**Classification Table (3 marks):**

| Energy Source | Feedstock | Process Name | Commercial Example |
|---------------|-----------|--------------|--------------------|
| Laser | Powder | Laser Metal Deposition (LMD) | Trumpf TruLaser Cell, DM3D DMD |
| Laser | Wire | Laser Wire DED | BeAM Magic |
| Electron Beam | Wire/Plasma | EBAM / EBF³ | Sciaky EBAM |
| Plasma Arc | Powder | Plasma Transferred Arc (PTA) | P-Tech |
| Arc (GTAW/GMAW) | Wire | Wire-Arc AM (WAAM) | RAMLAB, MX3D |

**Comparison: Laser-Powder DMD vs. WAAM (4 marks):**

| Criterion | Laser-Powder DMD | Wire-Arc DED (WAAM) |
|-----------|------------------|----------------------|
| Deposition rate | 0.1–2 kg/h | **2–10 kg/h** (10× higher) |
| Dimensional accuracy | **± 0.1–0.5 mm** (high) | ± 1–3 mm (lower) |
| Surface roughness (Ra) | 10–30 µm | 200–500 µm |
| Build envelope | Up to ~1.5 m | **> 5 m** (very large) |
| Material options | Wide (Ti, Ni, Co, steels, Al, refractories) | Limited (steels, Al, Ti — needs weldable alloys) |
| Energy efficiency | 30–40% (laser) | 60–80% (arc) |
| Typical applications | Aero engine blades, implants, repairs | Large structural components, ship propellers, pressure vessels |
| Cost per kg | High (powder expensive) | Low (wire inexpensive) |

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Choose **laser-powder DMD** for **precision, complex, small-to-medium parts in exotic alloys**; choose **WAAM** for **large, low-complexity structural parts in weldable alloys**.

---

**Part (b) — Defects in DMD (7 marks)**

| # | Defect | Root Cause | Preventive Measure |
|---|--------|------------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Lack-of-fusion porosity** | Insufficient energy density: scan speed too high, power too low, or excessive hatch spacing → unmelted regions between tracks/layers | Reduce scan speed, increase power, or reduce hatch spacing; ensure $E \geq E_{min} \approx 30 \; J/mm^3$ for Ti-6Al-4V |
| 2 | **Keyhole porosity** | Excessive energy density: power too high or speed too low → metal vaporization creates deep cavity that traps gas bubbles on solidification | Reduce laser power or increase scan speed; maintain $AR < 0.6$ for conduction mode |
| 3 | **Residual stress & distortion** | High thermal gradients (1000s of °C/mm) and repeated thermal cycling → plastic strain accumulation and non-uniform shrinkage | Use substrate preheating (300–500 °C for Ti), optimized scan strategy (e.g., checkerboard, island), and **post-process heat treatment** or **hot isostatic pressing (HIP)** |

**Additional defect (for completeness):**
- **Balling effect** — when molten track breaks into droplets due to poor wetting (e.g., on oxidized substrate) → mitigated by **substrate preheating** and **proper cleaning**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes students make in DMD questions (losing 2–3 marks each):**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors in $E$ formula** — Forgetting to convert $v$ from mm/min to mm/s. The KTU standard formula uses $v$ in **mm/s**. *Always show unit conversion steps.*
> 2. **Mixing up energy density $E$ ($J/mm^3$) with specific energy $E_s$ ($J/g$).** They are different. $E$ uses volume; $E_s$ uses mass.
> 3. **Confusing DMD with SLM** — DMD uses **directed energy + coaxial feedstock**; SLM uses **powder bed + laser scan**. They are in **different ISO/ASTM categories** (DED vs. PBF).
> 4. **Forgetting to multiply $VDR$ by density** to get $MDR$ — a 2-mark deduction trap.
> 5. **Writing "Conduction Mode" as the answer without justification** — Examiner expects **$AR < 0.6$** and **shallow, wide melt pool** as the reasoning.
> 6. **In numericals, not commenting on whether the result is in the safe process window** — always cross-check $E$ against $E_{min}$ and $E_{max}$.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize these bullet points before the exam:**

- **DMD = Directed Energy Deposition** of **metal** using a **focused energy source** (laser/EB/plasma) and **coaxial feedstock** (powder/wire).
- **ISO/ASTM 52900 category: DED** (distinct from PBF).
- **Process chain:** CAD → Slice → Toolpath → Argon Purge → Deposit Layer → Raise Z → Repeat → Cool → Post-process.
- **Energy Density Formula (most tested):** $E = P / (v \cdot d \cdot t)$ in $J/mm^3$.
- **Conduction mode ($AR < 0.6$) is preferred** over keyhole mode for low porosity.
- **Inert gas (Argon)** is **mandatory** — chamber O₂ < **50 ppm**, ideally < **10 ppm** for Ti/Al.
- **Ti-6Al-4V safe window:** $E \approx 30{-}150 \; J/mm^3$.
- **Deposition rate advantage:** DMD is **~10–100× faster than SLM** for large parts.
- **DMD surface is rougher than SLM** (Ra 10–30 µm vs. 5–15 µm) due to thicker layers.
- **Closed-loop melt-pool monitoring** with pyrometer/CCD is the key to **process stability**.
- **Dilution** = substrate-melted-into-deposit fraction, typically **5–25%**.
- **Deposition efficiency** = (deposited mass / fed mass) × 100%, typically **60–95%**.
- **Common defects:** Lack-of-fusion (low $E$), Keyhole porosity (high $E$), Residual stress (high thermal gradient).
- **DMD advantage over SLM:** Larger build envelope, faster for big parts, multi-material & repair-friendly.
- **DMD disadvantage vs. SLM:** Lower surface finish, lower dimensional accuracy, higher powder cost, more post-processing.
- **Commercial variants to remember:** **LMD, LENS®, DMD, EBAM, WAAM**.
- **Numerical trap:** Always convert $v$ to **mm/s** before applying $E$ formula.
- **High-yield numerical questions** ask: $E$, $VDR$, $MDR$, $AR$, and a 1-line process-window verdict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electron Beam Based Metal Deposition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Electron Beam Based Metal Deposition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Electron Beam Based Metal Deposition (EBM)** is a high-energy, powder-bed fusion (PBF) additive manufacturing process in which a focused, high-velocity stream of electrons — accelerated through a strong electric field under high vacuum — is used as a localized heat source to selectively melt metallic powder, layer upon layer, into a fully dense three-dimensional part. The process is classified under the ISO/ASTM 52900 standard (2015) as **"Powder Bed Fusion – Electron Beam Melting"** and is one of the two dominant industrial metal AM technologies, the other being Selective Laser Melting (SLM).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM) belongs to the **Powder Bed Fusion (PBF)** family. The energy source is an **electron beam (not a laser)**, and the build chamber operates under **high vacuum (≈ 10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar)**. This combination of high-energy electrons and inert vacuum makes EBM uniquely suitable for reactive metals like titanium, niobium, and tantalum.

The original and most widely cited commercial EBM system is the **Arcam EBM** (now part of GE Additive), capable of producing parts from **Ti-6Al-4V**, **TiAl**, **Inconel 718**, **CoCrMo**, and **pure copper** among others.

## 1.2 Core Physical Principle

An electron beam is generated by **thermionic emission** from a heated tungsten filament cathode. These free electrons are then accelerated toward a positively-charged anode by a high potential difference (typically **60 kV**). The accelerated electrons, traveling at roughly **0.55 c (about 165,000 km/s)**, are then focused and steered by **electromagnetic lenses and deflection coils** (analogous to optical lenses and mirrors in a light-based system). When the beam strikes the metal powder bed, its kinetic energy is rapidly converted into heat through inelastic collisions with the lattice atoms, causing localized melting.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy – "Lightning in a Sealed Vacuum Box"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Imagine trying to weld metal on the Moon, where there is no air to oxidize your weld and no wind to disturb the powder. Instead of holding a torch, you use a precisely steered bolt of "manufactured lightning" that is so tightly focused it can melt a single 0.1 mm line of titanium dust. Because the lightning bolt (electron beam) is steered by **magnetic fields** (invisible "mirrors and lenses"), you can deflect it thousands of times per second without any moving mechanical parts — essentially "painting" with heat. The vacuum chamber is your lunar-like environment that prevents any chemical contamination, and the powder layers act like a tightly packed sandbox waiting to be sculpted.

This mental model captures the three pillars of EBM:
1. **Electron beam** ≈ "manufactured lightning" used as a contactless heat source.
2. **High vacuum** ≈ "Moon-like environment" that eliminates oxygen and contamination.
3. **Electromagnetic steering** ≈ "invisible mirrors" that move the beam at relativistic speeds without inertia.

## 1.4 Why EBM Matters in Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Relevance:** EBM can process **reactive metals** (Ti, Al, Mg) that oxidize instantly in air. It produces parts with **residual stresses an order of magnitude lower** than laser-based systems because the build plate is maintained at **~700 °C** (versus ~200 °C in SLM). The aerospace industry uses EBM-built Ti-6Al-4V brackets in the **GE LEAP fuel nozzle**, and orthopedic surgeons use EBM-built porous titanium acetabular cups for **hip implants**.

## 1.5 Key Physical & Process Metrics (Bolded for Emphasis)

| Parameter | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Accelerating voltage | **60 kV** | Determines electron energy |
| Beam current | **1 – 50 mA** | Controls heat input |
| Beam power | **up to 3.5 kW** | Melting capacity |
| Vacuum pressure | **10⁻⁴ – 10⁻⁵ mbar** | Prevents oxidation & X-ray attenuation |
| Build plate temperature | **~700 °C** | Reduces residual stress |
| Layer thickness | **50 – 200 µm** | Z-resolution |
| Scan speed | **up to 10,000 mm/s** | Very fast scanning |
| Powder size | **45 – 105 µm** | Spreadability & melt pool stability |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Operational Logic

The EBM process is governed by a tightly choreographed sequence of events that repeat for every layer:

### Step 1 — Chamber Evacuation
The build chamber is pumped down to a high vacuum of **10⁻⁴ to 10⁻⁵ mbar** using a combination of rotary and turbomolecular pumps. This vacuum serves three purposes:
- Eliminates oxygen to prevent oxidation of reactive metals.
- Prevents scattering of electrons by air molecules (which would otherwise defocus the beam).
- Contains the soft X-rays generated when high-energy electrons strike metal.

### Step 2 — Build Plate Pre-Heating
The build platform is pre-heated to approximately **700 °C** for titanium alloys. This elevated base temperature is critical because it minimizes the thermal gradient between the newly melted layer and the previously solidified layers, thereby **reducing residual stresses** and preventing part warping.

### Step 3 — Powder Layer Deposition
A measured quantity of metal powder is distributed across the build platform by a **powder hopper and rake (or roller)** system. The powder layer thickness is precisely controlled, typically between **50 and 200 µm**, depending on the part's geometric complexity and material.

### Step 4 — Electron Beam Generation
A tungsten filament is heated to approximately **2,800 °C**, causing **thermionic emission** of electrons. These electrons are then accelerated across a potential difference of **60 kV** toward the anode, gaining a kinetic energy of:

$$E_{\text{electron}} = eV$$

where *e* = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C (electron charge) and *V* = accelerating voltage. At 60 kV, each electron carries an energy of approximately **60 keV (≈ 9.6 × 10⁻¹⁵ J)**.

### Step 5 — Beam Focusing and Deflection
Electromagnetic lenses (magnetic coils carrying high current) focus the diverging electron beam into a tight spot of approximately **0.1 – 0.4 mm** in diameter. Two pairs of electromagnetic **deflection coils** (X-coil and Y-coil) steer the beam across the powder bed. Because electrons have negligible inertia, the beam can be deflected at frequencies up to **several kHz**, enabling ultra-fast scan patterns.

### Step 6 — Localized Melting
When the high-energy electrons strike the powder, their kinetic energy is converted into heat through **inelastic collisions**. The energy is deposited within a depth of a few micrometres from the surface. The peak temperature in the melt pool can exceed **2,500 °C**, well above the melting point of Ti-6Al-4V (1,668 °C).

### Step 7 — Rapid Solidification
The beam moves on, and the melt pool cools by conduction into the surrounding powder and the build plate. Solidification rates are extremely high (up to **10⁵ K/s**), producing a fine-grained, often martensitic microstructure (in titanium, the α' phase).

### Step 8 — Layer Repeat
The build platform indexes downward by one layer thickness, a fresh powder layer is raked over the previous one, and the cycle repeats until the part is complete.

### Step 9 — Powder Recovery and Cool-Down
After the build, the chamber is backfilled with high-purity helium (to accelerate cooling and prevent oxidation at high temperature), the part is allowed to cool below **~200 °C** (about 2 – 3 hours), and the surrounding "cake" of unsintered powder is removed by bead blasting.

## 2.2 The Electromagnetic Deflection Mechanism — "Why It Works"

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Magnetic Deflection:** A moving electron in a magnetic field experiences the **Lorentz force** perpendicular to both its velocity and the field direction. This force continuously bends the electron's path without any physical contact, allowing the beam to be steered at almost unlimited speed. This is the same principle used in old cathode-ray tube (CRT) televisions.

The Lorentz force on a moving charge is given by:

$$\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$$

where *e* is the elementary charge, *v* is the electron velocity, and *B* is the magnetic flux density. This force causes the electron to follow a circular arc of radius:

$$r = \frac{mv}{eB}$$

By varying the magnetic field *B* via current-controlled coils, the radius of curvature — and thus the landing position of the beam on the powder bed — can be controlled with micrometre precision.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are board-exam relevant.** Master the units and physical meaning of each variable before attempting numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{k} = eV$ | Kinetic energy per electron; *e* = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C, *V* = accelerating voltage (V) | Determines how hot the melt pool can become |
| 2 | $P_{b} = V \cdot I_{b}$ | Beam power; *V* = voltage, *I_b* = beam current (A) | Total heat delivered to the build |
| 3 | $E_{v} = \dfrac{P_{b}}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | Volumetric energy density (J/mm³); *v* = scan speed (mm/s), *h* = hatch spacing (mm), *t* = layer thickness (mm) | Master quality control parameter |
| 4 | $r = \dfrac{mv}{eB}$ | Radius of electron beam deflection; *m* = electron mass, *v* = electron velocity, *B* = magnetic field | Determines beam steering geometry |
| 5 | $F = evB\sin\theta$ | Lorentz force on electron; $\theta$ = angle between v and B | Basis of electromagnetic lens/coil design |
| 6 | $\dot{Q} = \rho \cdot c_{p} \cdot \dfrac{dT}{dt}$ | Heat input rate; $\rho$ = density, $c_p$ = specific heat | Used in transient thermal analysis |
| 7 | $v_{e} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2eV}{m_{e}}}$ | Electron velocity from accelerating voltage (non-relativistic approximation) | Valid up to ~10 kV; at 60 kV relativistic correction is required |
| 8 | $\lambda_{dB} = \dfrac{h}{m_{e}v}$ | de Broglie wavelength of accelerated electron | Justifies wave-optics treatment of focusing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important notation rule:** In the KTU exam answer sheet, use `\vert` or `\mid` in place of `|` for absolute values to avoid LaTeX table-rendering issues. Example: write $E = \vert E \vert$ rather than $E = \vert E \vert$ with vertical bars.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The volumetric energy density equation is the single most important formula in the EBM process because it allows the engineer to predict and control part quality. By keeping *E_v* within an optimal "process window" (typically **40 – 80 J/mm³** for Ti-6Al-4V), the part will be fully dense, free of unmelted powder defects, and free of excessive evaporation. If *E_v* is too low, you get porosity; if too high, you get keyhole porosity, vaporization, and recoilless ejection of molten metal.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Anti-Truncation Mandate:** Every algebraic step is written out. Students should be able to reproduce each derivation on a KTU answer sheet without referring back to these notes.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Kinetic Energy of an Accelerated Electron

**Problem:** Show that the kinetic energy of an electron accelerated through a potential difference *V* is given by $E_k = eV$.

**Starting Principle:** Work–Energy Theorem — the work done by the electric field on the electron equals its gain in kinetic energy.

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{\text{field}} &= \Delta E_k \\
\int F \, dx &= E_k - 0 \\
\int eE \, dx &= E_k
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitution Step:** For a uniform potential difference *V*, the line integral simplifies to *eV*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_k &= eV \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Substantiation (KTU-favourite):** For *V* = 60 kV = 6 × 10⁴ V:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_k &= (1.602 \times 10^{-19}\ \text{C}) \times (6 \times 10^{4}\ \text{V}) \\
&= 9.612 \times 10^{-15}\ \text{J} \\
&= 60\ \text{keV}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation:** This is the energy of every single electron striking the powder. A 50 mA beam delivers $50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{C/s}$, so the rate of energy delivery (i.e., beam power) is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_b &= V \cdot I_b \\
&= (6 \times 10^{4}\ \text{V}) \times (50 \times 10^{-3}\ \text{A}) \\
&= 3000\ \text{W} = 3\ \text{kW}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Volumetric Energy Density

**Problem:** Derive the volumetric energy density expression $E_v = \dfrac{P_b}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ used to characterize EBM process parameters.

**Conceptual Setup:** The beam delivers power *P_b* to a region of the powder bed. The beam moves with linear speed *v* and traces lines that are spaced a distance *h* apart (hatch spacing). The depth of melting is approximately the layer thickness *t*. We want the energy delivered **per unit volume** of metal.

**Step 1 — Energy delivered per unit time:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dot{E} &= P_b \quad [\text{Joules per second}]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Volume melted per unit time:** The beam sweeps an area equal to *(hatch length) × h* per hatch pass. The depth melted in one layer is *t*. Thus, the volume rate of melting is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
