**Interpretation:** Higher energy density → larger grains. This is why excessive energy input in LPBF leads to coarse columnar grains and reduced mechanical performance.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Residual Stress from Thermal Gradient

**Objective:** Quantify residual stress in an AM part due to constrained thermal contraction.

### Step 1: Define the Thermal Strain

For a constrained element cooling from solidification temperature $T_s$ to room temperature $T_{RT}$:

$$\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \cdot (T_s - T_{RT})$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = Coefficient of thermal expansion (1/K)
- $T_s$ = Solidus temperature (K)
- $T_{RT}$ = Room temperature (K), typically 298 K

### Step 2: Apply Hooke's Law for Fully Constrained Strain

If the element cannot contract (fully constrained), the induced elastic stress is:

$$\sigma_{res} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{th} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Where:
- $E$ = Young's modulus (GPa)
- $\Delta T = T_s - T_{RT}$ (K)

### Step 3: Account for Partial Constraint

In reality, the constraint is partial. The actual residual stress is:

$$\sigma_{res} = f \cdot E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $f$ is the constraint factor, $0 \leq f \leq 1$.

### Step 4: Numerical Example for Ti-6Al-4V

Given:
- $E = 110$ GPa
- $\alpha = 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $T_s = 1605^\circ$C = 1878 K
- $T_{RT} = 298$ K
- $f = 0.5$ (partial constraint)

Compute:

$$\sigma_{res} = 0.5 \times 110 \times 10^9 \times 8.6 \times 10^{-6} \times (1878 - 298)$$

$$\sigma_{res} = 0.5 \times 110 \times 10^9 \times 8.6 \times 10^{-6} \times 1580$$

$$\sigma_{res} = 0.5 \times 110 \times 8.6 \times 1580 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{res} = 0.5 \times 1{,}494{,}680 \times 10^3 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\sigma_{res} \approx 747 \text{ MPa}$$

This is close to the yield strength of Ti-6Al-4V (~880 MPa), explaining why AM Ti parts often crack without preheating or stress relief.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: AM Process vs. Microstructural Features

| Process | Typical Cooling Rate (K/s) | Grain Morphology | Common Defects | Typical Grain Size |
|---------|---------------------------|------------------|----------------|-------------------|
| **SLM (LPBF)** | $10^5$ to $10^7$ | Fine columnar/equiaxed mix | Spherical porosity, lack of fusion | 5 to 50 $\mu$m |
| **EBM** | $10^3$ to $10^5$ | Coarse columnar | Layer bands, gas porosity | 50 to 200 $\mu$m |
| **DED (LMD)** | $10^3$ to $10^4$ | Coarse columnar | Lack of fusion, cracks | 100 to 500 $\mu$m |
| **WAAM** | $10$ to $10^2$ | Very coarse columnar | Porosity, HAZ softening | 0.5 to 5 mm |
| **FDM (polymers)** | $1$ to $10$ | Semi-crystalline spherulites | Inter-bead voids, warping | 5 to 100 $\mu$m |
| **SLA** | $0.1$ to $1$ | Cross-linked polymer network | Curing gradients, internal stress | Amorphous network |

## 3.5 Characterization Techniques — Decision Matrix

| Technique | Information Provided | Spatial Resolution | Depth Probed | Sample Prep |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|--------------|-------------|
| **Optical Microscopy** | Grain size, porosity, layer bands | ~1 $\mu$m | Surface only | Polishing, etching |
| **SEM** | Fracture surface, fine porosity | ~10 nm | Surface (or cross-section) | Polishing, conductive coating |
| **TEM** | Dislocation substructure, precipitates | ~0.1 nm | <100 nm foil | FIB lift-out, thinning |
| **XRD** | Phase identification, texture | Bulk | 1 to 10 $\mu$m | Flat surface |
| **EBSD** | Grain orientation, texture, GB character | ~50 nm | Surface | Polishing, ion milling |
| **EDS/WDS** | Elemental composition | ~1 $\mu$m (EDS) | ~1 $\mu$m | Polished surface |
| **CT Scanning** | Internal defects, 3D porosity | ~5 to 50 $\mu$m | Full volume | None (non-destructive) |
| **DIC** | Strain field mapping | Surface | Surface | Speckle pattern |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Microstructural Analysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[AM Built Component] --> B0[Sectioning and Mounting]
    B0 --> C0[Grinding and Polishing]
    C0 --> D0[Etching or Ion Milling]
    D0 --> E0{Multiple Characterization Paths}
    E0 --> F1[Optical Microscopy]
    E0 --> F2[Scanning Electron Microscopy]
    E0 --> F3[Transmission Electron Microscopy]
    E0 --> F4[X-Ray Diffraction]
    E0 --> F5[Electron Backscatter Diffraction]
    E0 --> F6[Computed Tomography]
    F1 --> G1[Grain Size, Pore Distribution, Layer Geometry]
    F2 --> G2[Fracture Features, Fine Porosity, Surface Topology]
    F3 --> G3[Dislocation Density, Precipitate Size and Distribution]
    F4 --> G4[Phase Identification, Crystallographic Texture]
    F5 --> G5[Grain Orientation, Misorientation, Boundary Character]
    F6 --> G6[Three-Dimensional Defect Population]
    G1 --> H0[Microstructural Database]
    G2 --> H0
    G3 --> H0
    G4 --> H0
    G5 --> H0
    G6 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Correlation with Process Parameters]
    I0 --> J0[Process Optimization and Certification]
```

## 4.2 Process-Parameter to Microstructure Linkage

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Laser Power P] --> M0[Melt Pool Dynamics]
    A2[Scan Speed v] --> M0
    A3[Hatch Spacing h] --> M0
    A4[Layer Thickness t] --> M0
    A5[Preheating T0] --> M0
    M0 --> N1[Thermal Gradient G]
    M0 --> N2[Solidification Rate R]
    M0 --> N3[Cooling Rate Tdot]
    N1 --> O1[Solidification Mode Map]
    N2 --> O1
    O1 --> P1[Planar]
    O1 --> P2[Cellular]
    O1 --> P3[Columnar Dendritic]
    O1 --> P4[Equiaxed Dendritic]
    P1 --> Q0[Final Grain Morphology]
    P2 --> Q0
    P3 --> Q0
    P4 --> Q0
    N3 --> R1[Grain Size d]
    N3 --> R2[SDAS Lambda 2]
    N3 --> R3[Residual Stress]
    R1 --> Q0
    R2 --> Q0
    R3 --> S0[Mechanical Properties]
    Q0 --> S0
```

## 4.3 Schematic of Grain Structure Evolution in AM Build

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Substrate[Substrate Region]
        S0a[Coarse Equiaxed Grains]
    end
    subgraph HeatAffectedZone[Heat Affected Zone HAZ]
        H0a[Coarsened Grains from Repeated Thermal Cycling]
    end
    subgraph MeltPoolCore[Melt Pool Core]
        M0a[Columnar Dendrites Aligned with Build Direction Z]
    end
    subgraph MeltPoolEdge[Melt Pool Edge]
        E0a[Fine Equiaxed Grains due to High G over R]
    end
    subgraph TopLayer[Top Surface Layer]
        T0a[Fine Equiaxed Grains Rapid Cooling]
    end
    Substrate --> HeatAffectedZone
    HeatAffectedZone --> MeltPoolCore
    MeltPoolCore --> MeltPoolEdge
    MeltPoolEdge --> TopLayer
```

## 4.4 Defect Formation Schematic in AM

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D0[Insufficient Energy Density] --> D1[Lack of Fusion Porosity]
    D0 --> D2[Unmelted Powder Inclusions]
    E0[Excessive Energy Density] --> E1[Keyhole Porosity]
    E0 --> E2[Vaporization and Balling]
    F0[High Thermal Gradient] --> F1[Residual Stress]
    F0 --> F2[Hot Cracking Solidification]
    F0 --> F3[Delamination Layer Interface]
    G0[Powder Contamination] --> G1[Inclusions and Inhomogeneous Melting]
    H0[Improper Scanning Strategy] --> H1[Anisotropic Texture]
    H0 --> H2[Staircase Effect on Curved Surfaces]
```

## 4.5 EBSD-to-Property Correlation Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A0[AM Sample] --> B0[EBSD Scan]
    B0 --> C0[Inverse Pole Figure Map]
    B0 --> D0[Kernel Average Misorientation KAM]
    B0 --> E0[Grain Boundary Character]
    C0 --> F0[Crystallographic Texture]
    D0 --> G0[Local Strain and Dislocation Density]
    E0 --> H0[Special vs Random Boundaries]
    F0 --> I0[Anisotropic Yield Behavior]
    G0 --> J0[Work Hardening Capacity]
    H0 --> K0[Intergranular Corrosion Resistance]
    I0 --> L0[Part Performance Prediction]
    J0 --> L0
    K0 --> L0
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
> *Differentiate between columnar and equiaxed grain morphologies observed in AM parts. Mention the process conditions that favor each.*

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Columnar Grains | Equiaxed Grains |
|---------|-----------------|-----------------|
| **Shape** | Long, elongated parallel to thermal gradient | Roughly equal in all dimensions |
| **Cause** | Directional heat extraction along build direction | Multi-directional nucleation, low G/R |
| **G/R Condition** | High G/R ratio | Low G/R ratio |
| **AM Location** | Bulk of melt pool, near substrate | Top surface, melt pool edges |
| **Properties** | Anisotropic, high creep resistance | Isotropic, higher ductility |

**Process Conditions Favoring Each:**
- **Columnar:** High scan speed, low energy density, preheated substrate, no inoculation
- **Equiaxed:** Low scan speed, high energy density, addition of inoculants (e.g., TiB₂ in Al alloys), higher layer thickness, scanning strategy with rotation

---

### Question 2
> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
> *Explain the significance of the G/R ratio in determining the solidification microstructure in additive manufacturing.*

**Model Answer:**

The **G/R ratio** is the ratio of the **thermal gradient (G)** at the solid-liquid interface to the **solidification rate (R)**. It is the **key parameter** that determines the morphology of the solidification front.

**Significance:**

1. **Constitutional Supercooling Criterion:** For a stable planar interface, $G/R \geq \Delta T_0 / D_L$. When $G/R$ drops below this threshold, the interface becomes morphologically unstable.

2. **Microstructural Transition:**
   - $G/R \to \infty$ → **Planar** front (no undercooling perturbation)
   - High $G/R$ → **Cellular** growth
   - Moderate $G/R$ → **Columnar dendritic** growth
   - Low $G/R$ → **Equiaxed dendritic** growth

3. **AM Implications:** In LPBF, $G$ is typically $10^5$ to $10^6$ K/m near the melt pool boundary and $10^3$ K/m at the bottom, while $R$ varies with scan speed. The local $G/R$ determines whether a region solidifies as columnar or equiaxed.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model]**
> **(a)** Discuss the various metallurgical techniques used for microstructural characterization of AM components. Explain the information obtained from each technique. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With suitable diagrams, explain how the thermal gradient (G) and solidification rate (R) influence the grain morphology in AM parts. Construct the G vs R solidification map. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Metallurgical Characterization Techniques (7 marks):**

1. **Optical Microscopy (OM):**
   - *Information:* Grain size, grain shape, porosity, layer bands, melt pool geometry
   - *Procedure:* Sectioning → mounting → grinding (SiC paper 80 to 2000 grit) → polishing (diamond suspension 1 $\mu$m, then 0.05 $\mu$m colloidal silica) → chemical etching (e.g., Kroll's reagent for Ti alloys: 2% HF, 6% HNO₃, 92% H₂O)
   - *Limitation:* Resolution limited to ~1 $\mu$m

2. **Scanning Electron Microscopy (SEM):**
   - *Information:* High-magnification porosity, fracture surface features, second-phase particles
   - *Modes:* Secondary electron (SE) for topography, backscattered electron (BSE) for composition contrast
   - *Advantage:* Depth of field and resolution (~10 nm) superior to OM

3. **Transmission Electron Microscopy (TEM):**
   - *Information:* Dislocation substructure, nano-precipitates, dislocation cells, sub-grain boundaries
   - *Sample Prep:* Focused Ion Beam (FIB) lift-out, followed by low-energy ion thinning
   - *Application:* Critical for studying strengthening mechanisms in AM alloys

4. **X-Ray Diffraction (XRD):**
   - *Information:* Phase identification, lattice parameter measurement, macro-texture (via pole figures)
   - *Modes:* $\theta$-2$\theta$ scan for phase analysis, rocking curves for texture
   - *Significance:* Detects non-equilibrium phases unique to AM (e.g., supersaturated solid solutions)

5. **Electron Backscatter Diffraction (EBSD):**
   - *Information:* Grain orientation, misorientation distribution, grain boundary character (CSL boundaries), local strain via KAM
   - *Application:* Quantifies crystallographic texture that drives anisotropy
   - *Output:* Inverse Pole Figure (IPF) maps, Orientation Distribution Functions (ODF)

6. **X-Ray Computed Tomography (XCT):**
   - *Information:* 3D distribution of internal defects (porosity, cracks)
   - *Advantage:* Non-destructive, provides full volumetric data
   - *Limitation:* Resolution ~5 to 50 $\mu$m depending on sample size and setup

**(b) G vs R Influence on Grain Morphology (7 marks):**

The **solidification map** plots $G$ (vertical axis) vs $R$ (horizontal axis). Four distinct regions emerge:

| Region | Condition | Morphology |
|--------|-----------|------------|
| **I (Top)** | $G/R \geq \Delta T_0/D_L$ (high G, low R) | Planar |
| **II** | Moderate G/R | Cellular |
| **III** | Lower G/R | Columnar Dendritic |
| **IV (Bottom)** | Very low G/R | Equiaxed Dendritic |

**Process Implications in AM:**

- **Near Substrate:** High $G$ (heat sinks downward) + low $R$ → Planar/cellular
- **Mid-Build:** Moderate $G$ and increasing $R$ with scan speed → Columnar dendritic (dominant in DED and EBM)
- **Top Surface:** Low $G$ (heat lost to convection) + high $R$ → Equiaxed

The **G·R product** (cooling rate) further refines this map, since high $G \cdot R$ produces finer dendrite arm spacing:

$$\lambda_2 \propto (G \cdot R)^{-1/3}$$

**Schematic of G vs R map (described):**
- Y-axis: $G$ (K/m) from low to high
- X-axis: $R$ (m/s) from low to high
- Top-left: Planar region
- Top-right: Cellular
- Middle: Columnar dendritic
- Bottom-right: Equiaxed dendritic

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **[KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model - Alternative]**
> **(a)** Explain the Hall-Petch relationship and discuss how grain size variation in AM parts affects mechanical properties. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A Ti-6Al-4V part is built using LPBF with laser power 200 W, scan speed 800 mm/s, hatch spacing 0.1 mm, and layer thickness 30 $\mu$m. Calculate the volumetric energy density. If the average grain size is 25 $\mu$m and the Hall-Petch coefficients are $\sigma_0 = 750$ MPa and $k_y = 0.35$ MPa·m$^{1/2}$, estimate the yield strength. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hall-Petch Relationship and AM Implications (7 marks):**

The **Hall-Petch relationship** describes how the yield strength of a polycrystalline metal depends on its average grain size:

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength
- $\sigma_0$ = Lattice friction stress (resistance to dislocation motion in single crystal)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (related to dislocation pile-up stress)
- $d$ = Average grain diameter

**Physical Origin:**
Grain boundaries act as barriers to dislocation motion. Dislocations pile up at boundaries, creating a back stress. The stress required to propagate slip into the next grain increases with decreasing grain size (smaller $d$ means more boundaries per unit volume).

**Effect on AM Parts:**

1. **Build Direction vs. Transverse:** AM parts often have elongated columnar grains along the build direction. The effective $d$ differs depending on the loading direction:
   - Tensile along build: $d$ is the transverse width (often small → high strength)
   - Tensile transverse: $d$ is the long dimension (large → lower strength)
   - This creates **anisotropy ratios** of 1.05 to 1.20 in yield strength.

2. **Process-Property Link:** Finer grains are achieved by:
   - Higher cooling rate (high $G \cdot R$)
   - Higher scan speed
   - Lower energy density (to a point)
   - Use of inoculants (e.g., nano-TiB₂, Sc additions in Al alloys)

3. **Layer Bands and HAZ:** Repeated thermal cycling can coarsen grains in the HAZ, creating localized "weak zones" that reduce overall $k_y$ effectiveness.

4. **Hall-Petch Breakdown:** At very fine grain sizes (d < 100 nm), the Hall-Petch relationship breaks down due to grain boundary sliding (inverse Hall-Petch). This is rarely relevant in AM due to typical grain sizes of 5 to 100 $\mu$m.

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Calculate Volumetric Energy Density**

$$E = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$$

Given:
- $P = 200$ W
- $v = 800$ mm/s
- $h = 0.1$ mm
- $t = 30 \mu m = 0.03$ mm

Substitute:

$$E = \frac{200}{800 \times 0.1 \times 0.03}$$

$$E = \frac{200}{2.4}$$

$$E \approx 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3$$

**Step 2: Convert Grain Size to Meters**

$$d = 25 \mu m = 25 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3: Calculate $\sqrt{d}$**

$$\sqrt{d} = \sqrt{25 \times 10^{-6}} = 5 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^{1/2}$$

**Step 4: Apply Hall-Petch Equation**

$$\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$$

$$\sigma_y = 750 + \frac{0.35}{5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$\sigma_y = 750 + 70$$

$$\sigma_y = 820 \text{ MPa}$$

**Final Answer:**

$$\boxed{E \approx 83.33 \text{ J/mm}^3, \quad \sigma_y \approx 820 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Discussion:** This is consistent with reported yield strengths for LPBF Ti-6Al-4V (800 to 950 MPa), which is higher than wrought material (~880 MPa) due to refined martensitic $\alpha'$ microstructure from rapid cooling.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARK-LOSS ZONES IN MICROSTRUCTURAL STUDIES QUESTIONS**
>
> 1. **Confusing G and G·R:** Students often state that "grain size depends on G/R" but fail to mention that **G·R (cooling rate)** controls **dendrite arm spacing**, while **G/R** controls **morphology**. This is a 2-mark deduction.
>
> 2. **Missing Units in Hall-Petch:** $k_y$ is in **MPa·m$^{1/2}$** and $d$ must be in **meters**, not $\mu$m. Failing to convert $\mu$m → m leads to incorrect answers by a factor of $\sqrt{10^6} = 1000$.
>
> 3. **Energy Density Units:** Always express $E$ in J/mm³ for LPBF. Mixing units (e.g., using $\mu$m for layer thickness with mm for hatch spacing) causes order-of-magnitude errors.
>
> 4. **Forgetting Etching in OM Procedure:** Listing "polishing" without specifying the etching step is incomplete. Examiners expect Kroll's reagent (Ti alloys), Vilella's reagent (steels), or Keller's reagent (Al alloys) by name.
>
> 5. **Ignoring Anisotropy:** When discussing mechanical properties, always mention the **build direction vs. transverse direction** property difference. AM is intrinsically anisotropic at the microstructural level.
>
> 6. **Confusing SLM and EBM Microstructures:** EBM operates at higher temperature (preheated bed ~700°C) → coarser grains. SLM at near-room temperature → finer grains. Mixing these up costs 2-3 marks.
>
> 7. **Skipping the Schematic:** In a 7-mark question, a labeled diagram of the G vs R map or the AM build microstructure is mandatory. Text-only answers lose 1-2 marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — MICROSTRUCTURAL STUDIES IN AM**

### Core Concepts
- [ ] **G/R ratio** determines solidification morphology (planar, cellular, columnar, equiaxed)
- [ ] **G·R product** determines the scale of microstructure (dendrite arm spacing, grain size)
- [ ] **Constitutional supercooling criterion:** $G/R \geq \Delta T_0 / D_L$ for planar interface
- [ ] **Hall-Petch equation:** $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$ governs strength-grain size link
- [ ] **Energy density equation:** $E = P / (v \cdot h \cdot t)$ links process to microstructure

### Process-Microstructure Relationships
- [ ] **Higher scan speed** → higher cooling rate → finer grains, more equiaxed
- [ ] **Higher laser power** → larger melt pool → coarser grains, more columnar
- [ ] **EBM (high preheat)** → coarser columnar grains than SLM
- [ ] **DED** → coarse columnar, similar to welding microstructure
- [ ] **Preheating** → reduces residual stress, promotes columnar growth

### Characterization Techniques
- [ ] **OM** for grain size, porosity, layer bands
- [ ] **SEM** for fine porosity, fracture surfaces
- [ ] **TEM** for dislocations, nano-precipitates
- [ ] **XRD** for phases and macro-texture
- [ ] **EBSD** for grain orientation, KAM, boundary character
- [ ] **XCT** for 3D internal defect distribution

### AM-Specific Microstructural Features
- [ ] **Layer bands:** Composition/phase variation at layer interfaces
- [ ] **Melt pool boundaries:** Equiaxed-to-columnar transition zones
- [ ] **Lack-of-fusion defects:** Irregular, unmelted powder regions
- [ ] **Keyhole porosity:** Spherical pores from vaporization
- [ ] **Anisotropic grain structure:** Columnar grains along build direction
- [ ] **Non-equilibrium phases:** Martensite ($\alpha'$ in Ti alloys), supersaturated solid solutions
- [ ] **HAZ coarsening:** Grain growth from repeated thermal cycling

### Key Numerical Values to Remember
- [ ] Ti-6Al-4V martensite $\alpha'$ in SLM: ~800 to 950 MPa yield strength
- [ ] Inconel 718 columnar grain width in SLM: ~20 to 50 $\mu$m
- [ ] 316L stainless steel in SLM: ~500 to 600 MPa yield, fine cellular substructure
- [ ] Typical AM cooling rate: $10^3$ to $10^7$ K/s
- [ ] Typical energy density range: 30 to 200 J/mm³ for LPBF metals
- [ ] Hall-Petch $k_y$ for Ti: ~0.35 to 0.4 MPa·m$^{1/2}$
- [ ] Hall-Petch $k_y$ for steels: ~0.5 to 0.7 MPa·m$^{1/2}$
- [ ] Hall-Petch $k_y$ for Al: ~0.05 to 0.15 MPa·m$^{1/2}$

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- [ ] "Explain G vs R solidification map" → **always draw a schematic**
- [ ] "Differentiate SLM and EBM microstructure" → **mention cooling rate and preheat**
- [ ] "Calculate energy density" → **watch unit conversions (μm vs mm)**
- [ ] "Discuss Hall-Petch in AM context" → **mention anisotropy explicitly**
- [ ] "Characterization techniques" → **list OM, SEM, TEM, XRD, EBSD, XCT with specific outputs**
- [ ] "Defects in AM" → **categorize by origin: process, design, material**

### One-Line Definitions (for 1-mark sub-questions)
- [ ] **G/R:** Ratio of thermal gradient to solidification rate — controls interface morphology
- [ ] **Hall-Petch:** Empirical law relating yield strength to inverse square root of grain size
- [ ] **Energy Density:** Energy input per unit volume in LPBF
- [ ] **KAM (Kernel Average Misorientation):** EBSD-derived metric for local strain
- [ ] **PDAS/SDAS:** Primary/Secondary Dendrite Arm Spacing
- [ ] **Lack of fusion:** Defect due to insufficient energy to fully melt powder
- [ ] **Keyhole:** Deep, narrow vapor cavity in melt pool
- [ ] **Constitutional supercooling:** Condition leading to interface instability
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Structure-Property Relationship
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Structure-Property Relationship

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Structure-Property Relationship (SPR)** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) refers to the scientifically established correlation between the **microstructural characteristics** of an AM-built component and its resulting **mechanical, physical, chemical, and functional properties**. This relationship forms the central paradigm of materials science and is uniquely complex in AM due to the layer-by-layer, localized melting-solidification cycles, and rapid thermal gradients.

> [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> The systematic study of how process-induced microstructural features (grain morphology, porosity, phase distribution, residual stress, texture, and layer interfaces) dictate the macroscopic engineering performance of additively manufactured components.

The **Process-Structure-Property (PSP) linkage** is the governing chain in AM:

$$\boxed{\text{Process Parameters} \longrightarrow \text{Microstructure (Structure)} \longrightarrow \text{Macroscopic Properties}}$$

In AM, process parameters such as **laser power (P)**, **scan speed (v)**, **layer thickness (t)**, **hatch spacing (h)**, **build orientation**, and **chamber atmosphere** act as primary independent variables that manipulate the thermal history, which in turn dictates the resulting solidification microstructure, and ultimately the engineering properties.

## 1.2 Key Structural Features in AM

| Structural Element | Description | Typical Scale |
|---|---|---|
| **Grain morphology** | Columnar, equiaxed, or mixed dendritic | 1–500 µm |
| **Melt pool geometry** | Width, depth, length of fusion zone | 50–500 µm |
| **Porosity** | Gas pores, keyhole pores, lack-of-fusion voids | 1–500 µm |
| **Texture** | Preferred crystallographic orientation | Bulk level |
| **Residual stress** | Locked-in thermal stresses | Bulk level |
| **Layer interfaces** | Bond planes between successive layers | 20–100 µm |
| **Sub-grain cellular structures** | Cellular/dendritic sub-structures within grains | 0.5–5 µm |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Concrete Slab" Model

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Building a Wall with Clay vs. Pouring Concrete:**
>
> Imagine you are building a wall. If you stack **pre-baked clay bricks** one-by-one (analogous to AM), the wall will have visible joints between bricks (analogous to layer interfaces), and the strength depends heavily on how well each brick is bonded to its neighbor. Now, if you bake some bricks (heat-treated), they become stronger but also shrink slightly, creating internal stresses. The bricks themselves have a grain pattern (microstructure) — fine-grained bricks are stronger than coarse-grained ones. The arrangement of bricks (build orientation) determines whether the wall is stronger horizontally or vertically.
>
> In AM, we control "how the bricks are made" (process parameters) and "how they are arranged" (build strategy). The result is a finished wall with specific load-bearing capacity (mechanical properties) — but unlike conventional casting, every "brick" in AM is made in-situ, in rapid succession, with overlapping thermal cycles.

## 1.4 The Four Levels of Structure in AM

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Structure Framework:**
>
> 1. **Macro-structure** → Overall part geometry, build orientation, surface topography
> 2. **Meso-structure** → Layer bands, melt pool tracks, scan strategy patterns
> 3. **Micro-structure** → Grain morphology, dendritic arm spacing, precipitates, phases
> 4. **Nano/Sub-structure** → Dislocation density, cellular sub-grain boundaries, solute segregation

Each level interacts with and influences the level above and below, creating a multi-scale property map.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Thermal Gradient–Solidification Rate (G–R) Theory

The most fundamental theoretical framework governing AM microstructure is the **thermal gradient (G)** vs **solidification growth rate (R)** map.

### 2.1.1 Definitions

- **Thermal Gradient, G** = Rate of temperature change in the liquid ahead of the solid-liquid interface (K/mm)
- **Solidification Rate, R** = Velocity of the solid-liquid interface (mm/s)

### 2.1.2 The Three Zones of Solidification

1. **Planar Growth Zone** → At very high $G/R$ ratios, the solid-liquid interface is planar.
2. **Cellular/Dendritic Zone** → At moderate $G/R$, cellular and dendritic structures form.
3. **Equiaxed Zone** → At low $G/R$, equiaxed grains nucleate ahead of the interface.

### 2.1.3 Cooling Rate

$$\varepsilon = G \times R$$

The cooling rate $\varepsilon$ (K/s) is the product of thermal gradient and growth rate.

### 2.1.4 Constitutional Supercooling Criterion

$$\frac{G}{R} \geq \frac{\Delta T}{D_L}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Thermal gradient (K/m)
- $R$ = Solidification growth rate (m/s)
- $\Delta T$ = Freezing range of alloy (K)
- $D_L$ = Solute diffusivity in liquid (m²/s)

When this criterion is satisfied, **planar growth** occurs; when violated, **cellular/dendritic/equiaxed growth** develops.

> [!NOTE]
> **In AM processes**, extremely high cooling rates (10³–10⁸ K/s) are common, especially in L-PBF. This leads to refined microstructures with suppressed segregation.

## 2.2 Grain Size and Morphology Control

### 2.2.1 Hall-Petch Relationship

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_y$ = Yield strength (MPa)
- $\sigma_0$ = Friction stress (lattice resistance) (MPa)
- $k_y$ = Hall-Petch coefficient (MPa·µm^½)
- $d$ = Average grain diameter (µm)

**Engineering Insight:** Smaller grains → more grain boundaries → stronger material.

### 2.2.2 Dendritic Arm Spacing (DAS) Relationship

$$\lambda = A \cdot \varepsilon^{-n}$$

Where:
- $\lambda$ = Primary or Secondary Dendritic Arm Spacing (µm)
- $A$ = Material-dependent constant
- $\varepsilon$ = Cooling rate (K/s)
- $n$ = Exponent (typically 0.3–0.5 for primary DAS, ~0.33)

### 2.2.3 Aspect Ratio of Columnar Grains

$$AR = \frac{L_c}{W_c}$$

Where $L_c$ = columnar length, $W_c$ = columnar width. Columnar grains typically have $AR > 5$.

## 2.3 Volumetric Energy Density (VED)

VED is the master parameter linking process inputs to thermal cycles:

$$\boxed{E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}}$$

Where:
- $P$ = Laser power (W)
- $v$ = Scan speed (mm/s)
- $h$ = Hatch spacing (mm)
- $t$ = Layer thickness (mm)
- $E_v$ = Volumetric energy density (J/mm³)

**Typical Ranges in AM:**
- L-PBF (Ti-6Al-4V): 40–120 J/mm³
- L-PBF (AlSi10Mg): 30–80 J/mm³
- DED: 20–100 J/mm³

## 2.4 Porosity Models

### 2.4.1 Spherical Gas Pore Model

$$P_{pore} = \frac{4\gamma}{r}$$

Where:
- $P_{pore}$ = Internal pressure of gas pore (Pa)
- $\gamma$ = Surface tension of liquid metal (N/m)
- $r$ = Pore radius (m)

### 2.4.2 Lack-of-Fusion Threshold

For adequate fusion between adjacent tracks:

$$h_{max} \leq \frac{W_m}{2} \cdot \tan\left(\frac{\theta_m}{2}\right)$$

Where $W_m$ = melt pool width, $\theta_m$ = melt pool contact angle.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT] > **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — For Rapid Revision:**

| # | Formula | Symbols & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + \frac{k_y}{\sqrt{d}}$ | $\sigma_y$ (MPa), $d$ (µm) | Strength-grain size linkage |
| 2 | $\lambda = A \cdot \varepsilon^{-n}$ | $\lambda$ (µm), $\varepsilon$ (K/s) | DAS vs cooling rate |
| 3 | $\varepsilon = G \cdot R$ | $G$ (K/m), $R$ (m/s) | Cooling rate calculation |
| 4 | $\frac{G}{R} \geq \frac{\Delta T}{D_L}$ | All in SI | Constitutional supercooling |
| 5 | $E_v = \frac{P}{v \cdot h \cdot t}$ | $E_v$ (J/mm³) | Volumetric energy density |
| 6 | $R_a = \frac{h^2}{6r}$ (idealized) | $r$ = layer radius of curvature | Surface roughness from stair-stepping |
| 7 | $\sigma_{r,max} = \frac{\alpha \cdot E \cdot \Delta T}{1-\nu}$ | $\alpha$ = CTE, $E$ = modulus, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio | Max residual stress |
| 8 | $\rho_{rel} = 1 - \frac{V_{pore}}{V_{total}}$ | dimensionless | Relative density |
| 9 | $\eta = \frac{\sigma_{UTS}^{AM}}{\sigma_{UTS}^{conv}}$ | dimensionless | AM vs conventional strength ratio |
| 10 | $K_{IC} = Y \cdot \sigma_c \cdot \sqrt{\pi a}$ | $K_{IC}$ (MPa·m^½) | Fracture toughness |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Why This Topic Matters in Industry:**
>
> - **Aerospace** — Ti-6Al-4V AM blades require fine α-lamellar microstructure for fatigue resistance; grain morphology directly impacts HCF life.
> - **Medical Implants** — Co-Cr and Ti implants demand specific porosity (50–80%) for osseointegration; SPR predicts stiffness match with bone.
> - **Automotive** — AlSi10Mg AM parts need HIP processing to eliminate lack-of-fusion pores and improve ductility.
> - **Tooling** — Maraging steel AM inserts require aging heat treatment; SPR maps hardness to aging temperature/time.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Hall-Petch Relationship

We will derive the famous Hall-Petch equation from fundamental dislocation mechanics.

### Step 1 — Consider a Dislocation Pile-up

When a dislocation moving on a slip plane encounters a grain boundary, it cannot easily cross. Multiple dislocations pile up against the boundary.

Let $n$ be the number of dislocations in the pile-up.

### Step 2 — Stress Concentration at Pile-up Tip

By dislocation mechanics, the stress concentration at the tip of a pile-up of $n$ dislocations is:

$$\tau_{tip} = n \cdot \tau_{applied}$$

The pile-up length $L$ for $n$ dislocations under applied shear stress $\tau_a$ is:

$$L = \frac{n \cdot G \cdot b}{\tau_a}$$

Where:
- $G$ = Shear modulus
- $b$ = Burgers vector magnitude

### Step 3 — Equating to Critical Stress

For yielding to occur in the adjacent grain, the tip stress must equal a critical value $\tau_c$ (the stress to nucleate slip in the next grain):

$$n \cdot \tau_a = \tau_c$$

### Step 4 — Relating Pile-up Length to Grain Size

The pile-up length scales with grain diameter $d$:

$$L \approx d \quad \text{(pile-up spans the grain)}$$

Therefore:

$$n = \frac{d \cdot \tau_a}{G \cdot b}$$

### Step 5 — Substitute and Rearrange

Substitute into the yielding condition:

$$\frac{d \cdot \tau_a}{G \cdot b} \cdot \tau_a = \tau_c$$

Solving for $\tau_a$:

$$\tau_a^2 = \frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{d}$$

$$\tau_a = \sqrt{\frac{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}{d}}$$

Writing in terms of yield stress $\sigma_y$:

$$\boxed{\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}}$$

Where:
- $\sigma_0$ = Lattice friction stress (resistance to dislocation motion in single crystal)
- $k_y = \sqrt{\tau_c \cdot G \cdot b}$ = Hall-Petch slope

**Conclusion:** The yield strength scales inversely with the square root of grain diameter — finer grains yield stronger materials.

---

## 3.2 Numerical Example: Cooling Rate from G–R Parameters

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example for KTU Exam Practice:**

**Problem:** In an L-PBF process for Inconel 718, the thermal gradient near the solid-liquid interface is $G = 5 \times 10^5$ K/m, and the solidification growth rate is $R = 10$ mm/s. Calculate the cooling rate and the secondary dendritic arm spacing if the material constant $A = 50$ µm·(K/s)^n and exponent $n = 0.33$.

### Step 1 — Convert Units to SI

$$G = 5 \times 10^5 \text{ K/m}$$

$$R = 10 \text{ mm/s} = 10 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m/s} = 0.01 \text{ m/s}$$

### Step 2 — Calculate Cooling Rate

$$\varepsilon = G \times R = (5 \times 10^5) \times (0.01)$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 5000 \text{ K/s} = 5 \times 10^3 \text{ K/s}}$$

### Step 3 — Calculate Secondary DAS

$$\lambda = A \cdot \varepsilon^{-n}$$

$$\lambda = 50 \cdot (5000)^{-0.33}$$

Computing $(5000)^{0.33}$:

$$\ln(5000) = 8.517$$

$$0.33 \times 8.517 = 2.811$$

$$e^{2.811} = 16.64$$

So:

$$\lambda = \frac{50}{16.64}$$

$$\boxed{\lambda \approx 3.00 \text{ µm}}$$

This is a fine microstructure, typical of L-PBF Inconel 718.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Residual Stress in AM Layer

### Step 1 — Define Geometry

Consider a single AM track cooling from solidus $T_s$ to room temperature $T_0$:

$$\Delta T = T_s - T_0$$

### Step 2 — Constrained Thermal Contraction

If the track is fully constrained (rigid substrate), the elastic strain that would develop is:

$$\varepsilon_{thermal} = \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

Where $\alpha$ = coefficient of thermal expansion.

### Step 3 — Induced Elastic Stress

$$\sigma_{elastic} = E \cdot \varepsilon_{thermal} = E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$$

### Step 4 — Account for Poisson's Ratio (Plane Stress)

For biaxial plane stress (typical AM surface):

$$\boxed{\sigma_r = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}}$$

Where $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

### Step 5 — Numerical Estimate for Ti-6Al-4V

Given:
- $E = 110$ GPa = $110 \times 10^3$ MPa
- $\alpha = 8.6 \times 10^{-6}$ /K
- $\Delta T = 1600 - 25 = 1575$ K
- $\nu = 0.34$

$$\sigma_r = \frac{(110 \times 10^3)(8.6 \times 10^{-6})(1575)}{1 - 0.34}$$

Numerator:

$$110 \times 10^3 \times 8.6 \times 10^{-6} = 0.946$$

$$0.946 \times 1575 = 1490.0$$

Denominator: $1 - 0.34 = 0.66$

$$\sigma_r = \frac{1490.0}{0.66} \approx 2257 \text{ MPa}$$

This exceeds the yield strength (~880 MPa), so yielding occurs — explaining why AM parts often display **plastic deformation** and **warpage** in extreme cases.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Process → Structure → Property

| Process Parameter | Resulting Microstructure | Impact on Property |
|---|---|---|
| ↑ Laser Power | Coarser grains, larger melt pool | ↓ Strength, ↑ Porosity |
| ↑ Scan Speed | Refined grains, possible lack-of-fusion | ↑ Strength (if fused), ↓ Ductility |
| ↓ Hatch Spacing | More re-melting, denser part | ↑ Strength, ↑ Anisotropy |
| ↑ Layer Thickness | Coarser columnar grains | ↓ Mechanical properties |
| Build orientation change | Different texture direction | Anisotropic UTS, fatigue |
| Substrate preheating | Reduced cooling rate | ↓ Residual stress |
| HIP post-processing | Closure of internal pores | ↑ Fatigue life, ↑ Ductility |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process-Structure-Property (PSP) Linkage Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Process Parameters] --> B[Thermal History]
    B --> C[Microstructure Evolution]
    C --> D[Mechanical Properties]
    C --> E[Physical Properties]
    C --> F[Chemical Properties]
    A --> A1[Laser Power P]
    A --> A2[Scan Speed v]
    A --> A3[Hatch Spacing h]
    A --> A4[Layer Thickness t]
    A --> A5[Build Orientation]
    A --> A6[Atmosphere]
    B --> B1[Temperature Gradient G]
    B --> B2[Cooling Rate eps]
    B --> B3[Melt Pool Geometry]
    B --> B4[Thermal Cycles]
    C --> C1[Grain Morphology]
    C --> C2[Porosity]
    C --> C3[Phase Composition]
    C --> C4[Residual Stress]
    C --> C5[Texture]
    D --> D1[Yield Strength]
    D --> D2[Ultimate Tensile Strength]
    D --> D3[Fatigue Life]
    D --> D4[Ductility]
    D --> D5[Hardness]
    D --> D6[Fracture Toughness]
    E --> E1[Density]
    E --> E2[Thermal Conductivity]
    E --> E3[Electrical Resistivity]
    F --> F1[Corrosion Resistance]
    F --> F2[Oxidation Behavior]
```

## 4.2 Microstructure Formation in AM — Block Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Powder Layer Deposited]) --> Melt[Laser Melts Powder]
    Melt --> Solidif[Rapid Solidification]
    Solidif --> Branch{G/R Ratio?}
    Branch -->|Very High| Planar[Planar Growth]
    Branch -->|Moderate| Cellular[Cellular Dendritic]
    Branch -->|Low| Equiaxed[Equiaxed Grains]
    Planar --> Output1[Anisotropic Texture]
    Cellular --> Output2[Columnar Dendrites]
    Equiaxed --> Output3[Isotropic Fine Grains]
    Output1 --> PropA[Directional Strength]
    Output2 --> PropB[High UTS, Low Ductility]
    Output3 --> PropC[Balanced Properties]
    Solidif --> Reheat[Adjacent Track Reheats]
    Reheat --> Temper[Tempering / Phase Transform]
    Temper --> Final[Final Microstructure]
```

## 4.3 Build Orientation vs Anisotropy Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Build Orientation] --> B[Vertical 0 deg]
    A --> C[Horizontal 90 deg]
    A --> D[Inclined 45 deg]
    B --> B1[Layer planes perpendicular to load]
    B --> B2[Weakest UTS]
    B --> B3[Maximum porosity alignment]
    C --> C1[Layer planes parallel to load]
    C --> C2[Strongest UTS]
    C --> C3[Best fatigue life]
    D --> D1[Stepped stair effect]
    D --> D2[Intermediate properties]
    D --> D3[Surface roughness critical]
```

## 4.4 Heat Treatment Effect on Structure-Property Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    AM[As-built AM Part] --> HT1[Stress Relief SR]
    AM --> HT2[Hot Isostatic Pressing HIP]
    AM --> HT3[Solution Treatment]
    AM --> HT4[Aging]
    HT1 --> SRP[Reduced Residual Stress]
    HT1 --> SRM[Minimal microstructure change]
    HT2 --> HIPP[Closed porosity]
    HIPP --> HIPS[Improved ductility and fatigue]
    HT3 --> STG[Homogenized microstructure]
    STG --> STA[Improved toughness]
    HT4 --> AGP[Precipitate strengthening]
    AGP --> AGH[Increased hardness and strength]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** This block diagram shows how different post-processing routes modify the AM microstructure to achieve target property profiles. The **as-built** state is rarely the final usable state for critical engineering applications.

## 4.5 Defect–Property Degradation Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Def[Defect Types in AM] --> DP1[Gas Porosity]
    Def --> DP2[Lack of Fusion]
    Def --> DP3[Keyhole Porosity]
    Def --> DP4[Cracks]
    Def --> DP5[Unmelted Powder]
    DP1 --> EFF1[Fatigue life reduction 10 to 50x]
    DP2 --> EFF2[Stress concentration peaks]
    DP3 --> EFF3[Local stress raisers]
    DP4 --> EFF4[Catastrophic failure risk]
    DP5 --> EFF5[Surface roughness increase]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Explain the Hall-Petch relationship and its relevance to additive manufacturing microstructures.**

**Model Answer:**

The Hall-Petch equation $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y \cdot d^{-1/2}$ expresses the dependence of yield strength on grain size. In AM, extremely high cooling rates (10³–10⁶ K/s) produce fine microstructures with grain diameters typically 5–50 µm. The Hall-Petch slope $k_y$ for AM Ti-6Al-4V is around 0.6–0.8 MPa·m^½. Since $d$ is small in AM, the second term $k_y/\sqrt{d}$ is large, contributing to high yield strength. However, the relationship may deviate for very fine grains (<1 µm) due to the inverse Hall-Petch effect, and texture/anisotropy must also be considered.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for equation+meaning, 1 for AM relevance, 0.5 for limits)*

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**What is constitutional supercooling? How does it relate to microstructure formation in additive manufacturing?**

**Model Answer:**

Constitutional supercooling occurs when the temperature gradient ahead of the solid-liquid interface is shallower than the liquidus gradient established by solute partitioning. Criterion: $G/R < \Delta T/D_L$. When this is satisfied, dendritic/equiaxed growth occurs. In AM, rapid scanning creates high $G$ and $R$, often satisfying planar growth conditions locally. However, in the trailing edge of the melt pool, $G$ drops sharply, causing constitutional supercooling and columnar-to-equiaxed transition (CET). This phenomenon governs grain morphology in AM-built alloys.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for definition+criterion, 1 for AM relevance, 0.5 for CET)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the Hall-Petch equation starting from dislocation pile-up mechanics and discuss its application to L-PBF processed Ti-6Al-4V. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** With the help of a G–R solidification map, explain how process parameters in laser powder bed fusion influence grain morphology in Inconel 718. Calculate the cooling rate when $G = 3 \times 10^5$ K/m and $R = 8$ mm/s, and the secondary DAS using $A = 50$ µm·(K/s)^n and $n = 0.33$. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) Hall-Petch Derivation:**

Starting from dislocation pile-up mechanics:

Step 1: A pile-up of $n$ dislocations against a grain boundary creates a tip stress $n\tau_a$.

Step 2: The pile-up length is $L = nGb/\tau_a$.

Step 3: For the pile-up to span the grain, $L \propto d$, giving $n = d\tau_a/(Gb)$.

Step 4: Yielding occurs when $n\tau_a = \tau_c$.

Step 5: Substituting yields $\tau_a^2 = \tau_c G b/d$, hence $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$.

**Application to L-PBF Ti-6Al-4V:**
- Cooling rates of 10⁴–10⁶ K/s → α′ martensite needles with width 0.5–2 µm.
- After heat treatment, transformed to fine α+β microstructure with grain size ~2–5 µm.
- Hall-Petch strengthening contributes significantly to UTS values reaching 1000–1100 MPa.
- Anisotropy arises because columnar prior-β grains elongate along the build direction.

*(7 marks: 4 for derivation, 2 for AM context, 1 for properties)*

**(b) G–R Map Analysis:**

The G–R solidification map divides microstructure regimes:

- High $G/R$ → Planar growth (rare in AM, only at very low scan speeds)
- Moderate $G/R$ → Cellular/Columnar dendritic growth (dominant in AM)
- Low $G/R$ → Equiaxed grains (occurs at melt pool tail, center of thick deposits)

**Effect of L-PBF Process Parameters:**

- ↑ Laser Power → Larger melt pool → Lower $G$ → Promotes equiaxed growth
- ↑ Scan Speed → Higher $R$ → Lower $G/R$ → Refined columnar dendrites
- ↑ Hatch spacing → Localized remelting reduces → Coarser structure
- ↓ Layer thickness → Faster cooling → Finer microstructure

**Numerical Calculation:**

Step 1: Convert $R$ to SI: $R = 8$ mm/s = $8 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s.

Step 2: Cooling rate:

$$\varepsilon = G \times R = (3 \times 10^5) \times (8 \times 10^{-3}) = 2400 \text{ K/s}$$

Step 3: Secondary DAS:

$$\lambda = A \cdot \varepsilon^{-n} = 50 \cdot (2400)^{-0.33}$$

Compute $(2400)^{0.33}$:

$$\ln(2400) = 7.783$$

$$0.33 \times 7.783 = 2.568$$

$$e^{2.568} = 13.04$$

$$\lambda = \frac{50}{13.04} = 3.83 \text{ µm}$$

The fine DAS contributes to high strength via Hall-Petch strengthening.

*(7 marks: 3 for G-R map, 2 for parameter effects, 2 for numerical solution)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Explain the Process-Structure-Property (PSP) linkage in additive manufacturing with a suitable block diagram. Discuss how build orientation affects the anisotropy of mechanical properties in L-PBF AlSi10Mg. **[(7 Marks)]**

**(b)** Discuss the various types of porosity defects in L-PBF processes and explain their effects on fatigue and tensile properties. Derive the equation for maximum residual thermal stress in a constrained AM layer. **[(7 Marks)]**

#### Model Solution:

**(a) PSP Linkage and Build Orientation Effects:**

The Process-Structure-Property linkage is the central paradigm in AM:

Process Parameters (P, v, h, t, orientation) → Thermal History (G, R, cooling rate) → Microstructure (grain size, porosity, texture, phases) → Properties (strength, ductility, fatigue).

**Block Diagram (refer to Section 4.1):**

The flow proceeds in this chain: process parameters control thermal input which controls solidification, which sets the final microstructure, which determines all mechanical responses.

**Effect of Build Orientation in L-PBF AlSi10Mg:**

- **Horizontal (XY) builds** → Layer planes lie parallel to loading axis → Higher UTS (~400–450 MPa) and elongation (~8–12%) → Fine cellular sub-grain structure aligned with scan direction
- **Vertical (Z) builds** → Layer planes perpendicular to loading axis → Lower UTS (~350–400 MPa) and elongation (~4–6%) → Lack-of-fusion defects aligned with build direction act as crack initiators
- **45° inclined** → Stair-step effect and intermediate properties → Surface roughness affects fatigue life

The anisotropy factor (ratio of vertical to horizontal strength) is typically 0.8–0.9 for AlSi10Mg.

*(7 marks: 3 for PSP linkage, 4 for orientation effects)*

**(b) Porosity Defects and Residual Stress Derivation:**

**Types of Porosity in L-PBF:**

1. **Gas Porosity** — Trapped gas in powder (typically Argon or Nitrogen) → Spherical pores of 10–100 µm → Reduce fatigue life but minimal effect on tensile strength.

2. **Lack-of-Fusion (LoF) Defects** — Insufficient energy density → Irregular, sharp-edged voids aligned between layers → Act as crack initiation sites → Catastrophic effect on fatigue.

3. **Keyhole Porosity** — Excessive energy density → Vaporization of metal → Spindle-shaped pores at melt pool bottom → Local stress concentrators.

4. **Solidification Cracking** — Hot tearing in alloys with wide freezing range → Crack-like defects perpendicular to scan direction.

**Effects on Properties:**

- Fatigue life drops by **1–2 orders of magnitude** for >2% porosity
- UTS reduces by 10–30% depending on defect type
- Fracture toughness $K_{IC}$ reduces significantly with LoF defects

**Residual Stress Derivation:**

For a constrained layer cooling from $T_s$ to $T_0$ with CTE $\alpha$ and substrate modulus $E$:

Step 1: Free thermal contraction: $\varepsilon_{th} = \alpha \Delta T$.

Step 2: Constraint induces elastic strain: $\varepsilon_{elastic} = \alpha \Delta T$.

Step 3: Stress under biaxial constraint:

$$\sigma_r = \frac{E \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \nu}$$

For Ti-6Al-4V: $\sigma_r \approx 2257$ MPa (as calculated earlier), far exceeding yield, hence plastic deformation and warpage.

*(7 marks: 3 for porosity classification, 2 for property effects, 2 for derivation)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Missing Units** — Always include units in numerical answers (K/s, µm, MPa). The examiner deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 2. **Confusing G/R and G×R** — Students often mix up the constitutional supercooling criterion ($G/R$) with the cooling rate formula ($G \times R = \varepsilon$).
> 3. **Ignoring Anisotropy** — Many answers assume isotropic properties, which is incorrect for AM parts. Always mention the build-direction dependence.
> 4. **Skipping the Diagram** — KTU examiners expect a labeled diagram in 7-mark sub-questions. A missing diagram costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **No Numerical Verification** — When asked to "derive," provide a numerical sanity check at the end.
> 6. **Forgetting the Hall-Petch Limit** — The Hall-Petch equation breaks down for nanocrystalline grains (<100 nm) — a common viva question.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist:**
>
> ☐ **Process-Structure-Property (PSP) Chain** — Master parameter → microstructure → property
> ☐ **Hall-Petch Equation** — $\sigma_y = \sigma_0 + k_y/\sqrt{d}$; finer grains = stronger
> ☐ **Cooling Rate** — $\varepsilon = G \times R$ (K/s); L-PBF gives 10³–10⁶ K/s
> ☐ **Constitutional Supercooling** — $G/R \geq \Delta T/D_L$ for planar growth
> ☐ **Columnar-to-Equiaxed Transition (CET)** — Governed by $G/R$ and alloy composition
> ☐ **Volumetric Energy Density** — $E_v = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$; typical range 30–120 J/mm³
> ☐ **Porosity Types** — Gas pores, Lack-of-Fusion, Keyhole; each with distinct morphology
> ☐ **Anisotropy** — Vertical builds weaker than horizontal for L-PBF alloys
> ☐ **Residual Stress** — $\sigma_r = E \alpha \Delta T / (1-\nu)$; often exceeds yield → plastic deformation
> ☐ **Heat Treatment Routes** — Stress Relief, HIP, Solution Treatment, Aging
> ☐ **DAS vs Cooling Rate** — $\lambda = A \cdot \varepsilon^{-n}$, $n \approx 0.33$
> ☐ **Build Orientation Effects** — Fatigue life most sensitive to surface stair-stepping
> ☐ **Typical AM Materials** — Ti-6Al-4V, Inconel 718, AlSi10Mg, 316L SS, Co-Cr
> ☐ **Property Benchmark** — AM parts often match or exceed wrought properties after HIP
> ☐ **Hierarchical Structure** — Macro → Meso → Micro → Nano; all levels affect final properties

**End of Notes — Structure-Property Relationship**
*Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme Board Examination Standards*

topics.title: Three-Dimensional Printing: Principle, Process, Advantages and Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Three-Dimensional Printing: Principle, Process, Advantages and Applications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Three-Dimensional Printing (3DP)** is an **Additive Manufacturing (AM)** process classified under **Material Jetting / Binder Jetting** (ISO/ASTM 52900) in which a **liquid bonding agent (binder)** is selectively deposited, drop-by-drop, onto a **bed of powder particles** using an **inkjet print head**. The binder droplets locally fuse the powder grains to form a solid cross-section. After each layer is printed, the build platform is lowered by one **layer thickness (typically 0.1 mm)**, a fresh powder layer is recoated, and the cycle repeats until the part is fully realized.

The technology was **invented at MIT in 1989 (Sachs, Cima, Cornie)** and commercialised in the 1990s by companies such as **Z Corporation, 3D Systems, ExOne (now part of Desktop Metal)**, and **Voxeljet**. It is **NOT the same as FDM, SLA, or SLS**, although the term "3D Printing" is colloquially (and incorrectly) used for all additive processes.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** In 3DP, the *joining* is achieved by a **chemical binder**, not by melting (SLS/SLM), photopolymerization (SLA), or extrusion (FDM). The unsintered powder itself acts as the **natural support material**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are making a **sand sculpture on a beach** for a school project, but instead of using water to wet the sand, you have a **syringe that squirts a special glue** onto the sand. Here's how the process would unfold:

1. You pour a thin, even layer of **dry sand** into a tray.
2. Using your syringe (the *print head*), you squirt glue in a specific 2D pattern (say, a circle).
3. The glue seeps into the sand and **sticks those grains together** to form a solid 2D disc.
4. You pour **another thin layer of sand** on top.
5. You squirt glue again — this time drawing a slightly smaller circle.
6. The glue from this layer **binds the new sand** AND partially seeps down to strengthen the previous layer.
7. After repeating this 50–100 times, you have a **solid 3D sand-castle** sitting inside a tray of loose sand.
8. You brush away the loose sand, and **your 3D-printed object emerges** — complete, with no need for additional scaffolding.

That syringe-and-sand mechanism is **exactly how a 3D printer works** — the *sand* is the **powder** (plaster, metal, sand, ceramic), and the *glue* is the **liquid binder** (water-based, polymer, or colloidal silica).

## 1.3 Process Family & Material Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Powder Bed Resolution:** **0.1 mm** layer thickness
> **Standard Drop Volume:** **30–80 picolitres (pL)** per droplet
> **Build Speed:** ~**2–4 vertical mm per hour** depending on part cross-section
> **Material Waste Rate:** ~**95 % powder is recyclable** (only 5 % is bound into the part)

| 3DP Variant | Powder | Binder | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Color 3DP (ZPrint) | Plaster / Starch | Color aqueous binder | Concept models, medical visualization |
| Metal 3DP (DMP) | Stainless steel, Inconel, Ti | Polymer / Metallurgical binder | Functional metal parts (after sintering) |
| Sand 3DP (Voxeljet) | Foundry sand | Furanic resin binder | Casting patterns & cores |
| Ceramic 3DP | Alumina, Zirconia | Colloidal silica | Bioceramic scaffolds |

## 1.4 Why 3DP Matters in Engineering

- **Zero support structures** required — the surrounding powder bed supports overhangs and internal cavities.
- **Multi-material and full-colour** printing possible by varying binder composition.
- **High throughput** for concept models compared to SLA.
- **Scalable to large parts** (foundry patterns up to several metres).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle — The Physics of Binding

The 3DP process is governed by **three concurrent physical phenomena**:

1. **Drop-on-Demand (DOD) Inkjet Actuation**
   - A **piezoelectric** or **thermal-bubble** print head ejects a calibrated droplet of binder.
   - Droplet diameter at the powder surface: $d_d \approx 50$–$80 \ \mu m$.

2. **Capillary Infiltration of Binder into Powder**
   - The liquid binder flows into the **inter-particle pore network** by capillary action (governed by the **Washburn equation**, see §3).
   - Infiltration depth: typically 1.5–2× the layer thickness to ensure inter-layer bonding.

3. **Adhesive / Chemical Curing**
   - For plaster/starch: binder **dissolves a starch component** that subsequently hardens.
   - For metals: binder acts as an **adhesive green body**; final strength comes from a **post-process sintering step**.
   - For foundry sand: binder is a **resin** that polymerises thermally.

## 2.2 Process Walkthrough — 8 Logical Steps

| Step | Action | Engineering Function |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | CAD model is **sliced** into 2D layers (typical slice = 0.1 mm) | Translate geometry to machine toolpath |
| 2 | **Recoater blade** spreads a uniform powder layer | Establishes the printable surface |
| 3 | **Print head** scans across the bed, jetting binder droplets | Forms the cross-section |
| 4 | Binder **infiltrates and bonds** the powder grains | Creates a solid 2D region |
| 5 | Build platform **lowers by one layer thickness** | Prepares for next layer |
| 6 | **Repeat** Steps 2–5 until the part is complete | Accretion of layers |
| 7 | Build chamber is **vacuumed / excavated** to recover loose powder | Part extraction |
| 8 | **Post-processing**: de-powdering, curing, infiltration, sintering | Final mechanical/functional properties |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is the unsintered powder so useful as a support?**
> Because the powder has a **bulk density of ~45–55 % of theoretical**, it behaves like a **cohesive granular fluid**. It can support overhangs of nearly **90° (vertical)** without collapse and can be **brushed out** from any internal channel after printing.

## 2.3 Critical Process Parameters

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Effect on Part |
|---|---|---|---|
| Layer thickness | $h_L$ | 0.08 – 0.20 mm | Resolution vs. build time trade-off |
| Binder saturation | $S$ | 0.50 – 0.85 | Strength vs. distortion balance |
| Drop volume | $V_d$ | 30 – 80 pL | Line width & surface finish |
| Powder size | $d_p$ | 20 – 100 µm | Surface finish & flowability |
| Recoater speed | $v_r$ | 50 – 200 mm/s | Layer uniformity |
| Print head resolution | $R_{xy}$ | 300 – 600 DPI | In-plane accuracy |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are fair-game for KTU University Exam derivations and numerical problems.

| # | Formula | Meaning of Terms | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_L = \dfrac{h_{part}}{h_L}$ | Number of layers required | dimensionless |
| 2 | $T_{build} = N_L \cdot (t_{spread} + t_{print} + t_{dwell})$ | Total build time | s or h |
| 3 | $t_{print} = \dfrac{A_{build}}{v_{head} \cdot w_{nozzle}}$ | Time to print one layer | s |
| 4 | $V_{binder} = A_{layer} \cdot h_L \cdot \phi \cdot S$ | Volume of binder per layer | m³ |
| 5 | $M_{powder} = V_{build} \cdot \rho_{powder} \cdot (1 + k_{recycle})$ | Powder mass consumed | kg |
| 6 | $\ell^2 = \dfrac{\gamma \cdot r \cdot \cos\theta}{2\eta} \cdot t$ | Washburn infiltration length | m |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{V_{binder}}{V_{pores}}$ | Definition of binder saturation | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\rho_{green} = S \cdot \rho_{binder} + (1 - \phi) \cdot \rho_{solid}$ | Green (post-print) part density | kg/m³ |
| 9 | $R_{xy} = \dfrac{d_d}{\sqrt{2}}$ | Minimum in-plane feature size | m |
| 10 | $\eta_{powder} = \dfrac{M_{bound}}{M_{bed}} \times 100$ | Powder utilisation efficiency | % |

> **Where:**
> - $h_{part}$ = part height, $h_L$ = layer thickness
> - $A_{build}$ = build envelope area, $A_{layer}$ = part cross-section at that layer
> - $v_{head}$ = print head traverse speed, $w_{nozzle}$ = effective nozzle swath width
> - $\phi$ = powder bed porosity (typically 0.45–0.55)
> - $S$ = binder saturation, $\gamma$ = binder surface tension, $r$ = effective pore radius
> - $\theta$ = contact angle, $\eta$ = binder viscosity, $t$ = infiltration time
> - $k_{recycle}$ = powder recycle factor (typically 0.05–0.10)

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Foundry Industry:** 3D-printed sand moulds and cores eliminate the wooden pattern → **40–60 % lead time reduction** in casting tooling.
- **Medical Field:** Full-colour anatomical models from **CT/MRI DICOM data** for pre-surgical planning.
- **Architecture:** Detailed scale models with **multiple materials (translucent, opaque, coloured)**.
- **Aerospace & Automotive (Metal 3DP):** Low-volume production of **Inconel fuel nozzles, titanium brackets**, followed by sintering and HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing).
- **Education & Research:** Rapid low-cost concept models in mechanical design studios.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Total Build Time

**Given:** A part of height $h_{part} = 50$ mm is to be printed on a 3DP machine with the following parameters:
- Layer thickness $h_L = 0.1$ mm
- Build envelope area $A_{build} = 250 \times 250$ mm²
- Print head speed $v_{head} = 500$ mm/s
- Effective nozzle swath $w_{nozzle} = 0.5$ mm
- Recoating time $t_{spread} = 4$ s/layer
- Dwell time $t_{dwell} = 1$ s/layer

**Step 1: Number of Layers**
$$N_L = \frac{h_{part}}{h_L} = \frac{50 \text{ mm}}{0.1 \text{ mm}} = 500 \text{ layers}$$

**Step 2: Time to print ONE layer**
$$t_{print} = \frac{A_{build}}{v_{head} \cdot w_{nozzle}}$$

Substituting values (with unit consistency: convert $A_{build}$ to mm²):
$$t_{print} = \frac{250 \times 250}{500 \times 0.5} = \frac{62{,}500}{250} = 250 \text{ s}$$

**Step 3: Total time per layer**
$$t_{layer} = t_{spread} + t_{print} + t_{dwell} = 4 + 250 + 1 = 255 \text{ s/layer}$$

**Step 4: Total build time**
$$T_{build} = N_L \cdot t_{layer} = 500 \times 255 = 127{,}500 \text{ s}$$

Converting to hours:
$$T_{build} = \frac{127{,}500}{3600} \approx 35.4 \text{ hours}$$

> **Insight for KTU:** The printing of a single 50 mm tall part in this 3DP machine takes **~1.5 days** — this is why 3DP is favoured for **concept models**, not high-throughput production.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Binder Volume Requirement

**Given:** A cylindrical part of radius $r = 20$ mm and height $h_{part} = 30$ mm. Powder porosity $\phi = 0.5$. Required binder saturation $S = 0.7$.

**Step 1: Part volume and cross-sectional area**
$$V_{part} = \pi r^2 \cdot h_{part} = \pi (20)^2 \cdot 30 = 12{,}000 \pi \text{ mm}^3$$
$$A_{layer} = \pi r^2 = 400 \pi \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 2: Binder volume per layer**
$$V_{binder,layer} = A_{layer} \cdot h_L \cdot \phi \cdot S$$

Assuming effective layer thickness infiltrated equals the layer thickness $h_L = 0.1$ mm:
$$V_{binder,layer} = 400 \pi \cdot 0.1 \cdot 0.5 \cdot 0.7 = 14 \pi \text{ mm}^3 \approx 43.98 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Total binder volume**
$$N_L = \frac{30}{0.1} = 300 \text{ layers}$$
$$V_{binder,total} = 300 \cdot 14 \pi = 4{,}200 \pi \text{ mm}^3 \approx 13{,}195 \text{ mm}^3 \approx 13.2 \text{ mL}$$

> **Insight for KTU:** A *very small* volume of binder is required because the binder only fills the pore network. This is one reason 3DP is **cost-effective for prototypes**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Washburn Equation — Capillary Infiltration Depth

The **Washburn equation** governs how fast the binder penetrates the powder bed horizontally under capillary action. It is derived by balancing **capillary pressure** against **viscous drag (Hagen-Poiseuille flow)** in a cylindrical pore of radius $r$.

**Step 1: Capillary pressure (Young-Laplace)**
$$P_c = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

**Step 2: Viscous flow rate through a cylindrical pore (Hagen-Poiseuille)**
$$Q = \frac{\pi r^4}{8 \eta \ell} \cdot P_c$$

**Step 3: Relating flow to infiltration depth**

Since the infiltrated volume $V = A \cdot \ell = \pi r^2 \cdot \ell$, and $Q = \dfrac{dV}{dt} = \pi r^2 \dfrac{d\ell}{dt}$, we get:
$$\pi r^2 \frac{d\ell}{dt} = \frac{\pi r^4}{8 \eta \ell} \cdot \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$

Simplifying:
$$\frac{d\ell}{dt} = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta \ell}$$

Separating variables and integrating from 0 to $\ell$ over time $t$:
$$\ell \, d\ell = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \, dt$$

$$\int_0^{\ell} \ell' \, d\ell' = \frac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{4 \eta} \int_0^t dt$$

$$\boxed{\ell^2 = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2 \eta} \cdot t}$$

**Step 4: Infiltration time for known depth**

To achieve infiltration depth $\ell = 0.15$ mm (1.5 × layer thickness for inter-layer bonding) in a pore of $r = 25 \ \mu m$, with $\gamma = 0.05$ N/m, $\theta = 30°$, $\eta = 1.5 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s:
$$t = \frac{2 \eta \ell^2}{r \gamma \cos\theta}$$

$$t = \frac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.15 \times 10^{-3})^2}{25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 \times \cos 30°}$$

Numerator:
$$2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-3} \times 2.25 \times 10^{-8} = 6.75 \times 10^{-11}$$

Denominator:
$$25 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.05 \times 0.866 = 1.0825 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$t = \frac{6.75 \times 10^{-11}}{1.0825 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 6.24 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} = 62.4 \ \mu s$$

> **Insight for KTU:** The infiltration is **virtually instantaneous** at the pore scale — meaning the dwell time is dominated by **mechanical motion** (recoater + print head), not by capillary kinetics.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — 3DP vs. Other AM Processes

| Criterion | 3DP (Binder Jetting) | FDM | SLA | SLS |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binding mechanism | **Chemical binder** | Thermal extrusion | Photopolymerization | Thermal sintering |
| Support needed | **No** (powder bed) | Yes (raft/wall) | Yes (chassis) | No (powder bed) |
| Multi-colour | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| Typical accuracy | ±0.2 mm | ±0.5 mm | ±0.1 mm | ±0.3 mm |
| Post-processing | **Mandatory** (sintering/infiltration) | Minimal | UV cure + support removal | De-powdering |
| Best suited for | **Concept models, casting patterns** | Functional prototypes | High-detail prototypes | Functional polymer parts |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a 3D Printer

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **subsystems and their information/material flow** during a 3DP build cycle.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    cadInput["CAD MODEL INPUT"] --> slicer["SLICER SOFTWARE"]
    slicer --> toolpath["LAYER TOOLPATH GENERATION"]
    
    subgraph FEED["MATERIAL FEED SUBSYSTEM"]
        powderHopper["POWDER HOPPER"]
        recoater["RECOATER BLADE / ROLLER"]
        powderHopper --> recoater
    end
    
    subgraph BUILD["BUILD CHAMBER"]
        platform["BUILD PLATFORM PISTON"]
        powderBed["POWDER BED"]
        platform --- powderBed
        printHead["PIEZOELECTRIC PRINT HEAD"]
        binderReservoir["BINDER RESERVOIR"]
        binderReservoir --> printHead
        printHead -. jets binder droplets .-> powderBed
    end
    
    subgraph CTRL["CONTROL SUBSYSTEM"]
        motionCtrl["MOTION CONTROLLER"]
        tempCtrl["ENVIRONMENTAL CONTROL"]
        visionSys["OPTICAL MONITOR"]
    end
    
    toolpath --> motionCtrl
    motionCtrl --> recoater
    motionCtrl --> printHead
    motionCtrl --> platform
    tempCtrl --> BUILD
    visionSys -. feedback .-> motionCtrl
    
    powderBed --> dePowder["DE-POWDERING STATION"]
    dePowder --> greenPart["GREEN PART"]
    greenPart --> postProcess["POST-PROCESSING UNIT"]
    postProcess --> finalPart["FINAL SINTERED / INFILTRATED PART"]
    
    recoater -. unused powder .-> powderHopper
```

## 4.2 Sequential Process Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["START: LOAD CAD FILE"] --> B["SLICE INTO 2D LAYERS"]
    B --> C{"LAYER COUNTER N = 0"}
    C --> D["RECOAT POWDER LAYER"]
    D --> E["JET BINDER VIA PRINT HEAD"]
    E --> F["BINDER INFILTRATES POWDER"]
    F --> G["LOWER PLATFORM BY 0.1 MM"]
    G --> H{"N = TOTAL LAYERS?"}
    H -- "NO" --> I["INCREMENT N BY 1"] --> D
    H -- "YES" --> J["DE-POWDER GREEN PART"]
    J --> K["POST-PROCESS: CURE / SINTER / INFILTRATE"]
    K --> L["FINAL 3D PRINTED PART"]
    L --> M["END"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Powder Bed & Print Head Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Cross["CROSS-SECTION VIEW OF BUILD CHAMBER"]
        direction TB
        PISTON["BUILD PISTON (MOVABLE)"]
        BED["FRESH POWDER LAYER (hL = 0.1 mm)"]
        BOUND["BOUND REGION (PRINTHIS LAYER)"]
        NEXTBED["POWDER ABOVE (RECYCLED)"]
        PISTON --- BED
        BED --- BOUND
        BOUND --- NEXTBED
    end
    
    HEAD["PRINT HEAD ARRAY"] -. drops .-> BOUND
    
    RECOATERBLADE["RECOATER BLADE"] -. spreads new powder .-> BED
    NEXTBED -. excess .-> RECYCLER["POWDER RECYCLER HOPPER"]
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Differentiate 3DP from FDM.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 3DP | FDM |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Binder jetting onto powder | Thermoplastic extrusion |
| Binding mechanism | Chemical adhesion | Thermal fusion |
| Support | Not required (powder bed) | Required (extruded) |
| Material state | Powder + Liquid | Solid filament |
| Post-processing | Mandatory (curing/sintering) | Minimal |

*(1 mark for principle, 1 mark for binding/support, 1 mark for material/post-processing)*

---

### **Q2. List any four advantages of 3DP over traditional manufacturing.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **No support structures** required — the loose powder itself supports overhangs. *(0.75)*
2. **Multi-colour and multi-material** capability in a single build (e.g., ZPrint systems). *(0.75)*
3. **High material utilisation** — only ~5 % powder is bound; rest is recycled. *(0.75)*
4. **Fast turnaround for concept models** with no tooling required. *(0.75)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Do **NOT** confuse 3DP (binder jetting) with SLS (laser sintering) or FDM (extrusion). Examiners award zero marks for incorrect process identification.
> 2. **Always** mention **post-processing** (curing/infiltration/sintering) — omitting this loses 2 marks.
> 3. For numericals, **show unit conversion** (mm² to m², s to h). Examiner key deducts 0.5 marks per missing unit.
> 4. Do not skip the **powder bed porosity (φ)** in binder-volume derivations.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 (Model)]`

#### **(a) Explain the principle and step-by-step process of Three-Dimensional Printing with a neat schematic. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle (2 marks):**
3DP is an additive manufacturing process that builds parts by **selectively depositing a liquid binder onto a bed of powder** using an inkjet print head. Where the binder lands, the powder particles are locally joined; layer by layer, a 3D solid is formed within the unbound powder matrix.

**Process Steps (4 marks):**
1. **Slicing:** The 3D CAD model is digitally sliced into 2D horizontal layers of thickness $h_L = 0.1$ mm.
2. **Powder deposition:** A recoater spreads a uniform layer of powder (plaster, metal, or sand) over the build platform.
3. **Binder jetting:** A print head scans the bed and deposits binder droplets only over the cross-sectional area of the part.
4. **Curing/bonding:** The binder infiltrates the pores and bonds the powder particles.
5. **Layer lowering:** The platform descends by one layer thickness; a new powder layer is spread.
6. **Repetition:** Steps 2–5 repeat until the part is fully formed inside the powder bed.
7. **De-powdering:** Loose powder is vacuumed away, revealing the green part.
8. **Post-processing:** Curing, infiltration (for plaster), or sintering (for metal) yields the final part.

**Schematic (1 mark):** Refer to §4.1 / §4.2 of these notes.

#### **(b) A rectangular part of dimensions 80 mm × 40 mm × 20 mm is to be produced in a 3DP machine. Calculate the total binder volume required and the build time. Given: layer thickness 0.1 mm, powder porosity 0.5, binder saturation 0.7, recoating time 3 s, dwell time 1 s, print head speed 400 mm/s, swath width 0.4 mm, build envelope 300 × 300 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Part: $80 \times 40 \times 20$ mm
- $h_L = 0.1$ mm, $\phi = 0.5$, $S = 0.7$
- $v_{head} = 400$ mm/s, $w_{nozzle} = 0.4$ mm
- $A_{build} = 300 \times 300 = 90{,}000$ mm²
- $t_{spread} = 3$ s, $t_{dwell} = 1$ s

**Step 1: Number of layers** *(1 mark)*
$$N_L = \frac{20}{0.1} = 200 \text{ layers}$$

**Step 2: Binder volume per layer** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{binder,layer} = A_{layer} \cdot h_L \cdot \phi \cdot S = (80 \times 40) \times 0.1 \times 0.5 \times 0.7 = 112 \text{ mm}^3$$

**Step 3: Total binder volume** *(1 mark)*
$$V_{binder,total} = 200 \times 112 = 22{,}400 \text{ mm}^3 = 22.4 \text{ mL}$$

**Step 4: Time per layer** *(2 marks)*
$$t_{print} = \frac{A_{build}}{v_{head} \cdot w_{nozzle}} = \frac{90{,}000}{400 \times 0.4} = \frac{90{,}000}{160} = 562.5 \text{ s}$$
$$t_{layer} = 3 + 562.5 + 1 = 566.5 \text{ s}$$

**Step 5: Total build time** *(2 marks)*
$$T_{build} = 200 \times 566.5 = 113{,}300 \text{ s} = 31.47 \text{ h}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_{binder} = 22.4$ mL, $T_{build} = 31.47$ hours.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 (Model)]`

#### **(a) Discuss the advantages, limitations, and applications of 3D Printing in detail. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Advantages (3 marks):**
1. **No support structures** — surrounding powder bed supports overhangs and internal cavities.
2. **Multi-colour / multi-material** capability (e.g., Z Corp. ZPrinter 650 prints in millions of colours).
3. **High material efficiency** — only 5–10 % of powder is bound, the rest is recycled.
4. **Large build envelopes** — possible for sand moulds of several metres.
5. **Fast concept model production** — no tooling or moulds required.

**Limitations (2 marks):**
1. **Low green strength** — parts are fragile and need post-processing.
2. **Limited mechanical properties** — inferior to injection moulding or SLS.
3. **Surface finish** is grainy; secondary finishing may be needed.
4. **Long build times** for fully dense metal parts.
5. **Powder handling hazards** — fine powders can be combustible (e.g., titanium, aluminium).

**Applications (2 marks):**
- **Conceptual design verification** in automotive and consumer goods.
- **Anatomical models** for pre-surgical planning (DICOM-to-3DP).
- **Foundry moulds and cores** for casting.
- **Architectural scale models.**
- **Low-volume functional metal parts** (after sintering + HIP).

#### **(b) Explain the Washburn equation as applied to binder infiltration in 3DP. A binder of surface tension 0.06 N/m and viscosity $2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s penetrates a powder bed of effective pore radius 30 µm. The contact angle is 25°. Find the time required for the binder to infiltrate to a depth of 0.2 mm. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Theoretical Explanation (3 marks):**

The **Washburn equation** describes the capillary-driven infiltration of a liquid binder into the porous powder network. It is derived by balancing the **Young-Laplace capillary pressure** with the **viscous drag (Hagen-Poiseuille flow)** through cylindrical pores.

The integrated form gives the **infiltration depth squared** as proportional to time:
$$\ell^2 = \frac{r \, \gamma \, \cos\theta}{2 \eta} \cdot t$$

Where:
- $\ell$ = infiltration depth
- $r$ = effective pore radius
- $\gamma$ = binder surface tension
- $\theta$ = contact angle
- $\eta$ = binder dynamic viscosity
- $t$ = infiltration time

In 3DP, this equation explains why **layer thickness and droplet volume must be tuned** to ensure the binder reaches the previous (underlying) layer for inter-layer fusion.

**Numerical Solution (4 marks):**

**Given:**
- $\gamma = 0.06$ N/m
- $\eta = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $r = 30 \ \mu m = 30 \times 10^{-6}$ m
- $\theta = 25°$
- $\ell = 0.2 \text{ mm} = 0.2 \times 10^{-3}$ m

**Rearranging the Washburn equation for $t$:**
$$t = \frac{2 \eta \ell^2}{r \gamma \cos\theta}$$

**Numerator:**
$$2 \times 2 \times 10^{-3} \times (0.2 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 4 \times 10^{-3} \times 4 \times 10^{-8} = 1.6 \times 10^{-10}$$

**Denominator:**
$$30 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.06 \times \cos 25° = 1.8 \times 10^{-6} \times 0.9063 = 1.6314 \times 10^{-6}$$

**Final Calculation:**
$$t = \frac{1.6 \times 10^{-10}}{1.6314 \times 10^{-6}} = 9.81 \times 10^{-5} \text{ s} \approx 98 \ \mu s$$

**Final Answer:** The binder takes approximately **$9.8 \times 10^{-5}$ seconds (~98 microseconds)** to infiltrate 0.2 mm. This confirms that **mechanical motion** (recoating, print head travel) is the rate-limiting step in 3DP, not capillary kinetics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (KTU Module 3 — 3DP)**

- **Inventor:** MIT, 1989 — Sachs, Cima, Cornie (Emanual Sachs et al.).
- **Principle:** **Inkjet-based binder deposition** onto a powder bed (NOT extrusion, NOT photopolymerization, NOT laser melting).
- **Standard layer thickness:** **0.1 mm**.
- **Drop volume:** **30–80 pL** (pico-litres).
- **Powder porosity $\phi$:** typically **0.45–0.55**.
- **Binder saturation $S$:** typically **0.5–0.85**; low = weak part, high = distortion.
- **Supports:** **NOT required** — unsintered powder supports overhangs.
- **Build time equation:** $T_{build} = \dfrac{h_{part}}{h_L} \cdot \left(t_{spread} + \dfrac{A_{build}}{v_{head} w_{nozzle}} + t_{dwell}\right)$.
- **Binder volume equation:** $V_{binder} = A_{layer} \cdot h_L \cdot \phi \cdot S$ (per layer).
- **Washburn infiltration depth:** $\ell^2 = \dfrac{r \gamma \cos\theta}{2 \eta} \cdot t$.
- **Post-processing is MANDATORY** for strength — curing (plaster), infiltration (epoxy), or sintering (metal).
- **Material families:** Plaster/starch, metal powders (SS, Inconel, Ti), foundry sand, ceramics.
- **Major commercial 3DP systems:** Z Corp (now 3D Systems), ExOne/Desktop Metal, Voxeljet, HP Multi Jet Fusion (derivative).
- **Best applications:** Concept models, anatomical visualisation, foundry moulds/cores, architectural models, low-volume metal parts.
- **Key limitation:** Green parts are **fragile**; mechanical strength only after post-processing.
- **KTU 14-mark favourite:** Process explanation + numerical on build time / binder volume / Washburn equation.
- **Examiner trap:** Do not call 3DP "3D printing" generically — specify **binder jetting** in your answer.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
