# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**
**Define customized implants. List any four advantages of additive manufacturing in producing customized implants compared to conventional manufacturing.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (1 mark):**
Customized implants are patient-specific medical devices designed and fabricated using additive manufacturing by processing bio-CAD models derived from clinical imaging data (CT/MRI), tailored to match the unique anatomical geometry of an individual patient.

**Advantages over conventional manufacturing (4 × 0.5 = 2 marks):**

1. **Mass Customization** — each implant is unique without retooling cost
2. **Complex Internal Geometries** — lattice structures for osseointegration impossible to machine
3. **Reduced Surgical Time** — pre-planned, pre-fit implants minimize intra-operative modifications
4. **Material Efficiency** — ~95% utilization vs. 10–20% for subtractive methods
5. **Lightweight Design** — topology optimization reduces implant mass by 40–70%
6. **Improved Patient Outcomes** — better anatomical fit, faster recovery, lower revision rates

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**
**What is the Hounsfield Unit (HU)? Mention the typical HU range used to segment cortical bone from a CT scan.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Definition (2 marks):**
The Hounsfield Unit (HU) is a dimensionless quantitative scale describing radiodensity used in CT imaging. It is defined as:

$$HU = 1000 \times \dfrac{\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water}}{\mu_{water}}$$

where $\mu$ is the linear attenuation coefficient.

**Cortical bone HU range (1 mark):**
Cortical bone typically has a HU value between **+700 and +3000**, while cancellous (trabecular) bone ranges from **+200 to +700**. A typical threshold of **HU ≥ 200** is used to segment bone tissue from soft tissue in CT scans.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the complete Bio-CAD workflow for designing and producing a customized cranio-facial implant using additive manufacturing. Include all six stages from patient scanning to sterilization. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss Selective Laser Melting (SLM) and Electron Beam Melting (EBM) for Ti6Al4V implant production. Compare their process parameters, build environment, advantages, and limitations. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Stage 1: Medical Image Acquisition (1 mark)**
High-resolution **CT scanning** is performed with slice thickness 0.5–1.0 mm, generating a stack of DICOM files. CT is preferred over MRI for craniofacial implants because bone has high radiodensity and clear contrast.

**Stage 2: Image Segmentation (1 mark)**
Using software (Mimics, 3D Slicer, OsiriX), the DICOM stack is segmented by **Hounsfield Unit thresholding** (typically HU > 200 for bone). The 2D slices are stacked and reconstructed into a 3D STL mesh of the patient's skull defect region.

**Stage 3: Bio-CAD Modeling (1.5 marks)**
The defect region is mirrored from the contralateral (healthy) side or reconstructed using NURBS surface modeling. Boolean operations generate the implant geometry, including a **solid outer shell** for protection and an **inner porous lattice** (typically gyroid TPMS) for osseointegration with pore size 200–500 µm.

**Stage 4: Design Validation (1 mark)**
Finite Element Analysis (FEA) in ANSYS or ABAQUS simulates physiological loads (masticatory forces, impact). Maximum von Mises stress should be below the fatigue limit of Ti6Al4V (≈ 500 MPa). Topology optimization adjusts the lattice density gradient.

**Stage 5: AM Fabrication (1.5 marks)**
The validated STL is sliced using Magics or Materialise 3-matic, and built on an **EBM** or **SLM** machine using ASTM F136-grade Ti6Al4V ELI powder (particle size 45–105 µm). The implant is built with build orientation optimized to minimize support structures and post-processing.

**Stage 6: Post-Processing and Sterilization (1 mark)**
- Powder removal (blasting, vacuuming)
- Stress relief heat treatment
- Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) at 920°C / 100 MPa
- Surface finishing (polishing of bone-contact surfaces)
- Sterilization by **autoclave (121°C, 15 psi, 20 min)** or **gamma irradiation**
- Quality control via CT scanning
- Surgical implantation with pre-planned fixation screws

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

| Parameter | SLM | EBM |
|-----------|-----|-----|
| Energy source | Fiber laser (1070 nm) | Electron beam (60 kV) |
| Build environment | Inert argon atmosphere | Vacuum (~10⁻⁴ mbar) |
| Layer thickness | 20–50 µm | 50–100 µm |
| Beam power | 100–400 W | 1000–3000 W |
| Scan speed | 400–1600 mm/s | Up to 10,000 mm/s |
| Residual stress | Higher (post-heat treatment needed) | Lower (high temp build) |
| Build time | Slower | Faster (2–3×) |
| Surface finish | Smoother (Ra 5–10 µm) | Rougher (Ra 10–20 µm) |
| Powder reuse | Limited (oxidation) | Better (vacuum) |
| Energy density | 60–120 J/mm³ | Lower required |
| Dimensional accuracy | ±0.05 mm | ±0.1–0.2 mm |
| Part density | > 99.5% | > 99% |
| Material options | Ti6Al4V, CoCr, Inconel | Mainly Ti6Al4V |

**Advantages of SLM (1 mark):**
- Higher accuracy and resolution
- Smoother surfaces, less post-processing
- Wider material range
- Better for fine lattice features

**Advantages of EBM (1 mark):**
- Faster build times
- Lower residual stress
- Better for bulky orthopedic implants
- Vacuum prevents oxygen contamination

**Limitations of SLM (0.5 mark):** Argon cost, support structures required, slower.
**Limitations of EBM (0.5 mark):** X-ray generation safety, limited materials, rougher surface.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice Alternative)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** What are TPMS-based lattice structures? Explain the design of a gyroid lattice for a porous bone implant, including the surface equation and its advantages for osseointegration. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A customized Ti6Al4V hip implant uses a porous lattice with relative density 0.25. Using the Gibson-Ashby model with $C_1 = 1.0$ and $n = 2$, calculate the effective modulus of the lattice. The solid Ti6Al4V has $E_s = 110$ GPa. Comment on the suitability of this modulus with respect to cortical bone ($E \approx 15$ GPa). **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer for Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
**Triply Periodic Minimal Surfaces (TPMS)** are mathematically defined, infinitely repeating, zero-mean-curvature surfaces in 3D space. Common TPMS types for implants include **Gyroid, Schwarz Primitive, Schwarz Diamond, and Neovius**. They are generated as implicit equations and can be converted to solid lattice structures with controllable porosity.

**Gyroid surface equation (2 marks):**
The gyroid TPMS is defined by the implicit equation:

$$\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$$

where $t$ is the iso-surface threshold controlling wall thickness. By varying $t$, the volume fraction (and thus relative density) can be tuned continuously from 0 to 1. A typical design uses $t$ in the range −1.0 to +1.0 to produce 20–80% porosity.

**Pore size control (1 mark):**
The characteristic pore size $d_{pore}$ scales as:

$$d_{pore} = \dfrac{2\pi}{k}$$

where $k$ is the wave number (unit cell count per mm). For bone ingrowth, $k$ is chosen to yield pores of 300–500 µm.

**Advantages of gyroid for osseointegration (2.5 marks):**
1. **Zero sharp corners** — minimizes stress concentration and crack initiation
2. **High surface area-to-volume ratio** — promotes cell attachment
3. **Open, interconnected porosity** — permits vascularization and nutrient transport
4. **Tunable stiffness** — modulus can be matched to host bone via relative density (Gibson-Ashby model)
5. **Manufacturable in SLM** — self-supporting, minimal support structures needed
6. **Smooth fluid flow** — no dead zones, mimicking natural bone architecture

#### Model Answer for Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Relative density $\rho^*/\rho_s = 0.25$
- $E_s = 110$ GPa
- $C_1 = 1.0$, $n = 2$
- Cortical bone $E_{bone} \approx 15$ GPa

**Step 1: Apply the Gibson-Ashby equation (3 marks)**

$$\dfrac{E^*}{E_s} = C_1 \left(\dfrac{\rho^*}{\rho_s}\right)^n$$

**Step 2: Substitute values (2 marks)**

$$\dfrac{E^*}{E_s} = 1.0 \times (0.25)^2 = 0.0625$$

**Step 3: Solve for $E^*$ (1 mark)**

$$E^* = 0.0625 \times 110 = 6.875 \text{ GPa}$$

**Step 4: Comparison and comment (1 mark)**
The lattice modulus **6.875 GPa** is well below cortical bone (15 GPa) but in the range of **cancellous bone (0.5–2 GPa)**. This indicates:
- If the implant interfaces primarily with cancellous bone, the modulus is **well-matched** — minimal stress shielding.
- If the implant interfaces with cortical bone, the modulus is **lower than ideal** — possible micromotion at the bone-implant interface.
- **Recommendation:** Increase relative density to ~0.35–0.40 to raise modulus to ~13–17 GPa, matching cortical bone. Use **gradient lattice** with denser periphery and porous core.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** in the final answer (must state GPa)
> 2. **Wrong identification of $C_1$ and $n$** — these are specific to the cell geometry; do NOT assume universal values
> 3. **Not commenting on physiological relevance** — examiners expect a clinical interpretation, not just a numerical answer
> 4. **Confusing cortical and cancellous bone modulus** — cortical ≈ 15 GPa; cancellous ≈ 0.5–2 GPa
> 5. **Not mentioning stress shielding** as a consequence of modulus mismatch
> 6. In workflow questions, students often **skip the FE validation stage** — it carries marks

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

### ⚡ High-Yield Points (Must Memorize for KTU ESE)

- [ ] **HU scale:** Air = −1000, Water = 0, Bone = +200 to +3000, Metal = > +3000
- [ ] **HU equation:** $HU = 1000 \times (\mu_{tissue} - \mu_{water})/\mu_{water}$
- [ ] **Optimal pore size for bone ingrowth:** **200–500 µm**
- [ ] **Standard bone-ingrowth porosity range:** **50–80%**
- [ ] **Ti6Al4V modulus:** Solid = **110 GPa**, Cortical bone = **15 GPa**, Cancellous = **0.5–2 GPa**
- [ ] **VED formula:** $E_d = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$, units **J/mm³**
- [ ] **Optimum VED for Ti6Al4V SLM:** **80–100 J/mm³**
- [ ] **Gibson-Ashby:** $E^*/E_s = C_1 (\rho^*/\rho_s)^n$, with **n ≈ 2** for open-cell
- [ ] **Gyroid TPMS equation:** $\sin(x)\cos(y) + \sin(y)\cos(z) + \sin(z)\cos(x) = t$
- [ ] **Bio-CAD workflow:** 6 stages — Scan → Segment → CAD → Validate → Fabricate → Sterilize
- [ ] **Sterilization standard:** Autoclave **121°C / 15 psi / 20 min**
- [ ] **DICOM** = medical imaging file format; **STL** = 3D mesh format
- [ ] **ASTM standards for medical Ti:** F136 (Ti6Al4V ELI), F75 (CoCrMo)
- [ ] **Biocompatibility standard:** ISO 10993

### ⚠️ Conceptual Distinctions (Frequently Confused)

- **Implant vs Prosthesis:** Implant = inserted into body; Prosthesis = replaces missing part (may be external)
- **SLM vs EBM:** SLM uses laser in argon; EBM uses electron beam in vacuum
- **Scaffold vs Implant:** Scaffold is bioresorbable and temporary; implant is permanent
- **CT vs MRI:** CT for bone; MRI for soft tissue
- **Stress shielding vs Stress concentration:** Shielding = implant too stiff; Concentration = geometric sharp corner

### 🏭 Industry-Famous AM Implant Examples (Examiner Likes These)

- **Hearing aids** — 99% produced by SLA globally
- **Cranial plates (OXPEKK, Ti6Al4V EBM)** — 5000+ surgeries worldwide
- **Tracheal splints** (University of Michigan) — bioresorbable PCL
- **Mandibular reconstruction** (LayerWise, Materialise)
- **Custom knee implants** (ConforMIS)
- **Dental crowns** (same-day dentistry)

### 📌 KTU Mark Distribution Tip

For a 14-mark Part B question, allocate as follows:

| Component | Marks |
|-----------|-------|
| Definition / concept | 1–2 |
| Diagram / workflow | 2–3 |
| Process/material detail | 3–4 |
| Comparison table | 2–3 |
| Numerical / derivation | 2–3 |
| Clinical application | 1–2 |

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute KTU Tip:** If asked *"Explain the role of AM in customized implants,"* always structure the answer in the 6-stage Bio-CAD pipeline, name the Hounsfield scale, mention Ti6Al4V + EBM/SLM, cite one clinical example (e.g., hearing aids or cranial plates), and conclude with the stress-shielding mitigation through lattice design.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Bio-Additive Manufacturing: Computer Aided Tissue Engineering (CATE)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Bio-Additive Manufacturing: Computer Aided Tissue Engineering (CATE)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Computer-Aided Tissue Engineering (CATE)** is a digitally integrated, multi-disciplinary paradigm that synergizes **medical imaging**, **computational modeling (CAD/CAE)**, **biomaterial science**, and **additive biofabrication (3D bioprinting)** to design, simulate, manufacture, and validate patient-specific living tissue constructs and organ substitutes.

In the KTU 2024 context, CATE represents the convergence of:
- **Digital Layer (CAD/CAM/CAE)** → geometric design + process simulation
- **Biological Layer (Cells + Scaffolds + Signals)** → the tissue engineering triad
- **Manufacturing Layer (Bio-AM)** → layer-by-layer deposition of living matter

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of CATE like "3D Printing a Living LEGO House":**
> Imagine a damaged human organ (say, a piece of skin or cartilage). A traditional doctor might transplant from a donor. In CATE, we:
> 1. **Scan** the damaged region like 3D-scanning a broken LEGO piece.
> 2. **Digitally design** a perfect replacement scaffold on a computer.
> 3. **Load a "biological ink"** (called **bioink** — a mixture of living cells + hydrogel) into a 3D printer.
> 4. **Print** the scaffold layer-by-layer in a sterile environment.
> 5. The cells then **grow, mature, and integrate** with the patient's body — just like seeds sprouting in a custom-built greenhouse.

## 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Constants

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Scaffold Design Metrics (KTU Board Emphasis):**
> - **Pore Size:** **100–500 μm** (optimal for cell infiltration and vascularization)
> - **Porosity:** **≥ 70 %** (for nutrient transport and cell migration)
> - **Interconnectivity:** **> 90 %** (open, connected pores)
> - **Young's Modulus Target:** matched to native tissue (bone ≈ **10–20 GPa**, cartilage ≈ **0.5–1 MPa**)
> - **Surface Area-to-Volume Ratio:** maximized for cell attachment

## 1.4 The Tissue Engineering Triad

| Pillar | Engineering Term | Biological Function |
|---|---|---|
| **Scaffold** | 3D printed matrix | Structural support, mechanical integrity |
| **Cells** | Living "ink" | Tissue formation, regeneration |
| **Signals** (Biochemical + Mechanical) | Growth factors, bioreactors | Direct cell differentiation and maturation |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The CATE Workflow — 7 Logical Steps

> [!NOTE]
> This workflow is the **most frequently asked 14-mark question** in KTU ESE on this topic. Memorize it in order.

1. **Medical Imaging Acquisition** → CT / MRI / μCT scans yield DICOM data of the defect site.
2. **Image Segmentation & 3D Reconstruction** → software (Mimics, 3D Slicer) converts scans into a **STL / .mgf point cloud**.
3. **CAD Scaffold Design** → explicit porous architecture modeled in SolidWorks / Rhino / nTopology.
4. **Biomechanical Simulation (CAE)** → **Finite Element Analysis (FEA)** predicts stress, strain, and nutrient diffusion through the scaffold.
5. **Biomaterial & Bioink Formulation** → selection of hydrogels (alginate, GelMA, collagen, PCL).
6. **Bio-Additive Manufacturing (BAM)** → bioprinting via extrusion / inkjet / laser / SLA.
7. **Post-Processing & Bioreactor Maturation** → cell seeding, perfusion, in-vitro conditioning.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Imaging step** ensures *anatomical fidelity* — the implant must match the defect geometry to within **±100 μm**.
- **CAD step** allows *patient-specific customization* — a key differentiator from traditional implants.
- **CAE step** prevents *trial-and-error in vitro* — saves time and reduces animal testing.
- **Bioink step** balances *printability* (viscosity, shear-thinning) with *cell viability* (shear stress < **5 kPa**).
- **BAM step** enables *complex internal architecture* impossible with conventional machining.
- **Bioreactor step** provides *dynamic mechanical stimulation* mimicking in-vivo conditions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable in Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions.

| # | Formula | Description | Key Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P \;=\; \dfrac{V_{\text{voids}}}{V_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | Scaffold porosity | % |
| 2 | $k \;=\; \dfrac{\mu \, Q \, L}{A \, \Delta P}$ | Darcy's Law — permeability | m² |
| 3 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{\pi \, \Delta P \, r^{4}}{8 \, \mu \, L}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille flow in pore | m³/s |
| 4 | $J \;=\; -D \, \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ | Fick's 1st Law — nutrient diffusion | mol/(m²·s) |
| 5 | $E_{\text{scaffold}} \;=\; E_{\text{matrix}} (1-P)^{n}$ | Gibson-Ashby model for cellular solids | Pa |
| 6 | $\tau \;=\; \mu \, \dot{\gamma}$ | Newtonian shear stress in bioink | Pa |
| 7 | $\eta(\dot{\gamma}) \;=\; K \, \dot{\gamma}^{n-1}$ | Power-law (shear-thinning) viscosity | Pa·s |
| 8 | $\sigma_{\text{cell}} \;=\; \eta \dot{\gamma}$ | Cell shear stress during extrusion | Pa |
| 9 | $\phi \;=\; \dfrac{\text{V}_{\text{cells}}}{\text{V}_{\text{bioink}}}$ | Cell density in bioink | cells/mL |
| 10 | $\dfrac{dX}{dt} \;=\; \mu_{\max} X \left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{\max}}\right)$ | Logistic cell growth kinetics | cells/mL |
| 11 | $Da \;=\; \dfrac{k \, L^{2}}{D}$ | Damköhler number (reaction vs diffusion) | dimensionless |
| 12 | $P_e \;=\; \dfrac{v \, L}{D}$ | Péclet number (advection vs diffusion) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Hint:** The **Gibson-Ashby model** (Formula 5) and **Darcy's Law** (Formula 2) are the two highest-yield equations. Always state the assumption *n ≈ 1.5–2* for open-cell foams in the board exam.

## 2.4 Biofabrication Process Map (BAM Techniques)

| Technique | Resolution | Cell Viability | Material | KTU Exam Frequency |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion** | 100–500 μm | 40–80 % | Hydrogels, pastes | ★★★★★ |
| **Inkjet** | 10–50 μm | > 85 % | Low-viscosity bioinks | ★★★ |
| **Laser-Assisted (LIFT)** | 10–100 μm | > 95 % | High-viscosity, solid | ★★★ |
| **Stereolithography (SLA)** | 25–100 μm | > 85 % | Photocurable resins | ★★★★ |
| **FDM (synthetic scaffolds)** | 100–250 μm | N/A (post-seed) | PCL, PLA, PLGA | ★★★★★ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Orthopedics:** Patient-specific bone grafts from CT data.
- **Cardiology:** Vascularized cardiac patches.
- **Dermatology:** Skin substitutes for burn victims.
- **Dental:** Customized scaffolds for periodontal regeneration.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** 3D-printed organ-on-chip for drug testing (reduces animal trials).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Hagen-Poiseuille Flow Through a Scaffold Pore

**Problem Setup:** Nutrient medium (viscosity $\mu$) flows through a cylindrical pore (radius $r$, length $L$) of the scaffold under pressure gradient $\Delta P$. Derive the volumetric flow rate.

### Step 1 — Newton's Law of Viscosity
For steady, laminar, incompressible flow in a cylindrical pore, the shear stress at radius $r$ is:

$$\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

### Step 2 — Force Balance on a Cylindrical Fluid Element
Consider a cylindrical control volume of radius $r$ and length $L$. The forces are pressure force on the ends and shear force on the surface.

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = -\tau_{rz} \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot L$$

### Step 3 — Solve for $\tau_{rz}$

$$\tau_{rz} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

### Step 4 — Equate to Newton's Law of Viscosity

$$-\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L}$$

### Step 5 — Integrate with Boundary Condition $v_z(R) = 0$ (no-slip)

$$v_z(r) = \int \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} r \, dr = \frac{\Delta P}{2\mu L} \cdot \frac{r^{2}}{2} + C_{1}$$

Applying $v_z(R)=0$:

$$C_{1} = -\frac{\Delta P \, R^{2}}{4\mu L}$$

### Step 6 — Velocity Profile

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4\mu L}\left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

### Step 7 — Integrate Over the Cross-Section to Get Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = \int_{0}^{R} v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr$$

$$Q = \frac{2\pi \Delta P}{4\mu L} \int_{0}^{R} \left(R^{2} r - r^{3}\right) dr$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left[ \frac{R^{2} r^{2}}{2} - \frac{r^{4}}{4} \right]_{0}^{R}$$

$$Q = \frac{\pi \Delta P}{2\mu L} \left(\frac{R^{4}}{2} - \frac{R^{4}}{4}\right)$$

$$\boxed{Q = \frac{\pi \, \Delta P \, R^{4}}{8 \mu L}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation**, foundational for analyzing nutrient transport in CATE scaffolds. Note the **R⁴ dependence** — doubling the pore radius increases flow by **16×**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gibson-Ashby Model for Scaffold Modulus

**Background:** A 3D-printed scaffold is treated as an open-cell foam. Derive the relationship between relative density and relative modulus.

### Step 1 — Define Relative Density
For an open-cell foam, relative density $\rho^* / \rho_s$ is related to porosity $P$:

$$\frac{\rho^*}{\rho_s} = 1 - P$$

### Step 2 — Gibson-Ashby Empirical Law
For open-cell foams under linear elastic loading:

$$\frac{E^*}{E_s} = C \left( \frac{\rho^*}{\rho_s} \right)^{n}$$

where $C \approx 1$ and $n \approx 2$ for open-cell polymer foams.

### Step 3 — Substitute Porosity Relation

$$\boxed{E_{\text{scaffold}} = E_{\text{matrix}} (1 - P)^{2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** If scaffold porosity is **80 %** $(1-P = 0.2)$, then the modulus drops to **4 %** of the base material. This is the **mechanical-biologicial trade-off** the examiner loves to test.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Bioink Rheology Decision

| Bioink Type | Viscosity Behavior | Printability Cell Viability | Shear Stress During Print |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Alginate (2 % w/v)** | Newtonian, $\eta \approx 0.1$–$1$ Pa·s | High / Medium | Low |
| **GelMA (10 %)** | Shear-thinning, $\eta \approx 0.5$–$5$ Pa·s | Medium / High | Medium |
| **Collagen (3 mg/mL)** | Shear-thinning, $\eta \approx 0.3$–$3$ Pa·s | Low / High | Low |
| **Pluronic F-127** | Thermoreversible, $\eta \approx 100$ Pa·s at RT | High / Low (wash-out needed) | High |
| **PCL (molten)** | Newtonian at 90 °C, $\eta \approx 1000$ Pa·s | High (synthetic, post-seed) | N/A |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical constraint during extrusion bioprinting:** Cell shear stress $\sigma_{\text{cell}} = \eta \cdot \dot{\gamma}$ should be kept **< 5 kPa** to maintain viability **> 70 %**.

---

## 3.4 Computational FEA in CATE — The Numerical Loop

A typical CATE FEA workflow involves:

1. **Geometry import** (STL from medical imaging)
2. **Meshing** (tetrahedral elements, size ≈ **0.1–0.5 mm**)
3. **Material assignment** (hyperelastic for hydrogel, elastic for polymer)
4. **Boundary conditions** (physiological loads: compression **2–10 %** for cartilage)
5. **Solver** (Abaqus, ANSYS, COMSOL Multiphysics)
6. **Post-processing** — von Mises stress, fluid velocity, oxygen concentration

The output drives the **iterative redesign loop** until scaffold mechanics match native tissue within **±10 %**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 End-to-End CATE Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Patient Defect Scan] --> B[CT / MRI / μCT Imaging]
    B --> C[DICOM Data Extraction]
    C --> D[Image Segmentation Tool]
    D --> E[3D Anatomical Model STL]
    E --> F[Scaffold CAD Design]
    F --> G[Biomaterial Selection Module]
    G --> H[Bioink Formulation Lab]
    H --> I[Bioprinter Layer by Layer Deposition]
    I --> J[In Vitro Cell Seeding]
    J --> K[Bioreactor Maturation]
    K --> L[Functional Tissue Construct]
    L --> M[Implant into Patient]
    F --> N[FEA Biomechanical Simulation]
    N --> O{Design Meets Criteria}
    O -->|No| F
    O -->|Yes| I
    I --> P[Process Monitoring Sensors]
    P --> Q[Quality Control Output]
```

## 4.2 CATE Triad — Scaffold, Cells, Signals

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Scaffold Engineering] --> A1[3D Porous Architecture]
    A --> A2[Biocompatible Material]
    A --> A3[Controlled Degradation]
    B[Cell Component] --> B1[Autologous Stem Cells]
    B --> B2[Differentiated Cell Line]
    B --> B3[Cell Density Optimization]
    C[Signals Component] --> C1[Growth Factor BMP TGF]
    C --> C2[Mechanical Stimulation]
    C --> C3[Electrical Cues]
    A1 --> D[Functional Tissue]
    B1 --> D
    C2 --> D
```

## 4.3 Bio-Additive Manufacturing Process Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Bio Additive Manufacturing] --> B1[Extrusion Based]
    A0 --> B2[Inkjet Based]
    A0 --> B3[Laser Assisted LIFT]
    A0 --> B4[Stereolithography SLA]
    A0 --> B5[FDM Synthetic Scaffold]
    B1 --> C1[Pneumatic Piston Screw]
    B2 --> C2[Thermal Piezoelectric]
    B3 --> C3[Laser Induced Forward Transfer]
    B4 --> C4[DLP Two Photon Polymerization]
    B5 --> C5[PCL PLA PLGA Filament]
    C1 --> D1[Hydrogel Bioink]
    C2 --> D2[Low Viscosity Bioink]
    C3 --> D3[High Viscosity Cell Suspension]
    C4 --> D4[Photocurable Bio Resin]
    C5 --> D5[Post Seeding Required]
```

## 4.4 CAD CAE BAM Integration in CATE

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Medical Imaging Module] --> B[3D Reconstruction Module]
    B --> C[CAD Scaffold Designer]
    C --> D[Biomaterial Database]
    D --> E[Process Simulation CAE]
    E --> F[Bioprinter Slicer Software]
    F --> G[Bio Additive Manufacturing Cell]
    G --> H[In Vitro Bioreactor]
    H --> I[In Vivo Implantation]
    I --> J[Clinical Feedback Loop]
    J --> C
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KTU Board Tip):** The **feedback loop from clinical feedback to CAD designer** is what makes CATE truly *patient-specific* and is a frequently asked **3-mark short-answer** question.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
Define **Computer-Aided Tissue Engineering (CATE)**. List the **three pillars** of tissue engineering.

**Model Answer:**

> **CATE** is an integrated computational and manufacturing framework that combines medical imaging, CAD/CAE-based design, biomaterial science, and additive bioprinting to fabricate patient-specific living tissue constructs.
>
> **Three Pillars of Tissue Engineering:**
> 1. **Scaffold** — 3D matrix providing structural and mechanical support
> 2. **Cells** — living biological component (e.g., stem cells, chondrocytes)
> 3. **Signals** — biochemical (growth factors) + biophysical (mechanical, electrical) cues directing cell behavior

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
What is a **bioink**? State **two key rheological properties** required for an ideal bioink.

**Model Answer:**

> A **bioink** is a printable formulation consisting of living cells encapsulated within a biocompatible hydrogel matrix (e.g., alginate, GelMA, collagen).
>
> **Key Rheological Properties:**
> 1. **Shear-thinning behavior** — viscosity decreases under shear, enabling extrusion while protecting cells
> 2. **Rapid post-printing crosslinking** — solidification (ionic, photo, thermal) to retain 3D shape
>
> *Bonus point:* Cell viability > **70 %** after printing.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students often **skip the digital workflow steps** (imaging → segmentation → CAD). Always draw the **complete pipeline** in your answer.
> - **Do not** confuse **bioink** (with cells) with **biomaterial ink** (cell-free scaffold material).
> - Always **state units** of porosity, modulus, and flow rate — losing **1 mark** is common.
> - In numerical questions, **show all substitution steps** — partial marking is strict.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 4]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the **complete CATE workflow** from medical imaging to in-vivo implantation. Highlight the role of **CAD and CAE** in the process. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **Hagen-Poiseuille equation** for nutrient flow through a cylindrical scaffold pore. A scaffold has average pore radius **200 μm** and length **2 mm**, with a pressure difference of **10 kPa**. If the culture medium viscosity is **1 mPa·s**, calculate the volumetric flow rate per pore. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) CATE Workflow (7 marks)**

The complete CATE pipeline is:

**Step 1 — Medical Imaging:** CT/MRI scan of the defect site → produces **DICOM** data of patient anatomy.

**Step 2 — Image Segmentation & Reconstruction:** Software (Mimics, 3D Slicer) segments the region of interest → generates a **3D anatomical model** in STL format.

**Step 3 — CAD Scaffold Design:** Using SolidWorks/Rhino/nTopology, the surgeon/engineer designs the **scaffold with controlled porosity, pore size, and internal architecture**.

**Step 4 — CAE Simulation:** FEA predicts **mechanical strength**, CFD simulates **nutrient/waste transport**, and diffusion analysis evaluates **oxygen gradients**.

**Step 5 — Biomaterial & Bioink Selection:** Hydrogel (alginate, GelMA) is mixed with autologous cells.

**Step 6 — Bio-Additive Manufacturing:** Layer-by-layer bioprinting (extrusion/inkjet/SLA) fabricates the scaffold.

**Step 7 — Post-Processing & Bioreactor:** Perfusion bioreactor provides dynamic mechanical stimulation for **14–28 days** for tissue maturation.

**Step 8 — Surgical Implantation** into the patient.

**Role of CAD:** Enables patient-specific geometric customization and complex internal porous architecture impossible with conventional manufacturing.

**Role of CAE:** Reduces experimental trial-and-error; predicts stress distribution, nutrient diffusion, and degradation kinetics *in silico*.

*(Marking: 5 marks for workflow + 2 marks for CAD/CAE role explanation.)*

---

**(b) Hagen-Poiseuille Derivation (7 marks)**

Starting from Newton's law of viscosity for steady laminar flow in a cylindrical pore:

$$\tau_{rz} = -\mu \frac{dv_z}{dr}$$

**Force balance** on a fluid cylinder of radius $r$ and length $L$:

$$\Delta P \cdot \pi r^{2} = -\tau_{rz} \cdot 2 \pi r \cdot L$$

Solving for shear stress:

$$\tau_{rz} = -\frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2L}$$

Equating to Newton's law:

$$\frac{dv_z}{dr} = \frac{\Delta P \cdot r}{2 \mu L}$$

Integrating with no-slip BC $v_z(R) = 0$:

$$v_z(r) = \frac{\Delta P}{4 \mu L} \left(R^{2} - r^{2}\right)$$

Integrating velocity across the cross-section:

$$Q = \int_0^R v_z(r) \cdot 2\pi r \, dr = \frac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$$

**Numerical Substitution:**

Given: $R = 200 \, \mu\text{m} = 200 \times 10^{-6}$ m, $L = 2$ mm $= 2 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\Delta P = 10$ kPa $= 10^4$ Pa, $\mu = 1$ mPa·s $= 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.

$$Q = \frac{\pi \times 10^4 \times (200 \times 10^{-6})^4}{8 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Numerator:**

$$\pi \times 10^4 \times (200)^4 \times 10^{-24} = \pi \times 10^4 \times 1.6 \times 10^{9} \times 10^{-24}$$

$$= \pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-11} = 5.0265 \times 10^{-11} \text{ Pa·m}^4$$

**Denominator:**

$$8 \times 10^{-3} \times 2 \times 10^{-3} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5} \text{ Pa·s·m}$$

**Result:**

$$Q = \frac{5.0265 \times 10^{-11}}{1.6 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.14 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$\boxed{Q \approx 3.14 \text{ μL/s}}$$

*(Marking: 3 marks derivation + 1 mark formula + 2 marks substitution + 1 mark final answer with units.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 4]

**(a)** Explain the **four major bio-additive manufacturing techniques** with their **resolution, cell viability, and material compatibility**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** What is the **Gibson-Ashby model**? A PCL scaffold has base matrix modulus **400 MPa** and porosity **75 %**. Calculate the effective scaffold modulus using $n = 2$. Comment on the mechanical-biological trade-off. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) BAM Techniques (7 marks)**

| Technique | Working Principle | Resolution | Cell Viability | Material |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Extrusion** | Pneumatic/piston/screw-driven filament deposition | **100–500 μm** | **40–80 %** | Viscous hydrogels (alginate, GelMA) |
| **Inkjet** | Droplet ejection via thermal/piezoelectric actuator | **10–50 μm** | **> 85 %** | Low-viscosity bioinks |
| **Laser-Assisted (LIFT)** | Laser pulse transfers bioink from donor ribbon to substrate | **10–100 μm** | **> 95 %** | High-viscosity, solid bioinks |
| **Stereolithography (SLA/DLP)** | Photopolymerization of bio-resin layer-by-layer | **25–100 μm** | **> 85 %** | Photocurable bio-resins (GelMA) |

*Key advantage of each: Extrusion = high density; Inkjet = high speed; LIFT = nozzle-free (avoids clogging); SLA = high resolution.*

*(Marking: 1.5 marks per technique × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for comparison/choice.)*

---

**(b) Gibson-Ashby Model (7 marks)**

The **Gibson-Ashby model** is an empirical constitutive relation describing the **mechanical behavior of cellular solids (foams/scaffolds)** based on their relative density. For open-cell polymer foams:

$$\frac{E^*}{E_s} = C \left( \frac{\rho^*}{\rho_s} \right)^{n}$$

where $C \approx 1$, $n \approx 2$ for open-cell structures, and relative density $\rho^*/\rho_s = 1 - P$.

**Simplified Form:**

$$E_{\text{scaffold}} = E_{\text{matrix}} (1 - P)^{n}$$

**Numerical Calculation:**

Given: $E_{\text{matrix}} = 400$ MPa, $P = 0.75$, $n = 2$.

$$E_{\text{scaffold}} = 400 \times (1 - 0.75)^2$$

$$= 400 \times (0.25)^2 = 400 \times 0.0625$$

$$\boxed{E_{\text{scaffold}} = 25 \text{ MPa}}$$

**Mechanical-Biological Trade-off:**

Increasing porosity (P) → improves **cell infiltration, vascularization, and nutrient diffusion** (biological benefit) BUT → drastically **reduces mechanical strength** (engineering drawback). For example, increasing porosity from 50 % to 75 % reduces modulus from **100 MPa → 25 MPa** (a **75 % drop**). Hence, the design engineer must **optimize P** such that scaffold mechanics match native tissue *and* biology is supported.

*(Marking: 2 marks model + 2 marks derivation of simplified form + 2 marks calculation + 1 mark trade-off discussion.)*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for CATE (Module 4):**

- [ ] **CATE** = Computer-Aided Tissue Engineering = digital design + biofabrication of living tissue
- [ ] **Tissue Engineering Triad** = **Scaffold + Cells + Signals** (high-yield 3-marker)
- [ ] **CATE Workflow** = 7 steps: Imaging → Segmentation → CAD → CAE → Bioink → BAM → Bioreactor
- [ ] **Ideal scaffold porosity ≥ 70 %**, pore size **100–500 μm**, interconnectivity **> 90 %**
- [ ] **Bioink** = cells + hydrogel (NOT the same as biomaterial ink)
- [ ] **Four BAM techniques**: Extrusion, Inkjet, LIFT, SLA — know resolution & viability
- [ ] **Hagen-Poiseuille:** $Q = \dfrac{\pi \Delta P R^{4}}{8 \mu L}$ (R⁴ dependence)
- [ ] **Darcy's Law:** $k = \dfrac{\mu Q L}{A \Delta P}$ (permeability of porous scaffold)
- [ ] **Gibson-Ashby:** $E_{\text{scaffold}} = E_{\text{matrix}}(1-P)^n$, $n \approx 2$
- [ ] **Fick's 1st Law:** $J = -D \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ (nutrient diffusion)
- [ ] **Cell shear stress** during extrusion must be **< 5 kPa** for viability
- [ ] **Péclet number** $P_e$ compares advection vs diffusion; **Damköhler** compares reaction vs diffusion
- [ ] **FDM** is used for **synthetic (cell-free) scaffolds** requiring post-seeding; **Extrusion/SLA** for cell-laden bioinks
- [ ] **Patient-specific design** is the key differentiator of CATE over conventional implants
- [ ] **FEA + CFD** reduce in-vitro trial-and-error; predict mechanics and nutrient transport
- [ ] **Mechanobiology trade-off:** ↑ porosity → ↑ biology, ↓ mechanics — must optimize

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Case Studies
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEPOT634 - ADDITIVE MANUFACTURING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Case Studies

# Case Studies in Additive Manufacturing

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Case Study** in Additive Manufacturing (AM) is a systematic, evidence-based investigation of a real-world industrial, medical, aerospace, automotive, or consumer application where AM technologies have been deployed to solve specific engineering challenges. Each case study captures the **design intent**, **process selection rationale**, **material justification**, **post-processing workflow**, **performance validation**, and **commercial / functional outcomes** of an AM-produced part or system.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus, Module 4 evaluates the student's ability to correlate theoretical AM principles with industrial translation — i.e., translating **Design for Additive Manufacturing (DfAM)**, process capability, and material behavior into tangible, validated products.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Case studies are NOT abstract descriptions. The examiner expects a structured discussion linking **Process ↔ Material ↔ Geometry ↔ Application ↔ Outcome (P-M-G-A-O framework)**.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a chef who learns every cooking technique (boiling, frying, baking, grilling). Knowing the techniques is necessary, but **insight** comes from studying signature recipes — *why* a soufflé is baked rather than fried, *why* a steak is seared on cast iron instead of a non-stick pan. Similarly, AM theory teaches the *processes*, but **case studies teach the engineering judgment** of *which process, which material, which geometry solves which real problem*.

Each case study is essentially a **"signature recipe of the modern manufacturing engineer."**

### 1.3 The P-M-G-A-O Analytical Framework

Every case study analyzed in this module can be broken down using the structured **P-M-G-A-O framework**:

| Letter | Stands For          | What It Describes                                                  |
|--------|---------------------|--------------------------------------------------------------------|
| **P**  | Process             | Specific AM technology used (SLS, SLA, DMLS, FDM, EBM, BJ, etc.)   |
| **M**  | Material            | Polymer, metal alloy, ceramic, composite, or biomaterial used       |
| **G**  | Geometry            | Complexity enabled: lattice, internal channels, topology-optimized  |
| **A**  | Application         | Industry sector and functional purpose of the part                 |
| **O**  | Outcome             | Performance gain, mass reduction, lead-time reduction, cost impact |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics in AM Case Studies:**
> - **Mass Reduction:** typically **15%–60%** versus conventionally machined parts.
> - **Lead-Time Compression:** part consolidation from **20+ components → 1 component**.
> - **Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BTF):** reduced from **>10:1 to ~1:1** in aerospace.
> - **Time-to-Market:** prototype iteration reduced from **weeks to days**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of AM Case Studies by Industry Sector

KTU examiners typically group case studies into **five canonical sectors**. Each sector exploits a specific strength of AM technology.

### 2.2 Aerospace Case Studies — The Flagship Domain

Aerospace is the **highest-impact** adopter of metal AM because:
- Buy-to-Fly ratio is critical (titanium is expensive).
- Lightweighting translates directly to fuel burn / payload.
- Complex internal lattice structures cannot be cast or machined.

**Sector Strength:** Topology optimization + lattice infill + part consolidation.

### 2.3 Medical & Biomedical Case Studies

Medical AM focuses on **patient-specific customization** (mass personalization).

**Sector Strength:** Anatomical data (CT/MRI) → STL → Custom implants.

### 2.4 Automotive Case Studies

Automotive focuses on **lightweighting, rapid prototyping, and custom low-volume tooling**.

**Sector Strength:** Rapid iteration + functional end-use parts.

### 2.5 Consumer, Architecture, and Food Case Studies

These case studies demonstrate **non-traditional** AM adoption where design freedom, mass customization, and tooling elimination dominate the value proposition.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet — Metrics Used in Case Study Analysis

The following equations are routinely used by KTU examiners when comparing conventional manufacturing with AM in case studies.

**Buy-to-Fly Ratio (BTF):**

$$\text{BTF} = \frac{M_{\text{raw stock}}}{M_{\text{final part}}}$$

where $M_{\text{raw stock}}$ is the mass of the initial billet and $M_{\text{final part}}$ is the mass of the finished part.

**Mass Reduction Percentage:**

$$\Delta m_{\%} = \frac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100$$

where $m_{\text{conv}}$ is the conventional part mass and $m_{\text{AM}}$ is the additively manufactured part mass.

**Part Consolidation Ratio:**

$$N_{c} = \frac{N_{\text{conv}}}{N_{\text{AM}}}$$

where $N_{\text{conv}}$ is the number of components in a conventional assembly and $N_{\text{AM}}$ is the number of components after AM consolidation.

**Volumetric Build Time (approximation):**

$$t_{\text{build}} = \frac{V_{\text{part}}}{R_{\text{dep}}}$$

where $V_{\text{part}}$ is the part volume (in $\text{cm}^3$) and $R_{\text{dep}}$ is the volumetric deposition rate (in $\text{cm}^3/\text{h}$).

**Effective Cost per Part:**

$$C_{\text{eff}} = C_{\text{material}} + C_{\text{machine-time}} + C_{\text{post-proc}} + C_{\text{tooling-amortized}}$$

where each term represents the respective cost contribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Reality:** In AM case studies, $C_{\text{tooling-amortized}}$ is **zero** for low-volume production. This is the *primary* economic justification for AM in niche / low-volume parts.

### 2.7 Industrial Utility of These Equations

- **BTF** is the aerospace engineer's "litmus test" — a value of **>5:1** means conventional subtractive machining is economically absurd for the chosen alloy.
- **$\Delta m_{\%}$** is quoted in KTU answer scripts to justify **lightweighting** claims.
- **$N_c$** is quoted to justify **part consolidation** claims.
- **$t_{\text{build}}$** is used to compare process productivity (e.g., DMLS vs. EBM).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Analysis of Representative AM Case Studies

This section provides an exhaustive, multi-step analytical breakdown of four benchmark case studies that frequently appear in KTU university examinations.

### 3.1 Case Study A — GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle (Aerospace, DMLS)

**Background:** The GE LEAP fuel nozzle was the **first mass-produced, FAA-certified 3D-printed engine part** in commercial aviation history.

**Conventional Part (Reference):** The nozzle was originally an assembly of **18 individual parts** welded together from Inconel 718.

#### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify P, M, G, A, O:**
- **P (Process):** Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) on an EOS M280 / Concept Laser M2 system.
- **M (Material):** Inconel 718 (nickel-based superalloy), chosen for its high-temperature creep resistance beyond **650 °C**.
- **G (Geometry):** Topology-optimized, internal conformal cooling/lubrication channels that follow the fuel flow path (not straight bores).
- **A (Application):** Combustion fuel nozzle in the LEAP-1A/1B/1C turbofan engine used on Airbus A320neo, Boeing 737 MAX, and COMAC C919.
- **O (Outcome):** Part count reduced from **18 → 1**, mass reduced by **~25%**, and durability increased by **5×** compared to the conventional nozzle.

**Step 2 — Apply Part Consolidation Equation:**

The original assembly has $N_{\text{conv}} = 18$ components. The AM version has $N_{\text{AM}} = 1$.

$$N_c = \frac{N_{\text{conv}}}{N_{\text{AM}}} = \frac{18}{1} = 18$$

Therefore, the part consolidation ratio is **18**, meaning 18 discrete components were replaced by a single printed part.

**Step 3 — Apply Mass Reduction Equation:**

Let $m_{\text{conv}} = 320 \text{ g}$ (typical published reference value) and $m_{\text{AM}} = 240 \text{ g}$.

$$\Delta m_{\%} = \frac{320 - 240}{320} \times 100 = \frac{80}{320} \times 100$$

$$\Delta m_{\%} = 25\%$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the Buy-to-Fly Ratio:**

For Inconel 718 billet machining, the BTF ratio of the conventional nozzle is approximately **6:1**. For the DMLS nozzle, BTF approaches **1:1** because powder is melted only where needed.

$$\text{BTF}_{\text{conv}} \approx 6:1, \quad \text{BTF}_{\text{AM}} \approx 1:1$$

**Step 5 — Engineering Justification:**

The internal conformal flow channels in the DMLS nozzle cannot be produced by conventional drilling because drill bits are straight and cannot create curved internal manifolds. This geometric freedom is **the defining advantage** of AM over subtractive processes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Scale:** GE Aviation committed to producing **100,000+** such 3D-printed fuel nozzles cumulatively across the LEAP engine program — the largest single AM deployment in aerospace history.

### 3.2 Case Study B — SpaceX SuperDraco Engine Chamber (Aerospace, DMLS)

**Background:** SpaceX redesigned the SuperDraco rocket engine combustion chamber using DMLS, allowing the Crew Dragon spacecraft to perform **propulsive landing and launch-escape maneuvers**.

#### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify P, M, G, A, O:**
- **P (Process):** DMLS using a customized EOS M400-4 quad-laser system.
- **M (Material):** Inconel 625 (nickel superalloy with superior corrosion resistance).
- **G (Geometry):** Regeneratively-cooled thrust chamber with internal **lattice-structured cooling channels**.
- **A (Application):** Crew Dragon launch escape / supersonic retro-propulsion system.
- **O (Outcome):** Thrust chamber produced as a **single piece**, eliminating **~200+** weld joints.

**Step 2 — Discuss Material Selection Rationale:**

Inconel 625 was selected over Inconel 718 because:
- Better weldability for post-print HIP (Hot Isostatic Pressing).
- Superior corrosion resistance against the **NTO/MMH** hypergolic propellant combination.
- Higher ductility for the **launch-escape shock loading** profile.

**Step 3 — Discuss Cooling Channel Innovation:**

The chamber uses **printed regenerative cooling channels** that follow the contour of the throat contour at variable cross-section. This cannot be produced by any conventional process because:
- Conventional channels are drilled (straight) or cast (limited geometric freedom).
- Variable cross-section enables **local heat-flux matching**.

**Step 4 — Discuss Qualification Challenges:**

The chamber must undergo:
- X-ray CT inspection for internal defects.
- HIP at approximately **1163 °C / 100 MPa** for pore closure.
- Pressure burst testing at **>1.5×** operating pressure.

### 3.3 Case Study C — Custom Cranial Implant (Medical, EBM/PBF)

**Background:** Patient-specific cranial implants are produced from CT scan data for cranioplasty patients (trauma / tumor reconstruction).

#### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Workflow Overview:**

The medical AM workflow is unique. It begins with **patient imaging**, not a CAD model.

$$\text{CT/MRI Scan} \rightarrow \text{DICOM Data} \rightarrow \text{STL Reconstruction} \rightarrow \text{CAD Design} \rightarrow \text{AM Build} \rightarrow \text{Implant}$$

**Step 2 — Identify P, M, G, A, O:**
- **P (Process):** Electron Beam Melting (EBM, Arcam Q20plus) or Laser Powder Bed Fusion (LPBF).
- **M (Material):** Ti-6Al-4V (medical grade, ELI grade — Extra Low Interstitial).
- **G (Geometry):** Patient-matched curvature with optional porous mesh interface to promote **osseointegration**.
- **A (Application):** Cranial defect reconstruction in neurosurgery.
- **O (Outcome):** Surgery time reduced by **~30–50%**, fit accuracy within **<1 mm**, no intra-operative shaping required.

**Step 3 — Justify Ti-6Al-4V Selection:**

Ti-6Al-4V is chosen because:
- **Biocompatible** (ISO 5832-3 compliant).
- **Elastic modulus ≈ 110 GPa** — closer to bone (~15–20 GPa) than CoCrMo (~210 GPa), reducing **stress shielding**.
- Excellent corrosion resistance from the passive TiO$_2$ oxide layer.

**Step 4 — Discuss Post-Processing:**

- Annealing at **~700–750 °C** to relieve residual stress.
- Pickling / passivation for surface oxide uniformity.
- Sterilization via **gamma irradiation** or **autoclaving at 134 °C**.
- Final dimensional CT scan of the implant before surgery.

### 3.4 Case Study D — 3D-Printed Hearing Aid Shells (Consumer Medical, SLA/MJF)

**Background:** The global hearing aid industry was **the first** to fully transition to mass-customized AM production. By **~2017**, more than **99%** of custom in-ear hearing aids were 3D-printed.

#### Step-by-Step Analysis

**Step 1 — Identify P, M, G, A, O:**
- **P (Process):** Stereolithography (SLA, originally) → Multi Jet Fusion (MJF, currently) for higher throughput.
- **M (Material):** Photopolymer resin (SLA era) → PA12 (Nylon 12) via MJF.
- **G (Geometry):** Fully bespoke shape derived from **ear-canal impression scan**, capturing micro-features like the **anti-helix** and **concha bowl**.
- **A (Application):** Custom in-canal hearing aid shells.
- **O (Outcome):** Mass customization at scale, lead time reduced from **weeks → 1–2 days**, comfort fit precision.

**Step 2 — Explain the Mass Customization Logic:**

Each patient's ear canal is **unique** (length, curvature, cross-section). Conventional shell production required:
- Hand-labored acrylic shells (3–5 hours per shell).
- Multiple patient visits for fitting.
- Skilled labor scarcity.

With AM, the entire workflow is digitized:

$$\text{Ear Impression} \rightarrow \text{3D Scan} \rightarrow \text{CAD Adjustment} \rightarrow \text{SLA/MJF Print} \rightarrow \text{Shell Finish}$$

**Step 3 — Engineering Insight:**

This case study is frequently cited in KTU exams to demonstrate that AM is not limited to high-cost aerospace — it **democratizes** manufacturing for low-cost, high-volume medical consumer products.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 P-M-G-A-O Analytical Framework Flow

The diagram below shows how a case study is systematically decomposed for KTU-style answers.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[AM Case Study] --> B[Process P]
    A --> C[Material M]
    A --> D[Geometry G]
    A --> E[Application A]
    A --> F[Outcome O]
    B --> B1[DMLS]
    B --> B2[SLA]
    B --> B3[EBM]
    B --> B4[FDM]
    B --> B5[SLS]
    C --> C1[Inconel 718]
    C --> C2[Ti-6Al-4V]
    C --> C3[PA12 Nylon]
    C --> C4[Photopolymer]
    D --> D1[Lattice]
    D --> D2[Topology Opt]
    D --> D3[Conformal Channel]
    D --> D4[Bionic Shape]
    E --> E1[Aerospace]
    E --> E2[Medical]
    E --> E3[Automotive]
    E --> E4[Consumer]
    F --> F1[Mass Reduction]
    F --> F2[Lead Time]
    F --> F3[Part Consolidation]
    F --> F4[Customization]
```

### 4.2 Aerospace AM Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the full workflow of a metallic aerospace AM case study (e.g., LEAP nozzle or SuperDraco chamber).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[CAD Design] --> A2[Topology Optimization]
    A2 --> A3[STL Slicing]
    A3 --> A4[DMLS Build Chamber]
    A4 --> A5[Powder Bed Fusion]
    A5 --> A6[Stress Relief Anneal]
    A6 --> A7[HIP Densification]
    A7 --> A8[CNC Interface Machining]
    A8 --> A9[CT Inspection]
    A9 --> A10[Surface Finish]
    A10 --> A11[QA Certification]
    A11 --> A12[FAA Certification]
    A12 --> A13[Flight Integration]
```

### 4.3 Medical AM Workflow — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The diagram below maps the patient-specific medical AM case study workflow.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M1[Patient CT or MRI Scan] --> M2[DICOM Data]
    M2 --> M3[STL Reconstruction]
    M3 --> M4[Segmentation Software]
    M4 --> M5[Implant CAD]
    M5 --> M6[Lattice Interface Design]
    M6 --> M7[EBM or LPBF Build]
    M7 --> M8[Heat Treatment]
    M8 --> M9[Surface Finish]
    M9 --> M10[CT Verification Scan]
    M10 --> M11[Sterilization]
    M11 --> M12[Surgical Implant]
```

### 4.4 Industry vs. AM Strength Mapping

The diagram below maps each industrial sector with the AM strength it primarily exploits.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Aerospace] --> T1[Lattice and Topology Opt]
    S2[Medical] --> T2[Patient Specific Customization]
    S3[Automotive] --> T3[Rapid Prototyping]
    S4[Consumer] --> T4[Mass Customization]
    S5[Architecture] --> T5[Geometric Freedom]
    S6[Defense] --> T6[Supply Chain Resilience]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List any three industrial case studies where additive manufacturing has been successfully deployed, and identify the AM process used in each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle (Aerospace):** Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DMLS) of Inconel 718; 18 parts consolidated to 1.
2. **Patient-Specific Cranial Implant (Medical):** Electron Beam Melting (EBM) of Ti-6Al-4V; reconstructed from CT data.
3. **Custom Hearing Aid Shell (Consumer Medical):** Stereolithography (SLA) / Multi Jet Fusion (MJF) of photopolymer resin or PA12 Nylon; mass-customized.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define Buy-to-Fly (BTF) ratio. Why is BTF important in aerospace AM case studies?*

**Model Answer:**

$$\text{BTF} = \frac{M_{\text{raw stock}}}{M_{\text{final part}}}$$

BTF measures material efficiency. In aerospace, raw stock (e.g., Inconel or Titanium billet) is extremely expensive. A conventional machined part may have BTF of **>10:1**, meaning **90%+ of the billet is wasted as chips**. AM reduces BTF toward **1:1** because powder is selectively melted only where required, dramatically lowering material cost and improving sustainability.

---

### Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module-Level, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Many students write **only the application** without quantifying it using **$\Delta m_{\%}$, $N_c$, or BTF**. Always attach a number to your claim.
> 2. Do not confuse **process names** — *DMLS, SLM, and LPBF* are similar but technically distinct; use the correct term as per the original case study.
> 3. Always mention the **post-processing chain** (stress relief, HIP, surface finish, inspection). Skipping this loses **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Bilateral symmetry** in answers: For each AM advantage, briefly note the **limitation** (residual stress, surface roughness, cost). This shows examiner awareness and earns full marks.

---

#### Question A — 14 Marks (Option 1)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] With reference to the **GE LEAP fuel nozzle** case study, explain how Direct Metal Laser Sintering (DLS/DMLS) enabled part consolidation. Compute the part consolidation ratio and discuss the engineering rationale for using **Inconel 718**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Discuss the **post-processing workflow** required to qualify an aerospace DMLS part for flight certification. Include at least **three** post-processing steps and **one** non-destructive testing method.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Process Identification:**
The GE LEAP fuel nozzle is manufactured using **DMLS (Direct Metal Laser Sintering)**, where a Yb-fiber laser selectively melts **Inconel 718** powder layer-by-layer (typical layer thickness **20–40 μm**).

**Step 2 — Original Assembly:**
The conventional nozzle was an assembly of **18 welded components** (fuel manifold, swirl vanes, feed arms, etc.).

**Step 3 — Part Consolidation:**
The DMLS version prints all 18 functions into **one monolithic part**.

$$N_c = \frac{N_{\text{conv}}}{N_{\text{AM}}} = \frac{18}{1} = 18$$

**Step 4 — Why Inconel 718:**
- High-temperature strength retention above **650 °C**.
- Excellent oxidation and corrosion resistance in combustion environments.
- Well-characterized for AM (extensive EOS parameter library).
- Compatible with Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP) for densification.

**Step 5 — Mass Reduction:**
Published mass reduction: **~25%** versus conventional nozzle.

$$\Delta m_{\%} = \frac{m_{\text{conv}} - m_{\text{AM}}}{m_{\text{conv}}} \times 100 = \frac{320 - 240}{320} \times 100 = 25\%$$

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The post-processing chain typically includes:

1. **Stress Relief Annealing** — Heating to ~**1060 °C** for Inconel 718 to relieve residual stress from rapid solidification.
2. **Hot Isostatic Pressing (HIP)** — High-pressure inert gas treatment (~**100–200 MPa** at ~**1160–1200 °C**) to close internal pores and improve fatigue life.
3. **Support Removal and Surface Machining** — CNC machining of critical interfaces (bolt patterns, sealing surfaces) to achieve required tolerances.
4. **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT):** Industrial **Computed Tomography (CT) scanning** is used to detect internal defects below **~50 μm**.

These steps collectively transition the part from "as-built" to "flight-qualified" condition.

---

#### Question B — 14 Marks (Option 2)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** [7 Marks] Describe the **patient-specific cranial implant** case study in medical additive manufacturing. Explain the workflow from CT scan to surgical implant and justify the use of **Ti-6Al-4V (ELI)**.

**(b)** [7 Marks] Explain why the **hearing aid industry** became the first sector to fully transition to mass-customized AM production. List two AM processes used and discuss the engineering rationale.

**Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Workflow (P-M-G-A-O mapping):**

- **P:** Electron Beam Melting (EBM) — Arcam Q20plus, or alternatively Laser PBF.
- **M:** Ti-6Al-4V ELI (Extra Low Interstitial) — medical grade per ASTM F136 / ISO 5832-3.
- **G:** Patient-matched curvature + porous interface for bone in-growth.
- **A:** Cranioplasty for skull defects (trauma / tumor).
- **O:** Surgery time reduced by **30–50%**; fit accuracy **<1 mm**.

**Workflow Steps:**

$$\text{Patient CT Scan} \rightarrow \text{DICOM} \rightarrow \text{STL} \rightarrow \text{Segmentation} \rightarrow \text{CAD} \rightarrow \text{EBM Print} \rightarrow \text{HT + Finish} \rightarrow \text{Sterilization} \rightarrow \text{Implant}$$

**Why Ti-6Al-4V ELI:**
- **Biocompatible** (no adverse tissue reaction).
- **Elastic modulus ≈ 110 GPa** — closer to cortical bone (~15–20 GPa) than CoCr alloys, reducing **stress shielding**.
- The **ELI grade** restricts interstitial O, N, C content, improving fracture toughness and ductility — critical for load-bearing implant sites.
- **Passive TiO₂ oxide layer** provides exceptional corrosion resistance in physiological saline.

**Model Solution — Part (b):**

The hearing aid industry transitioned to AM because:

1. **Every ear canal is geometrically unique** — mass customization is mandatory, not optional.
2. **Conventional shell production** required hand-crafted acrylic shells (~3–5 hours of skilled labor per shell).
3. **AM digitization** shortened lead time from **weeks → 1–2 days**.

**Two AM Processes Used:**
- **SLA (Stereolithography)** — Originally used with photopolymer resin for fine surface detail.
- **MJF (Multi Jet Fusion, HP)** — Currently preferred, using **PA12 Nylon**, offering higher throughput and superior mechanical robustness.

**Engineering Rationale:**
- Patient's ear impression is **3D scanned** to create a digital model.
- Shell is **CAD-modified** (vent holes, receiver channel, faceplate fit) and printed.
- Post-processing includes **UV curing** (SLA) or **bead blasting** (MJF).
- The result is a **precisely fitting, comfortable, custom device** with no manual artistry required.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Always use the P-M-G-A-O framework** when answering AM case study questions.
- **Quantify your claims:** never say "mass reduced" — say **"$\Delta m_{\%} = 25\%$"** or similar.
- **Top 4 Must-Know Case Studies for KTU 2024 Scheme:**
  1. **GE LEAP Fuel Nozzle** — DMLS / Inconel 718 / Aerospace / 18→1 part consolidation / ~25% mass reduction.
  2. **SpaceX SuperDraco Chamber** — DMLS / Inconel 625 / Regeneratively cooled / 200+ weld joints eliminated.
  3. **Custom Cranial Implant** — EBM / Ti-6Al-4V ELI / Medical / CT-based customization.
  4. **Hearing Aid Shells** — SLA or MJF / PA12 or Photopolymer / Consumer Medical / Mass customization.
- **Always mention at least one post-processing step** (stress relief, HIP, surface finish, NDT).
- **Always note one limitation** of AM (residual stress, surface roughness, build size, cost of powder).
- **Key equations to memorize:**
  - BTF ratio
  - Mass reduction percentage $\Delta m_{\%}$
  - Part consolidation ratio $N_c$
  - Build time $t_{\text{build}} = V_{\text{part}}/R_{\text{dep}}$
- **Hot topics to remember:** Buy-to-Fly ratio, lattice structures, topology optimization, patient-specific implants, conformal cooling, mass customization.
- **KTU frequently used taglines:** *"Lightweighting," "part consolidation," "mass customization," "tooling elimination," "supply chain resilience," "design freedom."*
- **Common powder materials to know:** Inconel 718, Inconel 625, Ti-6Al-4V, AlSi10Mg, 316L Stainless Steel, CoCrMo, PA12, PA11, ABS, photopolymer resins.
- **Process names — do not confuse:** DMLS ≠ SLM ≠ EBM ≠ BJ ≠ MJF. Know which process suits which case.
